Introduction

Vernacular architecture is now the term most widely used to
denote indigenous, tribal, folk, peasant and traditional architecture. The intention ofthe Encyclopedia of Vernacular Architecture
ofthe World (acronym EVAW) is to co-ordinate knowledge of
such building traditions in all continents and to compile and
publish this as an accessible resource. No comparable work on
the vernacular traditions in world architecture has been published to-date.

The Encyclopedia covers traditional self-built and community-built buildings, both secular and sacred. Knowledge of
earlier building types in many parts ofthe world is limited since
serious research is comparatively recent; consequently, the
focus ofthe Encyclopedia is on vernacular architecture that has
been in use in the zoth century.

As building traditions vary widely within some countries and
extend across the political boundaries ofothers, the Encyclopedia considers vernacular architecture within its environmental
and cultural, rather than its national, contexts. Environmental
conditions, material resources, structural systems and technologies have bearing on architectural form. Similarly, many
aspects ofsocial structure, belief systems and behavioural patterns strongly influence building types, their functions and
meanings, though they differ in their specific applications
among peoples throughout the world. To accommodate this
dual nature of vernacular architecture the work is compiled in
two parts of which Part One is concerned with Theories and
Principles and Part Two with Cultures and Habitats.

Defining Vernacular Architecture

A number of attempts have been made to find an overall definition ofvernacular architecture. It is not surprising that these
attempts have been unsuccessful for the term is used to embrace
an immense range ofbuildingtypes, forms, traditions, uses and
contexts. Architecture has been frequently defined as the science (orart) ofbuilding, the word being derived from the Latin
architectura. In turn this stems from the Greek arkhitekton, the
combination ofarchos, chief, and tekton, builder, thus placing the
emphasis on the master builder and the product ofhis design and
construction. In conventional histories and encyclopedias the
word architecture has been applied to monumental and prestigious buildings thatsignify power orwealth-palaces, temples,
cathedrals, seats of government, airports, opera houses among
them. Many of these were designed by master builders or by
their successors, professional architects, to whom occupationally differentiated societies have delegated the responsibility to
identify and solve the problems of design and construction.
Directly, as master craftsmen, or indirectly, as architect-designers, they have led the teams that have erected the buildings. Mil-

itary works and public utilities designed by engineers are the
product ofsimilar processes ofproblem isolation and solution.
While many are not buildings, engineering works greatly
increase the number ofdesigner-built occupiable constructions.

However, by far the majority of buildings are dwellings, and
in much of the world these are still built by their owners, by
communities that pool resources, or by local specialized
builders and craftsmen. Generallyunknown and, until recently,
little studied, these builders have shaped much of man’s built
environment, even if they have not been responsible for its
most massive constructions. Their works comprise the subject
of this Encyclopedia. Numerous encyclopedias of architecture
have been published in Europe and the United States, concerned with both historical and modern architecture, principally ofthe western tradition and, more rarely, with Indian and
Oriental architecture. Apart from token references to ‘primitive’ architecture most of these works are devoted entirely to
formal and monumental structures, with considerable emphasis laid upon designs by professional architects in the West.
The majority of the world’s buildings are systematically omitted from these publications; in others, recognition is made to
them only in passing. Consequently, there has been no generallyaccepted term for this neglected heritage. Even in the 1980s
the phrase ‘primitive architecture’ was used where a distinction
was being made between the primal and the cultivated, with no
apparent awareness ofits perjorative implications.

The term ‘shelter’ was one I used formerly to emphasize the
fundamental motivation for building, but its application to
many buildings is inadequate. Some writers have referred to
“indigenous architecture’, though much non-formal architecture was built by immigrants and colonists and not by indigenes. ‘Anonymous architecture’ reflects the observer’s bias
towards buildings by named architects, while ‘spontaneous
architecture’ implies that buildings are constructed without
conscious volition. Though it is popular in the United States,
the term ‘folk architecture’ has overtones of class distinction
elsewhere, while adjectives such as ‘peasant’ or ‘rural’ are too
narrowly applied. Most widely used of the alternative terms is
“traditional architecture’ but while most of the building forms
discussed in this Encyclopedia embody traditions, the phrase is
also broadly applicable to a variety of monumental and architect-designed constructions.

In the study of language the vernacular is the indigenous
idiom; the word derives from the Latin vernaculus, native.
Extending the linguistic analogy that is frequently applied to
the language, grammar and syntax, and even the style or manner of its expression of formal architecture, the vernacular is
the local or regional dialect, the common speech of building.

TE 95, 0 a

## S. 4

Introduction

As such it comprises a range of building traditions as wide as
that ofthe linguistic traditions. First used in England in 1839, it
was subsequently employed intermittently, only gaining a
wider application in the 1950. Sometimes tribal and localized,
sometimes of peasant origin, sometimes unified by resources
or religion, vernacular traditions are extremely diverse. They
can be traced across sub-continents though, like languages,
they may develop dialectual differences, in some cases being
found uniquely among insular communities.

Distinetions can be made between formal, architect-designed
architecture and vernacular architecture, and between these
and what may be termed popular architecture, Since the close
of the ıgth century the extensive growth of suburbs, designed
and built as speculative developments, has changed the interface of town and country. Such popular architecture has been
supported by the provision of shops, public facilities, places of
entertainment and commercial services related to food and
transport. Frequently designed with corporate imagery, they
have been termed a zoth-century vernacular by some authors,
especially in the United States. They are not the subject of the
present work, which recognizes a broad distinction between
such popular architecture and the vernacular, even if it is not
clearly defined in some instances. Since World War II, however,
there has been a phenomenal growth in unauthorized or squatter settlements around the cities of the developing world, and
increasingly, in many cities of the industrialized nations.
Owner-built in the majority of instances, they have been
regarded, with more justification, as neo-vernacular. While they
differ in many respects from the vernacular traditions represented here, acknowledgement of the relationship of squatter
settlements to the vernacular is made in anumber ofentries. To
compound the problems of nomenclature ‘neo-vernacular’ is
sometimes applied to architect-designed buildings influenced
by, orin the forms of, a vernacular tradition.

Atthe close ofthe 2oth century, itis evident that the majority
ofthe peoples of the world still live, work and worship in vernacular buildings. In the order of 800 million dwellings come
within this immense but largely unrecognized phenomenon,
ranging from simple shelters to many-storeyed tower houses.
Ofthese the majority that survive are rural, relating to different
kinds of economy, from pursuit to pastoralism, mixed agriculture to commodity production and marketing. In towns and
cities where protection or communication were facilitated by
close proximity, houses and vernacular buildings from which
authority was exercised are notinfrequent, and shrines, temples
or other places ofworship are ubiquitous. Where manufacture
and trading economies have flourished specialized accommodations may be found as natural resources have been exploited
with the aid oftechnologies harnessing wind, water or animal
power. While the greater proportion of urban buildings were
architect-designed, or were contractor-built popular architecture, a significant number are survivors ofvernacular traditions.

With such a breadth of generalized categories of building in
conditions that are so diverse, it is reasonable to question
whether there is any justification for the consideration of vernacular architecture as a single phenomenon. Nevertheless, a
number ofcharacteristics can be identified which are common

xx

to a great many types of vernacular architecture, and some that
are common to most. Every building exists in an environmental
context, whether it is situated in the depth of the forest or
exposed on the plain, clinging to the mountain slope or rising
from the desert. Conditioned by the capacity ofthe land to support a given population, the economy of a culture affects the
choice ofsite. In turn, this has bearing on the structures thatare
possible, for each building has to be constructed of materials
which, in the vernacular, are most frequently obtained locally
from the natural resources ofaregion. Subjecttothevagaries of
weather, buildings are required to protect their occupants from
environmental extremes and to create a micro-climate compatible with human physical comfort. By satisfying the demands
placed upon it by the nature of its site, and the forces exerted
upon it by the loads that it carries and the capacity ofthe materials employed to meet them, a vernacular building in one part
of the world may display solutions to structural problems that
are similar to those in other regions. In technology, as in function, they may be seen to be typologically similar, though they
are created and sustained through independent evolution and
culturally accumulated wisdom.

Itis a particular characteristic ofvernacular architecture that
each tradition is intimately related to social and economic
imperatives; it has developed to meet specific needs within
each cultural milieu. Seen in the context ofthe hamlet, village
or town, the larger frame ofthe social structure has bearing on
the spatial organization of settlements. Families may require
proximity to kinsfolk and clan members or to the sites associated with ancestors. Exogamous marriage, inheritance rights
and other specifics of custom and tradition can affect location
and settlement growth. Within the dwelling, customary norms
in organizing and utilizing space may persist through generations, requiring places for working and sleeping, or for the
preparation and cooking offood. Often they are gender or hierarchically differentiated, changing rarely and only as societies
change.

Vernacular builders are customarily from the communities
which use the structures and are frequently owner-builderoccupiers, the processes of building being learned by each successive generation. In some societies craftsmen may be more
specialized and, occasionally, may be organized in guilds. In
others, rule systems or geomancy may give directions for location, construction or use, such esoteric knowledge being the
preserve of a priesthood which gives guidance to the builders.
Vernacular architecture generally embodies community values,
and less evidently, may symbolize concepts of the cosmos, or
act as an analogue for the abstractions of belief. Thus, even a
simple dwelling may reflect both the material and spiritual
worlds ofits builders and occupiers.

Itis evidentthatthe range offorms ofconstruction, variety of
uses, layers of meanings and complexity of cultural milieu of
vernacular buildings is multifarious. Therefore, to seeka single
definition ofvernacular architecture is probably ill-advised, for
it attempts to reduce the richness and diversity of these traditions to a simplistic description, inevitably diminishing them
in the process. But in summary and for the present undertaking, the following is offered as a working definition:

Vernacular architecture comprises the dwellings and all other
buildings ofthe people. Related to their environmental contexts
and available resources, they are customarily owner- or community-built, utilizing traditional technologies. All forms of
vernacular architecture are built to meet specific needs, accommodating the values, economies and ways of living of the cultures that produce them.

The Study ofVernacular Architecture

Before the late ıgth century most writings on the subject were
embedded in travellers’ and adventurers’ accounts, sometimes
included in reports for scientific expeditions but often as part of
the record of individually motivated explorations. Many of
these are informative on ‘pre-contact’ indigenous architectural
traditions, tempered by the knowledge, curiosity and opinions
of the observer. With the development of ethnographical
research in support of archaeological, evolutionary and other
theories, more systematic studies integrated buildings within
the record of material culture, particularly as they represented
‘primitive’ man and ‘savage’ states of society — terms which
persisted in writing and research for many decades.

With the expansionism oftthe western colonial powers such
studies were given a particular thrust, a requirement to understand ‘native’ cultures being regarded as a necessary adjunct to
effective administration. Though exploitation ofresources was
the ultimate motivation behind this research, District Commissioners, Civil Servants and other officers ofthe colonial governments left many detailed studies which included valuable notes
on architecture. These were complemented by the writings of
missionaries who, notwithstandingtheir commitmenttospread
the gospel and ‘convert the heathen‘, also contributed important
ethnographies, in some of which local architectural traditions
were represented. With certain notable exceptions, few ethnographers and anthropologists gave much attention to architecture.

Though research in vernacular architecture may have to wait
some time before it has an historian, it seems that the majority
of early studies by architects were made in Europe. Considerable impetus was given to them by the rapid growth and popularity ofthe Arts and Crafts movement, which had its turn-ofthe-century counterparts ina number ofNordic, Germanicand
Central European countries. Often neglected in their home
countries, examples ofvernacular architecture were featured in
international expositions as expressions of national identity or
of benevolent colonialism. The association of the vernacular
with nationality seems to have reinforced the constraint of
many studies imposed by political boundaries, without regard
for cultural considerations that did not necessarily accord with
those boundaries.

As the 2oth century opened, published works on vernacular
architecture were often tinged by nostalgia for those rural traditions which were then in decline. Technically sound architectural surveys ofbuildings in the first halfofthe 20th century were
seldom supported by an examination of their social contexts
although they were sometimes accompanied by historical annotations. Instead, the established concerns of art and architectural historians with such issues as the classification ofaesthetic

‘styles’ persisted. Modernistarchitects turned to the vernacular

Introduction

as a source of inspiration, finding in it an endorsement ofthe
functionalistaesthetic. Vernacular precedents were claimed for
formal qualities which they espoused, ranging from modular
co-ordination to megastructure design. Lessons for modernism were implicit in the 1964 exhibition, Architecture Without
Architects, atthe Museum ofModern Art, New York. Essentiallya
visual anthology, the exhibition and the choice of the venue in
which itwas held, marked a growth in popular awareness ofthe
subject. Butithad been preceded by other cross-cultural works,
and the publication of systematic studies of the vernacular
architecture of entire countries, such as Cameroon (1954),
Romania (1958) and Portugal (1961). These indicated advances
in co-ordinated research, presaging some of the approaches
which have developed in the second halfofthe century.

As well as architects, anthropologists, geographers and historians, development planners and members of international
aid organisations have conducted researches into settlements
and dwellings in the course of their professional work. Butthe
study ofvernacular architecture is profoundly indebted to many
committed enthusiasts, whose work in the field has been incidental to their occupations but who have brought dedication
and independence ofthought to the subject.

The diversity of the approaches and methods employed is
reflected in the variety of publications where information on
vernacular traditions may be found - from studies in art history
to papers on Third World housing. But the standpoint of the
researcher and the intended application ofthe results considerably affect the way in which the work is conducted and the priorities placed on the kinds ofstudy undertaken. Though these
differ in motivation and method, common to many is the
urgent drive to record vernacular buildings in the face ofrecent
or impending deterioration or destruction.

As monumental architecture has become international, the
survival ofthe vernacular, like that ofthe peoples who build it,
is threatened. Cultures in formerly remote parts ofthe world,
such as Amazonia or New Guinea, have been subjected to pressures for change, accelerated by the exploitation oftheir lands
and mineral resources and the building of modern communications. Industrial materials have been adopted and traditional
forms of building have been rejected. Vernacular buildings are
seen by politicians and populace alike as representative of a
backward past opposed to their modern ideas and aspirations.
The relentless drive towards modernization has attracted millions ofpeoples from rural areas to the cities ofthe‘Third World’,
resulting in rapid urban growth and the abandonment oftheir
former homes. Natural and man-made disasters and wars have
afflicted many countries, forcing massive migrations and the
replacement ofthe village settlement by the refugee camp.

Destruction of the vernacular environment has not been
confined to the industrializing nations; countless buildings
have been lost with post-industrial expansion, while the decay
or demolition of traditional villages in favour of housing projects has been the result of modernization policies in many
countries. In much of Europe building traditions have steadily
declined as the suburbs have expanded, and as craft skills have
been displaced by machine tools and electronic profiling. Yet
there are curious contradictions and ambivalences in attitudes

xxili

Tee 66€ ee ET

## S. 5

Introduction

to the vernacular; as the traditions have been lost they have also
been romanticized, the cottage becoming a British ideal of
home, the Greek village a magnet for German or Scandinavian
tourists. Farm buildings are gentrified as second homes, and
for those who cannot aafford them, replica details have become
a feature of the vernacularization of popular architecture; the
romantic image replaces the authentic.

As early as the turn of the century and noticeably since the
19308, vernacular buildings in many countries have become
objects of display, being collected in open-air museums.
Undoubtedly, such assemblies have saved many hundreds of
fine examples which might otherwise have been destroyed, and
have drawn the attention ofa sector ofthe public to their qualities. In Africa, India and Southeast Asia they have often become
symbolic ofethnicity or nationality, butin general, themuseum
emphasis on exceptional, well-maintained examples, may have
had an adverse effect on attitudes to the more modest or commonplace buildings in town and country. Increasingly, the
importance of the conservation of vernacular environments
was recognised in the 1960s and 7os. In the European Architectural Heritage Year, 1975, the Council of Europe Committee on
Monuments and Sites acknowledged thatthe architectural heritage was not solely monumental but included the ‘groups or
lesser buildings in our old towns and characteristic villages in
their natural and man made settings’. Ictwas amark ofprogress
in therecognition ofthis heritage thatthe Conseil International
des Monuments et des Sites (ICOMOS) should form in 1976a
Comite International d’Architecture Vernaculaire with its first
headquarters in Bulgaria.

Important as this recognition may be, the definition ofwhat
constitutes the vernacular remained a matter of contention
within the Comite. The lack ofa forum in which to discuss such
issues was soon apparent in many countries. Regular conferences have been held by the Vernacular Architecture Group in
Great Britain since 1960, setting a pattern for the Vernacular
Architecture Forum in the United States and subsequently the
Centre d’Etudes et de Recherches sur l’Architecture Vernaculaire
(France), the Abeitskreis for Hausforschung (Germany) and
the Nordisk Forum für Utvecklingstudier vid Arkiteksklorna
(Scandinavia). Some ofthese organizations published their own
journals and have established means ofinformation exchange.
As an interdisciplinary network through which new research
could bemade known and exchanged did not exist, anumber of
international conferences on vernacular architecture, oron cultural aspects ofarchitecture that gave prominence to vernacular
traditions, commenced in the 19808. They included the Built
Form and Culture Research conferences, held biennially in the
United States, and the Symposia of the International Association for the Study of Traditional Environments, held in the
alternate years, which were initiated in the USA but which have
also been held in other countries. Other national and international meetings held in every continent, including specialized
conferences on materials or building types, are evidence ofthe
expanding awareness ofthe importance ofvernacular architecture.

In spite ofthis rapidly growing international and interdisciplinary concern the limited availability of publications and the

xxiv

general unfamiliarity with parallel work in other disciplines
have had serious implications for the study of the subject.
These range from lacunae in knowledge about certain aspects,
to the unnecessary duplication ofresearch efforts in others.

A World Encyclopedia of Vernacular Architecture

There is still an immense amount to be learned about and from
the vernacular architectural traditions of the world. But there
are few specialised libraries in the subjectand there has been no
single depository of the knowledge gained up to the present
which may provide a resource base for future research. Though
the phrase ‘ofthe world’ is theoretically tautologous in an encyclopedia, the fact that previous encyclopedias of architecture
emphasize (sometimes exclusively) the monumental architecture ofthe West nnecessitated its inclusion. Asan outcome ofthe
preparatory stages of research, it was decided that the new
Encyclopedia would seek to provide a comprehensive reference
work for vernacular architecture studies, now and in the future.
This required a multidisciplinary work that synthesized past
writings with recent and current research, acknowledging the
diversity of philosophical concepts of vernacular architecture
and the varied disciplinary and interdisciplinary approaches to
the subject.

Categorization ofassubject is fundamental to the nature ofan
encyclopedia, but this presented many problems of policy in
relation to vernacular architecture, which is often classified ata
local or regional scale by the materials used in its construction.
Other commonly employed means of classifying buildings
relate to the structural systems used, the resultant forms, or the
types or styles which they represent. None ofthese proved to be
practicable for categorization on a world basis.

The only consistent classification that emerged was by the
cultures which build vernacular architecture appropriate to
their resources, technologies and physical or spiritual needs.
But even categorization by cultures presents problems where,
as in the British Isles, traditions may vary by region, even
though cultural distinctions are less clearly defined or named.

Due consideration had also to be given to the basic environmental, resource and technological principles which affect
structural types, assembly, spatial organization and functions
of buildings in different cultural contexts. Similarly, it was
imperative that the Encyclopedia should demonstrate those
aspects ofsocial structures and value systems which contribute
to the shaping of diverse built environments, including economy, territory, settlement pattern, inheritance, spatial hierarchies, beliefs and symbolism. To meet these objectives it was
clearly necessary to locate cultures and societies not only within
their geographic and geo-political situations but also within
larger cultural regions which reflect cultural and architectural
affinities.

In view ofthe complexity ofthese conditioning factors itwas
essential that a framework was planned for the presentation of
this material in a logically integrated and related form, which
expressed the relationship between entries and which facilitated its use. A conventional single sequence A-Z work would
notaccomplish this, as the accident of alphabetical succession
alone could not reveal relationships between subjects. Accord-

ingly, an alternative structure was devised and submitted, with
ascheme ofproposed entries, to a specially appointed international board of editorial consultants and advisers for detailed
comment. After their observations had been accommodated,
this was adopted. EVAW is planned in sequences that correspond as closely as possible to subject relationships. This facilitates the reading ofsuccessive texts on related themes within a
category, or in an entire section, rather than as single references. Nevertheless, it is important that an item may be found
when sought, even ifthis normally necessitates foreknowledge
oftitles and categories. To assist the process oftracing entries,
alphabetical sequences are still used, though on a hierarchical,
triple-sequence system. Categories are as logically related as
practicable, subject to the constraints of alphabeticization.
While a single sequence A-Z obscures what is missing in an
encyclopedia, asystem oflogically-related entries reveals omissions and serves to indicate areas where research is needed.

In broad terms it can be argued that research in vernacular
architecture stems either from the study ofthe buildings as artefacts, or from the study ofthe cultures that produce them. EVAW
has multiple tasks for it has to cover what may be known about
buildings, their materials and construction, their enrichment
and meaning, typesand uses, as well as the specific circumstances in which they are built by diverse cultures.

With recognition ofthese tasks as a guide in the ordering of
the work, Part One (Volume 1), Theories and Principles, reviews
some ofthe theoretical aspects ofthe study ofvernacular architecture. Summarizing fundamental principles ofsuch building
ona cross-cultural, comparative basis, it moves from conceptual
positions to cultural traits, and from environmental contexts,
through construction, to buildings in use. Part Two (Volumes 2
and 3), Cultures and Habitats, considers these principles within
specific cultural and societal contexts. Thetwo volumes are thus
interactive; acommon building feature (eg. ahipped roof) ora
common cultural phenomenon (e.g. matrilocal residence) is
notexplained in Part'Two everytimeareference is madetoit;an
explanation ofits relevance to building is to be found in Part One,
sometimes through a specific example. In addition, a definition
may be found in the Glossary.

A major undertaking was the obtaining of contributors but
the network of contacts built over three decades provided the
nucleus, and this was supplemented with suggestions from the
consulting editors and advisers. Specialistanthropological and
architectural journals devoted space to the proposed Encyclopedia which elicited many offers to contribute, while the meetings of the international conferences provided many more.
Some contributors were interested in comparative and crosscultural aspects ofthe subject; others in focused and specialized
studies which were sometimes culturally based, or essentially
thematic.

Part One: Theories and Principles

Inacknowledgement ofthe different positions taken in research,
EVAW opens with a section ofmain entries on Approaches and
Concepts (1.1). These are notintended to summarize particular
disciplines (e.g. Archaeology or History) but to discuss aspects
oftheir approach to the subject ofvernacular architecture. Nor

Introduction

do the entries summarize the approaches from within disciplines
alone; they also include certain theoretical concepts which have
shaped past or recent research. The list is by no means exhaustive; other approaches to the vernacular have been applied and
still others will supersede them.

A further section of main entries follows, Culture Traits and
Attributes (1.11), in which the bearing of socio-cultural phenomena on building is outlined. These refer to aspects of the
family and society, ofeconomic activities and religious beliefs,
or oftradition and values. Again, such a list oftraits is not complete, nor can it be: a great many traits that have little relevance
to architecture arenotincluded. Butthe fact is thatthetraits are
themselves an abstraction, a means ofidentifying and classifying observed phenomena. Together the main entries in section
1.1 and 1.1I summarize some of the principal conceptual and
cultural factors which have bearing on the etic, external, objective approaches to vernacular architecture, and the emic, internal and subjective experience ofit. The Categories in these two
sections have main entries only; allsubsequent sections in both
volumes have categories that comprise a main entry which provides an overview and a succession of secondary entries of a
more specialist nature. Each section is preceded by a short section introduction, which explains its content and the decisions
taken with regard to categories and the selection ofrepresentative examples.

Section 1.1IL is concerned with the Environment, themainand
secondary entries considering those aspects ofclimate, susceptibility to natural hazard, and conditions oflocation which affect
the siting ofvillages and communities, the movement ofpopulations and the pattern of settlement within definitions ofterritory. The following section 1.1v, examines the available Materials
and Resources from which dwellings are constructed. Nomads
and pastoralists may use animal products, while earths and clays
are among the materials most widely utilized by sedentary peoples. Grasses, bamboos and palms are employed for cladding,
while rocks, stones and timbers make more durable structures
possible. Such materials may be worked before being utilized
but others are transformed through a physical process, or manufactured. The newcomers to the world’s expanding cities reuse the waste materials ofthe more affluent.

Building Production is the subject of section ı.v in which
some ofthe principal stages in assembly are summarized. Certain structural principles apply to all buildings, among them
the weight and thrusts of building components. After completion of the basic construction, cladding of exposed elements
may be necessary. Finishing gives craftspersons the opportunities to exercise their skills, especially on details. Openings for
access are necessary, while fittings may be required for closure.
Some furniture and fixtures can be built-in as integral parts of
the structure. For. a building to function effectively it frequently
requires Service Systems, the subject of1.vI. Some are passive,
such as the circulation systems between activity spaces; others
consume energy, heating being often combined with cooking
and sometimes with bathing. Most produce waste and create
sanitation and hygiene problems. Passive systems ofventilation
and cooling contribute to comfort, butthe provision ofwater is
the mostvital of services.

a EEE

## S. 6

Introduction

Section ı.vır is devoted to Symbolism and Decoration. On
completion ofthe building some surfaces may be ornamented.
Structural elements may bear inscriptions, but many more are
decorated with motifs which are often archaic and frequently
have religious connotations. Overall schematic designs employ
varied patterns, while symbolic decoration communicates complex meanings to the initiated. More abstractly, the orientation,
siting, spaces or components of many buildings may be perceived in terms ofcosmic, ancestral or other symbolism.

While the kinds of vernacular building are numerous there
are some aspects thatare consistentamong them. Sectionı.vIn
on Typologies summarizes certain ofthe corresponding characteristics which may initiate a taxonomy of vernacular building; ofthese, perhaps the most familiar are the elevations that
present the public and private faces ofbuildings. Roofs too, are
notable features in building form, butitis the plans that generate the volumes in three dimensions. These in turn are shaped
by the spatial relationships that are determined by social
demand. All are synthesized in the structures ofthe buildings
which may be typologically classified.

Ofthetypologies thatare used byresearchers and lay persons
alike, their Uses and Functions, section 1.1x, are the most common. Implicit in society is social structure, authority and status
often being marked by differentiation in building scale, according to rank, power or wealth. Though settlements may frequently be hierarchic, domestic buildings are characteristic of
all cultures, butas they constitute the majority of entries in the
encyclopedia they are not the subject of a separate category in
this Section. However, many domestic buildings are related to
subsistence farming economies, augmented by the buildings
constructed to accommodate livestock and the outbuildings
and dependencies that support an economic system.

Spiritual and supernatural power are conspicuous in the
temples and places of worship attended by members of religions of all kinds, and the shrines and tombs accorded the
deceased. Social building types that are essential to the workings ofspecific cultures, whether in council, rites of passage or
simply in recreation, are as functionally significant as the technological structures in which materials are transformed and
energy converted. All these kinds of building are the static
structures of sedentary societies; though smaller in number,
transportable tents, boat-houses and vehicular dwellings are
also reviewed in the final section of Volume 1.

Part Two: Cultures and Habitats

Classification in Part Two, Volumes 2 and 3, is notby countries:
the determination of national boundaries in many parts ofthe
world took place in the ıgth and earlier 2oth centuries, long
after numerous vernacular traditions had been established.
Frequently contentious, they have been the subject of considerable dispute and change even duringthe period ofthe compilation of EVAW. Nevertheless, it is accepted that entries may be
sought by the names ofthe countries in which they occur, and
these are therefore given after the title ofa culture or habitat,
and are also separately indexed. The names of distinet cultures
which collectively perceive their own identity, or are the names
of pluralistic societies which share traits in common are

xxvi

ee 2

employed. As many ofthese are representative only, an adjectival form is used with appropriate suffixes where necessary.

Attempts at cultural mapping on a global scale are comparatively few, and for the most part were devised in the 19508. Reference was made to Spencer and Johnson’s Anthropological Atlas,
the Culture Worlds of Russell and Kniffen, G.P Murdock’s Ethno-
‚graphical Atlas, the related Atlas ofWorld Cultures by D.H. Priceand
the Ethnologue of the Summer Institute. They were not applicable to this project since there was a frequent lack of correspondence with vernacular architecture, and a general west to east
organization. For the purposes of EVAW a new map ofccultures
and habitats has been drawn. It is intended that the culture
areas should also be related as far as possible to geo-physical
and climatic features, and thatthey should broadly correspond
with the distribution ofvernacular architecture traditions.

Sections in Part Two are identified as continental regions of
approximately equal emphasis, in a sequence that moves from
north to south (Arctic to Australasia/Oceania) and east to west
(Asia, Europe, and the Mediterranean), and from the Old World
to the New World, (including Latin America, North America,
Sub-Saharan and Saharan Africa). This sequence notionally
reflects the diffusion of cultural influences, the movement of
populations and world expansionism which have influenced
the vernacular forms that have survived to the present century.
Where distinet cultural groups are present in definable culture
areas they are identified by name, sometimes in the derived or
adjectival form. Areas where specific culture groups are no
longer named but where local identity persists in acknowledged environments or sub-regions, are termed habitats.

Section 2.1 (i.e. section 1in Volume 2) is devoted to Asia, East
and Central. Peoples of Arctic Asia and Siberia are first discussed in one ofthe largest categories by land mass in the Encyclopedia. Another immense but sparsely inhabited region includes
Central Asia and Mongolia, while the populous North and South
of China are divided. The Indian sub-continent is similarly discussed with regard to the North/Northeast, and the South. But
the Northwest, the Bastern and Western Himalayas have been
more extensively studied and, like Japan, Thailand and mainland Southeast Asia, have their own categories.

Australasia and Oceania, subject of section 2.11, deals with
the Pacific insular cultures including the continent of Australia,
the archipelago ofthe Philippines, and the chain ofislands of
Indonesia and Malaysia, with the Malay peninsula. Melanesia
and Micronesia are treated together but the large island of New
Guinea is culturally diverse, and is considered separately. The
scattered islands of Polynesia, including New Zealand, representa vast region, though some ethnic minorities have little of
their vernacular architecture remaining. The communities of
Taiwan conclude the section.

Extending west from Asia the old cultures of Europe and
Eurasia (section 2.111) present considerable problems. The cultural complexity of this region, added to the lack of definition
of specific culture groups within many parts, has meant that
some compromises have been made. There are ethnic implications in the shifting frontiers of Eastern and Central Europe
and the independence of the Baltic states, the divide between
the Gallic and Germanic speaking domains, and the definition

of northern Europe, including the Lowlands, the British Isles
and the Nordic peninsula. In view of the extent of vernacular
architecture research in Europe, there was a risk of imbalance
in the Encyclopedia as a whole. This has been minimized by
limiting the number of entries, though with some injustice to
the extensive work done on a national basis. European Russia,
and the Ukraine with Eastern Europe have separate categories.
Again, itshould be emphasized that the criteria for inclusion in
one category or another are not political.

Southern Europe is incorporated in section 2.1v, Mediterranean and Southwest Asia. Here Arab and Islamic influences
from the Middle East to the circum-Mediterranean regions
have given some measure ofunity among habitats and vernacular architecture traditions. The Arabian Peninsula, the Middle
East, the much disputed areas ofthe Balkans, Asia Minor, the
Caucasus, the eastern Mediterranean and the Levant are considered with political impartiality. Bounded by the Alps and the
Pyrenees, the Italian and Iberian peninsulas penetrate and
define the northern Mediterranean. Across a sea peppered with
islands each with a distinct vernacular tradition, North Africa
and the Maghreb gird the northern limits ofthe Sahara desert.

European expansion westward left a permanent impression
on the cultures of the Americas but in Volume 3, section 3.v,
Latin America, the African and subsequent immigrant presences
have also been marked. Afro-Caribbean and Colonial Caribbean
influences intermingle in the Greater and Lesser Antilles and
the larger Carib islands but Indian and Mestizo traditions survivein Mexico, Yucatan, and much ofCentral America. In South
America, Portuguese and Spanish colonial or settler architecture is widely dispersed, augmented by the housing of later
European immigrants. Pressures exerted by the destruction of
parts ofthe rain forests and the spread of new settlement have
had damaging effects on South American Indian cultures, but
though numerically small, many still use traditional building
forms.

In North America, section 3.v1, the indigenous cultures were
deeply scarred by ıgth-century repression and subsequent policies of assimilation, yet architectural traditions were tenacious
among cultures in the Arctic. In the Southwest, others survived,
or in some form have been revived. While the vernacular architecture ofthe colonists was dominated by British settlers, generations of immigrant groups retained the evidence of their
European heritage, mostevidentlyamongreligious sects. African
‚Americans have made significant impressions on building form
and use.

Apart from African-American instances the vernacular traditions of Sub-Saharan Africa (3.vır) have not been influential
elsewhere. Years of conflict and drought have limited recent
research in some areas. South of the thinly populated Sahara
and Sahel, the Savanna grasslands and the Nigerian plateau
support many cultures. The architecture ofthe Guinea Coast is
partially documented; that of Equatorial and Central Africa
much less so. The peoples of Eastern and Southern Africa build
using age-old forms, yet Colonial architecture in these regions
also has a long history. In East Africa, the Arab-Swahili architecture of the Zanj resonates with the echoes of Africa’s past,
and its links with Asia. However, itis in Madagascar, where ves-

Introduction

tiges still remain of Indonesian settlement, that vernacular
architecture completes a global circle.

Elsewhere in Aftica, as in therockart of Tibestiin the Sahara,
some impression of built structures can be deduced from prehistoric paintings. Information on historic forms ofvernacular
architecture is variable; there is evidence in the Middle Bastand
China ofthe persistence ofsome building forms and structural
systems over thousands ofyears. In other parts ofthe world, the
historice record is debatable or non-existent. Accepting this,
EVAW is nota work ofarchaeology or ofhhistory; its focus is on
vernacular architecture which has been in use in the 2oth century when research has been most extensive. Though there may
be references to its historical background, there is therefore no
“timeline”. On the other hand, the fictive ‘ethnographical present’ is notemployed, and entries are only in the present tense
where it is known that the buildings described are in use atthe
time of writing.

Research and Compilation

The Encyclopedia of Vernacular Architecture ofthe World seeks to be
representative of what has been learned about the subject during the past century. It cannot fully satisfy this aim for many
reasons. Such studies as have been made are sometimes intensive in some areas, while theyare almostnon-existentin others,
so thatthe geographical spread ofresearch is unequal. Inclusion
byvirtue ofpopulation figures is misleading; some ofthe world’s
most remarkable buildings are erected by minority cultures.
Concentration on the vernacular ofthe most numerous would
lead to the serious neglect of buildings among peoples whose
survival is most threatened. Yet the converse also raises problems: local and sub-regional variants in the vernacular become
of greater significance to those researching the more numerous
and widely known, or more fully documented traditions.

EVAW brings together the outcome of research in a wide
range of aspects of vernacular architecture and a worldwide
compass of cultures and societies. It has not been possible to
initiate new research beyond that necessitated in devising its
structure and the testingand substantiating ofthat structure by
field visits to many parts ofthe world. During the course ofthe
work on the encyclopedia the Editor has visited every continental region represented by the sections in Volumes 2 and 3 and
these visits presented opportunities to meet contributors, to
seek others, and to discuss any problems that have arisen. Ithas
been possible to speak aboutthe work ofthe Encyclopedia inall
these regions to architects, anthropologists, and educationalists, and in specialist and multidisciplinary gatherings, which
have further tested the validity ofthe structure of EVAW.

While hundreds - even thousands — of vernacular traditions
in countries the world over have never been adequately studied,
much new work was undertaken by contributors during the
course of preparation. In many instances, appearance in EVAW
is the first publication ofthe results ofsuch work; in others, previous research has been augmented. Writers who were actively
engaged in research wereableto incorporate theirrecentfindings,
including evidence ofchange, while others assisted with obscure
and out-of-print text references. Not all the research on which
entries are based is recent; in some cases the only systematic

xxvii

## S. 7

Introduction

studies were undertaken earlier this century. In such instances
they have been summarized by contributors who indicate the
date ofthe field-work, where this is known. Inevitably, there are
many lacunae. In preference to leaving some aspects totally
unrepresented, research papers and books have been used to
fill in some ofthe gaps.

Some of the most recurrent problems arose over definition,
for the shades of meaning of the term vernacular persist. The
transition from the vernacular across what has been termed the
polite threshold to the fringes ofmore formally designed architecture, varies according to context, while the activities and
skills of master-builders and guild members, and the development of artisan and worker housing, also raise problems of
boundary. Among the more intractable difficulties have been
the trickle down ofsophisticated and designed details and features from formal to vernacular on the one hand, and the vernacularization of popular architecture on the other. Here, as in
other disputed areas, the principle has been dependent partly
on the understanding of the vernacular from the authors’
milieux, and partly on the desirability ofbeingrepresentative of
the more contentious and ambiguous corners ofthe field.

Illustrations and Maps

An important feature of EVAW is its illustrations. Drawings can
often be far more eloquent than paragraphs of text in describing terms or techniques. Perspectives can be misleadingand so
they have been used sparingly. Architectural conventions of
plan, section and elevation are understood by those within the
profession, though isometric and axonometric projections permit the expression of three-dimensional volume while retaining many of the dimensional characteristics of orthographic
conventions. Drawings are important in EVAW in providing a
variety of graphic means of presenting technical information
and field data, notnormally available in anthropological sources.
Some are drawn precisely to scale, others are sketch plans,
while still others are diagrammatic.

Photographs have been used where they best portray the
building, especially in its environmental and cultural context.
Historic photographs from earlier in the zoth century have
sometimes been reproduced, but a high proportion of both
drawings and photographs have been made by contributors,
thus ensuringa loser correspondence between textand image,
arising directly from observation in the field. Though the aesthetic qualities of vernacular buildings in many traditions can
be expressed by photographs, their value in conveying information has been a prime consideration in their selection.

Culture Maps are important when indicating the location of
particular societies and their architectural traditions. These may
sometimes be defined by topographical features but the limits
ofthe distributions ofpeoples or building types are seldom precisely delineated; cultural groups and habitats are therefore
broadly indicated, defined by a balance of cultural, environmental and cartographic factors. Though they are seldom used
in the Encyclopedia to identify peoples or territories, political

xxvili

boundaries are shown on the maps together with coastlines,
mountain ranges, rivers, and capital or major cities. This information is provided in order to facilitate comparison with conventional atlases, from which data on elevations, vegetation,
water resources and urban locations can be obtained.

Conclusion

To compensate for the neglect ofmuch ofthe world’s buildings
in encyclopedias ofmonumental architecture EVAW aims to give
as balanceda coverage ofworld vernacular as available research
and authors permit. The cultures and societies of the world
number several thousands: it cannot be comprehensive therefore, though itendeavours to be representative. Wherethereare
major gaps in the cultures covered, these generally give a clear
indication as to where future research could be conducted.

EVAW is notaddressed to a particular readership, nor does it
advocate a particular approach to its subject. No method of
research is implied by its structure; the fragmentation of the
subjectand its organization are the outcome ofits function as a
reference work. Interdisciplinary in conception and transcultural in content, it draws on the research and knowledge of
scholars from many fields. It is hoped that it will serve to further links, assisting in the sharing of perceptions and methods
in the study ofthe vernacular. When work on the Encyclopedia
commenced several scholars believed that the number of
potential authors could not exceed 250; in fact, the number
contracted to write entries was more than three times this estimate, drawn from over 80 countries. Many more were invited
but, for various reasons, were unable to contribute. Though
many authors were internationally known and with longstanding established reputations, the number of younger contributors undertaking research was encouraging for the future
ofvernacular architecture studies. While contributors from the
cultures and regions discussed were sought wherever possible,
every attempt has been made to avoid nationalistic, ethnocentric or gender bias.

This Encyclopedia draws attention to the richness, diversity
and ingenuity ofthe world’s vernacular architecture which has
been built, and in many parts ofthe world is still being built, by
the locally skilled. In any society buildings are generally the
largest, frequently the most permanent, and often invested with
the most profound meanings, of any human artefacts. Hundreds of examples of domestic and other buildings in specific
cultures and habitats are represented in EVAW, which will be
used by architects, anthropologists, archaeologists, folklorists,
geographers, historians, planners and educationalists.

But the Encyclopedia of Vernacular Architecture of the World may
achieve much more, ifthose responsible for the future environment discover in its volumes the quality and potential of the
buildings described and depicted in its pages. Traditional wisdom and lore in building, using renewable resources and
indigenous skills, may still offer wisely managed, economically
effective and culturally appropriate solutions to the world’s
increasing housing needs. PAUL OLIVER

## S. 12

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

All vernacular cultures exist within, largely depend on and are partially shaped by their
environmental contexts. The diverse forms of vernacular architecture are an expression
ofthe cultures that build them, butthe capacity of cultural groups to cope with the
physical conditions they encounter, the kinds ofeconomy which they can pursue and on
which their life support depends, their relationship to the land which they occupy and
the forms that their occupancy may take are intimately bound to the quality and
potential ofthe terrain.

No environmental factor plays a greater part in the development of human life than
climate. The practical limits ofhuman existence are largely defined by physical and
psychological tolerance to extremes of heat or cold, while the effects of climate upon
the distribution of vegetation have direct bearing on life support. Humans have
developed ecological means ofrealizing the potential ofthe land to provide the foods
they require, subject to climatic variables. In the Climate category (1.111.1) the principal
climatic zones and the architectural responses to prevalent weather conditions are
broadly summarized, but entries in Part Two are related to the specific climatic ranges
of cultural areas. -

Water is essential to life, and human settlement is conditioned by the necessity of
access to water supplies, either through river and lake systems, or by tapping surface
and underground water resources. These are still influenced by climate; the Arctic
regions may have an abundance of water, but the extremes of cold that have created the
taiga and permafrost exercise profound constraints on human ecology. Extremes of
heat and aridity in desertic regions are as limiting.

Many millions of people live below ground while others depend on oases and lakes.
Immense grasslands and savannas with relatively few trees may be lightly populated,
but so too are many heavily forested regions. Upland regions tend to be thinly
populated, and many have only been settled in recent times. Hillslopes can seasonally
support pastoral economies, butthe lowlands and valleys, the riparian and coastal
locations are often more densely settled. Within these generalized Locations cultures
build on those specific Sites (1.1I1.2) perceived as being the most advantageous in
meeting their priorities.

Often, the conjunction of climatic extremes and particular landscape locations
increases the risk ofexposure to hazards. Some Natural Disasters (1.111.3), such as soil
erosion or desertification, may be slow to manifest their effects; others, such as high
winds and hurricanes, may recurrently destroy housing but are anticipated. Sustained
and heavy rainfall may lead to flooding ofsome ofthe most fertile valleys, as well as the
release ofthe destructive forces of avalanches and landslides. Most devastating are the
earthquakes and volcanic eruptions that have a rapid and often unpredictable impact.
Avoidance strategies and risk reduction measures in building may diminish

## S. 13

1.1

ENVIRONMENT

vulnerability, but mankind’s exploitation ofnatural resources can contribute to
environmental deterioration and heighten proneness to disasters, in which the poor
and their dwellings frequently suffer most. Yet living in hazard-prone locations can be a
calculated trade-off between risk ofhazards and access to life-sustaining resources.

The settlement of peoples reflects a complex history ofneed, competition, curiosity,
exploration, conquest and exploitation. Contemporary Population (1.111.4) distribution
is largely the outcome ofthe major movements of former generations; autochthonous
or indigenous peoples are small in number in most culture regions which have been
settled in historical times. Natural increase, lower mortality rates and cultural
imperatives to raise large families, have promoted high populations in many Tegians,
Former building traditions have seldom met the demands of urban immigrants in
southern latitudes. Squatter settlements are beyond the scope ofthis work buta few are
included where distinct, ifrecent, traditions have emerged.

'Topography and orientation, availability ofland and building resources, defence and
sociospatial relationships communications and market opportunities are among the
factors that influence the diverse forms ofvernacular settlement patterns. No two are
exactly alike, though many share morphological characteristics with the pa
employed by other cultures. The classification of Settlement pattern (1.I11.5) is not
standardized, but the prevalent types are summarized using widely employed
terminology.

Itis debatable whether the drives to claim Territory (1.111.6) are instinctual or
cultural, but group identity is frequently associated with the land. Dominion over a
terrain and its resources may be defined by natural or intercultural boundaries, whether
tacitly observed or disputed and counterclaimed, to be hunted over, exploited,
possessed, apportioned or sold according to the prevailing concepts ofusage or
ownership. Supported in some circumstances by the force oflaw, these have political
dimensions; where relevant to the vernacular such ramifications are intimated in certain

entries in Part Two.
PAUL OLIVER

126

‚Nukak.dwellings in the

„u Climate

Climate is the pattern of weather or incident atmospheric conditions near the ground over a sequence of years,
especially as it affects biological life. The Greek word klima and
Latin clima (‘slope’) implied how the angle of solar incidence
governed local environmental conditions. That scholarly belief
from the Greek classical period later inspired Ptolemy, the
astronomer of the and century AD, to divide the earth into
‘climates’ or zones corresponding to patterns of air temperature believed to be induced by the angle ofthe sun: the tropics
or torrid zones surrounding the Equator where the sun is usually vertically overhead at midday; the temperate zones north
and south with intermediate incident angles at solar noon; and
the frigid or cold zones around the poles with low solar angles
and even an absence of sun for part ofthe year. The European
prejudice favouring temperate climates also assumed only
latitude as a climatic determinate. Global exploration and
scientific instrumentation have changed these perceptions:
for instance, the hottest climates on earth are in the deserts of
the Sahara and the Saudi peninsula, not on the Equator.

Classifications

Climates stimulate needs for shelter and influence local cultures, but also provide the conditions of local building materials. Global classifications typically cluster prominent climatic
data into zones of similitude for biological study, especially
agriculture. Bycombiningthe de Candolles’ vegetation maps of
the 1860s with maps of mean monthly temperature and precipitation, Köppen and Geiger developed the first widely accepted
system in their encyclopedic handbook. The Köppen-Geiger

system identifies five basic zones: tropical-rainy; dıy; warmtemperate; cool-snow-frost; and polar. Trewartha modified the
system from 1954, in response to certain criticisms. Geiger
extended microclimatic definitions.

Since Vitruvius (c. 90-20 BC) prominent architectural theorists have often included select climatic parameters as design
determinates without being comprehensive. This continues to
be appropriate since designs or performances based on broad
climatic type are strongly modified by microclimate. But all
architectural features have climatic consequences. By definition
indigenous architecture is sensitive to local variants such as
microclimate. Cereghini (1950) identified many local aspects of
the rustic Alpine vernacular of Europe includingaaltitude, orientation and materials as well as culture. He also discussed other
mountainous locations such as Tibet together with examples of
modern architecture for the mountains.

Because climate is not the single determinant of vernacular
architecture it has rarely been the basis of geographic mapping.
Dollfus (1954) correlated regional housetypes identified primarily by roofs with thermal and precipitation zones. Higher roof
slopes were used in wet-temperate and cooler zones. Flat roofs
occurred in hot dry zones, and slightly inclined roofs were found
in temperate climates with consistently dry summers. Zones
where the roofwas more essential than the walls, which may not
have been needed at all, included rainy Equatorial forests and
tropical savannas of Africa, monsoon Asia, Australia, Polynesia
and the Amazon. Fitch and Branch (1960) popularized the scientific performance analysis of vernacular architecture based on
climate by identifying seven zones and focusing on four where
the greatest variety of design responses were found: Arctic and
sub-Arctic; continental steppe; desert; and tropical rain forest.

Aronin (1953) initiated Climate and Architecture with observations about indigenous architectural responses to climate, as
well as historical references starting with Vitruvius. Fry and
Drew (1956) began Tropical Architecture in the Humid Zone by
analysing both warm and hot climates but considered only a
few vernacular or traditional examples. Thereafter texts based
on performance for professional designers correlated climate
with vernacular architectural models as prototypes. Grillo
(1960, 1975) included chapters on climate and on orientation.
His global map showed four generic roof slopes, with France
having three distinct roof pitches. Within the 160 km (100 mi)
from Lyons (elevation 156 m [511 ft]) to Chamonix, Switzerland
(elevation 1004 m [3290 ft]) four separate roofstyles responsive
to climate were described.

Olgyay (1963) proposed a bioclimatic approach to architectural regionalism based on climatic analysis and vernacular

127

## S. 14

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

examples by identifying four zones in the United States and
Canada: hot and humid, hot and arid, temperate, and cool.
Based on the tropical vernacular, Koenigsberger et al. (1974)
used the Atkinson climate classification ofair temperature and
humidity as the sources of discomfort. Their three major warm
climatic zones (with three subgroups) include: warm-humid
Equatorial (and warm humid island or trade wind); hot dıy
desert or semi-desert (and hot dry maritime desert); composite
or monsoon - a combination ofthe first two zones (and tropical upland).

Talib (1984) organized Saudi vernacular by desert climate
categories. Concerning the rural vernacular in India, Bansal
and Minke (1988) identified six zones: hot and dry; warm and
humid; moderate; cold and cloudy; cold and sunny; and composite. Thus, sun was included as well as air temperature and
humidity.

Watson and Labs’ (1983) scientific method of ‘energyefficient building principles and practices’ avoided mapping
the United States, but quantified all climatic factors that could
lead to building elements, and thus become architectural
design through climate. Viaro and Ziegler (1983) discussed
traditional dwellings according to the dominant climatic elements, describing solar radiation, temperature, humidity,
precipitation and winds.

By combining temperature and moisture conditions, any
number of climatic types may be defined. The earth can be
divided into humid and arid regions, with semi-humid and

IT] Arctic and subarctic
|| 1. TUNDRA

2. CONTINENTAL

Arctic and Subarctic

1. TAIGA 3. DESERT

128

Bo

semi-arid between, butthere are many subtle variants. The nine
secondary entries which follow this also recognize climatic
diversity based on architectural impact:

ı Arctic and sub-Arctic, where extreme cold and frozen ground
dominate

2 Continental, with cold and windy winters and warm summers

3 Desert, or arid, with clear skies and strong daily and seasonal
temperature swings

4 Maritime, or temperate but often changeable, from the
influence of oceans

5 Mediterranean, with warm dependable summers,
and short wet winters

6 Monsoon, with along hot dry period interrupted by a hot,
rainy season

7 Montane, influenced by altitude and terrain, but usually
dominated by precipitation

8 Subtropical, with continuous humidity through long warm
summers, and short winters

9 Tropical, with stable year-round warmth and humidity.

Climate causes

Global climate is driven by natural phenomena attempting to
balance the energy content of the atmosphere: solar radiation
in the upper atmosphere; albedo, or reflection of radiation
from clouds, snow, ground and water surfaces; distribution of
water and landmasses; and topography including the elevation
and distribution of land features. Their integrated net effects
determine a macroclimate oraregional climate.

Evaporation of water, especially over the oceans, initiates the
hydraulic cycle. Water vapour transport and precipitation
transform solar energy. Winds are the major equalizing and
dissipating mechanism of global irregularity in solar radiation.
The westerly jet stream in the upper atmosphere is imperceptible at ground level where the general pattern is of warm air
flowing toward the poles and cold air from the poles moving
toward the Equator. The movement of these air masses largely
determines local climate. Predictable winds provide the stable
sunny climates of deserts, as well as the stable temperatures,
high humidity, variable cloudiness and frequent rainfall of
tropical islands in the trade wind zones. In contrast are the
shores of continents between the latitudes of 40° and 60° both
North and South where tropical and polar air clash causing the
changeable conditions legendary to England, New England
and elsewhere.

Measures

Von Humboldt’s 1845 definition of climate included those
‘changes in the atmosphere which sensibly affect our organs:
the temperature, the humidity, the changes in barometric pressure, the calms or the effect of the different winds, the electric
field, the purity of the atmosphere or its contamination with
more or less gaseous exhalations; finally the degree ofthe usual
transparence and clearness of the sky which is not only important for the increased heat radiation ofthe soil, but also for the
well-being and moods of humans’.

Yet in the late-2oth century only four weather measures are
regularly taken, for instance, by official weather stations in the
United States: air temperature, precipitation in rain equivalent,
wind by direction and speed, and barometric pressure. Solar
radiation or net radiation records typically are not made,
although visual observations about average sky cover in tenths
sometimes are. These numerical data are assembled as annual
summaries and presented with 30-year running averages as
Local Climatological Data, primarily for aeronautical, nautical
and agricultural purposes.

Numerical description is largely unknown to vernacular populations whose culture is typically oral, whose knowledge is
experience-based and whose acuity is much more subtle than
single instruments. Qualitative information about weather,
and wisdom about climate are integral aspects of daily communication. Vivid descriptions are also the settings in story and
song for the transference of cultural values. Thus, Arctic
natives have more than 20 words for snow, as least as many for
sun and daylight, and about 5o for ice and icy conditions. Alternatively, there are many examples of indigenous cultures who
defy climaticwisdom such as in Bhutan where augurybyalocal
lama determines building decisions.

Comfort

Late-2oth-century scientific definitions of comfort also defy
usefulness for vernacular peoples. The word ‘comfort’ entered
English from French only within the past two centuries, first
meaning ‘solace’, a feeling of relief or consolation. Most
recently it defines that neutral state without bodily sensation.
The authority among current environmental engineers is

Climate 1.111.1
Fanger (1970) whose Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) establishes
standards at which there is a minimum ofadvice from a population whether they are warm or cool. Among highly industrialized societies absolute interior environmental control is
pursued by mechanical equipment. The six measures in
Fanger’s PMV model are air temperature, mean radiant temperation, air motion, relative humidity, metabolism (‘met’) and the
thermal quality of clothing (‘clo’ value). Although these may
describe universal human physiology thermally they do not
define tolerance or preference. Missing are definitions of the
luminous, olfactory and acoustic environment, to say nothing
about cultural appreciation, such as the arrival ofthe first rains
ofthe monsoon.

Among vernacular populations comfort, or rather environmental standards for buildings, are more complex. Studies
occasionally mention air quality such as the unhealthy environmentin chimneyless dwellings, but daylighting and ventilation
are seldom addressed. The variation of climatic experience
both indoors and out is often an essential part of life - and to
some it is the essence of being alive. There are virtually no
authorities or standards on human preferences ofconditions in
the ‘precomfort’ era. Butsurvival under threatening conditions
must be a minimum condition. Beyond that, the dialogue
between metabolism and ‘clo’ value, and environmental conditions provides many acceptable settings. Thus, the protective
and provident climatic role of vernacular architecture is
profoundly different from the expectation of industrialized
architecture. Shelter has multiple climatic purposes, not comparable to the recent concept ofcomfort. Thus, climate data for
vernacular architecture must be particularized to those issues
valued culturally beyond survival, and extreme distress. The
field is open for research.

Climate variability

Vernacular nomadic architecture provides special cases of climatic responsiveness. The identical architectural solution such
as the conical tent, or the domical yurt may perform well in a
variety of natural environments and thus challenge climaticbased typologies. And cooking, space heating and other activities are integral to architectural performance. For instance, the
heated sleeping platform (kang) attached to a stove is critical to
the earthen and subterranean dwellings of China.

Few places on earth are exempt from extremes or from
unusual or exceptional weather events; hailstones, heat waves
and hurricanes are only a few of the perturbations of climatic
predictability. Many have architectural consequence. Only close
the Equator is the climate continuously relatively stable.
Although it is recorded as a median of conditions, climate is
seldom an average. Current documentation of desertification,
global warming and so on confirms climatic shifts. Butchanges
have always characterized climate and have been critical in the
adaptable evolution of human life. JEFFREY COOK

Arctic and sub-Arctic

Allareas north ofthe Arctic Circle, 66.33 °N latitude,
are Arctic. The sub-Arctic includes areas south of the Arctic
Circle that approximate those conditions. In Antarctica there

1.111.1.a

129

## S. 15

1. ENVIRONMENT

are no indigenous human settlements, but conditions are comparable to the Arctic.

The Arctic and sub-Arctic consist of four distinet biological
regions or biomes: the uninhabitable frozen polar seas, and the
glaciers, including the one that covers all of Greenland except
its coast (II per cent ofthe land ofthe world is under ice); the
coasts and islands with their marine climates; the treeless tundra plain with its continuous permafrost (ro per cent ofthe
world’s land); the taiga, the largely coniferous boreal forest
that covers more than half of Canada, most of Alaska, and
much of Scandinavia and Siberia.

The taiga begins in the south where trees have an annual
growing season of approximately 150 days and where permafrost is discontinuous. At the northern treeline of the
stunted boreal forest it dissipates into the vast rolling tundra.
Here the growing season is go days or less, and permafrost
becomes continuous beneath the surface even during the summer. Permaftost, concrete-hard frozen ground, is 300 m (1000
ft) thick in northern Alaska and a mile (1600 m) thick in
Siberia’s northern Yakutia.

Cold hunting cultures are divided between those who hunt
inland for caribou or reindeer, and those who hunt on the ice
and the shore for birds and sea life. The coasts and islands of
the Arctic and sub-Arctic are the longest and most intricate
shoreline system ofthe world. Although locked in ice or storms
for up to ro months oftheyear, the Arctic seas are extraordinarily rich in foods. Water modifies local climatic conditions, so
spring and autumn come late and summer is longer. The western coast of Greenland, the eastern half of Baffin Island,
Novaya Zemlya, the Kurils and the Aleutians are also each
special climatic situations dominated by the presence of the
sea.

The Arctic is a frigid, high-latitude desert, with sparse vegetation and strong daily and seasonal swings ofair temperature.
But during days of 24-hour darkness or 24 hours of light air
temperatures change little. As in the hot deserts, air temperatureandradiation in Arctic climates are factors critical to human
habitation: here outgoing radiation steals much of the incoming solar energy because of clear air and low water vapour.

Neyvertheless, the Arctic can annually receive more direct
sunlight as well as up to 3.5 times more twilight than at the
Equator. Sunlightmay be reduced by clouds and ice fog, as well
as the turbidity ofthe air, although aerosols and water vapour
arelargely absent. Incoming solar radiation is decreased because
of the low solar angles, but radiation exchange and loss are
increased by reflection from snow and ice on the flatlandscape.

Winters are longand cold, summers in July and early August
are short and cool. Mean annual temperatures at settlements
are seldom above freezing and sometimes are below -12 °C (to
°F). Mean monthly temperatures range between -1 °C (30 °F)
and 21 °C (70 °F). The sub-Arctic inland has the lowest winter
temperatures and coldest on earth.

Precipitation on the tundra is slight - 25 cm (10 in) or less
annually. Some major areas have 12.5 cm (5 in) or less. Surprisingly, blizzards are often only wind-blown old snow. The cold
in winter is extremely dry. A typical winter day temperature
ranges between —23 °C (-9 °F) and -34 °C (-29 °P). The sea is

130

EDGE INSE SET EREETEERISSEEDESIESTOTERETNETETE= nenn

frozen from mid-October until late June. The short summer of
complete daylight seldom gets above 10 °C (50 °P). The most
northern people on earth, the Polar Inuit of northwest Greenland, live in a tundra area where only one month, July, has average temperatures that rise above freezing.

The taiga or boreal region is the largest forest on earth, primarily coniferous. The position ofthe treeline and the distribution ofwoody plants are generally determined by temperatures
during the growing season. Precipitation in the taiga is variable
buttypically is between 25 cm (to in) annually (sometimes considered the lower limit for appreciable vegetation) and 50 cm
(20 in). The mean annual temperature is between -I0 °C (14 °F)
and -4°C (25 °F). Summer of one to three months with mean
temperatures above 10 °C (50 °F) is considered short and hot.
Mosquitoes and other pestilent insects thrive. Extensive bogs
(muskeg), innumerable lakes and flowing streams punctuate
the rolling terrain with its shallow poor soil. Marginal agriculture is possible with a freeze-free season of between 70 and go
days. The long summer daylight hours enhance fast growth
and early ripening.

Ocean temperatures are a major climatic influence. Melted
polar ice provides cold water. Tropically warmed ocean currents rotate to the north and east making the west coasts of
North America and Europe temperate and humid into the high
latitudes. Thus, Scandinavia has milder winters and less severe
climates than Siberia on the same latitude.

Although relatively rich in seasonal game and sea mammals,
the Arctic is potentially the most challenging environment
on earth for humans. Sea mammals thrive in polar waters
normally.ato°C (32 °F) in part because the temperature gradient
to their deep body temperature is only half ofthe 50 °C (125 °F)
gradient between deep body temperature and the environment
ofmostterrestrialanimals and humans. For seals, walruses and
whales, who have little hair on their skin, a layer of blubber
serves as insulation. The musk-ox has two layers of fur - a very
dense layer of wool close to the skin and an outer layer oflong
hairs. Humans use both body fatand animals furs together with

strategically layered architectural shelter, to respond.

Vernacular response

Furs are worn, and skins or furs are often used in construction,
both as radiant shields and as insulating layers against heat
conduction inside shelters. But controlling the cold wind is the
first strategy. Until recently only those culturally skilled in the
climate could survive.

The human potential for modifying extreme climates is best
demonstrated architecturally by the performance of the snow
iglu (igluviga) of the Inuit, made of blocks of wind-packed dıy
snow. Interior temperatures may run 36 °C (65 °F) or more
above outdoor values, and with a lining may reach 15 °C (59 °F).
Although this dwelling type provides conditions far below the
comfort range ofindustrialized peoples, this ingenious microclimatic control using only snow provides amenable indoor
living well above a survival mode. Earth and timber iglus provide
even more insulation opportunity. Snow is stacked against the
more popular Inuit karmet or qummik of stone, whalebone and
sod, and even against tents (tupig) for its insulation value.

rences
