ms), northern
Sattinavia.

jammer, Fred, and Tay-

Dleffrey Cook

The conical winter huts, or turf’kata, ofthe Lapps (Sami) of
northern Scandinavia sit deep in well-protected taiga forest,
and illustrate environmental fit. Layers oflogs, and bark, peat
and turf make a substantial, continuous all-roof enclosure
supported by curved rafters similar to the tent kata. The ground
is insulated with a thick layer of spruce boughs, or birch
twigs and covered with reindeer skins. The central firepit, with
its ring of'hearth stones, and the suspended hot metal pot provide multiple radiant heat sources. Cracks in the plank door
allow some ventilation air, although smoke and an ice fog
caused by cooking and breathing fill the interior to close to the
ground. JEFFREY COOK

1.11.1.b Continental

Continental climates dominate the large flat landmasses at mid-latitudes, isolated from the maritime influence
ofooceans. They include extensive areas that stretch from semihumid to semi-arid areas and from subtropical to sub-Arctic
regions oftemperature. Strong winds, large diurnal temperature swings, large seasonal differences, and generally low
humidity characterize all continental climates. Both winter
and summer are relatively long. Spring and autumn are shorter
with rapid transitions to the two long seasons. The strong
differences between the warmest and coldest monthly
mean temperatures are a way of expressing the continentality
of climates. In north-central Asia, the range is about 67°C
(120°F) and in North America the largest range is about 50°C
(go°P); in the southern hemisphere it rarely exceeds 17°C
(ZIP).

Cold, cool and temperate continental climates dominate the
midland centres of North America and Asia. They extend southward from the sub-Arctic taiga through the mid-latitude grasslands and hardwood forests; from semi-arid mid-latitude areas
towards the hotdeserts to subtropicaledges. The North American
Great Plains and grass prairie have their Russian counterpart in
the semi-arid steppe. However, the grasslands of Bastern Europe

Climate 1.1.1

are not so continental: their humidity and less severe summers
and winters are influenced by the Atlantic Ocean. In Russia, the
influence of the Atlantic extends as far as the Ural mountains
where continental characteristics dominate.

In the southern hemisphere warm continental climates with
hot summers and cool winters are found roughly between
latitudes 20 °Sand 40 °S, butalso extending between desertand
maritime regions. Annual precipitation under 50 cm (20 in)
usually produces dominant grasslands. In Australia these continental grasslands and treeless plains are known as ‘downs’,
in southern Africa as veld, and in South America as the Pampas
of Argentina.

Continental climates have wide contrasts in the seasons:
warm or hot summers and cool or cold winters. The openness
of the terrain allows exposure to winds and major storm systems. The European four seasons of spring, summer, autumn
or fall, and winter are revised for the continental climates of
Russia by adding ‘pre-winter’, November-December, and ‘presummer’, May-June to make six two-month seasons. Annual
precipitation is modest but usually adequate for crops and
native tree and grass species. Winter weather is dominated by
frigid polar cold fronts.

In the northern hemisphere the growing season is restricted
to between 100 and 140 continuous frost-free days. The mean
temperature is below freezing for one to four months. Snow
cover is commonly continuous in winter. In the northern hemisphere, January is the coldest month and July the warmest.
Deciduous trees include beech, birch, chestnut, elm, maple,
oak and poplar. Fruit trees include apple, cherry, pear and
plum. Trees become stunted and sparse at higher latitudes

where with increased aridity they give way to natural grassland
or the cultivation of hardy grains such as winter wheat and
barley.

Vernacular responses

Architectural and planning responses are primarily to winter
and include the use of trees and earth as wind protection.
Dwellings are builtin the shelter ofwooded ravines and valleys,
or behind planted landscapes serving as wind-breaks. Winter
houses ofthe first natives on the Great Plains of North America
such as the Hidatsa and Pawnee earth lodges are low profile,
earth embraced, and aerodynamic in form to reduce surface
exposure. Elsewhere are found earth beams and partially
underground construction including the lowered floors of pit
houses with earth-covered walls, or airtight fabric or skin
construction — techniques of wind avoidance that also take
advantage ofthe thermal capacity ofthe earth.

Nomadic shelters such as the conical tipi of the American
Plains Indians are particular about orientation, with their
entrance to the east, and the steeper rear side braced against
prevailing westerly winds. Continuous wind-breaks and snow
fences of cane or saplings are built to windward and are sometimes up to 3 m (10 ft) high. Adjustable smoke flaps sensitive to
changing weather conditions allow full control of ventilation
through the top. With their aerodynamic design, tipis are
surprisingly comfortable in winter when a central fire and
additional liners ofskins or fabric are used. For insulation, grass

131

## S. 16

Fr nenn m al u u Een no

1. ENVIRONMENT
is stuffed between the liners, and snow is banked on the outside
around the base.

The indigenous persistence of rammed earth and sod wall
construction introduced by European settlers in the Great
Plains of the United States indicates the advantages of heavy
mass and relatively draught-free techniques in these windy
climates ofextreme temperature change. Rammed earth is also
used extensively in Asia. In the 19805 at least oo million
Chinese lived in cave dwellings and other earthen buildings,
including 30 million underground in the loess plateau.

JEFFREY COOK
1.111.1.c Desert
Desert climates are defined by dryness or absence of
moisture, not primarily by high temperatures. But few clouds
mean clear skies and dependable sunshine. The proportion of
precipitation to evaporation defines aridity. Köppen defined
deserts as ‘where moisture that could evaporate, if available, is
at least double actual precipitation’. But Phoenix, Arizona, has
a ratio of 1:16, with 1275 mm (7 in) ofrain annually and 2.921m
(115 in) of pan evaporation. More scientifically, Thornthwaite
has used the term ‘evapotranspiration’ to describe the quantity
ofmoisture lost through evaporation from the soil and transpiration from vegetation in comparison to monthly precipitation.

The major hot ortropical deserts ofthe world occupy over 20
per cent ofthe Barth’s surface and have an average annual temperature above 18 °C (64 °F). They are found discontinuously at
approximately 15-30 °N and °S ofthe Equator at places ofhigh
atmospheric pressure and often in therain shadow ofmountain
ranges. Hot deserts dominated by the highest summer air temperatures are the Sahara, the Arabian desert, the Sonora desert
of northwest Mexico and parts of Arizona and California, the
'Thar of northwestern India, the Kalahari of southwestern
Africa, and the interior of Australia. Rare variants that have similar conditions, but with high humidities because of proximity

to the sea are the maritime deserts characteristic of Jeddah,
Karachi, Kuwait and the coast ofPeru.

Mid-latitude or cool deserts in both hemispheres are caused
by topography and geographic location deep within a continent. The rain shadow of high mountain ranges accounts for
the Great Basin ofthe western United States and the Patagonian deserts east ofthe Andes in South America. The dry air of
continental interiors explains the Turkestan desert east ofthe
Caspian Sea, andthe Gobi and Taklemakan ofcentral Asia. The
latter have the greatest diurnal and seasonal temperature
ranges ofany place on Earth.

Although defined by aridity, deserts are known for their
extremes: strong sun and high temperatures. All the deserts of
the world average over 3000 hours of sunshine annually, and
parts ofthe Sonora and Sahara deserts receive more than 4000
hours. Summer daytime air temperatures easily reach 43 °C (1ro
°F), with more than 100 continuous days having temperatures
over 37.5 °C (100 °F). Because oflow water vapour in the atmosphere, both incoming solar radiation and outgoing radiation
are high, causing rapid air temperature changes, as much as 3
°C (5 °P) per hour. Sunny hot days and rapidly cooling nights
give large diurnal cycles with 16.5-27.5°C (30-50°F) differ-

132

ences between maximum and minimum air temperatures. The
hottest deserts can have winter frosts.

In all deserts there tend to be only two seasons: hot summer
and mild winter, with very short transitions between. Typically,
rainfall is erratic, ranging from less than 250 mm (to in) annually to no precipitation. Several rainless years can be broken by
adeluge. The intensity ofbrief precipitation and the baked and
rocky conditions of the earth with sparse vegetation cause
flooding. Only the Sonora desert is green, because ofpreeipitation both in midwinter and in July and August from a moist
‘monsoon’ condition. In maritime deserts, rainfall is below
250 mm (1o in), but normal humidities between 45 and go per
cent promote vegetation and add sky glare. In other deserts
vegetation is scarcer or non-existent. Desert vegetation demonstrates evolutionary adaptation to extreme conditions similar
to successful human patterns to deal with the scarcity of water
and high temperatures.

The Gobi, Patagonia and other mid-latitude deserts are as
dry as the hot deserts but have lower sets of temperatures.
The Gobi deserts have violent and severe blizzards, with areas
having as many as six months with mean temperatures below
freezing. Yet summer average maximums of 40.5-43°C
(105-110 °F) are possible.

Desert terrain is extremely varied including rugged topography and broken rock. Contrary to popular opinion loose sand
characterizes only 2 per cent ofthe terrain of North American
deserts, rı per centofthe Sahara and 30 per centofthe Arabian
desert. All deserts are subject to erratic winds, which atspeeds
between 15 and 30 mph can pick up fine ground particles. Sand

or dust storms vary from small whirlwinds or twisters (dust
devils”) to violent regional events, as well as persistent dust
haze. Each has local names.

Vernacular response

Cave and cliff dwellings provide natural shelter from desert
temperature extremes. Massive buildings are often designed in
imitation, with blank walls, shuttered openings, courtyards
and narrow streets to baffle dusty winds. Heavy construction
materials using earth, stone or other masonry can mediate and
delay the substantial thermal flux. Walls need to bea minimum
of25 cm (10 in) thick to provide an eight-hour thermal lag, but
walls 50 cm (20 in) and thicker are more common. The groundfloor plan of an Afghan fortified farm compound (qala) illustrates the use of heavy mud walls for three dwellings around a
courtyard. The outer walls and tower are also defensive, but the
inner walls and splayed windows are as climatically responsive
as the seasonal changes in use.

The intense solar heating of surfaces can be reduced by
reflective surfaces or byventilated double construction. Vaulted
and domed roofs by their larger surfaces exposed to the night
sky vault can reradiate heat better than flatroofs.

Courtyards through variations of proportions and surface
materials can provide a great variety of microclimatic modifications, sometimes in different parts of the same space at the
same time. Daily human vertical movement within different
parts of.a building parallels the diurnal adaptive patterns of
many plants and animals. In traditional Baghdad houses under-

References I
Hatton, E.M., 1979
Nabokov, Peter, andka
ton, Robert, 1989
Qimen Jing, Lan Sana
Song Haillang, 1995. I
Trewartha, Glenn Tu

‚Central Asia

De

summer village
ind house (vahki)

ground rooms are 29-32 °C (84-90 °F) between May and September while outdoor temperatures may reach 50 °C (122 °F).
Seasonal migrations are also vernacular responses to desert
climates. The Papago (Tohono O’Odham) of the southern
Arizona Sonora desert move seasonally to ‘follow the water’.
Extreme variations in radiation and temperatures also make
the opposite ofheavy massive enclosures equally desirable, such
as shade roofs, arbours or ramadas. These low mass or no mass
overhead structures made with brush on wood frames, or as
fabric tensile structures, are found in many unrelated cultures
throughout the desert world. The Papago summer ‘field village’
includes the circular ‘rainhouse’, ‘round house’, or vahki with
vertical walls ofdense brush, and dry earth over the brush roofto
resist the sun. The vahki is always accompanied by an arbour
(watto). Since desert winds are normally light, and much of
the thermal exchange in deserts is by radiation, tents can also
provide considerable climatic shelter. The nomadic ‘black tent’
of both Asia and Africa integrates a number of climatic
responses, primarily as a radiation shield and exchanger.

Ventilation is not a usual summer desert cooling strategy
because air temperature is mostly above skin temperature. But
areas open to the night sky can provide human comfort by
radiation to outer space even when air temperatures are elevated. Windows are usually shuttered to allow movable control.
Lace-like screens or lattices are self-shading and reduce the
glare of the brilliant sky. The cantilevered wood space of the
mashrabiyya of Islamic architecture with its latticed openings
integrate several cooling strategies. In selective locations such
as parts of Iran and Sindh, where predictable cooler winds are
available, architectural forms of wind-towers (badgirs) have
evolved together with naturally driven ventilation systems. The
evaporation of water for cooling takes many architectural
forms in desert climates.

The mid-latitude deserts such as the Gobi are dominated by
severe cold in winter and require strategies similar to sub-Arcticand continental climates. Thus, many varieties ofthe yurt of
central Asia demonstrate wind-baffling, aerodynamic forms
and the insulation value of animal fur and fibres such as felt
mats over sapling wood lattices.

JEFFREY COOK

Climate 1.111.1

1.11.1.d Maritime

The term ‘maritime’ describes mid-latitude coastal
climates continuously dominated by ocean influences. They are
humid, temperate and changeable, having short, cool summers
and short, mild winters with some frost but not continuous
snow. Both springand autumn are extended in length. There are
small differences between monthly mean temperatures. Precipitation occurs in all seasons.

The land edges on all oceans between latitudes 40° and 60°,
both North and South, have temperate maritime climates. The
northwest, northeast and mid-Atlantic coasts of the United
States, and the Atlantic provinces and west coast of Canada
have characteristic maritime climates, like the coasts of Chile,
New Zealand, the southeast coast of Australia and most of
Japan. Most maritime climates are restricted by coastal mountain ranges or by prevailing winds off the land. Western and
central Europe are the exceptions, where the absence of highland or mountain barrier leaves the continent open to the wind
and moisture effects of the Atlantic. This makes the climate
warmer than similar latitudes elsewhere, comparable to atleast
10° latitude closer to the Equator. The tempering effect of the
Atlantic is reinforced by the presence to the south of the
Mediterranean Sea and to alesser extent by the Caspian and the
Black Seas. However, the position of Spain and the mountainous block ofthe Alps separate the climates of western, central
and northern Europe from countries surrounding the Mediterranean.

Maritime climates are changeable with moderate conditions
within a narrow temperature range. Deciduous vegetation
characterizes the landscape. There are no dry seasons. Fog,
dampness and overcast conditions can occur throughout the
year, but this description best fits winter. Ocean influences
make August the warmest month in the northern hemisphere.
In the southern hemisphere it is February. Typically, daily conditions in mid-ocean have an average difference of1.7°C (3 °F)
between the highest and lowest temperature. On small islands
and immediately on the coast this range is less than 5.5 °C (1o
°P). But the microclimate influence ofthe nearby ocean usually
dissipates within 160 km (100 mi) of most coasts. Continuous

westerly and northwest winds make Europe unique.

Western, northern and central Europe provide an exception,
because the ocean influences are strong for over 3200 km (2000
mi). Locations far from the Atlantic such as Belgrade and
Budapest receive slightly more rain than Paris or London. In
spite of the changeability of the weather there is a relatively
narrow range of conditions. European culture measures climate into four equal seasons of spring, summer, autumn or
fall, and winter. The annual precipitation in Atlantic Europe is
moderate, between 510 mm (20 in) and 760 mm (30 in).

Vernacular responses

Architectural responses to changeable temperate maritime climates are not consistent, although there is always a sensitivity
to dampness. There is much less recognition of climate in the
literature of European vernacular architecture than in studies in
other climates and locations. However, similar roof pitches
have been noted in Ireland, Hungary and Japan, as have similar

133

## S. 17

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

uses of thatch or reeds as a roofing and insulation material.

Large roofs ofinsulated materials and limited wall areas reduce

thermal loss and maximize the advantage of a large central
oven to heat the entire house. But in similar climates both in
Europe and elsewhere there are also traditions of roofing with
slate, terracotta tile, or flagstones - more permanent and fire-

resistant materials that have much less insulative value. Orientation, when consciously practised, avoids the direction of
stormy winds. Stormy exposures presumably are the basis of
small, widely spaced windows in locations such as Ireland. But
large windows for adequate light are an architectural compensation for overcast maritime skies in some other locations at
higher latitudes such as Scandinavia and the low countries of
Europe.

Masonry walls, or stucco or lime washes over earthen oradobe
walls may be responses to damp conditions. When wood is
exposed, the tradition ofpaint is often more than aesthetic. Alternatively, the vernacular uses of corrugated iron metal roofs with
generous porches in southeast Australia and New Zealand may
have their indigenous counterparts in the wooden assembly
houses and porches ofthe Maori in New Zealand, and the plank
roofs ofthe Haida ofthe westcoastofCanada. The local presence
of rot-resistant timbers such as red cedar on the west coast of
Canada supports the exposed wood structures ofthe Haida and
Nootka. In cooler and more variable maritime climates, such as
New England in the United States and the Atlantic provinces of
Canada, wood houses have only slight overhangs, and continuously painted surfaces address storms from all directions.

Hungarian village houses illustrate traditions evolved over
almost a millennium that combine social custom and response
to predictable maritime effects coming from the north and west.
The house is entered from the south and from thesside. The plan
is alinear pattern ofrooms in asingle row, broadside to the sun.
There are no openings in the north wall that sits on the property
line. The gable-ends of houses define the street. By the end of
the 18th century and the early-ıgth century, continuous porches
were often added along the south wall. The wind-protected yard
is fenced and gated to the streetand provides a discrete outdoor
space usable most ofthe year. JEFFREY COOK
1.11.1.e Mediterranean
Mediterranean climates are moderate and coastal
with sunny, dry, warm summers, and mild or cool, damp winters. With infrequent freezingtemperatures and with most ofits
modest precipitation in the winter, this subtropical zone ofthe

134

citrus and the olive tree is essentially a climate of two contrasting seasons.

Although this climate is named after the lands around the
Mediterranean Sea, it has been argued that not all European
borderlands above latitude 40 °N share its qualities. Similarly,
the tempering effects of the Mediterranean Sea do not penetrate very far inland on the North African side, which quickly
becomes desert. Mediterranean climates range in latitude from
45 °N in the Adriatic and Black Seas to 30 °N in Libya. Mediterranean characteristics are also found in areas south ofthe Black
and Caspian Seas, and on the west coasts of all continents at
similar latitudes, such as parts of California, Chile, South
Africa, and southern and southwest Australia.

This well-known climate has alongand rich legacy ofhuman
settlements and culture around the Mediterranean. Generally it
is a two-season climate with brief transitions between the
unsettled skies of winter and the sunny days ofsummer. Summer comes late. Although the Mediterranean receives an
amount ofannual precipitation similar to that of western and
central Europe, between 500 mm (20 in) and 760 mm (30 in),
most of it is delivered in winter, when the sea surface is 3-4 °C
(5-7 °P) warmer than the average air temperature. However,
some regions, generally those between latitudes 40 and 45 °N,
have more complex rainfall patterns, usually with two maxima
and two minima months. In other locations of the world
Mediterranean climates have somewhat different timetables,
although annual patterns are similar and the wet cool winter is
shorter. The climates inland and on adjacent uplands also have
two seasons, but with stronger diurnal and annual ranges.

The unique situation of the Mediterranean Sea, a large deep
body ofthermally stable water surrounded by land at mid-latitudes, is not repeated elsewhere in the world. Thus, there are
few similar climates elsewhere. The tempering effect of this
large body of warm water stabilizes air temperatures and
reduces diurnal swings. Another distinctive aspect of the
Mediterranean basin is its rugged terrain and broken surrounding highlands, especially the Alps, which produce many local
and regional microclimates and anomalies. Local names for
special winds are numerous. The hot dry sirocco blows from the
Sahara. Cold northerly winds are the mistral in central southern
France and the bora in Greece.

Vernacular response

Although Mediterranean climates are characterized as mild
with little diurnal variation, indigenous architecture is usually
heavy masonry regardless of local culture. This construction
passively provides extremely stable interior conditions that are
desirable in summer, but require tolerance and heating in winter. Although is it assumed that masonry became popular to
provide thermal mass as a climatic response, it may be noted
that there are few forests or other regional construction
resources for building apart from stone. In addition, there may
also be cultural preferences for permanent construction.

An analysis ofa massive seasonal agricultural dwelling used
from April to October in southern Italy reveals typical performance during a summer day. A typical trullo in Apulia is built of
small fieldstone and covered with conical roofs. This trullo had

thatched roof,

Guidoni, Enrici

whitewashed
12x05, cyclades

a single door and a small window on the south, and one tiny
window to the north. Indoor maximum air temperatures were 4
°C (7 °E) below the outdoor maximum. The inside diurnal temperature swing was around 4 °C (7 °F) while outside it was
around 10°C (18 °F).

The popular whitewash and white stucco that characterize
the villages of Cyclades Islands and the white towns of southern Italy, Spain and North Africa have the effect of being the
most advantageous surface to reflect the heat of the summer
sun. Whitewashing both inside and out distributes light and
allows smaller windows. Annual whitewashing also achieves a
hygienic resultin aregion where cleansing rains are scarce. But
these architectural responses do not recognize the winter need
for solar heat. Inclusion of clean rainwater catchments and cisterns is a necessary practice where rains come only in winter.

Accommodation for outdoor living in the design ofloggias,
balconies, outdoor stairs, terraces, porticos, patios, walled gardens, decorative water features and plazas reflects architectural
and climatic interaction, primarily for summer. Deliberate
planning in the layout of contained courtyards and narrow
streets also provides alternative outdoor use spaces, as well as
microclimatically controlled outlooks ftom built spaces. The
custom of the afternoon siesta is reinforced by architectural
Provision of shutters and blinds. These longstanding features
are also associated with adjacent desert climates, but here they
incur a penalty in winter. JEFFREY COOK
1.111.1.f Monsoon
Monsoon (Arabic: mausim, ‘climate’) climates are
warm or hot with two, or sometimes three, distinct seasons
includinga wet one. These climates are also called ‘composite’.
Although a larger part of the year is dry, the monsoon period

Climate 1.111.1
ofup to four months is warm and humid with daily rain, sometimes heavy. ‘Monsoon’ can refer to any wind thatreverses itself
seasonally or blows continuously between land and adjacent
water, but it especially refers to that of southern Asia where
between April and October it moves from the southwest
bringing moisture and rain from the Indian Ocean. Throughout the rest of the year the wind blows from the northeast towards the ocean, providing much of India with a dry
season.

Monsoon climates occur in large landmasses near the tropics of Cancer and Capricorn where they are generated by the
annual global shifts of solar radiation, and thus of wind. Locations furthest away from the Equator such as north-central
India often havea third season which is cool and dry in addition
to the two warm or hot seasons, one wet and one dry. Aside
from south and central India, much of Southeast Asia, inland
parts of Vietnam, Cambodia, Thailand and Burma, the southern half of Brazil, most of Venezuela and parts of Mexico
and the West Indies in the Caribbean Sea have warm or hot.climates with distincet dry and wet seasons caused by reversing
wind patterns.

Africa has two major bands of monsoon climates, on either
side ofthe Equator: between latitudes 3 and 18 °S, and between
latitudes 5 and ı2 °N. Though unreliable, the alternation of a
dry season with several wet months generates the typical tropical grassland, or savanna, thatcharacterizes the natural African
landscape oflarge animals.

In a three-season monsoon climate Table ı describes
monthly mean air temperatures.

Tableı. Monthly mean air temperatures

Time Hot-dry Warm-humid Cool-dry
season season season
Daytime max 32-43 °C] 27-32°C/ upto27°C/
90-110 °F 81-90 °F 81°E
Night min 21-27°C 24-27°C 4-10°C
70-81°F 75-81 °F 39-50 °F
Diurnal range 1-22 °C 3-6°C 11-22 °C
20-40 °F 5-11 °F 20-40 °F

Thus, the hot-dry season has hot dry days but pleasant
nights. The monsoon, or warm-humid, season is the most
uncomfortable with very high humidity. The rains can be some
relief, but nights are also uncomfortably humid, and mosquitoes can carry malaria. The winter, or cool-dry, season provides
pleasant sunny days but cool and even chilly nights.

The skies are clear and deep blue during the dry season when
winds are hot and dusty. At the onset of the monsoon the sky
becomes hazier with increased brightness and glare. The relative humidity during the dry season of 20-55 per cent rises in
the wet season to between 55 and 95 per cent. The wet season
coincides with summer or the high sun period. Skies then
become heavily overcast and grey. Annual rainfall of 500-1300
mm (20-51 in) all comes in the wet season. Rain intensity can
bring 25-38 mm (1-1.5 in) per hour.

135

## S. 18

DJeffrey Cook after Gupta

1. ENVIRONMENT

Vernacular response

The strongly contrasting seasons imply either an architecture
of contrasting or movable parts or movable functions, or one
that is compromised in at least one of the seasons. The grass
hipped roof as a parasol over mud walls with few small openings and with a recessed porch typifies the dilemma. However,
responses can often be integrated. The bungalow is a wellknown hot climate housetype that originated as a rural peasant
dwelling in the composite climate of Bengal, now partly
Bangladesh. This single household dwelling is now a onestorey design typically on a raised floor found on every continent and the term ‘bungalow’ itself has entered many
languages. Its design with a deep, well-sheltered porch at least
on one side, and deep overhangs on all sides encourages ventilative cooling, including the convective dissipation of attic
heat from its hipped or gabled roof.

Since the directions of the direct solar radiation and of the
strong rain-laden winds are predictable, orientation of elements is effective. Typically, the predominant season directs
the prime response. Thus a hot dry season encourages massive
walls on the ground floor, with a storey of lightweight construction above for sleeping, or an open roof deck. Elements
such as porches or verandas can provide shade during the hot
dıy period, but also rain protection during the wet season ofa
composite climate. Alternatively, separate parts ofa house can
be used in different seasons: an enclosed but ventilated roof
bedroom pavilion (saiwaan or barsati) for the monsoon and an
open sleeping terrace during the dıy season. Within walled Old
Delhi, Shahjahanabad, the kucha house is just one localized
example that characterizes such a generalized description.

Such a timber-framed multistorey house with brick infill
stuccoed on both faces characterizes other Indian monsoon
cities and even villages. With shared perimeter walls only the
narrow front wall is exposed to the weather on a narrow street.
Both that surface and the sides ofthe courtyard are faced with
intricate wood carving and shutters, reducing solar gain and
providing options for thermal control. Their small courtyards
(chowk) of 6-10 sq m (65-100 sq ft) allow fresh air to enter only
when that air is cooler than the building. These urban selfshaded courtyard houses, such as the pol houses ofAhmedabad
and the haveli of Gujarat and Jaisalmer perform best during the
desert-like hot dry season. Especially the lower floors provide
cooler but more humid spaces during the day. At nightthe roof
terraces are washed and used for social and sleeping purposes.
Parapets are not built around the chowks to allow cooler and
heavier night air to flush the interior. During the monsoon the

D)

RAD. TO SKY

COURTYARD

STREET

136

chowks are now sometimes covered with sheets of plastic to
keep out the rain, but this intensifies the build-up ofhumidity
in the interior by restricting ventilation. Ceiling fans help, but
sleeping in small rooms on the roof brings more relief aside
from the mosquitoes. During the winter the house returns to
diurnal functions. Upper storeys and roof are used in the daytime, but nights are chilly everywhere since there is no heating
system. These patterns of comfort performance are reinforced
by daily and seasonal patterns oflivingand household management. JEFFREY COOK
Montane

Regardless of latitude, the altitude of the land and
the orientation of slopes always influence local climate. These
topographic variations are exaggerated in mountainous areas
by the contrast between ridges, slopes and plateau, and valleys
and canyons, often within short horizontal distances. The
complexity of these varying local conditions distinguishes
montane climates from lowland conditions at the same latitude. Frequent precipitation, dominant diurnal wind patterns,
distinet seasons, many local microclimates and seasonal snow
describe montane climates.

Mountainous geology generates islands ofdistinct climate at
all latitudes and on all continents. For instance, Arctic conditions can exist on the Equator. In addition to local effects, large
mountain ranges have a major impact on global climate. The
massive high relief ofthe Himalayas and the Andes, where the
elevations of over 5500 m (18000 ft) extend over hundreds of
miles, presents a continuous barrier to all movement of
weather systems in the lower halfofthe atmosphere. Thus, they
develop their own distinet climates while causing major climatic shifts on both the windward and lee regions. Of importance to human settlement are the interrelated mountain and
valley systems as well as broad highland plateaux that offer climates distinet from adjacent lower locations. Variations in
soils and terrain add to the choices both of human sustenance
and the availability ofbuilding materials.

The most elementary montane climate effect is that air temperatures decrease at a rate of 5-Io °C/ı000 m (3-6 °F/rooo ft)
of altitude increase. This major temperature change causes
changes in the length ofthe growing season that modify vegetation and also affects human settlements. Italso provides temperate or cold climates attropical and subtropical elevations, as
well as many localized microclimates and microenvironments.

The complexity of montane climates requires detailed local
examination and comparison with nearby conditions. All locations have strong diurnal and seasonal patterns ofchange. Generally, mountains have higher rainfalls and snowfalls because
cumulonimbus clouds are more frequent. They are formed by
the heating and rising of air on the sunlit mountain slopes. In
addition, the uplift of air streams on the windward sides of
mountains causes condensation of moisture and increased preeipitation on thatside. The reverse occurs on the leeward, causing a rain shadow and dry winds. Gaps or passes in long
mountain barriers can cause strong local continuous winds.

While mountains form barriers to large-scale wind penetrations, protected valleys usually develop local winds. Sun-

ERMUNINUNERNEN IN

warmed slopes cause air to move up the slopes and valleys. At
nightwhen the air is clear, outgoing radiation cools the ground
surface and the contiguous air. The heavier cool air will move
down the slope, flowing into the valley and following water
drainage topography. Since the cold air drainage may be less
than 3 m (to ft) deep, itcan be blocked by walls or dense plantings to protect gardens and houses.

Vernacular response
The diversity of local conditions affects human settlement in
montane climates by providing ecological variety and interlocking food gatheringaand agricultural choices, as well as construction alternatives. Seasonal human movement takes
advantage of climate-based opportunities such as summer
grazing at high elevations. Typically, housing is located neither
in the bottom ofvvalleys because of both potential flooding and
cold air drainage nor on ridges which are exposed to winds;
rather, it is along sunny, protected slopes except in tropical
locations. Multi-storey houses, farm accommodation and living-quarters under the same roof, and the tight organization of
farms and villages result from climatic need as well as the
steepness of terrain and the availability of agricultural land.
Responses to snow and rain dominate architectural details.

Settlements on plateaux are especially governed by exposure.
For instance, a traditional Slovene shepherd’s hut from Velika
Plana in the Julian Alps responds to the changeable wind, snow
and fogon Alpine pastures. An aerodynamic form allows any orientation and shelters an attached fenced yard. The lean-to outer
ring provides storage for forage, supplies which are insulative,
and also animals who produce heat. The protected rectangular
inner room with raised floor, stove, table, bunks and a tiny window among the shingles provides refuge through storms.

High precipitation, which can occur daily in parts ofmontane
climates, typically increases agricultural or forestry potential.

BANN Ih
’ Ah MIN \
Zum EN

SITE HB nV nu. 2

Climate 1.1.1
Multi-storey houses in these climates are usually dominated by
expansive roofs with generous overhangs to keep rain and
melted snow away from the walls. Sheltered raised porches are
also characteristic to provide for dry outdoor work, laundry,
firewood storage and living space. Relatively shallow roof
slopes and hay storage in the attic allow insulating winter snows
to accumulate. Barns also provide sheltered outdoor work space
and have similar roofs. These features are prototypical in the
Swiss chalet, but are found in many different cultures with similar montane conditions such as Nepal. Construction details
such as the cold roofand the use ofa stone-walled ground floor
to resist rotting from rain and snow, and with timber-framed
overhanging upper storeys are also characteristic. Housing animals and other barn activities are sometimes incorporated at
ground or basement level of houses for economy of both land
and construction. In cold montane conditions such as Ladakh,
wooden floor boards above allow animal heat to rise into inhabited space. JEFFREY COOK
1.11.1.h Subtropical

Humid warm coastal climates are called ‘subtropical’ because they are warm but outside the tropics. Annual
conditions are usually not as intense or continuous as in the
tropics because of the multiple seasons. The cool or mild winter is shortand damp with rain, but seldom with snow, and the
frost-free period is atleastnine months. The humid summer is
long, warm and muggy. But there is appreciable humidity
throughout the year. Similar conditions with variable seasons
also characterize the uplands and plateaux within the tropies
including at the Equator.

Large lowland areas with humid warm climates are on the
east or southeast coasts of four continents, between latitudes
25° and 38°: the southern states ofthe United States along the
Gulf of Mexico; parts of Brazil and Argentina; the east coast
of Queensland in Australia; and most of southeast China.
Secondary areas of humid warm climate are the tropical and
Equatorial uplands and plateaux that have elevations of more
than 900-1200 m (3000-4000 ft). Such locations include areas
near Addis Ababa, Bogotä, Mexico City and Nairobi.

Humid subtropical summers rarely have average monthly
temperatures above 25 °C (77 °F). However, in some places,
summer conditions of heat and humidity can exceed those of
the tropics. Cool humid winters rarely have average monthly
temperatures below 10 °C (50 °F). The continuous humidity
provides a narrow annual range of temperatures, as well as a
small diurnal temperature swing. Rainfall and temperature
patterns may vary based on both topography and on ocean
influences. Rain is ample, spread through the year for agriculture or Jush natural landscapes. Precipitation may range up to
1400 mm (55 in) a year. Average wind speeds are low, although
these locations often bear the brunt of seasonal tropical storms
such as hurricanes from the Caribbean Sea, or typhoons from
the China Sea.

Tropical uplands also have humidities between 45 and nearly
100 per cent, with variable precipitation throughout the year
totalling above 1000 mm (40 in) annually. Temperatures and
other conditions are similar to those ofthe humid warm climates

137

## S. 19

1.11 ENVIRONMENT

of the subtropics, temperature ranges depending on latitude.
Close to the Equator the monthly temperature variation is very
small, as is the distinction ofseasons. Near the tropics of Cancer
and Capricorn monthly mean temperatures may vary with a
differential range between ır and 20 °C (20 and 36 °F); seasonal
variations are significant. Some locations have modest monsoon
patterns but without the extreme conditions found in such composite climates at lower elevations. Daytime high temperatures
and humidity may be more uncomfortable because ofthe more
intense radiation at these higher elevations. But nights may be
cooler because ofincreased radiation loss.

Vernacular response

Subtropical climates require dual, conflicting responses.
Because winters, however uncomfortable, are usually short,
typical architectural responses allow full enclosure to benefit
from burning fuels for heating. But these strategies are compromised by designs that favour the overheated periods with
responses similar to those of tropical climates: avoid the sun
and catch the breezes - although in the subtropics the sun
angle and other climatic dimensions are different and more
variable.

The sky is never blue because it is full of water vapour and
thus often has a bright glare in summer. Solar glare control is
provided through overhangs or porches, and with reduced
openings, shutters or screens on the sunny side. Typically,
houses are one room thick with high ceilings, and large openings encourage air motion, especially cross-ventilation as, for
example, in the wood houses of Charleston, South Carolina, in
the United States. The low mass New Orleans ‘shotgun’ house
thatis also found throughoutthe southeast ofthe United States
and can be closed up for heating in winter is another good
example. Large porches keep off rain and sun while providing
Protected outdoor space exposed to breezes. Louvered openings, jalousied windows with large shutters, or grilled ports
allow ventilation without admitting rain. Screening against
insects is also often necessary. To increase ventilation and
maintain privacy, buildings well spaced with generous gardens
and large canopy shade trees are preferred.

Condensation when the dew point at midday is above the
air temperature at night can be a seasonal problem, while
domestic activities can add to critical conditions of humidity.
However, since condensation occurs on the surface and not
interstitially, the result is a consciousness of paint and of raw
exposed surfaces, as well as the presence ofmoss and algae on
architectural surfaces. Trapped condensation in closets and
cupboards can cause mould and mildew and require louvered
doors or lack of enclosure in interior details. Furnishings also
need to be light and open to avoid trapping heat or moisture.

Light frame construction of fibre, wood, or even metal is
favoured, with materials of heavy mass usually avoided.
Although the passive cooling performance ofthose few indigenous buildings of heavy mass in these climates has not been
sufficiently analysed, the general beliefis that they offer no particular advantages since diurnal and seasonal temperature
cycles are modest. Light mass construction that offers little
thermal storage or time lag is assumed most appropriate in

138

spite of the variability of the seasons. The lushness of native
vegetation provides plentiful fibres and light woods for
construction. Thus, the natural resources and appropriate
materials of these climates are almost the opposite of those
subtropical climates called ‘Mediterranean’ where stone
masonty is so prevalent. The one- and two-storey vernacular
wood frame ‘cracker’ houses of subtropical Florida also raise
such typical questions as ‘is it the availability of materials,
climatic suitability, or other factors that is the primary determinant ofan integrated solution?’

In Zhejiang province at latitude 28 °N on the East China Sea,
traditional feng-shui dietates appropriate orientation. White
lime plastered on thick rammed earth walls tempers summer
heat. Few windows face the sun, and being small, are selfshaded in deep walls. They are placed high, reducing ground
radiation and providing security and deep illumination.
Dwellings are generally more than 5 m (16 ft) deep, about twice
the depth ofthose in the north where full solar penetration in
winter is desirable. In larger houses the porches or verandas
(lang or yanlang) are elaborately developed.

In the Equatorial and tropical uplands, where both incoming
and outgoing radiation are stronger than in coastal locations,
there is an increased appreciation of thermal mass which is
expressed in the more frequent use of eaxth or stone walls. But
light mass porches and shaded ventilated outdoor spaces
respond to daytime needs. JEFFREY COOK
1.1.1.1 Tropical
Tropical climates are warm and wet throughout the
year with little variation. There are no distinctseasons, and only
slight differences between day and night conditions. Humidity
is continuously high and precipitation falls throughout the
year. Also tropical are the Equatorial grasslands with seasonal
or occasional rain and humidity but stable temperatures.

Tropical climates include the rain forests and jungles of the
Caribbean, Central America, Guyana, Amazonia, Equatorial
Africa, the east coast of Madagascar, as well as New Guinea,
Indonesia, Malaysia, south India and coastal Southeast Asia.

Painted woodenh ulhouses, Malay
with generousporı
provide shade onti

south side, Charle: on,
South Carolina, Unltei |
States. \

2.1.4 China, South
3.V.6 Lowland, Soul

Reference Bi
Knapp, Ronald C., 198% N
BR Which conform

lonal responses
tection and
Ion, Singapore.

Typically, this includes most areas within 10° of the Equator,
although tropical climates range from 23 °S at the tropic of
Capricorn on the coasts of Brazil and Madagascar, northwards
to the tropic of Cancer in Bangladesh. Also included are islands
between the tropiclines, and islands affected by the trade winds.

Tropical climates have stable conditions, with a narrow
range of temperature daily and annually. Typically, the mean
temperature in all months is above 18 °C (64 °F). As maximum
air temperatures are usually between 27 °C (81°F) and 32 °C (90
°F) and only very rarely exceed human skin temperatures, they
are not ‘hot’. However, since relative humidity may vary
between 55 and almost 100 per cent, averaging around 75 per
cent, the effect on non-natives can be continuous discomfort.
There is little seasonal variation and close to the Equator especially lack of wind or strong storms prevail. Precipitation is
usually high, 1250-1800 mm (50-70 in). Butin many areas preeipitation is higher, up to 5000 mm (200 in). While skies on
islands may be normally clear or filled with white, broken
clouds, the skies over land may be overcast between 60 and go
per cent of the time but with a high brightness. Atmospheric
turbidity is caused by the high relative humidity which prevents
solar heat from dissipating. Frequent rain storms can be
accompanied by thunder and lightning.

“u

[3%

Ill
Ill

Climate 1.1.1

Vernacular response

In all tropical areas, the architectural response is to provide
extensive roofs for shade, well sloped to shed rain. Hooded
gable vents, can keep off the rain while increasing natural
conyection including stack ventilation. Open skeletal frame
construction, gaps between floorboards and sometimes even
an absence of walls allow ventilation from the slightest breeze.
Louvers, jalousies and shutters add some control, as do sleeping porches, for instance, in Singapore. Generous spaces
contrast with minimal clothing, furnishings and belongings.
Mobility allows humans to seek the slightest climatic advantage to maintain a stable body temperature and to reduce the
impact ofhumidity.

As is typical, vernacular architecture here is usually built
of local lightweight organic materials. Luxuriant vegetation
provides bamboo pole frames, with fibres or leaves woven into
roofs or screens using vines as fastening. Humid tropical conditions encourage raised floors and platforms to provide
protection from floods, dampness and animal pests, and ventilation under and through the floor. Architecturally, the use of
a foundation stone that raises the bottom of each post above
damp soil or vegetation in many cultures is a construction
detail of wet climate origins.

Houseboats, lake villages and pole houses over water are
locations with access to the slightest breeze, and yet are not as
'humid as within dense vegetation. In addition, bodies ofwater
provide a lower radiantenvironmental temperature for much of
the day. Tree houses have similar advantages.

Although tropical climates are relatively uniform, potential
inconsistencies within the architecture have complex explanations. For example, the roof slope ofthe main part ofa traditional Malay house, the rumah ibu, is relatively steep to shed
rain, but the attached scambi samanaik and serambi gantung have
flatter roofs and thus are less responsive to precipitation,
although they throw rainwater further away from the walls. A
roof with a lower slope also reduces natural convection,
although the adjacent high loft space initiates a stack effect that
can pull air from the lower parts of the space, demonstrating
the interaction of the parts. The Malay house has a kitchen or
stove house (rumah dapur), which is sometimes attached and
articulated, and sometimes separated. This hygienic accommodation of food preparation in tropical dwellings is at least
partially a climatic response to remove heat, smells, insects,
rodents and potential fires from the living space, a parallel
to the summer kitchen of other climates and cultures. Thus
climate is a major voice in the formative dialogue ofvernacular

architecture. JEFFREY COOK

139

## S. 20

ın2 Location and Site

The concept of location and site encompasses the
geographical situation of architectural examples with all the
physical aspects which affect and have affected the evaluation
and interpretation of ‘place’ as a field of action.

Locational context helps to explain vernacular architecture
as a direct expression of the daily life of the people. Furthermore, it may also be the key to gaining a deeper understanding
ofsettlements and buildings. The terrain, as given by nature, is
theresult ofalong-term evolution through geological, climatic
and biological processes. From this raw material man, by cultivatingand exploiting nature, has formed the culture landscape.
This landscape is partly the source from which people get their
daily bread and partly it is a location — background and place -
of settling and living in general. Houses established in the
landscape indicate in direct and indirect ways how people have
related to nature and what kind of existence they have created
there. To establish a background for the secondary entries
which handle different location types in detail, this entry will
emphasize conceptual aspects oflocation and site as objects of
interpretation and evaluation.

Interpretation of settlement in its context may follow many
paths. Here three categories of attitude will be distinguished.
The first has as a basic hypothesis the conception that a settlement is expressing an essence of existence which, for man, is
common and universal. Its hypothesis is also that this essence
could be maintained by certain notions and modes of description, which for example, is the aim of Norberg Schultz. The
second takes as a premise that ifthere is an existential essence
which might be comprehended as common and universal, itis
impossible to maintain it by definite notions and ways of
description. It will, possibly and hopefully, appear in short
glimpses during the act ofinterpreting the local phenomena in
space and time. An example is the way Taoists conceive and
describe the concrete world. The third takes as granted that
there is no such universal, existential essence or at least, it is
futile to search for it

The common existential essence may be considered in two
ways: either through describing it by conceptions offorces outside the human being itselfand by the human attitude to those

forces (God, Nature, and so on), or through describing it by
conceptions of structures within human beings themselves,
like the composition of the human brain and its functional
properties and semiotic structures (Levi Strauss).

To relate the interpretation oflocation and site asa context of
settlement to any kind of universal, existential essence,
described by definite notions, is too ambitious and uncertain,
though not exeluding the possibility that there is a common,

140

existential essence behind, which, more or less arbitrarily, may
be revealed in glimpses. The consequence of such a standpoint
is primarily to search among the local and special properties of
the location to understand a settlement in its context.

Location as a set ofresources

Economy and ecology have been basic factors when people
have chosen their place of living and their way of life: various
forces and incidents have caused people to leave a place. It is,
however, hard to imagine that people should settle somewhere
without having the confidence thatitshould be possible to subsist and to live a dignified life there. History is loaded with
examples ofhow people’s lifestyle adapts to the limitations and
the possibilities of the location of their settlement. The
dynamic interplay between needs and resources indicates the
inadequacy of considering location and site as a set of
resources which undergoes a continual change according to
the altering needs of the people. To understand location and
site within the framework ofresource management, therefore,
seems to be a possible point of departure.

The conception based on this framework should nourish a
holistic understanding of the locational context. The notion
‘resources’ should then be articulated in a wide sense and has
to include more than justthe natural resources. The interpretation of locational context refers to the cultural and functional
use ofa site, resulting in consideration of the following: firstly
the dynamic element of all cultural processes —- namely the
human resources; secondly the physical phenomena, the material resources; and thirdly the cultural resources ofthe location.
Byintroducing such a wide resource conceptas a base, itis possible to understand settlement in its locational context without
falling into either mechanical functionalism or naturalism.

To exemplify this a Romanian case may be chosen. In the
Danube delta people live mainly by fishing, in acomposite pursuit culture. The villages are linear settlements along the water
edge, the houses built mainly oflocal materials by the villagers
themselves. Human resources are the persons involved — with
their knowledge and experience, their physical strength, temper, intelligence and so on. Material resources are, for example,
the climate, the mud and the water, thereeds and the trees, the
village structure and the working tools. The cultural resources
include the local economy, the collective property pattern, the
concept ofstyle and living standard, the tradition ofjoining the
neighbours in giving a hand with the construction ofa house,
and the layout patterns.

Against this background it is possible to distinguish
resources which have been given more importance and weight

jnalfisherman’s

= danube delta,
ia,

than others, thus establishing how people have given priorities
to their architecture, while at the same time maintaining the
holistic perspective. This is the case also when considering settlements under marginal conditions, close to the limits of
mankind’s habitable environment. It is then necessary to view
specific resources, or resource combinations, which occur ina
marginal way, as critical factors, and to set more or less
absolute limits to how people can act vis-A-vis the other
resources ofthe location. An example ofthis may be the precipitation and the slopes of the narrow alpine valleys of central
Europe. On the one hand the abundant precipitation promises
excellent grass production which is what makes the region
attractive and makes pastoral farming here possible. On the
other hand, the heavy snowfall during the winter represents a
constant threatto the settlements, which have to protect themselves against the avalanches in many ways. Here precipitation
combined with topography can be characterized as a critical
factor, as resources occurring in a marginal way and easily legible in the vernacular architecture of the localities.

Creativity and change
Location as the context of settlement should be understood by
articulating the set ofresources ofthe place as well as the man-

Location and Site 1.1112

agement of the resources through the ages. The notion
‘resource’ is usually conceived as anything that may meet
human demands. The occurring phenomena ofa place exist as
neutral until a mental or physical activity takes place there. As
soon as this activity commences to meet needs, interpretation
also starts: what fulfils what purpose, what gives meaning? The
phenomena are then sorted and divided to be parameters,
define action or to be resources, which give possibilities. In
practice, there is a shifting movement between what is considered to be limiting and what is considered to be a potential.
What is seen as a parameter in the first place, may later be
shown to be a resource, and vice versa (Bräten and Hpyem).

The ability to master and make the most of those shifts is a
erucial part ofwhat we call creativity. In other words, by studying the ways people have related to the resources in developing
the settlement according to the potential of the location, it is
possible to understand the creativity ofa people. Very often this
creativity is used to operate from a holistic perspective, even
under margirläl conditions.

Resources vary from time to time, both the manmade ones
and those given by nature. Location has then to be understood
as a stage of continual transformation of resources. Examples
show that although the natural conditions might change for
the worse by, for instance, aggravation ofa harsh climate, it is
possible to maintain, or even improve the living standard and
the lifestyle ofthe location. This is made possible by accumulating wealth through building, cultivating the landscape,
improving transport systems, employing wiser technologies
and so on. A historic example is the settling of the Walser
people in the canton of Valais in Switzerland. During the 8th
century the colonists arrived from the north. Throughout the
Dark Ages the climate was rather mild, making it possible to
reside ata high altitude. The limits ofvegetation were some 100
metres higher than today. When the climate worsened in the
17th century, the vegetation limits and the glaciers were
pressed downwards. The people had, through the generations,
developed production methods, organizing patterns and building types well adapted to the changing conditions. Through
this resource accumulation it was possible to maintain most of
the settlements even after the worsening of the climate. The
high-altitude locations would otherwise have been impossible
to settle under the new, cool climatic situation of the region
(Fibicher).

Wise generations have administered their funds in away that
made it possible for the succeeding generation to have a better
life. Resources have been accumulated in the location, developed further and taken well care ofin such a way that next generation is given a better platform than the previous one. This is
perhaps the mostimportantlesson thatvernacular architecture
has to give the present generation.

Atotal conception

Our conception oflocation and site is a result ofa transformation. There is a transformation from a location in its natural
state to alocation where the culture landscape is featured bythe
resource management of the generations through time
(Tobey).

141

## S. 21

© Harald Kayem

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

In the following entries location and site are described as
geographical types, categorized according to physical properties of terrain such as topography, climate, soil, water and
vegetation. The geographical types, however, imply more than
their physical properties. They also convey supra-physical
associations such as myths, tales, images and fantasies where
landscape and human life unite in an integrated totality. Real or
unreal, present or historic, these associations cannot be
ignored since they form an important part ofour conception of
location and site.

The idea of experieneing and interpreting a location
approaches the notion ofthe ‘spiritofplace’, or‘genius loci’. The
atmosphere is experienced through a filter of cultural background, a background of experience, temper, level of knowledge and so on. To describe the identity of a place in the
framework ofresource management, theresources ofthe place
have to be considered and evaluated — whether they represent
limitations or potentialities, how they have been managed in
the past and how they are handled at present - all from the
perspective ofthe directions that future transformations might
take. The sum of resources, their management through time
and the potential of the location, will be the object of experience and interpretation; this has to be conceived as ‘the spiritof
place’ within the framework sketched above.

The location and site should not be considered as merely
physical occurrences, but rather as a complex totality based on
natural occurrences and human attitudes and actions through
history. HARALD HOYEM

142

1.11.2.a Below-ground

The term ‘below-ground location’ indicates a main
distinction between above-ground and below-ground locations. Above-ground locations are differentiated (coastal,
desertic, forest, etc); below-ground locations are here treated as
one category. Cases of below-ground sites occur in different
geographical contexts: in desert areas, in valleys, in mountain
areas and in plains. Common in all cases is that the ground
itselfserves both as site and building envelope.

The history of troglodytes (derived from Greek trogle, cave,
and duo enter) is as old as the history of man. Natural caves
served as shelter for the ancestor of man; most famous, perhaps, is Peking Man, Homo erectus, who lived in caves 1700 000
years ago. Manmade cave dwellings as old as 3000 Bc are found
in Gezer in Palestine (Kempe). Today a surprisingly large number of people live in below-ground sites: approximately 25 000
in France and 30-40 million in China, for example (Golany).

Caves formed by nature and inhabited by man are usually
rock caves. Limestone is the prevailing site basis for cave formation. Its softness and its chemical composition (CaCO3)
have given erosion processes (both mechanical and chemical
ones) easy access to sculpture the stone masses. However, tufa,
sandstone and even harder materials, such as granite, are presentin rocks where sea water, frosterosion, glaciers and underground rivers have formed caves or grottoes.

Soft rocks are also sculptured by man to become belowground, habitable space, as for example limestone (in
Provence, France, and in Mindanao, in the Philippine islands)
and tufa stone (in Anatolia, Turkey and in the Greek islands).
Loess soil dwellings are the most numerous manmade type,
mainly due to the extent of Chinese below-ground communities. Loess soil is found in several places: in Siberia, the Mississippi valley, southern Israel, southern Tunisia and China. With
its structural uniformity, the loess soil gives ideal conditions
for easy digging and presents possibilities for forming vertical,
stable walls, vault formations and so on. Loess consists of
accumulated layers of fine particles. Its mineral contents and

the stability ofthe soil are due to the source ofthe particles and
to the age ofthe layers in the location.

Below-ground dwellings in loess soil are found in hillside
locations in canyons (cliff cave dwelling type), as well as in
plains area location (pit cave dwelling type). In plains the sites
are elaborated by digging a courtyard pit. The caves face this
courtyard and are dug in the mass surrounding the pit. There
are several hybrid types of loess soil courtyards: partly pit
cave-partly cliff cave; partly cave-partly building. Access systems are developed both above the ground and in tunnels.

The availability ofresources which is fundamental for people
to make a living, may differ from case to case in below-ground
locations. The ability to settle underground may release land
for other purposes, as seen in Chinese examples where village
activities are removed to caves in hillside locations, thus releasing valuable farmland. From the perspective of resource management, however, the qualities of the ground are of greatest
interest, mainly in terms of its use, site medium and building
composite. Important considerations are the structure of the
soil or rock and their suitability to function as construction

Settlement in va,

Switzerland, shoyjr,
the ground floor
pasture used in gay,
spring and lateayıj,

floor’ usedas Summe
pasture, %

seeaso
1.1.10 Ecological
4.11.2 Economy

References
Bräten, K.H., and
H., 1991
