stability, are they abundant, and are the building costs low?),
and the climatic conditions below ground (e.g. warm in winter
and cool in summer, and are fluctuations of day-to-night temperatures reduced?). The special climatic conditions of underground space are suitable for different activities of the
domestic sphere including the daily activities of the daily life
ofthe family, all kinds of storing (even food storing), growing
plants (such as mushroom and rhubarb) and housing animals.
HARALD HOYEM
1.11.2.b Coastal
The zone on both sides ofa shorelineis the coast. The
shoreline is the rather exact border between land and sea. It is
shaped by tectonics, by the erosion caused by waves and winds,
by the sedimentation performed by the sea, the winds and the
rivers, and by the eustatic changes. To define the landside coastal
zone, it is necessary to take into account both the local climate —
caused by the meeting between seaand land-and the perception
of the sea. It is reasonable to limit the category to settlements
which directly relate to activities atsea.

Access to resources on both land and sea is a common
feature of coasts, and the passage between sea and land is the
prevailing, physical property of ‘coast’ as a location type. As
distinct from lacustrine locations, coastal locations relate to
salt or brackish water. Differences between various coastal
locations are numerous. The topography has variations- rocks
plunging into the sea, deep beach planes, fjords, lagoons, delta
landscapes, sea bottom orography and the formation of skerries. Climatic differences and specific movements ofthe water
(waves and tides), the flora and fauna resources at sea, as well
as soils and vegetation on land, are decisive factors enabling
settlements and the development ofthe local vernacular architecture.

Changes in sea level are caused by variations in the water
bulk ofthe oceans when huge volumes of ice drastically alter
their size. The land itself will rise or fall for similar or other
reasons. The result is long-term displacement of the coastal
line, frequently over long distances (Press and Siever). Such

Location and Site 1.1.2

displacements have serious consequences for settlements
close to the coast, whether they are located below or above sea
level.

Short-term displacements must also be mentioned as a
factor especially affecting the location of settlements: waves,
tides, floods, mud sedimentation, volcanic activity, coral formation and winds can all change a shoreline in a short time.
Even resistant species of rocks may be eroded over time by the
inexorable forces of the waves. Sandbanks are removed by
erosion, the undermining by the waves and particle transport
by the winds. Rivers transport small particles to the ocean,
form new land areas and change their course. Consciousness
of such changes leads either to locating the settlement so far
from the sea that it never will be affected by the movements of
the shoreline, or to resisting the changes by physical defensive
measures such as planting, dykes and barriers.

The protected harbour is essential for life along the coast.
The protection may be in the form of surrounding islands or
skerries. Or itmay occur by situating the harbour in a lagoon or
fjord, or upstream of an abundant river which meets the coast
with a delta or estuary. Many communities are situated at the
point where river and harbour meet. Smaller, coastal settlements as well profit from the combination ofariverwitha good
harbour, where the brook provides the necessary fresh water
supply. The harbour is primarily a place where fishing vessels
can stay protected, landing what is brought from the sea. It
involves activities like transport services, collecting of customs
duty, trade, accommodation of travellers and refining raw
materials from the sea. On this basis the cities, towns and villages establish their culture and their architectural responses
to the needs connected to residing and working there. Individual habitats also need a protected place for boats, both when
the work at sea is the only occupation, and when it is part ofa
composite pursuit culture.

A mixed economy culture is common in coastal settlements
because resources on land and at sea are exploitable from one
and the same location (Heathcote). Generally it can be maintained that along coasts different building types are used
specifically for different purposes, reflecting access to various
resources and the compound nature ofhuman occupation. The

various activities need their places, constructions and buildings, usually based on the local vernacular architecture. However, direct communication with other regions and other
countries by sea has opened coastal locations to the influence

## S. 22

1.11 ENVIRONMENT

of other cultures. These may be influences of style, the importing of technology and new building materials, or even the
transportation ofwhole houses by sea.

The coast gives associations of exposure and contrasts: on
one hand the ruthless dynamics of natural elements, on the
other hand the meeting of water and earth as eternal elements.
There is a feeling oftension between stable settlements rooted
on land and the restless exploration ofthe world and the harvesting ofthe resource out there. HARALD HOYEM
1.1l1.2.c Desertic
Deserts are large tracts of land where rainfall is
infrequent, or in some instances, as in the Atacama desert of
northern Chile, virtually unknown. The Atacama is a coastal
desert on the western limits ofthe continent; such deserts are
subject to cold offshore currents and, for their latitude, may
have moderate summer temperatures.

A desert like the African Sahara is prone to hot trade winds,
with areas ofhigh atmospheric pressure and low rainfall. Many
deserts are arid with insufficient water in any form to support
vegetation or agriculture; others have poor sandy soils but
enough moisture to support desert scrub vegetation. Another
type, such as the Gobi desert, is to be found in continental interiors with high temperatures in summer but low in winter.
Technically, the polar regions ofthe Arctic and the Antarctic are
also deserts.

Some desertic conditions may be ameliorated by oases,
where there is sufficientwaterin an area to permitthe raising of
plants, such as date palms. Such oases occur where there is an
artesian basin, where an aquifer, or stratum of permeable rock
which can hold water, lies above a layer ofimpermeable rock.
The upper surface ofan aquifer, or water-table, may be below
the desert floor, necessitating the construction ofdeep wells to
maintain settlements and raise crops.

The location and site of desert settlements respond to the
extreme conditions ofclimate, soiland availability ofwater. The
need for water and shade, which in less extreme conditions of
water and vegetation are only two factors out of many in settlement location, are primary in the desert; butalso importantare
questions ofsecurity and the territoriality oftribes and clans. As
in other places, the area of land that is necessary to sustain a
human group and/or its herd ofanimals helps to determine the
spacing of settlements. In deserts that area may be so large that
not only is population density extremely low, but also it turns
outto be impossible for a group to have a fixed location for settlement. Nomadism (in which people move on a continuous

basis from place to place within a certain territory) and transhumance (in which people move back and forth between two
areas, sometimes upland and lowland, on a seasonal basis) are
both ways of life that influence desertic settlement location.
Furthermore, site location may also be connected to the way in
which social groups disperse and congregate: the Paiute Indians of North America, for example, whose traditional habitat
was the semi-desertto the east ofthe Sierra Nevada, would congregate together in bands ofabouta hundred people during the
winter, and break up into individual family groups, during the
summer, ranging over all the territory ofthe band.

144

Nomadism and transhumance mean that more than with
most other settlement types, desertic settlements are temporal
in nature in thattheir permanence is variable, and their location
often cyclical. Some Bedouin traditionally moved in a rough
circle every year around the northern part ofthe Arabian peninsula, stopping every summer at oasis settlements not far from
the sea. Some settlements may be permanent, as at oases or at
the desert edge (where the desert meets the sea or land that is
more fertile); others may be occupied on an intermittent but
regular basis, such as places where there is a source of water, a
few trees for shade butlittle other vegetation, or vegetation that
occurs only after infrequent rain; others may be occupied only
irregularly and unpredictably, such as the many places where
groups of people may spend only a single night. Locations are
therefore dependent not only on the long-term influences of
climate and geography, but equally on short-term changes in
vegetation patterns and water supply — and the response to
such short-term changes may be extremely subtle, depending
on acute perceptions and the ability to respond almost immediately to such perceptions in decisions about the movement
from one settlement location to the next.

The location of permanent settlements in deserts is
influenced by trade and travel routes that are used on a regular
basis, and the consequent need to control those routes and provide known places for people to stay en route. In places where
Muslims passed through on the pilgrimage to Mecca, frequent
and regular rest stops were also necessary. These needs helped
determine the location of caravanserais and other fortified
structures, sometimes with minarets helping to act as markers
for travellers in the desert. The places where bridges were necessary to cross rivers or where trade routes crossed each other

(particularly when this crossing happened ata source of water)

- were also places where settlement would naturally occur.

HOWARD DAVIS
1.11.2.d Forest
In the mid-ızth century the English word ‘forest’
was applicable to any wild area of land, whether or not it had
tree cover. This reflected a tradition of medieval origin, where
certain lands, generally wooded and managed by “foresters’,

See also
1.11.1.d Maritime
2.1.5 Melanesja an.
Micronesia
2.1.8-A Polynesiaa,
2.1V.6 Insular Medien
ranean 2

References
Carter, George, 1,
Heathcote, RL,

See also
1.111.1.c Desert

References
Forde, C.D., 1934
Golany, Gideon,
1982a, 19825.
Nir, Dov, 1974
Thesiger, Wilfred,

Bedouin tent neard
Saudi Arabia.

‚dhouse with
earing, Puerta
costa Rica.

En
Stopical/Tropical

1960

minas, George,

Donald, F., 1939
1,1989

were reserved for the hunting of boar, deer and other game by

the King and his retinue. Here, however, ‘forest’ is used as.an

encompassing term to include all kinds of extensive tracts of
land under tree cover, whether coniferous or. deciduous, temperate or tropical, woodland or jungle.

Forest locations are dependent on rainfall and water supply,
both for the sustaining of human communities, and for the
maintenance of the forest flora and fauna on which they rely.
These in turn relate to the prevailingssoil types and to theunderlying topography, whether it be a flat terrain, a river basin, ora
mountain range. Areas of high rainfall can support relatively
high densities, with little need to domesticate species of plants
oranimals, owing to the range of available sources offood and
the abundant materials for building construction. Areas with
less forest cover and variable levels of water supply necessitate
dispersed settlement, with domesticated food sources and
sometimes mud or stone used with timber in building. Muchof
Europe was once covered in forest which was cleared for cultivation; a region such as the Belgian Ardennes still bears the
irregular shapes ofcleared and forested lands.

Broadly, forest peoples may be classified as hunter-gatherers
and foragers, or as cultivators. Using the dynamic balance
maintained between a population and therelativeabundance of
resources asa base, hunter-gatherers may be nomadic, like the
Hadza of Tanzania who follow a seasonal cycle of available
foods, ortthey may have developed permanent settlements from
which they forage, as have many other African peoples.

While the distinction depends on access to food and water
resources italso has bearing on settlement forms and building
construction methods. Some peoples, such as the Punan of
Borneo, may build light shelters which offer temporary protection and which are easily and swiftly dismantled; others, like
the Mboti pygmies, may construct woven domes of saplings
and thin branches which may be thatched with leaves to Produce a shelter capable of lasting for some months. In Siberia
the extensive forests support nomadic hunters who move to
more exposed sites on rivers for summer fishing. Other,
Eurasian nomads breed reindeer and seasonally move between
forestand tundra.

Cultivators of the forest can be broadly divided between
those who directly cultivate indigenous plants, and those who
eultivate more domesticated species. Some tropical rain forest
peoples harvestyams, coconuts, plantains and bananas. Some,
like the Asante who live on lateritic soils, are careful to protect
the forest floor from direct sunlight. Other peoples use swidden, or ‘slash and burn’ cultivation systems, clearing areas of
forest within their domain and burning off the accumulated
brush, releasing nutrients into the soil in the process. This
practice is common in Central American rain forests and as far
eastas the Southeast Asian highlands. Vietnamese highlanders
speak of ‘eating the forest’ as land is cleared for swidden cultivation; after two or three years of cropping the site is left to
recover for some two decades.

Forest-dwelling peoples often have a highly developed
understanding of animals, plants and products, identifying
scores, even hundreds ofspecies and exploiting their medicinal
and other properties. In the vastness of the forests the seden-

Location and Site

1.1.2

tary cultures frequently live in close association, often in community or village houses which accommodate several families,
as is the case with Amazonian malocas and Borneo longhouses.
Many cultures are animist, who fear the spirits ofthe forest and
the blowpipes or traps of other cultures, contributing to their
isolation. While most cultures have been ‘contacted’ (.e. by
western explorers and missionaries) and many documented by
anthropologists, the critical rate of loss oftheir habitat to beef
Production and mining interests is threatening their survival.
Industrial demands and global markets are causing serious
destruction of forest resources, while indigenous peoples are
losing their traditional ways oflife, including their settlements.

DONNA LUCKEY and PAUL OLIVER

1.11.2.e Grassland

Approximately a quarter ofthe world’s land surface
is mantled in grass. Grasses constitute the kind of vegetation
on which mankind depends most for food, particularly in the
form ofcereals, such as wheat, andrice and corn. They are also
a major constituent ofanimal feed, sustaining flocks ofsheep,
herds of cattle and a range of other domesticated animals
including horses, yaks, llamas and goats, raised for meat, dairy
products, hides and hair, or for exploitation of their muscle
power and as a means oftransportation.

In excess of 4500 species of grass are known, ranging from
European meadow grasses (pasto tierno) to the tall bunch grass
ofthe Argentinian pampas (pasto duro). Bamboos and canes are
grasses which may grow to over 18 m (60 ft) in height, some
having stems over 30 cm (1 ft) in diameter.

Capable of being sown by man in a wide variety of climatic
and environmental conditions, grasses may have been cultivated for food as grain or rice in the Nile valley and the “fertile
erescent’ (from the Levanttothealluvial plain ofMesopotamia)
10000 years ago. By 3000 BC cultivated grasses were being
grown for cereal crops in the Indus valley, and later they were
raised in Meso-America and the central Andes. The cultivation
of grasses for food and the rise of agricultural societies were
significant in the domestication of plants, but natural grasslands of great variety still covered much ofEurope, Asiaand the
Americas in the ıgth century. The grasslands range from the
puszta of the Hungarian Danubian plain to the undulating veld
ofthe South African plateau.

There is considerable evidence to suggest that many of the
former grasslands were created by mankind. Burning off the

145

a

ZBsL JanloInedo

## S. 23

TRoEEE U 2

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

forest cover to encourage subsequent replacement by grasses
may have been extensively practised. Improving pastures by
burning to replace nutrients and stimulate new growth led to
swidden or fallow farming, still widely employed today in the
savanna grasslands of Africa. A narrow strip of steppe lies
south ofthe Sahara extending from Mauritania to the Horn of
Africa. South ofthis are the open savannas some 650 km (400
mi) wide, with grasslands, scrub and woodlands dotted with
the dispersed settlements and compounds ofliterally hundreds
oftribal groups. Savanna grasslands extend across West Africa
and the Nigerian plateau to join the regions of the upper Nile
and are still extensively used according to traditional patterns
of cultivation and, in areas free ofthe tsetse fly, for raising livestock. Dipping south in the Rift Valley they cover much ofthe
southern halfof the continent culminating in the varied grasslands oftthe veld. Though the African savannas are partly open
woodland where grass grows high among scattered trees in a
parkland setting, they hosta great variety ofgrazinganimals. In
much ofeastern and southern Africa both pastoral and agricultural or mixed economies exist, with the herdsman’s cattle
enclosures (kraals) and the agriculturist’s villages among tilled
fields contributing to the essential landscape.

While considerable expanses of grasslands still exist, colonial expansion in the ıgth century and global population
growth in the 2oth have seen immense areas under the plough
or developed for stock raising. Some grasslands are of vast
scale, such as the black earth or chernozem belt some 320 km
(200 mi) wide that stretches north and east from the Carpathians to the Tien-Shan mountains on the western edge ofChina.
Here the soils are enriched by thousands of'years of grass crop
decay and new growth, watered by adequate rains of up to 50
cm (20 in) a year. South of the belt lies the steppe, no less
immense, but its short grass, under less rainfall, is of lower
fertility. Treeless and exposed, the true steppe is thinly populated, but since the ıgth century the chernozem belt has been
intensively farmed, though interrupted with desertic regions,
such as the Caspian Depression. Comparable are the grassy
plains of North America which extend west from the 98th parallel to the foothills ofthe Rockies. Named by the French as the
prairie, a term for meadow, this is a region of coarse, tufted
grass which was the domain of the American bison until the
latter part of the ıgth century. Ranching and the planting of
wheat and cultivated grasses changed its ecology and established a pattern of dispersed settlements served by railtoads,
and more recently, by highways.

A similar change of use altered the great Argentinian plain,
termed the pampa by Spanish settlers. The extensive temperate
grasslands on loess and alluvial sedimentary deposits were
turned over to cattle ranching or ploughed by European settlers
and replanted with wheat and alfalfa. Between the Amazon
basin and the pampas lie the savannas and selvas ofBrazil, with
grasslands giving way to tropical scrub and woodlands.

Consequently, in the present century the grasslands have
undergone great changes while still retaining their name. Such
changes in turn have affected the indigenous peoples, largely
nomadic hunters, who once thinly populated the grassland
regions. Marginalized today, in the Americas especially, many

146

have been replaced by colonists or have been absorbed into
modern agricultural systems. Grasslands exert limitations on
built structures. Hunters often use temporary shelters, either
made of skins stretched over poles which have been obtained
from woodland areas, or constructed of grass. In some cases,
notably in Southern Africa, grass has been used to construct
domes. Dwellings are principally built from the lateritic earths
that underlie the savannas of much of Africa, the tall ‘elephant
grass’ being used to thatch roofs. Nomadic herders still people
the grasslands of inner Asia, covering their lattice structures
with felts made from the wool oftheir flocks. More established
settlements are built from the compacted soils, but whatever
the means of livelihood and of building construction, these
solutions to housing in the grassland steppes, prairies and
savannas are evidence of mankind’s ingenuity and tenacious
hold on life. PAUL OLIVER
1.11.2f Lacustrine

Lake-dwelling, or lacustrine, peoples build their
settlements either atthe edge oflakes or over thelake, orliveon
manmade islands on the lake itself.

Reasons for siting dwellings and settlements in the vicinity
of bodies of water are access to water, climatic advantages,
exploitation ofnatural resources for a livelihood, facilitation of
transport between settlements, and lastly, for defence and
protection. Some of the oldest settlements found are located
near bodies of water. The post ice-age mesolithic settlement of
Star Carr in the northeast of England, dating back to 8000 BC,
consisted of a settlement of tent-like huts on a lake shore.
Archaeological finds atthe shores ofLake Onega, in the former
USSR, prove the presence of settlements ofhunters and fishers
since 1000-2000 Bc. Remains of wooden poles and artefacts
have been found atthe shores of many Swiss lakes, but itis not
yet clear ifthe wooden huts over water were used as dwellings
or for storage. Villages on lake shores, however, were quite
common, such as Lucerne and Zurich.

As in Canada, large forest areas and numerous lakes and
rivers in the northern regions of the former USSR influenced
the means of livelihood and settlement form (linear villages
with buildings). In Finland with over 55 000 lakes, three major

Prairie grasslanı

Idaho, United State
Treesareplanted
around farmhousefor
protection.

3.V.3-A Argentina

3.V1.5-A Prairie Plalns

3.VIl.9 Savanna Gras
lands

References
Brittain, Robert, 1958
Broek, Jan O.M., and
Webb, John W,
Clapham Ir, W.
Demont, Ren, 196
Hyams, Edward, 19

lake areas are interconnected and were used to transport
wooden logs felled in remote forest areas. The importance of
waterways for trade and communication led to the consolidation of fishing villages into cities with harbours, for example
Birka, on the islands of Björko on Lake Mälar in Sweden which
became the major trade centre ofthe Vikings in the gth century.
The presence of water in the desert in the form of oases was
eritical in the planning oftrade routes (e.g. in North Africa and
the Middle East). Natural water-sources are often extended by
manmade water supply systems, for example by tunnels tap-
Ping mountain streams and artesian wells. Oases are characterized by intensive and differentiated use oflimited irrigated land
for growingvegetables, grains, oil, dates and fruittrees. Houses
use locally available materials - palm frond, thatch and mud.
Many, butnotall the lake-dwelling peoples are dependenton
fishing for their livelihood. The Urus, living on manmade reed
islands in Lake Titicaca in Peru, are fisherfolk but also hunt
birds and sell reed products. The Turkana, a semi-nomadic
tribe living by the shores of Lake Turkana, have cattle herds and
were until now only marginally engaged in fishing. The
Kanembu and Buduma, living on the shores of Lake Chad in
Niger, are involved in farming, fishing and cattle-rearing. Lake
Chad, a sweet-water lake, has natrium and soda deposits in its
eastern part (in Borkou and Baga-Sola in Chad) and salt is produced here.
The lake-dwelling communities take advantage of the locational peculiarities and resources to evolve special methods of
income generation. In shallow waters artificial islands (e.g.
‘Chinampas’ in Mexico and those in Dal lake, India) made of
reeds and mud are fastened with poles to the lake bed and used
for growing vegetables. Floating vegetation which remains
after a seasonal flood, for instance in lagoons, is used as a fertilizer and animal fodder. Lake Tonle Sap in western Cambodia
is connected to the Mekong river and, with its annual flooding,
expands from 3000 sq km (1150 sq mi) to 20000sq km (7800sq
mi) and in depth from 3 m (to ft) to 14 m (46 fi). Alluvial
deposits make the lake shores a major rice growing area. The
Buduma rear a special breed of cattle which is well adapted to
its environmentand can swim to small islands in Lake Chad for
grazing.

Location and Site 1.1.2

Topographical as well as manmade changes can affect the
development of the lake-dwelling communities. The seminomadic lifestyle oftheLuo, who originally lived in the swamps
in Sudan, was dictated by the seasonal overflooding ofthe Nile,
where new fertile grazing and settlement sites were easily
found. The Luo live today on the shores of Lake Victoria in
Kenya and have to deal with the pressures of high population
density and limited land, which is reflected in their settlement
form.

In instances where the settlements are built on stilts over
water it can be speculated that they were originally located
along the shore and only seasonally cut off from land. Settlements which are built in the lake itself are either located on
natural or manmade (sometimes floating) islands or constructed on stilts. This settlement form offers the advantages of
protection against enemies. The El Molo of Kenya are said to
have lived until the last century on islands in Lake Turkana out
offear ofattack from neighbouring tribes. They survived solely
on fish and by drinking the alkaline lake water which has been
part-responsible for bone deformities. The Urus, who called
themselves ‘Kot-suns’, meaning ‘lake-people’, took advantage
ofthe thickly reeded lake area and changed the location oftheir
swimming homes to avoid paying the taxes that the Incas
demanded from them. Siting and climatic advantages of lake
islands were capitalized by royalty for their pleasure gardens
and summer villas (for example Udaipur, India, and Lake Mag-

giore, Italy).

Materials that are locally available atlittle or no costare used
for building the houses and artefacts such as boats, fishing gear
and storage containers. The settlements ofthe Urus in Peruand
the Ma’dan tribe in the swamps between the Euphrates and
Tigris in Iraq are excellent examples of the use of indigenous
material and technology. Their canoes, made ofbundled reeds
and grass, are extremely manoeuyrable and can withstand
heavy storms. Mud, reeds and grass growing in the water or on
the shores are used to create floating islands on which reed huts
are erected. RITA MROTZEK-SAMPAT
Lowland
By far the majority ofthe world’s population resides
in the lowland areas which, though imprecisely defined, are
frequently accepted as Iying at less than 300 m (1000 ft) above
sea level. These regions below the hills and foothills of the
uplands are generally but by no means always in the margins
between them and the sea. Though lowland regions take many
forms including the depressions ofthe Dead Sea or the Mohave
desert perhaps the most important are the alluvial plains made
fertile by the deposits of great rivers flowing to deltas, like the
Nile, the Ganges, the Amazon or the Mississippi. Here
emerged hydraulic agricultural societies who developed and
maintained systems ofirrigation, flood control and drainage of
agricultural lands. Some societies dependent upon hydraulic
agticulture also evolved religious systems which related the
productivity of the land to the divine nature of the river that
made it possible.

In such lowland regions settled communities ofagriculturalists are to be found, in some cases supporting plantation

1.1.2.8

147

## S. 24

1. ENVIRONMENT

economies, or exploiting the wetlands for the cultivation of
rice. In other cases refined irrigation systems have been devised
and are controlled by social organizations such as the Balinese
Subak.

Certain lowlands, such as the peat bogs of western Europe,
or the Anglian fens in England needed to be drained. In Anglia
as in the Netherlands, windmills were used for this purpose. In
Holland land was reclaimed from the ocean by the making of
the polders through drainage and the construction ofdykes.

Excessive melting of upland snows may lead to the inundation ofhundreds of square miles in lowland regions, as occurs
frequently in Bangladesh or the Punjab. Levees and bunds may
be builtas defensive barriers against the spread offloodwaters.
In lowland regions dwellings of reeds or compacted earth are
common, for the resources are readily available and building
skills widely shared. But they are both prone to collapse in a
flood.

Like the uplands, perception ofthe lowlands is relative as the
flatterrain is seen againsta backdrop ofhillsand mountains. In
some instances, such as the grass prairies of North America,
the great plains of the Russian steppes, or the flat pampas of
Argentine the landscape was virtually unrelieved. Even though
the western third ofthe American continents is upland including the Rockies and the Andes, which are among the longest
and highest mountain ranges of the world, much is lowland.
Though interrupted by the Appalachians, lowland North America extends from the east coast, across the Mississippi to east
Texas, while the Amazonian lowlands extend to the selvas and
the Gran Chaco. The western savanna of West Africa, the desertic plains of Australia and part of the North China plain, are
below 300 m (1000 ft). But so too is much ofthe Siberian plain
anda broad swathe around Hudson Bay in the Canadian Arctic
and sub-Arctic regions. Spanning every climatic and vegetal
zone, there is little that is in common among the lowlands,
which cover a quarter ofthe world’s landmasses, except their
lack ofaltitude.

When the deserts, rain forests and arctic regions are subtracted, a much smaller proportion of habitable lowlands
remains. The highest concentrations of population follow the
major rivers of northern Europe, the Ganges, the Yangtze and
the Nile Valley, or are distributed, principally in the cities, on
the coastal plains. Hundreds oftowns and many ofthe world’s
great cities- London, New York, Cairo, Calcutta, Buenos Aires
among them - are sited close to where rivers discharge into the
oceans; they are entrepöts between the inland trade by road
and river, and external trade by sea. The appropriateness for
settlement ofthe lowlands in the temperate regions lies largely
in the fact that they were well watered, frequently with the
benefit of silts and alluvial deposits enriched by the washing
down ofsoils from hills.

In flator undulating terrain, tillage by hoe or digging stick is
less arduous, ploughs can be drawn and larger cultivated areas
made more possible. The clearing of the temperate forests of
northern Europe opened up lands for agriculture but reduced
grazing for animals. Shifting cultivation followed, as trees and
scrub were burned, the land was worked and then left as its fertility was lowered. It is possible that crop rotation farming

148

developed as untilled fallow land was used for the grazing of
domesticated animals. In turn this may have led to the use of
natural fertilizer.

Access between dwelling and fields is more efficient in flatter
lands; the ratio of distance to time spent reaching the fields is
more favourable, permitting the effective cultivation of larger
areas. Communications between farms and hamlets, between
peasant communities and the towns, and between towns and
trade routes are all facilitated by a greater choice of means of
transport — by river, by coast and by road networks that are
more easily negotiated on horseback or wagons drawn by
draught animals. The lowland clays and soils frequently make
good building material. Grasses and reeds provide thatch for
roofs and may be woven into mats for walls, while timber from
the forest edge or stone from river-beds or talus is more readily
transported. Without the restrictions of narrow sites or the
limitations exerted by steep inclination or dense undergrowth
the vernacular architecture ofthe lowlands often gains in flexibility and space. But the very benefits gained from lowland
settlementaare those that have made them vulnerable to floods,
fire and land-hungry invaders. PAUL OLIVER
Marine
Many traditional modes of existence are associated
with water. A variety ofactivities, most commonly fishing, provide subsistence for entire communities, often in association
with subsidiary tasks such as trade. Dependence on the water
leads to the creation of essential artefacts — boats, sails, nets,
lines, traps — which possess great importance in the lives of
those who use them. In many societies this has led to these
objects being endowed with great symbolic power, even shaping into similar forms other objects not associated with
maritime activities. Several cultures have developed boat-like
forms for their buildings, possibly for symbolic reasons (e.g.
the Batak of Sumatra, Indonesia) or perhaps as adaptations of
the skills of the boat maker in the manufacture of buildings
(e.g. the Toraja people of Sulawesi [Celebes]).

Some groups, however, have completely denied the need for
buildings based on land. The Orang Laut of peninsular
Malaysia have existed for centuries as fishermen and traders,

1.11.2.h

walls with
‚dharbour
‚coverack,

Lowland farms near |
Vadstena, south
Sweden. |

2.11.7-A Lowlands |
3.V1.6-A South: Low)

References
Hudson, F:S., 1970
Perpillou, Aim& Vincent
1966
Prothro, R. Mansell, |
Thomas, Arthur, 1967 7°
"alongside the

Nielaka, Malay
I,

|, British Isles.

often sailing great distances, but basing their home villages in
marginal coastal sites. The most sophisticated water-based
structure built by these people is an intricate tidal fishing trap
(kelong), built seasonally from March to June, often far from the
coast. The dwellings, eating houses, shops and communal
facilities of the village on Pulau Sialu off the west coast of
Johore are gathered together, with streets, pathways and
bridges all elevated on poles. A small group of Orang Laut,
called the Orang Selitar, live in even more intimate contactwith
the sea, passing their entire lives in their boats which they use
for catching crabs and shellfish. Though they never leave the
coastal waters ofJohore, these people generally have no permanent base, changing location from day to day. Though their
existence is harsh (the living space of father, mother and
children consists of a wind shelter on a small, permanently
afloat boat), this minority group prize their freedom.

In China the rivers are traditional transport routes. Timber
from the Guizhou region (eastern China) is floated down the
Red Yuan river in great rafts 20 m (65 ft) wide and up to ı5o m
(490 ft) long. The raft is not only a means of transport for the
wood but also for its crew, the lumbermen, their families,
passengers and additional cargo. The inhabitants of this temporary village (the journey takes five months) live in wooden
buildings built on an improvised deck; livestock and a submerged cage for live fish form their provisions.

Ithas been estimated that up to 500 000 people lived in sampans on the Pearl (Zhu Jiang) river atGuangzhou before the cul-

Location and Site 1.1.2
tural revolution, a community that amounted to a water-borne
city environment. Though most ofthese people have now been
resettled on land there are still many thousands of Chinese
people who live permanently on boats. Hong Kong has the
largest concentration of permanent junk dwellers, about
30000, primarily Tanka, people who traditionally exist from
fishing. The teak boats are up to 25 m (80 ft) in length, though
many are much smaller, often housing an extended family of
three generations. They are moored side by side with a gap of5
m (16 ft) between the bow of one and the stern of another, to
allow access and the passage of floating shops serving food
and providing services that even include schools and medical
treatment. ROBERT KRONENBURG
1.1.2.1 Riparian

Riparian settlements are those in which agriculture,
economic life and social life are so dependent on rivers,
streams or canals that settlementlocation and sitingare closely
tied to riverine geography. Religious, sacred and symbolic
aspects of rivers, as well as their use for transportation, as a
source of water for drinking, washing, irrigating crops, and for
fishing have all influenced the location and siting of settlements. Specific factors may include the location of the most
fertile land relative to the river banks, the productivity of
thatland, the ability to fish as well as to farm, the navigability of
the river channel, the cyclical rise and fall of the level of the
water, and the protection of potential fish catches - which
sometimes causes houses to be built on piles directly over the
water itself. Rivers sometimes lend themselves to linear development along them, in response to the need for each house
or farmstead to have a similar relationship to both fertile land
and the river.

In the wet-rice growing subsistence cultures of southeast
Asia, the lowlands of the river banks are the most valuable for
agriculture, and settlement has happened in a way that puts
houses adjacent to this land. In the Malay Peninsula, for example, traditional river settlement occurs in long thin strips, parallel to the river, with houses built just at the point where the
relatively horizontal rice-growing river land slopes upward to
the rubber-growing hillside. To preserve as much rice paddy
and rubber-producing trees as possible, settlement will tend
not to be more than two houses deep, perpendicular to the
river’s course. The density of settlement increases as it
approaches the mouth of the river, and the richest soil that is
usually located there.

In Zhejiang province, and in other parts of China, feng-shui
principles indicated that flowing water was beneficial to settlement location; the northern banks of streams were particularly
favoured, so that houses could take advantage of a southern
exposure and be protected from cold winds in the winter.
In some cases transportation to other villages was only by
water.

Among the Tukanoan cultures of Colombia, factors relating
to gender influence the location of communal longhouses and
the manioc gardens that surround them. These settlements
are widely dispersed among river locations near the junctions
between secondary streams and the main river. In such cases,

149

## S. 25

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

the main stream will be used by the men as a port, source
of water and place to bathe, and the secondary stream by
the women as their source of water and bathing place. This
distinction between a major and a minor river affects the use of
the longhouse itself, where the end towards the men’s port is
used by the men, and that toward the women’s port by the
women.

Along the rivers of northern New Mexico, for example the
Chama and the Rio Grande, where agriculture is dependenton
river water for irrigation, settlement typically happens close
to the river; the river water is distributed through a complex
system of canals and irrigation ditches known as acequias. In
many of these cases the fields are located between the acequia,
which is higher than the river, and the river itself, with houses
above the fields. Individual farms are dispersed, but the common need to maintain the acequias free of plant growth and
debris has led to a traditional annual cleaning in which crews
of workers, taken from the various farms that are served by a
particular acequia, work co-operatively to clear it each spring.

HOWARD DAVIS
1.112] Slope
There are several reasons why hill and mountain
slope areas have been used as sites for settlement. Construction, transportation and agriculture are more efficient on flat
terrain; however, nucleated settlements have been established
on sloped sites because of advantages that offset the difficulties
ofbuilding on and inhabiting inclined land. These include ease
of.defence, disease avoidance, safety from floods, preservation
of flatter sites for agriculture, proximity to pastures, ease of
waste disposal onto lower lands and land availability for disenfranchised groups.

During centuries of political instability defence was especially crucial in establishing new settlements on slope sites.
From the ıoth to the ı4th centuries new settlements were
founded from the Iberian peninsula, along the entire Mediterranean basin, to the foothills ofthe Himalayas, as populations
soughtrefuge on higher elevations from the Islamic expansion.
Hill towns and monasteries in Spain, Italy, Greece, and Nepal
were built perched on steep slopes by Christian or Buddhist
populations who resisted conversion to the newer religion.

Malaria depopulated many flat areas along the Mediterranean where stagnant waters became breeding grounds for
mosquitoes (Carver). The banks ofthe Black Volta are left uninhabited because of sleeping sickness and river blindness
(Prussin). Sloping terrain provides good drainage and thus
protection from diseases that are carried by water-borne
insects.

Foothills also provide protection from floods. Along the
flood plains of the River Nile settlements are located on
low hills which provide refuge from the annual deluge that
entiches the soil for agriculture. Slopes in Asia and Europe,
from the Philippines to Nepal and Austria, have been terraced
into strips offlat plots protecting soil from erosion and making
eultivation possible. In an effortto preserve scarce flat areas for
eultivation which is valuable for agriculture, agricultural societies in several alpine villages and in China builttheir houses on

150

the incline. Sloping terrain is also used for seasonal settlements by pastoralists who climb to high elevations to exploit
summer pasture lands in the Balkans.

The disenfranchised poor have established shanty towns on
land in the vieinity of large megalopolises on sloped areas left
undeveloped because ofthe difficulties oflarge-scale construction. Since the 1980s shanty towns on slopes have housed tens
of millions around most of the big cities of the less affluent
countries such as Egypt, Mexico, Turkey and Peru.

Locating buildings on sloping sites requires preparation.
Gentlyrising earth is easily flattened by cutting into the ground,
using fill to level the site, or bya combination of cutand fill. An
alternative strategy is placing the buildings on stilts and leaving
the land underneath undisturbed. Elevating the building on
poles keeps humidity, vermin and snakes away, and when the
slope is not too steep provides an area protected from sun or
rain for work or storage. Rocky, precipitous sites require
construction of battered walls tilting inward for support.
Unwilling or unable to flatten all rocky site features, builders
often fit houses among rocks or incorporate rocks into walls
(Carver).

Level streets for pedestrian and animal traffic run parallel
with the hillside forming passages shaped by the convex or
concave contours. Every house directly faces the street, generating a sense of enclosed exterior space. Ramps or stepped
paths of varying slope are sculpted into the topography, frequently passing under and through buildings and meeting in
spatially complex intersections. It is hard to distinguish where
the public and the private domains are demarcated along stairs
linking stepped streets with house entrances. Streets are
shaped to accommodate drainage.

Entering buildings on sloping sites often takes place at
different levels from the downhill and uphill directions, which
frequently results in split-level, dynamic interior spaces. Exterior spaces also become dynamic as they are sculpted into
terraces, as seen in the cascading courtyards of adobe compound houses on the hills ofCameroon.

Frequently, a roof terrace provides exterior space for activities associated with courtyards which are limited by the uneven
terrain. In many settlements, for example around the Mediter-

ziallanı Peninsula

ass
orman, 1985

Zn Enrico, 1978

 labelle, 1969

2.1.4 China, South

References

Hugh-Jones, C., 1979

Kuchiba, M., Tsoubou |

Wilson, C. and ‚Kammer,
D., 1989

eedge ofthe
'Törbel, Valais,

Precipitousssite of
village near Gap,

Maritimes, France, Id.

ranean, in Afghanistan and in Nepal, the roof of a house
becomes the terrace ofthe house above it.

Interiors are often extended with cantilevers over the sloping
terrain at times forming vertigo-inducing balconies or rooms.
These precarious constructions exploit the opportunities for
distant views and refreshing breezes. The sculptural plasticity
ofthese protruding masses, juxtaposed to the buttressed base,
is characteristic of buildings on sloping sites in many parts of
the world, for example the multi-storey adobe brick houses of
Yemen, the Buddhist monasteries in Nepal and Christian
Orthodox monasteries in Mount Athos in Greece.

ELEFTHERIOS PAVLIDES
1.11.2.k Upland
Though frequently used to describe high and hilly
country the term ‘upland’ is essentially comparative, such areas
being seen as relative to the ‘lowlands’ of a given region, and
often considered as the foothills ofhigher mountain ranges. A
complementary term often used to emphasize mountainous,
as distinct from hilly, terrain, ‘highland’ is also relative. Thus,
the Highlands ofScotland seldom reach 1000 m (4000 ft) while
the Nilgiri Hills of India rise above 2600 m (9000 ft). Whereas
the uplands are frequently perceived as grass-covered and
moulded in form, highlands and mountains are often regarded
as bare, exposed and rugged. But these are features that differ
widely, depending on the age ofthe rocks, the extent of forest
and grass cover, the decay of vegetal matter and the depth of
soil, the precipitation of rain and snow, and exposure to wind
and sun.

Broadly speaking, highlands and mountain regions are thinly
populated, their effects as physical barriers often restricting
communications either to strenuous traverse on footor the use
ofstrings ofpack animals, and reducing their potential for settlement. Even the widespread practice oftranshumance, where
animals are moved over mountains to summer pastures, to take
advantage of new grass and to rest lower grazing lands, is still
constrained by the limited accessibility ofthe mountains.

Permanent settlements in upland regions have been conditioned by altitude, latitude and climate and by the lack oflevel
sites, or by specific topographic features. In general, the more

Location and Site

1.1.2

northerly the uplands the lower the settlements; in warmer climates settlements may be found at higher altitudes. So itis possible to find substantial towns above 2000 m (6500 ft), among
them Addis Ababa in Ethiopia, San’ä in Yemen or Mexico City,
Mexico. There are many that are situated far higher, especially
where minerals are mined, though with exceptions, these are
largely industrial towns of comparatively recent date. In hot
and humid regions, many tropical plantations are situated in
the uplands, often ataltitudes ofseveral thousand feet, asin the
Philippines, the Malay Peninsulaand much ofIndonesia, notto
mention Costa Rica or Brazil, where coffee, tea, cacao, andrubber may be raised.

Hill sites have often been developed defensively, with settlements surrounding castles or donjons, and with small towns,
as in much ofItaly or France, clustered on the crests ofhills rising from the undulating plain. Houses have been established
by partially excavating the rock and using this material to build
a plinth to a level suitable for habitation; the cellars that make
up the space between inclination and floor are a feature ofinnumerable hill towns. In such circumstances access paths and
rock-strewn roads wind and bend to reduce the steepness of
ascent; houses are crowded together, though often with asummit terrace for a church, market and community functions.

In many parts ofthe world, such as the Deccan of India, the
uplands rise to a high plateau region. Again, the definition of
‘plateaux’ is imprecise, though they are widely understood to
be relatively uniform at a high altitude, approached by steep
slopes or an escarpment as at Bandiagara, Mali. It should be
noted that, technically, most of the African continent is a
plateau rising rapidly from the coasts. Some plateaux, such as
the Great Basin west ofthe Rockies or much ofthe highlands of
Tibet, are enclosed between ranges. Above thetimber-linesuch
plateaux may be cold, but suitable for summer grazing and
nomadic herdsmen. Certain tableland plateaux, such as Acoma
Rock or the Hopi mesa, both in Arizona, have sustained pueblo
villages for many centuries. But others of similar configuration, such as Table Mountain in South Africa, are uninhabited.

Human settlement has often been inhibited by the climatic
extremes of upland regions — fogs, heavy snows, avalanches
and high levels of insolation. In such conditions the raising of
animals — sheep, goats, llamas — for their wool as well as for
food is necessary for warm clothing. Timber from mountain
pine forests is often used in wide spans between mass stone
walls, and in supporting stone and slate clad roofs. Dwellings
are built to deflect precipitation, offering deep eaves and sheltered balconies with stone roofs or deep thatch made as imper-

vious as possible.

On plateaux and the more level summits of the uplands,
buildings may be more dispersed, though the need for shelter
from prevailing winds may encourage more compact and
enclosed structures. Buildings must often be resistant to
ground movementand erosion, so that substantial footings, or
deep earthfast piles are desirable. Upland settlements are often
remote, independent, conservative and slow to change, and
contact with other settlements may be intermittent, made possible only by the construction of passes, roads and bridges
thrown across clefts and ravines.

151

## S. 26

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

It is this isolation which has contributed to the preservation
of traditional lifeways and customs including those of building, encouraging anthropological research in upland areas
where the impact of external contact was minimized. Consequently, alarge number ofstudies have been made of, for example, cultures in Nepal and the Himalayas where the diversity of
solutions to upland housing is remarkable. But even here the
density of population is not high. In spite of the looseness of
definition the uplands support only a small proportion of the
world’s population. PAUL OLIVER

1.1.21 Valley

A valley is defined as a stretch ofland between hills
or mountains. As aresultofcomplex and long-lasting geologic
and climatic processes (such as eruptions, sedimentations,
tectonic activities, erosions and particle transport), there is a
wide range of valley formations in nature: narrow V-shaped
valleys, wide U-shaped valleys, canyons, gulches, gullies and so
on (Press and Siever).

The valley as a location type includes a number of common
features. There is a valley floor, usually with a watercourse and
walls on both sides which define the valley space parallel to the
direction of the watercourse. For a discussion of location and
site in cases where the river and its banks are large, it is
sufficient to refer to the river rather than the valley.

As locations for settlements there are great variations in valley landscapes. They will differ both in scale and proportion
and in climate, vegetation and soils. The longitudinal direction, the angle ofinclination, the curves and the forms ofthe
slopes also vary. These kinds of difference have been crucial
when eyaluating a place as a possible domicile and its capacity
to provide daily bread.

The valley bottom usually contains the landward communication lines parallel to theriver. The valleys’ transport activities for
the movement of goods, produce and surpluses have given
livelihood both as a main occupation and secondarily by collecting taxes, accommodating travellers, and lately- by tourism.

The river has a potential for irrigating purposes and for use
as household water. It gives food and is an energy source. The
forestryllogrunning/sawmill combination has been essential to
the development ofthe wooden architecture ofmany regions.

A wide valley bottom may provide space for building sites
and for farming areas. Floods, avalanches, violent winds and
cold air flowing down the valleys will reduce the possibilities of
exploiting and settling a fertile valley bottom. In many valleys,
however, the bottom is the only possible location for settlement.

The valley slopes have potential as arable land because of
their special irrigation and drainage possibilities as well as
their particular growing conditions — brought about by
different soils and the topographic properties of slope declination and orientation. Steep slopes are awkward to exploit. This
disadvantage can be compensated for by a favourable exposure

to the sun and by a location above the problems of floods and
cold air rivers in the valley bottom. People reorganize the slope
by building terraces, constructing devices to protect against
avalanche, planting protective woods, developing plant species
suited to the special conditions and so on.

Asaresidential site the valley slope has several advantages: it
is possible to control the climate surrounding the houses and
to protectagainsttheriverfloods by choosingtherightaltitude.
Ifthe soil conditions are favourable, the valley slope can be the
dwelling itself, slightly excavated to produce stone or earth
caves.

The compound pattern of resources in a valley location
makes it possible and efficient to develop a composite pursuit
culture. The valley space may be justa part oftheterritory: there
are exploitable resources beyond the walls ofthe valley. Transitory use of the location contributes to profitable occupation.
Valley peoples have usually been very conscious ofthe potential
ofthe territory and have developed cultures which even in the
architecture have reflected the ability to use the local resources
without exceeding or overtaxing the capacity of the territory
(Carlen).

The climate, the topography and the soils define the production areas. The situation of those areas, the communication
lines and the role ofthe river, as well as the climatic and topographic factors, decide the physical features ofthe settlements
— their location, group formation, orientation and often house
forms.

The linear character ofthe valley evokes associations ofrestlessness and movement, oftravel upand down (and out of) the
valley. The spatially defined character of the valley’s cross-section conveys associations of constancy and stability, even isolation and confinement. HARALD HOYEM

1982

valley near the Lirlien

Wake of 1969-70.

„us Natural Disasters

Buildings are designed and built as a response to
everyday needs, including protection from the weather. Some
mustalso cope with regular climatic fluctuations such as monsoon rains or winter snowfall. However, structures are only
created to cope with extreme, irregular and infrequentclimatic,
hydrological or geophysical hazardous events as a result of
adaptation following observed failure. This can lead to the
development of the essential knowledge required for safe
building, and under extreme conditions also creates a strong
desire on the part ofthe occupants and builders to reduce, or
“mitigate’ future losses through various means. These include
siting ofbuildings, structural measures as well as environmental protection and social adjustments in patterns of building
use.

A principle therefore applies: ‘changes to achieve safety follow failure’. However there isa condition to the effectthat form
and constructional detailing may only ‘follow failure’ for a
short time before they revert to their vulnerable norm as concern for safe construction declines to a distant memory. More
frequently, form will only follow failure when safety measures
are required through laws which are enforced, and when
changes are culturally acceptable, socially convenient and
financially affordable to the occupants (Davis, 1983).

After decades of confidence in the need to change patterns of
building towards safe ‘engineered solutions’, major international donors of disaster assistance are beginning to recognize
the need to respect local wisdom and knowledge of safe building practice before importing an alien technology (IFRCS).

Hazard and vulnerability

Most vernacular buildings, and their occupants, are exposed to
some threat posed by extreme natural or ‘manmade’ events.
The latter can be divided into hazards that threaten lives, those
that affect property and those that threaten both. A drought or
a chemical spillage of toxic fumes can kill the occupants of a
dwelling, but it may leave the structure unaffected.

Whilst the great majority of all the earthquake deaths result
from building failure this is notthe case with the other hazards
where the causes of death are from other factors. In famines
people die from lack of food and water or diseases relating to
malnutrition. Obviously deaths result from drowning in the
case of floods and, in high winds, death and injury may result
from drowning in flood surge areas and very occasionally from
the impact of fiying debris.

Despite considerable progress in disaster preparedness
planning, the overall numbers of dead and injured are still
increasing as a result of the powerful forces of population
growth, urbanization and environmental degradation. Increasingly large numbers of marginalized communities have little
alternative other than to occupy unsafe sites on unstable slopes
and flood plains.

A modified order ofrelative impact can be seen from statistics concerning the ‘homeless population’, people who have
been rendered homeless as a result of their dwellings being
destroyed or damaged.

Average homeless populations in a given year were as follows:

Famine/drought: 22720
Earthquake: 224.006
High winds: 989 544
Floods: 2870831
Volcanic: 10604
Landslide: 106824

The average number of homeless persons per annum for all
disasters is 4224529.

Highly vulnerable countries, such as some South Pacific
island countries (including Papua New Guinea), the Philippines, Colombia and Mexico are subject to the full spectrum of
hazards: droughts, earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, floods
and cyclonic storms. Inevitably this concentration results in a
far higher ‘perception of risk’ on the part of such populations
anda greater likelihood ofbuilding and settlement adaptation.

Global hazard distribution is varied: drought and flood risk
are dispersed throughout the world, seismic areas are concentrated attectonic plate boundaries and cyclonic storms are generated by warm tropical seas in areas 4° to 5° north or south of
the Equator.

## S. 27

1.11 ENVIRONMENT

Opportunities and constraints for safe construction

Whilst damage to buildings from natural hazards is concentrated in ‘non-engineered’ vernacular buildings, there are certain notable exceptions where a process of adaptation occurs
whenever certain hazards are common events in a society. For
example, within the Philippines 272 natural disasters occurred
between 1966 and 1990. Damage assessment reports from
many ofthese events have highlighted the good performance of
certain vernacular structures, particularly against cyclonic
wind forces. Thus it has been suggested that an awareness of
hazards has been incorporated into building form, selection of
materials, structure, constructional detailing and siting.

Inevitably a safe settlement comprising secure dwellings will
cost more to construct than unsafe versions. Cheap, unreinforced adobe walls will need to be inserted into a frame to resist
lateral shaking and foundations may need to be stronger.
Authorities may outlaw unsafe cheap hillside sites in favour of
flat, more expensive sites. But the unsafe location may be occupied in preference to the safe alternative in response to such
everyday benefits as shelter from prevailing winds, good
drainage and the release of flat sites for productive agricultural
use.

A wide repertoire of methods is adopted by communities to
reduce the risks they face. These include the mapping of
hazards, ways to reduce the power of hazards, changes in the
form of dwellings, structural measures to protect buildings
and social and physical preparedness measures (Arya).

Measures to reduce risks begin with accurate assessment.
Hazard mapping reveals the areas which are particularly susceptible to flood, drought, avalanche and high wind forces.
Hazard information is needed on such matters as location,
frequency, duration and severity (i.e. wind speeds, water flow
data and the like). Examples have been recorded of hazard
mapping being conducted by communities as a traditional
practice. There is evidence that the Inuit were able to map risks
relating to the incidence of snow and ice prior to the selection
oftheir settlement locations.

Vulnerable communities frequently devise ways to reduce the
power and impact of a hazard. For example, the planting of a
dense belt of trees can reduce the impact of wind forces on a
settlement or the planting of mangrove in a coastal region can
significantly modify the destructive power ofhigh ocean waves
that accompany a tropical cyclone. As yet there are no equivalent measures available to reduce the destructive power of
earthquakes and volcanoes.

Hazard-resistantdesign may require achange in theroofshape
or plan form. For example an L-shaped plan is a common shape
for farm buildings in eastern Turkey with the domestic quarters
having the addition of a small attached wing for livestock. However, because of differential movement under seismic loading a
square or rectangular plan is preferable, if less desirable from a
functional standpoint.

The impulse to change is strongest during reconstruction.
With an all-pervasive visual aid of failure to build safely there is
a short-lived but powerful incentive present to find ways to site
the reconstructed settlement to resist future impact. This context presents a unique opportunity; once passed, a swift return

154

tothe dangerous norms of unresistant siting and constructional practice can result. The effectiveness of such a response
can be conditioned by the availability of expertise to inform
local builders how to build safely by access to alternative building materials and by affordable building construction techniques.

Structural measures to protect buildings could include such
‘macro-elements’ as improved lateral framing using diagonal
timber members to resist powerful wind forces or small constructional details, such as ‘hurricane straps’ used to fix roof
rafters to purlins. These measures are sometimes added as a
form of strengthening to an existing structure (retrofit), but
this is rare practice in vernacular building. As noted above a
much more common adaptation takes place when structural
changes occur after disasters.

In areas where hazards are frequent or seasonal, communities often develop effective ways to prepare for them and cope
with the consequences. A good example is the demountable
bamboo-framed house as used in north Bihar, India, which can
be taken to pieces and temporarily moved to higher ground,
such asa raised embankment, in the event ofannual flooding.

Safe construction appears to be one of many ‘trade-offs’ facing local builders and dwelling occupants. The change to safe
construction may require a decision to depart from a wellestablished tradition of building and this choice could be a luxury, or even pose an economic threat for a poor family living at
a subsistence level. A typical example of this process occurs
when heavy, flat, unframed earthen roofs are replaced by sloping, lightweight structures, which can be more expensive,
more noisy in heavy rainfall, may not provide a platform for
domestic functions, may require additional maintenance and
will certainly give much less protection from climatic extremes
(Aysan and Oliver).

Thus, against any desire there may be for building change in
the interests ofsafety there can be opposing influences: conservatism, apathy, convenience or the reality of limited resources.
These forces are normally more powerful than a desire for protection from a once-in-a-lifetime earthquake, cyclone or flood
(de Sousa). IAN DAVIS
Desertification
Desertification is the spread of desert-like conditions, or ecological degradation in arid, semi-arid and subhumid lands. The agents that cause desertification are varied
and include poor land management, salinization, waterlog-

1.111.3.a

ging, overgrazing, poor cropping methods and unsuitable soil
conservation methods. Deforestation is another catalyst of
land exhaustion and soil erosion.

Itis estimated thatin Africa more than go per cent ofallwood
is used for cookingand other energy needs and this demand for
fuel wood has grown since the oil price rise in 1974. The traditional system ofland use in fragile environments has been broken down to become a key factor in promoting desertification.
A further, less frequent cause is climate change, which can be
exacerbated by deforestation.

This creeping disaster is occurring on a global scale in arid,
semi-arid and sub-humid lands within Africa, the Middle East,

oslon, oklahoma,
states.

ideon (ed.), 1982
„ullos, Panayis,

4 Mohammed,

See also
1.V.7.h Maintenance

References
Alexander, David, 19%
Arya, A.S., 1994
Aysan, Y.F., 1993
‚Aysan, Yasemin, and
oliver, Paul, 1987
CAR and OCDS, 1994,
1995
Davis, I., 1983, 1984
Smith, Keith, 1992
Sousa, F. de, 1984

Pakistan, India, China, Australia, the Commonwealth ofIndependent States (CIS), the USA and Greece. Thus, desertification is a slow-onset disaster that leads to increased poverty,
malnutrition and disease and has the effect of destabilizingthe
socialand economic bases ofaffected countries.

In scale of impact the creeping process of desertification is
vastly more significant than the relatively localized effect of
earthquakes, volcanoes, floods and tropical cyclones. The
annual loss of productive land is in the order of 60000 sq km
(23 400 sq mi), distributed between 100 countries. It has been
calculated that 14 per cent of the world’s population, amounting to 600-700 million, live in areas of dıylands and 80 million
are directly threatened by desertification.

Unlike other hazards desertification does not damage or
destroy buildings or settlements, but it has a significant impact
upon them. This is a progressive process of socialleconomic
change in response to crisis conditions. At an early stage in
desertification there will be destruction of vegetation that may
be a basic requirement for building construction and in
extreme desertification situations water will notbeavailable for
use in building mud or adobe walls.

Competition for increasingly scarce resources leads to the
displacement of families to occupy makeshift camps near large
towns where there are expectations of improved livelihoods or
ofrelief distribution. These population increases within towns
and cities that become the hosts of ‘environmental refugees’
then experience a further set ofresource problems as aresultof
the increase in demand for land, water, fuel wood, meat, vegetables and building materials. However, the ‘forced migration’ that takes place when a community is obliged to leave its
settlementis partof complex range ofactivities that communities under severe pressure adopt to maintain their survival.
Population movement can be accompanied by the sale of
household assets, and adapted farming methods, includingthe
sale of livestock. There is an extensive literature on the subject
of‘coping mechanisms’ (Chambers).

The influx of migrants from rural areas can pose a further
environmental health and fire risk whenever large concentrations of people are crowded together within insubstantial temporary accommodation. Such families are obliged to liveon the
least favoured land that others have avoided. This is frequently

Natural Disasters 1.111.3
hazard prone, typically in flood plains, on steep slopes vulnerable to landslides and in areas subject to epidemics.

There is some evidence that the layout and building forms in
certain areas subject to desertification such as El Oued in Algeria have been partially successful in mitigating the deposition
of sand (Sherzad). Where desertification has led to a severe
drought the population may be too malnourished to have the
strength to build or maintain their dwellings. A large influx of
families into an area can have severe ecological consequences
as local vegetation can be stripped to secure shelter materials or
provide essential fuel wood. A response of nomadic pastoralists to the risks posed by droughtand possibly desertification is
to achieve a high degree ofmobility. IAN DAVIS
1.11.3.b Earthquake
Barthquakes result from the release of tectonic
energy caused by a shift in the Earth’s crust or volcanic activity.
This can fracture the surface ofthe ground and cause ground
vibration, which in turn can cause massive building failures.
Barthquakes are measured on a ‘Richter scale’ to indicate
their magnitude, a logarithmic index of released seismic
energy, whilst their intensity is a record of observed localized
impact.

Seismic activity mainly coincides with the boundary movementoflithospheric plates which are about 60 km (37 mi) thick
and move slowly across the globe atabout 2-5 cm (0.8-2 in) per
annum, above a softer interior in the plate tectonics process.
Whilst knowledge concerning the nature of earthquakes and
their location grows rapidly it remains impossible to predict
the time, place or severity ofa future event with any precision,
although a global search continues to develop accurate predictive tools leading to seismic warning systems.

Primary impact comes either from ground shaking, causing
unresistant buildings to collapse, or from liquefaction where a
normally stable sandy soil is transformed into a muddy liquid
state into which buildings may sink. Barthquakes induce a
number of secondary hazards: fires, rockfalls, landslides,
avalanches, floods from dam failures and tsunamis.

Between 1900 and 1990 earthquakes caused 1.52 million
deaths in at least 75 countries. This total includes casualties
sustained in the great Tangshan earthquake in China in 1976
which caused 242 000 deaths. About 75 per cent ofall earthquake deaths were caused by the collapse of buildings, and of
these deaths 60-70 per cent resulted from the collapse of
masonry buildings.

Various research findings indicate how adaptation to seismic
forces may occur. Five factors have been observed: more robust
construction; selection of resistant building materials;
modified structural systems; adapted building forms to minimize seismic damage; and the avoidance of unsafe sites.

Examples of strong construction details include energyabsorbing wooden joints in traditional Japanese construction.
A technique used in the Alpine-Himalayan seismic belt in
Nuristan (Afghanistan), Pakistan and Kashmir sets timber
spacing members atregular intervals in stone masonry walling.
These reinforce weak walling and may form rudimentary ring
beams providing seismic protection.

155

## S. 28

Stan Davis

1. ENVIRONMENT

In Peru and other parts of Central and South America the
advantages of composite earth and timber construction
(bahareque or taquezal construction) that combine heavy mass
for thermal protection with the ductility of timber have been
demonstrated repeatedly in earthquake conditions (Langenbach). Traditional dwellings in western Turkey were constructed using similar timber from and infill systems: Bagdadi
and Himis. Both systems had been used in the Gediz area, and
survived the severe shaking experienced in the major earthquake of1970.

When the earthquake in the Latur region ofIndia occurred in
September 1993, causing an estimated 30000 deaths, the
dwellings of poorer families, constructed from bamboo and
sugar cane set within lightweight timber frames with grass or
palm roofing, survived the disaster. More expensive, masonrywalled houses, with heavy flatroofs oftimber beamsandathick
cover of compacted mud, suffered very severe damage. The
reconstruction that followed has incorporated some important
innovations. Perhaps for the firsttime an entirettown, Bhandar-
Karthe, has been strengthened with a ‘retrofit’ project. Vernacular dwellings have been modified by measures such as the
reduction ofroofloads, the introduction ofring beams and the
bracketing oftimber joints.

In the Karakoram area of northern Pakistan (Coburn and
Spence), in Bali and in Greece (Ferrigne etal.) a double structural system has been devised. The roofis supported on a dual
system of walls and posts, allowing posts to keep the roof in
place even when the walls have collapsed, as in an earthquake.
This system has been adopted on the island of Lefkas in Greece
where a double wood-framed structural system has been developed so that masonıy walls can collapse leaving the building
intact. This form of construction also contains timber jointing
with a range ofsimple energy-absorbing devices (Touliatos).

Builders in Arcquipa, southern Peru, have devised a timber
frame to fit within the triangular gable-end of a dwelling. This
is set behind the adobe blocks that form the end wall of the
dwelling, so that in the event ofseismic shaking the blocks fall

Bagdadi form of

156

outwards rather than into the building. Arch and dome and
barrel-vault construction in the Dasht-e-Bayez area of Iran was
reported to have been more resistant to seismic damage than
rectilinear construction (Coburn and Spence).

Following earthquakes it is noticeable that builders modify
traditional ‘cut and fill’ techniques on hillside sites to a purely
‘cut excavation’ from their observation of failure when using
the former approach. IAN DAVIS
Flood
Flooding comes in many forms: river floods; rainfall
floods; flooding due to severe storms including cyclonie conditions; and tsunami. Tsunami (Japanese: ‘harbour wave’) are
giant waves causing coastal flooding generated by underwater
earthquakes. Floods vary from the slow-rising overtopping ofa
river, to the raging torrent of a fast-moving flash flood. They
cause serious damage to buildings and the urban environment,
agriculture and topsoil and contaminate water supplies. As
well as riverine flooding, coastal areas can suffer severe flooding from high-tide conditions accompanied by storms. Floods
are linked to a variety of other hazards including landslides
induced by torrential rainfall, Jahars — rivers of hot volcanic
deposits interspersed with water — and coastal storm surge
flooding which accompanies tropical storms.

Flood intensity is increased by the intervention of people
with the environment in ways that include deforestation, poor
farming techniques such as overgrazing, and increased urbanization which prevents the ground absorption ofrainfall. Flood
disasters are second only to droughts in terms of numbers of
people affected worldwide. Between 1980 and 1985, 162 floods
resulted in the loss 0f33 542 lives (an average of 27 floods causing 5590 deaths per annum).

Flood damage to buildings includes building decay through
rot caused by consistent soaking and dıying oftimbers, the lifting offoundations through flotation ofrising floodwaters, and
erosion of ground support due to undercutting by the scouring
action of water. Total destruction can occur when the building
is washed away by the impact ofeither fast-moving floodwaters
or floating, water-borne debris.
