Techniques for flood protection for buildings take the form
of small annual adjustments as building occupants react in a
progressive manner with a constant process of rebuilding,
repair, raising the ground level, creating flood walls, filling
sand bags and so on (Hodgson and Whaites). A traditional
device is to raise thresholds and insert floodboards which are
dropped in front of a building entrance, in the manner of a
porteullis, reinforced with sandbags to reduce floodwater penetration.

Erosion is the basic problem of flood impact on wall materials through moving water or prolonged inundation. Traditional buildings constructed from mud or wattle and daub are
particularly at risk from flood damage. Various methods of
‘waterproofing’ these materials are often adopted by the mixing ofcement with adobe or natural bonding agents. There are
a wide variety of strengthening measures, including the reinforcement of foundations and the protection of footings with
tiling or rendering.

A vital safety element is to ensure escape routes for the occupants, which may be the provision of an access route to a roof,
the choice of a fat roof in lieu ofa pitched roof to provide an
escape platform or the creation oftwo-storey buildings to facilitate vertical evacuation oftrapped occupants to the upper floor.
In Bangladesh during exceptionally high floods families may
live on platforms (machas) within their house. Ifthe waters rise
they may have to moye to the roofs oftheir house or evacuate in
boats to higher ground (Brammer).

The flood-prone town of Bang Liin Thailand contains acomplex of two-storey longhouses on two interconnected levels.
During the dry season cars use the streets in thenormal manner.
Howerer, during the flood season the inhabitants move goods
and belongings to the second level where previous ground-floor
functions continue, butwith boataccess (Jumsai).

In Bangladesh, as a result of the acute shortage of land for
building, families have traditionally built on the ‘char lands’ —
silt islands set within the vast river deltas. As floodwaters rise
some dwellings are demounted for relocation. Lightweight
buildings are carried to riverside embankments where they
remain until the waters subside (Brammer).

Natural Disasters 1.1.3

Where timber is available as a building material it is common
to build on stilts, set into a river- or sea-bed. In Bangladesh
some buildings can be raised or lowered on their bamboo stilts
in accordance with rising flood levels. Stilt houses are also
extensivelyadopted throughout Southeast Asia. In Malaysia the
tradition of building in flood plains on timber stilts has been
adapted in new building traditions by using concrete posts.
Individual buildings can be lifted to earth platforms above normal flood levels. Communities co-operate in Andhra Pradesh,
India, to raise the level of homes on higher earth platforms
above anticipated floodlevels. A pit excavated to build the platform can then become a fishpond for aquaculture.

In Bangkok, the predominate building type was a floating
house until 1900. In the mid-rgth century the population was
400 000 of which 350 000 lived in floating raft homes along the
rivers and canals. The remainder lived in stilted houses on
river-banks. In Tha Khanon in Surat Thani province of the
Malay peninsula the annual monsoon produces sudden flooding, covering the village to a depth of 10-20 m (33-65 ft); the
dwellings rest on a raft of bamboos which float as the waters
rise. Anchorage is provided by the house being tethered to four
corner bamboos (Jumsai). A similar pattern prevails in the
upper Amazon town of Iquitos, Peru, where annual flooding
has resulted in the construction ofhouses ofcane and thatch on
wooden rafts which float during flood conditions (Davis).

TAN DAVIS
1.11.3.d Landslide
The term ‘landslide’ covers a wide variety of land
forms and processes relating to the movement ofrock and soil
down slopes caused by gravitational forces often in combination with other hazards: floods, tropical cyclones, volcanoes
and earthquakes. For example, in 1970 the debris slide on the
slopes of Huascaran, Peru, initiated by an earthquake, killed
over 18000 people when the town of Yungay was engulfed.
Landslides can also create other hazards such as flash flooding
which can result when a natural dam is formed (and may later
burst), as a result oflandslide debris blocking a river. This is a
frequent occurrence in Nepal where in 1993 heavy monsoon
rains resulted in extensive flooding and landslides, some of
which temporarily blocked rivers causing subsequent flooding
resulting in 1300 deaths.

Globally an average of 6.6 landslides cause 499 deaths per
annum; thus in comparison with other hazards landslides
which are not secondary to another hazard result in relatively
low casualty statistics. However, the data are misleading since
landslides often do occur as a secondary consequence of
another type ofhazard, such as flooding, a cyclonic storm or as
aresult ofan earthquake.

Sudden or slow, changes in the composition, structure,
hydrology and vegetation ofa slope can cause landslides. They
can derive from vibration created by earthquakes, blasting,
thunderstorms or even traffic movement. A change in the water
content ofthe soil as a result of heavy rainfall or a rise in the
ground-water table can also cause them to occur. In addition,
lateral support to slopes can lead to these hazards as a result of
erosion, building construction, road building, deforestation or

157

## S. 29

DEE EREEESESEERESEIISEDIEEESBEEEEEEIGEumErOEuEmannEGmneBee

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

the loss of stabilizing vegetation. When a landslide took place
in Thailand in 1988 killing 400 people the most common explanation was extensive deforestation in the area (Blaikie etal.).

The weight ofbuildings, loose rock or volcanic material can
cause landslides. Within urban areas the most common causes
are human actions, such as the interruption of water couxses,
changes in the water-table or construction ofroads and buildings using ‘cutand fill’ technique.

There is limited evidence that in areas with frequent land-

slides builders recognize certain priorities, such as the need to
build using lightweight building materials or the need to avoid
building using cut and fill ground preparation, since ‘made-up
ground’ is likely to fall away with the building that rests on it
when a landslide occurs. This pattern has been observed in the
barrio settlements of Caracas, Venezuela (Jimenez-Diaz).
However, builders are rarely aware ofthe importance of piping
away any waste water to avoid it seeping into the land adjacent
to dwellings and contributing to slope instability. Leaking
tubewells, standpipes and septic tanks were blamed as the primary causes ofdevastating landslides in Puerto Rico (1985) and
Rio de Janeiro (1988) (Blaikie etal.).

The construction offirm foundations is nota significant factor in reducing damage or destruction from landslides, nor is
the practice ofbuilding on stilts, prevalent in Nuristan and the
Philippines - a landslide will move the stilts as well as the rest
of the building. A landslide does not ‘pass below’ a building
although the use ofsstilts may allow minor rockfalls to do so.

Settlements are highly vulnerable when they are built on
steep slopes. However, there is evidence of widespread use of
unstable slopes for building due to the pressure on land availability, particularly in urban areas, and the general attraction of
hillside sites because oftheir relative low cost compared to flat
sites, shelter from prevailing winds, good drainage and the
release of Hat sites for productive agricultural use. A common
protective measure is to plant trees on unstable slopes to prevent soil erosion and bind subsoils together. In Jamaica and
Taiwan an innovative practice that costs virtually nothing has
been developed by communities atrisk from landslides. This is

to lay disused tyres on the exposed sections ofan unstable hillside. The tyres are then roped together andaseedling is planted
in earth ofthe centre holes. IAN DAVIS

Snow/avalanche

Avalanches occur in mountainous regions where
snow masses hanging on the slopes are precipitated under the
effects ofboth internal and external forces into rapidly accelerating drifts towards the valley basin. Moving as rapidly as 304
kmj/hr (189 milhr) snow and ice are highly destructive and can
wipe out entire settlements, uproot trees, and destroy roads,
railways and power supplies. Yet avalanche casualty statistics
indicate that 30-40 per cent ofall vietims caught in their path

1.11.3.e

survive.

The internal forces’ that cause avalanches relate to the
nature ofthe snow mass - its form, temperature, volume andso
on. ‘External forces’ relatetoacombination oftopographicand
meteorological conditions. Topographie features include a
number of critical factors that relate to slope conditions, such
as slopes which are newly covered, slope angles over 35°, slopes
covered with old snow that has accumulated over along period
and those open to preyailing winds. In addition, mountain
ridges and vegetation patterns affect proneness to avalanches.

Dangerous meteorological circumstances include a temperature over o °C (32 °F) that persists for over 36 hours after a
snowstorm, a new snowfall ofover 25 cm (0 in) onto an existing snow cover and strong winds of over 7 misec (23 ft/sec)
velocity for 24-48 hours during a snowstorm. In addition, hazardous conditions can be intensified by rain falling onto existing snow cover, and by rapid snow melting as a result of
sunshine.

Passive protective measures include the temporary closure of
settlements and highways and the protection of roads and
energy transmission lines by constructing tunnels to enable
avalanches to pass overhead. Active measures include erecting
barriers on mountain or slope crests to prevent ‘snow cornices’
from developing. These are traditionally constructed from
wooden or metal trellises or fences. Large settlements can be
protected with snow barriers, constructed from steel, timber
and concrete.

Individual buildings can be protected by triangular concrete
structures. Alpine communities have adopted tree-planting
techniques where the edges of forested areas on slopes are in
ragged lines to avoid creating ‘corridors’ for avalanche paths.
Where itis impossible to planttrees in avalanche-prone slopes,
some communities inserta series ofdiagonally placed poles, or
piles of carefully stacked logs to form a protective device.

Isolated dwellings are much more exposed to avalanches
than clustered settlements as expensive environmental protection measures are not feasible for every building. However, for
farming communities living in alpine areas, thereisan obvious
conflict here since the nature of their landholdings will determine their farm location — and it may not be possible to site
their farm within a settlement.

A recognizable roofing pattern is for buildings constructed
in areas that are particularly avalanche-prone to have a monopitch roof with the slope corresponding to the ground slope.
This is to present less obstacles to moving snow — as well as
making it easy to remove heavy snow from the roof. It is also
clear that steep roofslopes are adopted to reduce the accumulation of heavy snow.

»

Damage surveys of buildings that fail under snow loads in
avalanches indicate that the walls and roof must be capable of
withstanding a load of up to 3 tons/sq m. This requirement is
likely to be well in excess ofthe design load of any traditionally
built structure constructed from timber or masonry.

Because of their dead weight on a structure snow and ice
pose a threat to buildings and their occupants; there are also
risks of personal injury either from melting snow or ice falling
from a roof, or from someone slipping on ice adjacent to a
building. Whilst avalanche conditions are the most extreme
hazard facing buildings in alpine regions, the predictable and
regular risk of the weight of heavy snowfall or ice damaging a
roof or falling from it is accommodated in many ways (Cereghini). A pattern has been observed in British Columbia where the
roof pitch of buildings steepens in direct ratio to the scale of
anticipated snowfall to allow snow to fall offbefore it accumulates into aload capable of causing structural damage. External
steps and stairs in certain Russian houses are covered with
pitched roofs to provide protection from rain and probably
more specifically from ice forming on the steps posing a very
serious risk ofinjury. In Poland, covered external steps ofsome
mountain dwellings allow access to the first floor as an alternative to ground-floor entry, which may be impossible because of
deep snow drifts. In towns and villages in the Alps it is traditional for buildings to turn their gable facades to the street,
avoiding heavy melting snow and ice falling from a roof over
the street, risking injuryto people who are more likelyto be presentin the street than adjacenttto side walls. IAN DAVIS
1.11.3f Tropical cyclone
The generic term ‘tropical cyclone’ is used to cover
weather systems where winds exceed gale force (minimum 34
knots or 63 km/hr [39 milhr]). Called ‘hurricanes’ in the
Caribbean, ‘typhoons’ in parts of Asiaand ‘tropical cyclones’ in
the South Pacific and the Indian subcontinent, these vast
storms originate over warm tropical oceans and are rotating,
intense, low-pressure systems with wind speeds that can reach
above 63 knots or 117 km/hr (73 mi/hr). The atmospheric and
oceanic conditions necessary for the development of cyclonic

Natural Disasters 1.1.3
storms relate to warm seas, high humidity, unstable atmospherie conditions and a location 4-5 latitude degrees from the
Equator which allows the influence of forces from the Earth’s
rotation, the ‘Coriolis force’.

Cyclones are the most destructive of seasonally recurring
rapid-onset natural hazards, with 80-100 occurring annually
throughout the world. The storms bring wind damage, which
causes few deaths but where surge flooding occurs this can
result in massive casualties. The most severe cyclones in the
2oth century have occurred in a similar location ofthe coast of
Bangladesh in 1970 and 1991 when 300 000 and 139 oo0 respectively are estimated to have died. The torrential rainfall experienced in cyclones can lead to flooding and landslides, the
contamination ofwater supplies and increased risks ofmalaria
and other viral conditions.

Coastal surges can be reduced by the construction of
embankments with rocks placed on the seaward side to break
up wave strength. However, such embankments may eventually
create more risks than they reduce, and it is more effective to
recognize the dynamic nature of the relationship between a
shoreline and the sea, rather than to build embankments or
flood walls, and plan accordingly (Pilkney etal.).

A natural defence in many South Pacific islands and on the
Pacific coast of Colombia are mangroves, planted and maintained in coastal waters to break up waves that cause extensive
coastal damage in cyclone conditions. Wind speeds can be
reduced by planting shelter breaks of variable-height trees and
shrubs. In France rows of poplar trees are used to defend fields
from high winds, especially the mistral which can devastate
crops. A similar pattern occurs in the Netherlands where
densely planted trees protect the polders from high winds coming from the North Sea.

There are three underlying principles of cyclone-resistant
construction. Buildings must be firmly attached to foundations; they must have a structure where every element is tied
together to develop a chain of strength against uplift that is
firmly anchored to the ground; and the structural elements
must be integrated in a manner that prevents the building
breaking into separate elements (Oakley). A typical and vital
cyclone-resistant detail in Jamaica is a ‘hurricane strap’, a bent
piece of metal, sometimes improvised by cutting a strip offa
disused oil can, to hold a roof rafter to a purlin as a much
stronger connection than a nail or screw fixing to resist the
powerful lifting forces of cyclonic winds. In timber-framed
dwellings it is common for builders to insert diagonal bracing
into the roof construction or into wall panels between vertical
posts. Following the introduction of well-framed timber
dwellings, known as the ‘Core Housing Programme’, in the
Philippines local builders have inserted diagonal bracing into
their own dwellings.

In Guam the flexibility of the housing superstructure with
tied, non-rigid joints, and the fact that the roof can be entirely
lifted offand replaced, are both considered to be adaptations to
the effects of cyclone winds. An ideal roof form to withstand
ceyclonic wind forces is aroofpitch over 40°, with a hipped form
— thus avoiding exposed gable-ends. Very low-pitched roofs
using metal sheet roofing are particularly vulnerable owing to

159

## S. 30

1. ENVIRONMENT

the suction effect of high winds, and the air pressure differential between the interior and exterior that occurs with high
wind speeds. Thus there is a need to add weight to a roof to
resist the lifting effect of wind forces. Tiled roofs in cycloneprone areas of India often have bands of cement at about 3 m
(10 ft) centres thatrun upand down therooffrom eaves to ridge
to eaves.

An improvised alternative that is often applied is to tie ropes
overtheroofatthe outsetofthe cyclone season with large rocks
tied ateither end ofthe ropes. In fishing communities an adaptation ofthis pattern is to tie a fishing net over the entire roofof
a dwelling with stones inserted around the perimeter. On the
Aran islands offthe west coast ofIreland, which are exposed to
severe Atlantic gales, ropes with attached weights are a permanent feature ofthatched roofs, butto give additional protection
vertical ropes are horizontally connected.

From the end of the 19705 there has been significant
progress in the ability to identify cyclones, map their progress,
forecast when they will affect a coastline and issue accurate
warnings. Such warnings are useful in areas with cyclone shel-

160

ters. In areas subject to severe surge flooding there isalong tradition ofshelter building which has now entered the vernacular
tradition in India and Bangladesh. Cyclone shelters are robust
structures. Built on higher ground above surge flood levels,
they can temporarily accommodate large populations of evacuated families for the duration ofa cyclone. They have a normal
function as clinics, schools or places to serve community activities. Before specific structures were built for this purpose,
schools, churches, mosques and temples were often used by
families seeking shelter. IAN DAVIS

1.11.3.g _Volcano

Volcanoes are vents in the crust ofthe Earth through
which molten rock is extruded as lava or ejected as ash or
coarser debris, sometimes accompanied by steam, and hotand
dangerous gases (Davis and Gupta). Associated hazards
include earthquakes, tsunamis, lahars (hot flows of volcanic
debris mixed with water) and rock slides.

The 17 most severe volcanic eruptions of this century have
resulted in a total of75 735 deaths, with the most catastrophic
eruption occurring in Montagne Pelee in Martinique in 1902
when 29.000 were killed, and the second most severe event
beingthe eruption ofNevado del Ruiz in Colombia in 1985 with
the loss of 23 000 lives. The remaining 15 volcanic eruptions
averaged 1582 deaths per event (Muir wood).

Between 1980 and 1985, eight volcanic eruptions were
reported which resulted in the loss of23 773 lives (an average of
1.6 eruptions causing 4754 deaths per annum). However, this is
a distorted figure owing to the high death toll in Nevado del
Ruiz which was the major event in this five-year period. Amore
accurate average figure can be obtained by examining the
statistics between 1968 and 1992 when deaths per annum
averaged 100g (Baxter).

Damage to buildings as a result of volcanic eruptions
includes collapse due to the weight of ash, burial, inundation,
flooding, ignition, beingtransported on a lava flow, foundation
failure, undermining, erosion and the impact of projectiles
(Blong). S .

Volcanic areas attract people who make a trade-off between
the long-term risks they face against short-term economic
benefits from enhanced crop yields. In the immediate vicinity
ofan active volcano the risks are lava flows, hot gases, projectiles, ash falls and lahars. In the path of pyroclastic mud or lava
flows complete destruction of everything in its path should be
expected including vegetation, agricultural land, settlements
and all structures such as roads and bridges.

Volcanic risks are faced by the entire population of a highrisk zone regardless oftheir type or location ofdwelling. Therefore limited ways to reduce risks have concentrated on a series
of approaches involving warning systems, evacuation planning, siting of settlements away from known lava flow routes
and heat-resistant construction.

Most traditional societies living near volcanoes have developed their own local wisdom concerning an impending eruption and the need to evacuate. This knowledge includes an
acute sensitivity to seismic foreshocks, noting a sudden influx
of dead fish in lakes or rivers adjacent to the volcano as well as

Hurricane damage

Natural Disasters 1.1.3

observation ofunusual animal behaviour. This knowledge also
extends to their community evacuation plans. In addition, volcanologists have developed increasingly sophisticated warning
systems which resulted in a very accurate warning before the
eruption of Mt Pinatubo in the Philippines in ıg9r, which
undoubtedly saved thousands oflives.

Following volcanic eruptions in Costa Rica, the Philippines
and Nicaragua, builders replace thatch roofing, which has
often ignited from hot ashfalls, or has collapsed under its
weight, with a metal sheet roof set on a more robust timber
frame. Therefore the roof design of all buildings in volcanic
areas needs to be fire-resistant, pitched in order to remove
accumulated ash and sufficiently strong to take the very heavy
dead load of accumulated volcanic ash. In Costa Rica metal
sheets are used for external wall covering and as ceiling panels,
to give protection from fire. Such measures are essentially a
repair or reconstruction approach as opposed to pre-eruption
protection measures. IAN DAVIS

161

## S. 31

1.1.4 Population

Study of population size, growth and distribution is
essential to an understanding of human society and settlements. Long historical perspectives are required, since earlier
population and settlement patterns have strongly influenced
those of today and explanation of present and future population trends must be based on understanding of the past
interaction of demographic variables (births, deaths and
migrations).

Demography

World demographic data are deficient in coverage and quality,
their reliability varying considerably between regions and
generally diminishing as we go back in time. The practice of
conducting censuses, begun in the mid-ı8th century in continental Europe, only became institutionalized in European
countries and anumber ofLatin American countries duringthe
ıgth century. The USA conducted its first census in 1790. Even
today the United Nations, estimates of world population are
very approximate, with China a major source of uncertainty.
From around 1700, inferences from socioeconomic information, such as taxation and housing records, along with the limited demographic data available, permit reasonably accurate
estimates ofpopulation size, globally and in major regions. For
earliertimes, population estimates are increasingly conjectural
but documentary and archaeological data of different kinds, if
judiciously combined, are adequate to indicate broad paths of
population growth and decline. Explanation of trends is more
contentious, largely because oftheabsence ofreliable data, and
there is no generally accepted interpretation.

World population growth is determined by only two forces,
births and deaths, with migration as an additional force at
regional and local levels. Accurate information on birth and
death rates is extremely limited until very recent times and for
some parts of the world is still lacking, and demographers
must rely on samples and estimates. Tentative estimates of
mortalityand survival rates in early times can be based on inferences drawn from the demographic features of contemporary
hunter-gatherers and, for example, from analysis of ages on
tombstones in the Graeco-Roman world or anatomical study of
skeletal remains in neolithic and Bronze Age communal tombs
in western Europe. Evidence suggests that until the industrial
era death rates were normally high owing to multiple factors,
such as food shortages, epidemic diseases and confliet, with
50 per cent ofthe population dying in their teens. Long-term
survival of communities could only be ensured by high fertility
and this was generally encouraged by social and religious
institutions.

162

There is some evidence that the low, relatively stable population densities characteristic of mobile hunter-gatherer groups
and some shifting cultivators which prevented undue pressure
on environmental resources were partly owing to cultural
checks, such as infanticide or birth control, and not solely to
high mortality. However, with the growing density and nucleation of population that accompanied the introduction and
intensification of agriculture, and especially with civilization
and crowding of populations in large cities, health conditions
changed substantially, serious epidemics increased and, in
response to rising death rates, cultural restraints on fertility
may have been relaxed. Prehistoric acceleration of population
growth after the adopting offarming and sedentism mightthus
be partly explained by fertility increases. Cities invariably
required a steady compensating stream of migrants from highfertility rural areas to offset their high mortality rates.

Each major civilized region developed its own peculiar mix of
infections to which the population of its crowded cities and
densely peopled agricultural hinterlands in time developed
some resistance. These populations were biologically dangerous to neighbours who lacked any acquired immunity to their
infections and, as cultural and commercial contacts increased
in the modern world, devastating infections spread from one
‘disease pool’ to another, affecting in particular long-isolated
peoples brought into sudden contact with Europeans.

World population in the middle palaeolithic era, approximately ı million years ago, may have been around ı million;
humans were then largely confined to the tropical and lowmiddle latitudes of Africa and Asia and everywhere organized
in small mobile groups or ‘bands’ dependent on a foraging
economy. By the beginning of the neolithic era, some 10000
years ago, extensive migrations had dispersed hunting groups

Growth of human
numbers overtheß!
half-million yearsı

throughout Africa, Europe, Asia and the Americas but still
world population is unlikely to have exceeded ıo million.
Global expansion was achieved by cultural adaptation rather
than biological adjustment, through, for example, the invention of different sorts of clothing and shelter which created
microenvironments suitable to the survival of an essentially
tropical creature under widely varying conditions. Through
cultural adaptations humans moved into zones extending well
beyond those occupied by the several species of non-human
primates.

Agriculture, society and settlement

Population growth was probably implicated in the gradual
transition from foraging to a more intensive farming or foodproducing economy, although whether it was a primary cause
is controversial. Once farming spread, human numbers grew
significantly, population densities rose in some areas to levels
several hundred times greater than those of pre-agricultural
times, sedentism and larger settlements developed, material
culture expanded, and profound environmental modifications
began, mostnotably the clearance offorests for animal grazing
and crop growth. The roots ofhistoric agrarian systems, settlement patterns and vernacular building traditions are often
traceable to these early formative phases of agricultural settlement and ecological adjustment. Thus, as early as the sth
millennium sc fundamental and long-enduring differences in
agricultural traditions had emerged in China between the
rice-growing south with a wide use ofirrigation and the wheator millet-growing north with dryland agriculture.

Originating roughly contemporaneously in various nuclear
zones in Asia and Meso-America, the agricultural way of life
involving domesticated plants and animals was gradually dispersed widely in the world either through population movements or through the diffusion of ideas, or both. Peasant
cultivators came to dominate most of the fertile areas of the
world and hunter-gatherers were increasingly confined to marginal locations unattractive to cultivators, ranging from the
northern circumpolar zones to tropical rain forest and desert
margins.

Compact villages early became the characteristic settlement
form of most agricultural societies, although dispersed farm
settlement is perhaps indigenous in some areas, mainly
uplands where arable land is scarce and fragmented. Eventually, agricultural intensification, population increase and the
emergence of increasing sociopolitical complexity led to
growth in the number and size of settlements and distinctive
settlement hierarchies appear. Sedentism led to more substantial and durable dwellings and some tendency to adopt
rectangular forms in place ofthe round forms common in simpler societies. Growing complexity and differentiation were
evident, too, within individual settlements and buildings.
Functionally specialized buildings were introduced; houses
were initially uniform but variations in size and type subsequently appeared and individual houses showed more internal
subdivisions with specialized use. Fixed spatial organization in
settlement layout also grew, reflecting the need for regulation
in enlarged communities and an increasing emphasis on land

Population 1.1.4

and property in contrast to the design flexibility of small-scale
hunter-gatherer settlements. In stillmore complex groups with
marked social hierarchies, settlement organization also
reflects privileged placements of buildings and authoritarian
decisions by various leaders and secular and religious institutions.

Regionalism inevitably grew with agriculture and sedentism.
Distinctive agricultural systems emerged, each adjusted to
local environments and demographic pressures, of varying
productivity and settlement permanence and utilizing different
combinations of crops and animals. Extensive, shifting agriculture with impermanent settlements and low population
densities came to characterize many zones oflow-fertility soils,
especially in the tropics; fallowing systems with fixed settlements and intermediate population densities emerged where
soils were more productive; and permanent cultivation and
multi-cropping capable of supporting dense populations
developed in areas of high fertility, often in association with
irrigation systems. Specialized buildings were developed for
local social and economic needs (for residence, crop storage,
livestock, social, ceremonial and defence purposes) and built
forms were sensitively adjusted to local environments through
use oflocal building materials and climatic adaptation.

City states

After 4000 BC the emergence ofcity-centred societies, basedon
more advanced farming on productive river plains and often
aided by irrigation, generated a zone ofhigh population density
from the Mediterranean across the “fertile crescent’ of southwest Asia into northwest India and the valleys ofthe great Chinese rivers. Within these zones a succession of strongly
centralized empires developed: firstly in the Middle East and
subsequently in the north Mediterranean, India and China.
Ancient empires could provide stable conditions for population growth and concentration, but this was not sustained and
the areas were characterized by a sequence of phases of growth
and decline. In America, the Mayan, Aztec and Inca empires
flourished before the European conquests of the ı6th century.
During the first century of European occupation the American
population declined dramatically from c.4o million to c.ı5
million as a result of confliet, extermination and Europeanintroduced diseases.

Improved farming techniques, often developed in the
ancient civilizations, eventually permitted food surpluses to be
obtained from soils of only moderate fertility: urban life could
then be established over wide areas, structuring peasant society
into a mosaic of local, almost self-sufficient communities
superimposed on the basic patterns produced by ecological
adaptations. Small-scale peasant cultivators under centralized
urban control became the most widespread and enduring pattern of society, still surviving widely in countries of the industrializing world although in the process of transformation by
modern industrial forces.

Pastoral nomadism
In contrast to well-peopled agricultural areas were wide semiarid zones in inner Asia and North Africa generally unsuited to

163

## S. 32

1.11 ENVIRONMENT

mixed farming but capable of supporting light populations
dependent on mobile livestock herding. Pastoral nomadism, a
highly specialized symbiosis between humans and a limited
range of gregarious herd animals, was a late offshoot from
mixed agriculture, utilizing and adapting elements of neolithic
technology, especially the knowledge ofanimal domestication.
Tents and other portable shelters used by nomads often suggest evolution from permanent dwellings; conversely, tents
have evolved into huts following sedentarization of nomads in
modern times. Pastoral nomads frequently invaded settled
peasant communities but the two ways of life were generally
interdependent. Indeed, societies might gradually shift
towards one or the other way oflife in response to environmental changes, historical forces and population pressures.

Semi-nomadism developed first, using sheep, goats and the
ass with mat and skin tents. Later, the horse and camel provided more powerful means of transportation and complete
nomadism was possible utilizing sophisticated woollen and
felt tents. A form ofnomadic cattle herding from fixed villages
spread widely on savanna grasslands in sub-Saharan Aftica. In
the circumpolar taiga and tundra belts ofnorthern Eurasia pastoral nomadism based on herding of semi-domesticated reindeer evolved directly from hunting, perhaps through contacts
with cattle-keeping communities to the south. Comparable
cultures in North America were based on caribou hunting not
their domestication. In the New World, fully-fledged nomadic
pastoralism with domesticated animals did not emerge until
after European contacts, although degrees of pastoralism were
found earlier on the Andean plateaux. Since the ı6th century
nomadic pastoralism has generally declined in competition
with expanding sedentary societies. Marked decline has
occurred in the ıgth and 2oth centuries; nomads have become
semi-nomadic and semi-nomads fully sedentary, many forcibly
converted to cattle ranching.

In mountainous areas of Eurasia, transhumance or seasonal
shifts of herdsmen and animals from permanent lowland villages to summer dwellings on upland pastures was a recurrent
feature oftraditional peasant life, surviving now only in limited
areas. Under population pressure, transhumant organization
could be extended or intensified, frequently resulting in permanent settlements on once seasonal sites.

Traditional settlement in the modern world

Since 1700 the spread of European influence and colonization
and, eventually, the growth ofa much enlarged population, ofa
technologically advanced and urbanized society and an international political economy have radically changed the fabric of
traditional societies. Agrarian and pastoralist cultures and their
environmental adaptations have been undermined and
increasingly homogenized, and their long-standing settlement
and dwelling traditions much attenuated or abandoned. It is
unclear how far the diverse building traditions ofthe world will
survive the flux and pressures of population growth and modernization and be successfully adapted for modern life, or
whether new local traditions will emerge in therapidly growing
cities and towns alongside, and in competition with, standardized international building styles. F.H.A. AALEN

164

Autochthonous

In many parts of the world there are groups who,
because of geographical isolation or social structure, have
retaineda culture and way oflife thatare different from those of
the predominant people of the region. Unlike other ethnic
minorities they are notimmigrants but the original inhabitants
of lands that today form the territories of nation states. Such
groups are usually presumed to form the oldest ethnological
sector of the nation and are often defined as “indigenous’ or
‘native’ in legal and administrative provisions. They include,
for example, the Indians and Inuit of North America, the Indians ofCentral and South America, the Sami ofScandinavia, the
Ainu ofJapan, diverse tribal groups in India, southeast Asiaand
Indonesia, aborigines of Australia and various indigenous
minorities within the former USSR.

Despite the variety among indigenous peoples some generalizations may be briefly made. Characteristically they are distinct in their physical appearance as well as their language and
religion, are organized in bands or tribes with low overall population densities, inhabit wide areas of forests, mountains,
tundra and other inaccessible territory and have traditionally
depended economically on huntingand gathering, subsistence
shifting agriculture or pastoral nomadism. Cultural and commercial contacts with the remainder ofthe population vary considerably from group to group but are generally limited.

By and large, indigenous peoples are politically weak, economically marginal and culturally stigmatized members ofthe
national societies encompassing them. However, modern economic and social developments have often reduced social and
cultural isolation and encouraged acculturation and assimilation; boundaries have become blurred through miscegenation,

bilingualism and borrowing, so that it is increasingly difficult
to distinguish aboriginal from other elements of the population either physically or culturally. Reliable demographic and
other socioeconomic data are often lacking, but there are perhaps roughly 200 million indigenous peoples in the world,
some 3-4 per cent ofthetotal population.

In the Americas, the indigenous peoples are the Amerindians who originally inhabited the land at the time of European
conquest; although subsequently they have been widely supplanted by European settlers, they survive in areas of North,
Central and South America. However, marginalized indigenous groups are not a product only of European colonization
but exist within regions characterized by large-scale stability
and continuity of their populations and cultural traditions.
They are found, for example, in India, Southeast Asia and
Indonesia where the Europeans have come and gone, introducing certain cultural and technological changes but no substantial colonial population. Indigenous groups here are located
within larger populations of very long standing and there is
uncertainty and controversy about the origins and relative
antiquity ofthe two elements.

Indigenous societies have been subjected to government
policies ranging from genocide to segregation or forced assimilation butit is transformation oftheir traditional economy and
environment which has most seriously undermined them.
Their lands have been encroached on, and often appropriated
without compensation, for national development projects
involving, for example, agricultural expansion, irrigation
schemes, dams, highways, mining and timber extraction. Dispossessed indigenous peoples are then often drawn into the
national economy as cheap, often migrant, labour. In America,
Africa and Asia, growing population and economic pressure
has forced tribal people to move from shifting cultivation to
more intensive methods of farming and to produce commodities for the market. Sedentarization of pastoral nomads is
official policy in many countries, whose governments regard
them as an embarrassing symptom of backwardness. Hunter-

Population 1.1.4
gatherers have been markedly reduced. Surviving groups are
found in extremely varied environments, generally harsh and
remote, which, however, may not always be their traditional
habitats but recent refuges into which they have been forced,
initially by pressure of competition from expanding farming
communities and, later, by colonial conquests or modern economic developments. First is the tropical rain forest; in equatorial Africa it is the habitat of the pygmies, and in India and
southeast Asia the home of a number of widely separated
groups includingthe Semang of Malaya and the Kubu in Sumatra. Hunting communities also occur in hot, arid areas, such as
the San or Bushmen ofthe African Kalahari and Botswana, and
the aboriginal inhabitants of northern and western Australia.
The Eskimo and other hunting peoples are associated with the
North American sub-Arctic tundra and forests, while hunters
and reindeer herders, including the Samoyed, Ostiak and Tungus, inhabit comparable habitats in northern Asia.

F. H. A. AALEN
1.111.4.b Distribution and density
Outstanding characteristics of human populations
are their widespread distribution and extreme unevenness.
Colonization of every major ecological zone is attributable to
the possession of culture (social organization and technology)
which can be endlessly adapted to a wide range ofenvironmental conditions. Widespread expansion was achieved in early
prehistoric times and the boundaries of the habitable world
have remained relatively stable for thousands ofyears; the main
tendency of population has been to intensify within already
populated zones especially those favourable to intensive agriculture.

Mankind lives in denser concentrations than other apes and
primates. Thus the average density ofworld population (39 per
sq km in 1990) is not a very useful figure, since approximately
halfofthe population is contained within 5 per cent oftheland
area and 60 per cent ofthe land area contains less than 5 per
cent of the population. There is no easy explanation of these
great density variations which are influenced by a wide range of
environmental, cultural and historical factors. High densities
are notsimply correlated with levels of economic development
but occur in regions of simple subsistence farming (China and
India) and in countries with advanced technical civilizations
such as in northwest Europe. Moreover, high densities occur in
highly contrasted environments, and environmental influences
are clearly not immutable or fixed but relative to culture; their
significance changes when culture changes and zones attractive to high culture may be adverse to primitive and vice versa.
Demographie trends and migration continue to change the relative number of people in various regions.

Areas ofdense population
These vary greatly in character but have certain features in common such as association with large riverine lowlands and
advanced systems ofpermanent cultivation emphasizingcereal
production.

The most extensive concentrations of dense population are
in, first, Europe and the eastern United States and, second,

165

## S. 33

© Lindsay asquith

1.ıll ENVIRONMENT

monsoon Asia: together they contain approximately threequarters ofthe world population. Although they are separated
by the Atlantic, close cultural and economic linkages justify
treatment of Europe and the eastern United States as a unit.
Outstanding features are the high degree ofindustrial development and associated urbanization which have developed in
recent centuries from a basis ofintensive commercialized agriculture, and the dependence on vast trade contacts. In the
ıgth century, Europe was a great centre of emigration, Populating new nations in America, Africa and Australasia which
have grown rapidly owing to immigration and high natural
increase.

The great population concentration in Asia has grown enormouslyin the 2oth century but, unlike Europe, is along-standing
feature ofthe population map. A further contrast with Europe is
dependence on intensive subsistence agriculture. Wide plains
and fertile deltas along with plentiful rain from the summer
monsoon provide a highly nourishing plant environment and
irrigation allows multi-cropping. The prolific rice crop is the
main foodstuff and, traditionally, little meat or milk was consumed. Japan is exceptional in Asia in following the European
pattern of intensive agriculture and large-scale industry. However, industrial societies are emerging elsewhere in the region
with numerous, rapidly growing, large cities.

Sparsely inhabited and uninhabited areas

These include diverse regions. The most conspicuous blank
spaces are the great hot and cold deserts where vegetation is

166

absent or sparse: savanna grasslands and tropical forests, polar
ice-caps, and the northern tundra and coniferous belt.
Population usually becomes sparser in mountains but in
colder regions sparseness begins at much lower levels and in
the tropics elevated areas have often attracted population. In
semi-arid areas pastoral economies occur with animals exceeding humans in number. This is characteristic of traditional
societies, as in the Mzab, and of areas with advanced commercial farming such as the semi-arid outback of Australia.
F.H. A. AALEN

Growth

World population has grown markedly in the last
three hundred years, multiplying approximately eight times,
and growth has taken place atincreasingly rapid rates. Itis estimated thatworld population was under 700 million in 1700 but
in 1990 totalled about 5300 million.

Throughout most of human history population has been
characterized by a precarious balance between high levels of
fertility and mortality which permitted only slow, fluctuating
growth. Modern population increase reflects a decisive shift
away from traditional regimes initiated essentially bya marked
decline in mortality rather than increased fertility. In most
developed countries there has been a characteristic sequence of
changes in vital rates, the so-called ‘demographic transition’,
from a pre-modern regime of high mortality and fertility to a
regime of low mortality and fertility. The precipitating factor
was a fall in mortality: fertility remained high and the widening
gap between rates produced a ‘population explosion’. Eventually birth rates declined, death rates stabilized and population
growth was moderated. In Europe the demographic transition
broadly accompanied long-term change from a largely rural,
agrarian society to an urban, industrial one, although the
precise relationship between population trends and the multiple social and economic developments which constitute
modernization are complex and incompletely understood. The
early phase of growth from c. 1750 to 1850, just preceding the
industrial revolution, is particularly contentious, and contemporaneous upsurges in population occurred in China and
various other agrarian societies where industrialization was
feeble and long-delayed. Several key interacting processes
appear to have influenced death rates downwards, amongthem
improved nutrition (which may have preceded large-scale
industrialization), medical and public health developments,
and autonomous declines in the virulence of certain diseases.
The relative importance of these processes varied spatially
and in time and there was consequently considerable variety
in demographic experience between and within western

1.111.4.c

nations.

In the West mortality decline preceded fertility decline by
abouta century. Movements ofbirth rates are subject to acomplex interplay of many forces, cultural and economic, butthere
seems to be a close link between lasting curtailment of fertility
and the substantial improvements in material living standards
associated with high levels of industrialization and urbanization. When living standards had sufficiently improved, fertility
slowly adjusted to lowered mortality.

housing, gy;
South China,

Whether and how a western-type demographic transition will
occur widely in the industrializing world is a crucial issue. A
marked fall in death rates began there around the mid-2oth century which was notalways followed bythemodernization changes
which western experience indicated were necessarytoreducehigh
fertility. There was a tendency to attribute mortality declines to
imported advanced preventive medicine rather than economic
development. Whatever the reasons, death rates have fallen faster
than they ever did in western Europe where knowledge of death
control was a slow indigenous development and only gradually
applied. This lies behind the violent contemporary population
explosion in the industrially underdeveloped countties.

Global growth rates appear to have peaked in the quinquennium 1965-70 and there is now evidence ofwidespread fertility
declines in the less-developed world characterized by considerable geographical variability. The most appreciable falls are in
the more industrialized parts, South Korea and Taiwan for
example, and in urban rather than rural areas. They are occur-

ring too in relatively poor and dominantly agrarian countries
which have strong policies of fertility regulation such as China,
Thailand and Indonesia, suggesting that European experience
may not be a reliable guide to what will happen elsewhere.,
Despite moderation of growth rates, the present size and
youthfulness of the world’s population makes further rapid
increase a certainty and in poor countries the need for large
families to provide a domestic labour force will discourage fertility limitation. Global population in AD 2020 is likely to
exceed 8 billion; and regional shares in growth will continue to
be highly unequal and regional differences sharpened, with,
for example, Africa’s share ofthe world’s population growing
while Europe’s share falls.

Fertility levels no longer divide developed from less-developed
countries so consistentlyand completely as hitherto, butregional
variations in demographic conditions are nevertheless just as
striking and significant as rapid world growth of population. In
Africa, for example, birth rates in the period 1985-90 were 45 per
1000, death rates 15, and annual rates of increase 3 per cent,
implying a doubling time of 24 years. Europe (excluding the
USSR) on the other hand, had birth rates (13) and death rates (ır)
almostin balance, with annualrates ofincreaseato.2 percentand

adoubling time of 600 years. F.H.A. AALEN

## S. 34

1. ENVIRONMENT
1.11.4.d Migration

Large-scale population movements are evident

throughout prehistory and history but their complex causation

limits the validity of broad generalizations on why they occur.

Movement has often been linked to food dearths caused by

population growth or environmental changes largely indepen-

dent of man such as the periodic desiccations in central Asia.

People in poor hill or steppe environments frequently raided

and eventually settled in adjoining cultivated lowlands.

A search for freedom from social or religious bondage has
resulted in large-scale migrations, such as the trekking of
South African Boers or the exodus of Hebrew bondsmen from
Egypt. Religious and missionary enthusiasms may also stimulate population shifts, as seen in the history of Islam. Abrupt
movements of whole peoples are often less important than
gradual, long-term infiltration such as has characterized Chinese movement into Southeast Asia and Indonesia, for example, or the continuing emigration from Europe to America in
modern times. The direct demographic significance ofeven the
largest migrations has been marginal in comparison to that of
natural increase determined by the balance of births and
deaths.

The cultural consequences of migrations have been important. Immigrant peoples may bring culture and language
permanently affecting the original populations although, ifthe
latter survive in sufficient numbers, their culture can sometimes reassert itself and become predominant once more.
Evidence ofmigrations in preliterate times is to be found in folk
traditions, archaeological remains, racial characteristics, customs and language. Traditional housetypes too provide
significant clues to cultural history and dispersals.

Early migration flows were often channelled by geographical
conditions, impeded by mountain barriers or densely populated, well-defended territories, for example, or facilitated by
river valleys and forest-free open country. Steppes impeded
agriculturalists but were natural avenues for pastoralists. In
Africa, disease and insect pests such as the tsetse fly have been
barriers to migration and settlement. Lowered sea levels during
the last major glaciation shortened sea crossings for early
migrants from Asia into North America and Australia, while
the retreat ofthe ice masses freed extensive northern territories
for migrant hunting communities who greatly extended the
inhabited area ofthe earth.

Long-distance migrations have been an outstanding feature of
the world’s modern population geography, carried outmainly by
Europeans, or at their instigation in the case ofthe transportation of Africans as slaves to the New World or movements of
indentured labour from India and China. Starting from the end
of the ısth century, after the discovery of the New World and
circum-Africa routes to the Orient, almost the whole world was
brought under the economic suzerainty of western Europe.
Equally important was a massive growth of European population
and large-scale dispersal of some 70 million Europeans.

Outflows were initially modest but in the ıgth century mass
emigration commenced, mainly into sparsely populated ‘new
lands’ such as North America and Australia where pre-existing
peoples and cultures were pushed aside or annihilated and

168

where culture and the bulk ofthe population are today almost
wholly of European derivation. Hybrid and plural cultures also
became a striking feature ofthe world. Some areas, including
sub-Saharan Africa, came under European political or economie control butnewcomers only partially replaced indigenes
whose culture has survived, although it is increasingly westernized. In other regions, such as Meso- and South America, the
European and aboriginal population lived alongside each other
orinterbred and new cultures evolved in which European practices were dominant. Russian expansion eastwards into Asia
commenced in the 16th century, gainingmomentum in the gth
century and reaching massive proportions with the building
of the trans-Siberian railway in the 1890s. Cultural impacts
on indigenous peoples in central Asia and Siberia have been
profound. China and Japan, while resisting European colonization, nevertheless attempted to emulate the European model.
Large-scale Chinese expansion northwards into Manchuria
occurred in the early-2oth century.

Although European emigration was unevenly dispersed
there was everywhere some infusion of European technology
and ideas. European medical and other techniques of death
control were widely exported and adopted, but the European
pattern of family limitation was less readily accepted, resulting
in large population increases.

European emigration reached its peak in the ıgth and 2oth
century and is still significant even if much reduced. Immigration into Australia and North America is still mainly from
Europe although supplemented from Latin America and Southeast Asia. Important migratory streams, predominantly determined by differential employment opportunities, now also run
from countries of the south towards the north and between
countries ofthe south. Since the late 1950s, western Europe has
become a major goal of immigrants mainly from former English, French and Dutch colonies and from the Mediterranean
basin. 16 million foreigners officially resided there by the late
1980s plus some 2 million clandestines, producing a new,
strong cultural diversity. This pattern, however, could again
change considerably with movement from eastern Europe.
Migration into the oil states of the Middle East from poor
surrounding countries (Egypt, Yemen, Pakistan and India) is
also significant. F. H.A. AALEN
Urbanization
Cities and towns have grown prodigiously in
modern times, especially since the Second World War. In 1700
the world was largely agrarian with less than xo per cent ofthe
population living in urban areas, only five cities exceeding a
population of 500.000 and no city with over ı million inhabitants. As late as 1900 only 16 cities exceeded ı million but since
1950 their number has increased rapidly; around 400 now
exceed the million mark and they contain nearly goo million
people. Moreover, many of these large cities have grown in
industrializing countries. With increasing dependence on
industrial and service employment, urban agglomerations have
emerged as the dominating force in the organization of the
human population.

Trends in urbanization vary regionally, with important con-

1.11.4.e

r

see also

1.11.20 Westernizallil

3.V Latin America
3.VI North America
3.v11.10-A South ei
3.v11.11-A Southem)

ers“ dwellings
‚nalighat,

hate

8

trasts between mature industrial countries and developing
countries. In Europe industrialization and urbanization commenced in the late-ı8th century but, fed by rural-urban migration, they increased markedly in the second half of the ıgth
century with a progressive slowing down thereafter. In northwest Europe close on nine-tenths ofthe population lived in the
countryside in the mid-ı8th century while today four-fifths live
in urban centres. Rates of migration to the cities are now feeble
largely because the reduced rural population has no large
resources of migrants.

In the developing world, rapid industrialization and urbanization started later (mainly after the Second World War) but is
now proceeding faster than was ever achieved in the western
world; while migration from the countıyside is an important
contributory factor, some 80 per cent of growth is attributable
to high natural increase of the urban population produced by
high fertility and falling death rates. Cityward migration is particularly intense in the lightlyurbanized countries of Africaand
the Middle East. China, however, although lightly urbanized
has relatively low rates ofrural-urban migration.

Most ofthe world’s cities with populations exceeding ı million and its fastest growing cities now lie in less-developed
countries and one in ten ofthe world’s people live in these settlements. Nevertheless, most less-developed countries still
have a low level of urbanization. In most African and Asian
countries the urban proportion is generally below 25 per cent;
their large rural populations, however, provide an immense
reservoir of future migrants to the cities.

Well-urbanized countries are now located in diverse areas:
these include the western industrial nations with a general level
0f 75-80 per centand cities with extensive suburban zones; the
major petroleum-producing states of the Middle East and several countries in Latin America with levels of 60-75 per cent
and South Africa, various countries in eastern Europe and also
in the Islamic world with levels between 40 and 60 per cent. The
countries with the lowest levels of urbanization are in Africa
and Asia (India, China, Southeast Asia and Indonesia). There is
a tendency for rates of total population growth to be highest
where levels of urbanization are lowest in, for example, the
poorer African nations and the Middle East.

Migration into cities and towns is an influential elementand
cause of modernization and change. It remains unclear, however, whether standardized international building styles will

2000
Developed regions

Developing regions

&
8
Ss

1000

Urban population (millions)
8

1970 1975 1980 1985 1990 1995 2000

Population 1.111.4

whollyreplace local and regional styles in the cities ofthe developing world. Acute urban housing shortages have arisen with
extensive slums in old, overcrowded areas and, more commonly, in new squatter settlements within or on the periphery
ofthe built-up area. Erected by the occupants, dwellings in the
new areas vary with the materials available but are mostly
one-storey, makeshift huts of mud, scrap lumber and metal.
Such ‘spontaneous settlements’ have become a major urban
form. So formidable are the housing problems that ideas of
wholesale slum clearance and the erection of costly improved
housing have been replaced by increasing emphasis on the positive aspects of spontaneous settlements and their gradual
improvement. The precise nature ofhousing policy will thus be
determined by local circumstances, and hence some diversity
of building styles, perhaps flavoured by local tradition, could
survive and develop. Moreover, many city migrants retain roots
in their rural community-a widespread pattern in sub-Saharan
Africa, much of Asia and the Pacific. Hence, Third World cities
are ‘an agglomeration of folk societies’, more rural and traditional in their psychological and sociological characteristics
than those of the west and often characterized by tribal and
village segregation. This spatial grouping slows down the rate
ofacculturation and helps to account for the survival ofindigenous culture patterns close to the metropolis; migrants shape
the culture of the cities as much as they are adjusting to it.
Indigenous rural-type buildings are thus characteristically
interspersed with western building forms, as in the villages
(kampungs) of Southeast Asia or the registered slums (bustees) of

Calcutta. F. H. A. AALEN

1861 ano nee

## S. 35

1.111.5 Settlement

Research that is explicitly focused on settlement
Patterns is concerned with understanding and explaining the
relationships between the static patterns ofsettlementthat can
be observed atany onetime, and the dynamic spatial process of
human behaviour that results in those patterns. Geographers,
cultural anthropologists and sociologists study the ongoing
economic and social processes of a society to discover and
model the locational decisions and use of space that follow
behaviour, while archaeologists study the patterned distribution ofremnant settlements and other features in order to infer
past human behaviour. Regardless of the directionality of a
particular study, settlement pattern research recognizes a number ofeconomic, social and symbolic aspects of human behavjour that are expressed in the patterned use of space at scales
ranging from the individual structure to the region.

The physical content of space consists of natural resources
and the built environment of structures, roads and other facilities. These natural and built features can be summarized as the
basic geometric forms of points, lines and areas to which
attributes of content can be assigned. All spatial relationships
among basic geometric forms can be expressed bythe concepts
of direction, distance and relative location. The most basic

process that structures human spatial behaviour is interaction _

in space and through time. Settlement pattern research seeks to
define the social, economic and symbolic behaviours that facilitate or inhibit interaction among humans, and between
humans and the physical environment.

Spatial interaction refers to the movement of people, goods
and information across space and through time. While consideration ofthe frictional effects ofdistance as expressed by travel
and transportation costs is the primary concern of most formal geographic models of economic interaction, social and
symbolic consideration ofdistance may be better defined by the
concept ofaccessibility rather than simple spatial proximity. At
the scale of the individual dwelling, or even the settlement,
spatial distance is often a secondary consideration after social
distance. At the local scale, interaction is structured primarily
by accessibility as defined by kinship, gender, friendship,
socioeconomic status, ethnicity and religion. As the scale of
interaction increases, so does the significance of economic
costs related to spatial distance, although cultural factors certainly do not disappear.

Point pattern distributions

The distribution of dwellings within a settlement, or of settlements within a region, can easily be conceived ofas a scatter of
points across the landscape at varying scales of resolution. At

170

either the settlement or theregional scale, these point distributions may be characterized by patterns ofrelative location ranging from random to either clustered or dispersed. These
patterns lend themselves to some basic behavioural interpretations.

A clustered settlement pattern implies some element of
attraction among the settlement units, or between settlement
units and some restricted resource. A clustered settlement may
be focused upon a restricted resource such as a waterhole, or
may facilitate organization and co-ordination of task groups
involved with communal activities, such as the maintenance
and operation of a complex irrigation system. A tendency
towards settlement dispersion indicates repulsion among settlement units resulting from competition, often over land available to individual households orvillages. A variety of statistical
methods are available for measuring the degree of either clustering or dispersion ofsettlements as compared to a theoretical
‘null hypothesis’ ofa random distribution as might be expected
by change alone (Boots and Getis).

The geographer Hudson has proposed a locational theory
for rural settlement pattern change through time that identifies
three successive phases of settlement pattern developmentand
the patterns associated with each. Colonization is the first of
these phases by which a population expands into a region
resulting in a random settlement distribution; spread then
oceurs, resulting in a clustered pattern as settlements increase
in number and disperse short distances. This is followed by
competition, the process that results in a regular spacing of
settlements as increasing numbers ofrural settlers compete for
land. Hudson successfully tested the validity of the theory on
changing settlement distributions in Iowa between 1870 and
1960.

As settlement patterns develop in aregion, so do hierarchies
of settlements. Hamlets, villages, towns and cities differing in
size and character develop and grow in response to settlements
that surround them. Geographers have described an empirical
regularity displayed among settlements within a region that
have been rank-ordered according to population size. Accordingto the ‘rank-size rule’, the population ofa particular ranked
settlement can be predicted by knowing the population size of
the largest settlement in the region. Thus, second-ranked
settlement should have a population one-half the size of the
first-ranked, while the tenth-ranked settlement should have a
population one-tenth the size ofthe first-ranked. A settlement

size distribution that follows the rank-size rule is generally
interpreted as representing a well integrated regional economic system, while distributions dominated by an overly large

r ofcentral

ö

iket centres in

primate city suggest hypercentralization. Distributions with a
multiplicity of mid-sized settlements indicate low system integration. Economic and political change through time has also
been investigated through application ofthe rank-size rule for
regions such as the United States, Israel, and England and
Wales.

The spatial implications of regional settlement hierarchies
are addressed by central place theory, as developed by German
economic geographers, Walter Christaller and August Lösch.

2
/ Central market town and
Fi marketplace
/ Intermediate market town
/ & Peripheral market town
/[ &- us, o Indian bulking center
un

1. Quezaltenango
2. San Francisco el Alto

Settlement 1.1115

Central place theory offers an explanation for the relative location ofurban places that vary in size and in the number ofsupport activities offered to people living in the surrounding rural
area. The relative spacing of urban places is determined by the
‘range’, or maximum distance a consumer is willing to travel in
order to obtain a particular good or service, and the ‘threshold’,
or minimum number of prospective customers a supplier
needs in order for a business to remain profitable. Urban places
offering goods and services with greater ranges will be spaced
at relatively large intervals in order to maintain threshold levels
of customers, while places offering goods and services with
shorter ranges must be spaced at relatively short intervals in
order to draw sufficient numbers of customers from the rural
areas surrounding them. While the classical formulation of
central place theory assumes the fixed location of businesses
and the movement of people to and from central places, many
researchers have adapted the basic theoretical framework used

in studies of periodic trade and the circulation of retailers in
societies that lack permanent markets. Skinner’s application of
central place theory for the study ofmarketing and social struc-

ture in rural China prior to 1949 integrates the classical formu-

lation ofthe theory and a consideration of periodic marketing.

Constructive critiques and resulting modifications of central

place theory (Smith; King) have demonstrated that the general

principles of the theory are applicable to the interpretation of
regional settlement hierarchies under a variety of social and

cultural settings, from the ancient Middle Eastand Mesoamer-

ica to modern industrialized nations.

Nodes within a route network

An alternative approach to settlement pattern studies is to
focus on the networks that are formed by settlements and their
interconnecting transportation and communication corridors
such as trails, roads and streams. The study of routes among
settlements can provide many insights into patterns of social
and economic interactions. The same set of settlements can be
joined by potential routes ranging from maximum connectivity
to maximum centrality, with many possible intermediary solutions. Natural corridors, such as streams, can structure the
physical pattern of settlement as well as the hierarchical relationships among those settlements. The construction oftransportation routes will also shape the subsequent development of
regional settlement, often resulting in linear settlement patterns. The network of routes that develop among settlements
generally reflects the settlement hierarchy ofa region through
both the pattern and capacity oftransportation corridors. Such
networks can be used as alternative or supplementary sources
of data in the application of central place theory.

Settlements associated with areas

In societies based upon an agricultural economy, the distribution ofland resources and the spatial structure of landuse are
significant factors in settlement pattern. The most commonly
recognized goal in decisions relating to settlement location
and cultivation pattern is the optimization of returns for the
amount of labour invested in agriculture. Economists refer to
the factors that influence differential returns to agricultural

171

## S. 36

1.11

ENVIRONMENT

Asmall city
with its own
production zones

Horticulture and dairying - no fixed
rotation
Sylvieulture
GZZR Intensive arable rotation
N) R
NIS Araıe with long ley

EI 1tree-tieid arabıe
un

Stock farming and industrial crops

@ central city
. Navigable river

labour as “fertility differentials’ and ‘locational differentials’.
The theoretical foundations for these two complementary perspectives on agricultural land use patterns can be found in the
works of David Ricardo (1772-1823) and Johann Heinrich von
Thünen (1783-1850). Ricardo emphasized the economics of
natural variability in land fertility, while von Thünen concentrated on locational economics as related to travel and transportation costs.

Ricardo is credited with formalizing the observation that
natural variability in the productivity of land resources will
affect the returns to labour and, thus, the pattern of settlement
and land use. According to Ricardo, the more fertile the land,
the more intense will be its use, because of more favourable
returns in crop yields per unit of labour invested. Extending
this to a temporal sequence, Ricardo reasoned that the more
fertile land would first be cultivated, followed by land of successively poorer quality as demand for agricultural production
increased.

In contrast to Ricardo, von Thünen held land quality constantand modelled land use according to spatial relationships.

Von Thünen developed his original models from empirical
observations oflocalized landuse as integrated with an isolated
market town. He recognized that his basic principles are
applicable not only to the localized marketing system but also
to the smaller scale ofthe farming household and to the larger
scale ofregional marketing. Von Thünen’s models would predict that farming plots located in close proximity to a settlement (residence, village or market town) would be more
intensively cultivated or would be used to produce crops with
high transportation costs, while more distant plots would be

172

cultivated less intensively or be used for production of crops
requiring less attention, in general, and lower transportation
costs. Numerous case studies of labour-based economies suggestthatagricultural plots, including those not intensively cultivated, tend to be located within ı km (0.6 mi) ofthe residence
and that the frictional effects of distance increase dramatically
beyond that. The farthest non-intensively cultivated plots are
rarely located more than 4-5 km (2.5-3 mi) distant (Chisholm,
PP. 35-59). Von Thünen, like Ricardo, recognized thattherealworld landscape does notofferan undifferentiated plain ofuniform land resources. He feltthatthe main spatial relationships
between labour input and land use around a settlement would
nevertheless obtain, although with adjustments in accordance
with variability in land quality and available transportation corridors. The obverse of von Thünen’s approach, which would
also incorporate Ricardo’s notions, is to determine the optimal
location ofthe settlement in relation to the available resources.
Farming settlements would be expected to locate in optimal
relation to prime agricultural lands, where the more intensive
cultivation could be practised. At the same time, cultivation
requiring lower labour inputs would be carried out at greater
distances from the settlement; labour input would be adjusted,
as appropriate, for variability in the quality of surrounding
lands.

This basic model of settlement and land use has long served
as atool for evaluation of settlement patterns and their change
through time. It also serves as a baseline model for assessing
the role of other economic, as well as social and political, factors that might influence decisions relating to settlement location. SCOTT L. FEDICK

Cluster

Ofallterms employed to identify the morphology of
discrete settlements or of related buildings grouped together
within an urban context, ‘cluster’ is the most widely used and
the least defined. Etymologically it is related to ‘clot’ (a coagulated Auid) and ‘clod’ (a lump of matter, particularly earth or
clay). Deriving from the German Kloss (clot) and Kluster, it is
unrelated to ‘cloister’ (from the Latin caustrum), an enclosed
space. The essential nature of the cluster is that it is used to
