Land use arounda f
central Mexlcanfarll )

identify a group of similar objects, fruit, animals, people - or
dwellings. This distinguishes it from the contiguity ofthecompact settlement.

A ccharacteristic ofmany clusters is the similarity ofthe building units to each other when they are disposed together.
Restricted sites for building, single access routes, shortage of
agricultural land or other limiting factors may necessitate their
close proximity. These are typical constraints that influence the
form of clustered villages in the Alpine Valais, and comparable
settlements in many montane environments. In such cases the
buildings have often been constructed by collective local labour
and share the same form, scale and materials.

People who cluster in groups may share a common objective
or gather abouta feature ofcommon interest. Similarly, there is
frequently a focus to the settlement cluster, being nucleated
abouta common facility. Often this is a religious building, but
itmay be aspring, orasmall market-place, as is the case in the
hilltop villages ofthe Correze, south-central France. Nucleated
clusters may signal their character by a church spire, aminaret
or other constructed element rising above the commonality of
the dwellings about it.

Other clusters evolved from the need for security or social
interaction, whether for mutual support and exchange, or for
co-ordinated activity when required. Social factors play a considerable part in the development of settlement clusters; often
specifically associated with lineage and kinship, members of
the same family groups living in close proximity tracing their
former generations to a common ancestor. Exogamous marriage with members of other clans may result in complex networks of social relations and sometimes the differentiation of
moieties within the social structure, which can find physical
expression in the dwelling cluster.

Caste and class may also account for the clustering of
dwellings and their occupants of similar social status. For
example, the camars or members ofthe lowest caste ofa Räjput
village in Uttar Pradesh live in clusters that are peripheral to,
but nonetheless associated with, the clusters ofthe ruling caste
ofthakurs.

Settlement 1.1.5

Many villages and towns may have clusters within them, like
the mahalles in Turkish settlements, often comprising families
related to each other. When the families migrate to the cities it
is often to find a place within an urban mahalle based on blood
relationship (akrabalik) and shared village origin (heserilik).
Urban clusters in many large cities may have a high measure of
ethnic homogeneity, even, as Tyron demonstrated, in the San
Francısco-East Bay region of California.

Some sociologists have proposed economic arguments that
account, not for the form of the cluster itself, but at a larger
scale, for the clustering of small settlements. An early model
was advanced by Kolb who emphasized the clustering of ‘lower
order settlements’ as specialized centres away from a larger settlementand closer to each other, rather than at similar intervals
as in classic central place theory. PAUL OLIVER
1.11,5.b Compact
Compact settlements are those in which the amount

of built-up area is maximized, with buildings that are either
contiguous or nearly so. In many cases the buildings share
party walls, and the ratio of area of private territory to that of
public territory may be quite large, with a high population density.

Compact settlements are characteristic ofcultures in hot, dry
climates where the close packing of buildings reduces the
external surface area thatis exposed to the sun, and putsa good
deal of both private and open public space in shade. Compact
settlements have other origins also. These include security and
defence considerations, as in Moroccan fortified villages
(ksour), or the kastros ofthe Greek islands, and the desire to preserve valuable agricultural land as in Tuscan villages and the
rice-cultivating areas in South and Southeast Asia. Open-field
agricultural systems, in which families work many fields scattered all over the village lands, means that they all put their
houses in the same central location, and the nature of property
division at the time of inheritance may have the effect of
increasing the number of nuclear families that occupy a given
area of land, for example in North African settlements and
towns. Proximity to each other in order to help guarantee the
maintenance ofthe tribal, clan or family organization is also a
factor. In any given case, a compact settlement is likely to bethe
resultofsome combination ofthese reasons, as well as upholding tradition.

Characteristic housetypes in compact settlements include
the courtyard, atrium, linear and hall forms, all ofthese shapes
allowing both the adjacent placing of houses as well as a relatively efficient means of public access to the house. Courtyards
may be found in China, the Middle East and North Africa; in
such cases a house may share as many as four exterior walls
with its neighbours, with access to the entrance gained by only
a narrow alley, or even by the roof. Linear forms may be found
in some Greek island settlements, such as Kastro on Sifnos
where the houses are two rooms deep, share party walls with
two neighbours and are located alonga narrow street thats the
centre of the village. In villages such as Pyrghi, Mesta and
Olympoi on the island of Chios, as well as in other villages in
the Aegean islands, the approximately round plan ofthe settle-

173

## S. 37

© Gregory Brokaw after $ Santell

1.

ENVIRONMENT

Fa

pe n

ment is defined by a defensive wall ofhouses. The roofs traditionally provided the only means of communication among
houses (and now provide the only private outdoor space available within the village walls); access to the houses today is by
means of nartow streets that were gradually spanned with
rooms. In all ofthese cases the street arrangement tends to be
highly efficient, with no more than the minimum length of
street absolutely required for access to house entrances.

Compact settlements may have different origins and modes
of transformation, ranging from planned villages and structures, to those that emerge and grow organically. Compact settlements that grow in an organic manner may have highly
sophisticated rule systems that guide their growth. Akbar and
Hakim point outthatthe traditional Muslim cityin North Africa
is guided by a system of laws, derived from the Koran, that
ensures good neighbourly relations in matters of building and
maintenance. These laws, based on generations of precedents,
atleast partly determine the detailed typology ofthe settlement.
They deal with such matters as responsibility for party walls
and common alleys, width of alleys and streets, rights to rainwater, and matters of privacy as they are affected by adjacent
roofs, windows and doors. Such laws, based on traditional customs, also exist, for example, in compact settlements in China
and the insular Mediterranean, providing a framework within
which people can live together in close proximity.

HOWARD DAVIS

1.11.5.c Compound
In 2oth-century usage a compound refers principally to a composite habitation formed by the enclosure of a
number ofdwelling or other separate units ofaccommodation.
Probably deriving from the Malay kampong, itwas applied in the
17th century to the residences or other properties of Europeans
which were defined by walls. Though the term still refers to the
enclosed residences ofexpatriates, these are seldom vernacular
structures; ‘compound’ is customarily applied to indigenous
household settlements within an enclosing wall, fence or
hedge. Many are to be found in the desertic regions of northwest India, notably the Thar desert of Rajasthan and the
Kachchh (Kutch) ofGujarat. Typically the compound may com-

174

prise one or more circular, mud-plastered and thatched huts
(jhumpas), raised platforms (otlas), clay grain storage bins anda
kitchen shelter setin an open yard. Many have a marked resemblance to the compounds of the savanna grasslands of West
Africa, ofwhich a description follows.

A .compound in rural West Africa usually designates a plural
entity formed by a number of households organized on the
basis of agnatic descent, cultivating separately and/or collectively the family farmland, and residing in the same composite
habitation. The compound houses anything from four to overa
hundred members whose lineage can be traced to amale ancestor (matrilineal communities are also encountered in West
Africa but are not common). Several generations offathers and
sons form this dwelling group, also known as the joint or
extended family. The compound is constituted by several individual dwelling units inhabited by kin households. The sign of
a family’s wealth and prosperity is in the number ofrooms and
dwelling units. Both children and ancestors are commonly
referred to as ‘builders up ofa compound’. The viability ofthe
family depends on both its guardians and its perpetuators.
Ancestral shrines (in the forms of conical earthen pillars or
mounds) are usually set up in front ofthe house portal or atthe
compound entrance. Although the western orientation of the
main entrance frequently encountered in West African
dwellings might respond to the wind, rain and topographical
factors, aTamberma villager explains that ‘the dead people are
in the west, therefore the doors of our house face west so that
they can return there’ (Blier).

The inside of a compound is the space where most of the
women’s activities occur. Even in societies where the patrilinear system dominates, men used to- many still do today-share
the same dwelling unit with their wives. In instances where the
man has more than one wife, he will alternately live with each of
them, but does not necessarily have a separate room ofhis own.
The compound is thus composed ofdwellings units that correspond to thenumber ofwives within the large family. Aslongas
the man still fulfils his role as impregnator, he remains with his
wife (or wives) within the compound. However, when with age
he is no longer in a position to do so, he may decide to move to
a unitssituated adjacent to the main entrance way, with or withouthis senior wife. This site ofcomings and goings is the point
of transition not only between the familial and the external
worlds, between life and after life, but also between the

compact planofa red settlement of

section of Tunis, Nor apesino houses,
Africa, u Rica.

woman’s and the man’s domains. Thus it is highly significant

that the eldest dwell at the threshold looking on to the outer

worlds. JEAN-PAUL BOURDIER

1.11.5.d Dispersed

Patterns of settlement may be differently defined at
various scales or levels ofresolution. Individual structures may
be grouped into tight clusters associated with individual households or kin groups. A village may consist of many dwelling
groups that are dispersed in a very regular pattern, providing
each household with adequate land for dooryard gardens
(home gardens or kitchen gardens) in residential plots. These
villages may in turn be spread randomly across the landscape,
or may cluster within certain land resource zones. Once a level
of analysis has been defined, the pattern that emerges can
provide insights into the basic economic and social processes
thatresulted in it.

Dispersed patterns are those whose trend is away from random and towards a regular spacing, with equal distances
among nearest neighbours. Such patterns of settlement are
generally associated with competition for the content of space
among settlement units, be they dwellings, hamlets, villages,
towns or cities. In agricultural societies, dispersion is associated with competition over cultivatable land resources, whilein
market economies the dispersion of commercial establishments and central places is associated with competition over
potential customers.

When competition over land becomes an important factor in
the distribution of agricultural villages across the landscape,
the distances among settlements will be related to the average
size ofthose settlements. The larger the settlement, the greater
the need for cultivatable land separating settlements. Government planning, in order to mitigate the effects of anticipated
competition, can also result in the regular spacing of settlements. A well known case is the ‘township and range’ system of
the United States, firstadopted by the Congress ofthe Confederation as the Land Ordinance of 1785. The Ordinance called
for cadastral surveys to be conducted in the western territories
in order to facilitate orderly and controlled settlement. The
basic division was the square township of6 mi (ro km) perside,
subdivided into 36 sections of sq mi (640 acres) each. Predetermined sections near the centre of each township were set
aside for public institutions. The result was the development
throughout the ıgth century of.a very regular pattern of towns
across the landscape. Plantations are another form of planned
development that result in dispersed settlements. With their
manor houses they are often spaced at regular intervals, reflectingastandardized land allotment or grant system.

Settlement dispersion at the level of the dwelling group is
generally associated with the cultivation of plots surrounding
homesteads that are worked by individual families. The cultivation ofdooryard gardens, such as those found in Malaysia, Java
and the Maya Lowlands of southern Mexico and Central Amer-

ica, results in a dispersed pattern of settlement in which each
dwelling group is surrounded by an intensively cultivated garden area of approximately 0.5-ı h (5000-10 000 sq m). Intensively cultivated dooryard gardens are often complemented by

Settlement 1.1.5

less intensively cultivated fields located at greater distances
from the dwelling. This ‘infield-outfield’ system can result in
dispersion at two scales: regular spacing of dwelling units
within a village (infields), and a trend from random to regular
spacing among villages as competition for outfield land
increases. While common in tropical areas, infield-outfield
systems also have deep historical roots in temperate regions
such as Ireland and Scotland, where the system is documented
back to the early-r7th century (McCourt).

A change from nucleated to dispersed settlement pattern is
often associated with increasing competition over land
resources. In a study of settlement pattern change among several eastern Nigerian societies, Udo traces the disintegration of
nucleated settlements into dispersed farmsteads. Under
increasing competition for land, resulting from population
increase, the communal land tenure and co-operative work
groups ofvillages have disintegrated into individual landholdings of dispersed family compounds. SCOTT L. FEDICK

1.11.5.e Grid

An organization of streets that lie perpendicular to
one another, the grid is among the most controlled of settlement patterns. The square or rectangular plots which the grid
defines exercise a discipline upon the form of buildings within
its compass. Though the imposition ofits formal structure has
been frequently for military and administrative purposes, it is
not by definition inimical to vernacular architecture, which is

frequently accommodated within its rectilinear geometry.

175

ZBsl JanloInedo

## S. 38

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

Grids which have rigorously repeated dimensions, such as
squares, are ‘modular’, but those which have been modified or
warped by natural landscape features, may be termed ‘lattices’.

Grid plans are rarely the result of social imperatives within
the vernacular cultures, but are almost invariably imposed. In
some instances the authority may be religious, the formal plan
corresponding with a conception of cosmic order or spiritual
discipline. An example is the Hindu Manaäsära, dating from the
4th century AD, whose eight canonical mandala plans were
defined figuratively as the swastika or the padmaka (lotus),
though they were axially divided and orthogonally subdivided
in parallel grids. The mandala plans were symbolic and rarely
applied in practice, but the codification of planning principles
in the Manäsära may have evolved over millennia: uniform
‘modules’ ofplots and buildings on grid plans have been traced
in the Indus valley from the 3rd millennium sc.

In the Mediterranean and the Middle East the grid has an
ancient history; the axial roads characteristic of many plans
were evident in the Egyptian New Kingdom city of Amarna
which dated from the 14th century sc. But it was the founding
of Alexandria in Egypt, in 331 Bc by Alexander the Greatand his
planner Dinocrates that defined the orthogonal plan that was to
leave its imprint on western towns for over two millennia.
Roman legions used the grid for military camps, and established their grid-planned colonia across their Empire, hundreds
in North Africa alone. The ruins of Timgad (Thamugadi), Algeria, built for Roman veterans and their families, reveal the use
ofa precise modular plan.

In northern Europe medieval towns were builton the Roman
model by the English garrisons in 13th-century France. Montpazier, founded 1284, is one of several former English bastides,
the grid plan, with its market square surrounded by arcades,
still intact. It represents both the disciplined organization of
the garrison town, and the accommodation of vernacular
buildings within its orthography. Contemporaneously, Spanish kings and feudal lords consolidated their domains with
strategically placed cities planned, like Briviesca, Castilla, on a
basic grid with church and civic buildings facingthe plaza mayor.

New towns in the Iberian penisula were a rehearsal for the
wholesale standardization of the city plans in the new dominions in the Americas. The Laws ofthe Indies, which prescribed
uniformidad en todo (“uniformity in everything’) were formulated
between 1523 and 1573 and applied throughout New Spain.
Drawing on the Roman treatises of Polybius and Vitruvius, the
Roman plan was adapted: the forum was replaced by the plaza
mayor, the temple and the arena by the cathedral and the bullring. The town was divided into four cuartas, or quarters, these
into barrios and they in turn into city blocks (manzanas) studded
with houses. From Mexico to Costa Rica, Venezuela to the
south of Chile, cities, towns and villages were planned on the
same principles, but within the rigorous system the vernacular
houses fronting the calles displayed modest variety.

Applied by Thomas Holme to William Penn’s new city of
Philadelphia a century later, the Roman model became national
policy in the United States when Congress passed in 1785 the
Land Ordinance which authorized the parcelling ofthe nation
in 6 mi (to km) townships and ı sq mi (640 acre) farms. The

176

vastness ofthe land and the diversity of its topography mereifully mediated its implementation, and resulted in widely distributed settlements. But distributed across the continent
thousands of small towns, divided by Main Street or the railroad tracks and centred on the courthouse square, conform to a
relentless grid.

Grid plans have been extensively adopted by development
planners and administrations seeking to regularize and contain the world’s squatter settlements of the late-2oth century.
Efficiency in the use of public land, minimizing of street
lengths, the provision of sites and services, sewage disposal
and electricity supply areamongthe economic arguments mustered in support of modern grid planning policies.

PAUL OLIVER
1.11.5.f Linear
Linear settlement patterns can be considered special cases of point pattern distributions that vary in one dimension as points along a line. Alternatively, linear settlement
Patterns may serve as origin points for settlement expansion as
it develops along, as well as away from, a linear pattern. Linear
settlement patterns are generally associated with linear features, either natural or human-built. Linear features in nature
that may influence settlement patterns include water courses,
shorelines, canyons, ridge-tops and boundaries between environmental zones. Built or defined linear features associated
with linear settlement patterns include roads and trails, canals
and even political boundaries. As with areal point patterns,

‚ghouses in Novy

The grid landscapg,,
(gech Republic.

western Wisconsin
lowa, USA.

1]
a

EN

&
B
&
°

one-dimensional point distributions may be characterized by
patterns along a line ranging from random to clustered or dispersed. Hierarchical relationships are encountered in linear
settlement systems, and can be approached within a modified
framework ofcentral place theory (Dacey et al.).

In the traditional Slavic domain of eastern Europe, planned
linear villages are laid out along a single straight street. In each
street-village (Strassendorf) the farmhouses are lined up along
the street, with gardens, farmyards and outbuildings contained
within the long-lot behind the houses. Larger agricultural
fields surround the linear villages. A similar pattern of linear

See also

2.1V.8 Mesopotamiaanl
Plateau

3.V.6-A Colombia and
North Coast |

3.V.7-A Mexico, Cenl
and South

References
Caminos, Horacl

1978
Gutkind, E.A., 1967

© Otekar Macel

Settlement 1.1.5
villages and field arrangements follows the network of canals
that cross the Yangtze Delta ofChina.

A distinctive system of linear settlement is associated with
early French occupation the St Lawrence river in the Canadian
province ofQuebec and along the bayous ofsouthern Louisiana
(Harris; Newton). The long-lot (arpent) system employed in
these areas parcelled out parallel strips of land to settlers in
such a manner that each had access to theriver. In both Quebec
and Louisiana terms ofthe land grants dictated that houses be
built in the front (river end) of each parcel with farms being
developed towards the back, resulting in the strong linear pattern of villages. Land inheritance rules in both areas required
forced heirship in which a deceased landowner’s property
would be divided among the heirs, splitting the long-lot into
successively narrow parcels while maintaining the linear settlement pattern.

Through time, some linear settlements expand in depth,
transforming into a clustered village type. In other cases, linear
villages expand through the addition ofhouses along lines perpendicular to the original village orientation, as in the case of
the “T-villages’ associated with the arpent system of Quebec and
southern Louisiana.

Ridges and other topographic features can also result in linear settlement patterns. In the highlands ofnorthern Thailand,
the Hmong often reside in linear villages along ridge-tops. The
surrounding slopes and hills are planted in maize and opium
which are cultivated in a long-fallow system. In Korea, villages
are often strung along the junction of mountain bases and the
wetland plains that are used for rice cultivation.

The developmentand expansion oflinear settlement patterns
is, of course, not restricted to points directly on or adjacent to
linear features. Linear features, such as streams, can serve asa
focal line, integrating surrounding settlements within an essentially linear settlement system. An interesting case is that ofthe
Iban of Borneo, as studied by anthropologist Derek Freeman.
The Iban are swidden (slash and burn) agriculturalists whose
shifting system of temporary residences pivots on permanent
longhouses that serve as the base for each community. The communal longhouses are regularly spaced along main rivers that
are navigable by canoe. Each longhouse forms the pivot for a
great cycle of settlement shifts in which the longhouse community divides into several smaller groups, each occupying a
secondary residence (dampa) that is situated on a tributary
branch ofthe main river. SCOTT L. FEDICK
Nodal
Applied in the 16th century to a knot or protuberance ona branch or root, the term ‘node’ subsequently referred
to a point where lines or curves intersected, and eventually to
the central point of any complex. Nodal settlements, whether
towns or villages, have come to be identified as those on which
roads and routes converge. Routes, however, are seldom unidirectional, but are two-way elements in a system or network of
communications.

In essence, the nodal settlementaacts both asa focusand asa
distributor, a target for the traveller and a base from which the
merchant ventures, but it is more than this, for the very nature

1.1.5.8

177

## S. 39

© Paul Ollver after a photograph (Fraser)

1.1 ENVIRONMENT

ofits incoming and outgoing traffic also facilitates exchange.
To Lukermann nodality is ‘a behavioural act ofman, notsimply
a geometric point or a circulation interesect. In behavioural
terms a nodal location is that place where the individual has the
greatest freedom to interact.’ He argued that this required of
the node an urban population density, access from the area in
which it was situated, and the availability ofa function within
its region. Locationally, ‘nodality and hierarchy are conceptually analogous. A spatial hierarchy is the specification of a
nodal system’ (Lukermann).

Its place within the hierarchical nature of settlements determines to a degree the scale and functions ofthe nodal village or
town in relation to others within its region, represented in the
close-packing diagrams of central place theory, and subject
to marketing, transportation and administrative principles
(Christaller). A more dynamic model was developed contemporaneously by August Lösch who sought to solve the problem
ofthe location ofeconomic activity. These and subsequent theories have been much debated, criticized and elaborated by
urban geographers (Haggett; Carter).

Yet, in the real circumstances of, for instance, the Yoruba villages and towns ofNigeria, the complexity ofthe market systems
illustrates the problems ofapplying theoretical constructs to live
situations. Villages that have areal specialization in commodities
such as pots or kola nuts, the operation offour-day periodic market ring systems and of daily morning and night markets, the
changes in rural cultivation from subsistence to cash crops, the
motorization oftransport and many other factors compound the
realities ofsettlement (Hodder and Ukwu; Mabogunje).

Nevertheless, the plans of many Yoruba towns, like Ado-
Ekiti, Oyo or Ilesha, with their roads to and from Oshogbo,
llorit, Ile-Ife or Benin city drawn together both by the marketplaces and the palaces (afins) ofthe chiefs (obas), confirm their
administrative, economic and interactive functions as nodes

(0jo). Hierarchy, not only ofeconomic location but in the order
ofchieftaincy; focus, not only of market, butalso ofshrine and
sanctuary; access, not only by car or camel but by boat and on
foot, together defined the Yoruba nodal town in practice.

Morphologically, when several roads cross or meetata point
the resultant pattern may be as effectively described as radial,
emanating in many directions from a central point, as nodal, or
meeting at that point, While the clustered village may be selfsufficient and autonomous, and the linear village may lie upon
a single route, the nodal village extends its roads to others
which depend upon it, or to towns on which it partially
depends. Though encircling streets may aid internal communications and zone its functional or residential areas, itis theweb
of two-way radial streets which characterize the nodal settlement. PAUL OLIVER
1.11.5.h Organic
An organic settlement is one that grows over time
without being planned in advance, its order generated by the
accumulation of many different acts of building, all of which
are carried out with similar rules and goals. Organic settlements are often contrasted with planned settlements, in which
the order ofthe settlement is determined in advance, and into
which individual acts of building must fit. The word ‘organic’
implies that such settlements grow in a way thatis analogous to
that ofbiological organisms, in which the general growth pattern is embedded in the genetic code for the organism, and in
which the details are dependent on the specifics of site and
environment. Such settlements grow and change over time,
with such change and growth occurring on a continuous basis
with respect to the overall settlement pattern as well as with
respect to the individual buildings within it. As a result, such
settlements are never static or ‘complete’, and there are often
buildings being erected at the same time that other buildings
are in astate ofdecay, asa normal part ofthe ongoing evolution
ofthe settlement.

Settlements that grow in an organic way may be dispersed or
compact, linear or centralized, and include large cities and
small villages. Growth will often take place in a way that preserves the morphological form of the settlement: for example,
new neighbourhoods of houses in Tunis may be built so that
they preserve the compact courtyard form of the existing distriet; new house compounds in West African villages will be of
asimilar form to existing ones, and spaced atsimilar distances;
houses in linear villages of Southeast Asia will be added so that
the linear street is extended.

Although the detailed form of an organic settlement cannot
be predicted in advance, it is nevertheless the case that within a
particular culture, its typological character may be specified
with a relatively small number of rules that are applicable to all
acts of building. In the process of building, these rules, when
applied to specific situations, result in the detailed form of
buildings and spaces. As a typical example, some stone villages
in the Ticino region of Switzerland are composed ofrectangular
houses, laid out on steep hillsides, whose plan andarrangement
may be specified with just a few rules. For example, streets
are parallel to contours connected by stairs perpendicular to

7

Carter, Harold, 1975

Haggett, Peter, 1955
Hodder, B.W,, and Un

Lukermann, F., 1966

compound oftheAffld]
and the eneireling

Aldo Rossiet 11985

0 Gregory Broka

contours; streets paving, house walls and roofs are stone; house
plans are rectangular with two or threerooms arranged linearly;
houses have two storeys; houses have gable roofs with ridges
thatare perpendicular to the contour lines. However, within the
framework of the rules, the specific form that the villages take
on, as buildings are added and changed, is determined largely
by the detailed topography and by the positions of existing
buildings that form the context of new ones. Thus, streets follow paths that are slightly ‘irregular,’ building lots and house
plans deviate slightly from perfect rectangles, positions of windows and doors may vary from house to house, the exact way in

Settlement 1.111.5
which different details that are typologically the same are executed may vary. The fact that specification may happen with an
extremely limited set of rules is essential to the coherence that
organic settlements exhibit.

There are also settlements that combine both planned and
organic aspects, in which, for example, the overall pattern of
streets is predetermined and laid out, within which individual
properties and buildings are located and designed in a piecemeal, organic way. This is the case in many contemporary settlements on the outskirts of cities in Asia, Latin America and
Aftica, where rectangular grid street patterns and house lots
are laid out by government agencies. This layout is followed by
organic development on each property, often resulting in a distriet that has a good deal of variety and individual expression,
within the constraints ofactions that are similar from family to
family. HOWARD DAVIS
1.1.5.1 Peripheral
Settlements that are at the boundaries of a territory
or site which define its circumference are ‘peripheral’. From
the building ofthe Great Wall ofChina to the 600 Roman cities
built in North Africa or the construction ofthe border castles of
the Welsh Marches, the definition, exploitation and protection
ofthe frontiers mark territorial peripheries. Atthe macro-scale
the pushing ofthe boundaries ofterritorial expansion, the penetration of new lands, the advancing of the frontier, the breaking ofground and the establishment ofpioneer homesteads are
all in effect peripheral in kind, though they may not describe
the total circumference. Such peripheral expansion did notend
with ıgth-century colonialism; between 1900 and 1930 the
frontiers of settlement were being advanced in Manitoba and

179

## S. 40

1. ENVIRONMENT

Manchuria, Southern Africa and Siberia, Paraguay and Peru
(Bowman).

Pioneer settlements that advance frontiers are inevitably
outward-looking, though they may be tied to the source of
expansion by the need for supplies. Common to all peripheral
settlements is their distance from the centre, which may be
remote and linked only by fragile communications, or which
may be within daily contact, or even be kept in sight. Those that
are inwardly focused are almost invariably bound by the circumference, sometimes for defensive purposes. The English
village green originated with the clearing of the forests by
Anglo-Saxon settlers who grouped their houses around common open spaces, some barely a couple of hectares, a few like
Great Bentley ten times that size, most being the sites for markets, fairs or cricket matches (Oliver).

Village greens are all peripheral but the 1500 that survive
today are ofvarious shapes. Their function is now mainly social,
but formerly it was protective with the animals pastured within
its defensive ring. Many, perhaps the majority, ofperipheral settlements are circular in plan, probably for similar reasons. Such
was the case with the great Ba Ila village, Zambia ofChief Mukobela in which a thousand huts encircled an enclosure further
lined by kraals for the villagers’ 5000 head of cattle. Though not
in the geometric centre, the Chief’s kraal, further encircled by

the huts and granaries of his wives, was sited within and on the
axis of the entrance and spirit grove (Gutkind). Though the
plans of African compounds are diverse, many are peripheral,
like the compounds ofthe Massa ofnorthern Cameroon. Others
that are densely packed with huts, granaries and stores are
encompassed by thickets or stone walls (Beguin etal.).

Spiritual and symbolic connotations of the circle with perceived solar phenomena permeated Native American thought, as
is evident in the Arikara medicine lodges which were circular,
with “each of the semi-cardinal quarters of the horizon consecrated to one of the four elements, four guardians under God’
(Howard). Similarly, the Bororo of the Mato Grosso arranged
their dwellings in acircle, divided by cardinalaxes which defined
moieties and clans, while the entire village of the Yanoama of
Venezuela is a continuous peripheral structure.

While they are not essentially peripheral, whole cities have
been astronomically or concentrically planned, from Khwarizm
on the Oxus (and century Bc) and the Baghdad of Caliph
al-Mansur (8th century AD) to the Israeli kibbutz, Nahalal
designed in the 19205 (Moholy-Nagy). Though formally
planned, they had the potential to accommodate vernacular
buildings, like the remarkable lagoon island town of Mexcaltitan on the Mexican Pacific coast, the centre ofthe universe to its

moon-worshipping inhabitants (Gerster). PAUL OLIVER

See also

2.111.3-A British Isles

Beguin, Jean-Pierre, etal,

1.1.6 Territory

A territory is a tract of land, delineated by more or
less precisely defined boundaries, which is occupied, claimed or
administered bya social group. The term originally referred to a
domain of political sovereignty orjurisdiction, such as when we
speak ofthe ‘territory’ ofa kingdom or of a nation state. But its
eonnotations were subsequently expanded in two directions.
The first was by extension, ftom the domains of centralized
polities to those of political associations which lack any central
authority, such as the lineage-based, tribal organizations of
many agricultural and pastoral peoples, and the so-called
‘bands’ of hunting and gathering societies. The second was by
analogy, from the order of human society to that ofthe animal
kingdom. Thus individuals or groups of certain animal species
were said to hold territories. Bya further extension, the concept
ofterritory as used in ethological descriptions ofanimal behavjour came to be applied in accounts ofthe supposedly ‘territorjal’ aspects ofhuman nature, This led to popular and sometimes
exaggerated claims that the territorial arrangements of human
groups — whether bands, tribes, chiefdoms or states — have a
natural foundation, as the expression of universal dispositions
established in the course of evolution (Ardrey). Others, however, have argued that these arrangements result from the play
ofsocial and historical forces, and have no natural or biological
basis whatsoever.

Territoriality

In the study of animal behaviour, territoriality refers to a predisposition to appropriate bounded areas and to defend them
against outside incursion. However, even if human beings or
animals of other species carry an innate territorial disposition,
its expression will depend upon prevailing conditions in the
environment. Among human hunter-gatherers, for example,
the establishment of exclusive territories may confer a considerable adaptive advantage when the resources being exploited
are both dense and predictable in their occurrence. In the
exploitation of sparse and unpredictable resources, on the
other hand, territorial behaviour could be profoundly maladaptive. We would therefore expect forms of territoriality to have
evolved that are sensitive to the variables of resource density
and predictability (Dyson-Hudson and Smith).

In the case of human beings, the possibility exists for territotial behaviour to be transmitted not genetically, butas partofan
acquired cultural tradition. But the human capacities for language and symbolic thought, which underwrite such tradition,
also havea crucial bearing on the ways in which territoriality will
be expressed. In animals, a key function ofthe kind of agonistic
behaviour known as ‘territorial defence’ is to communicate

information about the locations of individuals and resources.
Human beings, however, can communicate such information
verbally, in terms ofa shared symbolic map of the landscape,
prior to taking up their intended positions in it. Thus what animals achieve by agonistic display and defensive posturing,
humans achieve by verbal negotiation, which is much less costly
in terms of time and energy expenditure, It would therefore
seem unlikely thataggression or warfare in human societies can
be explained by appeal to some otherwise latent territorial
instinct, carried over from our evolutionary ancestty. Ifany such
instinct existed, it would be expressed in negotiation rather
than aggression, and in the opening rather than the closure of
channels ofcommunication (Ingold).

Territory and population

Much ofthe early anthropological interest in human territoriality was fuelled by a controversy concerning the growth and regulation of human population. It was supposed that in order to
achieve lasting adaptations to their environments, human
groups must have developed methods of ensuring that their
numbers did not grow to the point ofthreatening the renewal of
the natural resources on which they depended. These methods
were thought to lie in the conscious control offertility by means
of such practices as infanticide, abortion and postpartum
restrictions on intercourse. But to regulate its numbers in these
ways, in relation to available resources, a group would have to
know the precise extent ofthe territory atits disposal. Territorial
compartmentalization was thus seen as a necessary concomitantofpopulation regulation (Carr-Saunders).

These arguments carry little credibility today, for two reasons
in particular. First, the inexorable growth ofhuman population
proves that any territorially based mechanisms of regulation
that may have operated in the past have been singularly
ineffective. Secondly, although practices designed to reduce fertility have been ethnographically recorded in many societies,
there is no evidence that the size of the reduction factor
responds to fluctuations in overall population density.

The defence ofterritorial boundaries

As conventionally understood, territoriality has two components: attachment to a particular area and defence of external
boundaries. Bach of these, however, can occur without the
other. Where the everyday life-activities ofa group are generally
confined to a certain portion ofthe environment, perhaps centred on one or more fixed sites, butwhereno attemptis made to
retain this living area for the group’s exclusive use, it is more
appropriately known as a home-range than as a territory

181

## S. 41

1. ENVIRONMENT

(Burt). On the other hand, individuals or groups may carry
around with them a ‘personal space’, centred on their bodies,
and may react aggressively to any attempts by outsiders to penetrate it. But when not tied to any specific area or location, this
space is nota territory, and its defence is notterritorial defence.

Anthropologists have distinguished two ways in which
human groups may maintain particular areas, or resources
therein, for their exclusive use: perimeter defence and social
boundary defence (Cashdan). The first requires a continuous
patrolling of the external boundary of the territory, in order to
ward off intruders. For small groups in large territories this is
frequently impracticable — the perimeter is too long to be
policed effectively, given the competing demands on people’s
time in the sphere of essential, subsistence-related tasks. The
alternative is to defend the boundary not of the territory but of
the social group holding it, on the principle that only those persons admitted to membership ofthe group can enjoy access to
the resources ofits territory. Greeting ceremonies, and similar
ritual formalities that very often accompany the arrival of visitors to a host community, have been interpreted in this light as
forms of social boundary defence. (See, for example, Peterson
on Australian Aboriginal ceremonies.) They can serve as mechanisms of territorial exclusion, however, only if access to the
group - and thereby to the information in the possession of its
members - is a precondition for the effective exploitation of
common resources. Once again, territoriality appears to function basically as asystem ofinformation exchange-thatis, asa
form ofcommunication.

A further implication is that territorial boundaries may be
partially or completely open to movement across them. The
boundary between adjacent territories may merelymarka point
of status inversion in the relationship between hosts and visitors, such thata person who isahoston one side becomes avisitor on the other, and vice versa. The host-visitor relationship
typically contains an elementofdominance and subordination.
Thus, complete closure represents the limiting case where no
party is prepared to accept subordination to another.

Attachment to place

For human beings as for the majority ofother animals movement
isa condition oflife. Yet there is no human society, even themost
nomadic, for which movement is not in some way tied to place.
The first appearance of home-bases bearing the hallmarks of
dwelling - hearths, shelters, storage facilities, discard areas and
so on — was a significant turning-point in human evolution. Itis
through dwelling in a place, and moving through its environs,
that people come to perceive their surroundings in the ways that
theydo.

This perceived environment might be better called a country
than a territory. Whereas a territory is a particular block ofthe
landscape, one ofa number ofsuch blocks together making up
aterritorial mosaic, a country is the totality ofthe landscape as
experienced from a particular place within it. Countries thus
have horizons butnno external boundaries: itis possible to move
from one country to another without ever crossing a boundary,
for as one moves, horizons dissolve as new ones come up ahead.

It follows that when people distinguish between places, or

182

between countries, they do not thereby suppose the world to be
divided up into bounded segments. Places derive their identities
from their positioning in a network of relations with other
places, much as persons derive their identities from their relations with other persons. Indeed, in many societies relations
between persons are spoken ofin terms ofrelations between the
places to which they are considered to belong. This is most evident, for example, in Aboriginal Australia, where social relations
are understood in terms ofthe nexus ofties between totemic sites
established by the ancestors of present-day people in the era of
world-formation known as ‘the Dreaming’. Aboriginal paintings
ofthe landscape depict itas anetwork of sites, connected by the
paths between them. Such paintings maybe regarded, inasense,
as maps.

Territory and the state

In marked contrastto those usages thatregard territorialityas a
form of behaviour, whether innate or acquired, is the use of
the term to denote the claim to dominion of the state. In this
usage it denotes a form ofpolitical control exercised byaruling
body over the inhabitants of a bounded geographical region.
Thus, territoriality in this sense inevitably entails both social
inequality and differentials of power. Territorial claims may be
established through a history of conquest, or by appeal to
primordial ties to the land. In the modern world such claims
have been extended to cover not only the land, but also adjoining regions of the sea (‘territorial waters’) and of the air (‘air
space’). As the order ofnation states has wrapped itselfaround
the globe, in consequence of the history of colonialism,
the entire inhabited world appears to be divided into one allencompassing, territorial mosaic. TIM INGOLD
Communications

Essential to the definition and use ofa territory are

its lines of communication. Some territories in taiga and tun-

dra, pampas and plains are comparatively featureless with flat

terrains and few landmarks to give minimal cues to direction,

or vantage points from which to ascertain relative location. The

writings ofexplorers refer often to the skills ofindigenous peo-

ples in perceiving subtle features of the landscape by which

their position is recognized. Freedom of movement over
deserts or savannas may be possible in thinly populated

regions, but few cultural groups traverse large tracts of country
without direction or purpose; once potential destinations have

been reconnoitred communication routes may be established
that in themselves become landscape markers.

Other territories, in forest or jungle, are even harder to conceptualize, offering no perspectives and often limited light.
Forest dwellers may use changes in floraand fauna, differences
of soils or gradients by which to identify their position, but
streams and rivers are among the most significant indicators of
location. Not only do they follow courses but the flow is also a
sign of direction and often of territorial limits. They are also
major determinants of territory providing the vital access to
water, the means of communication between different parts of
aregion and access to, or routes through, territories claimed by
other cultures. Rivers may offer virtually the only effective means

1.11.6.a

References

Carr-Saunders,
M., 1922
Cashdan, Elizabeth, ii
Casimir, Michael
Rao, Aparna (eds), if
Dyson-Hudson, Rada,
‚Smith, Eric Alden,
Ingold, Tim, 1986
Peterson, Nicolas, 9]
Sack, D., 1987
Taylor, R.B., 1988

0

oPraul

of traversing large distances, as is the case in the Amazonian
basin. They may also be the only means of‘ maintaining contact
with other groups, as in the jungles ofKalimantan or Sarawak.

Communication routes serve a number of territorial purposes. Some, such as ocean routes, not only facilitate trade and
commerce but also avoid violation of other territorial claims.
Others offer routes within a territory for the maintainance of
lines of defence and communication between the periphery
and the centre, between the outposts and the seats of power
and administration. Often territories are established to control
acommunication route and may be placed atasignificant point
of access, such as the location of Istanbul on the Karadeniz
Bogazi (Bosporus) or of Gibraltar and Tangiers at the mouth of
the Mediterranean. Some cultures have held their grip onaterzitory through the command of communication routes, like the
Afridi Pashtuns who have for centuries controlled the Khyber
Pass between contemporary Afghanistan and the northwest
frontier of Pakistan.

The principal purposes of communication routes over land or
water are for the establishment and continuation of trade,
exchange and commerce. Systems such as the celebrated kula
canoe trading routes between Oceanic islands have ensured contact between remote regions, access to sought-after materials,
goods or foods, and the possibilities of exogamous marriages,
and intereultural alliances. Ancient transcontinental routes

established centuries, even millennia, ago have been imprinted
on the landscape even to the 2oth century - witness the Silk and
Spice Routes between the Eastand the West, orthemodern roads

Territory

1.1.6

systems that still trace the linear grids laid down by the Romans.
Until the use ofthe automobile became widespread in the 19205
mostrural roads were unmetalledandhadno Protective surfaces;
in country districts farm carts and wagons were in extensive use
until mid-century, and much later in many parts of central and
eastern Europe; they remain so in much ofrural Asia, Camel caravans still followed traditional communication routes from
Morocco to Kano or across the Iranian plateau until the 19708.

Highways crossed territories, sometimes over hundreds of
miles, such as the Grand Trunk Road from Kabul to Calcutta.
Couriers who followed imperial routes to the limits ofthe Ming
Empire were allowed two weeks to reach Shanghai, sixweeksto
reach Kunming. Considerable changes took place when the
empire builders of Britain and France built roads, bridges and
railways to establish networks of communications in their
colonies for military and administrative purposes. Where the
discovery of precious stones, minerals and metals, or fossil
fuels led to the exploitation of resources, road and rail networks capable oftransporting machinery, labour and products
were constructed. In South Africa, Australia, Brazil and North
America frontiers hardened and communication patterns were
transformed. All these aspects of communication had profound effects upon the vernacular architecture ofinnumerable
cultures. Some shifted the locus of settlements closer to roads,
or even suffered severance from resources by being bypassed by
railways. But they also responded to the movement of goods
and commodities, including building materials, from bricks to
balloon frames. PAUL OLIVER
1.111.6.b Defence
The need for defence and the forms which it may
take are related to the nature ofthe perceived dangerorthreatto
a culture. Anticipation ofa natural hazard or fear of destructive
natural forces can condition the choice of sites and the claiming ofterritory. While the perception ofnatural hazards differs
among cultures, awareness of the risks of high tides,
avalanches, flash floods, hurricanes or other recurrent destructive forces may be based on former experience, or be acquired
over a period of time. Intermittent or unpredictable hazards
such as prairie fire or earthquakes are less frequently anticipated in site selection.

Defence from predatory animals is a factor in location only
when the danger is clearly evident; fear oftigers in Bengal, of
erocodiles in East Africa or ofvenomous snakes in virtually any
tropical milieu, will lead to shrewd site avoidance. Survival
strategies in developing awareness of the habits of predators
coupled with wariness and the acquisition of combative skills
are as important.

Itis the fear ofhuman predators and hostile raids, ofrivals for
power or resources, of traditional enemies and political opponents and of conquest or annihilation in conflict with expansionist and imperialist forces that inspires the most deliberate
choice of secure positions, or the building of structural
defences. Though the distinctions between raiding, headhunting, piracy, banditry, feuding, vengeance killing, aggression and
warfare have been themselves the subject of (academic) dispute,
their relationship to defensive systems has been less debated,

183

## S. 42

© Paul ollver 1980

1.11 ENVIRONMENT

notwithstanding the theories that have been advanced concerning warfare and the origin ofthe state (Webster). Yet it is indisputable that defence is a significant aspect ofsite selection and
settlement organization (Rowlands).

The simplest form of defence may be provided by the terrain,
and the most economic means in terms of effort expended is
concealment. Coastal communities of New Guinea frequently
chose to hide their dwellings within the forest edge in order to
be inconspicuous from the sea. Others, as in the Amphletts,
selected rocky and storm-swept sites or precipitous promontories virtually inaccessible by boat (Malinowski). Forest peoples
may depend upon the relative impenetrability of their domain
butsome, like the Yanoama, use such physical barriers as hills,
swamps and rivers as natural defences when siting their circular villages (Chagnon). Other landscapes present problems in
defence; in the plains and flat terrain farmers have fewer natural phenomena on which to depend for concealment. Bogs
and marshes, rivers and ravines may help achieve security; the
literature of guerrilla warfare gives many accounts of guides
with local knowledge of such conditions. Pastoralists and
nomads, the pursuit ofwhose economy was subject to disputes
over rights and usufruct, could settle them by negotiation or
conflict avoidance, strategies that are often less practicable for
settled communities (Burham; Irons).

For sedentary societies oversight across the defended territory is important. The advantage of altitude in this respect is
evident in the hundreds ofhill towns in France, Spain and Italy
where princes and potentates built their castles and fortifications to supervise and defend their realms. The donjon tower or
its equivalent was often sited within town walls, to which a
community could take refuge. In spite of their bastions and
walls hilltop settlements were vulnerable to eneirclement and
siege.

Earthworks and revetments may be raised, the earth and
rocks obtained leaving trenches and dykes which themselves
actas defences. Lagoons and lakes have sometimes become the
natural moats for island communities, such as the immense
Kampong Ayer of Brunei, or Samosir Island in Lake Toba,
Sumatra. Yet the Toba Batak found it necessary to surround
their villages with high earth walls created with sharp bamboo
spines, while the Batak houses were constructed with thick
plank walls, and entered by concealed ladders beneath the
floors (Niesson). It is evident that for most cultures where
defence is a necessity, topography and site situation are strate-

184

gically significant, but rarely sufficient without devices and
structures that exploit locational advantages that increase their
potential for protection. PAUL OLIVER

Enclosure

The physical enclosure of ground is one ofthe most
fundamental means of defining the different realms of space
associated with a pattern of settlement. Enclosure may be
formed by buildings themselves, by other built structures such
as walls or fences, or by trees, hedges and other plants which
serve to define space.

Enclosed and defined outdoor space is often controlled by a
particular group, whose houses and other structures form the
enclosure. Enclosure may happen at different scales, ranging
from thatwhich surrounds an entire settlement, to thatofasstreet,
to thatassociated with agroup ofbuildings within a settlement, to
that associated with a single building. So depending on the culture, these different scales of enclosed space may correspond
with — and belong to — different social groups within the settlement, rangingfrom thecommunity as a whole to extended family
groups, to individual families. Although there is not necessarily a
one-to-one correspondence between an enclosed space and a
social group that belongs to it, amap ofa settlement that shows
enclosed outdoor space is often used in beginningto understanding the social groupings and hierarchies within a settlement.

Enclosed space may be impermanent, as it is with a cluster of
Sahara desert tents, all facing inward and themselves forming
the enclosure ofthe common land ofa settlementthat may exist
for only one night. In settlements thatare only somewhat more
permanentthan the nomadic tentencampment, enclosure may
be formed with onlythe materials athand, plants or plant materials, able to be erected quickly. Buildings themselves may form

1.111.6.c

the enclosures of settlements. For example, the villages of

Pyrghi, Mestaand Olympii on the Greek Aegean island ofChios
have a continuous enclosure, at the outer limit ofthe built-up
area of the village, formed by connected buildings. Such
strongly defined enclosure, around the entire settlement itself,
is also found in such situations as the round Hakka buildings of
Fujian province, China (where a rammed-earth wall forms the
outermost circle, and a wooden structure is built againstit on
the inside), in the ksour at the base of the Atlas mountains of
Morocco and in the lighter wooden structures ofthe Yanoama
of Brazil and Venezuela, where a continuous circular lean-to
forms the enclosure ofa large communal courtyard.

The above examples are extreme, and enclosure need not be
Physically complete in order for space to be well defined. For
example, the traditional Norwegian or Swedish farmyard, composed of one or two enclosed spaces, is surrounded by freestanding buildings with considerable separation between
them; yet a distinet definition of place is achieved with this
arrangement. Similar definition exists in a Sumatran village,
composed of one or more streets of separate buildings each
with a strong gable roof, and, too, in clusters of round mud
buildings in sub-Saharan Africa. In some ofthese cases enclosure is achieved with a combination ofbuildings and mud walls
that run between them.

In many situations when settlements or buildings within settlements are firstestablished, the physical enclosure formed by
walls or fences will precede even the construction of the first
building. This is the case, for example, in many contemporary
‘spontaneous’ settlements in Latin America and other parts of
the world, where people’s first act of construction after
identification of their plot is the building ofa solid wall at the
boundary. It is only after this initial act of enclosure that any
buildings are built. HOWARD DAVIS
1.11.6.d Physical boundaries
Boundaries may be physically manifested in the
landscape in a great variety of ways. Ofany boundary feature it
is, nevertheless, possible to ask two questions. First, how did it
come into being? And secondly, how does it function? Before
attempting to answer either question, however, itis essential to
realize that no feature ofthe landscape is, of itself, a boundary.
Itcan only become a boundary, or the indicator ofa boundary,
in relation to the activities of people (or animals). Whenever we
speak of a boundary in the world of nature, the presence of
some individual or group is implied for whom it is recognized
or experienced as such. And because the perception ofboundaries is, in this sense, a relative matter- where one observer may
see a divided terrain another may see only continuity, or they
may see the terrain divided up along quite different lines. For
example, in a terrain consisting of parallel river valleys with
intervening watersheds, for people with a land-based economy
- such as swidden cultivators - the rivers may mark the boundaries between adjacent territories, whereas for people whose
economy is based on the exploitation of riverine resources —
such as native Indian hunter-trappers of northeastern Canada
in the period when their livelihood centred upon the exploitation of beaver - the rivers will appear as thoroughfares for life
conducted in territories bounded by watersheds. Likewise, in a
modern city a major road that serves as a thoroughfare for
those who live along it may also figure as a boundary between
administrative districts.

Turning to the question oforigin, a broad distinction may be
drawn between boundary features thatare naturally given, such
as rives and watersheds in the example presented above, and
those thatare to varying degrees builtor constructed. The latter
are often called ‘artificial’. Roads and fences, for example, are
constructed whereas rivers and watersheds are not. The distinction, however, is byno means clearcut. Many boundary features, such as trees and hedgerows, are neither naturally given

Territory 1.1.6

nor artificially constructed, but are rather cultivated through
human effort. Others, such as the transition between field and
forest, are the by-products of cultivation, or of other unrelated
human activities. And even such purpose-built features as
stone walls or ditches, once completed, become subject to the
same processes of overgrowth, weathering and decomposition
that, in the long term, contribute to the shaping of every other
component of the environment. Unless regularly maintained,
they will eventually disappear from ordinary view, to be preserved only as features ofthe archaeological record.

With regard to the question of function, we can distinguish
between features that serve to obstruct movement from one
side ofa boundary to the other, and features which serveto indicate the presence ofa boundary without imposing any barrier
to movement. In short, boundary features may have either
mechanical or semiotic functions. A high wall, palisade or
barbed-wire fence is designed to prevent people from crossing,
and forms part ofthe apparatus ofperimeter defence. Geological features such as cliffs or ravines may, ofcourse, be co-opted
for the same purpose, and may be ingeniously combined with
built structures, as in many medieval European fortresses. But
boundaries are often marked by features that present no barrier
atallto movement: these may be ‘natural’, as in the case ofrecognizably distinct landmarks (peculiar rock-formations, hilltops, tall trees), or ‘artificial’, such as cairns, flags or road
signs. Their purpose is not to obstruct but to inform; indeed
they convey significant information only by virtue of the fact
that the boundary is there to be crossed (Williams). Not all
landmarks or signposts are indicators ofboundaries, however.
They may signify a division of paths or other routes of travel
into sections rather than a division ofthe terrain into blocks, or
else provide sets oforientations in relation to place.

TIM INGOLD
1.l1.6.e Place attachment
Misunderstood by many Westerners has been the
attachment of particular inhabitants of vernacular architecture
to specific places (Relph; Altman and Low). In part this misunderstanding reflects the strong global influence ofthe American
value structure, involving as it does an appraisive cognitive map
which positively values staying in one place, even temporarily,
but negatively values ‘attachment’ to that place. Among many
traditional peoples, however, this priority is reversed. Thus,

185

EN me ET EEE RE EEE ER VERIEPEIRIA EEG

## S. 43

en

DC.A. Hornstein

1. ENVIRONMENT

such nomadic or semi-nomadic groups as American Navajo,
North African Tuareg, Middle Eastern Bedouin or Australian
Aborigines move about but seem strongly attached to the territory defined by that movement (Stea).

The cognitive ties binding place to other aspects of culture
may be incorporated into stories, often creation stories. The
location ofBiblical creation and ofboth Testaments, however, is
remote from the habitations ofmost ‘people ofthe book’. This,
together with the dichotomous, dualistic separation frequently
established between humans and environment, has tended to
engender placeless spirituality and even a disdain for vernacular
landscape and ecology among certain very religious peoples
(Stahl).

By contrast, the stories of other traditional peoples, such as
the Australian Aboriginal ‘Dreamtime tales’, have both local site
and situation, and teach geography and navigation (spatial and
social cognitive mapping) as well as morality and spirituality.
Aboriginal songs and the Navajo creation myth are the non-
Western equivalents of Homer’s Odyssey. Aboriginal chants and
stories, as accounts of the travels ofthe Dreamtime Ancestors
and what they did in each place, are lessons in geography. Chapters of the Navajo creation story tell about Navajoland through
the wanderings over that same land ofthe sons ofthe mythological Changing Woman, and the sites oftheir adventures.

Place attachment is often intense. African forest-dwelling
pygmies described by Turnbull, whose very perception ofspace
appears conditioned by their environment, are said to have
undergone complete societal disintegration when moved to
mountain habitations. In the language of the semi-nomadic
Navajo, the word for ‘displacement’ means to go away and
never to be seen again; it is effectively thesameas thatfor death.

The existence ofstrong place attachments means thatthe location ofvernacular architecture is very important. Navajo mythology, as interpreted by certain ‘singers’ (who play the role of
architects and planners), carries information on building codes,
standards, and siting (where hogans may or may not be built).
Over the globe, vernacular architecture is sited in accordance
with well-understood rules concerning necessary relations to

mountains, rivers, forests, rocks and even features indistinguishable to Western eyes, notjust because ofthe scenicand economic
aspects of such features, but in relation to the beneficial and
malevolent spirits embodied therein. DAVID STEA

186

Political boundaries

Political systems may be viewed in two ways: as institutional mechanisms for maintaining order within a given territorial domain, and as arenas of competition for public power. In
both views boundaries are implicated, which may be ‘horizontal’ or ‘vertical’. Horizontal boundaries separate ruling Elites
from administered populations, or the leaders of competing
factions from their followers. They are often mapped out in the
spatial layout of settlements, as for example in the division
between the indigenous walled city of Old Delhi and the 2othcentury colonial district of New Delhi (King). Vertical boundaries surround domains of political jurisdiction and divide
factions off from one another. They too may be revealed in settlement patterns, as shown for example in Kuper’s map of the
Kgalagari village of Kuli, in Botswana, where residential sectors
correspond to factional alliances of family groups (Kuper).

It used to be assumed in much anthropological writing that
theworld was made up ofa greatmany separate peoples, cultures
or nations, each with its own polity, occupying a discrete territory, and isolated from its neighbours, both socially and geographically, within pre-existing boundaries. This has now given
way to a recognition of the interconnectedness of human cultures, which only in the most exceptional cases could be said
