Relph, E., 1976
Stahl, A., u
‚ea, D., 198
Tamhäl 6, 1961

to exist in isolation. It is evident that cultural diversity has persisted despite extensive contacts between peoples, and the
resulting flows of both personnel, commodities and information. Thus intergroup boundaries are maintained not because of
anaabsence ofexchange across them, butbecause ofthe presence
ofsuch exchange (Barth). In other words, ethnic or sociopolitical
boundaries are generated and held in place within fields ofsocial
interaction that cutacross them.

This means that political boundaries do not providean externally given framework for history, or for the separate histories
ofeach people, but rather emerge within an essentially continuous historical process. Though some boundaries are more
durable than others, none are everlasting. Thus, throughout
history boundaries have been drawn in different ways, circumscribing groups with different identities and affiliations. There
is no simple, one-to-one correlation, however, between groups
and territories, and hence between political boundaries and
territorial boundaries. A well-defined and clearly bounded
political group may nevertheless lack a coherent territorial
base; this is most often the case when the common interests
that bind members ofthe group together are in long-distance
trade and commerce. Conversely, a territorially cohesive population may lack political solidarity, as in many ‘tribal’ societies
whose maximal political units are relatively shallow lineages.

Itis in situations where power rests in control over thelandand
its resources that political boundaries will tend to be realized in
the concrete form of a division into discrete territories. Here a
decisive factor in the maintenance of boundaries is the use of
force. Warfare, particularly as conducted between centralized
states, is primarily a means to gain or defend territory. In nonstate societies, too, territorial gain (forthevictors) is often among
the prineipal consequences ofwar, even whereitisnotthe explieit
motive. However, warfare may also be interpreted as serving an
ecologically adaptive function, in securing an even distribution of
population over the land. Rappaportt, in his study of the rather
titualized form of warfare among the Maring of Highland New
Guinea, proposes just such an interpretation (Rappaport).

In the contemporary world of nation states the geographical
outline ofa state’s territory is often employed as a cartographic
symbol ofnational identity. Rarely, however, does the territorial
extentofthe state coincide with that ofthe regions inhabited by
the cultural groups to which its citizens see themselves as
belonging. This discrepancy between political and ethnic
boundaries has resulted in endemic and often bitter armed
conflict, wherein ethnically defined nations strive to secure
exclusive territories on which to establish their own
autonomous states. In the process entire populations may find
themselves debarred from their former homelands. Thus
nationalist attempts to enforce a correspondence between
political and ethnic boundaries, within a territorial framework,
inevitably lead to the emergence of peoples without territory -
namely, refugees. TIM INGOLD
1.1.6.8 Property
When people offer accounts of their relations to the
land, they use terms that ethnographers have often translated
as ‘ownership’ and ‘property’. Outside the context of Western

Territory 1.1.6

understandings ofthe individual, society and nature, however,
the applicability ofthese terms is highly problematic. In Western jurisprudence, property implies the rights to enjoy, to
exclude others from such enjoyment and to alienate. Thus
property in the form of land may be held by an individual as
against all other individuals (in ‘private ownership’), or by a
collectivity of some kind against all other collectivities of the
same order (in ‘public ownership’), in respect of a precisely
delineated portion ofthe natural environment. In most non-
Western societies, however, land is held with, or on behalf of,
other persons rather than against them; moreover, itis considered to be as much something to which they belong and from
which they derive their identity as it is something that belongs
to them. For this reason the land is regarded as fundamentally
inalienable.

These general points may be illustrated by means of three
examples. Among Australian Aboriginal hunter-gatherers, a
person may refer to the terrain surrounding a totemic site with
which he is identified (or to a group of sites comprising what
some anthropologists call an ‘estate’) as ‘my country’. Though
he could expect to be consulted by those intending to use its
resources, he is not generally in a position to refuse access to
those who ask permission in the proper way, Possession, here,
is a matter of looking after’ the country, of‘ tending the creative
powers that are thought to reside in its core locales in order to
ensure its fertility and renewal. So-called owners are thus, in
reality, the custodians of a world that belongs to all, and they
exercise their rights and responsibilities on behalf of everyone.
Whatan owner possesses, to the exclusion ofothers, is the Privilege of custodianship, and the ritual practices and performances — together with the associated insignia of designs,
songs and stories- which enable him to perform these custodial
functions (Myers).

The second example is drawn from Malinowski’s celebrated
accountofthe possession oflandamongthe Trobriand Islanders
of Melanesia (Malinowski). Malinowski was concerned to refüte
the arguments, prevalentatthe time, that so-so-called ‘primitive
societies’ had either an individualistic or a communalistic attitude to property. Trobriand property relations, he showed, could
not be understood in terms of this kind of private/public
dichotomy, but had to be viewed in the light of the relation
between individualand collective claims. An individual cultivator
can claim that he ‘owns’ the plotthathhe has cleared and planted,
but at the same time the plot can be said to belong to the entire
local group or ‘subelan’, which holds all the garden land ofthe
community. The individual’s tendance of his plot is part of his
responsibility towards the subclan of which he is a member.
Thus, individual claims nest within more inclusive, collective
claims, and this nesting reflects the fact that the individual
belongs to the subelan and has - by virtue oftthis membership —
the right to cultivate a plot on subclan land. In this as in numerous other examples the combination of more or less inclusive
claims in relation to the same land signifies a complex of'social
relations among the claimants.

The third example concerns an instance of chiefly ‘ownership’. Achiefamong the Bemba of Zambia can make the apparently sweeping claim that he ‘owns’ the land of his entire

187

## S. 44

1.11 ENVIRONMENT
chiefdom, as well as the labour of its inhabitants and the food
they produce (Richards). Such a claim, however, must be
understood in political rather than in strietly &onamie teims;
What the chief holds is the personal allegiance of his subjects,
expressed in the payment of tribute and reciprocated in periodic acts ofredistributive largesse. His claim to the land ia CN,
ditional on that allegiance and on the prerogatives it entails,
Thus the relation between the claim of the individual farming
household to its cultivated plot and the overarching claim of
chiefly ‘ownership’ reflects the relationship between the
householder and the chief. Just as the Aboriginal hunter-gatherer holds his country on behalf ofthe collectivity, and the Tiobriand gardener tills his plot on behalfofthe subelan, soin on
example, the Bemba householder cultivates his farm om behal
of the chief to whom he owes allegiance. Ownership is not
absolute but relative, depending upon the relational context of
the owner’s social position inthe community. TIM INGOLD

Realm .
Realm and domain are related eoncepts which are
often metaphorically employed in architectural iistoutse to
define space use, especially where this is differentiated by Aue:
tion and gender. Realm is used here where sovereignty is claimed
over a territory as a political entity. Such a ein eustomatıly
implies the existence of a state, generally with a hierarchical
social structure and frequently with a king or ruling power atits
head. Those who live in the vernacular milieu and who, in many
states, constitute the majority ofthe commonalty, are ‘subjects
of the sovereign power who may be called upon to defend the
territory or to participate in wars intended to estend the zealm,
an obligation that is as common in republics and political
democracies as itis in kingdoms and empires.

The ambition of states to gain control over communication

toutes, access to fertile lands and mineral or other resources,
and to extort taxes from the conquered has meant that the
impact ofimperial expansion through military campaign I
conquest has been felt by countless vernacular cultures over the
centuries. While this has frequently meant the wholesale burning and demolition of huts and houses, it Bes also meant the
introduction over time of new forms, materials and technologies by occupying and colonizing powers.

While the history of the process in terms of the vernacular
traditions ofthe world still has to be written, innumerable indicators are extant, even though the events that brought them
about were enacted centuries, even millennia ago. So for
instance, the introduction of Roman technologies suen as the
use of imbrices, or overlapping tiles, led to the widespread
diffusion ofa roofcladding system which was later introduced
to the Americas and elsewhere by the Spanish and Portuguese
in the periods ofexpansion oftheir realms. .

Similarly, the construction ofthe Great Wall of“ China, by 200
BC, some 2250 km (1400 mi) in length, introduced athird ofthe
male population to building in rammed earth. During the succeeding four centuries of the Han dynasty, structural
systems and building plans permeated throughout the zealın,
dictating the forms of domestic architecture which still pre-

dominate.

1.111.6.h

188

Aspirations ofempire may rise with dynasties, ideologies and
religions. A century after the death of Muhammad, _ Islamic
empire extended from the Pyrenees to the Indus, leaving a per
manent impression on the architectural forms, technologies
and details of the Middle East and the Mediterranean. Long
after, under Suleiman the Magnificent, the Ottoman Empire,
expanded into Europe; Hungary was taken and vienna
besieged. Centuries of Ottoman domination and administration resulted in the merging of Balkan and Turkish vernacular
building traditions. u

Expansion ofthe European realms through polieies oftrade,
exploration, colonization, exploitation and imperialism were
initiated as early as the ı6th century with the conquest ofMeso-
America and South America by the Iberians. Indigenous Aztec,
Maya and Inca cultures were subjugated, enslayed or isalated
with the destruction of their monumental religious architecture, though domestic building persisted where it offered no
threat. Independence from Spain and Portugal did not mean
the end ofcolonization which continued, as in North Auieriex
and Australia, until well into the 2oth century, resulting in the
development of new colonial vernacular forms. .
European imperialism in the ıgth century assumed a hith-

erto unprecedented scale as competitive aspirations and the
demand for material resources grew with industrialization.
The partition of Africa by the European powers at the dose of
the ıgth century created many ‘countries’ with artificially
drawn boundaries that sliced through cultures. Differing paltcies of colonization were applied to the territories over which
dominion was claimed by the Dutch, French, Germans, British
or Belgians, and these were reflected in the degree to which
vernacular traditions were permitted to continue, or we
‘“improved’ in the cause of Christian morality. As the colonizers
were mainly concerned with exploitation and their own material advancement on the European model, retention of the
vernacular served their interests in maintaining indigenous
underdevelopment.

Modernization, assimilation and integration had greater
impact upon vernacular architecture in the zoth senkuy; With
the expansion of the Soviet empire and its sarellites, mass
housing and high-rise developments reduced cultural diversity

>

to urban anonymity. Meanwhile, national independence from
the British Empire and other European imperialist powers was
achieved. Nationhood, itself largely a product ofthe ıgth century, had in-built assumptions of cultural identity with the
realm which have been challenged by distinct ethnic entities in
conflicts in many parts of the world. The new realms of the
“Third World’ sought parity ofindustrial development with the
western nations with consequent rural depopulation and rapid
urbanization.

Yet, as sovereignty over the realm has been habitually
expressed in castles, courts and congress buildings, designed
not only to accommodate administration but to protect. to
impress and to intimidate, these are rarely vernacular in character. In some respects, by emphasizing the distinction
between sovereign power and subject powerlessness, such
architectural discrimination has contributed to the continuity
of vernacular traditions into the 2oth century. Ironically, it is
development rather than imperial domination which may

threaten their survival. PAUL OLIVER

1.111.6.i Security

In numerous locations archaeology has revealed
the presence and the importance of protective structures in the
earliestofsites: the construction ofhigh walls to encirele towns
is more ancient than Uruk (5500 BP). But their relevance to
2oth-century vernacular structures has not been given similar
attention, in part due to therelative neglectofthe anthropology
of war, but also because of the worldwide distribution of
weapons of mass destruction that haye tendered traditional
defensive sites and protective devices virtually obsolete. Yet the
temains of immense earth walls are still to be seen round the
cities ofthe Hausa emirates, some beinggm Go fü) highand up
to 6 m (20 fi) in thickness, with high towers enclosing the
gates. Kano fell in 1903 when a gate was breached by a British
force, and the Fulani Empire collapsed soon after in the ruins
of Burmi (Johnston). They were not the last traditional
defences to fall, but they signify the destruction wrought by

western firepower as the century opened.

The security ofthe realm and its eitizens may be among the
first priorities ofa state, but in many vernacular contexts protection ofterritory involves the communityasawhole. However
organized, it involves co-ordination and the expenditure of
time and effort. Among the most impressive security systems
that confronted the assaulting troops of colonizing powers in
the 19th century were the breastworks, stockades, traverses and
bastions ofthe Fijians. Introduced as a response to firearms,
they were admired by military observers for their strength and
ingenuity. They were possibly related to the Maori hill fortifications (pa) with their scarps, palisades and rifle pits, which
were the most complex and effective indigeneous Protective
complexes encountered by European forces. After a whole
day’s firing, a 20 cm (8 in) field gun ‘scarcely succeeded in
making a practicable breach’ against one such pa (Best).

Traditional strongholds were often extensive. As late as
1890, the Spanish assault on Panape (Micronesia) had to contend with a stockade nearly 640 m (2100 ft) longand 3.4 m (m
ft) high. Some longhouses in Borneo were Protected by pal-

Territory 1.1.6

isades, attimes in a double rowwith poles as highasg m (30 fi)
carved with grotesque figures and monstrous animals to deter
opponents, with internal towers used to harass an attacking
force. Other longhouses were builton stout trunks 22 m (40 ft)
high and were virtually impregnable. Among the most secure
territories in Africa was that developed by the Ethiopian
Amhara who perceived social Space as concentric circles of
diminishing importance and replicated this in their planning.
Hilltop settlements established throughout Amhara territory
had the church or thechief”s palace onacentralhilland the sefer
garrisons ofthe guardian forces strategically placed around it.
Vacant land was preserved between the domains ofthe adjacent
chiefs, a settlement form which survived into the 1970s (Kifle).
Under the threat of Arab slave traders most Aftican settlements at the turn of the century were protected by earthen
walls, palisades or thickets, depending on resources. Complex
mazes were constructed to confuse and entrap enemies, and
entrance tunnels ofpoisonous euphorbia were planted to combat horsemen. Strong euphorbia hedges still encircle Maasai
enkangs, but should they be breached the plan ofthe dwelling
entrance exposes the flank ofan intruder, whether human or
animal. Security devices have been used by forest cultures in
many parts ofthe world; theJivaro ofthe western Amazon used
ingenious traps to shoot pointed chonta sticks at face levelwhen
trips were released, and spikes in ‘holes of death’ impaled
intruders who fell through their thin brushwood cover
(Karsten).
TheJivaro engaged in blood feuds, following the guidance of
omens to avenge the murder of relatives. Societies that have

189

## S. 45

1. ENVIRONMENT
institutionalized vengeance codes have been the most persistent in their use ofsecure structures. Fortified and armed compounds with high walls and gun-towers are occupied by some
millions of Pashtuns of Pakistan and Afghanistan, adherents to
the code of honour Pakhtunwali (Hart). In the Arabian peninsula and North Africa impressive tower houses and walled fortified villages (ksour) of rammed earth and stone still bear
witness in the 2oth century to histories ofconflict.

Security means more than safeguarding the populace
againstattack from enemies or raiders. As important is the protection of pastures and crops within the territory against natural hazards and predators. High watch-towers are still used by
many forest and savanna peoples for early warning of marauders, thieves, storms and fires, and plagues ofbirds and locusts.
In distant pastures herders build cabins while caring for their
livestock, while field shelters are constructed for the accommodation of farm workers safeguarding their crops. Less structural, but for their users no less effective, are the apotropaic
motifs that ensure that the territory is secure from malignant
spirits. PAUL OLIVER

Tenure

To speak ofthe ‘tenure’ ofland or territory is to sup-
Pose that it is in some way ‘held’ or appropriated, and ‘passed
on’ from one generation of holders to the next. It is through
tenure, therefore, thatland becomes a form of property and an
object of inheritance. Relations of tenure are social relations
between persons in respect ofwhat is held, and are necessarily
bound up with - and take their meaning from - the total configuration of relationships in a given society. In classical
anthropological accounts, however, the concept oftenure has
tended to be applied exclusively to agricultural societies, whilst
the claims of hunter-gatherers and nomadic pastoralists to the.
land were more usually brought under the rubric of“territoriality’. This reflects the mistaken view thatsince nomadic peoples
do not work to transform the land, but are simply the users of
their environments, they exercise no more rights to it than do
wild animals (which may likewise exhibit territorial dispositions). In many regions ofthe world this argument - that the
land was terra nullius, free for all - was employed by colonial
regimes to justify their usurpation of the territories of indigenous peoples.

Itis now clear that relations oftenure exist in all human societies, and notjustin those ofagriculturalists. What is held, however, maynotbe and’ inthesense in which this is conventionally
understood in the West, that is, as a uniform two-dimensional
substrate that is geometrically divisible into mutually exclusive
parcels or blocks (Bohannan and Bohannan). Indeed, the very
notion ofland is anabstraction, reducingall the variegated forms
and features ofthe visible landscape to a singlecommon denominator. Like the similarly abstract notions, current in Western
societies, oftime and value, this reflects the way in which the natural world, in these societies, has been made into a commodity.
In the context ofnon-Western societies, it may be more apposite
to speak ofthe tenure ofplaces, paths and ground surfaces.

For example, farmers among the Tiv of Nigeria come to

1.1.6

*hold’ their plots, and the crops growing on them, because of
the work they have put into clearing, planting, weeding and so
on. It is in the nature of such activities that they are distributed
across a surface, so that ‘farm tenure’ may be seen as relatingto
a certain area (Bohannan and Bohannan). For native hunters
and trappers of the boreal forest, by contrast, the object of
tenure is a trail, along which the hunter-trapper regularly
moves in the pursuit and harvesting of game. The Mistassini
Cree of northeastern Canada, for example, refer to what they
hold as ‘my path, or road’ (Speck). And among the San (or
Bushmen), hunter-gatherers of the Kalahari, the object of
tenure - typically a waterhole and its environs - is described by
a term which means ‘my place’ or ‘the place where I belong’
(Wilmsen). Indeed, in a sense it is the place that ‘holds’ the
people.

Importing their Western, two-dimensional concept ofland,
ethnographers have been inclined to assume that places and
paths are held byvirtue oftheir enclosure within bounded territorial blocks or strips. This, however, is to misinterpret the evidence, and to impute boundaries where none in fact exist. Even
in societies practising cultivation, plots or garden beds may be
distributed in dispersed locations, without being contained
within any encompassing boundary. This does not mean that
places, paths or plots can be considered as objects of tenure in
isolation from the surrounding country. For the place, path or
plot enfolds in its essence the environment in which itlies, just
as the latter is enfolded in the experience of those who make
their living there.

As a concrete process of appropriation, tenure has to be
understood in terms ofwhat people do rather than as the application ofan abstract set ofrules. This requires a focus on practical activities, on the ways in which persons inscribe their
identities and relationships into the environment as they go
about their everyday tasks. It is by way of such inscription that
features of the landscape are bound into the life-histories of
persons, or into the developmental trajectories of those
groups, domestic or otherwise, to which they belong. Through
tenure, the landscape acquires a history, and a human past.

TIM INGOLD

MATERIALS AND
BUILDING RESOURCES

With the exception ofa limited range of natural shelters, ofwhich rock shelters and
caves are the most permanent and perhaps the most numerous, all buildings are
dependent upon the availability of materials. While it may be argued that materials do
not determine the form and type of dwelling or other building constructed, their
availability exercises considerable constraints on what buildings may be possible.
Though there is evidence of exchange in materials for aesthetic and symbolic purposes
among pre-industrial societies, the scale of monumental architecture required
considerable organization of manpower to obtain massive materials; domestic
vernacular in most cultures has depended on readily available or easily portable
resources.

Though vernacular architecture has often been admired for its rational and thrifty use
ofscarce resources it is evident that the materials used are those that are abundant in
the given circumstances. Pursuit cultures, food gatherers and nomadic or seminomadic pastoral societies have depended on Animal Products (r.1v.1) for shelter
provisions. As is the case with most building materials, these require preparation -the
cleaning and curing of hides or the spinning and weaving of wool for tent cloths.

Ofthe natural materials available to sedentary societies, the earth on which they may
construct their buildings is potentially the most accessible resource. The building
strength of Earths and Clays (1.1v.2) depends on particle size and properties of
adhesion, different techniques being developed to exploit the plasticity or compression
strength ofthe locally obtained soil types. Most earths have to be protected against
weather erosion but firing the clay to make burnt brick creates a highly durable
material. The process requires the construction of kilns (for examples see 1.1x.7.
Technological).

Use ofsoil, earth and clays must be managed so as not to damage their capacity to
support vegetation. Grasses and Palms (1.1v.3) are the most widely distributed and
most easily gathered ofthe building materials. Straws are widely used for roof coverings
in the form ofthatch, while turfand reeds can provide durable and weather-resistant
roof cover. Field grasses are only rarely used as a structural material, though they can be
employed to make rope binding materials; reed is sometimes used structurally, while
bamboos and giant grasses have strength and elasticity, and are rapidly renewable. The
trunks, stems and leaves ofthe monocotyledons, or palms, provide important building
materials in the tropical world.

Rocks form the underlying geological structure ofthe earth’s crust, but for building
purposes the Rocks and Stones (1.1v.4) are largely drawn from those strata exposed at
the surface or excavated from layers of relatively shallow depth. Widely available, they
are extensively used where stability has to be ensured or where damp penetration is a
problem. Fragmented rocks and stones which have sheared into readily usable and

193

## S. 47

1.1V

MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

transportable sizes are often employed for building, while some rocks split in sheet
form to be suitable for roof cladding. The jointing of stone may require mortars while
the use ofdressed or worked stone implies access to metal tools. Iron or steel tools are
generally required for work in wood. Obtainable and sometimes used in their unworked
state, the Timbers (1.1V.5), like the rocks, have frequently to be transformed before use
and exploitation oftheir structural properties. All parts of the trees, including the
trunks, branches, bark, leaves and roots, have been used for building; examples of
woods after the initial stage of transformation are given. To those who consider that the
use oflocally available materials is a fundamental defining quality ofvernacular
architecture, its traditions are regarded as having ceased with the mid-ıgth-century
development ofthe railroads and commercial shipping, which facilitated international
and even intercontinental movement. Manufacture and wide distribution ofsome
materials, such as cement or glass, have a long history, but the export of manufactured
sheet materials, including corrugated and galvanized iron and roofing felt, have both
extended the life ofmany vernacular traditions and protected them at relative low cost.
Many 2oth-century materials, including the plastics, have been extensively re-used,
often being recycled after scavenging on rubbish tips or traded at depots of waste
building products; though not indigenous they are locally available. Transformed and
metamorphosed materials such as concrete block and soil-cement, introduced by the
modern building industry or in development projects, have considerably extended the
life of contemporary traditional building. Used and Manufactured (1.1v.6) materials
may yet provide the basis for the emergence of new vernacular forms.

PAUL OLIVER

194

.vı. Animal Products

Animal products are most frequently used in architecture by people whose livelihood is centred around animals,
and especially in areas where there is a shortage of other natural materials. Hunter-gatherer people, particularly those who
live in extreme climates such as the Arctic, often use animal
products, as do nomadic pastoralists and herdsmen.

Conversely, there are people whose religions or cultural
beliefs specifically prohibit them from using animal materials
in any part oftheir lives, and for those reasons they do not usually huntor herd animals. The most well known ofthese are the
Jains, certain Buddhist groups, and also Muslims, who have
prohibitions against pig products. Hindus do use leather but
only particular castes kill the animals or treat the skins.

Using animal materials usually involves shearing the animal
for its hair or wool, or killing it for its skin and other parts ofits
body. Among nomadic people, once the animals has been
killed, most of it will be used. A common architectural use for
animal materials among hunter-gatherers and nomadic pastoralists is for tent covers. People may use skins, woven goathair
or felted wool to cover their tents. All these materials require a
lot of preparation and the processes are often time-consuming.

Skins have to be dried, cured and scraped before they can be
used. Most skins have to be scraped and beaten for several days,
as well as being soaked in substances such as sour milk, camel
dung or bark fermented in urine in order to cure them. People
use the kinds of skins available to them. The Plains Indians used
buffalo skin, the Arctic peoples used seal skin or caribou while
the Tuareg and Turkana use goatskin. The main advantage of
skins is that they are usually windproof and waterproof, and can
be warm. Their disadvantage is thatthey are difficult to ventilate.

Feltmaking is hard physical work, often requiring a long
sodden roll of five or more fleeces to be rolled backwards and
forwards for many hours. The work is so arduous that it is often
impossible to do without other people’s help. This may result
in the making of felt being the occasion ofa kind of party so
that while the work is hard it is also quite enjoyable.

The felt cover for a nomadic yurt requires a great deal of
wool. Its size will often be limited by the size of the owner’s
sheep herd. Felt is warm, very good for insulation, easy to
ventilate and quite waterproof.

Woven goat hair is the fabric of which black tents are made.
These tents are lived in from North Africa as far as Tibet, with
only a few variations in design and materials. The goat hair
from which they are usually made has to be first spun, then
woven. Drop spinning is an immensely time-consuming
process, so women spin at all sorts of times. The weaving is
done on a ground loom and can also be very slow, butnomadic
women have often refined their techniqueand can work at quite
astonishing speeds. Once the bands of black cloth have been
woven, they are sewn together and the strength ofthe goat hair
makes a very suitable fabric for the highly tensile black tent.

The use of animal materials among nomadic people is not
just confined to tent covers. Hair is often used for rope - for
example, goat hair by the Tuareg, horsehair by the Tuvinians. Its
strength and tensile qualities make goat hair very suitable for
warp threads in rug weaving, and goat hair has often been used
for blanket and rug weaving among North American Indians.

Bone has been used for house and tent frames among Arctic
dwellers but now in general is only used for toggles, tent pegs
bynomads, decorative inlays and so on. Horn, hooves and animal claws are all composed of a fibrous material, keratin,
which is the principal constituent ofthe epidermis of skin, the
thin layer which protects the corium, or hair- and nerve-bearing inner skin. Horn can therefore be split or peeled and thin
layers used to make small window-panes; an assembly ofsix or
eight could permit a mellow lightthrough a small window.

Sheets of parchment were also widely used for window coverings which admitted a diffused light. The parchment was
covered with a mixture ofhoney, egg-white and gum arabicand
was then stretched over a frame while it was still wet. Once it
had contracted the surface was varnished and the parchment
panel inserted in the window-frame.

Sinew is frequently used for sewing tent covers together by
Arctic peoples. The South African Bushmen use sinew to sew
rushes for house covers. The Chukchi used twisted sinew for
guy ropes. Preferred sinew is often taken from the animal’s
back. In the Arctic, the women usually prepared it, carefully

195

## S. 48

&
E
5
3

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

removing it, spreading it flat on a scraping board and cleaning
off the fat. Having been washed, it was flattened out on a tent
cover to dry, and was then often smoked. Once dried, it was
pounded and then separated or combed out into shreds like
hemp fibres. Itcould then be twined intoa thread, ofanylength,
using a similar technique to rope making. The Tuvinians and
the Mongols use sinew for sewing leather for household artefacts and clothing. Apart from sewing, it was also used as a
warp thread in weaving by North American hunter- gatherers.

Feathers were used to strengthen warp threads in weaving
among the North American people in blanketwork. Goose and
duck down were spun in with dog hair by the Salish to make it
less stretchy. Feathers also had an important use in bedding.
The Qashqai and Bakhtiari stuffed their mattresses with partridge feathers. The original Swiss duvetused down or feathers.

Horsehair was also used for mattresses in Europe. The Central Asian nomads use soft lambswool and camel hair for the
same purpose. In polar regions, fur and skins were used both
as mattresses and covers.

Most nomadic people do notjust display skill in one particular technique for using animal materials, butare experts at processing the whole wide variety of artefacts necessary for living
their particular way of life with all the materials available to
them. For example, the nomadic Kirghiz make tent covers,
doors, rugs and bags for household goods out offelt. They also
weave, making woollen bands and tassels for their tents, as
well as woven grass screens called chij. They are experts at
leatherwork - making various containers for their milk products from skins, and also excel at embroidery and patchwork,
using silk to make decorated hangings for the trellis (tush kiiz)
and patchwork bedding.

In many nomadic cultures in Europe and Asia, it is usual for
the women to do the making while the men are more involved
in the hunting, herding and general animal husbandry, including the killing or shearing of the animal. Of course there are
exceptions. Navajo women keep the sheep, Mongol men make
felt, for example.

It is important to note that, an animal having been killed,
most of it will be used in some way. Little is wasted. This is
nowhere more true than among the Inuit. Theyused to use animal products for many things - skins for tent covers, bone for
tent poles, house frames and tools, inner membranes for windows, tendon for thread, rawhide from the waste edges ofskins
for lashing. Even blood was used - dissolved dried blood used

196

to glue tent poles made such strong joints that no lashing was
needed.

Settled people use animal products much less frequently in
architecture, but they are still used in the home for bedding,
bags, floor coverings, hangings and decorations. Specific uses
ofanimal products in building include plasterwork and glue. A
listofdifferentanimal materials known to have been mixed with
lime in 16th-century English stucco work includes ‘hog’s lard,
bullock’s blood, cow dung, wortand eggs, wortand beer, milk,
gluten, buttermilk, cheese, curdled milk, saponified beeswax...
all intended to improve its adhesive qualities’ (Davey). Hair is
also used in plasterwork to give added mechanical strength.

Animal dung is commonly used in many parts ofthe world in
daub and plasterwork. The Maasai, for instance, daub dung on
to the wattle roofs and walls oftheir houses. Clay and dung are
often mixed together for the ‘plastering’ ofhouses in Nepaland
Ladakh. The Nankani and Kassena do a kind of adobe work,
making clods ofearth mixed with dung into flattened bricks for
their houses, and then plastering them from the outside with a
blend ofsand, fresh cow dung and water.

Adhesives of animal glue are made by boiling the skins,
bones and horns ofanimals for long periods to produce gelatinous protein materials (collagen and ossein) which setas gels.
Fish glue is made by boiling the skin, bones and offal offish.

The properties of sinew and, to some extent, rawhide are
such that when used for lashing or binding they are literally
self-adhesive. The use of animal resources for the making of
glues and adhesives is worldwide. A glue could be made from
such parts ofthe animal as the muzzle or the sinews ofits feet.
Boiled and strained the mixture made a powerful adhesive
which could be used in carpentry. Lighter glues were made
from shreds and clippings of parchment made from the skins
of’goats, which were washed and soaked for a full day before
boiling. Such size glues could be used as a binder for pigments

and, when ground with plaster of Paris, could be laid as a gesso
ground for painting and gilding on wood.

After softening with water, cheese could be mixed with
quicklime to make a glue for bonding wood panels; in recent
times even condensed milk from the can has provided an
effective bond for wall tiles.

Dairy products were also used in decorating with casein
paint made ftom curds, milk being a suitable emulsifier,
though one which had a disagreeable smell in use. Eggs were
extensively employed in painting; the yolk was mixed with pigments to form a medium foratempera technique, theyolkofan
egg beingan emulsion ofoiland albumen which sets hard with
a clear and smooth surface, ideal for decorative painting on
door panels and integral furniture.

Animal hair and bristle have been used for paint brushes in
most parts ofthe world, and the hairs of squirrels and sables
made fine brushes for detailed decorative painting in Russia

and central Europe. For coarser work or for broad areas of
colour large brushes could be made ofhog and pig bristle. Its
softness and pliability allowed large quantities to becharged on
the brush. Thus it was useful for distempering. A mixture of
whiting or white lime and water with size glue made a whitewash distemper which has been extensively used in European

Wooden lock senyı
with camel sinay, |
(See also fig, zug) Ik

ai 1,1950

countries. Though the glue tended to discolour the whitewash,
this could be counteracted by adding blood and red ochre.

Blood was used in Nordic countries mixed with red ochre
and milk curds to produce a red, casein-based protective paint
for external wood walls. Cattle-raising peoples often mixed
calves’ blood or ox blood with a fine loam ofclayto make ahard
and shiny wall or flooring surface.

The use of animal materials for interior decoration is very
wide-ranging, from wool tapestries, to silk curtains; from felt
amulets and embroidered protective designs, to animal horns
fixed over doorways; from inlaid ivory in furniture to displays of
highly decorated bedding and trapped animal skins. Shell is
worth specifically mentioning and was used quite extensively
by maritime peoples. The Maori used mother of pearl to decorate the carvings in their assembly houses, and coral was said to
have been used to decorate portals in Tonga. The Kwakiutl use
abalone in decoration, and in the Philippines shell is used in
small grid-like frames for windows. Ostrich eggshells were used
in a finial to crestthatched roofs in some parts of South Africa.

It should be emphasized that in many parts of the world
these materials are no longer being used, and the techniques
are no longer being practised. The introduction of other materials such as canvas, metals and plastics, alongside the use of
factory machinery for spinning, weaving, tanning and feltmak-

ing, is largely responsible. STEPHANIE BUNN

1ıva-B Animal Shelter

No discussion of animal products used in vernacular architecture can ignore the question of the influence ofan
essential animal product: the shelter that certain species construct for themselves. Not all genera and species build shelters
— the bovine, equine, feline and porcine animals, known in
different species in several continents, are among those thatdo
not. But many ofthe smaller animals, including birds, rodents
and insects construct shelters or nests, or modify their environ-

Animal Products 1.1.1
ment by procedures and manipulation of resources which
correspond in many respects with building. The subject is
fraughtwith problems, often leading to expressions ofsurprise
at animal “ingenuity’ and the projection of conscious purpose
and will on the habits of animals that are instinctive. On the
other hand, it can lead to assumptions that building by humans
is also instinctual; neither is contended here.

The great Victorian railroad engineer Isambard Kingdom Brunel
invented the rotary shield for boringthe Thames Tunnel as a direct
result of his study of the rotational movement of the serratededged shell ofthe so-called ‘shipworm’ mollusc, and the liningof
its tunnel with a calcareous substance (Wood). Brunel was a singular genius whose processes of problem isolation and solution
by analogy were far removed from customary vernacular practice, but it is not inconceivable that indigenous builders found
inspiration in the activities of the creatures with which they
shared the land. Direct mimesis is less likely than experiment
based on observation, within the limits of available knowledge
and levels of environmental awareness. So, for instance, the use
ofthe earth ofant-hills for building purposes has been reported
among a number of cultures in Africa and Australia, but the
processes of making it could not be imitated without a knowledge ofthe secretions and excretions used byants and termitesto
bind the material. Similarly, the remarkable ventilation systems
within termite mounds are unlikely to be perceived as such and
imitated in human constructions.

With these considerations in mind it is nevertheless possible
that some animal processes may have been copied. The Ploceidae, the large family of‘weaver birds’, are extensively distributed
in Asia and Africa. Though there would have been no motivation to have imitated their nests, the remarkable range ofknots
and weaves used by the many species could have stimulated
human tying techniques. Spiral coils alternating weaves and
reverse bindings may be combined with single and interlocking
loops. Half-hitches, overhand and slipknots are among the
tying methods used by weaver birds, who learn to improve their
techniques and practise them on loose grasses (Hancocks). As
many as 10.000 knots and weaves made with ‘thrust and draw’
shuttle movements are needed to make a single oriole’s nest.
Literal stitching is used by the Euro-Asian tit Tamiz pendulinus,
with male and female bird working inside and outside the nest
respectively, while the tailor-bird uses its beak as a needle to sew
leaves with natural threads (Goodfellow).

Numerous species of birds and insects use clay to construct
their nests. In some cases the earth is supplemented by small
stones, and the inner surfaces may be plastered, or lined with
an insulating layer of feathers and other material, which
humans must often have witnessed. Itis possible that Eumenes,
the potter wasp, which builds narrow-necked jar-like nesting
chambers from pellets of clay, may have been the inspiration
for Native Americans who modelled clay pots without the
wheel. Butso have other cultures which have nothad the model
ofthe potter wasp’s vessels before them. There is evidence that
study of the nest-building propensities of other wasps led the
Chinese to develop the manufacture of paper (Frisch). Nevertheless, the efficient, close-packing geometry ofthe combs of
honeybees does notseem to have led to experiments in building

197

## S. 49

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
with hexagonal prisms or tetrahedra in vernacular contexts,
despite their structural advantages.

Ofthe many forms ofdwelling built byrodents, such as marmots, harvest mice and wood rats, none are more remarkable
than the dams ofthe beaver. Dams of too m (330 ft) ormorein
length are not uncommon, though the Jefferson river dam in
Montana, some 700 m (2300 ft) in length, was probably the
largest on record. Beavers build canals in order to transportthe
logs and branches they fell, and use available materials to construct their dams and lodges, sometimes building entirely in
mud and stones or even coal. They ‘ram strong sticks into the
bottom ofa stream or river-bed with considerable force’, fillthe
spaces with twigs held down with heavier branches, use forked
branches against them to resist the water pressure, insert crosspieces and use heavy stones for reinforcement. Making the
structure watertight with packed clay, beavers adapt the dam to
suitchanges in water level. Building in family and social groups,
they construct well-ventilated and insulated lodges which offer
protection, sometimes for centuries of continued occupancy.
Among animals beavers are exceptional in adapting an environment ‘which in its natural state is not fitted to their needs’
and transforming it ‘in such a way that it becomes suitable for
their settlements’ (Frisch).

Beavers were rendered virtually extinctin most ofEurope but
in Siberia and in North America their building activities were
widely reported, and often exaggerated. Little evidence was
given of any relationship between their extensive landscapetransforming constructions and the building structures or
methods of the peoples in the same regions. The legends and
myths of many peoples frequently use the behaviours of animals to point morals, though often through personification
and hence by attributing to them human thoughts and speech.
The problem remains as to whether any examples of animal
building behaviour were assimilated by the human cultures in
proximity with them, either as formative models or held in oral
lore as continuing examples; the sensitive subject requires
detailed and impartial inter-species and cross-cultural comparative research. PAUL OLIVER
1.1V1.a Bone
The use ofbone in vernacular architecture is especially
common in extreme climates, usually where there is a scarcity
ofany otherbuilding material- even wood. Ithas been frequently used by hunter-gatherers who live around the Arctic Circle.

The Alaskan and Greenland Inuit, as well as the maritime
Chukchi, are historically reported to have used whole whale jaw
and rib bones in constructing the frames of their dwellings.
Bogaras described two types ofwhalebone dwelling among the
Chukchi. There were tents where whale rib bones replaced
wood, and also quite ancient jawbone houses’ made of bone
and sod which were often partlyunderground. Nelson, in 1899,
described similar dwellings still lived in by the Inuit off the
Bering Strait. These had a 0.6-ı m (2-3 ft) high oval stone wall
on which was a framework of whale ribs lashed to the whale’s
jawbone which was positioned near the door. The whole thing
was covered with walrus hides. The Chukchi also used whalebones as wall supports in their underground provision cellars.

198

The Inuit made the use of bone into a fine art. Ifthere was no
wood for their ridge tent frames, they would modify the tent
structure slightly, making the centre pole from spliced antler or
other bone. They had great skill at piecing together short sections ofbone to make spliced pieces which had greater strength
than awooden pole.

Until the advent of metal, many Arctic people’s tools were
also made from bone. Many ofthese are important in the construction of dwellings. The Netsilik used snow shovels made
from split antlers with sealskins stretched across for building
iglus. The Chukchi used shovels made from wood and walrus
shoulder blades for cutting sod. The Inuit used strong birds’
wing bones for needles in the sewing of skin tents. Many
household items such as antler drying racks were also made
from bone. Clearly, in an area where other natural building
materials were very scarce, people made use of what was available, with maximum ingenuity and minimum waste.

Among other indigenous people, bone was generallyreserved
for the smaller details ofthe dwelling. For example, the Himalayan herdsmen ofnorthern Nepal sometimes used goats’ horn
for tentpegs. Yak horn is used for toggles in Tibetan black tents.
Bone toggles were used on tipis. In Pisange in the Himalayas,
yak horn is used for headers at the base ofwooden walls. Teeth
were used to decorate some buildings among South Sea Island
cultures. In India, ivory is sometimes used for decorative inlays
on doors and wooden furniture. The placing of.a pair of animal’s horns over the doorway is a fairly common phenomenon

worldwide, from North America to Lappland and Central Asia,
and seems to actas both symbol and decoration.

Finally, when ground down and boiled up in caustic soda,
hoofs (and horns and hair) can be transformed into keratin,
which is used to delay the setting point of plaster of Paris. This
is important in decorative plasterwork. STEPHANIE BUNN
1.1V1.b Dung
Dung is the by-product ofthe transformation offood

into energy. Its use in building is restricted to animal by-products, which are used extensively by cultures whose economies
are based on animal husbandry.

It is a frequently used additive to mud to form mud-plaster,
as amongstthe Maasai where straw is also added to the mixture
and the whole is leftto ferment. In Hausaland, donkey or horse
dungis mixed with earth and grassanda binding substance katsi,
or makubu used in the dyeing trade, made from the pod ofthe
locustbean tree, Parkia Biglobosa, and also leftto ferment (Krause,
1907; Greenwood); it is also used in the Sudan, where the fermented mixture ofdonkey dung is known aszibala (Danby) and
amongst the Barabaig (Klima) where itis used as abinder.

Dung has the additional advantage of acting as an insect
repellent. Research in George, a squatter area in Lusaka, reveals
that dung also adds to the longevity ofthe plaster, making itless
subjectto erosion, a principle cause for its failure. Despite these
Properties, its use is being attacked as cultural values and traditions are subjected to changing technologies and economies. In
Botswana the use of dung in plaster is believed to contribute to
the spread of tuberculosis. This belief and, in urban areas, the
difficulty in obtaining ready access to sources ofdung, will bea
major factor in its decline. The inevitable switch to imported or
less traditional building materials, such as cement brick or
block, plaster and corrugated iron, has resulted from a combination ofaspiration, availability, subsidy in the form of building

materials from government or donor-run housing programmes
and, in a less agriculturally based and more mobile population,
the need for reduced building maintenance. Its useasa building
material may also be affected by its use as a source offuel. Klima

Animal Products 1.V.1

notes how it is used for building by the Barabaig who have a
ready source of firewood to hand. In the Indian subcontinent
fuel is dried in circular discs by forming a paste and throwing
this against the wall ofthe housetto dtyin thesun. Thesun-dried
discs are then stacked along the parapets ofthe roof.

Coxe-Stevenson noted that cattle and goats’ blood was also
used as an additive amongst the Sia, particularly on floors,
where it was added as a final coat to an already stamped and
smoothed combination and freshly dug earth. Read notes how
the Ngoni use ant-hill as an additive, with river mud and cow
dung, and that the floor is polished with damp ashes and a
smear ofcow dung rubbed in with a smooth stone. A final high
polish is raised by rubbing the floor with clarified fat.

Montoy notes that with the Kachcha house of central India,
or houses made of traditional materials, there is a purifying
aspect to the periodic repairs, attributed to the magical properties in the cow dung.

Traditionally, the Maasai would not dig in the ground under
any circumstances and therefore plastered their houses with
cow dung, without mud. More recently, where a shortage of
cow dung is experienced and attitudes are changing, mud is
used as asubstitute. DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

1.1V1.c Felt

Felt is said to be the oldest known textile. In building
its significant use is to cover the nomadic trellis tents in Central
Asia. These tents are called yurts in the West; theyarethegersof
Mongolia and the oys ofthe Turkic-speaking people ofXinjiang
in China, and Kazakhstan, Kirghizia, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan, in inner Asia.

Felt is made from sheep’s wool. The size ofa family’s yurt
used to depend on the size oftheir herd ofsheep, because this
determined how much wool they had. The effects of collective
farming in the former Soviet Union has to some extent changed
this, as have, more seriously, the effects ofthe replacement of
traditional regional sheep with a more uniform breed kept for
meat. The wool ofthese new sheep is oflittle use for felting.

Usually, three or four pieces of oblong felt are used to cover
the sides ofa yurt. For the roof, two large semicircular pieces of
feltare used. There is asquare rooffelt used to cover the central
erown ofthe yurt, and this is opened and closed for ventilation.
Turkic yurts still sometimes have felt doors which arerolled up.
These are usually stitched and appliqued.

Felt is made by a similar method across Central Asia. The
main variations are in techniques ofrolling. Firstly the wool is
combed, bowed or beaten with sticks to separate the fibres. Itis
then laid down in even alternate layers on to an old reed mat, an
old felt (‘the mother offelts’) or a skin. There may be sixto ten

layers of wool which can reach a depth of 20-25 cm (8-10 in)
altogether. For the yurt cover plain white felt is preferred,
although floor felts and mosaic felts (shyrdak or surmac) will be
coloured. The wool is then sprinkled with hot water and sap
and rolled up carefully and tightly like a carpet. Once rolled up,
itis rolled backwards and forwards, and this movement, along
with the effects of hot water and soap, causes the fibres ofthe
woolto alterand become felted. Feltingis caused entirely bythe
properties inherent in the wool fibre.

199

N BE ESERRREE|

## S. 50

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

el

The felt will be rolled for about 20-30 minutes in one direction, and then unrolled and rolled up again from a different
side. The length oftime to felt wool depends very much on the
type ofwool and can take one and a half hours or more.

As many people as possible help to roll felt and this speeds
up the process. Feltmaking is often an excuse for a festive occasion and usually happens in the summer after shearing. Among
the Turkic Islamic peoples feltmaking is usually women’s
work. The women may work on their knees, using elbow pressure to roll the felt. Or the roll may be pulled backwards and
forwards with a rope. Among the Mongols the feltmaking is
done by both sexes and theroll is often pulled bya horse ridden
across the steppe by a Mongol horseman. Sadly, though, nowadays in Mongolia, many tents are covered with factory-made
cotton wadding. STEPHANIE BUNN

Ostepnanie Bunn

200

1.|V1.d Hair

While ‘hair’ is the general name for the fibres which
grow from an animal’s skin, it is important to differentiate
between the stronger, smoother more tensile qualities of hair
from animals such as goats and horses, and the finer, more
elastic qualities of sheep’s wool or camel’s hair. Hair has a
number of bracts, or scales, that cause it to interlock. The
shorter and finer the hair, the greater the number ofbracts and
so the better its qualities in spinning.

In architecture, probably the most significant use of hair is
for the woven covers of the black tents used by nomads from
Morocco to Tibet. The preferred fibre for almost all black tents
is goat hair. The main exception is in Tibet, where yak hair is
used. Both these kinds of hair have the right strength, length
and tensile qualities to fulfil the tensile requirements ofa black
tent. Sheep wool would be too stretchy, camel hair too short
and weak. Even where other fibres are mixed in, a certain percentage of goat’s hair is always needed.

The hair ofthe black tent is spun on a drop spindle, the oldest method ofspinning. A drop spindle consists of a stick with
a weight at one end. An initial thread is tied on and then the
spindle is dropped down and spun at the same time, while new
fibres are twisted into the original thread. It is a much slower
method than using a spinning wheel, but the spinner is free to
move about, so women and girls spin while walking, riding,
playing and so on - an advantage as spinning is so time-consuminganda lot ofthread is required to weave a black tent.

Theloom is ahorizontal ground loom, which is easy to setup
and can be taken down and rolled up without spoiling the work
when it is time to move on. The width ofthe woven cloth is the
basic unit from which the black tent cover is made up, several
pieces being sewn together lengthways. So the width of the
loom is directly proportional to the size ofthe tent. Even in biblical times, this system of construction is mentioned — the
‘eleven curtains of the tabernacle’ spoken ofin Exodus are the
tent cloth breadths.

Women weave the separate lengths alone, and then sewthem
together in groups. A tent cloth will last five or six years. The
loosely woven goat hair, or mix, is aresponsive material. When
it rains, the fibres swell as they take up the water, and this
together with the natural oiliness of the hair serves to keep out
much ofthe water. When the weather is hot and dry, the relatively open weave lets air flow through. The main disadvantage
is that if the tent becomes sodden with rain, it is almost too
heavy for the animals to carry.

Many black tent dwellers also sometimes use tent ropes
made from goat hair, as do the Tuareg. The Tibetans use yakhair ropes and yurt dwellers such as Kirghiz or Tuvinians make
ropes for their tents and braids from the hair of the horse’s
mane and tail. The tail hair is especially prized. Horsehair was
also used in mattresses in Europe until quite recently. Occasionally goat hair and camel hair are used in the many woven
artefacts - from bags to tension bands for carpets - essential to
the life ofthe nomadic tent dweller. The Turkmen, for example,
are famous for their knotted rugs and bags and they sometimes
use goat hair in the warp (which has to be particularly strong),
and in the kilim found at each end ofa carpet. Only fine goat

See also for 1.lVte
1.1X.8.x Yurt
2.1.2.f Mongol

Hyde, N.,1988

left Fr
Felt being prepäl
rolling up, Tamel!
village, Kirghizia

© Christopher Sainsbury

hair is used however; it is generally considered too coarse for
the sortofwork and wool is preferred.

In plasterwork, animal hair also has its uses — mainly to give
mechanical strength. Hair and lime form the base coat for Italjan scagiola work and, until very recently, chalk, lime, sand and
calf'hair were used together to make up English plaster.

STEPHANIE BUNN
LIVA.e Rawhide
Strips ofhide used as a form ofrope and known as
‘rawhide’ were employed by Native American peoples of the
southwest United States, and by Hispanic settlers there and in
Latin America. The hide might be that of a deer or sheep,
though horse and cow hide were also used. The methods of
skinning used by Navajo women involved the cutting of a slit
around theneck ofthe butchered animal, and a further slitfrom
sternum to neck. Other cuts were made round the legs on the
ventrical side and, after the hoofs were severed, the skin was
peeled back from the leg. The skin ofthe belly was cut and the
hide flayed from the leftside ofthe animal, though itremained
attached at the spine. The carcass was then turned over and the
process repeated, before the carcass was hung from a tree,
where the remainder ofthe hide was removed.

Once flayed, the hide was cleaned of remaining flesh, dried
and then rubbed with fat; after this itwas buried in damp earth
for a day, before being softened by being beaten with a rock.
Laid over a post or table, the hide was scraped of hair and then
stretched before being placed by a fire. Fat and tallow were
forced into it by hand, after which the pelt was buried in wet
ground for two days. When it was to be used as a form ofrope
the hide was cut spirally from the outside edge to the centre to
create a continuous length; finally, it was drawn through the
fork of tree to make it more flexible.

Similar methods of preparing rawhide appear to have been
followed among Spanish settlers in Arizona and New Mexico.
The rawhide rope was used for joining beams in building construction, the section permitting a flat lashing. It would be
soaked with water before being used, so that the rawhide was
stretched and malleable. After lashing it was exposed to the
heat of the sun, which caused it to contract and make a tight
joint. In Chile, panels of cactus board were drilled and joined
with rawhide rope, often in a crossed pattern to distribute the
tension and tauten the joint.

PAUL OLIVER

Animal Products 1.1V.1

1.1v1.f Shell

The shells of sea and freshwater fish have been used
for various purposes in vernacular architecture, Sea-shell is
chemically the same as chalk, marble and limestone (CaCO3),
and a number of cultures burn large quantities of sea-shells to
producea fine quality oflime. Lime from shells has also been used
as a flux in metal-casting. Some shells are translucent, and the
capiz shells, found in the shallow waters of the Capiz river and
elsewhere in the Philippines, have been used in narrow, gridded
wooden windows since the 17th century. Apart from presenting
an attractive appearance to the windows they give a soft light to
the interiors. Such shells are strong and resistant to typhoons.

Similar use is made of mother-of-pearl, nacre or karepas shells
which have a near rectangular form; they aremounted in wooden
window-frames in Goa, Macau and other former Portuguese
possessions.

Mollusc, or oyster, shells have also been used as building
material in North America, with ‘tabby’ having a local application on the East coast. Manucy acknowledges the derivation of
the English word ‘tabby’ from the Spanish tapia, indicating that
it was the Spanish who introduced the technique to Florida in
the 17th century. One ofthe earliest published references to the
word ‘tabby’ is in J. Morgan’s A Complete History of Algiers, London, 1731, where he describes its use in the construction ofthe
Casbah in Algiers. The technique is also described in Ibn Khaldun’s The Magaddimah, although by a different term in Rosenthal’s English translation. It is probable that the technique
takes its name originally, as does the cloth ‘tabby’, from Al
Attabiyah, Baghdad, for itwas, and still is, widely used in Iraq.

PAUL OLIVER and A.D.C.HYLAND

>

SEE

D

20I

EITRENENDENE

## S. 51

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

1.1V.1f-1 Tabby
Tabby is a type of primitive concrete made from
equal portions of lime, sand and oyster shells that was used
along the southeastern Atlantic coast of the United States primarily during the ı8th and ıgth centuries. A few local artisans
in the area still recall how to build with this material butthere is
little demand for it. The appearance of tabby is sometimes
achieved in contemporary construction by mixing in oyster
shells with conventional concrete. The term ‘tabby’, sometimes written or pronounced as ‘tappy’, is thought to derive
from the Spanish word tapia (mud or adobe mixed with lime),
but African origins for the name have also been suggested.

Colonial period settlers first employed tabby for the massive
walls of their forts in South Carolina as early as 1732 but the
material was more widely used in Georgia and Florida not only
at military sites but for houses and other domestic structures
until about 1750. After a hiatus of approximately 65 years,
Thomas Spalding, master of Ashantilly plantation near Darien,
Georgia, revived interest in the material. He used tabby for his
own two and a half storey residence and for his barns, mills,
and slave houses as well. Because these slave dwellings proved
so durable in the face ofthe annual hurricanes thatrravaged the
area neighbouring planters quickly saw the advantage of Spalding’s actions. After Spalding published an article in the Southern
Agriculturist in 1830 that not only extolled the virtues of tabby
butalso provided a careful description ofthe procedures to be
followed in raising walls with plank forms, tabby buildings
became aregular feature ofplantations alongthe Georgia coast
— some of the more venturesome builders even worked out
ways to construct tabby roofs.

Tabby is often confused on site with cocina stone. Cocinaisa
sedimentary stone composed of oyster shells and sand that is

© Paul Ollver 1972

quarried into blocks and then mortared with lime-based
cement to form a wall. Tabby walls raised in courses look very
much like cocina but obviously are made in a very different
manner. Tabby is basically an artificial creation of a naturally
occurring substance. JOHN MICHAEL VLACH
1.1V1g Skin and hide: Kirghiz

The word ‘hide’ usually refers to the skin of larger
animals — buffalo, horse or cattle, and ‘skin’ to that of smaller
animals — deer, goats, sheep, fish and so on. Skins and hides
were, and often are, used for frame tent covers, and for tent
accessories by hunter-gatherer people and pastoral nomads.
People used the skins of animals to which they had ready
access, either through herding them or through hunting them.
So, the coastal and Greenland Inuit used sealskin, the inland
Inuitand the Caribou Chukchi and other Siberian nomads used
caribou (reindeer) skin, the Plains Indians used buffalo hides,
the Okhotsk coastal dwellers used fishskins, the Tuareg still
use goatskin.

Apart from the variations in types of animal skins, there are
also significant differences in how they are treated. Once an
animal has been killed, it is only a matter of hours before it
begins to decompose, so itis cured or processed in some way to
prevent this. The aim is to convertan otherwise perishable skin
to a more stable and non-decayable one. The variations in
method depend on the particular cultural technique for curing,
the kind of skin used, the needs that the people have of the
skins — such as warmth and lightness - the location oftentand
time ofyear, and the precise use ofthe skin, whether for covers,
doors, windows and so on.

The basic principle of curing is as follows. Firstly, decay has
to be stopped until the treatment proper begins, so the skins
are usually dried straightaway or occasionally salted or pickled.
The skin is then soaked to remove any water-soluble materials
which might rot, and to make it more pliable and easy to work.
If it is necessary to remove the hair, it is often loosened at this
stage by soaking the skin in a solution of lime or letting it
decompose slightly for a few days. Then the hairs are scraped
off. All extra flesh and fat must be scraped away at this stage,
too. Scraping can be a very laborious process; Netsilik women
not only scraped their sealskins but sometimes also chewed
them thoroughly in order to break down and suck out the fat.
After the scraping, the hide is then washed again, and at this
point either simply dried until used, or else tanned, and sometimes dressed.

Tanning is the process by which the skin is converted to
leather. This is done by treating the skin with some kind oftanning agent which strengthens the fibrous protein in the skin
and dissolves allthe fats and non-fibrous proteins which might
cause rotting. Many indigenous peoples use vegetable tannins
found in barks, roots, berries and leaves. For example, having
scraped their reindeer skins and covered them with dung for a
night, the Chukchithen rubbed them with alder bark steeped in
human urine. This acted as both a dye and a tanning agent.

Another method oftanning requires the skin to be soaked in
fish or animal oiland pounded until the natural moisture ofthe
original skin has been replaced by the oil. This method is used

see also |
3.V1.6.N Rice Cogy

‚nddrying of
no, Nigeria.

References
Floyd, M.,1937
Griztner, 3.8.,1978,

Tabby wall, slave
quarters, South
Carolina, Unitedstale

Itmade from
ern Sahara.

for chamois leather and deerskin, skin which can be repeatediy
wetted and dried without damage. It is somewhat akin to the
way that buffalo hide was tanned by the Plains Indians using
buffalo brains, and the way that the taiga dwellers of Siberia
used deer liver to tan caribou skins.

The frequent scraping, beating or chewing function not only
to remove unwanted flesh butalso to soften the skin and make it
supple. This part ofthe process is known as dressing. The more
the skin is treated like this, the softer it becomes. Peoples who
excel at work with skins and leather, for example, the Chukchi,
the Tuvinians or the Tuareg, have often developed their own
system and specialist tools for this part ofthe process.

Finally, the skin will sometimes be smoked - this is said to
waterproof skin and was done by the Plains Indians, the taiga
dwellers and the Chukchi on their tent skins. The Tuareg, on the
other hand, rub butter into their skins to waterproof them and
then rub in red ochre and camel dung to dye them a reddish yellow. Sealskin is naturally waterproof. The skins are usually sewn
together with a sinew or a leather strip, usinga bone needle.

The requirements ofa tent have resulted in much variety in
treatment ofskins. For example, while a tent needs to be lightweightitalso needs to bewarm, and this determines how much

Animal Products

1.0.1

Bar

hair is left on. The Indians ofthe North American taiga killed
caribou for their tents when their winter fur was at its thickest
to ensure the best insulation. Summer tents were usually made
hairless and lighter. The Chukchi and the Koryak cycled the
skins on their tents to maximize warmth and wear. Theyfirstused
caribou skins with all the hair on for their inner sleeping tents,
then with the hair trimmed down for the outer cover ofthe winter tentand finally, when worn thin, for the summer tent cover.

Light was also important. The Greenland Inuit scraped their
sealskin tent covers thin and transparent near the entrance to
the tent to let in the light. Walrus gut was sometimes used by
Chukchi for yaranga windows, and the Nunamiut of north
Alaska used bear intestines for the same purpose.

Ventilation is a weak point of skin tents, and condensation
can be a problem. The Tuareg of North Africa who use either
mat or skin tents find skin much more suitable for the wet season because it is waterproof. In the dry season, however, it is
harder to ventilate than woven mats, so the skin tents are rarely
used or else are raised offthe ground.

The Chukchi resolved this problem in the following way. The
inner sleeping tents would get so wet each night that they were
put outside each morning in the snow. The moisture froze and
the ice was then beaten out by thewomen.

Skins were also used for tent fixtures and fittings. The Plains
Indians used rawhide for trunks and sometimes stretched it
across a willow frame for the tipi door. Central Asian nomads
like the Tuvinians and the Kirghiz used leather for containers to
hold liquids — especially for brewing fermented mare’s milk
(kumiss). Furs were almost always used by Arctic peoples for floor
covering and bedding. These were usually laid over a layer of
insulation such as caribou ribs, willow twigs or spruce branches.

Skin was also used for thongs, usually in the form ofuntanned
rawhide. The Netsilik made thongs by cutting scrap sealskin
into wide rings. They removed these from the animal’s body,
scraped off the blubber and then cut each ring spirally into a
long thong, which they stretched under great tension between
two rocks. These rawhide thongs were used plaited for tent
guys or for lashing poles together. Because rawhide has not
been tanned, if soaked and then stretched it will dry very tight

203

7961 3anlo InEa

## S. 52

1.IV MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

and hard. This makes it very strong in binding and it is used as
such in many parts ofthe world. The Mexicans and Spanish use
rawhide for lashing timbers. The Kirghiz use rawhide for the
links which join the laths of their tent trellises. It is threaded
through these laths while wet and supple, and then dries so
hard that each one is called in Kirghiz goq, which means nail.
The paisano Indians of Argentina stretch rawhide between
poles, like wire, and suspend grass over itto make grass walls.

In most parts ofthe world the major part ofthe very arduous
work necessary for the preparation of skins is done by women,
although in some areas, the men take on the heavier tasks such
as stretching sealskins.

Itis importantto emphasize that many ofthese methods and
techniques are no longer practised. This is because of the
change in lifestyle ofmany nomadic people and also because of
the introduction ofnew materials like canvas and plastic.

STEPHANIE BUNN

1.1V.1.g-1 Skin and hide: Inuit

Atthe beginning ofthe 2oth century Canadian Inuit,
especially Inuit living in the central Arctic, were nomadic and
had very little contact with non-Natives. Whalers influenced
specific groups in the eastern and western Arctic during the
180os. Missionaries, teachers, doctors, traders and other non-
Natives began interacting with Inuit by around the 1920s, the
dates varying from one region to the next. Prior to interaction
with non-Natives, Inuit material culture was heavily influenced
by the natural resources, environmentand cultural needs.

At the turn of the century, many Inuit depended on seals,
caribou, waterfowl, fish and a wide variety of other northern
animals for their clothing, food, tools, medicinal needs and
shelter. Inuit in the eastern Arctic used skin tents. The type of
skins used for tents varied with the availability of specific natural resources. Sealskins were used for tents by coastal Inuit.
Large ringed seals, harp seals and bearded seals were used,
depending on the availability of these skins. Skins from
bearded seals are quite thick and heavy; therefore, they were
sliced in halfto create two thinner, lighter skins that covered
twice as much area. This was done after the fat had been
removed with a sharp curved, woman’s knife (ulu). Skins were
washed in cold water and then hung out to dry partially. Once
the excess water was removed, skins were lashed to awooden
frame and allowed to dry completely. Usually the hair was left
on the skins used for tents; however, sometimes the hair was
shaved off. The dry skins were then sewn together usinga bone
or ivory needle, animal sinew and a waterproof stitch. Before
being sewn the ‘seam allowances’ or edges of each skin were
made pliable by being moistened. These production procedures are still used today by women interested in making a
traditional tent. Metal needles and cotton-polyester thread are
usually used instead ofthe historical materials.

Caribou skins were used for tents by Inuitliving in the inland
areas where it was difficult to get access to sealskins and there
were plenty of caribou. The fat and excess flesh were scraped
off the caribou skin before laying the skin out to dıy. A few
rocks were placed around the edge of the skin to prevent the
edges from curling. When a lighter tent was needed, the hair

204

was removed from caribou skins either by soaking them in
water until the hair loosened or by shaving offthe excess hair.
Dried skins were sewn together using the same materials and
stitch used for making sealskin tents. Contemporary Inuit live
primarily in centrally heated, wooden homes with all the modern amenities. When tents are used today they are made mainly
from canvas rather than skins. JILL OAKES

1.1V.1.g-Ii Skin and hide: Plains

Within the broad area of the Plains numerous different cultures thrive, including the Blackfeet, Sarsi, Plains Creeand
Plains Ojibwa, and the Assiniboin in the prairie region of Canada.
The warm, dry grasslands provided an ideal habitat for buffalo,
rabbit, prairie dogs and antelope. These resources were used
extensively for food, clothing, shelter and medicinal purposes.

Buffalo skins were prepared for tanning by removing the
excess fat, meatand fascia. Then the skins were stacked outon
the ground to dry. While they dried, animals’ brains were worked
into the skin in order to make itsoft and pliable. New tent (tipi)
hides were smoked in order to keep them supple and to make
them water-repellent.

Brain-tanned skins were then used for tipis, lodge covers, lodge
doors, lodge linings, bed covers, ornaments, robes, footwear and
other items such asrope. Each group of Plains Indians setup their
tipis in a manner unique to their region. The number of poles,
construction procedures and assembly methods varied regionally. Before horses were acquired by the Blackfeet, their tipis
required about five buffalo skins. Once they began using horses,
their tipis were increased in size using about 14 buffalo skins and
about 23 poles. Larger structures were used by prestigious warriors, and poor people had much smaller tipis. Designs such as
bison, bison skulls, tadpole-like figures and large circles were
painted on the tipiskins. The combination ofdesigns symbolized
a variety ofthings including a historical record of the significant
events thattook place over a period oftime.
