Contemporary Plains Indians live in modern, permanent
wood or concrete homes. At large gatherings canvas tipis are
commonly used. These structures are decorated using traditional symbols and some modern symbols. Skins are rarely
used today because ofthe lack of buffalo or other equally large
animals in the prairie region.

JILLOAKES

1.1.1.h Wool

Wool has been used in textiles for several thousand
years, especially by nomadic pastoralists who keep sheep.
Nowhere did it play such an important early role as among the
nomads of Southwest and Central Asia. The oldest wool pile
carpet in the world, the Pazyryck Carpet (sth century BC), was
found in the tomb ofa nomadic chiefin southern Siberia. Even
today, many peoples ofthis area still use wool made into feltto
cover their trellis tents or yurts and for floorcoverings and bags.

The properties of wool which make it so important include
the ability ofthe fibres to trap heatand keep in warmth, its elasticity, its natural flame resistance and its ability to absorb noise
and odours. While initially waterproof, when the fibres do take
in water, they give off'heat, so thatwhen wearing wool one can
feel warm when wet. The helical fibre structure of overlapping
scales enables wool to be easily spun into yarn for cloth and
also be transformed into felt - and felt is both insulating and
quite water-resistant.

There are other animals that grow fleece which can be spun
or felted, but the word wool is almost totally associated with
sheep. However, some of the world’s finest ‘wools’ are in fact
the hair of other animals. These include angora goats, the
camel (especially the Bactrian), the yak, the guanaco, the llama,
the alpaca, the vicuna, the goats of Kashmir (which produce
cashmere), Tibet and the Pamirs. Most animal hair is much
coarser (and stronger) than wool, however, and the scales are
less numerous, so it is harder to spin and felt.

Animal Products 1.1.1

There are a variety of different techniques used in the transformation offleece into fabricand these can be described as follows.
Firstly, wool has to be sorted. This involves dividing up a fleece
into different grades of wool, such as long fibres, short ones,
coarse onesand so on. Scouringinvolves cleaningthe wool ofdirt
and its natural grease. This is usually done by soaking the fleece
in hotwater and soap, butmany other substances have been used
such as soap wort, pounded yucca rootand urine.

The fibres have to be untangled and aligned. This can be done
by hand, but more usually is done by carding, which involves
using a comb, teasels or metal teeth on a brush. In some parts
ofthe world this untangling is done by bowing- for example in
Hungary and Tajikistan. Many Turkic peoples achieve the same
effect by beating the wool with two willow wands which lifts
and separates the fibres.

Spinning is the creation ofa continuous yarn by twisting the
fibres. This is done by usingeither a drop spindle or aspinning
wheel. Many nomadic people who keep sheep still use a drop
spindle. This is quite arhythmic and pleasanttask, butthe time
it takes is something that people who use factory-made fabrics
find hard to appreciate. Weaving is the creation of a fabric by
threadinga spun yarn backwards and forwards through a series
of parallel taut threads. All woven materials are made with the
help of a loom which holds these taut threads in place, and
under tension. The warp threads are the tautlongways threads,
and the weft is the yarn which is woven in between them. The
warp threads need to be strong, so theyare usually more tightly
spun and ofa more durable fibre than the weft. Theyshould not
easily stretch. The weft is more loosely spun and of a softer
material so that it can be beaten down well to cover the warp.
Most handwoven fabrics used in the house will either be flat
woven - called kilims when rugs- or knotted and so have pile.

For weaving, there are many different kinds ofloom, varying
according to the lifestyle ofthe people and the needs of mobility, what is to be made and the kind of material used for the

205

## S. 53

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

warp. One ofthe most basic is the warp-weighted loom which
holds the warp threads parallel under tension by tying them in
small bunches to weights of stone, pottery or metal. This kind
ofloom has been in use since neolithie times. Archaeological
evidence shows its use in Greece, Turkey and Scandinavia, and
it is still used in Norway today for rugs. A similar kind ofloom
was used by the Chilkat ofnorthwest America for weaving blankets until comparatively recently.

The horizontal ground loom is used by nomads, from the
Arabs and Berbers of North Africa to the Turks of Central Asia.
The Arabs weave a variety of carpets, kilims, tent bands, bags
and hangings. These looms can be pegged to the ground and
are easy to dismantle without damaging the work - so are ideal
for nomadic people.

In the Americas the use of wool is comparatively recent
because sheep were only introduced at the time ofthe Spanish
conquistadores. Before this, apart from cotton which was very
common, mountain goats’ hair was used, and the very soft hair
of the llama-related group of animals. North Pacific people,
such as the Salish, even wove with dog’s hair and bred a special
breed with long curly hair especially for weaving. (Unfortunately
they are now extinct.) Goose and duck feathers were often also
spun into the dog hair when used as warp to give added
strength.

In central and eastern Europe rug weaving is still done in the
home by some peasant families. Rugs are both flat weave (kilims) and knotted. Weaving may be done either on vertical or
horizontal looms. The horizontal looms usually have a device
called a heddle for separating the warp threads into upper and
lower sets (known as sheds) which makes weaving much easier. Often, several sheds are used alternately to create a pattern.
The heddles are often operated by a foot treadle which leaves
the hands free. Vertical looms do not have a treadle so theshed
usually has to be changed by hand and weaving is then amuch
slower process.

In Poland and the Ukraine the kilim is the most common
woven textile and the same designs (showing Turkish and Persian influence) have been used for centuries. In Romania and
Bulgaria both flat and tufted rugs are made. The advantage of
tufted rugs over kilims is that they tend to wear better, having
both a ground weave and a pile. Bulgarian tufted rugs (halishta)
are made mainly in the mountain regions from goat hair.

Nearly allwork with wool is done by women, although shearing may be seen as part of shepherding and therefore a man’s
task. Much wool weaving is now also done in factories.

STEPHANIE BUNN

1.1V.1.h-1 Wool: Turkic

The Turkmen ofIran and Turkmenistan are famous
for their knotted textiles. The women work in spring, using
wool ftom the first shearing. They used to collect plant materials for natural dyes, especially madder, which gave the carpets
their characteristic red colour. Aniline dyes took over from
these around the end of the last century. The ground loom is
threaded up as for flat weaving, but between each welt row a
row of knots are tied in. Then another welt row is woven
through to hold the knots in place. Several women sit working

206

attheloomatthe same time. Designs are knotted from memory
and are taught by the older women to the younger girls. Each
Turkmen tribe has its own characteristic motifs.

The width of the loom restricts the width ofthe weaving, so
only smaller pieces can be woven inside a tent. Larger weavings, such as carpets are either made outside or by settled Turkmen who have more space. Each woven Turkmen artefact has a
specific use and shape. There are carpets, door surrounds, animal trappings and bags for everything from cups to clothes to
salt. The interior ofa rich Turkmen yurt can be a visual feast of
bags suspended from the trellis, hangings and rugs, as well as
a pile of decorated bedding opposite the door. These days, as
more Turkmen become settled, the bags are made less, and a
family may weave only one or two carpets a year, as much for
capital investment or to sell, as for household use.

The Kirghizi are another nomadic Turkic people to make
good use of wool. Apart from feltmaking, they also use a kind
of ground loom (örmök) for weaving the many straps and bands
used to fasten their yurts. They also use lamb’s wool and camel
hair — both of which are very soft - for bedding. The Kirghizi
also make partitions (chij) from grasses wrapped with dyed
fleece and woven on a warp weighted loom. Ashkana chij are
used to divide off the kitchen in a Kirghiz yurt and chyrmagan
kanat chij are also stretched all around the tent between the felt
outer covering and the trellis. Ifthe felts are lifted or removed in
warm weather, the chij still covers the frame while allowingventilation. Felt is a fabric made out of raw fleece. This process
uses hot water, soap and friction, causing the fibres to shrink
and mat together— a unique natural property ofwool.

STEPHANIE BUNN

using a groundloon,

Loges, W.,1980

2.1.8.1 Turkmen!

v2 Earths and Clays

Covering much ofthe Earth’s surface, soils are readily accessible and, when mixed with water to a suitable consistency, they are also easily worked and moulded. Unstabilized
soil constitutes one ofthe earliest building materials and may
still be the most common domestic walling material in use
today. Clay is probably the mostubiquitous building material in
the world for vernacular architecture,

Soils are created when rocks are broken down by erosion and
weathering to form the larger particles consisting of gravels,
sands and silts; whilst in conjunction, silicates, soluble salts
and organic matter interact to form the considerably smaller
clay particles which are held together by the surface tension of
a water coating.

The suitability ofsoil for building varies enormously, not only
across the world’s surface butalso within the same building site.
Thus, despite its ease ofuse, and seeming economy, many buildings constructed of unstabilized soil have lacked strength and
durability for lack ofrigorous quality control ofthe raw material.

Classification and distribution ofbuilding soils

British Standards grade building soils according to the particle
size of their constituents. They are defined as a mixture of gravels (60-2 mm), sands (2-0.06 mm), silts (0.06-0.002 mm) and
clays (less than 0.002 mm). In those soils which can effectively
be used for building without further stabilization the clay acts as
abinder forthe sand and gravel particles. Silt, however, does not
contribute to cohesion, strength or durability and for this reason high proportions of this fraction are generally avoided.
Weaknesses occur when the moisture content of the clay
changes causing expansion or contraction. For this reason both

HNO!
WENDEN
Al

Fuer

the quantity and the plasticity of the clay is kept to a minimum
sufficient to fill voids and thinly coat the sand particles. Voids
are reduced by using sands and gravels with a wide range of particle sizes and by tapping, vibrating or compressing the mix.

Generally, those clays which are deposited nearer a river’s
source and which are lighter in both weightand colour, such as
kaolin, are of lower plasticity and therefore more useful than
the darker, heavier clays, such as montmorrillonite, which are
more likely to be deposited lower down the river, and more
specifically in delta regions. In addition, those soils which are
first wetted to a consistency suitable for moulding and then
dried hard in the sun and during this process shrink between 4
and 8 per cent are the most suitable for building. Those soils
which shrink less than 4 per cent probably contain too little
clay: those that shrink more than 8 per cent contain too much
ortoo plastic a clay and are likely to crack. Usually suitable soils
contain over 60 per centsandaand graveland between 20 and 30
per cent clay. Often the mixture of different soils is necessary to
achieve the required plasticity.

The above considerations go a long way towards describing
the characteristics ofbuildingssoils found in temperate and arid
zones. However in tropical zones, the abundant supplies oflaterites and lateritic soils are used for building without mixing,
whilst their performance depends as much on the way in which
the material has been formed and upon its chemical and mineralogical composition as upon the clay type and particle size
distribution. Laterites are found throughout the tropical or
subtropical zones of Central and South America, Africa, parts
ofIndia, Asia and Australia. They have been broken down more
finely than other soils. They contain ferric oxide and are usually
reddish brown, but can also be ochre or very dark in colour.
Buildings constructed of these soils are stronger and more
durable than those constructed of similar, non-lateritic soils.
Laterites are classified according to the proportion of sand to
the ferric and aluminium oxides which are present in their clay
fraction. Pure laterite has a ratio of less than 1.33 and, whilst
being easily shaped and formed into blocks when first quarried, hardens on contact with the atmosphere. Lateritic soils
have a ratio of between 1.33 and 2.00.

Climate and building soils

In temperate zones cohesion is obtained by using a more plastic soil than is usual in arid zones. Here its tendency to crack is
restrained by a wattle framework, or by adding a cementitious
binder such as lime, as in the case of Dorset clunch, or a waterproofing agent such as beeswax or tallow as in some Cornish
cob construction.

207

## S. 54

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

In arid zones, the high thermal capacity of earth when compared to other building materials has led to its use as a thermal
reservoir in climates with a high diurnal temperature range and
low rainfall, modifying the extremes of day and night temperature and contributing to thermal comfort. To achieve this,
thicker, rammed earth walls and heavy eartlı roofs are formed
usinga material which is less plastic than that generally used in
temperate zones. Stability is maintained by the careful blending ofsoils and their thorough mixture with small quantities of
water to obtain an even particle size distribution before compression by ramming. Walls tapering from about 1.2 m (4 ft) thickat
the base to perhaps only ı5 cm (6 in) atroof parapet level forming courtyard houses of six storeys or more can be found in the
Atlas mountains of Morocco. In the damp but otherwise similar conditions in the Asir mountains ofsouthwest Saudi Arabia
flat slate is laid to project between the courses oframmed earth
to shed the rain. Similar thick walled, earth houses, sometimes
10 storeys high, have been constructed without shuttering in
Sadah, North Yemen.

Roof construction with unstabilized earth is problematic in
all butthe driest ofclimates. Nevertheless, in the hot dry zones
of North Africa, the Middle East, China and Central America
solid earth roofs and suspended floors approximately 30 cm (12
in) thick are supported bya framework oftimberjoistsanda mat
of split bamboo or palm stems. In the arid parts of Iran, Iraq
and Egypt there is an ongoing tradition of earth vaulting constructed without shuttering, the earliest examples dating from
the 13th century sc. Even in the Sahel region of West Africa,
where there is some rain, roofs are formed by coveringa framework ofsaplings with earth. These structures are quite vulnerable and need to be regularly resurfaced. In northern Nigeria
mosques and palaces have been constructed using arches reinforced with cages ofazara poles. Flatearth roofs have been given
some moisture resistance by mixing with oils or by surfacing
with a lime-based screed, as in town houses of the old port of
Suakin on the Red Sea coast of Sudan.

Intropical, hot, wetzones with littlediurnalrange, the comfortrelated requirement for cross-ventilation has led to large openings in single banked rooms. Where lateritic soils are available
together with building timberthemostsuccessful earth buildings
are those in which the timber framed structure is independent of
the earth wall. This allows movements in both material elements,
associated with large seasonal variations in humidity, to have only
a limited effect on the strength and durability of the building.
Here, soil is either formed into bricks or, as in the case ofnorthern
Nigeria, conical lumps ofa convenient size for handling. Alternatively, in buildings with a shorter life, soil is plastered over a timber lattice structure as wattleand daub.

Vulnerability and protection of soil buildings

Whilsthavingan excellent fire resistance and good noise absorbent properties, earth structures are vulnerable to mechanical
impact; the effects ofearthquakes on earth and adobe buildings
can be lethal. The effect on their strength and durability due to
water penetration is, however, their most serious weakness:
flooding is amajor problem and can sweep away whole populations in the Punjab. This has led to various protective measures
being adopted, particularly in wet climates.

208

Materials impervious to water, such as stone or fired brickwork, are often used in foundations, plinths, quoins and as
surrounds to openings, leaving the weaker, less exposed parts
of the wall to be constructed of earthen components. In wet
climates, pitched roofs with large overhangs and verandas protect the upper part ofthe earthen wall from rain. In all but the
most temporary earth structures, an outer coat ofearth plaster
is used. This is formed from a similar material ofa finer consistency to the earth used in the main body ofthe wall. As long as
this plaster coatis replaced at regular intervals as it deteriorates
through weathering, the strength of the wall is maintained.
Timber reinforcement to mosques in the savanna belt of West
Africa is extended beyond the surface of the wall to provide
a permanent scaffold to facilitate regular repair of the outer
plaster coat. In the same way holes left by formwork ties in the
_rammed earth structures of Morocco allow the easy introduction ofnew scaffolding for the same purpose.

Vegetable oils as waterproofing agents, binders, such as lime,
and straw reinforcement have, at various times, been added to
help stabilize earth construction. Vast quantities of egg-white
are reputed to have been used to stabilize parts ofthe Topkapi
palace in Istanbul. More recently, laboratory techniques of soil

en

en

season.

Farm buildin;
of unstabiliz
bricks, quoini
of stone, trul
ofextended
near Lauzerte,

ready for
‚Anatolia,

analysis originally developed for road and airfield construction

in the Second World War have been used to predict, more accu-

rately, the performance ofearth stabilized with Portland cement.
The Cinva-Ram earth block ‚press, invented in Bogotä in the
19508, was the first of several such hand-operated machines
designed for low-cost stabilized earth construction in developing countries.

Decoration ofearth buildings

Its lack ofvirtually any resistance to tensile forces has resulted,
perhaps more than in any other material, in the unreinforced
arches, vaults and domes constructed of earth, being the most
structurally expressive of all forms in compression. The
earthen roofscape of Yazd in Iran, for example, is made up ofa
complex sea of catenaries.

Being both versatile and economical, traditional earth construction has offered amedium not only for architectural diversity
but also for popular cultural expression. Decoration inscribed
within or painted over the surface ofthe outer plaster coatis commonplace. Regularreplastering provides the opportunitynotonly
for the repetition of traditional motifs, such as can be found on
Asante priests’ houses in Ghana, but also the expression of contemporary patterns, pictures and symbols found on Hausa walls
in Kano and Zaria in northern Nigeria. $

The wide availability and relative labour intensity of earth
construction has led to its association with self-help and community construction even in developed societies in periods of
unemployment. In cash-based urban economies, however, the
high maintenance cost ofunstabilized earth construction often
exceeds its rental value. Such properties, being difficult to
mortgage, quickly deteriorate. MAURICE MITCHELL

1.V.2.a Adobe brick

Adobe, a Spanish word of Arabic derivation (toub or
uttub), has generally been used in reference to earthen wall construction, but more specifically it refers to sun-dried earthen
bricks used in masonry as distinct from rammed earth techniques, and fired brick. The term is most commonly used today
in the American southwest in reference to cast, sun-dried
earthen bricks used for wall construction.

Historically, adobe is one ofthe oldest building materials in
the world, widely used throughout the Near East and Mediterranean regions for thousands of years. Sun-dried brick dating
from 5000 Bc has been found in Iraq and examples from Xinjiang, western China, are more than two thousand years old.
Adobe can be found on all continents, in the context ofsophisticate masonry forms such as arches, domes and other vaulted
structures. The American Pueblo Indians were building with
earth before the arrival ofthe Spaniards, but they were pressing
damp clay by hand rather than casting a brick. Adobe was also
widely used in Peru, in Mexico and many other Latin American
countries in the pre-Spanish era. Particularly outstanding pre-
Spanish monumental examples of adobe can be found in the
north coastal plain of Peru. Adobe brick construction travelled
from North Africa to Spain in the 8th century ap and the Spanish brought the technique (and its use in masonry arches,
domes and vaults) to the Americas early in the ı6th century.

1.1.2

Usa alsceı

Still used throughout the world, the masonry unit is known
by different names in different locales, depending both upon
whether the masonry unit is handmoulded or cast, and upon its
shape. For example, in parts of Africa, the handmoulded version,
in the form ofa cone or a loaf of bread, is referred to as banco
(Manding), ortubali (Hausa), in Arabic it is called uttub or simply
toub and in Persian the term is kolukh. In the region ofthe upper
Niger delta and in association with a specific guild ofbuilders, a
handmoulded cylindrical version, laid vertically, is known as
ferey. In England the cast version is known as ‘clay lumps’; where
Arabic prevails it is called labina, libn or toub nayy. The shape ofa
handmoulded brick varies from spherical, to cylindrical, to
ovoid; cast bricks were, and still are, made in a variety of shapes

## S. 55

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

and sizes depending upon the type of mould used. They can be
rectangular, square, conical or wedge-shaped.

Adobe is often used in conjunction with other systems of
earthen construction such pise & terre (e.g. in the ksour in southeastern Morocco), in creating masonry domed and vaulted forms
(e.g. in the Near East), and in conjunction with stone foundations and buttressing (e.g. in Taos Pueblo, New Mexico, and
Timbuktu, Mali). Adobe brick has also been used as an infill in
timber wall framing.

Ideally, adobe bricks should be made of dense, fully compacted, consolidated soil. Sand and aggregate give strength, and
clay-silt provides waterproofingand a bonding agent. Traditionally, straw, chaff, dung and vegetal juices were added to the soil
to provide additional cohesion; today ingredients such as Portland cement, lime and asphalt can be added to provide additional strength. The vertical earthen surface can be additionally
protected from erosion and moisture by a surface coating of
plaster, stucco or cement.

Adobe is extremely cost-efficient. The use ofan adjacent borrow-pit eliminates transportation requirements and it lends
itself easily to owner-fabrication. Its thermal characteristics
and its thick porous walls make it an excellent solution for dry,
hotcclimates with major diurnal temperature ranges. Atthe same
time, adobe brick production is inhibited by snow, rain, freezing temperatures and high humidity which require considerable maintenance. Even though adobe walls are usually built
on stone foundations which provide better wall bearing, adobe
construction is frequently lethal in earthquake-prone regions.
Neyvertheless, it remains a viable solution for both urban and
rural dwellings in many parts ofthe world.

LABELLE PRUSSIN
1.1V.2.b Brick: burnt/fired
Burnt-clay bricks are perhaps the earliest example of
a manufactured building material. By moulding clay into standard sized bricks and firing them at around 1150 °C (2100 °F),
sintering and partial vitrification take place, permanently changing the properties of the clay. The load-bearing and weather
resisting characteristics of good quality bricks enabled early
builders to produce longlife, multi-storey structures atless cost
than was possible usingsstone. For this reason, brick production
is associated with urbanization where the need for large quantities ofcheap, strong, permanent, fireproof walling materials
is most pressing.

The earliest known examples ofthe use offired bricks is from
the Indus valley culture in Pakistan (2500-1500 Bc). Brick production spread westward to Sumeria on the Tigris and Euphrates
and was used later by the Assyrians in Babylon. Moving east,
brick production spread to India and Southeast Asia. In China
brick production was established by 200 sc.

The Romans used burntbrick extensively from the ıst century
AD. The early multi-storey brick and timber apartment blocks
such as those excavated in Rome’s port of Ostia were built to
house the newlyrich inhabitants ofthe empire’s rapidly expanding cities. Roman bricks were re-used duringthe medieval period
when the technology for firing new bricks was too expensive or
unobtainable. Brick production spread from Rome to the rest

of Europe, but here the damp weather, more difficult clays and
lack of urban growth, led to an essentially seasonal and rural
production.

The ease with which decorative patterns can be formed from
standardized brickwork encouraged its use from the 8th century
onwards by various early Islamic civilizations where the religious
requirement was for decoration to benon-figurative. Here, inthe
absence of stone, or the facility for its long-distance transport,
permanentbuilding materials were required to constructpalaces,
public buildings and mosques as well as multi-storey apartments.
The geometry associated with the specialized craft of brick-cutting and brick mosaic panelling was sometimes used to develop
the architectural geometry ofthe whole building.

In England, Hull, made rich by the wool trade, was the first
major town to be constructed of fired brick. Later Holland
became the medieval centre of brick production, constructing
permanent kilns fired with peat and exporting its bricks as
ship’s ballast to all parts ofthe globe.

Renaissance Florence and Rome, where the cost of firewood
to fuel the kilns was the most expensive item, built permanent
kilns which produced limeand charcoal as by-products. Despite
their preference for marble cladding, Renaissance buildings
were constructed of brickwork and an analysis of the brick
production figures for this period can be used to assess yearly
economic performance. The urbanization associated with
industrialization in the early modern period once again brought
about a boom in brick production which has continued until
the present day.

There are parts ofthe world, however, such as Africa south of
the Sahara, where, despite there being suitable supplies ofclay
and fuel, fired brick production has only been introduced duringtherecentcolonial period. Here brickwork has now to com-

. Pete with concrete blocks as a low-cost building material, at a

time when the mostcommon kiln fuel, firewood, is a diminishing resource and where higher priority is given to charcoal production from firewood for domestic fuel. In the Indian
subcontinent and in parts of Southeast Asia, however, where
the clay reserves, coal supplies, drying conditions and transport links, are appropriate, large quantities of bricks are still

ierlck Kilns

produced just outside urban centres, such as Hanoi, using
essentially traditional methods.

The feasibility of the production of fired clay bricks is governed by the quality of the clay mineral, the fuel supply, the
drying conditions and the proximity to market.

Clay which, when dried from its moulding consistency,
shrinks between approximately 8 and 12 per cent is most suitable for fired brick production. Clay mixing can be carried out
by weathering or tempering in a pit, or using an animal-driven
pugmill. Then the clay is moulded by throwing a clod of clay
into awooden mould and removing the surplus with awire cutter. Alternatively, the tempered clay can be extruded and wire
cut. The moulded bricks must be dried leather-hard before
firing and this process can take from a few days to several
months depending on the humidity at the time. In rural areas
and where markets are small and fluctuating, firing is carried
out in traditional clamp kilns, such as are used in India and
Nepal and since the late-rgth century in Sudan. Typically, these
temporary kilns consist oftruncated pyramids of dried bricks,
the lower courses of which are corbelled out to form fire tunnels across the breadth ofthe clamp. The fire is built up slowly
in the tunnels until the last evidence of‘ moisture, in the form of
steam, has been driven off. Firing continues day and night for
from four to six days until grass placed on top ofthe kiln will
ignite spontaneously. The clamp is then left to cool for abouta
week before being totally dismantled. Sintering will only take
place adequately for external facing brickwork at temperatures
in the region of 1150 °C (2100 °F). As the maximum achievable

with firewood is only 1250 °C (2280 °F) great care has always
been needed in clamp firing to obtain this quality.

On the edge of urban areas, where supplies of clay and fuel
are secure, permanent kilns and dıying sheds have been shown
to be cost-effective. Permanent kilns can produce higher temperatures and greater fuel efficiency. In this situation brick production became a labour-intensive, full-time activity.

Outside the urban context, brick production has been intermittent, often carried out by farmers in the dry season in the
tropics, or between planting and harvest in the more temperate
zones, when brick drying has been easiest out of doors. With
no more capital investment other than the moulds, seasonal
Producers are often obliged to use poor quality sandy, less plastic, clay because oftheir inability to control the drying process
as they are dependent on the seasons. Fired bricks of this
nature are often ofirregular shape and suffer from poor weathering characteristics. Sand moulding - the process by which
sand replaces water as the mould release agent- became widespread in the ıgth century in England as ameans ofproducing
a more regular faced brick from more plastic clays.

Brick sizes are governed on the one hand by the need to limit
differential shrinkage between the inside and outside of the
brick during dtying, and on the other by the requirements of
modular construction with a standardized component. A more
unusual requirement for a larger brick obtained foratime in the
18th century in England when each brick produced, whatever its
size, was taxed equally. With the former considerations in mind,
the Romans often used long and broad bricks (perhaps 500 x
600 mm [20 x 24 in]) which were only 50mm (2 in) deep. When

Earths and Clays 1.1.2

used as a facing for rubble/concrete they could be pointed in
plan on the inside face to provide a key for the concrete. Bricks
have become smaller, standardized and more modular over
time, making it possible for different types of bond, such as
Flemish and English bonds, to become commonplace.

Arched lintels, vaults and domes have been constructed of
brick. Shallow brick jack’ arches are still used to span between
steel or concrete beams to form suspended floors in multistorey structures. Fired bricks, often in a herringbone pattern,
have been used in half-timbered construction. Terracotta
mouldings were incorporated into facades in England in both
the 16th and ıgth centuries.

When fired, different chemicals present in the clay result in
alternative brick colouring. Coloured brickwork with alternating bonds has been used to provide decorative patterning from
the glazed work of ıoth-century Bukhara in Uzbekistan, to the
red and blue patterning ofthe walls of18th-century cottages at
Uckfield in Sussex, England.

Burnt clay roofing tile production seems always to have been

associated with and carried out alongside that of bricks. The
clay used tends to be finer and more plastic than that used for
brick production and for this reason the dıying and firing techniques require more control. Plastic clays with a low and controllable shrinkage are usually those associated with the upper
and middle reaches of large rivers, rather than settled upland
plateau soils. Temperature control in clamp kilns containing
both bricks and tiles is problematic and permanent tile kilns
are more usual. MAURICE MITCHELL
1.|V.2.c Clay bat (East Anglia)
In the East Anglian region of the British Isles an
earth building material was used in unfired rectangular blocks.
Clay bat is found mainly in Cambridgeshire, and Norfolk,
Suffolk and Essex (where it is called clay lump). Clay subsoil
(usually chalky boulder clay) was well-trodden with water and
straw, and formed into blocks in a bottomless wooden mould
on hard ground. The mould was lifted off, leaving blocks to dry
until firm. They were turned at least twice to dry evenly, andthen
stacked in honeycomb form for further drying. When fully dry
they were laid in stretcher bond on a foundation wall of flint
rubble or brickwork, using mortar of similar clay, but sieved
and worked to a finer consistency. Blocks of many sizes have
been found, butthe commonest size is 45x22 xı5 cm (18x8.6
x 6 in), used to make outer walls 22 cm (8.6 in) thick and partition walls 15 cm (6 in) thick. This material has been used mainly
for cottages and farm buildings, but in Norfolk also for larger
houses, industrial buildings, chapels and schools. Domestic
and public buildings were always rendered with clay and
chopped straw or lime and sand. Most other buildings were left
unrendered, protected externally by limewash or gas tar.

Although in hotter and drier parts of the world this is an
ancient building material (often incorrectly called ‘“adobe’), in
Britain itwas a late development. The early literature on vernacular architecture claimed that it was a traditional building
material in East Anglia, and cited r7th-century buildings in
Norfolk supposedly constructed ofit. Research in the 1980s has
shown this to be a fallacy. Clay bats had been used earlier to

## S. 56

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

form the nest-boxes ofdovecots, butthe material was first used
structurally in 1791, when a bricklayer named Joseph Austin
built himself a cottage of clay bats at Great Shelford, Cambridgeshire. His cottage attracted the attention ofthe Reverend
James Plumptree ofHinxton, a social reformer concerned with
the improvement ofrural housing, and in 1801 he published a
description of Austin’s innovation. From 1821 the process was
advocated by another reformer, John Denson of Waterbeach,
and under his influence many cottages of clay bats were builtin
Cambridgeshire.

From 1833 Denson’s description was republished by J. C.
Loudon. Itwas adopted on a major scale by progressive farmers
in south Norfolk and north-west Suffolk, where other building
materials were poor and expensive. Modern tests have shown
that it has as much compressive strength as the poor mortar of

thelocal brickwork. Experience showed thatitwas notsuitable -

for all farm buildings; it could be eroded by animals, and was
easily penetrated by brown rats. In Norfolk it continued in use
for housing until the early-2oth century. Many later houses have
22 cm (8.6 in) walls ofclay bats with an ır cm (4.3 in) outer skin
ofbrickwork, tied together with iron strips. JOHN MeCANN

1.V.2.d Cob (Devon)

Cob is a plastic building material made by mixing
fibre and water with clay and aggregate taken from below the
organic topsoils ofthe ground. The resulting damp mixture was
built up into thick mass walling and left to set, dry out and
harden into solid structures capable of bearing building loads.
The best cob was built off a stone base course. The balance of
aggregates in cob varied with the composition oflocal subsoils
and might comprise one or more aggregates of small stone,
gravel ‚sand and silt. Very stony and excessively clayey soils cannot be made into cob; sometimes therefore soils were blended
together to make them usable. Clay was never the main constituent in cob. Ratios of 20 per cent clay to 80 per cent aggregates are said to be typical of the material internationally.
Pulverized chalk can form another, or an alternative, element to
or alongside clay in the mix. In parts of Buckinghamshire and
Oxfordshire in England such anatural mixture ofclayand chalk,
known locally as ‘wichert’, was in use until quite recently. Scrap-

workers stood on the wall top trampling down the cob as it
rose. Shuttering, occasionally indicated by the imprint of hurdles on the face ofthe cob, was known but little used. In all
exposed locations ideal practice was to give cob walls a protective coating of limewash, or limewashed lime render, often
with a band of tar or bitumen at the foot ofthe wall. Recent
years have seen a modest revival in the craft of cob walling in
Devon. JOHN RAYMOND HARRISON

1.IV2.e Moulded mud (Ghana)

Moulded mud, irrespective of shape, is pre-dried
before being stacked and is then held in place with mud or
cement plaster to create thick, structurally sound, load-bearing
walls that can supporta variety ofbuilding shapes, wall heights
and roofpitches. Grass, sand-crete or land-crete are additional
manmade mixtures than can be used to stabilize mud prior to
moulding and curing.
ings from the surface of pre-tarmacadamed roads were once claylumpworks
another popular source of raw material. The best type offibre Sarboldisham,
for cob seems to have been barley straw but many other kinds of
plant stem were employed, depending on availability.

Use ofthe term ‘cob’ is confined to the English southwestern
counties of Cornwall, Devon and Somerset, and parts of
Dorset; elsewhere in the British Isles the material was generally 2
called ‘mud’ or ‘clay’. Devon cob often hasannaturallyoccurring » ciaylumpwithkej
aggtegate called ‘shillet’, composed of small slate particles. . Ju
Many thousands oftwo-storey cob farmhouses, farm buildings 4 ciaylump
and boundary walls, as well as later town houses, survive. The
earliest ofthese date from the ısth century, the latest from the
first decades of the zoth century. The method, once part of see.alsofort
the range of skills potentially available through experience to las “u
farmworkers everywhere, became truly vernacular in usage. Bere
Ingredients were cheap, practice was simple and tools were w1ccann, Johi
few. Materials were mixed in a heap on the ground. Walls were _1987b
raised in courses, which in Devon might be between 30 cm (12
in) and 100 cm (40 in) high with average thicknesses of 60 cm Rn.
(23 in), using only spades and hay- and dung-forks. Each ade
course was allowed to set before the nextwas superadded. The england.

The technique of placing uniform mud-lumps in horizontal
rows without any other structural support produces a highly
utilized construction method often found in regions where
there is a scarcity oftrees. Though mud is acommon building
material throughout the vernacular world, hand-moulded
mud-lump walls are sometimes not used in forest regions
where abundanttree stock can better provide wooden columns,
beams and latticing as the primary building support. Where
industrialized moulded mud uses brick presses and cement,
stabilized mud construction can be standardized and the technique distributed more ubiquitously throughout regions where
the availability of appropriate technology and materials does
not naturally exist.

topleft

3

Earths and Clays 1.1.2

While it is commonly agreed that the stacking strength and
regularity ofmoulded mud can allow for the construction oflarge,
often double-storeyed, monumental buildings such as mosques
and palaces, most commonly mud-lump is used to build onestorey, domestic compounds with linking rooms and walls.

Mud for moulding is geologically derived from laterite soils.
While thesered clay soils areabundantly distributed throughout
both tropical arid and tropical humid climatic zones and are
widely used for building, the composition of laterite soils,
classified into four major groups, can greatly influence construction technique and endurance. Laterite rock and boulder
are used for stone construction; laterite gravelly soils are often
mixed with cementfor land-crete; laterite soil with decomposed
vegetation is used for ‘swish’; laterite fine grain soils are often
mixed with cement for sand-crete. The best type ofunstabilized
soil (e.g., soil without cement) for moulded mud building is
derived from the last category, for these laterite fine-grained
soils possess a high degree of sand for strength, yet a suitable
amount of clay for plasticity. Manmade materials can also be
introduced to stabilize proportion of clay to sand.

These soils can further be categorized according to geoclimatic location. In Ghana, West Africa, arid regions in thenorth
produce a dry soil type called the ‘savanna orchosols’. These
sayanna soils contain more finely grained sand than do the
moist clay-like coastal orchosol soils from the south. In the
north, unstabilized sandy clay is used most often to create preeured, sun-dried, moulded mud. Too much sand, however, can
speed wall erosion.

Laterite soils have been used to make moulded mud for centuries of housebuilding; but often owing to poor soil selection
and inappropriate construction technology, houses built using
traditional methods are often accused ofbeing non-durable. The
most enduring solution (if permanence is an issue) uses natural
mud and manmade stabilizers. RENEE KEMP-ROTAN
1.1V2.f Rammed earth: pise ä terre

Rammed earth (pise & terre) differs from other unbaked earthen constructions in two ways: the stiff earthen mix
(in contrast to the more fluid mix required for hand moulding
or casting other earthen brick types) is kneaded or mixed with
gravel and shell or lime and rammed between boards, shutters
or slipforms in a nearly dry state, and wall homogeneity and
cohesion is achieved through compaction (rather than by additives to the earth) by vigorous tamping. The strength ofa pise
wall lies in its homogeneity and its mass.

The wall construction technique is analogous to that which
is currently used for concrete: two panels of wood (61-66 cm
[24-26 in] high, 2.4-3.7 m [8-12 ft] long) are assembled: the
panels are levelled, supported vertically and parallel to each
other by means of vertical studs, crossmembers and ropes; on
the interior ofthis formwork, the stiff, dry mud mixture (earth,
lime and water, cement and/or crushed ceramic), prepared in
advance, is packed, and then rammed or pounded into themould
while still moist with a tamper, by hand or mechanically. Once
the earth or clay has dried or hardened in situ, the coffrage, framing mould, planks or slipforms are removed to a new location,
reset, resecured and prepared for a new bed or layer. Slipforms

213

## S. 57

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

Oo Labelle Prussin after P Ricard

can be moved either horizontally or vertically. The keys or horizontal ties can either remain fixed in place or be removed, leaving transverse holes.

The basic material is earth, commonly found almost everywhere, whose soil in most locations has naturally usable proportions which do not require further tempering. The ideal soil
mixture will have less than 50 per cent clay and silt, and a maximum aggregate size of 6.4 mm (0.25 in). The solid mass is
dampened to a moisture content of approximately ro per cent
by weight of dıy soil. Saltwater should not be used under any
circumstances. Uniform compression and the elimination of
airpockets, essential for homogeneity, are achieved by tamping
down theearth with a rammer, traditionally made ofheartwood
and more recently ofiron.

Rammed earth wall construction is an old technique still
used effectively in many parts of the world and is still observ- -
able in vernacular housing in parts of England, in southern
Spain and Portugal, in southern France, in northwest Africa
and in China. An analogous process would have been used for a
monument from the end of the 4th millennium sc at Uruk
(Erech), Iraq, where the material within the planks is a sort of
gypsum with a binding agent of crushed pottery. In Sussex,
England, walls ofmud-built cottages formed ofclay mixed with
chopped straw or stones and packed down between boards or
hurdles, still survive.

BER

The etymology of the terms pise, pise & terre and pise en terre
spans many centuries and regions, so that today the terms are
used to describe various building materials and processes in
different cultures. The French term pise de terre translates into
the English ‘blocks of clay’ while mur en terre translates into
‘cob-wall’. It has also been suggested that terre a batir is the
French equivalent of ‘rammed earth’.

The current usage ofthe term pisein France, as defined in varjous building reference books, also includes technologies which
use materials such as brick, stone and metal for formwork on
one or both sides of the earthen infill, regardless of whether
these forms may remain permanently in place or not. Pise has
also become synonymous with any construction usingreinforced
earth, e.g. stabilized earth, with or without the use of shuttering.

It has been suggested that the Romans also introduced pise
construction to the Lyon region on the Mediterranean coast of
France. The French verb piser derives from the Latin pisare, to
pound or to pulverize. Although the term pise first appeared in
Lyons in 1562, its popularity as a building technology extended
not only to the Rhöne valley where it became known as pise de
terre but throughout Provence, France, where an identical technique was, and is designated as taapu, or tapi. In Spain, for
instance at Valencia, the term tapial is used, in Catalonia, tapia,
and in the Maghreb of North Africa, tabya refers to the very
same rammed earth construction.

The ancient city walls of Marrakech, built by the Almoravids
in the ııth century AD, were constructed in pise ä terre; centuries
later, the battlements ofthe ksour and ribats in the Dräaand Dad&s
valleys in southeastern Morocco were similarly constructed. A
description of this same technology can be found in the 14thcentury accounts by the Arab traveller Ibn Khaldun who referred
to itastabiyah. Atthe same time, the Arabic taufortufa has also
become a generic term for both piseand adobe construction.

Tapia, in Spanish, refers to both mud and adobe wall construction; when lime is added to the mix, it is called tapia real
and was not uncommon until the beginning of the aoth century. The Spanish brought the technique to the New World
where a monolithic masonry, composed oflime and sand, with
an aggregate of shell, gravel or stone, mixed with water, was
tamped between form boards (taperia).

Pise construction, like other earthen building techniques, is
limited to particular climatic conditions. It is found most frequently in the drier tropics. The choice of rammed earth construction is also a function of the additive resources which
were, and are available, and necessary to stabilize the earthen
fill, as well as the resources (usually wood planking) necessary
for the framing moulds or coffrage. Construction is usually
undertaken during the dry seasons, when agricultural activityis
less demanding on work priorities. Rammed earth construction continues to provide a vernacular solution to housing in
Spain and Portugal, northwest Africa and southern France and
is currently in favour as a viable earthen building technology in
the southwestern United States. LABELLE PRUSSIN
1.1V2g Sod

Sod used as a building material consists ofthe surface strata of Prairie Plains soils together with the live roots and
rhizomes which occupy it, and which give it strength. Building
sod is derived from mixed and short-grass prairies which first
began to develop in Miocene times. In North America they occupy a wide swath east ofthe Rocky Mountains from Texas and
New Mexico in the south to Montana, Alberta and Saskatchewan
in thenorth. Knownas the Great Plains, the area was left largely
unglaciated during the Dleistocene era.

The principal force in the development ofthis grass environment, and which distinguished it from the true prairie of the
central lowlands to the east, is the availability ofmoisture. From
the eastern edge ofthe plains, where annual precipitation averages 635 mm (25 in), rainfall diminishes in the westto averages
0f380 mm (15 in) orless. Strong winds and low humidity cause
high transevaporation rates. These conditions fostered distinctive root habits in dominant grasses.

Buffalo grass (Bulbilis dactyloides) illustrates the phenomenon,
and the strength ofthe material. Its sod is extraordinarily dense
and renders the soil extremely compact. Fine but very tough
and wiry roots spread widely in every lateral direction as well as
down, and in all directions between; they also branch and rebranch freely. This lateral system, which is absent in the buffalo
grass understorey ofthe true prairie, allows the plant to absorb
moisture from even the lightest rainfall. Together with the
roots ofadjacent plants, buffalo grass comprises a dense multidirectional system of great tensile strength.

Earths and Clays 1.1V.2

The expanse ofplains offers such diversity oftopography and
soils that many different grass habitats are present. Any nearly
level site with a stand ofa dominant sod-forming rather than
bunch grasses mightaafford suitable building material, but preferred sites were often found in moistvalleys and lowlands, and
in sinks known locally as ‘buffalo wallows’. Western extensions of suitable tall grasses such as big bluestem (Andropogon

Furcatus) and prairie cordgrass (Spartina pectinata) might even be

found here. Locally moist conditions sometimes encouraged
bunch grasses, such as little bluestem, to be present in a sodforming habit.

Systematic investigation is needed into the composition of
sods in walls to learn more about species selection by builders.
The data most relied upon at present is derived from reminiscences, which do not reflect whether selected grasses were in
mixed or pure stands. Those most commonly mentioned in the
central plains, in addition to those above, include wheat grass,
Indian grass and wire grass.

Details of technology and construction have not been ascertained for the earliest years of sod construction in the ıgth century. Reminiscences and photographs appear in the latter part of
the century and these, together with numerous extant structures,
allow accurate description of the technology which persisted
into the 2oth century.

The principal technology involved the ‘grasshopper’ plough.
Manufactured in and distributed widely throughout the central
plains, it differed from other breaking ploughs in its horizontally positioned blade and in its extended mould board ofseparate metal rods. Instead of digging, turning and breaking the
sod for planting, the grasshopper sliced parallel with the soil
surface and carefully turned the sod onto its top in an unbroken
mass. The depth of the cut was controlled, varying usually
between 7 cm (3 in) and 1o cm (4 in). The strips, typically 30-45
cm (12-18 in) wide, were then cutinto bricks ofvarying length,
the greatest said notto exceed ım (3 ft).

Bricks were laid in masonry fashion, grass side down, typically on undisturbed soil which had been cleared and levelled.
Walls were usually two widths wide, with staggered joints and
bond courses. Bach course was levelled with soil. Lumber was
used increasingly in the 2oth century near mid-wall to provide a
stable levelling surface. Single-storey buildings were the rule,

11V2i Sun-dried brick: Arab

Itis believed thatever since mankind congregated in
villages almost ten thousand years ago, unbaked earth has been
one ofthe principal building materials used in every continent,
The survival of. archaeological remains ofeities built entirely of
raw earth provides countless examples ofearth-based architecture throughout the world. These include Jericho, claimed to be
tss7 the world’s earliest eity, Gatal Huyuk in Turkey, Harappa and

Mohenjo-Daro in Pakistan, Akhlet-Aton in Egypt, Chan-Chan
in Peru, Babylon in Iraq, Zuheros near Cordoba in Spain and
Khirokitia in Cyprus. Many modern cities have been established on the sites of these ancient towns, and many of their
recent buildings have been constructed out ofraw earth.

Earths and Clays

1.02

According to the archaeologist Jose Imbelloni, earth bricks
might have passed through the following evolution: the bricks
would have initially been cone-shaped, then cylinder-cone
shaped, hemisphere-shaped, tooth-shaped and finally squareshaped. The cone shape is found, for example, in Peru during
the Cupisnic era (900-200 Bc). The pear-shape, common in.
West Africa, has been utilized in the construction of housing
for more than five thousand years. Called tubali, the bricks are
made without a mould, using a mixture ofearth and straw.

Jericho is believed to be the site of the

earliest evidence of
construction in sun:

-dried mud-bricks, made by hand in the
shape ofloaves ofbread. Later, the South Americans in the Chicama valley ofPeru and the Mesopotamians ofEridu (now Irag)
simultaneously invented the box mould in the 3rd millennium
BC. This invention is seen to coincide with the first use offired
bricks.

Although the mould box exists, making earth bricks byhand

has continued in some Scattered places throughout the world,
©.g. the Ksar of Taghit in Algeria. There the earth used to make
the loaf-like bricks was first dugand then mixed with water and
traditional stabilizers(s) such as straw, wood shavings, or animal dung that were Necessary to improve the resistance of the

blocks to shrinkage and eracking. The mixture was then left to
rot or ferment for acertain period

1.1V.2.i-i Sun-dried brick (Pakistan)

Sun-dried brick wall construction is the most widely

used technology employed in theruralareas of; upper Sindhand
the Punjab provinces ofPakis tan. Itis extensively used invil lage
houses, builtona self-help basis, because ofits low cost, ease
of construction and insulation qualities. Normally construction takes about three months.
To avoid breakages and minimize cartage costs, bricks are
usually moulded on-site from the soil ex:
farms. Bricks are laid with mud-mortar and the whole surface
finished with mud-plaster. Sincı
Protect the plaster is used, themud
renewed after every rainy season. Wallthickness varies between
46 cm (18 in) and gocm (3 fi) and provides much needed insulation during excessively hot and cold seasons.

cavated from nearby

© NO water-resistant material to

217

## S. 59

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

The soil for bricks consists of 60 per cent siltand clay and 40
per centsand. The mixture ofsoil and water (gara) is kept moist
for three to four days and is continuously kneaded to make it
elastic. Bricks ofsize23 xırx8cm (9x4.5 x3 in) are fabricated
usingwooden or steel moulds. Large-scale production ofbricks
is carried out by a team consisting of one brickmaker and two
helpers (patheras) who can produce up to 1000 bricks per day.

The patheras are often women because oftheir acknowledged
skill at producing better quality bricks. Traditionally women
also carry the responsibility of repairing and plastering their
houses.

During hot weather it is sufficient to leave bricks for one day
on each side to dry, while in cooler weather an extra day is
required for each side. After dıying bricks are laid with mudmortar. Sun-dried bricks are also used in domical roofs for
mosques or tombs. In the case of houses, flat roofs consisting
ofrough wood purlins are used, and finished with thatch and a
layer ofmud covering. Mud-plaster is applied to the whole sundried brick structure to provide a smooth finish and protection
from rain.

The mortar for laying and plastering is prepared by mixing
toori (long bhoosa or straw) in soil that has been soaked in water
for two days and kneaded for another two. Once the toori disintegrates and the colour of the mortar turns almost black, the
mortar is ready for use. Normally only one coat, approximately
13 mm (0.5 in) thick, is applied. For best results cow or buffalo
dung (gobar) is also mixed into the mortar.

The floors of houses are normally tamped earth plastered
with mud (leepai). YASMEEN LARI

swish (West Africa)

Swish is a building material made from wet mud
that is gathered from clay ‘puddling’ pits to create handrolled
mud balls for construction, amethod which is in contrastto the
use ofmoulds for pre-dried brick. Though the term has been in
recorded use since the mid-ıgth century itmay notbe of African
origin but a corruption of ‘Swiss’, the Basel mission having
used rammed earth for wall and road construction in West
Africa at that time.

Swish is a popular traditional building method in moisttropical climates or arid regions with distinct rainy seasons, where
damp clay can be easily dug from the earth, stamped by foot
until pliable and then set into wall courses by hand while still
malleable.

While swish always defines the use of wet, plastic mud as a
building material, it also uses two distinct construction techniques. The first swish method is referred to as the wet-coiled
method where irregularly shaped damp mud clods are wetpacked and coiled to form courses for walls. Here each wet
course requires a day for hardening before the next course is
laid. The second method is the wet brick/dry framing method
where the same moist mud balls are rolled and then wetstashed to fill in the gaps in the wood lattice used for structural
framing. In both methods there is little need for mortar as the
stickiness ofthe swish bricks creates their own ‘glue’. In both
cases, after damp-bricks are placed, a mud-plaster is applied to
the rough surfaces to smooth the final wall surface.

1.2)

218

While the wet bricks as material are used in each construction method, each method differs in its choice ofstructural system. In the wet-coiled method the wet brick is used to create
load-bearing mud walls. In the wet brick/dry framing method
wood framing provides stability and the swish wall provides
thickness as shelter from the sun, heatand rain.

In many tropical regions throughout the vernacular world
the swish pottery method is utilized where the absence ofwood
for framing is noticeable. Structural supports of wet-coiled
pottery walls are often held in place by conically tapered mud
buttresses that support compound walls externally. The buttresses are themselves often made ofrounds of stacked swish,
popular where wooden framing materials cannot be used in
abundance for internal wall support.

Many mud granaries throughout the world oftraditional settlements become sculptural when swish is creatively used to
form decorative storage silos for grain. Because swish without
latticework is not contained by framing, organic forms and
houses with rounded shapes and corners often identify wet]
pottery, swish construction.

Where ayailability of building material is a primary influence
on house form, traditional architecture often reflects the
advantages and limitations of its construction material and
technique. Thus, in Ghana for example, house form sometimes
changes from square to round, as one moves from one geoclimatic region to another. In the humid forest areas ofthe south,
wet brick/dry framed houses are far more common than the
more plastic and lyrical wet-coiled pottery method of swish
construction, used in the arid, treeless northern region. Many
round houses in arid northern Ghana use no internal framing
support for walls. This is because there are few trees for square
framing, mud is puddled in the rainy season, and mud finds its
strength in compression and the roundness of the walls evenly
distributes the loads.

Laterite soil type, climate, and rainfall not only play an important role in the use of swish as building material, but the availability (or otherwise) ofwood for framing can have an impacton
its construction methods and thereby on design forms as well.

In considering a list of laterite soil groups, it becomes evident that some are naturally more predisposed to wet/brick
construction than others. The most suitable soil for year-round
swish building is derived from laterite soils with decomposed
vegetation, for these moist, sticky soils possess a high degree
of clay for plasticity, necessary to the building of organic forms
and shaping.

More specifically, in Africa alongthe coastal forest zones, the
most ‘plastic’ soil for building comes under the forest ochrosol
grouping. These soils are ofthe common red clay laterite variety but contain large deposits of decomposed vegetation that
enhances stickiness and are therefore excellent for good cohesion. In drier regions of the savanna ochrosol, sandy soils are
mixed with water during the peak rain seasons to wet the mud.
Here the preparation of swish occurs during the middle ofthe
limited rainy season, where the moist brick is left to air just
before building starts in the dry season. Together, climate,
rainfall and timing all play contributory roles in the use of
swish material and building. RENEE KEMP-ROTAN

1.1V.2.k Termite mounds: antbed bricks (Australia)
Earth bricks are often manufactured in Africa, India
and elsewhere where termites abound, from reconstituted
termite mounds. Immigrant Europeans in northwest Australia
used this material in the 18gos. In constructing their mounds,
the termites mine clayey subsoils, glueing particles together
with their saliva, and then, having positioned the pellets ofclay
in the walls of their mound galleries, they smear their faecal
material over as a further binding agent. These substances provided natural binding for the ‘antbed’ bricks. In addition, short
lengths of chopped hummock grass (Triodia sp. with needle
leaves) were often added as amechanical binding matrix. Mound
soil was also used for compacted earth floors and as a weak,
albeit readily available, mortar for stone and earth brick walls.
PETER KELLEHER and PAUL MEMMOT
1.1V.2.| Terrones: brick earth
Terrones are blocks of compacted soils with matted
grass root systems cut from river-beds. When dried they are
used as bricks, well bonded with roots. The clays used to form
bricks in the vernacular are superficial clays and easily worked.
In certain rare instances they may occur naturally in a state that
is suitable for building, but more normally they must be carefully mixed and worked in order to achieve a suitable composition and consistence.

Dmochowski describes a process amongst the Nupe ofthe
Nigerian plateau, where building earth is dug from communally
owned pits. This is then broken into a pile of pieces ofup to 70
mm (2.7 in) and water is poured on the pile and left overnight,

Earths and Clays 1.1.2

until there is no trace oftthe separate lumps. A soft dry grass is
then broken into pieces about 100 mm (4 in) long, added to the
earth and trampled well. This is then covered with more grass
and again left overnight, until on the third day the consistency,
which has thickened considerably, is sampled. If necessary,
more grass or water during the harmattan is added and the mixture is ready for moulding into rectangular flat bricks. Themortar used to bind the bricks is formed from the same earth, using
a process which differs only in the amount of grass used.

In colder climates, brick earth is dug in the autumn and left
in a pile for the winter to be broken down by the freezing action
of the weather. In parts of China, brick earth is cut approximately every 1o years from the paddy-fields, which silt up from
regular inundation. This is essential in order to maintain the
right depth of water in the fields. After ploughing in the
autumn the surface is smoothed with a stone roller. Some days
after heavy rain has evaporated and the ground has dried to
the consistency of putty, the top layer of the field is cut with a
spade to a depth ofro cm (4 in) into brick shapes of 36 x 23cm
(14 x 9 in), which are finally cut from the side, piled vertically
into stacks and covered with straw as protection against subsequent rain, until the bricks are dry enough for use. In this area
of the country, adobe brick is frequently used in house construction (Hommel). A similar process is used in West Bengal,
where it is extremely localized, and in the boggy river lands of
New Mexico and Spanish America, where the thick, matted
root system of river grasses is cut into terrones and used as
bricks when dıy.

DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

219

## S. 60

na Grasses and Palms

Grasses and palms belong to the botanical family of
monocotyledons, flowering plants which have a single stem.

Bamboos, also grasses, are in the same family. Within the fam-

ily the diversity is enormous and the way in which the plants

have been used for building purposes is correspondingly varied.

There are over 2500 species of palms, the majority of which

grow within the limits of latitudes 40 °N and 40 °S and which
roughly define the area where palms are used in vernacular con-

struction. Palm trees sometimes have very short stems, and
appear to consist entirely of leaves: in the case of the palm
Teysmannia altifrons in Malaya, for example, the huge leaves are
used for covering walls and roofs. Perhaps more familiar are
the palms with long trunks crowned by a mass of long leaves:
typical ofthese, the date-palm (Phoenix dactylifera), the coconutpalm (Cocos nucifera), and the doum-palm (Hyphaenethebaica) are
all important providers of building materials using both the
trunk and the foliage. But palms are also highly valued for the
food and other products they can provide, and thus to cut a
palm tree has traditionally often been restricted — in Oman it
has been against the law to cuta date palm without permission
ftom the Sultan. Respect for the value ofthe palm has led to the

INNEN, 6

2
3 Ze

development of architectural techniques which use only
branches and leaves, as in the barasti houses on the Al Bätinah
coast of Oman. Elsewhere scarcity of building materials can
make such careful and ecological use of a plant difficult; in
parts ofthe Sahel, drought and the continued demand for roof
beams is leading to the depletion of doum-palm trees, in an
area where few other suitable trees are available. Alternative
techniques have to be found.

In the botanical subfamily ofgramineae, plants used in building include bamboos and reeds, Phragmites communis being one
ofthe best known and used for thatching in many countries;
other water plants are also used including rushes, cereals such
as rice, wheat, oats and millet, and grasses, of which elephant
grass (Imperata cylindrica) is probably the most widely used.

There are over 700 species ofbamboo, and many are used in
building. Bamboos grow very fast, normally reaching their full
size in one year and reaching maturity in three or four. The
stems — known as culms - are usually cylindrical and hollow.
Knots divide up the stem atregular intervals. Depending on the
species, the culms may beaslittleas 3 m (10 ft) orastallas 35 m
(115 ft). Culm diameters range from a few millimetres up to 15
cm (6 in) or more. Bamboos often grow wild, but in countries

- where they provide food and materials for many family needs,
asinLaos and Vietnam, itis common to see bamboos cultivated
inand around villages.

Reeds continue to be cultivated for thatching in many countries, including France, England, Austriaand Poland. In the past,
cereals such as wheat had long stems suitable for use in thatching, but the development of new high grain yield cereals has
tended towards shorter stem varieties. Fertilizers and especially
nitrogen have also led to reduced durability in stems: in Vietnam
villagers complain that new rice strains have shorter stems, less
useful for roofing, and in England to meet the demand for
thatching a few farmers grow old species of wheat specially.
Few grasses are actively cultivated for use in building, butin the
case of Imperata cylindrica, which has deep protected roots and
grows fast, burning is used in some African countries to remove
less resistant plants and encourage its growth for use in roofs.

Use as structural elements

Many types of palm tree-trunk are used for columns and for
beams spanning 3-4 m (10-13 ft). In old houses in the Arabian
peninsula, palm tree beams are often carved and painted for
decoration. A single trunk is usually cut into several segments
along its length. Date-palm trunks also make excellent water
channels and gutters when hollowed out. Local knowledge
about palms is important, because the quality of a single

gofthatching

Bamboo grove, Yıll
South China,

jalatfolia)

z
s

species can vary in different places: thus, in the Air region of
Niger the doum-palm is considered termite resistant, butin the
interior delta ofthe River Niger in Mali the same tree appears to
have poor durability and beams have to be replaced after three
or four years.

Bamboos with a large diameter are widely used as structural
elements in the Far Eastand Latin America, to make floor joists,
wall frames, columns and roof structures. Bamboo also makes
excellent scaffolding, and gutters and water pipes. Its high tensile
strength and straightness makes bamboo a valuable structural
material, and one that has been useful in achieving typhoontesistant structures. Joints are often effected using rope binding
and bamboo pegs, and skill is often required to achieve goodconnections. Some bamboos split very easily, and cannot be nailed,
although the bamboo Guadua augustifolia, as used, forinstance, in
Colombia, can be nailed quite successfully.

Bundles of reeds are also used in the structure of buildings,
as in the case of reed-ribs which form the large curved arches
supporting the mudhif in the Euphrates delta. The leaf-ribs of
date-palms are also used in bundles to form prefabricated
arches ofhuts or individually as ribs in tents.

Walls can be made from split bamboo, grasses, reeds and
palm leaves. The barasti house of the north coast of Oman has
wall panels made from date palm frond stems, used either with

Grasses and Palms 1.1.3
the leaves on or stripped ofleaves. Panels ofstems withoutleaves
are used to make screens which let in light and ventilation, similar to the Egyptian mashrabiyyat, whilst for winter use thicker
panels ofleafed stems provide insulation from cool winds.

In Colombia splitbamboo stems are used fattened and nailed
onto the supporting framework (ofbamboo or wood) to make
wall panels. In Laos bamboo woven mats are used in a similar
way, with many different styles ofweaving and thickness, some
giving lightand ventilation, others being tightlywoven. Similar
panels are used for making doors and shutters.

Walls are also made from grass and straw, mainly for lightweight shelters such as thetijira. Grass and straw mats are hung
outside earth walls in Bangladesh to protect them from rain
damage, and earth walls in southwest England are sometimes
thatched, although examples are now rare.

Split and flattened bamboos are also used to make floors, as
too are some species of palm tree trunk, sawn into boards.

As do the walls, the roof of the barasti house also largely
depends on the date-palm frond stem, starting with a grid of
stems on each pitch ofthe roof, over which palm mattingis laid,
and finished with rows of stems with leaves always pointing
down the slope ofthe roof. Coconut-palm leaves are used in a
similar way, but are less durable and stiff. Many ofthe broader
palm leaves are used as wall and roof covering, tied onto a sup-

porting framework, as in the case ofthe Teysmannia altifrons.

Thatching, however, remains one of the most widespread
uses of grasses, cereals, reeds and palms. The quality and performance ofthatch depends largely on the technique of laying,
the choice of material and the local climate. Good reed thatch
can last 60 or more years and, being thick, provides good insulation, but in many countries itis quite realistic to replaceathin
thatched covering every two or three years, using easily
replaced grasses or cereal stems. Bamboo is also used to make
half-round tiles, or split open to make flat tiles. In Laos a bamboo tile roof can last several years.

Bamboo has been used as the framing for earth walls and
provides some protection against earthquake damage, but typically the usefulness of this protection has been short-lived
because of decay caused by insects. Reeds, bamboo and palm
frond stems have all been used as reinforcement in elements
such as lintels, combined with plaster or earth. Learning from
this, more recent research has worked on using bamboo as
reinforcement in concrete, with only moderate success.

Shortcomings
Decay and insect attack are the main problems of using palms,
grasses and bamboos, and are the areas where affordable
improvements need to be made. Fire is also a hazard, and one
where treatment is seldom very effective. The fire risk has produced striet controls in the Morbihan area ofnorthwest France,
and traditional instructions on preventive measures required
equipment to be available near thatched houses in Luang
Prabang, Laos. Cost often makes preventive treatment against
decay and fire unrealistic. Good design and good use of materials can provide better protection.

In many countries, the parts of buildings which were previously made with grass and palms are being replaced, for those

## S. 61

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
who can afford it, by materials considered more durable such
as corrugated roof sheeting and concrete. But this change is
often made at the price ofloss of comfort and higher cost, and
sometimes without significant increase in durability or real
reduction in maintenance. In Europe, the aestheticand thermal
qualities of thatch are actually producing a re-emergence of
thatching as a desirable roofcovering. JOHN NORTON
1.1V.3.a Bamboo

The growth of bamboo cane is similar to that of
grasses (gramineae). Itreaches its fullheightand diameter within
a few weeks, but retains too much moisture at that time for
building purposes. Therefore three to five years go by before it
is cut, preferably during the dry season. After the branches are
removed, the cane becomes useful as a building material.

Itcan be used as full cane or split with a machete longitudinally into halved or quartered strips or segments. The bamboo
can be cut this way because ofits structural properties and long
fibres with their parallel orientation. Depending upon the age
and species, bamboo can have variable diameters (5-12 cm
[2-5 in] widths are frequently used for building). These characteristics (the eircular cross-section ofvariable diameter) and its
fast growth make bamboo an extremely popular building material. It is easily worked with rudimentary tools and eminently
available.

In the houses of Hani of Yunnan, China, bamboo plays two
major roles in construction: as a structure element in the form
of unworked canes (for posts, beams and rails), or stiffening
frame (e.g. alattice), andas cladding (for floor, wall or fence) in
the form of planks or lattice-boards. These components can
easily be prefabricated, assembled and replaced in the relevant
parts ofthe structure. .

In order to assemble the structural skeleton, canes of different diameters are joined by interpenetration, without auxiliary
means. Canes are inserted into slots ofthe same dimension. At
the ridge, the rafters of small cross-section (7 cm [3 in]) are
bent over the purlin, and sharp-cut to fit it exactly.

The floor consists of bamboo planks attached on halvedcanejoists by battens thathold them down flat. The lashings are
first softened before being used; they are then simply twisted
on themselves and become taut as they dıy. A plank 38 cm (15
in) wide can be produced from a cane with a diameter ofı2 cm
(4.5 in): slits are made atthe extremities and ateach node ofthe
cane, making sure that they are notaligned and therefore cannot split clean. Then, the cane is open longitudinally and
flattened.

Because the walls do not support any load, they are only
required to make protective screens, stiffened on a frame. They
have to provide ventilation and light through the interstices.
This can be obtained by interlacing the strips with each other to
produce weave structures in a variety of ways, varying from
open to tight. .

"The Hani pig paddock is enclosed by a self-supporting structure made ofunworked bamboo canes placed one on top ofthe
other. They are alternated in a perpendicular direction to form
the angle. The raised terrace has a self-supporting floor, also
made from full canes placed on two joists. MYRIAM DAO

222

1.1V.3.a-1 Bamboo huts (Bhutan)

Being cheap, easily available and strong, bamboo is
widely used as a building material in eastern Bhutan. Bamboo
huts are common along the main road, and are often gathered
on the outskirts of the village centres (bazar), but usually they
are spread throughout the steep slopes, one or two houses
being surrounded by small fields.

In most cases the bamboo houses are regarded as temporary
structures, butin fact many people live their whole lives insuch
huts. They are often inhabited by people owning no land (in
many cases Nepalese or Indian foreign workers), who move
around and make their living mainly from different kinds of
construction work. However, the less prosperous Sarchop
(eastern Bhutanese) farmers also live in bamboo huts, most of
them hoping to be able to builda more substantial stone house
some time in the future.

The bamboo huts are built usinga combination ofwoodand
bamboo. Wood is usually used for the load-cartying parts ofthe
walls, floor and roof, but bamboo culms are also used. Houses
standing on sloping terrain often reston wooden stilts.

utsat
