Au

The bamboo used in the walls, as roofing material and sometimes even for the floor, is split, fattened and woven into mats.
The mats used in the walls can be given diagonal patterns
through the use ofacombination ofdarker and lighter coloured
bamboo. Usually there areno windows, onlysmall square openings in the mats to provide a little light to the interior. In some
cases, though, there might be one or two traditional wooden
windows (butwithoutthe traditional surrounding.decorations).

Bamboo mats used as roofing material are common in eastern Bhutan in both traditional stone houses and simple huts.
They are laid in several layers, new ones being placed on top of
the old ifthe roof has deteriorated. The whole roof has to be
renewed every two or three years. The bamboo mats are either
tied directly to the rafters or fastened by wooden or bamboo
sticks placed on top. In some cases, long bamboo ‘threads’ are

woven into the upper mat and fastened directly to the ground.
The bamboo huts are rather small, often 12-15 sq m (130-160

sq ft), and consistofone or two rooms. They usually haveamud

stove with no chimney. Sometimes the fireplace is placed out-

side the hut, in an open shed by the entrance. ELI STOA

1.1V.3.b Cactus; cardön (Chile)

In the Altiplano and high Puna regions of northern
Chile, Argentina and Bolivia the only source of wooden building material is the once widely abundant cactus commonly
known as cardön or pasacana (Helianthocerus atacamensis). This

Grasses and Palms 1.103
columnar plantattains a height of 6 m (20 ft) with a diameter of
60 cm (2 ft) and grows between altitudes of 2500 m (8200 ft)
and 4000 m (13 100 ft).

After being felled the non-woody tip ofthe cactus is cut off
and the trunk is split into 10-20 cm (4-8 in) wide boards which
are then left to dry. The wood is very light in weight but once
dried becomes extremely hard and resistantto rot. Thelongitudinal fibres which form the inner part of the trunk grow in
irregular lines leaving elongated oval holes in the boards,
through which leather thongs are threaded to tie adjacent
boards together to construct doors, floors and ceilings. The
inner part ofthe bark has a denser structure and is used for window-frames, rafters and furniture-making.

CHRISTOPHER SAINSBURY

1.1V.3.c Coconut: cordage (Kiribati)

Ropes and cordage made from the coconut fibres
are produced in Micronesia and Melanesia, and in other parts
ofthe world where coconuts are grown and building materials
have lashed joints. Islanders in Kiribati (former Gilberts) use
the typical process, as follows. Several green nuts with
elongated husks (benu) are selected and are split on a koro, or
spiked tripod of branches. The pieces are placed in a basket
weighted with stones and immersed in shallow lagoon water
where they are left to rot. After ten weeks or so the husks are
retrieved and the woman who makes the cordage beats the bundles with a mallet. Beating helps separate the fibres from other
adhering substances after which they are laid out to dry.

When she commences making the cord, a few fibres ata time
are rolled between her thumbs and forefingers to make thin
strands (binoka). A pair ofsstrands are laid on her right thigh so
that they overlap, and rolled under her palm, further binoka
being fed into the twist so that the cord is extended. When it is

EN (ML

223

## S. 62

U ee = -

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

anticipated that the cord will have to be threaded a needle-like
pinnule ofa palm rib is woven into the end. .

Large rolls of the cordage may be stored for eventual use in
canoe- or house-building. When ropes are required that will
take considerable weight or strain the woman rolls stronger
binoka to make a thicker cord by the customary method. Three
ofher male kinsmen will then tie the leading ends ofthree bundles ofcords to alateral house-beam so that they fall in parallel.
Seated below they pass the cords between them twisting them
in rhythmic sequence so that the reverse tension is given to the
three-stranded rope. Each beam-to-floor span is hung up, the
ends secured to coconut husks until the process is completed
by splicing the ends together, the final rope being some ıs mm
(0.6 in) in diameter.

Cord lashings are used on the sleeping houses in the careful
setting out of the roof-frames, the lashing of the components
and, on completion, for the suspension of the pandanus mats
that are used as temporary walls when required. Butropes and
thick cordage are necessary to lash the heavy beams of coconut
palms and pandanus trunks ofthe great meeting houses (maneaba) ofthe i-Kiribati. PAUL OLIVER

1.1V.3.c-| Coconut palm: yagua (Dominican Republic)

The stem ofthe fronds ofcoconut-palm trees (called
yagua in the Spanish-speaking Caribbean) is a common building material in the Dominican Republic. Itis also the term used
to identify the type of construction which was once widely distributed in the Caribbean, based on yagua. The material is very
tough and fibrous, seemingly a thick woven fabric. To produce
the material, the frond is cut from a palm tree, andis fattened
and put under a press. After a few days of wetting and curing,
the material is ready for use. The final dimension ofthe frond
stem is a rectangular sheet approximately go cmx1.2m (1.3 ft
x4fl.

The frond stem is used for wall enclosures and roofing, as
well as for covering door and window openings. Typical wall
construction consists ofa wood or bamboo frame of vertical
posts and horizontal members 5 cm (2 in) in diameter orscem
(2 in) squareandaboutım (3 ft) on centres forming a large lattice. The frond stem is woven vertically between the horizontal
poles and lashed to them with rope also made from the fibres of
the frond stem. Roof construction is similar with the frond
stems each acting as a large shingle.

Palm-ftond sheets are used as the principal material for
small houses, detached kitchens, livestock shelter and other
utility buildings. These buildings are seldom larger than 4 x5
m (13 x 16 fi). Such buildings are only found in rural settings.

J1. Paul Thompson

B

224

Palm-frond sheets last three to five years before deterioration
requires their replacement. Traditionally the material was a
very low cost form of‘ construction. Owner-builders with palm
trees on their land could construct a building with little or no
cash. By the end ofthe 2oth century, restrictions or shortages
on harvesting coconut trees have resulted in frond sheets losing their economic advantage. PAUL THOMPSON
1.1V3.c-i Coconut: thatch (Oceania)

Coconut thatch is used for the construction ofroofs,
walls, blinds and ridge-capping in Oceania. Whole fronds are
cut from the coconut palm and soaked in brackish water for a
week or more to protect againstrot and insectattack. The thatch
iswoven while the fronds are still waterlogged, theleaves in this
condition being supple and easy to plait. The leaves on each side
ofthe midrib are plaited together on a single side ofthe rib to
form a closely woven waterproof' panel, with the ends tied off.

To prevent the thatch from loosening, a small section ofthe
central stem ofevery fourth or fifth leafis broken clear from the
leaf, bent down and pinned through the leafwhich it overlaps.
This is repeated about every fourth row of plaiting.

In the construction of walls and roofs, the thatch is tied to
battens which are spaced about 50 cm (20 in) apart, with one
row ofthatching every 7-10 cm (3-4 in) along the batten. Each
thatch is about 2 m (6 fi) longand is end-lapped by about2ocm
&in).

SBUMDOH uuor &

An average 25 sq m (270 sq ft) dwelling would require some
two to three hundred panels of thatch for the roof, and these
would normally be plaited by teenage and adult family members while the main house-frame was under construction.

JOHN HOCKINGS

1.1V.3.d Grasses: woven
Grasses include three distinct families, the true
grasses (Ponceae), the sedges (Cyperaceae) and the rushes (Juncaceae). While bamboo is technically considered a grass, its
applications are wide-reaching and are covered elsewhere. In
addition to related reeds and sedges, grass varieties used for
construction include elephant grass, wheat and rice straws,
broom and marrows. The hundreds of grasses available from
steppes, savannas, pampas, velds and prairies are used in many
ways and thatched roofs are found extensively in Europe,
Africa, the Americas and Asia. The insulating and water-shedding properties of grasses are enhanced by weaving or plaiting,
or using bundles. Grass cording is used to bind and tie grass
bundles and woven mats, and to frame structures; patterns of
application vary with climatic and cultural needs. The Zambian
rectilinear straw house exemplifies a range of techniques. In
addition to the thatched roof, the walls are sewn in a binding
technique, and wrapped grass bundles serve as banisters for
the entry steps. A reed hut is depicted on a seal from around
5000 BP, presumably the oldestrecord of grass shelter forms.

Further examples ofthe versatility of grass construction are
seen in the Ngwame houses. Knuffel documents this Bantu
house, describing a domed framework developed with a series
of overlapping arches. Layers of long grass mats, their sizes
specifically woven for each position, are applied for ceilingand
wall sheathing. Next loose grass between ceiling and roof
mats provides insulation; this is covered with roof mats, and
thatching to shed water is woven down. The door is woven separately, and exterior wind-sheltering walls are made of woven
or knotted bundles ofreeds. These stages require up to 65 days,
with work specialized between women and men. The lengthy
construction process assures better protection from the
temperature and high winds ofthe velds.

By contrast, weaving is the main construction method in the
Swazi house. Once the doorway and back are started, the sides
are woven upward. After seasoning occurs under a protecting
mat, other mats cover the original weaving. Fringes ofthatch are
layered from the walls upward, then bound, working from the

Grasses and Palms 1.1.3

top of the dome downward. The degree of portability of these
structures varies, as the Swazi do not move frequently; ifthey do,
itis a function of changing pastoral patterns or other nomadic
requirements. The transported house frameworkremains intact,
with a new grass thatching layer added after moving.

Woven grasses are also used for utensils such as baskets,
sleeping mats and brooms. Woven mats have other applications besides sheathing. Tutsi houses in Rwanda show reed,
cane or elephant grass mats used for interior screening around
bed sites. Elsewhere woven mats are the beds themselves.
Grasses also serve decorative and symbolic functions. Examples include the woven boat structure on extended ridge-posts
ofNage houses in Indonesia, or in Tonga ceremonies to establish a new homestead. Here a grass knot is made where the
headman and his primary wife have consecrated the site oftheir
future dwelling. The tribe members each step on this knot,
symbolically reaffirming their ties to the earth and their fealty
to the headman. DONNA LUCKEY
1.1V3.e Hemp cordage and sennit
Cordage or ropes, also collectively known as‘sennit',

are made from a wide range of vegetable materials. Among the
finest were made from the fibres of Linum usitatissimum, or common flax, widely used as a textile fibre in Europe for the making
of linen; the seeds of the flax plant were pressed to make linseed oil. The cortical, or outer, fibres ofa variety ofplants ofthe
order Urticaceae, or hemps, are used for cord and rope-making.
Among them are the Indian hemp (Cannabis indica beinga commonly used variety which is also the source of bhang or
hashish); the African or so-called ‘bowstring’ hemp, Sanseviera
guineansis, a form of African lily whose leaf fibres were used for
rope-making and cords; and the Agave sisilana, or sisal hemp,
grown in Yucatän.

Other natural materials, such as the hemp-palm, though not

a hemp obtained the name through its similar properties, the
fibres of Champaerops excelsa being used for making cordage in
China and Japan. Jute, its name derived from the Sanskritjata (a
braid of hair), is grown chiefly in Bengal, the bark fibres ofCorchorus plants being coarse but strong and used for making
cordage, canvas and gunny-sacking. The latter (from the Hindi
‚goni, asack) were also made from the leguminous shrub Crotalarea juncea, known by its Urdu name of‘Sunn’, grown throughout India for the making of cords and sacking.

The methods of making rope vary from one culture to
another, but they all incorporate the same principles. Hemp
fibres are often too coarse for twisting on conventional wheels
used for spinning cotton. Instead, the most common method
of making cord for building in Oceania is for the spinner to
twist it by hand. Seated on a stool and with the right thigh
exposed, the spinner selects a bundle offibres, of similar thickness and laid in the same direction. The ends ofasmallnumber
offibres are held firmly with the left hand, and the lengths laid
on the bare right thigh. The palm of the right hand is pressed
firmly on the fibres and rolled towards the knee, where they
are held while they are released from the left hand; the fibres
immediately coil, and the process is repeated with new fibres
being fed in at each stage until the desired length is reached.

225

## S. 63

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

For heavier cordage or rope, three lengths of the cord thus
produced are twisted together in the opposite direction to the
twist ofthe individual threads; this has the effect ofsetting upa
counter-tension which prevents the rope from untwisting.
Some cord-makers use a roofing tile to fit over the thigh to
reduce the roughening effect ofthe fibres on the leg.

A form of Indian hemp (Apocynum cannabium) was used by
Native Americans who collected the bark of the plant and
soaked it to separate the soft inner fibres which could then be
rubbed apart. Twine was made by rubbing over the thigh in the
same fashion as in Oceania, but fibres would be looped over a
branch for twisting or braiding into rope. Rope was also made
from wild hemp (Sesbania macrocarpa), which can grow over 3m
(10 ft) in height, providing long fibres. Dried nettle stalks were
prepared in similar fashion to produce a particularly strong
fibre for rope-making. Many other fibres were used by Native
Americans, including the reed grass (Phragmites communis
Trin.), known as carrizo in the Southwest, from which string
was produced. Yucca leaves were also used in the Southwest,
the leaves being cut into strips and pounded to loosen the
fibres. Three- and four-ply braided ropes were made from the
fibres which had to be used when green, as they became brittle
when dry. A preparation from heated leaves ofthe prickly pear
cactus could be used to keep theyuccarope pliable.

Among the most successful of fibres were gathered from the
leaves of the various species of agave, especially henequen
(Agave fourcroydes), a pineapple-like plant with long, fleshy but
thorny leaves which grew in the dıy limestone region of
Yucatän. The fibres were separated from the leaves and twisted
by the Maya in the usual fashion to make strong rope. Commercially produced on plantations, itdominated world production by 1910. Competition from East Africa where Tanzania
produced both henequen and the thornless Agave sisilana and
eventually, nylon rope, decimated the Mexican enterprises. But
the Maya continue to use henequen rope in Yucatän, and Tanzanian cultures to make sisal rope.

When hemp is to be spun for larger, though not commercial,
production a hemp spinning wheel may be used. A Chinese
type recorded by Hommel took the form ofatripedal stand with
one leg extended to take an open spool with four spokes at
either end, and crosspieces between them. The hemp cord
started over the thigh, was then attached to a crosspiece, the
spool commencing to revolve as the yarn-spinner moved the
lefthand inacircle, causing the cord to twist. PAUL OLIVER

1.1V.3.f Leaves
The mostcommon leaves used as building materials
are from the palms (technically grasses): platano palm leaves
(yolillo [Raphia taedigera]); royal palm leaves (corozoi), and cane
palms; butalso banana, bamboo, and bijagua and chonta leaves
are used. The application of leaves as a roofing material is
found in alltropical climates. They are also used as wall sheathing, for doors, and in woven mats. In this method, panels may
be constructed, with frames to hold the leaves as theyare woven
into the cross-braces. Then the panels are nailed or tied to the
roofgirders orto the walls, as seen in Costa Rica. Other domestic uses include bed stuffing, food, tobacco and crafts.

226

For roofing, leaves may be applied directlyto a framework, as
seen in Central America, Indonesia, Australia and Africa. For
instance, mongongo (a large chestnut-like tree) leaves are used
for roofing by Bambuti and other pygmy tribes. Or, the leaves
may be part ofthe roof thatching sometimes tied in bundles.
Marancaceous (banana-like) leaves are used for thatching in
Zaire for the first roof layer, then grass is applied. Palm leaves
are sometimes woven first, the two halves of the frond being
plaited together to form a small mat. This is seen in Nigeria,
where the mats are laid on the purlins like shingles in a thatch
pattern. Ina typical hut (rancho) in Central America, suita leaves
may be used as the roof sheathing. Readily available in this climatic zone, the leaves are sometimes woven, sometimes layered, like royal palm leaves; sometimes they are tied carefully,
like a braiding or weaving.

The latter process, called ‘pin-thatch’ in some regions ofthe
Afro-Caribbean zone, requires careful attention to details. The
phases ofthe moon must be considered: certain plants mustbe
cutthree or four days after the new moon; others require alater
date, closer to but always before the full moon. This timing
assures protection from insects by cutting the plants when sap
content is high. The leaves are removed from the branches and
dried for several days. Within a hierarchical structure a system
ofpaired cross-braces is tied to the roof framework. Two leaves
are tied together, doubled over the two braces at the top and
bottom, or threaded through between the two braces. The

—

Aa auuodo.

Baling coirfor
Navancore |

amount used varies according to the roof size and density. The
leaves themselves can be loosely placed, lasting only two to
three years, or woven more tightly, lasting up to 15 years.
Advantages in using leaves, as with other forms of natural
vegetation, include the low cost as long as the material is still
available in the immediate vicinity ofthe dwelling; the ability of
leaves to shed water while still allowing ventilation; and aesthetic and traditional considerations. Disadvantages include
potential problems with insects or vermin, and fire hazard. In
many developing nations newer materials and house forms are
introduced in response to these potential health problems. In
addition, the presence of'these different techniques in tropical
zones today varies widely. As the availability ofplants decreases
and craft knowledge is lost, construction quality and traditions
suffer. DONNA LUCKEY

1.1V3.g Palm

Ofthe plants used in building, the class ofPalmaceae,
or palms, is among the most important. Essentially tropical,
palms can grow as far north as southwest Ireland and as far
south as the South African Cape. Though there are numerous
varieties many are characterized by tall and unbranched stems
topped by a pinnate, a crown of branches on either side ofthe
main stem, a fan-form, or a cluster of overlapping leaves. Many
have edible fruits ranging from dates to coconuts, and their disteibution is such that their origins are often uncertain. Typically,
palms provide trunks for supports and roof-frames, long leaves
with pinnules attached to a spine rib, which are used in different ways for thatching or for weaving into mats, and theribs of
the leaves which are used for minor elements or cladding. But
each has its particular benefits for mankind.

Cocos nuciferae or coconut-palms, for instance, may have originated in South America or Oceania, buttheyare spread throughoutthe Philippines, Malaysia and India, and were introduced by
Europeans to West Africa and the Caribbean, where they luxuriate as iftthey were indigenous. They can grow at altitudes of
300 m (1000 ft), and flourish atsea level, liningisland coasts and
bays. The trunks may grow to as much as 30 m (100 ft) in height,

gailsland,

See also "3 and 45 cm (18 in) or more in diameter. Its immense leaves

Ial

thatch.

Grasses and Palms 1.103

bunched at the crest of the tree are used in their entirety with
layers placed from ridge to eaves, or the leaflets are stripped
from theribs, relaid and interwoven to make a more Permanent
roof cladding. Ribs of the leaves, which have tensile strength,
are used for making baskets, while palm fibres are spun into
cordage, with a three-pronged hand whorl.
Coconut shells are used to make drinking vessel and ladles,
while the fibres that surround them are used for thatching or
twisted into ropes and cords. No partofthetree is wasted: ithas
roots thatare a constituentin indigenous medicines oras astringents the ‘cabbage’; or leaf-bud is eaten as a salad or vegetable;
the coconut fruit, apart from providing milkand flesh, has an oil
which is used in liniments. When dried in kilns the white kernels
weressoldas copra, from which coconut oil was expressed. An intoxicating palm wine is made from the inflorescence before the
opening ofthe palm flowers, and thisis distilledas arrack.Vinegar
and a palm sugar can be produced through further Processing.

227

## S. 64

|

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

Many other palms are used for building, but the variety of
species means that applications may be specific to certain
localities — over 150 species of the date-palm are grown in
Mesopotamia alone. They are not without disadvantages: their
surfaces are often so hard that nails cannot be driven into them,
yet they are unsuitable for conventional joints. Palms cannot be
carved, nor can they be trimmed or planed into planks; they
therefore dictate in large measure the nature of the forms
employed. Eloeis guineensis, or the oil-palm of West Africa, the
doum-palm of Niger and the Sahara fringe, the sago-palm
(Metroxylon rumphii), of Malaysia and New Guinea, the palmyrapalm (Borassus flabellifer) and the pandanus palm (Pandanaceae)
are among the principal varieties which are used for building
purposes. Appropriately, the wild lontar-palm (Borassus sunbdaica
beccario) even has the prescriptions for Balinese architecture
inscribed and stored upon books of its leaves.

PAUL OLIVER

1.1V.3.g-1 Palm: cohune (Maya)

The cohune (Orbignya cohune) is one of the largest
and most common palm trees of Central America, reaching a
height of over 30 m (100 ft) in tropical forests. Throughout its
range it is highly valued for the edible oil extracted from its
abundant nuts, for its edible heart and for its long (up to 15 m
[50 ft}) and durable fronds, which are widely used as roofing
material. The wood is used for building docks, the nuts are
burned to drive away insects and the trees play a significant role
in regenerating the fertility of fallowed swidden farms. Many
communities prohibit the cutting of cohune trees, thus assuringa renewable supply of fronds and nuts. The fronds produce
aroofwhich is durable, waterproofand porous, allowing cooking inside the house without the need for a chimney. Their
thickness provides excellent insulation from the sun’s heat,
though also providing a ready habitat for vermin and insects.

The Kekchi Maya of southern Belize use cohune fronds for
thatching their houses. Between 600 and 1000 leaves are used
for each roof, which takes 15 men about 1o hours to finish, not
including the time ittakes to cutand haul the fronds.

The technique is to split each frond in half, up the middle of
therib. The fronds are then tied to the roof-poles in compound
bundles. At the top of the roof the bundles have eight halffronds; the next has seven (three above and four below), and the
rest have five (three below and two on top). The roofers move upwards, lapping upper bundles over lower ones. Atthetop ofthe
house, whole unsplitfronds cover the peak, and are tied down to
provide a watertight seal. Atthe eaves, the leaves are left untrimmed, so they can carry water well away from the house foundation. In the past vines or thin strips of green sapling bark were
used for binding the leaves; today baling-wire is sometimes
substituted.

A cohune roof lasts 5-15 years, depending on the local climate. While the materials are cheap and abundant, thatching
takes a good deal of labour and some skill. There is also no
way to put gutters on a cohune roof to capture rainwater for
domestic use. For these reasons, many rural people are giving
up cohune thatch for the perpetual discomfort of corrugated
steel. RICHARD WILK

1.1V.3.g-ii Palm: date (North Africa)

It is believed that the date-palm tree has been cultivated since antiquity. Several historians have reported that the
island of Herkan in Bahrain was its country of origin, from
where it was taken to Babel in Iraq, where it has a prominent
place amongstthe drawings still visible on the remaining ruins
of that civilization, and then it was taken to North Africa with
the Islamic conquests.

Date-palm trees tend to occupy the most important place
amongst the different agricultural crops which grow in the vast
desert areas of North Africa; the fruit is a nutritious food which
several ofthe people who live in the desertrely on for eatingand
as a source ofincome. It should, however, be noted that dates
are not the only element which makes the trees useful for the
people who cultivate them — there are also other important
components which are used by local people in the construction
oftheir dwellings.

In the Sahara desert, with its predominantly hot, dry climate,
there are several types of traditional settlement whose dwellings were built using local materials, such as earth, stones and
date-palm tree components including their leaves, trunks, ribs
and sheaths. However, this tree and its components are not
only used after its death (to prevent the destruction of‘the Work
of Allah’), but are also the main agricultural resource in the
area. In some cases, timber from other local trees such as willow tree branches has been used where components from datepalm trees cannot be used.

© &ousmaha Baiche

Indeed date-palm trees for construction are becoming
scarce. Some are still available in the palmeraies but can be used
in construction only after they have died. Access to date palm
tree components depends on who owns them. Some people
have to buy the components (palm trunks and sheaths) because
they do not own any trees. Others can obtain free components
because they either own palm trees in the palmeraie or look after
them for other people living outside the village. Since datepalm tree components were essential in the construction ofthe
dwellings in several traditional settlements in North Africa,
this situation raises the question of what might be used to
replace the date-palm tree components in the future.

The date-palm tree components have been used mainly for the
ceilings and roofs ofthe dwellings in the traditional settlements
in North Africa. The tree-trunks have been used as beams and
joists supporting the roof structure, and the palms, palm ribs
and palm sheaths function as the infilling units in the ceilings.

Traditionally doors were made out of chopped palm trunks
joined together by means of palm ribs passing transversally
through hollows which cross the joined trunks. The traditional
door usually rotates around a Y-form bar obtained from local
tree timber, such as willow tree branches. This Y-form cannot
be provided from palm tree timber. Such doors are no longer

Grasses and Palms 1.1V.3

made, because of the scarcity of either material or the craftsmen with skills to make them; they are found only in some
houses, mainly as front doors.

Generally, because of cultural and climatic influences, there
are no windows as such in the traditional dwellings in the
Ksour settlements of the Sahara desert in North Africa, but
some have openings to admit daylight to a few rooms. However, some houses do have windows with shutters made ofthin,
chopped palm trunk planks. Traditional lintels usually consist
of between two and four bars made out of palm trunks laid
close to each other. The number of bars used depends essentially on their thickness and the thickness ofthe wall where the
lintel is used; there is no standardized thickness of the bars
used. In some instances, the lintel ofthe door consists oftwo
spaced palm trunk bars on which palm sheaths are laid.

The main stairs link the ground floor with the upper oneand
are thus frequently used. They are usually built with stones and
mortar. In some cases, the main stairs include steps made out
of palm trunk bars and earth mortar. Usually these stairs are
supported by three to five palm trunks joined together. The
number is notstandard but depends mainly on the thickness of
each palm trunk used and width of the stairs concerned. In
addition, because ofthe limited span that can be covered by the
palm trunks, and in the case ofstairs having long flights, some
of the first steps can be built in solid material before starting
those supported by the palm trunks.

In the ksour, an indigenous technique is used to make the traditional notched ladders which are carved out of palm trunks to
create steps. Such ladders are not frequently used but they have
the advantage ofbeing portable. Their use is limited to providing access to storage rooms on the mezzanine floor which has
no stairs leading to it. They are also used to get to the roofs of
therooms on the upper floor, which may be used to dry dates or
other types oflocal vegetables. BOUSMAHA BAICHE

1.1V.3.g-Iii Palm: palmyra (Timor)

In Belu, central Timor, a ‘male’ shutter (lenik) is simple, without decoration and made by men. Itis basically a ‘raw’
bunch ofwide, fan-shaped, palmyra palm leaves, plaited onlyat
the periphery so as to bundle together the loose, lower ends of
the leaves ofa single complete bunch and thus to form in effect
a peripheral ‘lining’ that helps to shape and stiffen the lenik
properly. This makes the lenik so practical that it can be made
almost instantly from a bunch of palmyra leaves just chopped
off the tree, and can be used flexibly in a number of ways, for
example as an umbrella or parasol, and not berestricted simply
to use in the house. It is also sometimes used as a temporary
container for grain or other items beingworked on in the yards.
‘Female’ leniks are extremely well crafted, made in different patterns and in several instances are coloured. The materials are
properly prepared: not the raw palmyra palm leaves ofone single
bunch, but specially selected ones - more or less of the same
width and quality — that might come from different bunches.
They are restricted to use on the house only, being considered
‘the proper, permanent shutters’, unlike the male leniks which
can be removed from the house and used in different ways.

MARCO KUSUMAWIJAYA

229

## S. 65

1.V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
1.1V.3.h Pandanus (Oceania)

Pandanus (pandanaceae, screw-pine, found in Malay-

sia and Oceania), thatch is generally regarded as superior to

coconut thatch, giving better rainwater protection and having a

higher resistance to decay. Where coconut thatch might last
three to five years before requiring maintenance or replacement,

pandanus thatch will usually give five to seven years protection.

Leaves for thatching need to be some 2 m (6.5 ft) long. Large
numbers of dead leaves are collected, bundled and left to soak
in brackish water for a week, removed and left to dry. They are
then suitable for thatching, but are often rebundled and stored
for up to three years before use.

Because the leaves curl along their midrib as they die, and
remain so even after soaking, they must be flattened into a
shape suitable for thatching. This is done by pulling them back
and forth around a stake driven firmly into the ground, with the
back ofthe leafagainstthe stake.

A panel of thatch is made by wrapping individual leaves
around a 2 m (6.5 ft) length ofthe midrib of a coconut frond.
Beginning with a double layer of leaves, their broader end is
wrapped around the midrib and pinned with a curved bone
needle. The needle is then bent away allowing a skewer made
from the stem ofa single coconut leaf to be inserted to fasten
the thatch permanently to the midrib backbone.

The panels ofthatch are attached to the roof as for the coconut thatch, with the original upper leaf face forming the external face ofthe roof. It is common for the bottom finishing row
of thatch to be formed from a double layer of panels placed
reverse side up. When the thatching reaches the ridge it is overlaid with a woven coconut leaf ridge-capping, fastened with
wooden skewers pinned through theridge and passing under a

ridge-batten. JOHN HOCKINGS

Rattan (Southeast Asia; Oceania)
Structural elements such as round poles, and certain
materials such as bamboos and canes, can be difficult to combine with standard joints such as the mortise and tenon, or with
pegs and nails. Lashing, or binding and knotting, is the most
widelyemployed method ofjoining where such structural components are used. In forestenvironments certain binding materials occur naturally. Among these are the rattans (from the Malay
rautan, Javanese rotang) which are distributed throughout Southeast Asia and western Oceania. Members ofthe genus Calamus
(others include Korthalsia and Daemonkops), they are the species
of palm which are not self-supporting but climb forest trees,
clinging with hooks along their stems, and by the whips, or
probes. As many as fifty rattan stalks may grow from one clump,
each striving to reach the lightwith its upper, floweringtip. Many
rattans grow to remarkable lengths - ıro m (350 ft) is far from
uncommon, and many longer examples have been recorded.
Prolific, though difficult to harvest, the rattans are hauled
down from the trees in which they have become entangled, often
causing considerable pain from their hooks and whips in the
process. The stems are stripped ofthe hooks and leafssheaths by
being passed over a forked branch or through a hole in a post.
Slender, peeled rattans may be dried of their residual sap and
can then be coiled, ready for use as a binding material. Thicker
rattans, whose stems being trunks, are solid, are used to make
household articles including occasional items ofbuilt-in furniture. The rattans are propagated by seed, which is eaten by animals and birds and voided; they are hosts to biting ants and
‘slave’ scale insects which live in the orcea, or base sheath, and

1.1V.3.i

in therings ofhooks.

Lianas (probably from the French lianer, to bind) include a varietyofclimbing plants which are found in Latin American tropical
forests, and which are similarly used in Amazonian building.

1.1V.3.j Reed (Pakistan)

In most ofthe villages of Pakistan’s Sindh province,
houses are constructed with reeds and thatch. These structures
can be erected rapidly, taking no more than three to four weeks
from inception to occupation.

The houses normally consist of one enclosed room and a
veranda. Because of scarcity ofwood, itis used only in the erection of the primary structure. Posts are fashioned out of treetrunks and rough wooden logs used as joists. Sometimes sticks
(ballies) ofbamboo (banse) are also used as joists. Tree branches
or small bamboos are used as purlins. Compared to the flat
toofs ofmud houses in the Punjab province, Sindh houses have
sloping roofs. Since there is little rain in this region the adoption of pitched roofs may have been due to its ease of construction. The roof is covered with reed matting — locally known as
pakha or kana - covered with a final layer of mud which keeps
the matting in place and also provides much needed insulation.
Sometimes, polythene sheets are also used as a layer above the
matting to provide protection from rain.

The room is enclosed bya screen wall, which is erected by
usinga secondary support system consisting ofvertical and horizontal members composed ofa bunch ofreedstied together. The
infill consists ofthin branches oftrees woven together. Sometimes a diagonal pattern using pakha is also imposed on the
screen wall internally and externally, givingan attractive appearance to an otherwise rough structure. The woven twigs allow
eirculation of air through the entire screen wall, helping keep
the house cool during the hot weather. To minimize penetration
ofheat, smallwindows, no morethan 30x45 cm(rx1.5ftl)and
often placed a few inches above floor level, are used to provide
air movement inside the room.

The floors consist of mud-plaster on tamped earth, and are
renewed periodically. There are no foundations and all structural
members and the screen wall are directly placed into the soil.

The screen wall has a relatively short life, and must be
tenewed every four to five years. The pakha is often attacked by
termites in the air and so far no low-cost solution has been
found to protect it from attack. Because ofthe absence offoundations, the screen wall is also vulnerable to water seepage.

YASMEEN LARI

Me

Grasses and Palms 1.1.3

1.1V.3.k Sedge
A chij is a kind of grass mat or screen made by various Central Asian peoples, and taken to its mostelaborate form
by the Kirghiz and Kazakhs. It is made from a kind of sedge
(Lasiogustis splendidus) which grows in the foothills of the
steppe. This sedge is itself called chij by the Kirghiz and Kazakhs and has various uses. It may be woven as an undecorated
mat and used to put under floor felts as a Protection against
damp in the yurt. Such large undecorated chij are also used as
mats in which to roll felt. Decorated chij (choormagan ganat chij)
are also put in between the outer felt wall and the trellis (ganat)
ofthe yurt. On hot days the wall felts ofthe tent are removed so
that only the chij remains as an outer cover. It is very useful in
this role because it lets in air and helps ventilation. Decorated
chij can also be used as a tent divider or screen especially forthe
kitchen partoftheyurt, andarethen called ashkana chij (kitchen
chi”). The top part ofthe felt yurt door is also made from decorated chij.

A chij is made using a warp-weighted loom (said to be the
most ancient form of weaving loom). It is a most efficient and
simple system requiring only a stout, smooth, round bar of
wood and a number of long and evenly weighted stones. The
bar is tied to two posts or trees ofthe same height and a series
ofnotches is carved across the post according to the number of
warp threads required - usually between 12 and 14, according
to the width of the chij. The distance between each thread is
7.5-10 cm (3-4 in). Contrary to the weaving system of most
looms, the warp threads, which must be very strong, are
weighted taut in pairs by being wrapped around the stones
which are left hanging down. As each new chij is added, the
warp threads are physically lifted up over the chij and the bar,
and brought forward or back across the bar, thus creating a
erossover loop which holds the chij in place. The weaving grows
from the bar downwards. The decoration of chij is achieved by
wrapping wools of different colour around the individual chij

av tape" na

ung BlLeudans

231

## S. 66

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

Prior to weaving to form patterns which are created as the chij is
woven. Sometimes a little ash dissolved in water is added to the
wool to help keep it in place. Gaps are sometimes left to give
texture to the pattern. The weaving is done by women and the
chij master is called a chebet.

These days the chij is notso greatly needed because most peoplenow live in houses in villages. However, those who still have
yurts which they take to summer pastures (jailo) still make chij
for their tents. Modern chijtend to be ofa much simpler pattern
than in the past, and can often be quite figurative. Traditionally,
chij have very geometric designs, and the museums of Bishkek
in Kirghizia have some excellent examples ofchij with very intricate and fine patterns.

Other peoples who use chij include the Turkmen and the
Firuzuki of Afghanistan. Those Turkmen who still live in yurts
often use undecorated chij for the outside covers of their yurts.
This is necessary for ventilation in the extreme heat of Turkmenistan. STEPHANIE BUNN

1.1V3.| Straw (Japan)

Straw (wara) is the dried stem ofrice orwheatand is
the by-product after the taking ofthe crop. From ancient times
various articles made of straw have been produced in all districts in Japan. These straw products, which were produced on
the side by farmers, are not only useful living goods but also
beautiful formative arts ofhigh quality. All kinds of articles for
living are produced, for example, rope for binding, straw baskets for packaging and bales to pack the rice, straw hats, straw
sandals, straw boots and straw coats. These straw crafts have
been very common in the countryside; every man and woman
could make straw products for their living. Even for children,
the ability to plait a rope is required for them to be considered
fully ledged.

Straw is important for architecture and has long been used as
a part of building materials. In Japanese folk houses, straw is
used for thatch, as the base oftatami mats and to strengthen
walls, the cutstraw being mixed with clay to bind it. Straw is also
used as a wall material in these houses. The wall is built with a
lattice frame ofsplit bamboo and then plastered with claymixed
and strengthened with cutstraw. This wallissmoked overalong
period, a process which has a particular significance.

Sometimes, straw products are treated as sacred, and the
ornaments made of straw are used in annual events or rites of
passage. Shimenawa, the sacred straw festoon, and many kinds
ofstraw dolls and horses, and straw bales, are usually made for
ceremonies and as marks offaith.

Their use is related to the useful parts of a length of straw.
The soft part which corresponds to the blade is used for fuel;
the strong stem is used for crafts. Slightand elastic head is used
especially for brooms. Atthe end oftheir life these products are
burned and used as fuel, their ashes returned to the ground as
fertilizer and as a source ofthe next life ofthe rice plant. Thus,
from the ecological viewpoint, straw has a complete recycling
system.

Nowadays, very few straw crafts are produced as living ware,
but straw still provides a base for modern Japanese culture.

KIYOSHI MIYAZAKI

232

stub and roofs

1.1v.3.m Thatch

Thatch is widely used for roofing, particularly in
Asia, Polynesia, sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America. Thatching was widespread in Europe but declined during the 2oth
century owing to increasing costs as suitable materials became
hard to findand as labour costs rose, and equally because offire
regulations.

Thatch is generally considered a desirable covering for country homes in France, Holland and England, whilst in much of
Africa and Asia itis regarded as roofing for the poor.

Quality and durability vary enormously from region to region
and depending on the plants and techniques used. In Europe,
thatch using reed (Phragmites communis) can last sixty or more
years, and examples ofthatching with broom have lasted over a
hundred years in France. Almost identical methods are
employed in Holland using reed and in Zambia using elephant
grass, and both achieve good durability even in different climates. Durability is not, however, the sole criterion. Roofs that

005

See also for
2.1.2.d Kazakh
2.1.2. Kirghiz.

References fo;
Makimov, V.
Y.,1986
Marquian,
Thompson, 1,16

1.v11.6.j-1 Sacredb
‚shimenawa
2.1.8-D Tatami

Thatcher working
thatch roofat
‚Angmerin;
Sussex, Engl

last three or four years can suit the local climatic and socioeconomic context. In both Laos and Iran, for example, different
techniques have developed which make it easy to replace parts
ofthe roofannually, thus spreading over the years the effort of
collecting straw or grasses and the labour of thatching. Thin,
easily replaced roofs can be more realistic in hot humid conditions where durability is difficult to achieve. Thick thatched
roofs are good insulators, but if dampness penetrates they are
likely to remain humid. The roofmustbe prevented from acting
as asponge and a steep pitch is important to ensure that water
runs offthe roof quickly.

Dampness, especiallyunderwarm humid conditions, encourages the development of micro-organisms and fungi which in
turn attract insects to feed. The fungi and insects progressively
remove the host material, then birds are attracted by the insects,
and also remove material for making nests. Keepingtheroofdry
thus reduces these major factors of decay. Rodents are particularly attracted by grains on the stems. Treatment against insects
is possible, but often uneconomic compared with the benefits
achieved. Putting copper wire along the ridge allows copper
ions to be washed out by rain over the surface ofthe roof, and is
quite effective for reducing microbiological development. Wind
and sun also damage the exposed parts ofthe roof, but can help
to keep the stems dıy. On the shores of Lake Titicaca (Peru/
Bolivia), easily replaced grass and straw are placed over reeds to
make them last longer. Fire can be a problem, and the best protection is to keep the source of fire away from the thatch; coating
with earth also helps in highrriskareas. Researchers have worked
on fireproofing treatments, but cheap viable methods are difhieultto achieve.

Thatching techniques can be divided into three basic ‘families’: those that use rigid stiff stems, like reeds; those that use
supple stems; and those that use mats and leaves. In general,
the first is the most durable. Rigid stem thatching is done with
wheat ‘reed’ and water reed in England, with water reed in
France and Holland, and with stiff grass in Zambia. In Japan
similar techniques to those in Holland and Britain are used.

Grasses and Palms 1.1.3

The covering is thick, using stems some 1.2 m (4 ft) long. The
stiffness ofthe stems allows them to be laid so that only the last
2-3 cm (0.8-1.2 in) tip of each stem is exposed with a bristly
surface. Rainwater drips from stem tip to stem tip, so that, if
the root is well laid, water quickly runs off it and very little of
each stem gets damp. These dry stems therefore last longer.
The stiffness ofthe stems also reduces possible damage from
wind. To keep the stiffness, care must be taken in harvesting:
stems must not be bent or broken. Wheat ‘reed’ is actually
wheat whose grains are ‘combed’ offthe end ofthe stem withoutbreaking it. Ifwheat is putthrough a thresher to remove the
grains it breaks and bends stems so that they can only be used
with the less durable supple stem roofing techniques.

Supple stem thatch cannot achieve the bristly effect, and
instead presents a cascade of stems with several centimetres of
stem exposed to surface water and faster decay. Wind can move
the supple stems and damage them. Supple stem techniques
are used with rice-straw and rushes in northern Iran, where
individual bundles are ties onto the roof. In Vietnam and Laos
prefabricated panels using grasses also produce a supple stem
finish.

Palm and other large leaves are often used for thatching,
either laid in a manner similar to tiles, as in the case of large
pandanus leaves, or typically as parallel rows of date or coconut
fronds. Woven mats of fronds or split bamboos are also used
for fairly temporary roofing. JOHN NORTON
1.1V.3.m-i Thatch (Japan)

The roof cladding ofthe Japanese folk house may be
grouped into straw-thatched roof, shingle-roofed and tileroofed, according to the materials used. For the straw-thatched
roof, Kaya (including Imperata cylindrica, Miscanthus sinensis
Dapanese plume grass] and Miscanthus sacchariflorus), rice straw,
wheat straw and bamboo grass are variously used for the material. The folk house with straw thatch has been distributed inall
parts of Japan until modern times. But few of them remain
today because ofthe lack ofthatchers and suitable materials.

Straw thatch needs to be renovated every 20 or 30 years.
Because it required a lot of straw and labour to thatch a house
this work was usually held as a joint activity in the community.
To thatch the roof, some of the materials such as cogongrass,
straw and straw rope, were raised not only from the village but
also from other communities, while the labour was mainly
dependent on the villagers. This work was done by rotation in
the village year by year, but thatching as an anniversary event of
the whole village was lost with the appearance of the professional thatcher. However, renovation was still the greatest event
for the house owner.

Itis said thatthe durability of straw thatch may be sometimes
more than 50 years; the smoke from the fireplace (irori) seems
to relate to this. Fire in the irori is made for the purpose of cooking, lighting and also heating, but because the irori does not
have a chimney, the smoke passes among the beams and ceiling, and goes outside through a hole in the top ofthe roof. The
beams and thatch are thus smoked all the time and are protected from vermin and rotthrough the sterilization and dıying

effects ofthe smoke. KIYOSHI MIYAZAKI

233

## S. 67

IV MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

1.1V.3.m-ii Thatch (Vietnam)

The cover of all the vernacular buildings in Vietnam
is vegetal, southern groups using mainly grass (Imperata cylindrica) and those in the hilly regions using Corypha or Calamus
palm. Less frequently bamboo tiles or tree leaves were used by
the Södang; bamboo tiles were placed in the manner of clay
tiles while leaves were strung and tied.

Among the Jörai a handful of grass was placed over a pair of
bamboo laths, between which the upper ends ofthe grass were
wedged, the trailingends hanging free. The same principle was
employed by the Maa and the Nop who used palm leaves placed
individually over the laths.

Imperata cylindrica was used by the Jörai and Sre who cut several long internodes of bamboo (Oxytenanthera). Preserving the
node at one end, they split each internode into two or more
Pieces according to thesize and then spliteach piece to produce
a long, three-spline frond. This was placed on the ground and
thenodes held with the feet. A bunch offresh Imperata (French:
herbe & paillote) was held in the left hand and worked as for weaving, the bamboo fork being the warp and the grass the weft.
Handfuls of grass were alternately interwoven between the
three splines, with the grass either above, or below, the central
prong. Compressingthe bunches againsteach other, the process

3 N >
was continued until the ‘warp’ was filled. A grass ‘tile’ was now
ready to place on the roof. An average roof required 200-300
tiles. The tile was placed on the roof battens with the grass
stalks above, and fastened with bamboo ligatures (never with
rattan) from the roofeaves to therridge.

For the covering ofthe ridge ofthe roof separately made tiles
were necessary. A large bamboo with several internodes was
split into seven splines, three on each side oftheridge. The central spline was bentata rightaangle to rest over the ridge-pole.
JACQUES DOURNES

NUR ERS

woven 'tile$

ıva Rocks and Stone

Stoneasa building material consists ofrock, or inorganic materials, obtained from or near the surface ofthe earth
thatare usedas found, or shaped and assembled. In their natural
state rocks are geologically classified into three main groups.
Oldest are the igneous rocks which resulted from the cooling
ofthe molten magma as the lithosphere, or earth’s crust, was
formed. Many arehighin silicates, and the group includes granite, basalt and pumice. Sedimentary rocks were formed by the
compression ofstone detritus or sediments which had resulted
from the action ofwater and ice. Laidin beds, they bear in some
cases the remains of early life in the form ofshells and fossils.
They include the sandstones, shales, dolomite and limestones.
The third group, metamorphic rocks, have been produced bythe
transformation ofolder rocks and oils which have been. subject
to immense pressure and heat. They usually embody planes of
cleavage, are generally fine-grained, and include slate, marble,
schists and quartzite.

Distribution and accessibility of building stone

On the surface sedimentary rocks predominate, but they often
form onlyarelativelythin skin. Igneous rocks are presentin every
region, among the most conspicuous being in northern and
Alpine Europe, the former Soviet Union, Western Australia, and
the mountain chains ofboth Americas, from the Appalachians
and the Rockies to the Andes. Tufa, or sedimentary rock formed
from deep layers offalls ofvolcanicash, isalso widely dispersed;
in an intermittent belt from the Mediterranean to Chinaitis used

extensively in building. Metamorphic rocks are also well distributed; the most important sites are to be found in Canada, the
Arabian peninsula, south Asia, Brazil and parts of Africa.
Rocks can be modified by physical change caused by pressure, the action ofice or saltcrystals, differential expansionand
contraction, chemical reactions involving water as the agent,
and erosion by wind, rain or sea. These changes, generally
termed ‘weathering’, are accelerated in warm and wet climates,
Biological impact such as chemicals from lichens, the growth
of root systems, or the burrowing of animals or molluscs also
contributes to modification, and all such natural processes
cleave, fracture or break down rocks, making the material more
accessible for use. Suitable stone for building is frequently
found exposed, especially in upland areas of Europe, Asia and
the Americas. Talus, orrock fall caused byerosion, may provide
ample stone which requires little or no working. Strata oflimestone or sandstone are often evident on cuestas, or ridged
escarpments, and can be quarried with the aid ofwedges, drills
or explosives. Quarries sited to facilitate extraction may be near
building sites, but rollers, sledges and rafts, in addition to
vehicular transport, have been used to move building stone to
distant sites when it has been considered economic to do so,

Properties ofstone

Itcan be hypothesized thatthe original stone shelter was a cave
used without adaptation, and some caves and rock shelters, as
in southern Africa, have been in use for thousands of years.
Caves that have been further excavated or extended and
modified are widely dispersed, and dwellings formed by the
subtractive processes are found in the Asian tufa belt and in
parts of Ethiopia, North Africa and Europe.

Massive stone slabs are often used to pave roads and to construct causeways, jetties and harbours. Plinths of stone on
which buildings in other materials have been raised are found
in Melanesia and elsewhere, while foundations of stone, or
walls on which timber buildings have been erected, are common in Balkan and Alpine regions. But the common use of
stone in building is to enclose space by the construction of
walls supporting floors and roofs.

Although occasionally massive rocks of many tons in weight
have been used, as in pre-Columbian Peru, stone employed to
create shelter on the ground is normally obtained in sizes convenient for handling. The shaping and assembly of stone is
related to the type of rock source available, the variety and
sophistication of the tools and mechanisms utilized, and the
degree of skill ofthe builders or masons. Dry stone techniques
ofassembly may be used where exclusion ofrain or moisture is

235

## S. 68

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

not important, but mud or mortar is usually necessary as a
bonding agent for building with rubble.

As stone is strong in compression it has been widely used for
foundations, load-bearing walls, columns, arches, domes and
vaults where bending stresses are reduced to theminimum. Stone
is also used to support walls over window and door openings, but
only when the span is not great and the depth ofthe lintel is generous. Because ofits weightand density many forms ofstone were
able to resist cannon shot, and it has been used by many cultures
forthe construction offortifications. Its resistance to fire has reinforced the image ofstone as being permanentand safe.

Strength in stone is dependent on its density, or the relative
weight and close packing of its atomic structure, which ranges
from the hardness of igneous granite to the softness of sedimentary coral ragstone. Durability is also related to densityand
the ability to resist erosion, though in urban and industrial
areas acids from atmospheric pollution can cause deterioration. Stones which are cellular in structure, including coral
stone and pumice, are hygroscopic and absorb water, which
may limit their useful life. The thermal performance of stone is
also important in building, and the minute pockets of air in
pumice result in a high degree of insulation. Heavier and
denser stone may be used in thick walls to delay heat transmission andto provide a high thermal capacity. Stone floors are frequently preferred for their durability and appearance. As the
latent heat of stone is low, it is cool to the touch when in the
shade, a characteristic which is valued in warm climates,
though it may be seen as a disadvantage in colder ones.

By the use of corbelling, complete domed roof systems in
stone are possible. Flatroofs may be constructed with relatively
short and deep stone slabs supported over narrow spans on
arches or vaults. A mixture ofbroken stone aggregate and mor-

tar can provide a waterproof finish with a reasonable degree of
insulation. Some metamorphic rocks, such as slate, are naturally laminated and can be used for roof cladding laid to a fall
with overlapping joints. Used as roofing or walling material,
slate is both thin and dense and therefore poor as insulation.
Other materials, such as soil, timber or plaster, are often used
as thermal lining.

Aesthetic qualities
Finishes in stone vary from natural surfaces to smooth polishing
to textures obtained by toolingor hammering; rough and smooth
may be used in contrast. Thejoints between stones can be emphasized by pointing, a process known as rustication (i.e. in the rustic manner), which indicates its vernacular origins. Dressed
ashlar masony, which is carefully worked and shaped, requires
the time ofskilled masons and is therefore costlyand customarily
limited to the houses ofthe wealthy or to important buildings.
Sedimentary rocks are easier to work than igneous rocks,
while the compressed close texture of metamorphic rocks
enables them to take fine details. Both sedimentary limestones
and metamorphic marble afford opportunities for carving.
Columns, capitals, keystones, cornices and architraves, among
other details, are often carved with mouldings or human,
animal, floral or abstract motifs. Colour, patterns and textures
are almost unlimited, particularly in the different ranges of

236

marbles, which are frequently used for aesthetic reasons, either
in mass or in laminates. Stone in laminated form and in
different colours is also widely used as a floor finish, whether in
random arrangements or in elaborate geometric patterns.
Tesserae, or small units of stone, may be laid as mosaic set in
plaster, and wind- or water-worn pebbles from sea-shore or
river-bed are sometimes used to form floor patterns. Decorative stonework is employed for both internal and external wall
finishes, but for both aesthetic and sanitary reasons finely
polished surfaces may be required for internal walls and floors.
For structural purposes stone has been largely superseded by
reinforced concrete, and walls are frequently built in cement or
clinker block. Stone columns are now rarely used, exceptwhere
they have been recycled, and the making ofsuspended floors in
stone has virtuallyended. Stone, however, is still widely used for
walling and roof-covering and, in acknowledgement of ts aesthetic qualities, concrete or brick may be faced in stone where

the economics of building make this permissible.

1.1V4.a Ashlar

Applied to plain blocks of stone, the term ‘ashlar’
essentially means stone that is squared, finely dressed, and
jointed to given dimensions and usually laid in courses. The
squared stones are used for facing, dressed to provide various
textures. The term is also used for any squared, finished building stone unit with fairly fine dressings faced on both sides, as
for a parapet wall. Ashlar can be used for massive as well for
thin masonry.

The structure and form ofa building can be expressed by varying the use ofthe ashlar to combine functions with ornament.
Monotonous expanses of ashlar walls can be relieved by adding
a few courses ofashlar in differentshades and co-ordinated with,
for example, sills and lintels, to give a decorative appearance.

The exposed face may be sawn-cut with a saw, rubbed, rockor pitched-face, tooled, pointed, bush-hammered, and vermiculated or rusticated with various margins, usually ofa pattern
different to that ofthe central part. All these textures have been
obtained in the vernacular traditions by hand-dressing. In
handcutting, the ashlar is prepared either with hand tools,
rock-splitters or mechanical hammers and chisels.

Ashlar can be produced from any type of stone. The use of
fissile or sedimentary types requires special precautions. The
importance of bedding in an ashlar - or for that matter any
building - stone cannot be overemphasized. Rocks have been
laid down by nature in a balanced manner and putting them
down in any direction could gradually upset this careful stacking. Forthis reason stone is best laid down in the same way that
itwas deposited, or exactly theopposite way up when, however,
it may be prone to exfoliation. There are some exceptions to
this rule: in ashlar made of a stone of uniform structure, for
instance, the rule can be relaxed.

When masonry was carried out mainly by hand, the units
were usually produced at the quarry site and attention to these
details was taken for granted. Nowadays, stone may be fabricated many miles away from the quarry site, sometimes to the
detriment ofthe qualities required.

Rocks and Stone 1.1.4

To save weight during transportation, the stones or rough
blocks are shaped into unfinished squares or rectangles at the
quarries. Finishes of ashlar dressing can be specified in a general manner only, considering that tool widths, direction of
application and pressure intensities vary from mason to mason.
The types of ashlar indicated may have one or more of the
finishes described below.

In the case of rock- or pitch-faced ashlar the face is made to
resemble the natural break as it comes from the quarry, and the
joint edges are straightened. No tool marks are left on the
stone, unless specially required. Exaggerated rock face is usually applied to large blocks rather than to conventional ashlar
sizes which seldom exceed a course height of4o cm (16 in).

With picked ashlar rough-jointed and fine-pointed projections on the face are taken off by using a chisel point or pick,
leaving a pockmarked dressing. This is also known as handpicked, rough-picked, fine-picked or chisel-picked. The pick is
used for finer work than the cchisel, and the shallower and more
delicate effect is produced by a short, direct stroke.

Drafted margin ashlars are produced by cuttinga margin ata
few centimetres from the edge, with a chisel, axe or peen-hammer. This type of dressing is used extensively where rock-faced
bosses project from the back-set margins, typically finished off
byaserrated chisel dressing. In the larger stones, margins may
be 7-13 cm (3-5 in) wide and the bosses may project between 5
cm (2in)and 25 cm (10 in). The height ofthese stones usually
exceeds 50 cm (20 in) and they should, strictiy speaking, be
termed blocks rather than ashlars. Parallel, vertical or broken
horizontal lines ofa regular pattern may be produced with the
use ofa claw chisel which has a series ofsmall cutting edges.

Drag is similar to tooled work, but the grooves are discontinuous. It is usually applied by a serrated chisel-edged hammer
dragged over the surface. Short strokes are applied and the
incisions measure between 25 mm (tin) and 50 mm (2 in) in
length for each stroke. Bush-hammered ashlar is done with a
hammer to produce a rougher and more pitted appearance than
other tooled work. The bush-hammer is faced with sharp
pyramidical points, giving a dotted finish. Vermiculated is a
decorative dressing worked to give a worm-eaten appearance
in the form of irregularly shaped winding ridges. Rusticated
ashlar is a ‘rustic’ form of wall finish in which the main surface
is not dressed but has fluting between courses and, less frequently, between joints, forming a sunken dressed margin.

Rubbed ashlar is finished by hand to give a fairlysmooth surface. A ‘parpend’ ashlar is a Scottish term for ashlar which is
faced on both sides as for a parapet wall.

The type offinish used depends on the nature and the texture
of the ashlar. Generally a hard, smooth stone can take any
dressing. A hard, granular or calcareous stone should either be
sawn or else given a rock-faced finish.

With oolitic limestones, used extensively for instance in the
Cotswolds, sawing and rubbing may spoil the natural texture;
inacoarse-grained sandstone, quartz grains, beingresistantto
sawing or rubbing, are mostlikely to break outofthe cementing
material intact after sawing, thus providing a naturally porous
effect.

ASHER SHADMON

237

## S. 69

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
1.1V.4.b Cave shelter .
Cave shelter makes use of the structural properties
of rock or earth in situ. The use of natural caves predates the
emergence of Homo sapiens sapiens; however, the vast majority of
cave structures in use today are manmade.

Although cave spaces are formed by excavation rather than
construction, constructed elements may form an integral part
of their structure. The semi-underground hut, with a sunken
floor, partially earth-covered or beamed walls and a roof of
conventional materials (thatch, slate or tile on a timber framework), has a long history in the culture of Europe and elsewhere, The technique of earth sheltering furthermore uses a
thick layer ofearth backfill to give a constructed building some
ofthe characteristics ofa cave shelter. Cave structures are to be
found in many countries ofthe world, including Mediterranean
regions, north and central America, the Near and Middle East,
China and Australia. An estimated 5 million cave dwellings are
still in occupation, ofwhich over 80 per centare in China. j

Geology has an influence on siting: rocks must be easily
excavated but not too friable. Suitable geological conditions
can be offered by: limestones - often in regions where natural
caves have formed solution cavities, which can form the basis
of cave excavation, for example in southern Italy; volcanic
deposits of consolidated volcanic ash, basalt or tuff precipitated from hot springs, as for example in Cappadocia, Turkey;
loess - a consolidated deposit ofwind-blown soil as found, for
example, in central China and southern Tunisia; and softsandstones, for example in South Australia.

One ofthe benefits ofunderground dwellings, compared with
above-ground dwellings, is that they are warm in winter and cool
in summer. This is aresultofthe stability oftemperatures within
a cave dwelling, due to the moderating influence of the surrounding rock mass. This characteristic is most adyantageous
in climates with large diurnal and annual temperature swings,
for example South Australia. In moderate climates, thermal stability leads to a need to heat the dwelling throughout the year:
nevertheless, total fuel use may be less than that required by an
above-ground building oftraditional construction.

Thermal inertia, however, may contribute to dampness, even
inadwelling which does not suffer from infiltration of surface
water. Seasonal changes in rock temperatures lag behind those
ofthe ambient environment. In spring particularly, the surface
temperature of ccave walls is considerably below that ofthe outside airand condensation may be deposited on the internal surfaces of the cave. Ifthe cave is occupied, conditions are made
worse because ofthe additional water vapour generated within

the dwelling by respiration and household activities such as
cooking and washing. Infiltration of surface water is a fürthier
potential problem, which can be mitigated by good maintenance and management ofthe vegetation growing above.
There are remarkable similarities between cave dwellings in
different parts of the world. These include cliff, pit and dual

aspect dwellings. .

The cliff dwelling, or ‘elevational’ design, requires little sitework before excavation ofthe house, and is adapted to steeply
sloping sites. Examples are found in every cave dwelling region.

In the pit dwelling, or ‘courtyard’ type, cave rooms surround

238

b) earth shelter

a) semi-underground

d) cliff dwelling e) pit dwelling

a central sunken courtyard. This is often the entrance space, as
in Chinese and Tunisian examples, but may function simply as
alighting and ventilation well, for example in Aragön in northern Spain. The space may be partly orwholly covered to form an
atrium. The design is suited to flat sites.

Dual aspect, or ‘penetrational’ layout, can be found in rockcut dwellings, but only encountered in unusual formations
such as narrow outcrops ofthe “fairy chimneys’ ofCappadocia.

Site preparation for a typical cliff dwelling consists of levelling an entrance area — often adjacent to a roadway - and cuttingavertical facade. The living space is then hollowed out. The
front wall may be monolithic, or constructed of masonıy or
some other material after completion of digging; to some extent
this is dependent on excavational techniques and equipment.
Once the space has been dug out, additional structural members,
such as masonry arches or vault lining, may be put in place to
assist support. Dwellings may consist of a single unit of this

type, or parallel units linked together by lateral openings.

A window is usually setto one side ofthe door, and (in cooler
climates) the main chimney breast is also placed on the outside
wall, or on a side wall close to the facade so that a flue can be
bored through the rock where the thickness is nottoo great. In
the first few metres ofa cave, therefore, the daylighting, heating and ventilation are maximized: this zone close to the outside wall is thus used most intensively- darker alcoves towards
the rear being used for sleeping and for storage.
