1.1V.4.b-i Cave shelter: Han

Cave dwellings (yao dong) in China are almost always
cut from loess, a wind-blown deposit with the texture of soft
rock. Most are found in the area of the loess plateau, which
stretches northeast from the central drainage basin of the
Yellow River (Huanghe). This region extends into the provinces
of Henan, where 10 per cent ofthe 70 million inhabitants are
cave dwellers, Shanxi (with 5 million cave dwellers, a quarter of
the rural population), Shaanxi, Gansu and Qinghai, together
with parts ofthe autonomous regions of. Xinjiang, Ningxia-Hui
and Inner Mongolia.

An estimated 40 million Chinese live in caves today. However, freestanding terraces of earth-roofed ‘caves’ (dishang) are
included in the extension ofthis term in China. These are earthsheltered structures where the vault is constructed ofstoneand
adobe blocks - sometimes over formwork of loess, which is
then removed by excavation - and later backfilled to level the
roofterrace.

Chinese cave dwellings can be divided into those cut horizontally into a steep hillslope and those excavated out from a
vertical pit.

In cliff dwellings, a simple side-by-side array is most common, each household having at least two or three caves. Caves
may intercommunicate horizontally, so thatlateral caves do not
need an outside door. Occasionally, the house has a second
storey of caves above the first, which may be reached by an
external stairway ofstone or timber, oraccessed inside byaladder. Earth excavated from the caves is tamped to form access

open

courtyard daughter-in-law]

|ramp to groun.
level

EEE RETTET

Rocks and Stone 1.IV.4

toads to the terraces, front courtyards and outbuildings like
animal pens and latrines. Vegetables are stored in pits (jiaou)
within the courtyard area.

The pit dwelling is more suited to a flat site, The courtyard is
normally rectangular, aroundıom (33 ft) squareand6m (20 ft)
deep, giving 2.5-3 m (8-10 ft) overburden above the crown of
the cave vaults. Some large pits may be shared by up to ten families. However, ina more typical situation, two generations ofa
family will share a dwelling, each household having a separate
kitchen and livingaccommodation while latrines and stores are
shared. The household of the eldest son usually occupies the
caves to the north of the pit, that is, those with the most
favourable southern orientation. Parents and younger siblings
occupy the eastand west sides, while the shady south often has
a lean-to construction, or subsidiary stores and latrines. The
south, being the less desirable cave aspect, is also most commonly the side on which the entrance ramp or stairway issues.
Owing perhaps to the Chinese concern for Privacy, there is usually a single or double elbow on the descent, or a screen wall
blocking the view from a direct entıy. There may be stables
opening off this ramp, or a ‘farmyard’ court giving onto the
dwelling pit; and there is often a soakaway for waste water in
the centre ofthe court.

Both cliffand pit type dwellings are composed of groupings
ofcave elements which aretoa large extentstandardized within
regions. Differences between the three main regions can be
seen in the proportions ofthe cave in planand section, disposition ofinternal elements and facade treatment.

In the Longdong region, from southeast Gansu to central
Shaanxi, the arched shape of the vault is expressed on the
facade by an arch which encloses three openings: door to one
side, window to the other and a smaller ventilation opening at
top centre. The taller caves are sometimes subdivided horizontally to give an upper garret in timber. The kang, a raised brick
platform heated from within by a sort of hypocaust system, is
generally placed beneath the window on the front wall. This is
alsoacommon feature ofabove-ground houses in the region. It
is used as a bed in winter, and as a dais for taking meals.

Caves in the Yuxiregion, from thenorthern partofHenantothe
Yellow River, tend towards a bottle-shape opening out towards
the interior. Some are very long, particularly in Gongkian county

239

## S. 70

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

(Henan), where rooms are excavated in sequence back into the
clifF face, and separated by cloth screens. Openings are relatively
small, but in this more humid region, doors and windows are
usually framed in brick and often surmounted by oversailing
courses to throw stormwater clear.

In the Yenan region, northern Shaanxi, the vault is parallel,
and semicircular in cross-subsection. Around Yenan itself the
use of stone masonty for the vault enables the width between
adjacent caves to be reduced. Typically, the whole round-arched
facade is closed off’by a timber lattice screen, filled in with oiled
silk or rice-paper around the door and window openings. Allowing lightto penetrate deep into the interior enables the kang to be

placed towards the cave’s rear. HELEN MULLIGAN

1.\V.4.c Cobbles and setts

Related to the medieval English verb ‘cob’, to strike,

the name of the cobblestone recalls its early use as a striking

implement. Cobbles are rounded stones, generally of fist size

or capable of being held in the hand, which have been wor

smooth by the action of Ice Age glacial drift, or by water in or

near rivers, or where rivers have flowed in the past. Soemewhat
smaller, and often flatter, pebbles are similarly smoothed and
rounded by water action, and are frequently associated with
‘pebble beaches’ on the seashore. On the Wessex coast the 30
km (18 mi) Chesil Beach is remarkable for the natural grading
of such pebbles up to the size of small boulders but, being
subject to coating with sea salt, their use is limited. In spite of
their often attractive form and colour, cobbles and pebbles have
weaknesses for building, principally because their forms and
surfaces reduce friction and hence their suitability as a structural material.

Stones from river-beds, whether flowing or dried, are used
structurally in Afghanistan for example, butthey require bonding with concrete where possible, as without considerable use
ofa secure mortar they are subject to shear action and therefore
to wall collapse. They are also irregular in form and this too can
affect the stability ofa wall, a problem solved in parts ofBeiras,
Portugal, by splitting river stones so as to presentaa flat surface
on the wall face. In some circumstances cobbles may be used as
a filler between inner and outer wall skins. More often, as in
Roussillon in France, theyare combined with quoins ofdressed
ashlar, or in Weald and Anglia in the British Isles, with brickwork at corners, around doors and windows and other points
where structural strength is especially important. The cobbles
and pebbles may be impressed into cement and are often
coursed to heighten their decorative effect. Sometimes this is
further emphasized by ‘galleting’ or the marking of couxses
with rows ofsmall pebbles or splinters offlint.

Pebbles ofsmall diameter are used as a wall treatment, being
thrown or ‘dashed’ against pre-laid areas ofwet mortar or render; this method, though used traditionally in some parts of
Scotland, was popular in the 19205 and 1930s as a protective
surface for suburban houses. A related technique using a mixture of lime mortar and gravel was also employed; known as
‘rough-cast’ it was already in use in the ı6th century. .

Most frequently, cobbles are used as a durable paving or
flooring material, their hard, rounded surfaces being resistant

240

. cobbles.

to rutting by cartwheels and giving a secure footing for horses,
though they can cause some discomfort to pedestrians. Easily
washed down and safer than flagstones in icy weather, they are
extensively used beside harbours. Carefully selected for their
colour, which ranges from dark grey through shades of brown
to white, they can be laid in patterns, with simple devices and
even dates, as at Dartmouth in Devon. Cobbles can be embedded directly into clay, with the narrower surface uppermost,
or packed close together in a layer of sand, or laid evenly in a
bed of cement. Cobbled roads were extensively used throughout Europe for hundreds of years, and many archaeological
sites reveal the use of cobbles around and within houses and
byres. Innumerable examples survive in the Iberian and Italian
peninsulas.

While cobbles are generally used as found, setts or blocks of
granite, basalt or other igneous rocks were worked by road
paviers who split and dressed the stone into blocks, often a
handspan in length and a palm-breadth in width and depth.
The setts were laid in rows with central channels oflarger setts
for street drainage, but frequently the action of laying the
blocks from one position resulted in fan shapes. These became
recognized as having decorative value as did the radiating setts
laid around urban trees. During the ıgth century setts were
extensively laid in European towns, though the clatter of
horses’ hoofs and iron-bound wheels was often troublesome.
With the development ofthe automobile the layering of stones
of decreasing size in the system devised by John Loudon
McAdam (1756-1836) and the use ofasphalt, a mixture ofbitumen and aggregates, to ‘metal’ or ‘tar’ road surfaces became
widespread. The material was frequently laid over the existing
road surface, and in conservation projects or to deter motorists
has occasionally been removed to reveal the former setts or

PAUL OLIVER

1.1V.4.c-i Cobblestone

Western New York State is distinctive in its abundance of cobblestone houses, although the tradition moved
with settlers to areas ofthe upper Midwest, especially northern
Michigan. Architectural historians speculate that a source for

keil do Amaral, Fran
et al.,1961

lifton-Taylor, Al
Ireson, A.

Luisset, Christi

Cobbles andse

front of weavel

cottages, Cors Eährain
wiltshire, EI "al

forthe walls
house,

the construction is in Sussex, England, where a similar type of
mortar treatment with emphasized horizontal and vertical
jeints flourished. Pioneer farmers in western N: ew York State
used cobblestones formed ofred sandstonerounded byancient
glaciers to build foundations and walls. The completion ofthe
Erie Canal between Rochester and Buffalo during the 1820s
offered opportunities to expand the use ofcobblestone to whole
buildings. Construction ofthecanal produced tons ofunwanted
cobblestones and, after completion of the canal, the masons
applied their skills to the construction of houses in cobblestones for farmers and tradesmen. The cobblestones were used
in upright and wing forms, gable-ftont houses, four-over-four
‘Georgian’ styles, octagon structures and ‘English’ barns.
Many masons made innovations to thetraditional styles including creating herringbone and angled designs with the stones.
The exterior walls were composed of three vertical layers, with
the stones of the two outside sections nearly the same vertical
size and laid opposite each other. The mason placed stones in
the centre layer, alternating stones across from vertical spaces,
thus spacing joints to add strength. Early cobblestone buildings had irregular-shaped stones of different colours roughly
laid into the wall and defined with horizontal and vertical
joints. Later cobblestone houses had smoother, smaller stones
laid in straight lines formed with a beading tool.

SIMON J.BRONNER

1.1V.4.d Coral

Coral is a basic building material used in the construction of houses on the coastal towns and villages of the
southern Arabian/Persian Gulf, in Bahrain, Doha and Dubai.
Saukin, on the Red Sea Coast, is also famous for its coral stone
houses. Coral ragstone is also used in the Arab/Swahili towns
of the coast of East Africa. Deep footings (1 m [3 ft]) of coral
chips were laid, upon which were blocks of coral rag in red
earth: many buildings had carved coral stone details, for
instance in the historic sites of Gedi and Kilwa. Still standing
and in use are the coral houses ofLamu island.

In the absence of solid, workable building materials such as
stone, rubble and wood, masons tesorted to coral stone which
was in abundance along the shore and water inlets (Khor). Low
tides would provide opportunities for coral cutters to approach
either by footor small boatand harvest the chunks of. coral with
iron bars.

Coral stones were left on the shore for a season or two to be
washed by rainwater to remove the salt. They were then sold by
the pile or boat load in the town. The coral stone was often
salvaged from derelict buildings and re-used in new ones.

Coral consists mainly of caleium carbonate in the form of
calciate, found under the sea-bed as an accumulation ofcoral
polyps and other animals and plants. Coral stone is softer and
easier to work than sandstone and can be carved and sawn by
hand. Its porous, irregular surface gives an excellent key for
mortars and wall rendering.

Coral walls held best together iflaid three blocks thick. This
produced stable walls with staggered building blocks and an
overall width of at least 60 cm (2 ft). The coral was set in gyP-
sum mortar and bonded well together, producing strong walls

Rocks and Stone 1.IV.4

with good insulating qualities. Where smaller, thinner blocks
of coral were used they were set upright at an angle, with the
row above sloping in the opposite direction, Producing a herringbone effect.

Thin coral slabs could also be cut from the reef and these
were generally used for panels between the load-bearing
columns which supported the weight of the building. The
heavy pillars were square or oblong in section and set about
1-1.5 m (3-5 ft) apart. Between them were the windows, doors,
decorative screens, plain panels — perhaps with a false arch at
the top-and windscoop panels which allowed breezes to enter
between a double skin ofcoral slabs.

Foundations up to ım (3 fi) deep and about ı m (3 ft) wide
were laid below the walls, constructed of coral and mortar. In
Dubai the mortar used was sarooj. This was made by mixing
imported red clay with manure and water, drying the paste in
the sun, firing it for ten days, then pounding the baked mixture
to a powder and mixing it again with water. The resulting mixture was stronger than the more widely used gypsum, and
cooler than the cement used today.

Walls were bonded and plastered with a gypsum mortar
called juss. This was made by firing together a mixture of coral
stones and shells, which were then pounded to a fine powder

and mixed with water for plaster, and with sand and water for.
mortar. Gypsum was fired for 12-24 hours in a low. masonıy
kiln, with a perforated top on which the coral rock, shells and
perhaps limestone would be piled. The fire could be run at a
slightly lower temperature than for lime plaster, which was
generally reserved for waterproofing roofs and drainage channels. Some traditionallime kilns are still in use today in Bahrain
and in Oman. DARIUSH ZANDI

1.1V.4.e Coursed stone
Generally courses are layers of stone units of uniform height including the mortar joints. Whether rubble, ashlar or trimmings, stonework classes, dry and mortared, can be
coursed and the stones set in layers, more often than not ofa
similar height with the horizontal joints continuous around the
building.

An important element in courses is the height-width ratio,
that is, the relationship of vertical and horizontal sizes on
the face of the stone, as it determines the visual appearance.

241

## S. 71

FEUER EVA HSPSDESSENERSESTEnEmeznnssenmeernere

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

© Asher Shadmon

The ratio is dictated, especially in vernacular building, by the
local geological conditions and determines distinct regional
characteristics. u

There are many varieties and types ofeourseand the individual
stone units forming the course are not always ofthe same height
or part ofa continuous bed. Random rubble, for example, may
sometimes be brought to courses for coursing-in with quoin
stones.

Courses do not necessarily run horizontally; stone brought
to courses with the longer dimension in a vertical position is
used to give an impression ofadded height and courses can be
curved as, for example, over an arch. The course from which an
arch springs is called a springing course. Polygonally-shaped
stones are used for wavy, sinuous courses a8, for instance, in
Kyoto.

Courses may have an ornamental purpose, adapted to the
locally available shapes and sizes, thickness ofthe stone layers in
the quarry, logistics and other ergonomic factors. They may have
particular names after their position in the structure, the shape
and finish ofthe stone units, or the type of mortar infillings and
so on. The units may be uniformly dressed, roughly shaped, random sized, projecting or receding, flat, angled or curved.

The top of a wall footing is in a sense the lowest course or
base course and may be surmounted by a plinth (also known as
the water-table) which is a projecting course, sloping on top,
placed at or near the ground line.

String courses or belt courses are horizontal courses placed at
intervals on the face ofabuildingand can be used as claddingor
load-bearing elements. A stretching course is composed of
stretchers whereas a heading course consists of, ‚headers. A
bonding course is composed of bond stones. A corbel course

acts as a corbel projecting from the face of a wall to support a
course that projects still further. A cornice is an ornamental
courseand is setator nearthetop ofa wall. The blocking course
is a plain course which surmounts the cornice or the top course
ofawallto actas a counterbalance (and is sometimes applied to
a plain plinth).
The coping may be the finishing top course ofawalland consists of large stones projecting slightly over the wall at both

242

sides. The coping course is used to shelter the mortar in the
interior ofthe wall from weather and to cause water to run off
rapidly. .
Lacing courses are horizontal courses ofdressed stone built
into walls ofrandom rubble to give added stability or strength
and are typically applied in flint walls. A drip course is a projecting course ofstone to keep water from flowing down a wall
and prevent discolouration or damage. Currently, courses of
brick-sized stone have become fashionable in rural and urban
India alike because of the widespread presence of easily quarried and squared stone slabs. ASHER SHADMON

Fieldstone
The term “fieldstone’ is arbitrarily applied to any
loose stones, whether natural, shaped, worked or unworked; in
fact any mix which is suitable or available. Low-cost shelters or
walls are invariably built with stone available locally. Stone collection requires no special skill or equipment, and although
splitting can be helpful, broken or whole stones can be used.
The difference between astone walland a pile ofstones can be
seen in thelaying process. Besides pent-up energy, which enables
it to be broken with minimum effort, another important property of stone is its high relative density, which together with its
rigidityallows itto beusedto make strong, stablewalls. The eagential elements for fieldstone construction include layout, footings,
height-width relationships, tying-in, flats fortop stones, rectangulars for ends and corners, and longs for tying-in (binders).

There are many types ofstone source available for vernacular
constructions. Stones can be prised out with a bar from layers
on an inclined slope or at a steep angle; loose stones thrown
into the gap thereby created prevent the stone from falling
back. The correct positioning of a crowbar or jack can sometimes bring tons ofstone down a cliff.

Stone handling practices have been developed throughout
the centuries to ensure safety, the prevention of tiredness or
double-handling, the preservation of material and, last butnot
least, good housekeeping. Stones, especially those that are t00
heavy, need not be carried. The weight of the stone should
assistrather than counter the move. The stone can be tipped up
on its end or side, straddled or rolled between legs. When lifting, only the legs and arms need to be used, keeping the back
straight and the stone close to the body. The transportation of
stone along river-sides or shores requires less effort as it
weighs only halfas much when moved underwater. .

For the regular moving oflarger or cyclopean stones, weighing over 100 kg (200 Ib), a hoist is indispensable. A block and
tackle for heavier weights requires shear legs and gantries for

1.MAf

support.

Practices in laying a wall using fieldstones include the pioportioning of stone sizes to the dimensions ofthe wall during
sorting. Low walls require small and narrow stones, with larger
stones used for higher walls. The best flat face of the narıow
dimension of each stone faces outwards. Stones are dropped
into place, rather than put down, and find their own level.

The stones are bedded solidly on the stones below. Irregularities are clipped off a stone, rather than shimming or wedging
itwith small rocks, as small stones used in the outer faces may

Coursed Sandston,
rondavel, near ag,

u,

Lesotho, Southern
‚Africa.

See also

1.v.1.f-Jointingang
fixing: stone

1.V.1.h Laying

=

work loose in time. The thickest and heaviest rocks are placed
in the lower courses to avoid unnecessary lifting. Craftsmen
take care that joints between stones do not extend from course
to course forminga run, which would cause the wall to fold, as
dynamic stresses concentrate atthe weakest point. Every 2-3 m
(6.5-10 ft) a long tie-stone is present to tie one face to another.
Rectangular stones ofeven thickness, the longer the better, are
saved for ends and corners, especially outside edges.

There is no limitation on the lithology of stones used in dry
walling except their quality and this is best judged by their
behaviour in local constructions and other applications. Traditions in construction using locally obtained stone range ftom
the limestone settlements around Kingston, Ontario, the granite ambience of Aberdeen and the grey sandstone around Edinburgh in Scotland, the sandstone of New South Wales, the red
and cream limestones ofthe Judaean Hills, the red sandstones
in Rajasthan, India, and in the Glasgow region ofScotland, and
the coral stone of Zanzibar and Santo Domingo. All these and
many other places had their future fabric determined by dıy
stone walls, sometimes to be mortared if it was considered

essential to do so. ASHER SHADMON

1.1V.4g Flint

Flint is composed of about 98 per cent minutely
cıystalline pure silica. Itis intensely hard and almostindestructible, yet in the hands ofan experienced knapper can be easily
cleft along the natural lines of fracture. By tapping a flint the
knapper is able to judge from the sound the best way to strike
through the weakest section ofthe nodule.

The purest and most sought after nodular, or floorstone,
flints are obtained from the upper beds ofthe Great Chalk Belt
in southeast England. This chalk formation stretches in broad
bands eastwards from Dorset to eastern Kent and northeast via
Hampshire to Norfolk, east Lincolnshire and Humberside.
Abundant supplies were available and flint buildings in these
areas, particularly in Sussex and Norfolk, are said to be more
numerous than anywhere else in the world.

Flint was used for all types of buildings in areas where the
material could be easily obtained. The pattern of vernacular
building stemmed from the Roman, Saxon and Norman
influences, and often their older materials were re-used. The

walls can be approximately dated by their coursing, mortar and
knapping.

Rocks and Stone 1.1V.4

Flint on the face of, and in the body of, aRoman wall is characteristically a melange of fractured and unfractured nodules
picked up from the ground, together with beach pebbles, set
haphazardly in a hard mortar. Saxon and Norman walls were
roughly coursed with a predominance of mortar in relation to
the rubble content which contained mainly unfractured flints.
Flintwas much used in the Norman period for rubble cores and
facing. Herringbone work in flint, stone and re-used Roman
brick was seen in the late ııth century. Rough coursing continued into the early ızth century but flints became tightly packed
on the surface with less mortar visible. After 1250 flints were
laid in more regular courses. Walls faced entirely with split
flints were rare before the 12gos.
14th-century walls may be distinguished from earlier work by
the neater coursing. Knapped work, with flints roughly
squared, but ofirregular sizes, was common in superior buildings from the beginning of the century. Flushwork (knapped
flints set into dressed stone to form decorative panels) dates
from the 13208. Chequered patterns ofstone and flintappeared
during the late 14th century. Flint was carefully selected,
dressed and graded in the sth century. The chequered patterns
became smaller and finer in superior buildings but remained
erude in humble buildings. Flush tracery reached its zenith in
the second halfofthe century, continued until the middle ofthe
16th century and had a brief revival in the ıgth century. Vernacular buildings, in stone areas, were of flint, stone or rubble
throughout the Tudor and Elizabethan periods. Panels of fint
and mortar as infills to timber-framed buildings date mainly
from the 17th century. Large flint galleting, for filling mortar
joints, is common in the 18th century; the gallets (fragments of

FENIO Med O

## S. 72

1.V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
Aintor small stones) were decorativeand helped to consolidate
the mortar. Early galleting was designed to throw rainwater
clear ofthe wall surface. The Regency period regarded flintasa
fashionable material and it was used in conjunction with brick,
chalkaand stone. ADELA WRIGHT
1..4h Granite
An igneous, crystalline rock, granite is one of the
hardest natural materials used in traditional building. It cold
prises quartz, mica and feldspar containing silicate of: alumina
and potash. Varyingin use from silver to a dark grey, sometimes
with a red or brown tinge, granite has a coarse texture. Nevertheless, it is very strong and blocks can sustain considerable
spans especiallywhen placed on edge. Itis also virtually impervious to water, permitting its use for foundations as well as for
walls, resisting as it does the creeping action ofdamp. u
The principal problem for the builder is that granite ia
diffieultto work and heavy to handle. However, a characteristic
of granite is that it cleaves in blocks. This was particularly
advantageous for both the quarryman and the builder. A ine
of iron spikes could be driven into a granite rock at approximately 25 cm (10 in) intervals until itsplit. The granular texture
damaged blades and only the hardest metal tools could be
used on it. Deeply curved crosscut saws with centimetre-sized
teeth were used to cut thinner slabs, the saw sometimes
mounted in a frame with supports that ensured greater accuracy. The use ofabrasive stones and water to mould forms, and
the wearing effect of rope drawn across the block were also
employed to work granite. It could be polished, laboriously,
with siliceous sand or other abrasive and water. Fine workmanship and decorative detail were not achievable, but clear forms
and simple details were retained by the durable material over
centuries.

Often found in regions which support coniferous forests, such
as Maine in the northeast United States, granite is sometimes
used for the plinth or cellar structure ofa log or other timber construction. But it is seen at its best when used exclusively. The
longhouses of Dartmoor, the farmhouses of Brittany or the
villages of Beira Alta are without compare in their use of dlosefitted massive blocks. In thesilently assertive, immutable granite
houses ofthese and other regions the deep courses, monbolithic
columns, single-slab door-jambs and lintels, steps and hearthstones, combine in harmonious and timeless unity.

244

Granite has been used extensively for the paving of streets
with cobbles and setts and, with the development ofthe auto-

mobile, for kerbstones or protective edging of roadside pavements, PAUL OLIVER
1.1V.4i Mortar

Although in construction different types of mortar
have been and are used with stone, it is important that the mortar and stone are well harmonized.

A harmonized mortar consists ofa mixture offine aggregate,
water and hydraulic cement orlime (or a combination ofboth),
that hardens after application and is used for jointing. The use
ofan unstabilized mud-mortar causes the collapse ofwalls during the rainy season and is commonly happening in Nepal. The
addition oflime or cement stabilizes the mud-mortar. A proper
mortar is weather-resistant, and has adequate bond and compressive strength. A major advantage of mortar is that with its
use walls have to be less massive as it acts as bonding. Mortar
seals the joints between stones, enables the use of different and
irregular shapes and sizes, and facilitates the use of rendering.

An accurately proportioned mix of fine aggregate and lime,
thecementbeingadded on site immediately before application,
is termed ‘gauged mortar’. When liquid it can be hand-poured
or gravity- or pressure-fed into a wall or foundation to fill voids
and cavities; itis then called grout. Mortar for stonework is less
wet than mixtures used for other masonıy materials so as to
prevent dribbles and rime on the finished wall.

Whether to construct a dry wall or to use mortar and, if so,
how much, depends on many factors, including the kind of
stone available, the type and size of the structure required and
climatic conditions.

Stone is usually stronger and more durable than mortar. Well
tied and stacked stonework without mortar is more stable, if
not more attractive, than a wall held together with plenty of
mortar. Hard mortars are more vulnerable to stress inthewalls.

"The rougher the beds andjoints, the better themortarshould
be. A prineipal function ofthe mortar is to equalize pressure,
and the more nearly the stones are dressed to closely fitting surfaces, the less importantthe quality ofthe mortar.

There are various types of pointed mortar joints including:
flush joint; concave, keyed orcorejoint; struck joints; weathered

(struck) joint; square-sunk or rustic joint; raked joint; V-shaped
joint; beaded joint; raised joint; indented raised joint; and
feather-edge joint (irregular pointing) which may be indented.

The mortar is often kept back from the face of the wall for
about io mm (0.4in), orthejoints may be flushed full ofmortar
and immediately raked out, and pointed later. The pointing
mortar often contains non-staining cement and white sand. A
downwards and outwards type of pointing prevents water collecting in the joints.

One purpose of indenting, where large expanses of mortar
are present, is to keep the water from getting between the stone
and the mortar. Raised and feather-edge pointing tends to
cover rather than highlight the stonework. Feather-edging of
dense cement on a softer stone gradually ereates an open upper
edge which collects rainwater permitting it to penetrate into
the building.

See also forq,y,
2.1.3.8 Devonjan
2.11.3,f Edinburgh,
tenements
2.11.5.C Brittany,

References for
Keil do Amaral,
et al.,1961

Shepherd, Wal ı
Wright, Adela,

Lime production of vernacular building is more often than
nota locally based activity adapted to the limestone source and
method of burning. Pozzolanic materials containing reactive
silicates produce a hydraulic set in lime mortar. They may
include brick dustand crushed stone, or where the colour ofthe
mortar requires matching, white cement. ASHER SHADMON
1.1V.4.] Rock shelter
Rock shelters are caverns which do not provide

complete protection from the weather. Shelter is supplemented
with constructions which may be permanent or temporary: permanent structures typically using masonry ofrubble or adobe,
and temporary ones employing timber, hides and thatch. Some
rock shelters are large, and may have many structures within
them, for example, the Kurdish rock shelter at Shanidar in
northern Iraq (Solecki).

Natural cavities are formed in rock by underground watercourses in limestone, forming solution cavities; bytheaction of
waves ot fast-flowing rivers eroding softer strata within formations oflimestone, sandstone or soft volcanic deposits such as
basalt; or, thirdly, as laval tubes created during the formation of
igneous rocks by volcanic action. Natural rock shelters were
only one alternative among many for Early Man to find protection against the elements — the alternatives including simple
wind-breaks which could be constructed from branches,
leaves, bone and hide. However, rock shelters have provided a
favourable environment for the preservation of prehistoric
remains and thus include sites from which much ofour knowledge of early culture is derived.

Atthe cave site ofLe Lazaret in southwestern France (dated to
150.000 Bc), there is evidence that Homo neanderthaliswas already
beginning to use stoneimplemenis, andto modify the cave environment he found. Rubble walls gave protection from prevailing winds directed towards the mouth ofthe cave, and divided
the living space into zones which may well have been used for
different functions. Post holes indicate the presence ofa timber
framework to support wattling or hide curtains which further
divided the space. Present-day cultures which employ rock shelters include the Tarahumara of Chihuahua province in northwestern Mexico. There are also other peoples who still use rock
shelters, for instance the hunter-gatherer Hadza of Tanzania
who live in them in bad weather. HELEN MULLIGAN

1.1V.4.k Rubble
Acommon form of stone walling is made from rubble, or irregular pieces of stone of approximately equal size.
Rubble may be gathered from scree and talus, from heavily
eroded rock sites, from the detritus left after quarrying, from
river-beds, or as fieldstone. As in the case of dry stone walling,
it may be necessary to lay itas found in the rock bed, but ifitis
assembled without being squared off it has to be interlocked
with mortar joints. This method of ‘random rubble’ construction is economical on labour and, except for the mortar, on
materials. It is consequently common in vernacular building
and is evident in many parts ofthe world, but especially where
hard rocks which are difficult to work are found.

Rubble walling may be levelled off with roughly squared

Rocks and Stone 1.V.4
courses raised at intervals of 0.5 m (1.5 ft) or so, ensuring a
measure of stability, new starting levels, and some aesthetic
regularity. Regular ‘coursed rubble’ building is possible where
the rock cleaves in blocks along bedding planes, or where ithas
been roughly squared off by striking or cutting.

Rubblestone is also used for filling thick stone walls which
can be faced with more formal dressed ashlar masonry and
lined wıth internal plaster or dressed stone. This type of wall
can provide good thermal insulation and is often found in
mountain areas and extreme climates. Mortar or some other
bonding agent is usual to take up irregularities and to ensure
weather tightness. MILES DANBY

1.1V.4.| Slab

The term ‘slab’ was originally applied to a massive
rectangular piece of stone. Itis now applied to any rectangular
units larger than tile size. Stone slabs, or slab material, are
some ofthe most frequently used stone materials in vernacular
construction, especially if regular and parallel sided. It is an
instant structural building material and often requires only
trimming to size on four sides before placement.

Besides its use for walling when cutto size, in practical usea
slab can be the hearth ofa fireplace, a kitchen counter, the floor
or partition ofa latrine, paving slabs, wall panels, units for curtain walls (when they are known as facing slabs) and so on.

Slabs may occur, as such, in outcrops or layers in thin bedded
deposits whether igneous, metamorphic or sedimentary. Large
slab deposits may dominate the rural and urban nature and
character ofwhole regions.

Slabs can be shaped by dressing, slicing with a saw and then
smoothed with chisels or by rubbing with abrasive or harder
stones and eventually given a polished finish. Marble tabletops
are regularly crafted by hand on the island of Romblon in the
Philippines and used as kitchen counters. They are often
matched with hand-carved marble kitchen sinks and washbasins, in widespread use in Greece, Turkey and the eastern
Mediterranean islands.

In eastern Portugal thick granite slabs constitute whole
facades in the larger buildings and form important components in houses and cottages.

Flooring, stairways, steps and paving consume more slab
materials than any other type of construction. When used as
flooring for latrines the slabs have a hole in them; they are often
made ofslate as, for example, in the foothills ofthe Himalayas.

ASHER SHADMON

245

## S. 73

© Paulollver 1995

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

1.1V.4.m Slate

Slate is metamorphic igneous or sedimentary rock
formed by fine clay or shale deposited in water and subjected to
great vertical pressure. Cemented by carbonates or kaolin and
transformed by subsequent lateral pressure and intense heat,
slate formed with naturally occurring cleavage planes, created
by the mineral particles of silica and alumina being forced in
the process of transformation to lie perpendicular to the forces
exerted. Itis this property which makes slate an excellent building material. A skilled craftsman can cleave the slates into layers ofa centimetre or even less, depending upon its quality, by
striking the edge with hammer and chisel. Its colour ranges
from light green to a blue-grey or even to purple, which adds to
its aesthetic appeal.

Slate has been used for a variety of purposes, thick slabs
being laid as floors or dressed and polished as gravestones. Its
impermeability has made it especially suitable for lining water
tanks, or providing slabs for dairies and for food preparation.
Slate was likewise used for salting troughs, cheese-presses and
other purposes where a flat, virtually grainless surface was
desired and the weight ofthick slabs was nota problem.

In the British Isles slate is found frequently in areas where
granite is also present. North Wales, Argylishire in Scotland,
Connaught in Ireland, and Cumbria and the Devonian peninsula in England are among the principal quarry sites, with the
immense quarry at Delabole in Cornwall providing slate for
building since medieval times. In France, Limousin and
Guyenne have notable quarries; local traditions in the use ofthe

material for roof cladding, or as water repellents, are common
wherever the material occurs.

Thin slates are used extensively in the Asir uplands of Saudi
Arabia, the external surface ofthe tower houses and the houses
of prosperous farmers being banded in a dozen or more ‘shelf’
layers parallel to the ground. Projecting up to 25 cm (10 in) the
sometimes slightly inclined slates actas drip courses, throwing
the rainwater away from the mud walls. Less spectacularly,
slate has been used in the British Isles for drip courses on cob
buildings, especially around chimneys where thatched roofs
would be vulnerable to rain penetration. Its imperviousness to
water also makes slate an effective material for damp-proof
courses.

Itis, however, as a roofing material that slate has been most
extensively used. Thin sheets ofslate graded and laid provided
such an effective water-shield and protection against fire
sparks and cinders which could ignite thatched roofs that it
was sufficiently in demand for slates to be taxed in the 18308.
When the tax law was repealed slate was used to such an extent
that virtually all London was slate-roofed by the beginning of
the 2oth century. The use ofslate for roofingand hanging is discussed under 1.v.2.0. PAUL OLIVER
1.1V.4.n Tufa/tuff
One of the most convenient forms of rock for
domestic builders is tufa, otherwise known as tuff. Technically,
distinctions may be made between these and related materials
though in general usage the terms are often interchangeable.
Tufa and tufo (Italian), tuffe and tuf (French) and toph or tophe
(also English, but now obsolete) all derive from the Latin
tophus, used generically to refer to relatively soft and porous
stone types. It may be noted that a derivation from the Arabic
tauf has also been posited. Tuffs may be arenaceous with a
sandy, granular composition, or they may be calcareous, containing lime or limestone, such as may be deposited along calcareous springs. But the term is often applied with particular
reference to volcanic strata, particularly those deposited and
in layers ofvolcanic ash, which are vesicular, with minute cavities formed by bubbles of vapour or gas expelled at the time of
eruption.

Some geologists differentiate between tuff, which is loose
and porous rock, and tufa, which is considered to be harder and
in consolidated layers, identified by the Italian terrazzo, or terrace. As this term is used in English to identifya polished flooring of marble and stone chips set in cement, opportunities for
confusion remain. As described by Ozkan and Onur, whogivea
suceinct summary of its properties, ‘tuffa is basically unhardened calcium carbonate. Quite soft for carving, the surfaces
exposed get hardened as the product of the chemical reaction
with the carbon dioxide of the air, i.e. (Ca(OH), + CO, -
CaCO, + H,O) - apparently calcium hydroxide is the basic
composition ofthe soft tuffa.’ Volcanic dust, wind-driven from
a volcano, and settled in a thick layer develops ‘bonds among
the particles of volcanic dust, due to compression of its own
weightand precipitation, ie [CaO +H,-Ca(OH) pa

The subject of this description, the ‘Iunar’ landscape of
Cappadocia with tufa pinnacles honeycombed with excavated

See also

1.V.2.n Slateh;
roofing

2.1V.1.b Asir

dwellings, is very different from the plains of Apulia where the
trulli of Alberobello and the neighbouring farm settlements are

androck shelters were in use widely in Europe in the early partof
the zoth century. As Baring-Gould recorded, there were innu-

merable rock dwellings in volcanic breccia in the Puy-de-Döme

Rocks and Stone

region, in the Haute-Loire, Cevennes and Auvergne, some being
of several storeys. A broad belt of tufa and tuff may be traced
across Europe, the Mediterranean and the Middle East, in many
parts ofwhich the Ppropensity ofthessoftand easilyworkedrock
to harden with exposure has been discovered and exploited in
building by countless craftsmen over many centuries.

PAUL OLIVER

247

1.104

## S. 74

1.M5 Timbers

Man has a natural affinity for timber, for it is an

organic material unlike most other building materials. The texture, warmth of colour, ease of workability and high strength:
to-weightratio (both in compression and tension) have made it
a most important material for vernacular construction. Yet,
these very strengths of timber are also its weakness. Being
organically formed, the variability of the wood which accrues
from the natural process of growth gives inherent defects (such
as knots and splits), lack of uniformity of produet as well as
variability in strength and length oftimbers. Also, distinct limitations are found in the durability ofthe material through the
effects of agents ofdestruction such as insect and fungi attack,
weathering and fire. In most cases, vernacular builders address
these weaknesses through careful selection and arrangement
ofthe elements. For example, kitchens are separated from the
main building to minimize risk and damage by fire, eaves overhang walls in tropical regions to protect the timber from sun
and rain, and only highly durable timbers are used in exposed
locations such as the ground-level connection.

The selection of timber materials for building is, therefore,
an important issue. Choice is dictated by arange of factors and
is dependent on availability and purpose. Thus, yernacular
builders tend to use what is available and select within the
range ofwood species in the forest type ofthe region.

Iypes offorests, wood and timber j .
There are three main types of forest vegetation which give a
wide range ofwood qualities. The conifers (gymnosperms), or
evergreens, are called softwoods. They are found in temperate
and cold climates as well as in semi-arid tropical zones. The
second type, the monocots (monocotyledons), with one seed,
are the palms and bamboos; technically, these are grasses.
Bamboo is well known for its use in construction, paper and
food products.

The broadleaf forests (angiosperms), the third type, are the
hardwoods, and occur in three climatic groups: temperate —
oaks, beeches, ash, birch, alders; subtropical, or Mediterranean
climate — evergreen oaks, madrones; and tropical — evergreen
broadleaf, teak (Tectona grandis), mahogany (Swietenia macrophylla) and balsa (Ochroma pyramidale). .

Conifer forests developed approximately 375 million years
ago, while broadleaf forests appeared only 120 million years
ago. Following the last glacial period, about half of the land
surface ofthe earth was covered with forests. Virtually all elrmatic zones except the Arctic have some usable vegetation
forms; wetlands, beaches, desert, tundra, prairie and savanna,
however, are not forested, and therefore wood is not a major

248

construction material in these areas. Forest location is tied to
rainfall and water supply, which in turn relate to soil type and
ography.
ee an are use in a variety of ways for dwellings. Tree
trunks are used for ladders and bridges or, when hollowed,
become irrigation and water conduits or boats. They may also
be used for log cabins or as structural members. When
processed, trees become boards, shakes or shingles for sheathing and roofing. Branches are used as secondary, and sometimes as primary, structural members. They are also used as
wall sheathing with vertically placed sticks, twigs or split bamboo, the ends ofwhich may be anchored in the earth or tied wo a
base sill; these walls can breathe and allow air passage. Iftwigs
are used, theymay be covered with mud or plaster. Forked poles
are used structurally with beams laid across them; a framework
is developed which can be sheathed with cloth, hide, masonry,
grasses or leaves. Combination sheathing may be used, such as
adobe block used with straw or twigs, wattle and daub, or plasterand lath.

Many other parts offorest vegetation are used as well. Teäves
are used in many ways, usuallyas roofing or woven mats. Vines,
tree roots and bark are used for ropes and lashing fibres, as
seen in Central America. Forest products and wood are also
important for their aesthetic and landscape qualities. Symbolic
elements integral to the dwelling as well as discrete artefacts
like furniture, storage containers, baskets, benches and grinding tables are made of wood. Trees are used for shade and
evapotranspiration to direct or deflect breezes and winds, to
stabilize soils and slopes, and as snow fences and other forms
ofprotection.

A critical aspect of forest cover is that with proper stewardship a sustainable lumber supply is maintained, allowingwood
to survive as a renewable resource. However, present rates of
deforestation far exceed successful reafforestation efforts; this

EINST

Taos, New.

varies worldwide, but currently the mostcritical rate ofloss is in
tropical rain forests where indigenous people are losing their
settlements and entire tribal traditions. In addition there is a
global loss ofresources.

Traditional methods of timber extraction involved smallscale selective logging, where only the mature, desirable trees
were taken. Increasingly this practice has given way to clear
cutting ofwhole areas of forest using mechanized means. This
has resulted in both the demise ofmany indigenous forests and
a shortage oftimber for traditional construction.

The alternative to using native forest timber is to create a
man-made forest. This is normally referred to as ‘plantation
timber’. There are specific methods of providing plantation
timber for vernacular buildin g which involve careful nurturing
of trees. These methods are coppicing and pollarding. Cop-
Pices are small, self-contained woodlands which, in England
for instance, are formed of‘ single or mixed species of‘ oak, ash,
hazel and chestnut. The forests are managed to ensure thatthe
trunks grow straight and true, The process used relies on regular cutting and clearing oftrees. In this way only selected trees
reach maturity. Traditionally, three workers are used to carry
out the process, each having a specialized task. One works to
ensure that the woodland is cleared to the correct spacing of
trees by cutting out surplus growth. The next sorts, cuts, splits

and wires the timber into Piles, and the final worker is tesponsible for the organization ofthe Piles for transportation.
Pollarding differs from eoppicing in that, although a small
woodland is similarly planted, rather than thinning the trees
during the growth cycle by taking out trunks, as in coppicing,
pollarding relies on the Pruning of branches to give stronger
and straighter trunks. The trees grown will thus form useful
poles for traditional construction. Plantations ofsuch trees can
be found close to the dwellings of some peoples; for example,
in Kütahya, western Anatolia, larches are grown on plots adjacentto houses specifically for construction Purposes.
Increasingly, though, to meet the demands ofindustrial production, reafforestation Programmes have been developed to
meet ‘sustained’ yields of the forests. To increase these yields
species of timber that are of a lower grade and faster to grow
have been planted in Preference to native species. The infuence
of this practice on vernacular construction has been dramatic
with either the extinction ofthe species of wood for construction or the replacement of species with inferior species.
Even so, the properties of wood which make it an excellent
building material are density and specific gravity (the ratio of
the wood’s density to water). Density is important relative to
overall wood strength, with higher density indicating greater
strength. The moisture content is regulated by the shrink:swell
ratio and other mechanical Properties. Wood can absorb high
amounts ofwaterand mosttypes float. Itis also flexible and can
bend; when laminated it is used for boats, ceilings, walls and
decorative applications. Using sheets ofwood with Proper connections, structures resist seismic forces. Woods such as
African cedar resist rot and are also insect resistant. Wood
inspires many forms of Joint and allows light and air between
thin members. These favourable qualities are based on the cellular structure and the growth pattern ofwood.

Timbers 1.1.5

It is the fibrous material called ‘lignin’ that gives the tree its
structural strength. Trees grow from the inside Out; the centre
part is called the ‘heartwood’ and appears to provide the structural support for the tree. Its cells become blocked and contribute to the grain and colour ofthewood. The outer partis the
growth area ofthe tree and contains the sapwood, which is the
area most susceptible to insect attack,

Wood is converted into timber for construction purposes
and classified as hardwood or softwood. Softwoods are distinet
from hardwoods byvirtue oftheir cell structure. Hardwood has
large pores or vessels, softwood does not. More specific
classification and naming of wood and trees is complicated by
the wide range of species and how these are classified. Most
trees have a Latin botanical name and a common name. Thus
‘white cedar’ is called Melia azedarach. Even so, some anomalies
still occur. Hardwoods are not necessarily hard, for instance
balsa is classified as a hardwood yetis used as lightweight construction material, and yew is a softwood although nearly as
hard as oak and noted for its resistance,

Such terms are convenient for classification rather than as
indicators of application and performance. What is important
is thatthere is a wide variety ofspecies oftimber for vernacular
building and the correct type ofwood can be precisely selected
for its purpose. In most vernacular buildings this is achieved
and itis this factor that contributes to their longevity, and their
rich visual and construction qualities.

Sources oftimber

The commercialization and industrialization oftimber production means that many species are now available worldwide.
2500 million cu m oftimber are used by man each year; 54 per
cent is used in the country of origin, most of which is used as
fuel within a few miles of‘ felling. Timber remains significantly
aregional and local material.

Interestingly, though, with the demise of many species, particularly highly durable timbers, man-made methods of
improving durability through chemical Preservative treatment
(tanalization) are often utilized. The impact ofthis technology
is rarely considered. Recently in Papua New Guinea the use of

249

## S. 75

© Simon Bronner

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

preservatives has been considered for improving the longevity

oftraditional buildings. These buildings have a normal lifecy-

cle ofır years butitis hoped to increase this cycle and therefore

save timber. Nevertheless, this raises the question as to

whether the use of modern technology is appropriate in such a
context. Rebuilding every ıı years serves a cultural as well as a
skill development function which will be deferred, or may be

lost, if preservatives are used. There are many ofthese kinds of
question that underlie the development and availability oftimber for vernacular construction. Traditional methods of the
sustainable generation and use oftimber materials, as well as
the methods of conversion, are as relevant today as in the past.

It would be wise to learn from these traditions as a means of
informing the future, so that the evolution oftraditional timber
construction can progress in a sensible and natural progression.

The following entries focus in particular on the conversion
methods that have evolved over time. Two distinct processes
can be identified: the primary conversion process which
involves the felling, seasoning and cutting of trees to form
materials, and the secondary conversion process, or the transformation of materials to form shelter. The primary conversion
process is the subject of this category. For secondary conversion processes see 1.v.ı Assembly and 1.v.2 Cladding.

RICHARD HYDEand DONNA LUCKEY

Arboreal shelter

While the first habitations of mankind have long
been thought to have been caves, the first to have been constructed were believed to have been of interlocking branches.
LAbbe Laugier described man’s search for shelter: ‘some
branches broken offin the forest are material to his purposes.
He chooses four of the strongest and raises them perpendicularly to the ground, to form a square. On these four he supports
four others laid across them.’ Butthe drawing which accompanied the account showed these latter branches laid across the
forks oftrees still rooted in the ground (Laugier; Rykwert).

In the ıgth century ‘climbing down from the trees’ became a
racial stereotype of post-Darwinian evolutionary concepts, representing the transition of man from ape to sentient Kian
being. Reports of arboreal peoples appeared to support belief
in European racial superiority, yet conversely, tree-dwelling
became, in Victorian times, a symbol of the natural life. Tree

1.1V.5.a

250

houses were built to amuse the nobility, while children
delighted in playing in tree houses and have continued to do so

Stovewood useglj, n
sonstructionogg

Wisconsin hou:
States c.1884,
(See alsofig, Viah

to the present. j .

Ifarboreal shelter persists in popular imagery, its existence

in the 2oth century is relatively rare. There are few reports of
such houses among African peoples: occasional references to
trees being used as watch-towers, or for escape in times of
flood or under the threat ofattacking animals, do notindicate a
tendency to tree-dwelling. Yet there are other regions where
tree houses are built habitually, with their greatest concentrations in New Guinea and the Philippines. The Kati ofthe Mamberamo river region in Irian Jaya built pole frame houses with
slatted floors, secured where possible to widely spreading
branches some 15 m (50 ft) or more above the ground. These
dobo houses were built to protect unmarried women from
predatory tribesmen from other villages. Rocks were kept on
the floors ofthe houses with which to pelt any invaders while
the ladders oflong poles, stepped with lashed branches which
provided access, could be disengaged from the tree if necessary. Near the Markham river in the east of the island the Kai
also built tree houses, while substantial dwellings were raised
in the forests of the Mount Clarence region, with shallowpitched roofs and with independent platforms approached by
inclined ladders with handrails.

Such tree houses are similar to the pile dwellings of other
cultures. This was particularlytrue ofthe houses ofthe Manubo
of southern Mindanao, Philippines, which were made with a
rectangular frame, hipped roofand panelled walls. The floots
were built with strong joists to form a platform. A large tree
with many thick branches was lopped approximately 7.5 m (25
ft) from the ground, and the entire house constructed on the
stumps. The Mandaya of the Davao Gulf region of southeast
Mindanao construct two types ofarboreal structure, one ‘simply resting on the limbs of trees and conforming in size and

see also for {Vs
1.111.2.d Forest‘

Bates, M., 196
Bootle, K.R.,

JENIIO (Meg 1109 "SOGL YPUM we

shape to the nature of the surrounding branches’. The other,
more preyalent and better constructed, is a house built on the
stump of a large tree which has been cut off some 4.5-6 m
(15-20 ft) above ground. A tree with buttress roots is chosen
where possible and a framework is constructed on the top of
the stump which is further supported by slender poles; these
may rise to form the corner and intermediate posts of the
house. Lashed to the poles with rattan is a floor framework,
overlaid with beaten bark, and the supporting poles also serve
to secure a side wall frame to which woven nipa palm panels are
attached. A kingpost system supports the roof-ridge, from
which pole rafters are laid so as to extend over the walls leaving
a ventilation space; the roofis thatched with nipa palm. A long
ladder with lashed crosspieces and a handrail is placed atone
corner to give access to the house. Capable of‘ withstanding violentstorms, itstillshudders in the wind, and is secured to adjacent trees with rattan lines (Dacanay).

During the search for the reportedly cave-dwelling Tasaday
ofsouthern Mindanao, a party ofT’Boli tribesmen rapidly built
a treetop landing platform some 23 m (75 ft) above ground, on
to which nervous explorers were induced to deplane from a
helicopter. The use of the cluster of forks in the tree, the
efficiency of the landing platform of saplings and bamboo
poles placed over the supporting framework indicated a culture
that was not unused to building such structures. The relative
proximity of the T’Boli, Manobo and Mandaya peoples suggests that the reasons for tree house building are as much cultural as environmental.

The question remains whether arboreal shelter bears any
relation to the nest-building behaviour ofthe higher primates,
notably gorillas and chimpanzees, which has been the subject

Timbers 1.145

ofextensive research (Egenter). Apart ftom the lack ofeyidence
of shared environments with tree-house builders, the nests of
apes are constructed by enfolding branches inwards and interweaving them, a technique which human builders seem notto

employ. Furthermore, apes do not roof their structures, so
there is little likelihood that theyrepresentaprimordial stagein

the development ofarchitecture. By far the majority ofarboreal

dwellings are terrestrial houses raised in the air, their origins

more earthbound than emanating from a life in the treetops.

PAUL OLIVER

1.1V5.b Bark

Bark is the external sheath ofa tree which Protects
the pholem, or inner bark, which carries nutrients, and the
cambium, or single-cell layer, which encases thewood stem. In
periods ofgrowth the cambium cells divide to create new wood
cells and new pholem cells. Removal ofor encircling damage to
the bark will expose the cambium and kill the tree,

The properties of bark are noted more for their medicinal
purposes than for their use as components in vernacular buildings. An exception to this rule is the use ofthe bark ofcork oak,
eucalyptus and birch.

The cork oakis an evergreen oaktreewhich grows inwooded
and mountainous distriets of Spain and Portugal and other
western Mediterranean areas. The bark is stripped when the
tree is twenty years old. The stripping is achieved by a horizontal cut made through the bark at the base of the trunk and
another just below the first branches. Several vertical cuts are
also made and the bark is eased offthetree usinganaxe. Careis
taken not to damage the living bark. This stripping process,
rather than weakening the tree, seems to be beneficial and later
stripping at nine-yearly intervals often Pproduces higher quality
bark. The traditional use for cork has taken advantage of its

soft, high-insulation qualities; in traditional buildings the
material is used for flooring and as an insulating layer for cold
rooms.

Other forms of bark have been used more extensively as

building components. The bark from a species of eucalyptus,
such as the red river gum, Eucalyptus rostrata, is used in the construction of Australian Aboriginal shelters. The bark is stripped
from trees in a similar way to that of cork but in this case the
process is known to kill the trees. This does not involve the
destruction of forests as the red river gum trees are usually
sparsely spread through the forest. Sticks are prised under the
bark to bring it clear ofthe living tissue; the bark is then hung
up and cleaned using an adze. The size of a sheet ofthe bark
gained could be up to 3 m (to ft) long byım (3 ft) wide, providinga tough, continuous ‘skin’; this could then be placed overa
timber framework to form the shelter. It is interesting to note
that the structure ofthese buildings relies on the surface action
of the bark skin to give strength and stability to the frame.
When attached to the frame the thin layer braces all the connections. This quality is enhanced in some Aboriginal shelters
which take further advantage ofthe veneer quality ofthe bark,
using the pliability of the bark to form curved shapes and its
good insulation quality to resist sun and heat. This latter quality makes itan ideal material for roofing.

251

## S. 76

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

O Richard Hyde

Other types of bark, such as that from the broadleaf paper
bark (Melaleuca quinquenervia) can also be used, but only in
smaller-sized pieces similar to tiles. This type of material has a
loose, fibrous texture and is recognized as being resistant to
mosquitoes.

Settlers in Australia were quick to realize the potential ofthese
materials and many of the early settlement shelters followed a
similar use ofthem. Bark was used for walls, roofs and chimneys
(lined with stone). The bark skin was held in place by split logs
which formed external rails which could be tied to an inner frame.
The inner surface ofthe bark shelters often consisted of newspaper pasted onto the bark, using flour and water as an adhesive.

The use of large bark sheets in these early Aboriginal shelters
and the settler’s huts has some parallel development in the
northern hemisphere. Scandinavian and other European Nerzatular buildings used birchbark as a roof cladding material, with

turfforadded protection. In North Americathe AlgonquianIndi- _

ans used large birchbark sheets as roof cladding for their wigwams. The birchbark sheets were overlapped like shingles and
skilfully sewn together using splints ofash. The Winnebago and
Ojibwa tribes used bark in two oftheir building types, the round
and long lodges. These use ironwood to form a framework, with
basswood bark used for the bark connections and cedar bark is
for the cladding.

A similar method of conversion and application is found in
the traditional Taveta hut on the coast of Kenya. Allight Ktamework of insect-resistant timber such as a species of cedar is
thatched with the bark ofthe banana tree to give a thick impervious skin to the hut which makes it waterproof.

Finally, the use of bark for binding components is most
important. Indeed, itisa primary means of connection and can
be made from the bark of timbers such as the willow. In this
case the bark is stripped and then used in a green (unseasoned)
form to make the connection. RICHARD HYDE

Bark and root binding

Binding and lashing materials may be drawn directly
from forest plants (see 1.1v.3 Rattan) with little conversion Nee
essary. These include the genus Salix, trees and shrubs ofthe willow family, the bark ofwhose pliant branches can be peeled and
used green. A species (Salix viminalis) known as the osier, both

1.1V.5.b-i

252

flexible and strong, was much used for this purpose, as were
most.ofthewithies, known as bindingaand interlacing materials
since medieval times. Willows grow near streams and are most
suited to temperate climates, hence their use in Europe.

Young or thin bamboos are used for binding when prepared
as follows. After selection the bamboo stalks are split and the
lengths soaked in water overnight until they are more supple.
The outer layer is then shaved off and if fine work is intended
the strips, approximately 2 m (6.5 ft) in length, may be trinmiged
by being passed between a pair of knife blades mounted in a
wooden block. After further soakingthe strips are used to make
flat bindings, the ends being drawn under two or more turns of
the cane lengths to make a secure bond on drying.

Native American peoples in forest and woodland regions
used a variety of barks for tying and binding, depending on
their availability. In some cases the whole bark of the shoots
mightbe used for this purpose, such as young hickory, DawDaw
or leatherwood (Dirca palustris L.). Older hickory also provided
the material for bark rope, as did the slippery elm, white oak,
red cedar and basswood. The method employed was essentially
the same, the bark being peeled in long strips from the tree and
then soaked in water for aweek or more. The inner layer could
then be easily prised from the outer sheath, and was separated
into layers with the fingernail. To make the strips more pliable
they were sometimes boiled in water which had previously been
prepared by boiling wood ashes. After simmering for a day the
bark was drawn over a pole, causing it to divide in threads.
These were twisted over the thigh to make string.
Roots ofsome trees were also used for binding, being soaked
in water firstand then scraped. Hemlock, spruce, cedar, yellow
Pine and cottonwood are among the many roots that have been

noted. PAUL OLIVER

1IV5.c Log
The use of logs as components for buildings dates
backalongtime. Roman writers, for example, were acquainted
with structures of square hewn pine in Germany in which logs
were used to provide a walling element that interlocked at the
corners. The log element in this kind of construction method
should not be confused with pole construction, although in
both cases solid timber construction is used.
A good example ofthe log house is still found in Norway. The
conversion process for the building involves first selecting the

farleft
Bark house u;
Australian sang, %

'sowner-built

Bark lashings
construction of:
dwelling, Kenya

See also

fir trees, and then ‘topping’ them, using axes and saws, to provide the bare poles; the poles are left for two years to season
before being felled. Felling involves cutting kerfs in thetrunkon
alternate sides usinga felling axe or double-handed saw. This is
askilled process as the reducing thickness of the tree weakens
the trunk and care is taken to ensure that the tree will fall into
clearings in the forest rather than damaging adjacent trees.
Where sawing is used wedges may be driven to maintain
sufficient space for the saw cut. The secondary process has three
main activities — debarking, bevelling and eutting to size - to
provide wall components. The logs are then laid horizontally,
one on top ofthe other, and interlocked at the corners to form a
bearing wall structure. All this is achieved using axes and saws,
the former beingthemore important. Hewn timber, that is, timber shaped by axe, is preferred because the blow from the axe
compacts the surface and closes the pores ofthe wood against
moisture penetration, whereas a saw tips and opens the pores.

Species oftrees that have long straight trunks, such as euca-
Iyptus or pine, are ideal for providing these large log components. The main distinction of’ log construction as compared to
pole construction is that the elements are placed horizontally
and rely for their stability on mass and interlocking connection. As such, the components form a ‘bulk active’ structure
which relies on weight and mass and gravitational forces to
secure the joints, and which can easily resist harsh environmental conditions.

In the joint connections of log construction, the horizontal
Placement oflogs provides only a butt joint between logs. This
is potentially problematicas large building tolerances and poor
fit between logs mean that other materials such as cloth and
earth have to be used to weatherproofthejoints. Later forms of
construction overcome this problem by routing a groove in
each horizontal face ofthe log and insertinga small horizontal
bead. This acts as a Joint filler and gives a connection between
the logs. Removal oftimber from the bottom surface ofthe log
aids waterproofing by providing an overhang to thejoint.

A second type of joint is necessary, which has structural
importance-the interlocking corner/joint. Logs are commonly
eutnotrightattheend ofthe log, butatleastone log’s width in
from the end. Thisrequiresa partofthe logto be expendable, a
design strategy which recognized that timber is particularly
susceptible to decay through its end grain. Thus, the exposed
end is not used for structural Purposes.

Three main types of‘ interlocking joint are: the cradle jointin
which half-round cuts are made in the top face of the log so
each log is seated on another; the secure notch joint which uses
angled flat cuts to bed logs together; and the half dovetail
which allows a close interlocking fitand mechanically pulls the
logs together.

The use oflogs for construction may seem easy and simple,
but problems can occur if unsuitable logs are selected. Logs
with a minimum taper are preferred to avoid mismatch and
poor fitin the horizontaljoint; an average taper ofaboutiomm
(0.4 in) change in diameter per metre (3 fi) is sought. Problems
of straightness of timber usually limit the length of walling

units to amaximum of5 m (16 ft). Long lengths can also cause
handling problems.

Care is taken to select logs with a minimum of sapwood
which is the most liable to decay. The retention ofbark on logs
is often desired but whilst it looks attractive cannot always be
retained over the life ofthe building and can be Prone to rot.
It can also encourage woodlice and beetles. Seasoning of
large logs to prevent decay takes time as water has to emerge
from within the tree. From six months to two years is the
Customary time for this process, depending on the climate and
conditions.

As log construction uses timber in the round, variations in
the diameter of logs also provide difficulties during construction. Care is taken at the selection point to find suitable and
compatible logs. ‘Handing’ the logs during construction can
be used if there is a wide discrepancy in their taper. This
involves reversing the butt end (wider end ofthe trunk) ofalternate logs to accommodate the large taper. Even so, ifthe logs
vary in diameter considerably then the depth ofthe interlocking
notch needs to accommodate the difference.

As components of construction logs require a minimum of
conversion and log construction was used forthe earliest ofsettlement patterns in Europe, America and Australia. With some
mechanization and the development of more sophisticated
conversion practices more efficient use was made ofthe large
sections of timber, and thus lighter framing elements were
developed. Large structures using log construction can be
found in Switzerland where log houses have been built upto
five or six storeys in height, with masonty bases to safeguard
against fire. Many ofthese buildings have stood for four centuries, in spite ofthe harsh winter climates to which they have
been exposed. RICHARD HYDE

1.IV.5.d Pole

The pole is a form of construction that utilizes the
natural qualities of trees with the minimum of conversion to
form the construction element. N ormally poles are prepared in
a similar way to that for log construction by simply ‘dressing’
the tree trunks before construction. This involves the removal
of secondary branches and bark. The natural contours of the
trunk can be kept but sometimes sapwood is removed to prevent decay. After seasoning, the poles are ready for construction. However, unlike log construction, pole joints are not
prefabricated and are made after placement. Tree trunks that
are equally branched to make a natural fork are often used, as
they can receive a horizontal beam.

253

## S. 77

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

In most pole-frame buildings the poles are ‘earthfast’ or
embedded into the ground. In many parts ofthe world, such as
Papua New Guinea, Colombia and Southeast Asia, poles are
used in low-Iying coastal areas and by lake dwellers; in these
cases the poles are immersed in water. In both conditions care
is taken to select highly durable species oftimber.

Poles may be found as having variety ofuses as architectural
elements in many vernacular buildings around the world. The
Chinese tradition in timber construction utilizes the post-andbeam form. The pole that constitutes the post is usually of a
large, cylindrical form, and is used particularly in prestige
buildings. This type ofconstruction gives a ‘frame active’ structural system where all loads from the roofto the foundation are
transmitted through the frame, to points on, or in, the ground,
the walls being non-load-bearing. Such a form of construction
is highly respected and used extensively for traditional architecture despite a well advanced brick technology. The reasons for
this are not clear but the evidence points strongly to the good
resistance ofthese pole-frame structure to earthquake damage
and to the architectural opportunities ofnon-load-bearing wall
infill. The infill was often made of a lattice of withies, packed
with earth, which could be highly articulated.

Wider use of poles for housing has been found in many other
countries. Southeast Asia and Australia have good examples of
indigenous and vernacular houses built of pole construction.
In both cases the pole either simply supports floors from which
a lighter wood frame is built, usually of stick construction, or
the poles support floors and continue up to support the roof.

O Miles Dany

254

The walls are again formed of light construction; in Southeast
Asia this is frequently ofwoven bamboo slats. In the Thai house
the poles are of durable timbers such as teak (Tectona grandis)
and inclined inwards, presumably to give stability. These poles
are often 300-450 mm (12-18 in) in diameter and support a
layered roof structure. This roof structure is made up of a
number of beams supported by the poles, and tied to them by
brackets.

In Malaysia the use of poles is commonly found in the eastern states such as Sarawak. The poles ofthe longhouses are of
rough-hewn logs and support a lighter bamboo structure. In
the western parts of Malaysia poles are used in larger, prestige
buildings. Poles ofthe very durable chengal wood (Balanocarpus
heimii) are used to a height of three storeys and are spliced to
give structural continuity. Australian examples of the use of
poles are found in domestic construction, and usually ironbark
(Eucalyptus paniculata) and spotted gum (Eucalyptus maculata) are
used.

Perhaps the most interesting use of poles is in buildings at
the littoral zones. Here the poles are used to support platforms
above the high-tide mark. Structures such as the palafitos of
Colombia use timbers which are resistant to marine borers,
intermittent water saturation due to tide changes and consequential rot. The peoples of Sarawak and other parts of Southeast Asia use an ironwood (Backhousia myrtifolia) for these types
ofstructures. The tree is a hardwood with slender trunks up to
100 mm (4 in) in diameter. These types oftimber poles can be
laid horizontally to form a raft foundation on which other
forms ofconstruction can be placed.

Coconut wood (Cocos nucifera) is used in coastal regions of
Southeast Asia. This timber is from the dicotyledon group and
is unlike the structure of hardwoods and softwoods, in that
there are no knots or branches, and little bark. The important
feature of this wood for pole construction is that it has little
taper (5 mm [o.2 in] per metre [3 ft] in height) and reaches
heights of2o m (65 fi).

Pole elements are useful in areas with poor soil conditions.
The pole is dug into the ground, often to a depth of 1-2 m
(3-6.5 ft) until solid ground is reached. This enables a sound
foundation to be developed and, when back-filled, the poles
act like a pile. Their structural efficiency is increased as the
end stiffness from the ground gives a cantilever effect to
the pole, thus increasing its structural resistance to lateral
forces. Lighter wall, floor and roof elements can then be
attached. The use of large-diameter timber for poles gives
added longevity to the primary structure. Lighter, split logs
would easily rot.

The main joints thatrequire attention in pole construction are
ground embedment and the design of the floor and roof framing connections. The large size of pole members means that
connections are easily made by notching without significant
reduction in the strength ofthe member.

Pole-frame construction is still a viable form of vernacular
construction, particularly on steep sites and where aminimum
ofsite disturbance and ecological damage is desired.

RICHARD HYDE

Timber RDAC
