oral

D Edward $. Curtis AmCAS, Exeter University

Riven

Two of the main components in timber construction are the post and the beam. These take many forms, for
example as lintels, bearers and joists, and are frequently square
or rectangular in section, and straight. Planks are thinner rectangular sections often used as floors. These elemental forms
have many functions in timber buildings but are all formed in
the same way.

An early method of converting these pieces for the building
was by use of an axe or wedges to split the trunk into smaller
pieces, called riven timber. This process of conversion exploits
the natural quality of wood or the alignment of the cell structure along the length ofthe timber which is an inherent weakness in the material. Thus, pieces of timber are easily split
along, rather than across, the grain. Splitting made more
efficient use of the timber than leaving it in log form as size,
thickness and length could be tailored to the required purpose
to give a greater variety of building elements. Some species of
trees were more suitable than others. The British oak was
regarded as an important timber, not only because ofits ability
to resist decay and load capacity, but also because it could be
‘cleft’, or split from end to end. The conversion process
involved using a ‘beetle’ and wedge to split the logs in a controlled way. An adze is also used as a tool for trimming and
shaping by hacking or splitting small pieces off the main part
ofthe timber component. The shape ofthe adze has evolved to
accommodate this splitting process. Unlike an axe, where the
tapered metal blade is aligned in the direction oftthe shaft, the
blade ofthe adze is curved to facilitate splitting the timber and
is aligned at 90° to the shaft.

The use of split logs to form posts and beams was common
in many indigenous buildings, the highland huts in the Enga

1.1V.5.e

Timbers 1.1.5

region of Papıa New Guinea for instance using this form of
construction. The dwelling is circular in form with two concentie rings of vertical timber posts at close centres (usually the
thickness ofone post between them). The posts are made from
splitlogs and tapered ateach end, one end being driven into the
ground and the other connected to a timber ring beam. Horizontal planks of thinner split material are slotted between the
rings of posts to provide the cladding. A similar process is used
by the Haida peoples ofthe Pacific Northwest, who made large
planks by cutting kerfs into the timber. These kerfs formed
indentations in the tree trunk that allowed large timber planks
to be split off'the main bulk ofthe trunk to the desired length.

Other components are also formed by splitting. Shakes are
an example and are used forroofand sometimes wall cladding.
The shakes are in the form of timber tiles and are usually split
by hand. Heartwood bolts of timber are usually used, though
the species of timber varies depending on the location. Red
cedar is used in the United States while in Asia, particularly
Malaysia, bakau is used to produce thin, narrow shakes for
Prestige buildings.

The conversion process involves firstly cutting the felled timber into bolts, lengths of between approximately 450 mm (18
in) and 800 mm (31 in) being produced. The timber shakes are
then split from the boltusinga tool, called a ‘froe’, with a heavy
steel blade. In the Appalachians (USA), a ‘horse’ is used to
assist with the splitting ofthe shakes. This consists ofa device
like a saw trestle which has a clamp at one end to support the
bolt vertically and a seat at the other end. Shakes vary in thickness, size and colour, qualities that give a feeling of warmth,
craft and aesthetic appeal.

Various forms of shakes can be produced: a straight split,
which is a vertical splitofa desired thickness; a taper slit, where
blocks are upended after each splitto give the taper; anda hand
splitand resawn, in which a band saw is used to thin shakes and
to give a sawn surface on one side. The sawn surface is usually
the back ofthe shake, permitting a closer fit of the successive
layers. Shakes and shingles are usually attached to the roof
structure using pegs or nails, with two-thirds of each layer of
shakes covered by the layer above. RICHARD HYDE
11V5f Sawn
Sawn timber is a common material in vernacular
building which came into wide use with the acquisition ofmore
sophisticated tools and greater knowledge of timber. Thus
splitting gave way to sawing components as a major form of
conversion. With this came a skill and craft of the saw miller
which was equal to thatofa carpenter. Judgement is required to
saw the logto gain the optimum use oftimber. This process, as
with splitting timber, takes into account the natural qualities of
wood. Timber at the centre or heart of the log is likely to have
more defects than the outer parts. This can often render smaller
sections oftimber useless, and so this part ofthe trunk is often
used for heavy timber beams. The outer parts of the trunk are
more suitable for thinner, flatter pieces such as planks and
boards for flooring.

Principal methods of sawing are: the flitch, in which an arc
section ofthe log including the bark is cut, the removal of four

255

## S. 78

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

V
5

such arcs giving a squared log; square sawing, which involves
cuttingthe log first into four sections to give a variety ofplanks,
then cutting these again to give posts and beam sections; and
through cutting, in which simple horizontal cuts are made to
give planks ofvarying widths and thicknesses.

The cutting of timber using these methods is examined not
just in the light ofthe section quality ofthe component or the

likely defects to be encountered. Consideration is given to other
factors, such as the effects of seasoning on shape (timber cut
with horizontal grain is likely to ‘cup’ or warp) and also to symbolic purposes. Allthe main posts in the Malay house are taken
ftom one tree to give a sense of unity. The position in which
they are cut in section is translated into their plan position.
Thus, the centre post comes from the heart of the tree and is
positioned in the centre. Other posts are positioned according
to their original relationship to the heartwood. The beliefs that
link the ritual of cutting with the ritual of construction can be
seen to have practical as well as symbolic values to the house
construction system.

The process of sawing before mechanization was a laborious
and time-consuming process ofconversion, acommon method
ofwhich used a sawpit. The log was placed overa pitanda large
2m (6.5 ft) double-handled saw was used to cut the logs; the pit
enabled the saw to pass through the log. In the sawing process
one person held the handle above ground whilst the other held
the saw in the pit. The combined effort ofthe two people could
cut through large trunks to produce dimensionally accurate
components such as beams and columns. This form ofconversion was common in many parts of Europe, China and elsewhere. In Southeast Asia a saw trestle is constructed above
ground, in which the same sawing principle is applied.

In addition to structural elements thereisarange ofcladding
components, among them clapboard and shingles, which can
be produced by sawing. RICHARD HYDE

siab
Early shelters for Australia’s ıgth-century farming
settlers were commonly built of hardwood slabs split from
local timber, using rudimentary skills and equipment. Given
thegreat distances and poor transportation, this construction
system necessarily developed from locally available resources,
specifically the native hardwoods and softwoods being cleared
for farming. Working standards for structural sizes and jointing techniques evolved with the use ofaxe, wedge and .adze.
Although the standard technique for most timber building
soon became the stud-framed wall and timber floor set clear of
the ground on hardwood stumps, the slab system persisted. It
made few demands for squared timbers and the associated carpentıy skills and tools, requiring only workable timbers ofgenerous size. Numbers of these buildings still exist as rural

115.8

symbolie conner

See also
1.V.2.c Clapboard
1.V11.4.a-i Bull
Malay
1.1X.2.w WheelWrighl

References
Jayaretti, Lioı
Lim Jee Yuan,
Sunley, J.,and
(eds),1985

farleft
Saw trestle ('sawpll
central Java.

slab house
Queensland,

© Damien Pokarler

homes, farm buildings and even churches. As late as 1913 the
Queensland Agricultural Journal ran articles on how to make them.

This form of shelter commonly did not provide a suspended
floor. Instead the ground, appropriately modified, became the
floor, and the timber construction comprised essentially a bottom plate set on the ground, a top plate supporting the roof
framing and vertical slabs between the two, providing both
structure and cladding. A pitched roof, framed from bush
timbers, was sheeted with bark or wooden shingles, until
corrugated iron roofing became universal.

With rudimentary hand tools, only basic shaping was feasible. A freshly felled logwould have its bark stripped, aline axed
along its length and steel wedges inserted. The line ofwedges
would then be driven in until a slab split off. Slabs would be
adzed at each end to about 25 mm (1 in) thick. These could be
fitted into grooves in top and bottom plate, or more commonly
into a rebate cut out of the round surface of the plate, and
beaded in on the interior side. Door jambs between top and
bottom plate might be adzed to a square section. Windows
were few, joinery beinguncommon. As wall slabs mightbe over
30 cm (tft) wide, a basic window could be provided by cuttinga
rectangular shape out of two adjoining slabs. Vertical gaps,
from lateral shrinkage, could be closed simply by levering the
slabs together. When fully seasoned they could be weatherproofed by internal papering with newspapers or flourbags, or
occasionally plugged with clay. In a more sophisticated version, finely adzed slabs are set horizontally between posts.

Reasonably resilient and impermeable floors were built up
from a slurry variously including crushed antbed, cattle dung,
blood and oil. This system is susceptible to underground termites
(white ants), which rapidly eat out most local timber species
unless prevented from doing so by physical or chemical barriers.
Charring, tarring, soaking the timbers with brine or with sump
oil have been used as deterrents. Tallowwood (Eucalyptus microcorys), ironbark (Eucalyptus drepanophylla) and cypress pine (Callitiris glaucophylla) were among species considered resistant.
The old slab buildings remaining in use demonstrate thatsuch

timbers are not always lost to termites. IAN SINNAMON

Timbers 1.1.5

1.1V.5.h Stick

Sticks are defined as small pieces oftimber, no thicker
than one’s wrist, from the branching parts ofa tree that make up
the canopy. The use ofthe branches for components in timber
construction is not common owing to the variable structural
quality, the natural shape ofthe pieces and the inherent pliability ofthe material. Even so many indigenous buildings such as
the huts ofthe Turkana of Kenya and the Zulu indlu use sticks in
construction since there is a minimum of conversion from the
Primary source ofthe material to its placement in the building.

One ofthe main objectives in any timber building is to use all
the timber materials thatare formed in the primary construction
process ofthe building. At one level the skill ofthe carpenter is
used to ensure that this objective is met and that timber waste is
reduced to a minimum. Atanother level a by-product ofthe pri-
ımary conversion process produces many small-section pieces
oftimber. These are used for such elements as roof battens and
other secondary fixings including cover strips, architraves and
skirting boards. Cover strips can be used internally to hide gaps
between major components such as windows and walls. They
are also used on the exterior for covering joints in cladding. In
this case care is taken to seal joints behind the cover strip.

In making small sections of timber for use in vernacular
buildings consideration ofthe type oftimber is important as is
the function ofthe component. First, the use ofsticks for structural purposes does not seen feasible. Yet the characteristics of
the high load-bearing capacity of certain types of hardwood
and the resistance to insect attack and rot ofthese types oftimber provide for the use of this type of material for structural
framing, called ‘stick framing’.

Examples ofstick framing are found in the United States and
in Australian construction, in particular in the Queensland house.
In the traditional Queensland house the main structure uses a post
andrail system with verticaltongued and grooved boarding boards
between the main vertical and horizontal structural supports.
Dressed timbers were used which gave a high quality of finish.

Fundamental changes to this type of house took place at the
beginning ofthe ıgth century. The traditional system gave way to
the introduction of stud framing, which used the same principle
as the platform framing developed in the United States. The variation in Queensland was brought about by climatic factors and
the availability of hardwoods. Although high wind-loads are
found, particularly on the coast, the strength ofthese hardwoods
meantthatsstud size could be reduced down to as little as 73 x 35

apkH men

## S. 79

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

mm (2.9X 1.4 in). These studs could be used at45 cm (18 in) centres to form a bearing wall structure, giving rise to the term ‘stick
frame’ construction. The conversion process used for making
the sticks or studs was different from the earlier method. The
studs could be simply rough sawn with aminimum ofseasoning.
Sheet materials were placed over the studs internally and weatherboards (similar to clapboards) placed on the exterior.

The increasing shortage of rain forest timbers and concern
for the conservation of existing natural forests has initiated
changes in the vernacular architecture in Queensland. A new
reliance on plantation timbers, such as quick growing softwoods like pine, has had an impact on the vernacular construction system which is changing to accommodate this material
and has meantan increase in the size ofstuds and the demise of
stick framing as itwas commonly known.

Secondary elements use smaller sections of timber. One
example is found in the Australian lattice which is made up of
25 X 12.5 mm (IX 0.5 in) rectangular sawn sticks, often placed
diagonally. The components come in a variety ofssizes and are
used as privacy and security screens. The visual effects ofthese
stick components add considerably to the appeal of these
buildings. RICHARD HYDE
1.1V.5.i Wattle
Wattle is made by interweaving rods and twigs to
form the solid material offences, walls, roofs, floors and sometimes even trackways. Plastered with daub (a mixture ofmud or
clay, straw and urine), wattle is, and has been, used in the building ofhuts and dwellings all over the world.

Two ofthe most commonly used woods for wattle are willow
and hazel. This is both because they are pliable and because they
grow easily in places where other trees will not, especially in wetland areas and flat steppeland. Willow can be dried and, iflater
soaked for three or four days, will become extremely flexible, and
is therefore suitable for weaving. Hazel rods, which are worked
when green, are usually used whole for uprights and split for
weaving.

The individual wattle rods or wands are not especially strong;
their strength comes from thetension putinto the materialbythe
interweaving process. Many otherwoods are also used for wattle,
subjectto availability, as willow is vulnerable to themany domestic animals that have developed the taste for eating willow bark.

The archaeology ofwetland areas has revealed the use ofwattle going back well over four thousand years. Wattle and daub
were used in the lakeland pile settlements (crannogs) ofScotland
and Ireland. Some of the buildings on the mound settlements
(terps) of the Netherlands, North Germany and Denmark were
double-wattled for insulation. Trackways in the Boston region
ofthe United States and the English Somerset Levels (for example, the Eclipse Track) were made from floating wattle hurdles.

Wattle is still used extensively in the Puszta region of Hungary. The Puszta is a great steppe-like plain and willow is used
in themaking oflongrunning fences and also in the building of
small shepherds’ dwellings. The use of wattle is also found in
many other parts of eastern Europe.

The Maasai use a system very similar to wattleand daub in the
building of their houses. The houses are built by the Maasai

258

E

A,

RR
1
En
..
IS
SQ
en

women from a wood called leleshwa. The side walls consist of
thick leleshwa uprights, insulated with leaves. A ‘ring beam’
runs around these, holding them in place, and the roof is then
made of a lattice of thinner wands, woven or lashed together,
which hoops over the top. Theroofis ‘plastered’ with cow dung
and this ‘plaster’ is later extended down the outside walls ofthe
house, unless ventilation is required to keep the house cool.
The door is also wattle.

A similar process of bending and lashing wands is used to
make the benders and gellies of Scottish tinkers, English peace
campers and new age travellers. The wood used in benders is
usually hazel or willow. However, the bender is debatably also a
kind of tent, since the lattice is covered with carpets and tarpaulins, and it is also taken down and reassembled.

One ofthe best known uses of wattle is probably seen in the
European timber-framed house. The infill between the beams
was often done with wattle and daub. A variety of woods were
used-forexample, cleftoak forverticallaths, which were nailed
onto the horizontal timbers, and willow or splithazel werethen
interwoven. A serious fire in London, in 1212, resulted in adecree
declaring thatall houses then covered in rush or reed had to be
plastered, and the wattle and daub construction ofthe old English timber-framed cottage is said to have resulted from this.

The Atlas ofSiberia (Levine and Potapov) has a fine example of
the use ofwattle by Kyzyl nomads to constructa cylindrical yurtshaped dwelling. Such buildings form a small selection ofthe
wide variety ofsimilarly shaped, but diversely constructed dwellings made by the nomads ofsouthern Siberia and north Asia. It
is debatable whether the felt yurts ofthis region were inspired
by the form ofthe wattle dwellings or vice versa.

1vs Used and Manufactured

One of the most widely cited defining characteristics
of vernacular architecture is that it is built of local materials.
Though in broad terms this may be appropriate, itwould bemore
accurate to redefine this characteristic ofthe vernacular as being
built ofavailable resources. Vernacular traditions do not cease to
be valid because they use materials thatare notwholly indigenous
to the locality - if this were the case most European traditions
wherein window glass is used would have to be excluded from the
‘vernacular’. In fact, over the centuries and throughout the world
a great many traditions have been strengthened or reinforced by
the transportation, trade or purchase of materials.

Reasons for this are diverse: the exhaustion of a resource
which is still required and has to be obtained elsewhere; the
perpetuation ofa tradition in spite ofrelocation; the diffusion
of tools or other artefacts whose advantages are recognized
even though they are not indigenous; or the desirability of
resources made available through the use of advanced technologies and means ofdistribution.

It is to be expected that access to manufactured and industrial materials is to be found among peasant cultures whose
economy is based on marketing their surpluses in towns. Similarly, evidence oftechnological introductions and change may
be found among ‘Fourth World’ peoples-those traditionaland
frequently indigenous cultures that survive within the predominately industrialized societies ofthe Americas or Asia. But the
greatest use of industrial products is to be found in what are
termed (among others) the bidonuvilles, barriadas, canicos, favelas,

‚gecekondus—the regional types ofsquatter settlements that grow
by the hour on the peripheries of scores of the world’s cities.
Ankara, Bogotä, Bombay, Calcutta, Guayaquil, Istanbul,
Jakarta, Johannesburg, Karachi, Lima, Luanda, Manila, Mexico

City, Nairobi, Rio de Janeiro - the roster of these expanding
urban concentrations is conspicuous for its emphasis on the
developing nations ofthe South.

Variously described as ‘unauthorized’, ‘illegal’, ‘spontaneous’, ‘peri-urban’, or simply as ‘shanty towns’, many such
settlements have been despised, condemned, bulldozed and
cleared - only to return with more vigour. Others have been
established over decades, eventually to become the substance
of the city itself, resided in not only by its poor and needy but
also by its cleaners, shopkeepers and clerks, on whom the eity
depends but who have yet to be able to afford homes within it.
Though internal expansion by natural increase creates significant growth within the squatter settlements, much of the
expansion is created by waves of peasants for whom the rural
economy has failed, or who have sought a new future and the
satisfaction oftheir aspirations within the city.

Whatever the urban eircumstances, the availability of traditional resources diminishes. Many squatter homes are initially
built with timber, wattle and daub or frame and mat structures,
but increased demand on limited materials and the desire to
build structures that are more permanent and reflective ofthe
urban environment has stimulated the employment of used
and recycled resources. Though occasionally aided by such
measures as the provision ofstandpipes and latrine blocks, the
construction ofservice cores around which self-built structures
can beadded, orthe introduction ofsites-and-services schemes

which afford plots and utilities, the new urban dwellers are
dependent on their own initiatives for their survival.

Among the principal survival strategies is the re-use of discarded materials, and the organization oftheir availability and
distribution. The scavenging ofthe detritus ofthe city and the
unwanted wastes ofthe affluent is a significant aspect of this,
often by children or by social groups who have developed and
protected their specialization in the process. Related are the
obtaining and reprocessing of industrial wastes and the dis-

mantling and redeployment of manufactured materials and
products.

Witnessing the self-building of houses within the settlements, whether illegal or authorized, some observers may
regard the processes as those ofemerging neovernacular architecture. Others, noting the feedback of urban survival techniques into the rural areas, and the adoption of recycled and
manufactured materials in former traditional contexts, deplore
the rapid decline ofthe vernacular.

Though this Encylopedia is primarily concerned with the
recording and understanding of the world’s vernacular traditions, the recycling of wastes and the recontextualizing of

259

## S. 80

EEE

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

industrial products cannot be overlooked: examples of longstanding and recent ‘used and manufactured’ materials are
summarized in this category. In view ofthe trends and changes
that inevitably the 21st century will witness in the vernacular
traditions and in the evolving neovernacular building customs,
notes follow on the characteristics ofmanufactured materials,
and the possible implications of used resources on design and

R
development. PAUL OLIVE

1116-5 Transformed Materials

Transformed materials are those which have been
irreversibly changed from their natural ‘raw’ state by some
form of processing in order to improve their suitability for use
in building. Allmanufactured materials are transformed materials in this sense; but there are some transformed materials
which cannot be described as manufactured materials (in the
sense of factory-made), for instance burnt bricks and tiles
which are entirely made by craft processes.

Natural raw materials are transformed in order to improve one
ormore oftheir inherent characteristics to make them more suitable for use in building. Raw materials may be transformed in
order to improve their strength; their durability (i.e. resistance to
erosion and weathering, rot or insect attack, fire-resistance);
their shape or dimensional accuracy; their dimensional stability
(i.e. reduce expansion or shrinkage due to moisture movement);
their appearance. Transformation is frequently carried out in
order to achieve several ofthese improvements simultaneously;
for example the firing ofbricks improves their strength and durability and reduces their moisture movement compared with
unfired bricks.

Arange of different types oftransformation have been used
traditionally in craft processes. The traditional types of transformation include firing and heating, chemical processes,
intermixing of different materials, coating and rendering of
one material with another, and compaction through application oframming or pressure.

In these following entries on transformed materials, only
those materials which are invariably manufactured are included
-concrete and cement-based materials, lime and metals.

Firing and heating processes are applied to a number of
materials for which a rise in temperature causes a desirable
change in the state ofthe material. In the manufacture oflime
from limestone a temperature ofabout 900 °C (1650 °F) causes
dissociation of the caleium carbonate into calcium oxide
(quicklime) and carbon dioxide gas, which is driven off. As
clays are heated to a temperature of around 800-1000 °C

(1470-1830 °F) a series ofchanges occur, including the removal
of closely bonded water within the clay lattice, and the formation of permanent ceramic bonds between the individual clay
particles: these processes also take place in the manufacture of
clay bricks and tiles and clay pozzolanas. In the manufacture of
gypsum plaster the raw gypsum is heated to a temperature of
around 170 °C (340 °P), at which it dissociates into hemi-

260

hydrate (gypsum plaster) and water vapour which is driven off.
In glass manufacture the silica raw material is heated to a tem
perature in excess of 1500 °C (2730 °F) in order to cause it to
meltand the individual particles to fuse so thatit can be formed
into sheets or plates. In Portland cement manufacture a temperature of1450 °C (2640 °P) is required to transform the separate raw materials — limestone and clay — into new cement
minerals.

Allthese firing processes take place in various forms ofkiln,
the form of which derives from the problem of heat transfer
between the fuels used and the raw material, and varies widely
according to the type of fuel used, scale of production, the
degree oftemperature control needed and the materials available for construction. Some types ofkiln used for burning limestones and clays are described. In the manufacture ofclays and
tiles, lime and gypsum, traditional processes are still in use.

Chemical processes occur in all these firing processes, but

are also at work in some other low-temperature transformations. The main low-temperatures chemical processes used in
the transformation ofbuilding materials are those which occur
when cementitious material is mixed with water. The addition
of water to Portland cement, to a hydraulic lime or lime-pozzolana mixture or to gypsum causes a chemical reaction
(known as hydration) in which new crystalline minerals are
formed, bonding together the entire mixture into a continuous
hard mass. In gypsum production this chemical reaction takes
place very rapidly, releasing a lot of heat, but the hydration of
cements (which also releases heat) takes place more slowly,
over days and months. Other important chemical transformation processes occurring in materials made by non-industrial
processes are those which occur in slaking lime when water is
added to quicklime, converting it to hydrated lime, which
breaks it down to a fine powder; they also occur in the very slow
combination of lime in mortar and plaster through contact
with atmospheric carbon dioxide, which causes it to harden
and become permanently durable.

Irreversible transformations also take place when materials
from different sources are intermixed to form composites. The
addition of organic fibres to soil in adobe block manufacture is
one such transformation; the mixing ofaggregates with cementitious materials to form concrete, mortar or plaster is another.

Other transformation processes which are applied in other
situations include compaction or processing (used to remove
air from concrete, to increase the density of soil mixes or to
shape clay tiles for example), dıying (to remove moisture from
green timber or to harden clay products after formation) and
erushing and pulverizing (to pre-treat clays before moulding
and to reduce the size ofaggregates).

There are modern industrial versions ofall these transformation processes, the latter forming part of the technology of
today’s factory production processes. Indeed, except in the
metal and plastic industries, virtually all industrial processes in
building materials production derive from these basic transformation processes. In few cases does a material as used on site

undergo only one of these transformations; in most cases a
whole series of processes takes place, frequently at different
locations and using difference scales of operation.

see also forzuyg,
3.V.2.P Poblacjongs
3.V.4.8 Favelados

References for 7, 7
Butterworth, Do;
Chance, John,
Dwyer, D.1., 1975
Gilbert, Alan, and
Josef, 1981 ji
Karpat, Kemal H, 4
Mansgin, Wiliam Pya
Turner, John C., 1969,
Romanos, Aristigjsg
1969 j

burnt/fired
brick: Bull’s

The manufacture ofa concrete block involvesa large number
of transformations. First limestone and clay are quarried,
crushed, mixed and fired in a kiln to produce cement clinker.
The clinker is ground and mixed with gypsum to form Portland
cement. The cement is then mixed with water and aggregates,
cast into blocks, compacted by pressure or vibration, and
allowed to set and harden, before finally the block is laid with
mortar in a wall.

Steel roofing sheets are also manufactured in a three-stage
process. Iron ore is converted into pig-iron in a blast furnace;
iron is then converted into steel strip in a steel plant. This strip
is then converted into roofing sheets by being pressed and galvanized in a further process which is usually carried out on a
much smaller scale and closer to the market.

Manufactured materials and vernacular architecture
The widespread ayailability ofmanufactured materials through
trade and the spread ofindustrialization has causeda profound
change in the vernacular architecture ofmany regions. Insome
cases they have been added to improve performance and have
modified the appearance of buildings only superficially, while
in other cases more radical changes in form and appearance
have occurred; in some cases vernacular traditions have all but
disappeared following the introduction of new manufactured
materials. The spread of galvanized iron roofing sheets (Latin
America: ldmina; Africa: bati) is an example. Although expensive in relation to traditional thatch or tile roofs, they give considerable advantages - durability, better rain protection, fire
resistance and ease of assembly in particular. Originally they
were used for urban housing where collection of materials and
maintenance ofthatched roofs were time-consumingand firea
more serious threat. But galvanized steel roofing has increasingly spread to rural areas. In some places the plan form of
dwellings has changed from circular to rectangular in order to
facilitate use of galvanized iron sheets (Archer; Denyer). In
other cases, entirely new types ofbuilding (e.g. the guest-houses
ofnorthern Pakistan) have developed for which roof sheets are
the standard material (Dudley). Another example is the gradual
spread ofreinforced concrete tie-beams (hatils) within the stone
masonry walls ofeastern Anatolia, replacingthe previously used
timber hatils, where scarcity oftimber makes their continued use

Used and Manufactured

1.1.6

impossible (Spence and Coburn). In Ladakh the form oftraditional buildings has been transformed bythe increasing use of
sheet glass to provide ‘sun-rooms’for winter. warmth; and plastic
Pipes have almost everywhere become incorporated into water.
supply and drainage systems ofotherwise vernacular buildings
(Dudley).
The internationally funded relief and reconstruction pro-

grammes following disasters have frequently triggered large-

scale changes ofvernacular traditions through the introduction

of new materials. In northern Pakistan the rebuilding of the

villages on the Indus river after the 1974 earthquake brought
reinforced concrete frames into use alongside timber frames for

the first time (Davis); in eastern Turkey the concrete block and

steel-sheet roofed houses of the reconstruction Programmes

have influenced subsequentowner-building (Coburn, Leslieand

Tabban); in Fiji imported timber-panel houses, after the destruc-

tive cyclones of1972, 1975 and 1981, have undermined the belief
oflocal builders in thevalue oftheir building traditions, and have

led to their wholesale replacement within a very short period of
time (Cryer). In other cases similar large-scale changes in rural
house-form and materials occur more gradually through the
influence ofthe styles of building adopted by relatively wealthy
returned migrant workers from major cities or abroad (Aysan
and Oliver).

The late-2oth century urban vernacular architecture of‘ many
cities depends to a large extent on manufactured materials,
concrete and ceramic blocks, steel roof sheets, glass and reinforced concrete frames. In many earthquake areas, an entirely
new vernacular architecture of reinforced concrete houses has
been developing, the most striking feature of which is the
upward projecting reinforeing bars at the corner columns,
indicating a plan for subsequent extension. These projecting
bars may also serve to symbolize the state ofconstant change in
the nature of vernacular architecture worldwide which the
availability, technical qualities and the prestige ofthese industrially produced materials make inevitable. But conversely, the
adoption ofindustrially produced materials is likely to resultin
a permanent dependency on the industrial economy, with its
associated problems of commercial exploitation, dependency
on world market prices for materials, and loss ofself-sufficiengy.

ROBIN SPENCE

1.1.6-cC Waste and Re-use

The history of architecture has been the history of
certain ‘noble’ materials. Stone marked centuries of architectural evolution and, ifnotas monumental, the role ofbrick has
been as important since ancestral times as has for certain societies the use ofwood. The ıgth century broughtto architectural
history three new materials: iron, concrete and glass (although
they had been used earlier) and in recent years aluminium and
plastics have played an important role.

The history of architecture can continue with more sophisticated materials but unfortunately the problems that architects of

261

## S. 81

MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

1.1V

the 2oth century are facing go further than the plastic expression
ofold and new materials. Facingarchitectural practice is the terrible spectre ofa social demand for housing for a proletariatwhose
numbers seem to haveno limits; in view ofthis situation the use of
the most unglamorous building resources - ‘garbage’ appears as
an alternative thatshould not be neglected. In that context the reuse and recycling of materials has become an important input in
the evolution ofcontemporary vernacular and popular housing in
the industrializing, industrialized and post-industrial world.

To give a new use to refuse is not a new idea but its importance is increasing as its quantity increases. The lack of housingis notanew problem; ithas existed in different societies but
itbecomes more important when countries start the industrialization process as the developed countries did one or two
centuries ago. The developing countries are doing so now on
anunprecedented quantitativeand qualitativescaleand with an
intensiveness never seen before.

The use and recycling ofwaste materials can be focused seen
from two points of view: even if recycling or re-using waste
materials does not solve the housing problem, the practice represents an important alternative with a great potential in the
general solution, and even if such practice does not solve the
problems caused by solid wastes, it helps to diminish theimpaet
ofsuch environmental pollution. These perspectives represent
two ways of improving the level and the quality oflife ofthousands ofpeople. An integral approach to re-use and recycling is
useful everywhere but its application seems to be more necessary in developing countries where both environmental and
housing problems are a priority.

The use of waste materials has been practised as a spontaneous activity for many years not only by industrial producer
societies but also by industrial consumer societies in away that
can be considered as industrial vernacular architecture.

The development of new technologies based on the use of
waste materials can take three forms: the diffusion ofsimple technologies similar to those that have developed spontaneously
and in which existing popular and vernacular housing based on
re-useandrecycling is applied to different contexts; new intermediate technologies based on simple industrialized processes
implying the improvement of existing systems by adding new

262

© Jorge Gonzälez Claverän

elements to them; and new industrial products designed for an
alternative new use, this last option being more radical as it
implies the transformation of certain industrial products in
such a way that they will be able to have a secondary use.

The use of new construction techniques in these three directions has an enormous potential. If interesting construction
systems have been developed at a spontaneous level it can be
expected that at a second stage, with technical support, new
construction systems will be developed and applied to more
sophisticated production and thatata third stage the existence
ofnew industrial products will help to create a massive production. At this stage contemporary vernacular housing based on
re-use and recycling of waste materials may be envisaged at a
considerable scale.

The use of waste materials in construction in these three ways

„has already been approached. Re-use in vernacular housing is

an old tradition. Its rationalization through the development of
more sophisticated technologies using basically the same materials has been developed over many years and the production of
industrial objects with regard to their secondary use has already
been arrived atthough with severe limitations.

The ‘Wobo bottle’ designed by a brewery in Holland is perhaps the most interesting case of an industrial object produced
in relation to its secondary use. Once seen as waste material, the
bottle with plain walls became the basic element of a modular
system of construction. However, although it was technologically an excellent product it never reached mass production and
soon was no longer produced. Its apparent failure was dueto an
essential factor— itwas produced in a context in which itwas not
needed. Nevertheless, the lessons from the ‘Wobo experience’
can be successfully applied in an appropriate context.

The development of new construction systems based on the
re-use or recycling of waste materials requires the solution of
problems that until now have been common in the spontaneous
houses built with these systems. Until now waste materials
have been developed in places where there are no other possibilities for building. As other materials became accessible the
use of waste materials tends to disappear. Vernacular housing
based on re-use or recycling of waste materials, generally considered as marginal housing, has not had a long history. For

Re-use of discare
materials, settley
housing, “1

Iners used for

that reason, in most cases waste materials have not reached a
high level of development, so that construction based on the
use of waste material requires improvement.

Another problem is thatthe comfort level obtained in houses
built with waste materials has been until now relatively low. An
intensive technological development to solve comfort conditions is required. Insolation becomes a major issue, especially
in constructions made with metals and plastics that are poor
insulators and create severe thermal conditions.

Finally, houses made with waste materials are generally very
light; for that reason they do not presentany significant danger
in case ofearthquakes but they do in the case of hurricanes. In
order to improve resistance to disasters intensive work on
structural design is required.

The development of construction systems based on the re-use
ofwaste materials is important everywhere but its importance is
more significant in developing countries where both the housing
and the environmental problems are virtually out ofcontrol.

A characteristic of urban growth in the last 2000 years has
been thatthe biggest cities in the world have also been therichest ones but now this characteristic has changed and the cities
in developing countries will constitute the major urban centres
in the world, in which waste materials will playan importantrole
with their high potential for mass production.

In the context ofthe 2ıst century megalopolitan centres, with
high energy constraints and a lack ofthe conventional building
materials, the new urban vernacular housing will be as importantas was the rural vernacular housing ofthe gth century.

JORGE GONZÄLEZ CLAVERÄN
1.1V.6.a Beer containers
The Santo Domingo valley in the arid lands of Baja
California had an old traditional house (canastilla) (little basket
house’) that gave adequate shelter to the few inhabitants ofthis
unpopulated area. By 1950 the whole region was transformed
by the introduction ofan irrigation system thatled to the development of two important agricultural centres: Ciudad Constituciön and Ciudad Insurgentes.

As agricultural development took place it became ever more
difficult to obtain the traditional building materials which originally came from thelocal vegetation. On one hand the population
was growing fast and on the other the original fragile vegetation
ofthe desert was gradually superseded by agricultural fields. Very
soon the traditional vegetal house started to disappear and the
new population was faced with an important problem - how to
build. Locally there were not adequate materials and the main
cities were too far away.

Used and Manufactured

1.IV.6

Faced with the lack of cheap materials for building, people
spontaneously started to build with one ofthe most abundant
materials in the place - ‘tin’ cans from cold drinks. Great quantities of beer, fruit juice and indeed oil containers were subsequently collected. In this way, a new building system was
developed and, although beer containers had been used before
in other places, they had never been used in the quantityand the
manner in which they became used in the Santo Domingo valley.

The development ofsuch a building system was not easyina
region which has extreme temperatures and almost no rain.
Nevertheless metallic containers were very soon the dominant
material in the region, their popularity being due to the following: Santo Domingo is a long way from the country’s main
industrial centres which means that the use ofreturnable bottes is very expensive and so most items for local consumption
(beer, soda drinks, oil, fruit juices and conserves) are sold in
disposable containers; the quantity ofcans in the garbage was
increasing all the time; and the beer container, because of its
modular characteristics and size, represented an ideal opportunity to recycle as a building material.

The construction ofthe Santo Domingo house depends on
each family collecting metallic containers on the building site,
and when it has enough material to start building, it buys the
wood necessary to make the frames.

The first stage is the construction of the structure using
strong wooden columns. When this is finished rectangular
frames are built using thin wood. When all the wooden elements (structure and frames) are finished, the family starts to
build the walls, placing the metallic containers horizontally
with the hole facing the interior of the house, and securing
them with clay. When the walls are finished they are plastered
with clay to provide thermal insulation.

Then the roofs are made using palm leaves in exactly the
same way as they were used in the old traditional canastilla.
Palm leaves are the optimum material in terms ofthermal comfort in this region; sometimes asphaltic board is substituted
with very bad results.

The potential of this building system in the region is enormous when the annual consumption of metallic containers in
the Baja California peninsula is analysed. In 1978 during the
boom period ofthis kind ofconstruction, consumption ofbeer
in metallic containers alone represented 5.932 543 units and
this number is doubled if soft drinks, fruit juices, oil and conserve containers are taken into consideration. This number has
increased every year since then.

1f169 pieces are required to build x sq m (t1 sq ft) ofwall it
would have been possible with the 1978 production of beer to
build 35 103 sq m (377700 sq ft) of wall (in the hypothetical
case of 100 per cent of the cans being recycled). Each house
requires about 120 sq m (1300 sq ft) ofwall, so the annual production would be enough to build 290 houses.

The cities of Ciudad Constituciön and Ciudad Insurgentes
have now been consolidated and several kinds of building
materials have been brought from other regions which are now
accessible to most of the population. Nevertheless, houses
built with beer containers are still the dominant element in the

local townscape. JORGE GONZÄLEZ CLAVERÄN

263

## S. 82

1.V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
Cast iron (Australia)
Decorative cast ironwork is the most distinctive
characteristic of Australia’s oldest inner suburbs prior to World
War]. Atfirst, in the 1830s, it was imported in small quantities
from foundries such as Cottam and Hallen in London and Walter Macfarlane in Glasgow. The first Sydney foundries copied or
actually cast from these imported pieces, and Sydney iron
henceforward retained some English Regency characteristics,
such as a preponderance ofvoid over solid, the common use of
the openwork pilaster as opposed to the solid column and particular decorative motifs such as the anthemion.
A more distinctive and more specifically Victorian period
character is found in Victoria, as a result of the presence of
skilled pattern-makers and prolific foundries. In 1859 a slump
in the quartz mining industry caused a number of founders to
turn their attention from mining and crushing machinery to
decorative ironwork. The local designs tend to have more
three-dimensional modellingand a greater proportion ofsolid,
and the openwork pilaster is totally unknown except in the
regional centre of Ballarat. Victorian iron was exported especially to areas accessible by rail, such as southern New South
Wales, or by sea, such as northern Tasmania. Later foundries
were established in Adelaide, and they exported to Western
Australia until production based on Adelaide patterns, which in
turn derived from Melbourne, began in the 1890s at Subiaco.
This ornamental ironwork was not much favoured by architects, who by the 18708 spoke ofit as a necessary evil, and preferred to use much more expensive wrought iron decoration
whenever the budget permitted. Thus cast iron became the
province of the speculative builder, even though the designs
mostly emanated from specialist modellers. From the 18708,
when official registration of patterns began, there was an
increasing tendency to incorporate Australian motifs such as
fishtail ferns and cockatoos. These competed with introduced
fashions such as the Aesthetic Movement, and with the crowns
which became common at the time of Queen Victoria’s jubilee.
After the depression of the ı89os almost no new designs
appeared, but the old ones continued to be marketed for usein
humbler housing and in country areas. There was some tendency towards unmodelled patterns resembling fretwork, but
these had little currency at a vernacular level. Only with World
War I did the tradition cease, and after it ‘wrought iron’ (actually mild steel), played the same role in the urban vernacular.

1.1V.6.b

MILES LEWIS

Cementitious
Cementitious materials are mineral materials which,
when mixed with water, form strong and permanent bonds.
Thus, they can be used as binders to make inert pieces of other
materials cohereintoasingle, strong, solid mass. This definition
excludes several binding materials commonly used in vernacular
architecture; resins and glues are binders, but they are not
included here because they are not of mineral origin; and clays,
traditionally perhaps the most widely used binders, are not
cementitious in the above sense because the bonds they form
result only from drying, and are lost on rewetting. The class of
materials commonly referred to as cementitious materials
includes gypsum, lime and pozzolanas, all of which are of
ancientorigin, and the family of materials derived from Portland
cement, which was first manufactured in the ıgth century.
Gypsum may have been the earliest mineral material used
for making cement; there are many examples in the pyramids
at Giza, Egypt. It is derived from naturally occurring deposits
of mineral gypsum, and manufactured by this being heated
in a kiln or tank to a relatively low temperature — typically
around 170 °C (338 °F). Manufacturing processes and uses are
described below.
Lime is derived mainly from limestone but also from caleium
carbonate in the form of coral or even sea-shells. Lime is made
by burning (caleining) the raw material in a kiln ata temperature
in excess of 900 °C (1652 °F) when it dissociates, driving off carbon dioxide and leaving calcium oxide or quicklime. Quicklime
is very unstable, and reacts strongly with water, expanding and
disintegrating into a fine powder (slaked lime, hydrated lime or
lime putty) which is suitable for making mortars and plasters.
Pure lime is not a cementitious material in the strict sense,
because the addition ofwater does notresultin the formation of
permanent bonds, but many traditional sources of limestone
contain clayey impurities. When these materials are burned in a
kiln, they result in what are called ‘hydraulic limes’, which do
have weak cementing properties. The concretes and mortars
used in many great architectural traditions up to the 2oth century are made from hydraulic limes, and the use of hydraulic
lime in this way continues in many parts ofthe world, for example in South India and rural Tanzania.

A much better cementing material is made by mixing lime -
either pure or hydraulic - with a pozzolana. Pozzolanas are
materials which are not cementitious in themselves, butwhich
will combine with lime in the presence of water to form a
cementitious material. They are composed mainly ofsilica and
can be derived from both natural and artificial sources. Both
types have been used in vernacular architectural traditions.

The natural pozzolanas are glassy, fine materials of volcanic
origin, usually in the form ofash deposits. The term ‘pozzolana’
derives from the Italian port town ofPozzuoli near Naples, from
where the Romans obtained their first sources of the material.
Ash deposits have been exploited for their pozzolanic properties
in many parts of the world, notably in southern Europe (the
island of Santorini in the Aegean isttheremains ofavolcanoand

contains huge ash deposits), in the Indonesian island of Java
and in Japan. Although in Europe and Japan volcanic ash is
nowadays combined more commonly with Portland cement in

1.1V.6.C

columns, Vi
balustrade,

Yieru, northern
being quarried
;cale lime-
‚ment

wales, Australd‘

Robertsöl
Turner, Bul

large-scale cement manufacture, its use still continues in the
village-scale manufacture of lime-ash blocks, for instance the
batako blocks made near Bandung in Indonesia. And in several
recent development projects the ash deposits of other countries
have been utilized for village-scale cement manufacture - in
northern Tanzania and Rwanda in Africa, in Indonesia and on
the Pacific island of Vanuatu, for example.

Traditionally the most common form of artificial pozzolana
is crushed brick and tile; this was widely used in the Roman
Empire, but fell out ofuse in Europe later. In many other countries it has survived as the principal means ofmakinga cementing material without the use of factory-made cement. Thus,
erushed brick is widely sold in markets in India (surkhi), in
Egypt (homra), in Java (semen merah) and in Turkey (khorasan).
Mixed on site with lime and sand (or gravel), these materials are
used for mortars and low-grade concrete in masonıy work of
alltypes where cement is too expensive or unavailable. There is
also a growing need for these materials in repair and maintenance work on the ancient masonry monuments ofmany countries, for which modern materials are too strong. This demand
is keeping the tradition oftheir manufacture alive even in parts
of the world where cement is readily available, as in Crete.
Artificial pozzolanas can also be derived from many industrial
and agricultural wastes, but the use ofthese materials (such as
rice-husk ash) in vernacular architecture is only recent. Small
plants to make rice-husk ash cement are found in India, Nepal
and Malaysia.

Today, by far the most widely used cementitious material is
Portland cement. Its name derives from the similarity of the
colour of the hardened concrete to that of Portland stone, a
popular building stone found in Dorset in southern England.
Portland cement is made from two basic raw materials, limestone and clay (though other materials with similar composition are sometimes used); these materials are crushed and
mixed together and then fired in a kiln to a temperature of: 1450
°C (2642 °F) where they combine to form new minerals in the
form ofhard pellets. These pellets (clinker) are then ground to
a fine powder, and mixed with some gypsum to form the familiar Portland cement. Because of the high temperature and

SER ® RE S ar

Used and Manufactured

1.1V.6

grinding energy required, it has become a large-scale factorybased process, and most cement factories produce at scales
exceeding 2000 tonnes per day. In India, where transportation
distances are great, a small proportion of the cement is produced in a new generation of mini-cement plants. Because of
the high quality and uniformity of Portland cement compared
with other traditional cementing materials, it has gradually
been displacing those materials everywhere, and its availability
and comparative cheapness have had a profound effect on
many vernacular architectural traditions. ROBIN SPENCE
1.1V.6.d Concrete: mass

Concrete is a hard, strong, composite material made
by combining aggregates - commonly sand and gravel - with a
cementitious binder, such as Portland cement, and water. When
metal reinforcement - commonly in the form of steel rods - is
introduced the material is referred to as ‘reinforced concrete’
(.c.) or sometimes ‘reinforced cement concrete’ (r.c.c.).

Concrete is an ancient material, very extensively used by the
Romans and probably earlier. Unreinforced concrete (or mass
concrete) is suitable for formingstrong foundations for masonry
buildings, and for floors and paving: it is used worldwide for
these purposes. Concrete made from lime is sometimes used
to provide a waterproof covering for flat roofs made of timber
or of stone slabs, as in Rajasthan in India. Mass concrete is
distinguished from mortar by the size ofthe stone or aggregate
used and the proportioning ofthe materials. Mortars usually are
made with sand or stone ofless than 5 mm (0.2 in) diameter, and
contain a higher proportion of the cementing agent than concretes.

Unreinforced concrete cracks easily both as a result of
stresses caused by loading, and because concrete shrinks as it
hardens; so for most applications it is better if it can be reinforced. For structural applications reinforcement is essential.
Reinforced concrete is a relatively new material, dating only
from the latter part ofthe ıgth century in Europe and America,
when both cement and suitable reinforcing steel began to be
widely available. In the zoth century, the manufacture ofboth
materials has grown enormously worldwide and, as a result of
its comparative cheapness and the efficiency with which it can
be used, reinforced concrete has become a part ofmany vernacular building traditions and in many areas has totally replaced
older ones. ROBIN SPENCE
1.V.6.d-i Concrete: reinforced
Reinforced concrete is an engineering material, and
is used worldwide for buildings which are the products ofthe
modern construction industry. In these buildings engineers
and architects are employed, design calculations are made and
the building is required to conform to standards and building
regulations. But reinforced concrete is also used increasingly
commonly for ordinary dwellings, built by owner-builders or
craftsmen-builders, without reference to formal standards or
codes of practice. The builders use nominal rules, derived from
experience or local tradition, for the proportioning of mixes,
the sizing ofthe members and the distribution ofthe reinforcement, in just the way timber and masonry buildings are tradi-

265

## S. 83

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

tionally designed. Thus, concrete has become a part of, or the
main material used in, many 2oth-century vernacular architectural traditions. This is particularly true in areas where earthquakes are common, because the tensile strength which can be
achieved with a well-made concrete frame provides an excellent
protection from collapse during ground shaking.

The use of reinforced concrete as a vernacular material is
thus particularly strong in earthquake areas, especially where
there is now a shortage of timber, the material traditionally
used to provide the tensile strength needed for earthquake protection. Reinforced concrete frames (often filled with masonry
materials forming the walls) are now very common in Greece
and Turkey, in urban building in the Philippines and in the
earthquake-prone cities near the Pacific coasts ofLatin America
-Mexico Cityand Lima, for example.

The aggregates used for concrete are most commonly sands
and gravels from rivers or from beach sources but in some areas
as these sources get used up they are being replaced by crushed
rock. Cement is usually Portland cement, in most countries made
in local factories and supplied in paper or jute bags. Reinforcement is in the form ofround steel bars, which are often supplied
bent double, to reduce their length for transport. Formwork, in
which the concrete will be cast, is in the form ofrough-sawn timber boards. In areas where the reinforced concrete vernacular is
strong, all these materials can commonly be purchased from a
local building materials supplier or workshop. The local workshop may also manufacture for sale to individual housebuilders
various concrete products, such as concrete blocks, paving slabs
or preformed lintels to span window and door openings in
masonty structures. Some more sophisticated workshops also
supply water tanks and cisterns in concrete. These are sometimes
made from ferro-cement, a concrete reinforced with severallayers
ofsmall-diameter steel mesh.

The process of making an in situ concrete frame for a singleor two-storey house can be carried out entirely manually. Concrete is mixed using hand shovels, transported and placed
using barrows or head baskets, and compacted into place
around the reinforcement by rodding. It is then covered with
jute sacks or polythene and left while it sets and gains strength
for a few days before the formwork is removed.

Perfectiy adequate concrete can be made in this way, but
much of the concrete used in vernacular building is of rather
poor quality, because the process is as yet not well understood.
Common practices which lead to poor quality concrete include
allowing the steel or the aggregates to become contaminated
with earth; using insufficient quantities ofcement or too much
water; constructing formwork which leaks allowing cement
paste to escape during casting leaving void spaces; allowing
reinforcement to be too close to the face ofthe concrete, where
it can rust; not providing adequate overlaps between bars; and
not keeping the concrete surface wet for long enough to allow
the concrete to set properly. Collapse of reinforced concrete
frame buildings because of poor construction practice has
become common in some cities, and poorly constructed con-

crete buildings have been found to be particularly vulnerable to
earthquakes. ROBIN SPENCE

266

Concrete block

Concrete block is perhaps one of the most widely
produced building materials in the world. The materials are
usually readily available, the process is simple and the blocks
can be produced ata small scale- either as a small business or
at the building site. Well-made concrete blocks will provide a
strong and durable wall with thermaland sound insulation, fire
and rain resistance, and a smooth finish.

The most common shape of concrete blocks is rectangular
with hollows in the centre. These spaces make the block lighter
and easier to grip and place during construction. The hollows
reduce the amount of raw materials needed in production,
improve insulation and provide a space for reinforcement. Decorative blocks are also used, usuallyto create an attractive opening in a wall which provides light and ventilation without the
need for burglar-proofing.

Special moulds are available to produce concrete bricks, tiles
and U-shaped blocks. The latter are used for lintels - the blocks
are placed on top ofthewindow-ordoor-frame and reinforeing
bars and concrete are placed inside the block to form a lintel.

Different countries produce different-sized concrete blocks,
but most are close to the following nominal sizes (length x
heightx width): 400% 200x200 mm (16x 8x8 in); 400% 200x
150 mm (16x 8x 6 in); 400x 200x 100 mm (I6x8x4in).

In an industrial process concrete blocks are made by mixing
sand or aggregate, Portland cement and water and placing the
mixture in a mould where it is compacted. The block is then
removed and cured to have its strength increased by being kept
moist for a number of days. On a non-industrial scale, blocks
are made either with machines or by hand. Block-producing
machines are usually powered by electricity or diesel fuel. The
cement/sand/aggregate mixture is poured into thebedofmoulds
where vibration and pressure are applied by the machine to
produce a very dense block. The blocks are demoulded onto the
floor and the machine is repositioned for the next operation.

However, moulding blocks by hand is the most common
method ofsmall-scale production. The shape and size ofmoulds
vary from country to country butthe process remains essentially

1.1V.6.e

Infilled reinforced
concrete frame
construction in Pont
the new vernacular
construction In

seealso ,
1.111.3.b Earth

Standard Concrete Block

Decorative Block U-Block

the same. A wooden pallet is put into the mould box, a mixture
ofsand and cement is shovelled into the mould, aworker compacts the mixture by poundingitwith a piece ofwood orametal
tool, and the block is then removed from the mould.

As in the industrial process, after the block is released from
the mould, ideally it is cured by being covered and kept moist
for 7-10 days. After this period the block is ready for use in construction.

Production levels vary enormously between countries and
even between production sites, depending primarily on the availability of materials, the layout and organization of the workspace and the work-pace ofthe workers.

Produeing cement blocks locally or on a small scale can
result in a high-quality product. However, it is not uncommon
for the blocks to be weak and misshapen because ofinaccurate
measuring, careless handling and inadequate curing.

Concrete blocks are most commonly used in the construction ofthe main building structure-as a foundation materialas
wellas awalling material that is either plastered, painted or left
exposed. The 20 cm (8 in) wide blocks are most often used for
foundations, load-bearing walls, retaining walls, columns and
piers while the ı5 cm (6 in) orıocm (4 in) wide blocks are used
for non-bearing partition walls.

Blocks are also used for non-structural purposes such as
stairs, furniture, walkways and fences.

ANNE BEAMISH and WILL DONOVAN

Used and Manufactured 1.1.6
11V.6f Corrugated iron
Corrugated iron was first produced in England
around 1825. Itwas formed from sheet iron impressed mechanically so as to present a surface of semicircular ridges with
intervening furrows. Sheet iron had already been produced
since the late-r8th century in England. The sheets were formed
from iron bars in rolling mills.

The progress ofthe industrial revolution brought with it expansion in the construction industry, for there was an increasing
demand for much larger industrial buildings. The traditional
materials of bricks and stone provided the essential materials
for walls butthe traditional roofing materials oftiles and slates
were not suitable for the large spans of these new buildings.
Also, the traditional sheet roofing materials ofcopper and lead
were expensive and inappropriate for utility buildings. So as a
direct result, sheet iron came to be used for roofs, not onlyin
Britain but also in Russia and in America in the early part ofthe
19th century.

The first patent concerned with the corrugation of iron
sheets was granted to HenryR. Palmer, aLondon civil engineer,
in 1829. Palmer did not claim as his invention any particular
mode offorming the plates or sheets. Apparently the means of
producing such forms was already well known; it had by that
time been used for roofing in the London docks where Palmer
worked. However, he claimed originality for its application to
roofs and walls.

The advantages of corrugated iron over flat iron were significant. The corrugation ofthe material gives itan inherentstrength
and rigidity that it is not possible to obtain in sheet form. The
corrugation of sheet iron was an ingenious invention in itself,
butalmost at once it was found that by curving the sheet it could
be connected with other sheets to form a self-supporting roof.

The most significant legacy ofcorrugated iron is in its use for
portable buildings. The material was ideally suited to prefabrication in terms of its workable-sized sheets, its relative lightness, and its strength and compactness.

There were in the ıgth century a few pioneering manufacturers of portable buildings. By the 1840s several of the leading
firms were producing catalogues of their own work and then
advertising them in the journals ofthe day.

A few firms developed a definitive style in their buildings and
some firms specialized in producing particular types of buildings, for example, churches and chapels which are still standing
in rural parishes in England. The pioneering firms produced
some unique structures which attracted considerable public
interestatthe time. Illustrations ofthe buildings were regularly
featured in The Builder journal.

One such firm, John Walker, in 1849 (the year ofthe Californjan goldrush) shipped to San Francisco a corrugated iron storehouse, two storeys in height and 23 m (75 ft) long by ı2 m (40
ft) wide. In 1853 his firm built a large two-storey house of gal-

vanized corrugated iron in Chagres, Panama, for the Royal Mail
Steam Packet Company, which impressed the correspondent of
The Builder.

William Laycock of Liverpool, another pioneering firm, produced a prefabricated house in Africa for King Eyambo in 1843,
which also greatly impressed the journalists ofthe time. John

267

## S. 84

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

Porter of London exported buildings to Hong Kong, the West
Indies, Ceylon and Russia. One of his more unusual buildings
was a Market House for San Fernando, Trinidad. It was 24 m
(80 ft) longand 4 m (13 ft) high.

Following the success of these pioneer manufacturers, the
corrugated iron industry expanded considerably and corrugated iron buildings became a common sight. In the decade
1880-90 the industry was probably at its peak. The range of
buildings produced during these years was prodigious; it
included dwellings, industrial buildings, railway buildings,
public halls, pavilions and churches. ı

However, by the end oftthe ıgth century there were indications of disenchantment with the material. This was primarily
due to the poor quality ofsome ofthe sheets which led to rapid
corrosion.

In the early years ofthe 2oth century the new mass-produced
materials such as steel, concrete and corrugated asbestos

emerged as competitors to corrugated iron.
PAUL M. S. DADSON

Corrugated iron (Australia)
Building in corrugated iron has achieved a vernacular character in Australia notwithstanding the fact that the
material is a manufactured one, and until well into the ‚zoth
century was totally imported. Itis used for industrial buildings,
for complete houses in remote areas, more generally for höuse
-and especially veranda - roofing, and for the cylindrical rainwater tank. Smaller pitch ‘ripple iron’ has been used more for
ceilings and internal linings and generally in more tropical
areas. Itis not associated with any particular ethnic or cultural
group, except to the extent that remote locations have developed their own subculture (the Stubby and Thong peoples).
There are a handful of references to corrugated iron in Aus:
tralia during the 1830s, when it was still subject to he original
British patent ofH. R. Palmer, and was not galvanized. Galvanized iron first made its appearance in 1850 in the form of
imported British roofing tiles, but galvanized corrugated Iron
followed very quickly. The material had been used in buildings
sent to California in 1849, and enormous numbers ofsuch pre‘
fabricated buildings reached Victoria from the major English
ports from 1852 (whereas Glasgow was more responsible for
buildings of cast iron and Edinburgh for buildings ofzine).
The importation of prefabricated buildings had reduced
markedly by 1854, but corrugated iron remained henceforward
a part of the builder’s lingua franca. It was encouraged for
roofing in urban areas by building regulations which prohibited inflammable materials like thatch and shingles, for shop
awnings by by-laws which began to specifyitin place of American duck from 1859, but most of all for building in remote
areas by reason ofits compactness for transport and its relative
lightness. .
Corrugated curving machines were available in the colonies
from the 1850s, and they enabled the standard veranda to be
formed on a concave profile; subsequently ogee and convex
profiles were used, and then, around the turn of the century,
bullnose. In 1857 John Carter of Melbourne received a patent
for the corrugated iron rain-water tank, which was to become

1.1V.6.f-i

268

IH

|| | nl

VESE

VONAR BLENSTION

TRANS

ubiquitous in rural areas. Curved iron roofs were used in industrial buildings everywhere, and in South Australia and inland
areas where timber was short these roofs were also used for
railway stations, barns, shearing sheds and houses.

The main English brands, Morewood & Rogers, Porter & Co,
Walkers & Holland, Tupper & Co, Gospel Oak and others
increasingly gave way to that ofJohn Lysaght of Bristol, who
took a special interest in the Australian market (by contrast
with South Africa, which was dominated by George Adams &
Co ofthe Mars works, Wolverhampton). Lysaght established
agencies, then branches, and finally began to manufacture in

- Australia inıg21. Smaller pitch ripple iron, typically of 25 mm

(1 in) was extensively used in Chile before it had any impact in
Australia. Apart from one example in 1867, it was first used in
1888 and only achieved general acceptance in the 1890s, and
then almost entirely for internal linings.

The corrugated iron roofs of houses were commonly
finished at the corner with sheet metal acroteria (derived ultimately from Greek Revival sources), which rarely survive today.
In northern Queensland houses it is associated also with ornamental roof vents, frilly valences and window awnings 2
achieve a highly decorative effect. However, the distinctive
impact of corrugated iron is in the more remote inland areas,
