were its relative light weight and compactness gave it advantages in transport over other manufactured products. While
it is generally used in a utilitarian way, it achieves grandeur
in the larger shearing sheds, notably Cordillo Downs, South

Australia. MILES LEWIS

1.1V&g Fibre-cement

Fibre-cement is most widely used in the production
ofcorrugated roofing materials. Asbestos-cement roofing production is a well-established industry, but health concerns over
the use ofasbestos have caused the industry to find alternative
types of fibre. Since the mid-19708 research has focused on
techniques for producing fibre-cement roofing tiles and sheets
on a small scale. These techniques have spread quickly where
they have been introduced in Africa, India, the Caribbean, Asia
and Central America, and have become accepted building
materials in many areas for roofing housing, schools, clinics
and community buildings in both rural and urban locations.

Fibre-cement products are made with concrete mortar and

reinforced with a fibre. A variety of fibres can be used such as
flax, banana bark, coconut husk, polypropylene and glass
fibres. However, sisal is the most promising vegetable fibre
because of its properties, cost and ayailability. The main purpose of the fibre is to hold the concrete mortar together while
the sheet or tile is being shaped and to help reduce cracking
during dıying.

Fibre-cement roofing products come ina variety of corrugations and are made either in sheet or tile form. Roofing sheets
have the advantage that they require less timber in the roof
structure and can be used on roofs with a smaller slope, but
they also have a greater tendency to shrink during production
and break more easily during transportation and construction.
The smaller tiles have the advantage that they are easier to produce, suffer less shrinkage and cracking, are lighter and easier
to transport and use in construction, and can more easily
accommodate irregularities in the roof structure. However,
they do require somewhat more timber in the roof structure
and need a slightly steeper slope than the larger roofing sheet.

In addition to the sheets or tiles, fibre-cement can also be

used to produce ridge-caps, gutters and tubes. In recent years
there has also been some experimentation into using fibrecement to produce window-frames. Roofing sheets and tiles
can be made into almost any shape or size. However, roofing
sheets are most commonly 700x 1000 mm (27.5 x 39.5 in)and
8-10 mm (0.3-0.4 in) thick. Roofing tiles are usually 500x250
mm (20x 1Ioin)and6mm (0.25 in) thick.

There are four basic steps in the Production of fibre-cement
tiles or sheets: mixing, vibration/compaction, moulding and
euring. For tiles, one part of cement, three parts ofsand and
1.25 per cent of the weight of sand and cement in fibre are
mixed together with water, the fibre having been chopped into
short lengths and mixed in with the cement mixture. Handtamped roofsheets have a sand to cement ratio loser toI:ıand
the fibres are not mixed with the cementand sand butare added
atthe second, compaction, stage.

For tiles, the sand/cement/fibre mixture is then placed on a
plastic sheet in a rectangular frame where it is vibrated to

ER EEE EEE

Used and Manufactured 1.16
remove any air pockets. The vibration is done manually, or bya
battery-powered motor or bya manually vibrated frame. Larger
roofing sheets are produced by trowelling the sand and cement
mixture into a thin layer; the fibres are laid on top and pressed
in using a roller. Another layer of sand and cement mixture is
trowelled on and the process is repeated. Normally there are
three layers ofconcrete and two layers offibre.

The sheet of plastic with the mixture of sand, cement and
fibre is placed on a corrugated mould where the tile or roof
sheet is left dry for a day. In the final stage the tile or roofing
sheet is removed from the mould and is placed in a tank of
water for 10-14 days, after which it is removed from the tank
and placed in the shade for 7-10 days. Itisthen readyto beused
in construction. Fibre-cement tiles are best suited to sloping
roofs that have an angle greaterthan 23° (oraslope greaterthan
1:2). Roofing sheets can tolerate a somewhat smaller angle. A
tile is attached to the batten by being nailed either through a
hole in the nib or through a wire hook built into the nib. The
tiles or sheets are overlapped slightly to prevent rain penetration. Care must be taken by the roofers notto walk on the tiles
or sheets and cause breakage or eracking. Cement mortar is
used as a flashing in hips and ridges.

ANNE BEAMISH and WILL DONOYAN

1.V.6.h Glass

Glass, a transparent or transluscent vitreous, nonerystalline material, was probably first made in Mesopotamia
4000 years ago. Its use was limited to the making ofimitation
beads and ornaments, and eventually, to the making of vessels.
Allforms ofglass consistofa combination ofsand or silica and
soda ash which become a viscous liquid when heated to
approximately 1500 °C (2730 °P), at which stage it can be
moulded or blown. Impurities and trace elements of minerals
can beintroduced, eithertoaddtoits brilliance, as with lead, or
to impart colour to the mixture. Though largely the result of
accident, beautiful variations of colour could be achieved,
which were made intense and luminous when light was passed
through the glass.

The earliest glass in the British Isles was imported from

France, but local industry began in Chiddingfold in 1230.
Because of the labour and skills involved glass was not widely
used in building for several centuries, except in churches and
cathedrals where stained glass windows were extensively
employed. Pieces of glass were joined together with cames, or
strips oflead, rebated so as to receive the edges ofthe glass and
joined to iron saddle-bars and stanchions within stone mullions. Slowly glass was used for domestic windows, rectangles
or diamonds of glass being arranged inanetofcames attached
to iron casement frames; the resultant leaded lights’ produced
were fully glazed windows within pleasing geometric patterns
oflines.

Sheet glass was produced by blowing a molten globe which
was then attached to a pinti, or metal lance. The punctured
globe was spun rapidly on the pinti during reheating and
annealing, until it flared or burst centrifugally on a flat disc.
The effect was to produce thin glass atthe perimeter ofthe disc
and a lump at the centre. The thin, brilliant ‘crown glass’ was

269

## S. 85

EEE

11V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
cut in small pieces for use in the windows of the affluent; the
thicker glass, with curved ripples still evident, was used in the
windows of the less wealthy, while the central bull’s-eye was
used in panes which could admit light but did not permit clear
vision. These panes became virtually synonymous with the use
of glass in the rural vernacular.

Developed by medieval glassblowers in Normandy, the
‘crown glass’ method was introduced in Britain late in the ızth
century and was still occasionally used by Whitefriars glassworks until the 19508. Medieval German glassmakers invented
amethod ofproducing sheet glass by elongatinga blown globe
to a cylinder which was then cut, and on reheating opened out
flat. This, too, was introduced in England several centuries
later. In the North American colonies Dutch glass factories
were established in New Amsterdam (later, New York) in the
mid-r7th century, and a generation later Richard Wister was
advertising window glass in five standard sizes.

Late in the 18th century cast plate glass was developed in
England, when sheets over 1.8 m (6 ft) wide and twice as long
became possible. Glass had been repeatedly subjeetto taxation,
butthe end ofexcise duties on glass in 1845 and the final repeal
ofwindow tax six years later opened the way for industrialized
production. Plate glass made large panes for windows possible, meeting an increasing demand in shops and factories for
windows with uninterrupted glazing. But until the 2oth century
there was little further development in the methods of production ofglass, and most vernacular contexts in which ithad been
used did not require large panes.

Glass sheets can easily be fixed into window-frames, their
edges sealed with putty to permit room for the different
coefficients of expansion. The material is brittle but often has
an unexpected elasticity which makes it resistant even to high
winds. Even in some desertclimates glass has been used, as for
the non-opening but brilliantly coloured pierced lattices of
Jerusalem and Cairo and the lunettes in Yemen. Nevertheless, it
is in the cooler and temperate latitudes rather than the tropics
where glass has been most needed, and in places where
colonists took their living styles with them. As a resource it has
never been readily made by vernacular craft workers but has
been a product of highly specialized skills, often developed
under patronage. Butits commercial production in many countries has brought illumination and climate protection to all
levels of society. PAUL OLIVER

270

1.1V.6.1 Gypsum plaster

Gypsum plaster is a cementitious material which is
derived from various forms of calcium sulphate, the most common of which is generally known as gypsum. Gypsum plaster
has four main advantages over alternative cementitious materials: it does not shrink when it dries, so plastered walls do not
crack and fine decorative details may be achieved; it dries and
hardens quickly so a second coat may be applied promptly; itis
naturally fire-resistant; and in its manufacture the raw gypsum
need be heated only for a short time to a temperature of only
around 160 °C (320 °F), whereas lime requires go0-1100 °C
(1652-2012 °F) and Portland cement needs 1450 °C (2642 °F).
Its principal disadvantages are low structural strength and its
solubility; a Portland cement render may be readily used externally in damp conditions while gypsum plaster cannot.

Raw gypsum occurs as both rock and sand. The bulk of the

deposits are in rock form butin North Africa there are extensive
surface deposits of gypsum sand. Since the temperatures
needed to produce gypsum plaster are so low, the quantity of
fuel and the sophistication of the technology required are also
low. Where gypsum sand is available, the plaster may be produced on a tray over an open fire. In parts of Ladakh, in northern India, householders reported making plaster over their
domestic house fires. In the Cape Verde islands an artisan
industry has developed in which the kiln is a simple box with
limestone walls and a sheet of steel for a lid. A fire is lit in the
kiln and the gypsum sand is cooked on the lid. Rock gypsum
may be fired in open pyres. Lumps of gypsum are placed in a
pile and a bonfire is made over it. After a few days the pyre is
broken up and the plaster removed. In the Touggourt region of
Algeria dugoutkilns are formed by digginga pitand puttingthe
gypsum and the fuel into it. Where rock gypsum deposits are on
the surface a depression may be dug and a fire set within it. A
layer of plaster is formed on the surface ofthe depression. The
plaster which results from these pyres is inevitably contaminated and so is known as ‘ash plaster’. In North Africa it is
known as timchent or goss. Larger-scale production is achieved
with a shaft kiln; typically, in a quarty, an open-topped shaft
will be built against a rock face with an eye at the base through
which a fire is fed. Through the use of larger, walled kilns a
more pure form of plaster, such as plaster of Paris, may be produced. The walled kilns were developed in 17th-century France
after municipal ordinances in Paris required the timber frames
ofhouses to be protected from fire with gypsum plaster.

The prineipal traditional use of gypsum plaster is asarender.
An assortment ofadditives may be used in order to manipulate
the plaster’s properties. A common retarder, to slow down the
setting process, is keratin which may be obtained from hoofs
and horns. Keratin needs to be used in conjunetion with lime in
order to be effective, whereas sugar and molasses are among a
range ofretarders which do not. Historical reports indicate the
use ofbull’s blood, egg whites, residues from beer production
and urine among other things. In Paris gypsum plaster was
made more suitable for external use by adding lime. In Peru an
external render (diablo) has evolved of five parts gypsum plaster
to one part of Portland cement. Fibres, such as horsehair, are
often added, particularly to strengthen decorative plasterwork.

The fine stable surface of gypsum plaster provided the technological contextwhich spawned the art offresco painting. Plaster
may also be carved. In prestigious buildings in Arab countries
craftsmen will apply the plaster in thin layers and before each
layer is fully set they will beat it with a stone. The technique is
extremely time-consuming but the result is a hard and durable
surface which can take fine Islamic carving. More commonly,
gypsum plaster is used for decorations such as window surrounds. Gypsum plaster has also been widely used as a mortar.
For regular masonry the characteristic of quick drying makes
gypsum plaster unattractive, though not impossible. However,
for the construction ofdomes and vaults without formwork itis
ideal, since each brick or stone must be rapidly able to support
the weight ofthe next layer. ERIC DUDLEY
Lime
Atraditional building material for 2000 years, lime has
been avital ingredient in mortar, plaster, render and solid floors
and a hygenic paint for animal and human shelters alike. It is
still of paramount importance in building conservation where
modern materials cannot successfully be used as a substitute.
Most non-hydraulic building lime in Europe is produced by
burning limestone, although other forms of caleium carbonate
(e.g. marble or coral) are used in some parts ofthe world. Traditionally the burning was done in a wood- or coal-fired lime
kiln, specially constructed for the purpose, although simpler
methods were also used. Aminimum of880 °C (1616 °F) is necessary. During burning, carbon dioxide and water are driven off
and the resulting ‘super-dry’ product is known as ‘quicklime’

1.1V6J

Used and Manufactured 1.1.6
(calcium oxide). The quicklime is then slaked: combined with
water, the dry lime boils vigorously before subsiding into a
smooth, thick liquid which is called ‘lime putty’ (calcium hydroxide). This is then run offinto a pit, viaa series ofsieves, where it
settles and is stored. Provided it does not dry out and a layer of
lime-water is maintained on top of it, this material will last
indefinitely, improving with age as any tiny, unslaked particles
in itwill slake themselves during storage. Lime that is used too
young is prone to excess shrinking, cracking and ‘blows’- small
explosions caused by unslaked particles. When sufficiently
mature lime is used for building it will form a tough mortar or
plaster which will be permeable, allowing moisture to evaporate, and permitting the building to breathe.

In use, this non-hydraulic lime hardens both by drying out,
and by re-absorbing carbon dioxide from the air. This process
of carbonation continues for a long time (i.e. a matter of years
in mortar joints), progressively increasing the strength of the
material. As the lime must be exposed to carbon dioxide (in the
atmosphere) for this to take place, mortarjoints, render or plaster must not be used too deeply or applied too thickly. Ancient
buildings have been found to have lime-mortar still soft in the
core ofthick walls after hundreds ofyears.

To overcome the restrictions imposed by the need for exposure to carbon dioxide, various additives have been used with
lime to enable itto make its ‘set’ without contact with air. These
are generallytermed pozzolanic additives. Used by the Romans,
the volcanic ash at Pozzuoli in Italy gave its name to material
used in this way. Other pozzolanic additives include crushed
brick, tile or pottery. These materials do not adversely affectthe
porous nature of lime-mortar or render, whereas the common
modern practice ofadding cement does.

Hydraulic lime is documented in Roman texts, but its use
appears to have ceased until the 18th century. In simplified
terms, itis lime produced from limestone containinga proportion ofclay. When burnt with the limestone, the clay becomes a
pozzolanic material, already incorporated with the quicklime.

As this lime will harden without exposure to air, it is either
slaked in small quantities and used immediately, or kept as
hydrated lime, a dry powder.

Hydrated lime is often the most commonly available form of
lime and is quicklime which has been slaked, with aminimum
of water, to a powder. Itis used mainly as an extender and filler
in cement mixes although it is possible to make a lime-mortar
from it after soaking and thorough mixing. Although a set mix

## S. 86

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
containing lime putty and one made with hydrated lime are
chemically similar, in practice the lime putty mix is the better of
the two, having made its set where the strength is required, i.e.
on the wall.

After use, all lime becomes chemically similar to the stone
from which it originated. JANE SCHOFIELD

oil drums

Of recycled materials there are few that have been
more widely used than commercial oil drums. For the distribution and export of gasoline and crude oils during much of the
20th century, thevolume and dimensions ofthe 45-gallon drums
were standardized for ease of transportation. Often rolled on
ramps from the back of vehicles and then manhandled, the
drums had to be impact-resistant; the use ofribs in the section of
the sheet gave added strength to the sheet metal from which it
was made, and served as rollers for ease ofmovement.

With one or both ends removed oil drums have been used as
trash cans, as linings for narrow tunnels, as sections of widegauge drainage or sewer pipes and for similar functions that
exploit their welded tubular form. These uses necessitated the
removal ofthe ends, either by cutting outthe dises, orby eutting
through thesidewall. With thetop discretainedand thesidewall
cut, the ends of the drums became effective percussion instruments in the 1940s among the ‘steel band’ musicians ofTrinidad.
The heads were beaten with mallets until they stretchedand sarık
into a shallow concave, the steel being tempered on bonfires of
rubber tyres and discarded timber. At much the same time the
squatter residents of unauthorized settlements discovered the
potential ofthe oil drum as a significant building component.

Where oil drums were firstused for building is notestablished

with certainty, but it seems likely that it was in Casablanca,
Morocco. The oil drums were known as bidons in the Francophone countries and gave their name to the bidonvilles which were
built with them. The drums had to be cut so that the top was
removed and the side wall cut from top to bottom. Hacksaws
were used where they were obtainable, but the most widely
used tool was a leaf from truck or automobile springs. The leafspring was heated, beaten and sharpened on one side to produce a cutting edge, but with both ends left uncut to provide
handles. The blade would then be struck with a mallet or hammer to cut through the sheet steel. Opened out and flattened,
thedrum produced asheetapproximately 18m (6füxım@ fü
which made a comparatively light but impervious modular
building component, which could be nailed to a frame of
‘2x 48”. Though a one-room house took more than two dozen
oil drums to clad, the resultant dwelling could be durable,
impermeable and secure. Even ifthe interiors were hotaand airless, the metal took paintwell, and the surface could be painted
white to reflect the light and heat ofthe sun.

While the supply ofoil drums was continuous itwas possible
for whole settlements to be built ofthe sheet metal. They were
so much in evidence in the 1960s that it was rumoured that the
multinational oil companies were producing surplus drums
especially for the bidonvilles. Though itwas probably apocryphal,
some support for the rumour was given by the companies who
recognized the popularity of the steel bands, some of whose

names - Amoco Renegades, Iscott Casablanca and Witco Desperadoes - reflected their sponsorship. When the drums were
in shortsupply the smaller kerosene or paraffin cans were used;
easier to cutbutrequiring more fixings, they were often used as
metal shingles. PAUL OLIVER
1.1V.6| Plastic sheeting

Since it was first introduced, plastic sheeting has
been used for a variety of purposes. It has been widely used for
emergency housing and informal shelter as well as protection
for crops and animals. Both new and recycled, it is light,
strong, widely available and relatively inexpensive.

Plastic sheeting is made from a number ofmaterials, including
nylon, polyvinyl chloride (PVC), and Terylene, but poly-propylene
is the most common. Plastic sheeting, reinforced with fibre, is
also available; it is heavier and much stronger than the standard
plastic sheet, but is not suitable in situations where it will be
exposed to long periods ofdirectsunlightand high temperatures.

Clear or translucent plastic is resistant to water and chemicals, but becomes weaker and less flexible when exposed to
sunlight. To create shade and prevent deterioration ofthe plastic, the sheeting is covered with another material, sandwiched
between two materials, painted with a mud slurry or sprayed
with aluminium or light-coloured paint. Black-coloured sheeting is also resistantto waterand chemicals but, unlike the clear

The lime cycle,

1.1%.7.2-i Kiln: Iime“

See also for 1.l

References for,
‚Chatburn, Thoma A
Foster, Badi 6,
Pawley, Martin,

sScantling frame!
clad with flattel
drums (b/dons),
discs beneath
Parama, Corfu:

ı1r7

ral Disasters

im, and Spice, P,

type, it does not break down in sunlight. Deterioration of
plastic is accelerated when the sheeting is in contact with a
metal structure or any material that absorbs heat, but this is
solved by protecting the plastic from the sunlight either with
paint or with strips ofblack plastic tape.

Plastic sheeting remains flexible down to sub-freezing temperatures but at temperatures above 80 °C (176 °F), it starts to
soften and weaken, and will melt at ı15 °C (239 °F). Another
very useful characteristic is its impermeability to moisture and
ability to keep outrain, but this feature can also cause moisture
build-up within a shelter unless adequate ventilation is provided. Because plastic sheeting has no insulative properties,
cooler exterior temperatures can cause condensation on the
inside of the walls. However, this is overcome by creating a
panel with two sheets, filled with an insulating material.

Plastic sheeting ranges in thickness from 12 microns (50
gauge) to 375 microns (1500 gauge) and comes in widths up to
ıım (36 ft). Emergency housing usually requires plastic sheeting in the 1000-1500 gauge range. However, homeowners
often will use whatever thickness they can find for use in animal
shelters, or temporary repairs to their own homes. As well as
being used for emergency housing, plastic sheeting is used in
the construction of conventional housing in cold climates.
When a plastic membrane is part of the wall construction, it
prevents the warm moist air from the house from passing
through the wall where it could freeze and cause damage.

Plastic sheeting is a very versatile material and is widely used
in squatter settlements and informal housing. Homeowners
frequently re-use the large sheets of plastic that are used in
packaging oflarge goods such as mattresses and incorporate it
into their houses as wind and rain barriers. The plastic is used
in the walls, placed at roof joints to prevent leaking, and used
as door and window coverings. It is also used to form temporary shelters during the rainy season for outdoor cooking or for
protecting crops and animals. Though somewhat less useful in
housing, smaller plastic sheeting from plastic shopping bags is
also used for repairs to walls, roofs and windows.

ANNE BEAMISH and WILL DONOVAN

1.1V.6.m Plywood

Though it has seldom been held in high regard plywood has been extensively used in construction since the beginning.ofthe 2oth century. Itis nota new material; wood laminates
were found in some tombs of ancient Egypt, and they were
employed in furniture making over several centuries as veneers.
Modern plywood has a history of more than a century, ‘scaleboards’ being developed in the late ıgth century by John Mayo of
Maine, who invented cross-ply laminates. These were applied to
pianos, sewing machines, desk tops and in 1890, to doors. The
Schaumann mills ofJyväskyla, Finland, claim to be the firstto put
the new material into commercial production, while in the
United States the problem ofasuitable adhesive was metwith the
development of soybean glue by I. F. Laucks Inc. of Seattle.
Employed in the manufacture of aircraft in World War I and of
automobiles afterwards, the term ‘plywood’ replaced the slightly
Pejorative ‘veneer’, the industry becoming organized with the
formation ofthe Plywood Manufacturers Association in 1921.

Used and Manufactured 1.1.6
Plywood makes efhicient use ofwood, the timber that would
otherwise be wasted often being utilized in its manufacture. In
principle it is made in sheet form with thin layers laid over each
other and glued. As wood can be weak at right angles to the
grain, the grains ofalternate layers ofplyare laidatgo°, orat45°
to each other, thus imparting great structural strength to a relatively thin material. Plywoods are made ofthree, five, seven or
more odd layers, so that the external layers are laid with the
grain in the same direction, with three-ply being ofremarkable
strength in the direction ofthe external grain.

Barked logs are revolved and cutin a lathe to produce continuous sheet material whose thickness is from one-sixteenth to
one-quarter ofan inch (1.6-6.4 mm); it is then dried in ovens.
Imperfections such as knots or holes are patched and filled and
waste material is used for paper or particle board. After being
stacked with the grains laid as described the sheets are glued
and pressed. Finnish birch, Canadian Douglas fir and a variety
ofsoftwoods are used.

Plywood can behave structurally as timber and, combined
with framing, it makes an excellent cladding material. Often it
is used as an insulating layer of boarding over balloon framing,
with a cladding board laid over it. It can be rebated for use as
tongue and groove flooring, or as a subfloor layer below conventional floorboards. Plywoods were much used in the manufacture ofmobile homes, their cheapness, lightness yetimpactresistant properties suiting the economy ofthe industry and its
market. Heavy-duty Douglas fir plywood is also used for formwork for cast concrete; unplaned it can impart a strong texture
to the concrete after it has set. After the formwork has been
removed the heavy plywood may be taken to materials depots
for recycling, entering the domain ofthe squatter settlements.
Also greatly soughtafter in these settlements were the large (am
[6.5 ft] diameter) heavy Douglas fir ply drums from which electrieity cables were laid.

Until containerization ofinternational freight changed transport practice, one ofthe principal uses ofplywood was for the
making of packing cases, while smaller sizes were used for teachests. The plys were ofrelatively crude quality, buttheyhadthe
same merits—structural strength, large dimensions, durability
and cheapness. Plywoods can be handled easily and nailed close
to the sheet edge. They have thus been valued in illegal settlements where ease ofeerection by unskilled labour is important.
Plywoods depend on the strength and effective bonding ofthe
adhesives used, ‘weather and boil proof’ (WBP) synthetic resins
being regarded as the mostresistantto delamination as aresult
of exposure. Most are vulnerable to wood beetle and termites
and, ifuntreated, will buckle or delaminate in areas with heavy
rainfall. Such factors are ofless concern to the poorest builders,
for whom any plywood sheets are desirable.

Since the ı960s a variety of hardboards, particle boards,
blockboards, laminboards, batten boards and chipboards have
entered the market; heavier and requiring strong framing
and more secure fixing, they have challenged the supremacy of
plywood. In the illegal settlements all materials are welcome,
but plywood best meets the needs of their builders even if
they have no voice in, or influence on, the industry.

PAUL OLIVER

273

## S. 87

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES
41.V.6.n Railway coaches
In every continent where railways penetrated,
freight wagons, trucks and box cars acquired secondary uses
when their mobile life ended. They are reported, for example,
from Mexico and from the Waggonstadt on the allotment gardens outside Berlin, where some remain, having been in use .
habitations since the 19205. Passenger coaches, with their
high-quality, durable joinery, were especially valued for recycling as homes. They could be found after the First World War
in the wastelands north of Paris, contrasting favourably with
less watertight dwellings on plots of100 sq m (1roo sq fi) sold
at two or three francs a square metre. They can still be seen in
the ‘plotlands’ of southeast England where, when coaches
became obsolete through railway improvements and amalgamations, they were de-wheeled and sold for £15 (19208 price)
and towed to the site by teams of six horses on the flat chassis
normally used for moving tree-trunks or telegraph poles.

A single coach (unless of the desirable corridor pattern)
would be added to, with a covered access way down one side, a
verandaatoneendandan earth closetatthe other. But the bestdeveloped variety of railway coach home consists of two
coaches, spaced 3-4 m (10-13 ft) apart, with either a linking
structure to form an H-shape, or a full-length ‘platform’, with
thewhole roofed over, providing a bigliving-room with a series
of compartments opening off it. The pitched roof would be
covered with corrugated iron or with diagonally laid asbestoscement slates.

Railway coaches were used as holiday, retirementorpermanent
homes. Owners would gain an income by renting them to: summer visitors. They were seen by conservationist propagandists as
asymbol ofthe despoliation ofthe shorelineand countiyside.

In the 19308 a major aim of the owners was to disguise the
origins of the smart white-painted bungalow with is twostorey glazed terrace at the front. The side walls were hidden by
pebble dashed cement rendering, so that only those who could

recognize the typical rhythm ofrailway windows would discern
the building’s origins. But with the cycle oftaste, these buildings once regarded as reprehensible, became first curious and
then valuable. Later inheritors have gone to much trouble to
strip off the disguise and to varnish and polish the original
woodwork and replace its decoration.

There have been instances where the new occupiers have
consulted railway historians to uncover their history and, wanting to redevelop the site, have sought a railway preservation
societyneeding old rolling-stock, to putthe coachwork back on
wheels to begin its third life. COLIN WARD

Sheet metal

Among cladding materials available to the vernacular builder in the 2oth century, sheet metal has a number of
advantages. Itis durable, itis relatively lightto handle and itcan
be fixed to frame structures with nails. Sheet metal doesnotrot,
and it is resistant to damage from fire and damp. Iron- and
steel-based sheet metal is subject to rusting but it can be protected by paint, ideally with a metal primer, undercoat and
gloss topcoat, though it rarely receives such treatment in the
vernacular as this adds considerably to the cost.

Sheet metal for cladding purposes was shipped from manufacturers to the Canadian prairie states before World War I. In
Latin America sheet metal had evident benefits; itwas imported
from Belgium by CostaRicaasa fire-resistant cladding material
after molten lava from active volcanoes had caused conflagrations. Itis likely that Belgian sheet metal was designed for usein
the Congo.

Apart from being used in flat panel form, sheet metal could
be impressed with patterns; indented and bevelled surfaces
simulated stone, and a valley detail imitated mortared joints.
These provided a flat surface for nailing while conveying the
three-dimensional effect ofstone blocks. Itwas also possible to
imitate clapboard, but generally materials with superior status

Daem u109 ©

vative builder coated heavy paper with pine-tar and sprinkled it

were chosen by users. In East Africa and Latin America sheet
metal for ceilings become popular in the ıg20s and 19305;
impressed with foliated patterns and distempered or painted
white, it appeared like laid and moulded plasterwork.

By the 1940s a variety of sheet metals, including aluminium,
zinc, copper and bronze, were marketed in Australia, though
sheet steel remained the most popular. Standard sizes of1.8x1
m (6x3 ft), 1.8x 0.6m (6x2 ft), ım (zft) square, and 0.6m (2
ft) square were available for ceilings, the dimensions reflecting
typical plans for the arrangement of ceiling joists, though, as
with wall cladding, the metal could be fixed to battens atany suitable intervals. Metal ceilings were much used for suspended
awnings or verandas and stock details including soffits, mouldings, cornices and decorative fascias could be obtained. With
the popularization of mobile homes in the 1960s, especially in
the United States, aluminium displaced other forms of sheet
metal for siding and ‘trim’. PAUL OLIVER
1.1V.6.p Tarpaper
Tarpaper is a heavy paper, traditionally of high rag
content, coated or impregnated with coal-tar or asphalt and
manufactured in rolls for building construction. Itis also more
formally called ‘roofing felt’. Three closely related materials
are: rolled asphalt roofing, a much heavier and thus more
durable product often finished with a mineral granule surface;
asphalt siding, saturated felt with mineral granules on the
exposed side, often in coloured and embossed patterns to simulate brick or stone, produced in rolls, shingles or panels, and
once popular as a covering for damaged wood siding; and corrugated sheet roofing, asphalt-impregnated felt sheets shaped
like corrugated galvanized steel and used as an economical substitute. Roofing feltweighs 5-13.5 kg (11-30 lb) persquare (9sq
m [roo sq ft] of surface). Roll roofing with a smooth surface
weighs 20-30 kg (43-54 1b) per square, with a granule surface
34.kg (75 Ib) for single ply application and 50-82 kg (10-180
Ib) for double coverage with rolls having granules on only half
the width. By contrast, asphalt shingles weigh 95-147 kg
(210-325 1b) per square. Rolls are typically go cm (36 in) wide,
11-44 m (36-144 ft) long and seldom weigh over 40 kg (90 Ib).
The first recorded use oftarpaper was in 1840 when an inno-

Used and Manufactured 1.1.6

with sand before applying itto aroof. Samuel Warren observed
this, and in the late 1840s he opened the first composition
roofing company in the United States in Cincinnati, Ohio,
using coal-tar supplied to him at no charge by the Cincinnati
Gas, Light and Coke Company. By 1850 coal-tar was being
refined from turning coal into coke to supply the blast furnaces
ofthe steel industry.

Prior to World War II, arag paper was coated on one side with
coal-tar or asphalt and run over a series of rollers while being
sprayed with more tar or asphalt. The paper was entirely saturated and all water was driven out through the uncoated side.
This, referred to as the Robinson method, produced a paper
that was waterproof. Since World War II the rag content ofthe
paper has been lowered and more cellulose, or more recently,
fibreglass, used in its place. Also, the processing has changed
to a dipping tank technique which traps moisture in the felt,
producing a water-resistant, but not waterproof, paper.

In spite of manufacturers recommending tarpaper for use
only as an underlay for roofing or siding, it is commonly used
as a final exposed surface in many vernacular building types
worldwide because of its ease of use, availability and low cost.
Its use is especially widespread in temperature climates where
weather-proofing involves providing protection from wind and
rain as opposed to providing walls with insulative qualities.

Tarpaper, asphalt roll roofing and corrugated felt sheets are
all highly susceptible to punctures and are therefore often
attached in vernacular buildings with large-head roofing nails
or through prise-off bottle caps. Specific examples of vernacularresidential and farm buildings usingtarpaper as an exposed
surface material can be found in the Appalachian region ofthe
United States, in most city ghetto areas, on the Indian reservations ofthe American West and throughout Central and South

## S. 88

1.1V MATERIALS AND BUILDING RESOURCES

America. When its use is expanded to that ofa finish’ material,
it often takes on a tattered appearance with age and exposure;
hence the colloquial phrase ‘tarpaper shack’ which refers to
substandard single dwellings.

The widespread availability and low price of plastic sheeting
have resulted in a decline in tarpaper use in recent years.

CHELLE WARFIELD
1.1V.6.g Tyres
For any material to be adopted by vernacular and
neoyernacular builders there has to be sufficient available for
continued use. This has been the case with automobile tyres
which have been re-used in a variety of ways throughout the
world. Some ofthese have been simple and specific; for instance,
as fend-offs for boats, or in sections as protective surfaces over
dockside piers. Tyres have been frequently employed as defensive barriers around young trees and their dead weight used to
hold down large sheets of black plastic covering silage. Countless children have satina tyre used as an improvised swing, while
others have swum in safety with an inner tube as a lifebelt.

In squatter settlements tyres are an important and adaptable
resource, holding down the rags and sacking over hundreds of
thousands of dwellings in Indian bustees. While the relatively
hard and heavy outer casing is most frequently used in situations where ameasure of protection is required, the inner tube,
with its more flexible and elastic composition, is adapted for a
wide range of domestic uses. In Asian settlements where lashings are used to hold structural elements together, permitting
flexible movement in the face of hurricanes and high winds,
long strips cut from the inner tubes make a strong lashing
material. Short sections of tube twisted and slipped over the
ends ofpoles make a rapid binding, kept in tension without the
need for a flatknot.

Other short pieces oftyre or inner tube are used as hinges for
hut doors, each end held with two, or preferably three, galvanized, large-headed nails driven into the door and the frame.
The elasticity ofthe inner tube is effectively exploited by cutting
spirally long and continuous strips. Some Kamba migrants to
Nairobi established a small furniture repair industry in the

Mathare valley squatter settlements, using the rubber strips for
reseating discarded tubular steel chair frames by interweaving
them. As durable as webbing and for the settler, much cheaper,
long strips were also interwoven to make substitutes for bed
springs. Many beds in the valley dwellings were not freestanding, butwere fashioned from lengths ofwood supported across
the span ofthe hutby the side framing; the strips were interwoven in situ.

With the increasing use of radial and tubeless tyres some of
the traditional uses were frustrated. But in settlements where
ingenuity and resourcefulness are fundamental for survival,

new uses are soon devised. PAUL OLIVER

## S. 89

1V PRODUCTION

Once materials have been procured and prepared the process of building is initiated.
The production of architecture may involve preparatory rituals and may be pursued in
accordance with custom; these are discussed under 1.v11.4 Rule Systems and Rituals.
All cultures have developed technical processes of construction that have to be followed
to maximize the potential ofthe materials within the competence and technology ofthe
builders. In subtle ways the Assembly (1.v.ı) ofbuildings may differ with each culture,
but from dimensioning to forming, from framing to infilling, from jointing to laying
and with intermediary or alternative stages, the procedures follow sequences that
ensure the integrity ofthe structures for their intended occupants.

Great responsibility is borne on the shoulders ofthe builders and skills have been
developed, sometimes with craft specialization, to achieve the desired standards of
production. Assembly is essentially process, and can best be demonstrated over time;
in lieu ofa more dynamic medium the broad principles of assembly are, exceptionally,
graphically illustrated in stages.

On completion ofthe structure the surfaces may be enveloped with an external
protective skin, and may be further lined within. Almost invariably the roof structure
will be over-laid with a weather-resistant cladding; often the structural frame or the
enclosing walls may have a covering material affixed to them. The external skin may
hide the structure within, to become the surface by which the building is largely
identified, in recognition ofwhich a finishing layer of plaster or paint may be applied.
Representative examples of Cladding and Finishes (1.v.2) are included.

After the construction ofthe building has been completed little further work may be
necessary before initial occupation. Rooms are rendered more habitable with pieces of
furniture, whose independence of the building enables them to be moved or removed at
will. Though largely ignored in the literature of furniture, items of Integral Furniture
and Fixtures (1.v.3), from beds to closets, are constructed with the building or are built
into its fabric. They both accord with, and define, customs of space use and
organization. Connections between the inhabited building and its environmental
context of climate and location are sustained by Openings and Fittings (1.v.4) in the
walls or roof. Including windows and doors, smoke-holes and ventilators, the openings
are released or secured by the fittings. Occasionally carved, they are more frequently
wrought in iron by local blacksmiths. Further functions of openings are illustrated
under 1.v1.4 Ventilation and Cooling.

Few buildings are constructed ofa single material; the majority are assembled from a
number of materials, with wooden frames supporting wattle and daub infill, roofs clad
in ceramic tile or grass thatch, mass walls of stone or fired brick supporting roof
structures ofjointed timber. The differentials in performance of materials under
compression and tension, in conditions ofheat and cold, humidity and aridity, or when

s

279

## S. 90

1.V

PRODUCTION

subjected to variable loads in use for living or storage, may not be calculated by the
builders. But the transmission of knowledge, techniques and craft skills, the
accumulated wisdom of generations, and the demands of cultural traditions have led to
diverse holistic solutions to building problems. In particular, such problems arise from
the technical demands ofRoofs and Spans (1.V.5). For the vernacular builder the
greatest structural difficulties are normally presented by the bridging of spaces. Tents
and trusses, vaults and domes are among the spanning systems devised to cover voids
with the minimum ofinternal obstruction. Even so, the majority require Supports and
Floors (1.v.6) to provide the walls and frames that raise the roofs and establish the levels
that make shelter practicable. These have to withstand the loads thatthe spans exert;
summaries ofthe principles involved may be found under 1.vI11.5 Structural Types.
Finishes or furniture, fixings or fixtures, the details of building are often the aspects
of building production which require the highest degrees of craft ability. Techniques
and Skills (1.v.7) are devised, learned and protected to ensure reliability in fitand
quality in execution. While examples of craft in production are cited, its also in the
maintenance and conservation of buildings that traditional skills continue to be in

demand.
PAUL OLIVER

280

ıvı Assembly

The genesis ofall vernacular construction is the outcome of. long series of experiments and successive trials and
the practical improvement ofassembling materials against the
forces of gravity. Diverse techniques were invented intuitively,
then perfected by better knowledge of structural reality and the
production of new tools.

For less sophisticated societies division of labour between
different individuals in the community exists. Men, women and
children in the family or with their relatives and in groups each
have their own work, responsibilities and duties. There are those
who know how to do things, those who help and those who are
learning. To constructa house is often acommunal task, where
those involved learn through gesture and word while they are
building. This transmission of knowledge includes respecting
rules, which stem from past experience, and innovating and
perfectingthe process ofconstruction with the help ofnewtools.
Moreover, the tools are perfected and the fabrication techniques
become rich and developed.

Although constructions are collective works specializations
are apparent. In the Middle Ages artisans’ organizations
appeared not only for carpenters and masons but also for
roofers, tinsmiths and weavers. For all these skilled workmen
the fundamental concept is to form, superimpose, assemble,
lash, tie, hold in position and fix the elements or materials in
the most rational way and in an immutable hierarchy that can
be called the constituent elements ofthe fabric ofthe building.
Certain elements are essential because they are the horizontal
and vertical bearers. They are the principal structure or the
skeleton of the edifice and must assure stability. The assemblies or junctions as well as the dimensions of these elements
must be perfectly executed, otherwise the edifice will fall down.

The constituent elements of the finishing are the secondary
structures, which are not load-bearing, such as infilling, opening frames, partitions, flooring, wall and roof coverings. These
compartments must be watertight and well looked after or the
building will gradually collapse. The final operation is the
treatment, the last protective layer against interior or exterior
damage, and includes varnishing and other decorative elements.

Vernacular architecture, synonymous with ‘native to its
country’, is formed by house groupings: isolated or dispersed
houses, hamlets, villages, family compounds, small towns and
towns for one or more families living as acommunity.

The choice ofsite may be only after longdiscussions and may
depend on economic criteria, e.g. agriculture, animal rearing
and fishing, on social criteria such as the position, rank or the
role ofthe individual and family, and on cultural criteria like the

rites and symbolic practices of different human societies. Once
the situation has been determined the construction is divided
into two main phases — the preparation and the assembly of
materials.

In ancient societies the earth, forests and rock were common
property and, after certain rituals, corresponding to good use
of materials, material for building was available to all. For
example, the Lao in southeast Asia still attribute symbolic and
social functions to the wood to be used for the house. The presence offorest spirits is evoked by trees, the latter venerated and
even feared as if inhabited by divine beings. Rites and public
offerings determine the day on which trees can be cutand in this
way the gods protect the houses, villages and towns. In the Lao
houses the poles are chosen with particular care: astrologers
are consulted and on the fixed day the builders go to the forest
with friends, taking offerings such as banana leaves filled with
flowers, candles and rice which are placed at the base of the
tree. A prayer is said to the god ofthe tree: Itis you who will be
my palace, my house, my habitation, my refuge, it is said that
you are good, that you are excellent.’ One pole will be the soul
and a second the principal. The Lao have many criteria for the
choice ofthe poles which must be smooth, and without protuberances, creepers, too many knots or bad places. Trees with
holes, forks or dead branches, as well as those which are near
termite nests, overshadow a river or have fallen naturally are to
beavoided. Still other criteria intervene, all displaying the Lao’s
perfect knowledge ofthe material. They are distrustful ofa tree
which ‘cracks’ when being cut (i.e. has internal fissures) as this
is interpreted as a refusal ofthe god to become a house spirit.
The only tree which is not sacred is the bamboo and this is used
only for the finishing ofthe house. The Lao use onlyone tool for
this — the coupe-coupe.

To recapitulate, in the process of construction the longest
phase is the preparation. For wood, the first steps are choosing
the tree, cutting the branches and bark by axe, and transporting
them out of the forest to the site. This is done in autumn and
winter in cold countries. Transport is by rope and human or
animal effort, by sledge or cart or by floating down the river.
The trunks are cut lengthways by axe or saw and squared by axe
to make a rectangular cross-section. For planks or beams, the
logs are placed horizontally or vertically and then cut in the
same direction, following a chalk line and using a two-handed
saw. This wood is then stocked horizontally to dry, delayingthe
building until the following year. After the pieces have been
assembled on the ground, they are raised into position with the
help ofdifferentlifting systems. The use ofprovisional stabilizers, such as struts, and other methods are employed to help

281

## S. 91

1.V PRODUCTION

finish the building. Wood which is in contact with the earth is
treated with a protective layer like tar.

Stone is extracted directly from rock cavities in successive
layers with metallic tools. Quarries are often far from the building sites and stone is transported by cart or by rolling stone
slabs on tree-trunks. Near the site, the stones are roughly
formed, the exposed faces being slightly polished. For more
refined buildings made ofashlar, all faces are slightly polished
and the joints are very close, with regular measurements.

When earth is used in construction, it is often immediately
usable but there is frequently along dıying time involved in the
actual construction period, for example if pise (rammed earth)
is used.

Supple materials like animal skin, prepared in diverse ways
and materials like linen or woven matter, all require a lot of
preparation and must be planned in advance of the construction. The idea that vernacular architecture is spontaneous and
improvised, without plan or thought, must be dismissed:
preparation is scrupulous and time-consuming, demanding
exacting workmanship both diverse and specialized.

The preparation and realization of collective work attributes
tasks to each member ofthe community. The relation between
userandartisan can be varied. The user might provide the materialand also build the house as, for example, when a temporary
shelter is built by pygmy women, or the user might provide the
material and participate with family and friends to help build
butitis the artisan or specialist from the community who gives
the orders and is paid for the work. Or the user might haveasite
butask one or more artisans to execute his desires; the artisans
have no other work, specialize in construction and organize the
required materials.

The artisan has been an apprentice, travels and meets other
skilled workers to exchange experiences. Rules are made,
within an association, to protect their interests. In Europe during the Middle Ages le compagnonage developed, in particular
between masons and carpenters. Customs, rituals and craft
secrets are still preserved. Innovations are transmitted, and la
confrerie (guild or brotherhood) asks apprentices to travel across
the country and finish their apprenticeships by doing a very
important, personal piece ofwork, the ‘masterpiece’.

One ofthe general characteristics of vernacular architecture
is the use oflocal materials. Regardless oflatitude, wood is the
most used material in wooded terrain, stone and earth being
employed where the wood is of bad quality, in which case it is
used only for finishing elements like doors, windows or
infilling. Elsewhere, several other materials are employed -
stone for foundations or ramparts, wood for carpentry and
earth for infilling.

Climatic influence is important. Simply stated, habitation is
the shelter against exterior forces such as temperature, humidity, precipitation, air movement and atmospheric pressure, all
of which make great climatic differences: hot-dry, hot-humid,
cold-dry and cold-humid. The sun’s daily or seasonal movement, varying according to latitude and temperature, is directly
dependent on the angle of penetration of the sun’s rays.
Temperature is related to altitude, the temperature decreasing
1°C for each room (1-8 °F/330ft), and the higher the sun the

282

more intense the radiation. The proximity ofoceans has amoderatingeffectas large expanses of water are slow to heator cool,
thus reducing seasonal amplitudes.

Air movements can be regular, like the trade winds, or periodical, like the monsoon or sea or land breezes. Precipitation is
also a consideration especially in Equatorial countries where
there is flooding. These factors are important both for the exterior house form and the interior spatial organization.

In composite climates, there are two extremes — hot, dry
summers and cold winters with rain and snow. Heavy materials
are good for hot seasons but there must be double walls for
comfort in winter. There can be superimposition with services
like kitchens, stables or stores downstairs and living accommodation upstairs. However, climatic determinism is not the
most important influence in construction. Builder’s works are
determined by the most important climatic constraints, so itis
notrare to see, for example, seasonal migration in the interior
ofthe same house or between two houses. There is no overall
solution. The morphology of the terrain, the site exposure and
the environment can modify the conceptions ofthe buildings.

The second phase in the process of construction and the one
with which this category is principally concerned is the assembly of materials and the erection ofthe elements.

When siting buildings, workers begin by giving shape to the
foundation and layout, excavating and following the geometry
ofload-bearing elements such as walls and poles. Foundations
that are in stone and mortar can be made waterproof. Where
there is a ground level above a cellar, the walls can be in stone,
brick or wood.

Assembling the roof depends on the complexity oftthe construction. The roof can be pre-assembled on the ground or
Piece by piece in situ. When finished, there is a celebration; in
Switzerland, for example, asmall pine is placed on therooftop,
the roof covering being very important in ensuring that the
building is hors d’eau, or watertight.

Finally, with the installation of furniture and exterior additions, such as paths, porches and garden walls, there may bea
ritual or a festival (in French-speaking countries, for example,
the proprietor has a housewarming pend la cremaillere, ‘"hangs up
the creampot hanger’); this is the last festivity before the house
is inhabited. FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC
1.v1.a Dimensioning
Dwellings are made to protect man and his activities
and the measurements usually correlate with the human body.
This relation to human scale exists in all societies. The units of
measurement refer to paces, foot size, cubits, spans, fingers
and thumbs, which evoke something exact and instantly measurable.

The ancient civilizations of Babylon, Asia Minor and Egypt
preferred the cubit as a unit of measurement but the classical
Greek world used the foot, the relationship between the cubit
and the foot being 1:2. Until the metric system, which is
abstract, the foot was the basic measure from Europe to the Far
East: le pied in French, foot in English, shaku in Japanese, tchi in
Chinese. However, the foot length varied between 28 cm (10.9
in) and 32 cm (12.5 in), depending on the country. Also, each

© Frederic Aubry and Plamenka Supic.

Pace Foot

Ka —

'

profession had its own foot length: the military foot, the stone
hewer foot and the foot used in construction. However, counting on ten fingers, led to the adoption in many parts of the
world ofthe decimal system as the basis ofthe numeric system:
in China x tchan = 10 tchi = 100 tsun = 1000 fen; and in Japan
ıshaku =10tsun=100 bu=1000 rin. In certain epochs, counting
in twelves was more common than the decimal system. In
European countries fractions obtained by successive halving
were used: 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16. Itseemed natural to divide volume
and weight in two, and this is still used today: ı/2 litre, 1/4 litre;
500 grams, 250 grams, 125 grams. The same applies to
measures of length and these were used until the end of the
ıgth century: ı perch = 12 feet, ı foot= ı2 thumbs, ı thumb =
12 lines, 1 line = 12 points. In Anglo-Saxon countries the foot
was I2 inches and the inch was divided into eighths. In
Germany and Switzerland 1 foot = 10 inches; in multiples of
feet, 1 pace = 2 feet orı cubit, ıyard=3 feet, ı perch = 10 or 12
feetaccordingto the area.

Thus, thanks to these units of measurement related to the
human scale, living units of measurement are present in the
components and entirety ofthe construction — there is a relationship between a man and the building. For example, the
standardization of wood framing from Denmark and Lower
Saxony is remarkable. The distance between posts, called
Gefach, is 2 cubits (alen) = 4 Danish feet (fod) = about 125 cm
(4 fl). Generally, a big room is 5 gefach in length, a bedroom

Assembly 1.V.1

4.gefach, a kitchen 3 gefach and so on for all the rooms in the
house. A standardized unit of measurement is also found in
heights ofrooms and even in the carpentry.

In construction, the Chinese used the sacred numeric relationship 4+1=5, thatis 4 compass points with, in the centre, a
god. The number 4 is a symbolic value: 4 elements - earth, air,
fire and water 4 seasons, 4 stages oflife. For construction they
used the bay (jian) (the distance between the columns) as a
module. The number of’jian defined the size of the construction. Until the beginning of the 20th century, habitations for
the populace could be 3 jian, those for officials 7 jian and
palaces ıı jian. The depth and width of the jian varied from
region to region and depended on the quality ofthe wood used
for the structure, butwas generally from 3 m(iofl)tosm (16 ft)
and the form often a square frame.

For dimensions and construction Undagi, a traditional Balinese architect, refers to site implantation (hasta bumi) or design
(hasta kosali), which is the ritual philosophical or technical
knowledge transmitted orally or by palm leaf Iontar. The
universe is composed of three parts - bhur, bhuwah and swah
(the other side, the centre and the top). Man is a microcosm
divided in three - the bottom (the feet), the middle (the trunk),
the top (the head).

Man’s house also reflects his image. The physical image ofeach
proprietor is the basic module - height, arm length, foot, hand,
leg, finger, face — but also caste, character and aspirations.
These measurements are used for the house, site, position and
dimension of entry, and so on to the smallest measurements,
And in the dimensioning a ‘soul’ element must be added. For

A penyujuh

Cengkang

Lengkat

example, ifthe courtyard is 16 arm lengths, then ı hand, 3 fingers
and ı phalanx are added to make it a ‘living’ entity.

For land surveying, simple methods are used. Acircleis made
with a peg and a rope while a right angle is made with boards
superimposed at the corner ofa triangle with sides of3,4ands5
units. The horizontality ofa surface is verified with a trough-like
receptacle filled with water, verticality with three boards and a
verticalweight. FREDERIC AUBRYand PLEMENKA SUPIC
1.V1.b Excavating
The first part of construction is to establish a relationship between the intended building and the earth on which
it will stand by laying foundations, or by digging to find firm
ground clear ofvegetable matter, which is able to supporta structure. For level and for sloping ground, two types of foundation
are practical: continuous foundations, in the form ofwalls, and
punctuated foundations, made with isolated supports. In both
cases the principal aim is to transmitall the weight ofthe building to the solid earth. Where there is a difference between the
air atmosphere varying humidity and ventilation) and the ground
atmosphere varying humidity but no ventilation), a change may
be distinguished in building materials between the emerging

}- Batier board

Taut string

foundations and the walls they support. This building joint
between the foundations and the encasing walls is the expression of aan architectural conception of the base on which the
building rests.

Generally the foundations are in stone because this is more
resistant to the influences ofhumidity in the soil than are other
traditional materials. In particular, where there is an absence of
stones in the surrounding district the foundations are built of
other materials, such as brick or hardwood. In damp ground or
in the water they are often in hardwood.

When a building stands on wooden poles (10-20 cm [4-8
in]) these are embedded deeply in the ground. This gives good
stability to the building but the poles are prone to quicker
deterioration at soil level, where they are subject to continuous
variations in humidity and dryness. In general, among nomads
and travelling people, light buildings covered with animal skin
or woven vegetation can be found. The poles ofthese light constructions are driven only slightly into the soil and their diameters are smaller than those of more permanent structures.
When the wood used is thin and flexible, the rods are easily
planted in the soil, and may be bent to transmit the thrust of
dome forms. The rods cross in different directions, forming a

dome, and are bound with strips of bark and covered with
leaves, piles oftwigs or grass. Such temporary structures made
by hunters and gatherers may be abandoned after a short time.

Substantial buildings require deeper foundations. The builder
begins by digging a trench to establish a base for horizontal
masonty foundations that will transmit the loads vertically into
the earth. On sloping terrain it is possible to proceed by digging
trenches forming successive terraces, parallel or perpendicular
to the slope. The depth ofthe trench will vary from 30 cm (tft)
to more than 60 cm (2 ft) depending on ground conditions,
while the width depends primarily on the weight ofthe building- that is, on the height or number of floors in the building
and the materials used. The foundations are always wider than
the walls they support in order to distribute the load over a bigger surface. The foundations will be even thicker when they are
earth-retaining walls receiving horizontal pressure from above

2
DREIER
GOGIEEESRE
SH LISTE,
WERE
GGG EEERE
WERE TEERE

Assembly 1.0.1
the ground. The foundations may also receive a sidelong load,
created by the thrust ofvaults or arches in the building. In these
cases the thickness of the foundation will depend on the
breadth and curve ofthe archway; locally and regularly spaced
buttresses can reduce the required thickness ofthe foundation.

In wet regions the stone walls are built with particular care
below the ground surface to stop water infiltration, usingjointingin stone, rendered with exterior clay or chalk with bitumen,
or with pebbles piled up along the walls to help water drainage.

The natural cavities that develop in rock faces are often used
for shelter. In the Atlantic Pyrenees, or on the Vaucluse plateau
in France, rock shelters were used as permanent habitats.
For centuries men have carved dwellings in the tufa cones of
Cappadocia, while others have built the front part ofdwellings,
then excavated behind this into deep cavities (in Almanzora,
Almeria, Guadix and Setenil in southern Spain).

The most radical form of excavated troglodyte habitation
consists of digging deep courtyards around which are carved
rooms made by boring into the courtyard walls as, for example,
in thesiltlands of Matmata, south Tunisia, or in the loess ofthe
Chinese provinces of Henan, Shanxi and Jiangsu.

FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC
1.V.1.c Forming
‘Forming’ is the use of various, auxiliary structures,
employed as formworks to give shape to some construction
materials, such as moulds for sun-dried bricks or earth walls. It
can also include framework or centring used for arches or vaults
and scaffolding for building heights. Allthe elements can often
be moved and re-used and are temporary. Rammed earth construction is where a large wooden form can be moved horizontallyalong or vertically upwards to form a wall. There are three
or four boards, 3 cm (1.2 in) thick, 2-3.5 m (6.5-11.5 ft) long,
held upright by vertical poles.

## S. 93

© Fraderic aubry and Plamenka Suplc

1v PRODUCTION

To form bricks, damp earth is put into bottomless moulds
which are then placed on a clean, flat surface or straw, then
packed hard by hand and the surplus levelled off. The mould
is lifted up leaving one, two or more bricks to be sunbaked.
The shape and dimension of the mould vary from countty to
country. Sometimes one- or three-quarter sized bricks are
needed for wall construction.

Arches and barrel vaults made with bricks, adobe or cut
stone are almost always executed on a temporary framework
called centring. The shape of the centring varies, depending
on the cuwve and the weight of the supported material.
The assemblage is 1-2 m (3-6.5 ft) long, consists of wooden
boards fixed together with wooden or metal pegs and is lightweight and removable so it can be used again as the vault
progresses. The centring is constructed with larger boards
holding smaller boards in place, the latter following the curve
of the arch. Extra support is provided by diagonal struts.
The formwork is supported by scaffoldingmade with poles and
beams, the scaffolding poles resting on small wedges which

are taken outto enable the frame to drop a few centimetres, be
removed and reassembled. The scaffolding poles are braced.
The same centring is used for making arched windows, doors

and openings. FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC

286

1V.1.d Framing

Load-bearing walls nearly always enclose the building while the vertical load-bearing framework of post and
beams is often independent ofthe exterior casing.

The ridge roof, normally symmetrical, rests on two long,
guttered walls and two gable-ends. Different structures are
determined by the weight ofthe covering and the dimensions and
quality ofthe wood in relation to the span being bridged. Various
systems of rectangular-based ridge roofframing have been used.

In medieval Britain an original type of framework developed
using naturally curved wood from certain tree-trunks. The two
curved crucks rest on a stone base and form a painted arch
which holds the ridge-purlin. Rafters are then attached to the
purlins, forming a straight slope. An independent, inferior
purlin rests on the exterior load-bearing wall. A collar connects
thecrucks and leaves free space below. There are numerous varl-

ants in the framework but all are consistent with the base cruck
and sloping roof. The cruck frame was originally for housing
with attics but was also used for barns with large spans.
Timber box-framing is a very old, special technique which
was developed by craftsmen’s guilds. The combined operations
ofchoosing, cuttingand making wood assembly are called trait
de charpente, the art of Latin knowledge of carpentry. In short,
carpenters and assistants worked on horizontal surfaces

Raised cruck

sun-baked bricks,

tracing the principal pieces with a knife, following a particular
graphic codification. Then they put the wooden pieces one on
top ofthe other to make a very precise outlineon thewood, using
compasses, levels, plumb lines, tracing lines, pencils and rulers.
Each piece was marked and each assembly numbered while in
place. Then the pieces were adjusted and cut to the correct
length and the parts then puttogether to verify the exactitude of
the cutand prepare for augering holes for wooden pegs with an
auger. The assembling can be done piece by piece, or on the
ground and the whole then raised into place. Provisional pieces
are used to keep the structure firm until it is fitted into its final
position, which can be anywhere on the construction.

Differences between the carpentry systems in Europe and the
Far Eastcan be seen in the roof structures. To stabilize framing,
brace struts were used in Europe while T-heads or consoles,
erossing atthe top of posts, were used in the east.

Assembly 1.01

Provisionally holding the frame in place

Studding is asystem ofassembly with posts fortimber-framed
buildings. The walls are considered as load-bearing even if the
spaces between the posts make up 60-75 per centofthe walland
are larger than the post cross-sections. This mode of construction is called & colombage (Latin: columna; French colonne, English
column. It consists of vertical pieces, posts or studs, assembled
above or below the horizontal pieces using tenon and mortise
joints and pegs. The lowest horizontal piece or beam sole-plate is
placed on the foundation and the second horizontal piece is the
beam for the first-floor level or the ceiling joists. The distance
between the studs is generally 90-140 cm (3-4.6 ft). This method
of construction requires infilling between the studs.

In storeyed buildings the upper floors may be jettied, the
horizontal floor beams projecting beyond the facade to form
corbels, often used in constructions ofthe Middle Ages. Middle
rails are placed between beams to prevent buckling, to support
infilling or to permit openings. Timber-framed walls must
have wind-bracing or crossbracing and these allow multiple
geometric patterns and well-moulded figures to give very decorative facades. One special feature of timber-framed buildings
is the corner-post which is square and is sometimes decorative.

In the ıgth century industrialization of nail manufacturing
permitted a new type oftimber-framed building technique called
“balloon framing’. The wood has a smaller cross-section, 4-6 cm
(1.6-2.3 in) thick and 10-18 cm (4-7 in) long, and the spaces

287

## S. 94

Eu

1.V PRODUCTION

between the studding 40-65 cm (16-25 in). The first-floor level
joists are the same as the studs, with the same spacing between
them. For the openings, two or three studs can be cut and windows with lintelsandsills putin. The infilling is good, impervious
insulation and both the exterior and interior often have plywood
ortongueand groove planks which preventbuckling. When these
boards are placed diagonally, they act as wind-braeing.

1v.1.e Infilling

Infilling is necessary between the vertical elements
or frames ofa construction. The posts on the face can be2or3 m
(6.5 or 10 ft) or more apart and the infilling consists ofsmaller
frames which must be rigid but can have openings. Generally,
these frames are attached to the larger frames ofthe facade and
can be on the exterior, interior or middle axis ofthe posts ofthe
large frame. In this way facades can be designed in relief. When
the smaller frame is on the exterior side of the posts of the
larger frame, the exterior view can be covered with infilling.
Thesame applies to the interior, when the small frame is on the
interior side ofthe posts ofthe frame. Itis also possible to use a
double infilling like wooden boards, or a thick infilling like
earth. Materials used for infilling can be the same as those used
for the vertical elements - with brick framework infilling may
be in brick or another material.

Very often, there is awood or wattle framework filled in and
plastered with mud. In other cases infilling and framework are
completely covered with mortar, 3-4 cm (1.2-1.6 in) thick,
which is laid in several layers and stabilized with natural materials such as bitumen, asphalt, gum arabic, cactus juice, karite
(shea tree) butter, and horse or cow dung. Natural mortar based
on lime or hydraulic lime when pozzolana is added is also used.

Both post and beam and framework systems can be covered
with the same materials as roofs, such as thatch, tiles, slate,
shingle (thin, rectangular pieces ofwood) and panelling.

FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC

1v1f Jointing and fixing

The oldest principle ofwood assembly between two
vertical and horizontal pieces is the cleft post and binding.
They permit the support ofa third ancient element, the beam,
and the creation of a primary shelter of posts and beams using
rudimentary tools. These two systems originally used round
timber which was not squared. The cleft post system does not
allow vertical continuity, whereas assemblies which use posts,
particularly of bamboo joined by binding, allow spatial continuity in every direction. Wedges and pegs are commonly used
to secure the components.

The second principle oftimber assembly is the use ofnotching. The trunks of trees are pruned of their branches and the
bark is kept for other uses. Wood is a strong material consisting
of longitudinal fibres which are resistant to compression,
traction or bending. Assembly by use of notches has the function of transmitting strains from one piece to another, the logs
being parallel or perpendicular to the fibres.

In the corners where the walls cross, the heads ofthe trunks
extend beyond the wall plane some 30 cm (tft) or more, butthe
corners are such that they cannot be put out ofjoint.

Sometimes rounded logs are crossed at the corners with
only very slight notching to permit air circulation as in, for
example, a hayloft. Evolution oftechniques led to the squaring
of trunks on many faces. Longitudinal grooves, male and
female, guarantee a close fit. Some corner assemblies became

more sophisticated, the heads of the beams being notched in
dovetail form.

Wooden pegs, 2-3 cm (0.8-1.2 in) in diameter, may fix the
trunks at each level, preventing lateral movement and eventual
twisting. The need for imperviousness of joints between the
trunks means thata filling made from a compliant substance is
needed, such as pebbles, wood chippings, moss, fibres, or
coarse rope. The pegs or binding that are mainly used to fix the
pieces also serve another purpose - to ensure that the wood is
never inert as it is susceptible to alterations in humidity and
dryness.

As can be seen in the examination of traditional examples,
the vernacular building techniques derive from the combination and variation of notches of halved wood and mortise and
tenon in the construction ofvertical or horizontal timbers, and
the oblique braces between them. Joints are always ‘weakening’, because ofthe capacity ofwood resistance and the strain
ofthe joints is always transmitted by compression. The tension
is converted into compressive or shearing force. The strains
transmitted perpendicularly through the mortise and tenon
joints along the fibres are the most simple and, as the strains
must be transmitted in the direction ofthe fibres, itis necessary
to have a heel piece - an extension, beyond the joint — that is
liable to shear off because of compression. This is the case, for
example, atthe meetings between beams and principal rafters,

or main tie-beam and kingpost.
EFREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC

Jointing and fixing: stone
Stone is extracted from quarries today using the same
methods as three thousand years ago. There are opencast and
subterranean quarries, the former being the first to be ereateil
as they were more convenient. Shafts are used for extraction in
subterranean galleries and some quarries use both shaft and
opencast methods. Quarries are filled in after the stone has been
extracted: opencast by waste and shaft quarries sometimes by
becoming dwelling places or storesasin the Val deLoire, France.
The quarries are exploited in layers of various thicknesses -
from 30. cm (1 fi) to 4m (13 fl). Thetop layer is the bancroyal, the
second the banc blanc, the third the banc franc and the fourth the
banc jaune. The layers are detached using wedges and sledge
hammers, using natural fissures in the stone. The resulting

1.1

290

IT

stone blocks are cut, with the help of iron wedges, following
the natural quarty layers in varying thicknesses. Granite, for
example, can be cutto 10, 6 or even 3 cm (4, 2.4, 1.2 in) as in
Valle Maggia, Ticino, Switzerland.

The faces of stone blocks can be hewn to the desired form;
the cut is obtained by using a drove chisel for hard stone anda
pick for softer stone. Hewn stone blocks can be dressed an
used for facades or lintels with the two edges of a face being
chiselled out by 2-3 cm (0.8-1.2 in), then the remainder öfthe
now-protruding face chiselled to dress it. The top face is
dressed first by bush-hammer (& la boucharde) usingasquare on

farleft Carpent

scarf and doy, "Ybi

tal,

Stone blocks cu

Irma
iron wedges
