References for Wil
Chong, In-Kuk, 1974
Harris, Richard, 1978
Krstic, Petar, 1955

jofmoving and
one blocks.

the lateral sides. Then the order is facing, joints, sometimes
other faces and, finally, the bottom face.

In soft stone quarries, a special saw (scie crocodile) is used to
slice the blocks and good workers cutisq m (11 sq ft) in ı hour.

Ashlar is blocks of stone cut to plan. It must be carefully cut
and is a very expensive building material. As a result other materials have gradually replaced ashlar and now it is used only for
the facing. In constructions, ashlar is placed on small supports
ofequal size, made oflead, slate or wood, to regulate the width
ofthejoint. Itis then raised, the surface is wetted and an excess
offine lime-mortar is added. The stone is replaced and a mallet
used to hammer the excess mortar out. Clay is placed along the
joints to prevent the mortar staining the stone.

When there are no headers in the wall, the stone must be
made stable by the use of stone keys which can be bronze or
galvanized steel and in the form ofa cuboid (a gudgeon), dovetail, crampon or Y-shape. The wall can also be rendered solid by
different types ofjoints. A wooden horse, which can be taken
apart, serves as a hoist for lifting stone blocks.

FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC

Assembly 1.01

1.V.1.8 Lashing and tying

Lashingis the oldest, universal method used to assemble different materials. The procedure was employed in the
neolithic age for making the earliest tools like stone axes, adzes
and utensils such as receptacles made of bark pieces sewn
together. Skins and woven materials were also lashed. Vegetableor
animal matter was used, including wood fibres (from, for example, bamboo, rattan, mulberry wood [tapa], willow [wicker], jute,
rush, creeper and silver birchbark), and fibrous stems (from
palms, raffia, sorghum, hemp, nettles, straw, coconut, millet, leaf
veins and so on). Hair from goats, sheep, camels, Ilamas, and
bisonandanimalskinandtendons cutinto strips werealso usedas
were other products such as cotton and silk.

All these products are usable immediately or as soon as they
have been made supple, a condition achieved by making them
damp, by oiling and then twisting them by hand, by rubbing
them with oil while they are lying over the thigh, or by spinning
them with tools such as the bobbin and spinning wheel.
Threads can also be retwisted to make them thicker or made
into plied strands, a universal method for rope of different
thickness. Plaiting twigs is similar to weaving and these
techniques are used to make braids or long strips. Knots for
tightening are so varied that it is not possible to listthem all but
the most used are the simple and the flat knots. In other types of
knotting, whipping binds, the splicing fid (a conical wooden
pin) make or slacken knots and in order to tighten and slacken,
knots with small toggles are used.

## S. 96

1.V PRODUCTION

Assembly by ligature is used particularly in vernacular architecture for constructing wood framing but especially where
there is bamboo as in Southeast Asia. This material is light,
resistant, easy to cut lengthways and can be utilized for all
elements in a house, from the framing to the floorboards, and
for partitions, mats, covers, scaffolding and bindings. .

In Africa, there are round huts—habitations, rooms, kitchens,
granaries - covered by conical carpentty, sorghum and thatch,
andtied. In case offire and to avoid itspreading, the nr
blage can be pushed onto the ground. Numerous amp o
palisades are found and the supple matting separates, enabling
the resulting ‘rooms’ to be used for different purposes. .

All temporary nomadic constructions use ropes and binding
as tying media and to tighten and fix. Lashing techniques -
been used by the Ma’dan in the south of Iraq for five thousan
years. Very long sheaves of giant reeds are lashed in the form

of an arc, then, over these, horizontal ribbing is laid and then
tied. This is covered with woven reed mats. Itis perhaps the most
remarkable example ofasimple, efficientand durable technique.

The application ofbamboo as a material for building construction is extensive in the world. In Asia bamboo is more important
that timber; in Southeast Asia, many representative examples are
known such as the Toraja bamboo house from Sulawesi and the
elliptical hut on a platform from the island of Nias. Premiere

examples include the Nyakusa and Kinga bamboo huts of sou E

ern Tanzania, the shabano, or Yanoama village of‘ aggregated yahl,

single-sloped shelters from the Amazonian forest, and the A

apoo Indian house from Mexico. The stability of these bamboo

constructions depends on the strength of the material, on the
correcttight knots and the rope used for lashing.

material was generally a three-ply braid ofuki uki grass.

1.V.1.h Laying
There are few natural building materials which are

immediately usable; most require preparation and transformation (sawing, cutting, mixing)in accordance wich their physical
properties before being utilized. In order to realize all building
elements (wall posts, partitions, doors, windows, staits, roofing
and so on), systems have to be employed that exploit the fündamentaltechnical capacities of‘assemblies’, while workingwithin
the constraints of the materials. The use of various natural
materials can be examined.

The large balls ofclay (Great Britain: cob; and Blancel bauge)
are shaped by hand, and thrown with vertical force to build large
monolithic walls, 40-100 cm (16-40 in) thick or more. For higher
buildings, as in Yemen, the corners ofthe buildings are raised
to create greater stability or, on the contrary, as in the granaries
of Burkina Faso, lowered. j

Lumps of earth (USA: sod; Ireland: turf; Central and Latin
America: terrones) are raised by spade or plough in compact turfs
which are cut up to form regular sized bricks and piled up without mortar. Sometimes a narrow layer of reeds is laid between

each course.

Assembly 1.01

Adobe bricks, sun-dried on edge before being used, are
mortared with the same clay material in more liquid form. The
Pueblo Indians ftom New Mexico use straightforward tests to
establish how much pressure an adobe brick, supported at its two
ends, can take before it breaks. The large block is set up on two
supports and a man ofheavy build stands on itto apply pressure,

Walls can be made with logs, without bark, which are superimposed horizontally. The logs are 25-40 cm (10-16 in) in
diameter, sometimes more, 4-7 m (13-23 ft) long, and often
more than 400 kg (880 Ib in weight). When the log section
varies, thelogs are placed top to bottom, thin to thickends. They
are putin place by being rolled upwards over two inclined logs
resting against the wall, whilea man pulls from the top. When
the height becomes critical, a lever block and tackle are used.

For constructions which need good ventilation, logs are

placed with a space between them. For habitations it is importantthatthe logs have no spaces, so the logs are cut with an axe
to improve the contact ofthe horizontal faces, To keep the logs
in position firmly, wooden Ppegs may be inserted ı m (3 ft) apart
and the halved joints at the corners ensure a continuous connection. The logs protrude at the corners by 20-40 cm (8-16
in), regularly or irregularly and sometimes much more, andare
corbelled when a front overhang or gallery is desired. For better
habitability, logs are squared off to obtain flat surfaces. With
this method, the corner logs do not project because they are
dovetailed. Moss or crushed flax is forced between the squared
logs with a wedge. In Switzerland the triangular part of gableend walls, which does not Project, is strengthened with poles
placed vertically on either side ofthe wall and inserted through
holes in wooden corbels made for this Purpose.

Brick is a form ofreconstituted stone, obtained bythebaking
of potter’s clay (clay and sand) at high temperatures (1000 °C
11832 °F). The baked bricks are standardized and, being easy to
handle, are frequently found in vernacular architecture. The
courses are regular and allow a limited variety of bonds, or
combinations of stretchers and headers, set in joints in the
thickness ofthe wall, which can vary from 20 cm (8 in) to4o cm
(16 in) or more. To make up the desired bonds the bricks may
be cut in half or even in quarters. Bricks are bonded by using
mortar, a mixture of lime, sand and water. In Europe and the
Middle East, walls made up of alternate layers of stone and
brick are found. Likewise, the superimposition of layers of
stones and beams, especially where the stones may help to
economize on timber, assists resilience to earthquakes.

The bonding system for stone uses the material in parallel
layers which can be regular or irregular. The components are
erossed and the joint is filled with mortar or, in the case ofdıy
stone walling, left as it is. Such an assembly is not intrinsically
resistant to compression; it is necessary to have numerous
courses, which in some cases may be laid obliquely to take the
strain ofarchways, vaults or domes.

As the range of rubble obtained from cutting stone is very
wide, a variety of tools are used in stone masonry. These vary
according to the quality of the stone used (sandstone, limestone, schists, granite, tufa), the relative dimensions of the
stones (regular or mixed, large, medium and small) and by
the treatment ofthejoints.

293

## S. 97

1V PRODUCTION

The stones are always laid in the same way as the stratum
is found in the quarry as this natural position is more vesistant
to weight. To achieve a homogeneous wall at the intersections
ofthejoints, within the thickness (40-60 cm 16-23 ind, three
different forms ofstone are used: stretchers, which are laidlongwise and which penetrate abouta third ofthe depth ofthe wall;
headers, which are laid with the narrow dimension exposed; and
bondstones, which span the thickness ofthe wall. Thin slivers of
stone rather than mortar are used to make up the gaps in joints
thatare too large, butwhen mortar is used it penetrates the wall
from one side to the other. In order to ensure good stability and
thecontinuity ofstone construction between two walls meeting
at an angle, quoins or corners are constructed out ofstones of
larger dimensions. .

Dry stone walls need particular care to ensure the irregular
stones are superimposed on as large a flat surface as possible.
Slivers ofstone.are inserted, as the wall is constructed, to fillthe
cavities. FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC

14V. Moulding

Earth can be modelled in diverse forms with the
help ofmoulds or frames but the earth must have good plasticity. Innumerable earth constructions in Africa, Asia and South
America are built by hand and the construction materials are
often improved by the addition ofcertain substances of animal
or vegetable origin. Moulded earth constructions can be made
with thick or thin walls.

Constructions with thin walls are generally in round.or curyed
forms and are made in the same way as earthenware. The walls
are constructed in rings about 40 cm (16 in) in height, the zolls
or lumps of earth being placed by hand and smoothed with
stoneorwood.orby hand. The base ofthewallis slightly thicker
than thetop, 15-40 cm (6-16in) as opposed to 5-20 em &-8 in).
The wall finish is smooth but sometimes, on the exterior, each
successive layer overlaps the layer below. Lateritic earth is superior because it becomes hard in air. The plastie consistency IS
obtained by adding water, about 20 per cent, kneading by foot
and leaving the mixture for 24 hours before use. Each Jayer must
be dried for one or two days before the nextlayer is applied.

The Mousgoum build their famous conical huts with reinforcing elements, namely vertical moulding on the exterior of
the shell. This moulding serves for streaming water and as a
substitute for the scaffolding necessary for construction. There
is an incompatibility between the shell and the apertures, the
latter weakening the structure. The weakness is compensated
by extra thickness round the entry and ventilating office.

Forms can be very different when moulding directly from
earth. The Massa from northeast Cameroon build their huts in
rings of varying thickness, one on top of the other, bulging
inwards towards the bottom to formasslope on the interior side,
The exterior wall is scratched, by finger, andresembles matting.

The Tamberma (Batammaliba) of Atakoro, north Togo, construct the walls oftheir tata by hand, in the form ofa fortified
castle, using large balls of earth. The walls, built in successive
rings, are thicker than the Mousgoum walls. The hand is the
essential tool. The rings overlap, earth bulging out on either
side ofthe ring, to help stability. The walls are not able to sup-

port the weight of a roof oflogs and earth, so a multitude of
poles is erected inside.

The preparation ofthe earth and moulds is varied. 70 construct granaries like large-dimensioned jars, as in Afzica and
Mexico, vegetable fibres and earth are mixed. The partitions are
thin, onlya few centimetres, butthese constructions lastalong
time. This construction technique, with thin partitions, allows
very different architectural forms with reduced manpower and
simple tools, but the dıying of rings requires along time, the
material resistance is weakand the builders must be proficient.

In France and in the southwest ofEngland thick-walled constructions were built from large balls ofclay while in Iran, thick
walls several metres thick are built by this system to shelter icemaking ponds. In Yemen an almost identical system, characterized by courses, stepped atthe corners, is used to build homes
several storeys high. For this type of wall, the earth must contain about 20 per centofclayand up to 20 percent ofgraveland

straw. The layers are high (60 cm [2 ftl) and are left to dıy for
more than two days. Thus, the construction is slowly built.
Sometimes timber poles are placed between layers for reinforcement. Around the apertures there is stone, brick or wood.

Assembly 1.1.1
1V.1j Suspending
Roofs must protect inhabitants from climatic elements, and various materials are used. Wheat or rye straw (thatch
in Europe), maize, millet, rice or sorghum straw, dependingon
the culture and country, are formed into sheaves 20-40 cm
(8-16 in) in diameter and 80-150 cm (2.5-5 ft) long. The fibres
must notbe broken although the stalks are thrashed and grainless. The sheaves are tied together with straw ‘ropes’, wicker,
willow branches or rope. They are placed on horizontal battens,
which are at intervals ofabout 40 cm (16 in), from the bottom of
the roof upwards, each row overlapping by two-thirds, sometimes less. The sheaves are tied to the battens or fixed to the
rafters with iron hooks. Before being finally tied, the sheaves
must be carefully butted to level the ends, using a legget. This
‘driving? helps to tighten the face.
The thickness ofthe cover can vary from 30 cm (1 ft) to 60cm
@ fi) and is thicker on the lower part of the roof. Sometimes a
long, supple branch is laid across the sheaves to prevent them
slipping. The thatcher uses a special ladder to hook onto the
battens to enable him to work without disturbing the sheaves.
Sometimes small logs are fixed to the roofto provide ‘steps’ to
enable the thatcher to work without a ladder. The roof slope is
important-45°to 70°. In certain countries thesheaves onround
dwellings, as in large parts of Africa, are kept in place bya concentric circle of smaller sheaves made ofthe same materials as
the roofcovering. Sometimes matting, a clay pot, anetorropes
are put on the upper part.

Apart from these perfectiy executed types of covering in
Europe, Africa and Japan, there are other types of suspended
coverings using vegetable matter.

In Ethiopia grass sheaves are bound and placed on the shell
ofthe round dwellings, and the pygmies of Central Africa make
asimple domed framework, with branches pushed into the soil
and then covered with large leaves. On the Amazonian community house (maloca) palm leaves are woven together, while the
Ainu in the north of Japan sew leaves onto horizontal stems.
Elsewhere, palms are doubled over and sewn onto the roof;
other types ofleaves can be split, then putdirectly onto the roof.

The wickerwork technique ofthin strips ofwood and splitor
whole bamboo, is used for a great diversity of matting, which
varies in suppleness and rigidity. These mats and panels can be
used as light partitions, suspended screens or as coverings. The
lightweight matting is employed as covering for the semispherical huts which have light wooden frames, for example the
Somalian agal or theteda in Tibesti, Chad. Often these coverings
are held down by ropes and stones. In south Ethiopia the Dorse

constructsemicircular huts with aradius ofup to 5 m (16f), made
of densely woven split bamboo. They are covered with sheath
leaves or, ifbig, with leaves and then grass. One type ofcoveringis
formed by tying splitbamboos, reeds or splitstick into matsand
then suspending these mats on theside ofthe structure. The weaving on some coverings is sometimes diagonally to the borders.
Native American Indians and Inuits employ animals skins
which are sewn and tied together. The ger oryurtofCentral Asia
is covered with feltor woven material. The Yakut in Siberia suspend silver birchbark strips on their conical waha houses.

1.V.1.k Tensioning

The capacity of material resistance depends on the
internal structure ofthe form and the direction in which force is
exerted. Compression and traction are two opposing principal
forces in the elements of framework. Compression has a tendency to shorten materials and tension stretches them to
breaking point. Theoretically, when forces exert pressure and
the forces are parallel to the axis, a post for example, this is pure
compression. When a rope is stretched, this is pure tension.
When the charges are perpendicular to the axis, there is flexion
and this is a combination of compression on the superior part
of the element and tension on the inferior part. This association of two forces exists in all solid mass materials such as
beam supports, rafters, ridges, lintels and roof beams. In more
complex horizontal framework, the elements of compression
and traction can be separated. In a closed triangular frame, for
example, the two principalrafters and the struts are under compression, while thetie-beam and kingpostare under tension. In
the case of arches and vaults which are mainly under compression, it is important to retain the horizontal thrust by pulling
(tension) or by counterforce (compression), driving all the
oblique forces into the ground.

When the elements of framework are in thin, long, supple
wood, compression can be used in an arc form, like cylindrical
or circular huts. The elements are bundled together and put
into the earth and they form a shell under compression. In Toba
Batak constructions (Sumatra), the ridge-pole is bamboo, under
tension, has a natural convex form and is suspended at each
end. In nomadic constructions there is great diversity of light
framework made with poles and covered with skin, woven
material or bark. But in the ‘black tents’, tension is exploited;
the structure is minimal, and can be one pole under compression while the cover is under tension. This type of framework
can be seen from Mauritania to Tibet, from desert regions
to high mountains. Posts are in rows of two or three and the
number of rows indicates the wealth or importance of the
proprietor. The height can vary from 1.5 m (5 ft) to 4 m (13 ft)
and the centre of the tent is higher. In the centre, single or
double supports have a short wooden ridge-piece to prevent a

Assembly ERVA

Principal rafter

Rafters
Battens
Wall-plate

Purlin-cleat Post

hole wearing in the material. The cover is made of large bands
of material between 60 cm (23 in) and 70 cm (27 in) wide, sewn
together to form a rectangle, and made of goat, camel or yak
hair. Tension bands are sewn onto the interior face, thenumber
of bands depending on the number of rows of posts. On the
extremity of each tension band there is a stay fastener which is
attached to a hemp or woolrope. A peg, wooden stake or large
stone buried in the sand secures the rope and holds ittaut. The
tension on the material is two-directional — transversal along
the rows of posts, and longitudinal; this produces diagonal
tension ateach corner.

Curtains are used to separate or divide the tent. These dividing curtains can be independent ofthe cover and made of the
same material or different materials such as woven reeds.
The curtain can be used as part ofthe cover but it is not under
tension and hangs freely, being opened if necessary.

FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC

Srreden

## S. 99

v2 Cladding and Finishes

‘Cladding’ is the term given to the weatherproof: skin
or means of enclosure of a building, and is essentially nonal. Pen
Reken building can be said to be clad. In certain building
forms the enclosing material is used structurally or to supplement structural frame, as in the brick panels used as infill in
i framing.
one — are regarded as cladding materials. Planks
and boards, reed and grass, shingle, tiles and slates are all
in this category although timber from which planks come
and even reed, itselfa type ofgrass, can be used steueturally. In
addition to support, cladding can have a significant bracing
function as for instance in the diagonal boarding to be found in
American barns or the plywood stiffening in ballaon framing.
Cladding must satisfy certain functional requirements. At
the most basic level, it defines the building envelope, wEilst
staking the boundary of the unit and providing some visual
privacy. It need have no supplementary functions, such as
audio-privacy, thermal insulation, weather-proofing or even
defence, but very often does fulfil atleast one other unetion,
Availability, the nature ofthe materialand its function a
to agreatextenthow and where itwill be used as eladding. Mu n
is rarely used as a roof cladding material in climatic zones N
high rainfall without an additional cap of thatch to Shed t r
water and to protect the underlying mud finish. Matertals üse
to resist heavy or even frequent rain are chosen for their ability
to shed water and to withstand damage in the process, Where
the individual unit used in cladding is small and, unlike mud,
does not undergo a mechanical bonding, its watershedding
performance is achieved by overlapping a larger number of
these units, so that the rain will run off the roof yatler than
penetrate it. Each element is lapped by those above and likewise
laps those below; only approximately one third of the
cutaneous, shiny and therefore most water-resistant surface of
the material is ever exposed to the weather. Examples of such
cladding are various forms of thatch (reed, straw, grass, leaf,
even heather); tile, shingle and slate. .

The performance of thatch is also affected by the pitch at
which its laid, and pitches for thatched buildings are generally
no shallower than 40°, so that the rain can be shed rapidly to
preventitfrom soaking through the layers ofthatch. Pitches on
tiled or slated buildings were traditionally rarely less than 36° to
40° to prevent water backing up or being drawn up between the
layers by capillary action. The texture of the material is important as the risk of capillary action is far greater where surfaces
are very smooth and adjoining surfaces fit closely together, as

in cleftshingles compared to sawn shingles.

298

Modern examples of roofs laid at lesser pitches resist water
penetration with the aid of underlays, which prevent water
infiltration, such as sarking felts laid with laps of 659 mm
(23 in) or even in double thicknesses with staggered joints, and
modern plastics laid with taped or welded jeints.

Torching, or the infill ofthe gaps between tiles using moss,
clay or straw, was common practice in parts of Kent. Elsewhere
in England, examples of roofs pargeted or plastered from the
inside survive. This may have had the function of inereasing
insulation, particularly in buildings used for habitation, or
of excluding damp, or may have acted to fix the roof to the
structural timbers more securely.

In Badakhshän, Afghanistan, reed matting (chegh) formed of
reeds tied simply at four or five places along their length to
form extended rolls of mat, are wrapped around the stfuetural
poles ofthe house frame by nomad Arabs to provide, privacy to
their summer huts. In the Badakhshän hut the entire structure
is fabricated from six poles bent over in pairs to form hoops
joined by a single ridge-pole, over which a white or felt cloth
is draped to form the roof and around which chegh matting is
wrapped to form the walls. The components for the ChEIE
hut constitute less than one camel load. The Baluch tamarisk

hut (kodik), formed of bundles of scrub tamarisk boughs, is

occupied all year round and uses similar constrüctiont techniques, but a layer of mud is added to the matting to increase
the insulation ofthe construction. During the summer the mud
is omitted from the roofto increase ventilation. f

The addition of a final layer or treatment to underlying
materials to render them water-resistant introduces a new
building element, that ofthe finish. . Singen

Finishes are generally a thin layer applied to the construction. bare, ea
They can be applied externally to render it more weather-proof, Temesse, united
or both internally and externally for decorative purposes. States.

85

arfield, 7.

ng in that it is used
strueturally in masonry construction andas cladding in daub, it
is applied to wattle, Pole or even reed construction to increase
acoustic and thermal insulating Properties, and it is used to
‘chink’ or fill the gaps left between elements ofthe underlying
material or even ofthe structural walling, such as in blockbau.
The waterproofing and weather-resistant qualities ofmud are
dependent on the cohesion established between individual soil
and other mineral molecules which, when combined, undergo a
chemical reaction, thereby increasingtheir resistance to erosion.

As mud-plaster, earth is used as a finish which, when mixed
with certain additives, can extend the longevity of the wall
and can enhance its appearance through the introduction or
elimination oftexture or ofcolour. The boundary between earth
as a cladding material and as a finish is therefore precise.

In the Mazury and Pomeranian regions of northern Poland,
laths or even twisted straw rope infill are plastered over, thus
tendering them weather- and cold-resistantand perhaps more
aesthetically uniform. Massive log construction, characteristic
ofparts of, Mazury, is also plastered over, the projecting corners
often expressed byanalmost classical raised quoin, which may
be dictated Purely by circumstance or may reflect aesthetic
intention and a deliberate portrayal of classical features
imported from the grander buildings ofthe gentry.

Other finishes, while decorative, increase the longevity ofthe
actual materials themselves, such as creosotes and varnishes
which have been in use since the 14th century, and raddle, used
extensively in medieval Europe.

Render is plaster used externally to weather-proof construction made up of many units, such as masonry construction,
where a render coat of about 15-40 mm (0.5-2 in) provides a
coating designed to assist in shedding water and to prevent
moisture from penetrating the mortar between the individual
stones. In conditions of high rainfall, such as in Scotland or
Ireland, an aggregate ofsmall stones may be introduced to the
render as in pebble dash, where small stones of roughly iomm
(0.4 in) are contained in a cementitious medium.The strength

ofthe bonds formed between the medium and the stones has
led to its adoption in places where the driving rain index, used

nn

Cladding and Finishes 1.02

currently to measure the intensity ofwind-driven rain, is highest. Theaggregatealsoaddstothe water resistance ofthe finish
by fragmenting the driving rain and causing itto divert around
the raised texture of the aggregate in much the same way as,
and with a similar intention to, the effects achieved by certain
eultures who form patterns with corn cobs impressed into the
mud-plaster finish oftheir buildings or with fan-shaped sweeps
ofthe fingers in the wet surface ofthe plaster.

Formerly, lime was a Primary constituent of plaster and
mortar. Itwas produced byburninglimestoneto yield quicklime.
The ‘good’ lumps of. quicklime, pure whitein colour, were sorted
out.and used for mortar, plaster or whitewash. The remainder,
mixed with the residue of the turf with which it was burned,
was used as fertilizer. The use of lime in mortars is nowadays
decliningas modern additives provide more convenientmethods
ofachieving similar results. Lime is regarded as a retardantora
softener used with modern cementand is an important additive
in work to older buildings, where cementrendermaybe stronger
than the underlying material. In such instances, it is not uncommon forthe cementactuallyto hasten the decayoftheunder-

Iying wall by loosening older and more essential bonds.
Whitewash, used internally and externally, was basically a
lime plaster diluted to the consistency ofthick eream, made by
blending powdered quicklime with water, which dried leaving
a protective white residue on the limewashed surface. With
regular reapplication to Counteract the erosive effects of both
weather and animals, this wash built up in thickness until the
sharp faces of stones were softened or rounded. The stark,
white limewashed houses of} rural Ireland or the dazzling white
buildings ofparts of. Spain are examples ofits use,
In other situations, the basic medium was mixed with ochres,
siennas, umbers and iron oxides to produce colour washes in
the red, brown and yellow ranges, which held their colour betterthan greens and blues. The substantial stone houses built by
the wealthy wool merchants in Cotswold villages in the 17th
century in warn yellow stone were washed in different colours.
The Reckitts Blue bag, once used extensively in the laundering
trade to enhance the brillance of white sheets and cloths, was
used to colour the limewash in certain areas, as among the
Ndebele of southern Africa, where coloured decoration has
become the norm in the latter half of the 20th century. Limewash used on timber was also a usefüıl insect repellent.

Internal finishes have a long history, either providing a level
surface over which other decorative layers such as colours or
papers might be added, or as decoration in their own right. In
many cultures, the printed wordhashada secondary useasa wall
covering, orasan underlay to thatch, where newspaper has been
plastered over the rooftimbers and the underside ofthe roofto
Prevent dust, dirt or insects from fallingon the occupants below.
In the essentially dıy climate ofthe American plains, newsprint
was extensively used to reduce or eliminate dustand draughts.

Cladding and finishes, while a very small proportion ofthe
total fabric ofa building, havea disproportionate impact on its
appearance. They are the surface thatis visible to the observercladding concealing the basic forms of. construction beneath,
and finish increasing the impactofthe building through colour

or texture. DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

299

## S. 100

u

1X PRODUCTION
1.1.2.2 Box and strip (North America)

In West Texas, ‘box and strip’ is the colloquial term
applied to board and batten construction. Like board and batten, itis a siding of mill-cut lumber applied to wood structures
based on the light balloon frame that developed in the United
States in the 18308. A characteristic of box and strip siding is
the use oflongvertical boards laid close together with the gaps
covered by narrow strips. The boards are nailed to a plate and
sill attop and bottom ofthe framing. Mass production ofnails
in the early ıgth century released carpenters from the time-consuming labour of using mortise and tenon jointures to fasten
heavy wood framing. With the laying of railroad tracks, nails
and milled lumber were not expensive to deliver to the expanding frontier. Box and strip buildings met the requirements for
speed in transforming virgin territory into ranches, farms and
towns. The ‘do it yourself’ method using mass-produced parts
eliminated the need for trained architects. Box and strip construction could be mastered quickly by anyone who could handle a hammer and saw. .

Itresponded to the needs ofa growing family. After life ina

dugout, there was at last space to spread out. A lean-to kitchen
was usually added atthe backandaa porch atthe front. Asecond
storey was also possible. Roof styles varied from side gabled to
pyramidalroofs over square boxes. Because ofits versatility box
and strip was adapted for barns, blacksmith shops, stores,
business offices, printers’ establishments, even jails and the
privies out back. Schools and churches shared the same basic
framing, but shiplap cladding was the preferred siding. Shiplap
is applied horizontally instead ofvertically. The top and bottom
edges are dressed to fit closely or make a lapped joint: Some
buildings were leftunpainted, butwhite paint foradwelling gave
a neat and clean appearance in a dusty landscape. ‘Barn’ red
remains synonymous with utilitarian farm structures.

Box and strip has been described as the producer ofbare and
monotonous cubes devoid of any evidence of cultivated taste,
but such buildings were symbols of a developing technology,
industrialization and a people’s willingness to leave old homes
and accept new utilitarian substitutes. The changes resulted from
mass production of standardized units for building, eohiimercially manufactured paint products, house plans printed in

300

books, and popular magazines that encouraged improvisation.
A modern vernacular architecture was taking root. It reflected
the American spirit that overflowed with optimism, anda belief
in economic opportunities and the ownership ofa piece ofland
with a house on a farm or in a prosperous community.
ELIZABETH S. SASSER

Bundle thatch (Belgium)

Vernacular timber-framed buildings in the Walloon

region of Belgium for a long time made intensive use of Toeally

available materials owing to a combination of economic

poverty and lack of communications, and this can be seen in

the eastern reaches of the Sambre-Meuse valley, especially,

though not exclusively, in the herbaceous wood cladding used

there. This cladding varies with geographical locality and is an
excellent guide for typology and dating. The practice is clearly
discernible from the 18th century onwards and may date back
further still. Vertical, butt-jointed boards can be found in the
northern Ardennes (Hautes Fagnes and the Ambl£ve valley),
horizontal cladding in the Cul-des-Sarts region (south of
Chimay), and a widespread use ofbroom and occasionally, in a
few pockets in the Ardennes, of various types ofgrass mounted
on horizontal racks. Polytrichum is used, but only in the high
plateaux ofthe northern Ardennes. .

In rye thatch the polytrichum is inserted into louvers in the
cob and forms part of the infrastructure. On account of the
meticulous work involved, the polytrichum cladding is almost
entirely confined to west-facing gables exposed to frequent AM:
The very few surviving examples are all confined to villages in
the eastern peat bogs. More widespread is the use ofrye bundle
thatch (torchettes), ofwhich some possible isolated examples are
to be found west ofthe Sambre-Meuse (Gembloux).

Therye thatch is tiedattheends into bundlesabout8 cm (3 in)
in diameter and these are inserted into thedamp cobatan angle
of45° and in horizontal lines. The bundles require combinglike
any thatched roof. Because ofthe steep laying angle and the verticality ofthe cladding it can last for 50 years or more, whereas
ordinary rye straw thatch lasts only about 5 years. The rye used

came from cultivation after slash and burn clearance (essartage)
which alone could produce a sufficiently long, tough stem.
FRANCIS TINCHI

1.v.2.b

1.1.2.C Clapboard

Clapboard is a form ofhorizontal timber plank wall
cladding that is tapered in section across the grain. In North
America the types oftimber used are oak, cypress, redwood and
cedar. The best comes from British Columbia where the large
diameter ofthe tree trunkaand slow growth produce lengths with
fine grain and few knots. The western red cedar is favoured for
its moisture resistance which comes ftom the natural preservative oil.

Timber-framed houses are clad with weatherboards, overlapped to protect the grain of the timber. The boards are cleft
from the log radially with the thin section in the heart ofthe log.
Pine boards are sawn to give a wedge shape. The simplest type
of board is a ‘feather-edge’ with the thinnest part placed at the
top and the thicker part to the bottom. This has the effect of
“throwing’ the water offthe face ofthe board. To improve weathering performance and prevent water ‘creeping’ or travelling up
behind the boards in damp conditions, improvements have been
made which interlock the boards. Rebated feather-edge boards
have a section cut out ofthe back ofthethicker part ofthe board.
This allows the feather-edge that is overlapped to have a tighter
joint. Refinements ofthis latter principle are seen in a variety of
flat boards, such as shiplap and V-notched types. Shiplap has a
eurved top section, whilsta V-joint has an angle cutateach edge.
Wain-edge is a further type of board which has an irregular
profile to the lower edge. This irregular edge results from the
untrimmed character ofthe log from which the board is cut.
The success of these clapboard components is seen particularly in North America, Europe and Australia. In Europe many
houses, barns, boat-houses, watermills and windmills use this

type ofcladding. RICHARD HYDE

1.v.2.d Corrugated metal (Iceland)

Turf and sod remained a common mode of construction in rural Iceland until the beginning of the 2oth
century. The turf house itself developed considerably over a
thousand years, both in plan and construction technique. It
was always dependent on a wood frame for structural support

Cladding and Finishes 1.0.2

of the roof which then was covered with sod. Wood was also
used for both interior and exterior cladding.

From the 17th century onwards houses built entirely ofwood
became common in trading places along the shore although
the vast majority of the population still lived in sod and turf
houses. The increasing use ofwood went hand in hand with the
development of seaside towns and villages, which served
as centres of trade and as fisheries. The style, proportion and
technology of wooden houses in Iceland were derived from
Scandinavian wood-building tradition buttheywere adaptedto
Icelandic conditions in thatthey had cladding of mainlyvertical
boards as protection from the rain.

In the late 18708 the import of corrugated sheet metal from
England began. This material became an instant success and its
use soon developed into true Icelandic vernacular as itwas used
as roofand wall cladding on buildings ofall sizes and function.
The corrugated metal had great advantages for Icelandic condi-

301

## S. 101

1.v PRODUCTION

tions where the combination ofrain and heavy winds makes the
waterproofing of walls very difficult. It is this special feature of
Icelandic weather that explains the extensive use of this material, regardless ofthe economic status or class ofthe builder. .

Today the traditional use of corrugated metal is still

respected by educated designers and architects, despite the.
development of new materials and techniques in exterior
cladding. GEIRHURDUR THORSTEINSSON
Limewash .
An ancient and very widespread wall covering,
limewash has been used throughout the world as a paint
for both human and animal dwellings. Its mildly antiseptic
properties help to reduce infections and repel insects. Itcan be
used on buildings of stone, timber and earth, and can be
recoated time and time again forming a thick protective layer.
It creates a permeable coating, allowing damp in the wall to
evaporate away without damage to either the wall or itself. .

In its simplest form limewash is applied as putty lime
(CaOH,) diluted with water. It absorbs carbon dioxide as
it dries and hardens to form calcium carbonate - chemically
similar to the limestone from which it was originally derived.
Limewashing is effectively a means oftransferring a thin layer
ofrock onto a wall to protect it.

Where buildings have been constructed with lime-mortar,
and plastered or rendered with a lime mix, limewash bonds
with the actual structure of the building forming a breathing,
protective layer inside and out. Although it allows a ital
amount of damp to penetrate into the wall (e.g- from rain or
condensation), it evaporates it away harmlessly. Supposedly
“waterproof” coverings inevitably break down to allow damp to
be sucked in through hairline cracks and tiny holes, butremain

sufficiently impermeable to prevent it from evaporating freely,
with the result that damp builds up behind the coating, eventually forcing ifoffthe wall.

Limewash has been used for mural decoration andasawood
protection - timber-framed buildings with plaster panels were
traditionally limewashed all over, timber and infill panels alike.
In Mediterranean countries the use of limewash extended to
pathways and narrow streets, and the tradition oflimewashing
around doorways (often in red ochre) is widespread. .

Limewash can be coloured by a vast range of materials
although traditional colours often predominate: earth pigments,
ochres, oxides, blood, soot and plant dyes have all been used
although not all dyes are limefast. The colour when mixed
appears much darker than the final dry result because the lime
isvirtually transparentwhen wet. Asitdries, itturns white, thus

lightening the tint considerably. .
Although good quality lime, applied in several very thin coats
and allowed to dıy slowly, will make a durable wash, many
additives have been used to make it more water-resistantor less
likely to rub off. (Lack ofdurability and surface dusting, however,
are more likely to be caused by limewash used too thickly, too
quickly or made from hydrated lime.) Additives include tallow
(refined animal fat added during slaking), casein (usually
skimmed milk), oil (linseed), glue size, alum, formaldehyde
JANE SCHOFIELD

1.V1.2.e

and pozzolanic materials.

302

Sul 2 an Es Seesuue

Matting u
Matting is cladding formed from different varieties
of palm and rattan and is widely used in the construction of
buildings and shelters throughout the world, principally in
walls, but also for the roof.

It is essentially a prefabricated panel, either rigid for fixed
use or flexible to allow rolling, involving a variety offabrication
methods. The mats are made ofwhatever materials are available
locally. In Asiaand Oceania, the pandanus palm, found throughout Malaysia and Polynesia, known locally in Malaysia as the
‘screw-pine’ and woven into mats for floor and wall panels, is
only one of over six hundred rattan palms native to the tropical
rain forests of Southeast Asia. A common and particularly
attractive mat is made up of the split midrib of the ‘kumbar’
palm; the outer side of which is dark brown and the inner a
light straw, which when worked produces a variety of patterns.
The coconut palm is also widely used, for instance in Tuvalu, as
is bamboo in mainland Southeast Asia. In the Americas, agaves
and reed are used for matting, while reed, palm and agave are
all indigenous and are widely used in Aftica.

Matting is lightand has been particularly useful to the nomad,
who may carry his building components with him. Arab nomads
in Badakhshan in central Iran use long mats (chegh), woven by
tying the transverse reeds at a number of places along their
length, to form walls wrapped around the corner poles oftheir
huts. In southeastern Afghanistan the Baluch weave tamarisk
into loose mats which are used to cover an arched framework,
which is then mudded over entirely for winter use.

®

The Khoikhoi migrant pastoralists of Southern Africa, who
formerly inhabited the Cape, the Orange Free State and southern Botswana before White settiement, built either a hemispherical or a barrel-vaulted framework of saplings which they
overlaid with reed matting, forming a wind-break at the ends
with another mat set vertically in a semicircle.

The Namas of southern Namibia have traditionally accommodated themselves in a hemispherical subframe, sheathed in
woven rush mats. In the dry heat the rushes contract and a
breeze can permeate the interior. When rain falls, the rush
fibres expand to form an impervious surface. Nowadays, in
Namaqualand, just south of the Namibian border, these
matjieshuisies can be found, covered with an additional layer of
sacking to provide extra protection. Rushes have become very
scarce, however, and the sapling frames are now most often
covered by alayer of flattened paraffin and cooking oil cans.

Mats are also used extensively in more permanent houses,
but by their very nature are less durable and as new materials
become readily available are frequently supplanted. In the Gulf
region houses built ofpalm frond and known as barasti ‘arish or
khaimah, depending on the district, were widely the most common sort ofhome. They are depicted in early Portuguese drawings of the ı6th and ızth centuries and are considered to

account for the open spaces between more permanent buildings in ancient towns such as Ad Dour in the United Arab Emirates. They provided an excellent adaptation to the climate and
were very often used in conjunction with amud or stone house
in the same walled enclosure, the latter being warmer in the
cold winter. On occasion, a wind-tower was added to the house”
to which matting or sacking was also attached.

Houses in Tuvalu and Kiribati are open on three sides for
ventilation and have retractable mats formed out of pandanus
or coconut palm, which are strung together on two lines of
sennittwine in groups ofsixand are long enough to reach from
eaves to floor. The screens are suspended from the eaves and
secured by a string around the middle which can be released to
lower the walls. Kennedy describes how these screens are fabricated from newly fallen coconutleaves, with 0.6-ı m (2-3 ft) of

unrinn
By,

er 5
Ds

Te,
- ron

Cladding and Finishes 1V.2

the thin end and the heavy butt end discarded. The remaining
middle portion of the leaf, with its pinnules on both sides, is
split and folded, the pinnules then being plaited together in a
twill pattern, with the lower pinnules forming the warp and the
upper pinnules the weft. The ends of the leaflets are braided
into a three-plait ridge which draws the successive warp and
weft strands in as it passes. The side facing up while being
woven becomes the external face ofthe building.

Matting is most commonly made by the women, which
affects ownership. Prussin has described how amongst the
Wogo of West Africa (related to the matrilinear Dyerma
nomads) the tent which was traditionally the product and the
property of the women, has given way to a more sedentary
lifestyle and to earthen walls constructed by the men. Ownership of the house has thus passed to the husband, while the
wife retains ownership of the vestigial tent, which takes the
form ofa canopy bed.

The importance of matting in Japan, where it is used most
commonly as a floor covering, relates to its use as a determinant ofsize. Room sizes are described officially and in conversation using the unit of a tatami, as a ‘six-mat room’ or a
“four-and-a-half-mat room’. Tatami, or the thick straw mats,
comprisinga 5 cm (2 in) thick core layer ofstraw covered by an
outer rush mat bound on the long edges with cotton, are now
universally found throughout Japan.

DEIRDRE McDERMOTT
1V2.8 Mud-plaster
Used externally, mud-plaster generally relies on the
use ofadditives to a water-resistant finish to a structure. Used
internally, italso renders the structure asmooth surface, which
may be washed with colour or covered with another applied
surface decoration, even wallpaper as in Scotland (Thompson).

Mud-plaster occurs most in areas where the soil is suitable
for building, such as throughout South and Central Africa, and
the southern states of North America, and much ofEuropeand
Asia. While itis commonly used as a finishing coat on a variety
ofearth structures, to which it has excellent cohesion, it is also
frequently applied thickly over structures made of post, reed,
bamboo, timber, plaited straw, plank and even beer cans,
concealing and protecting the materials below.

Almost always, mud-plaster includes a number of additives,
most commonly animal dung, which has the additional advantage of acting as an insect repellent. Bourdier also notes that
potash specifically repels ants, while shell-lime, used as an
additive by the Konkombas in northern Ghana, or the
fermented pod ofthe locust-bean tree, used in Hausaland, add
to the cohesive properties ofthe plaster. McHenry suggests that
mud-plaster can last from 10 to 15 years, but notes that where
colour wash is applied over the plaster, this surface layer is very
fragile and needs frequent reapplication.

Its use may be functional or decorative or both. It may be
incised, moulded or coloured with different colour washes. It
may be smoothed with a pebble or flat stone till the surface
is highly polished, or it may have patterns impressed in its
surface by various objects, such as corn cobs or fingers. These
impressions invariably provide the building with a character-

303

## S. 102

1.V PRODUCTION

isticorunique pattern. In Poland plaster finish applied overlog
construction in such a way as to conceal the notched corners
suggests a transition to the raised stucco corners, which, with
string courses, dripstopsandraised window surrounds, are common render features readily influenced by classical traditions.

The use of certain coloured clays or compounds may be
interpreted largely as decorative, as amongst the Lela (Bourdier), with lighter or darker clays being applied in patterns, or
in bands as amongst the Tswana. It may also have functional
use, with the lighter ash and potash acting both as an insect
repellent and to reduce the heat gain as noted elsewhere. The
dark strips below Lela gutters are formed with additional gravel
and coated with a vegetal waterproofing compound, which
Moughtin notes as the best and most expensive finish for
earthen walls. Amongst the Newar of Nepal, red earth (sindur)
is used with a mixture ofoil (tel) and resin (saldup) to waterproof
the joints between the bricks (Korn).

Patterns in mud-plaster made with cobs and other objects are
believed to assist in the drying process of the wall, helping to
prevent cracks (or presumably, where these inevitably occur, to
conceal them) as well as dispersing concentrated flows ofrain. It
has also been suggested that the fluting of walls amongst the
Benin, quite apart from its decorative quality, also improves the
walls’ properties of heat insulation, with the horizontal part of
each flute shading the part below, the majority ofthe wall thus
being left permanently in shadow. In addition, the increased
surface area of the wall allows greater radiation of the heat at
night. DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

1\.2.h Painted brickwork

There was a widespread tradition in many European
countries of applying a unifying wash of red ochre paint, in
English records often called ‘rodel’ or ‘ruddle’, to exposed
brickwork. This arose from the difficulty of creating accurate
and thin mortar joints with the uneven bricks that were the
norm before the r7th century. To disguise the deficiency, colour
was applied as a wash.

Additional colour could be given to the external mortar

mixes by the inclusion ofcrushed red tile before the ruddle was
painted over. Used internally, the pigment required a binder of
size, but presumably an additional water-resistant component
was added externally to repel rainwater. Before the ruddle was
applied, the bricklayer smeared the final coat ofmortar orafine
plaster over defective bricks and flushed thejoints. Scored lines
were frequently added along each mortar course and vertically
between the bricks: until the ı7th century this was generally
done freehand. With ruddling completed, the scoring could act
as a guide for the addition of thin lines ofwhite or black paint
(pencilling’), creating the illusion of: finely-pointed brickwork.
The more careful method of beaked pointing, called ‘doublestruck’, was the alternative to scored joints. This was found
mainly on high-status buildings of the 16th century, because
excellent bricks were required to achieve the necessary thin
mortar courses; ruddle was still applied but pencilling is rare.

The practice of mortar scoring, ruddling and pencilling can

be traced back to the and century AD. By the ısth century in
Venice, patterns were developed using staggered white pencil

304

lines that created diaper designs. Several early-r6th-century
Venetian paintings show these and other elaborate painted patterns applied externally to many parts of brick buildings from
the chimneys downwards. True and imitation diaperwork,
together with the decorative use ofexternal colour on chimneys,
is also found surviving on English buildings from the same
period. Contemporary Flemish paintings have accurate pictorial
representations of both unpainted and painted brickwork,
though the former is in the majority because of the more
advanced methods ofbrick production in ısth-century Flanders.

When fine gauged brickwork was introduced in the ı7th
century to define the surrounds ofwindows and doors, applied
colour still played its part with the mortar mixes and washes to

- the surrounding walls. From the mid-ı7th century, casualscored

lines gave way to more precise ruled joints made with a tool that
created a U-shaped groove. In sunlight this created the illusion
of black peneilled lines by shadowing the upper part of each
groove. This technique, like pencilling, was carried on into the
2oth century.

The practice known as ‘tuck-pointing’, where the mortar was
coloured and preise lines were created using insetstrips oflime
putty, was recorded in use early in the 18th century, and seems to
echo the pencilling tradition. This method seems to have been
more commonly used in the later ıgth century. Bythen the combination of more precise sized and even coloured bricks meant
thatthe overall painting ofthe brick face became less necessary.

In the late-2oth century the desirability of mixing brick
colours, combined with planning requirements for a more
standard form of presentation, has meant that the painted
tradition has largely disappeared. TIMOTHY EASTON

1V.2. Palm cladding: barasti (Arabian pen)

At the end ofthe date-palm season in the Arabian
peninsula, and particularly in the United Arab Emirates, the
dead branches were cut from the trunk ofthe tree. Depending
on their use a variety of weaves was used for creating a barasti
house. A finer weave was used for the wall, so all the leaves
would have been removed, keeping only the stem. For roofing

Thompson, R,

see also

salzman, L-

orshade, the palm branch was used in its entirety with the leaves
intact, as well as a fine weave layer, making two or more layers
for the roof. The average frond is about 2 m (6.5 ft) longso, when
used vertically, it is the right height for a human inhabitant.

The palm-frond house is based on a framework of mangrove
poles (chandels), palm trunks or any other straight wood. This
framework usually formed a rectangular building with a flat,
rounded or pitched roof, although in the United Arab Emirates
in the pastsome ofthese houses were found with adomed roof.
The walls of the house were made of palm-frond fencing, the
fronds having been tied together in advance for density. The
fences were made by a group of men who laid the palm fronds
in an orderly fashion on the ground, then tied three or four rows
of cord all along the line of fronds. These cords were placed
approximately 30 cm (1 ft) apart. They could be strengthened
with crossbars offurther palm fronds, laid atrightangles to the
original ones. The fencing panels were then rolled for easy
transportation by camel to the market-place for sale.

These panels were used to enclose a compound, to fence in
animals, to make the walls and roofs of houses, to provide
shade over windows and to act as doors, wind-breaks or barriers against drifting sand. Inside, during winter, they would be
lined with palm-frond matting to provide protection from the
wind; in summer, when breezes were welcome, this matting
could be removed.

Palm-frond fencing was also used in the construction ofroof
shades: a thatch of palm frond was supported on mud or
wooden pillars to give a shaded veranda to the house. The
shaded area was often completely freestanding and served as
an additional outdoor living area.

For extra reinforcement, crossbars made of tree branches
were usually used for the exterior walls. Wooden beams were
also used as tie-beams within the walls themselves, strengthening the masonıy against cracking from subsidence or other
movement. Such tie-beams were made of both palm logs and
imported hardwood.

The gabled house (khaimah) has a rectangular ground plan
and is constructed exclusively of material derived from the
date-palm. Its element of stability is provided by a frame of
sticks to which the walls, made of closely bound sticks of datepalm branches, are joined. Mats made of date-palm leaves
cover the roof-frame and serve as covering for the inside walls.
Openings underneath the gables for light and air circulation
are not generally found in this housetype.

The rectangular arish hut, which was used for the summer
quarters, has an independent construction. Itis built entirely of
date-palm branches. In constructing the 2.2-2.4 m (7.2-7.8 ft)
high side walls, holes were dug about 20 cm (8 in) apart, into
which bundles of date-palm branches were placed. These bundles were bound together with crossbars. In this way walls were
made which allowed air through from both sides and also
allowed light to enter. The flatroof, a kind ofsunshade, lay on
the frame of date-palm branches to which the side walls were
affixed by means ofthe crossbars. Latticework was created for

windows and doors by tyingthe branches apart from each other.

The actual construction of the barasti was simple in theory
and complex in execution. Not a single nail was used in its

Cladding and Finishes 1.V.2

construction, the entire structure being held together by cord.
The palm cladding was vulnerable to many hazards such as fire,
sandstorms, rain and wind. As quickly as the huts were erected,
they could be as easily destroyed. DARIUSH ZANDI
1.V.2. Plaster: carved (Yemen)

Rendering of interior and exterior surfaces in limeand gypsum-plaster is common to the coastand themountains
of Yemen. The technique of lime-plaster (nura, gaddad), arduous and now abandoned, was responsible for the waterproofing of mosque domes (with exemplary carvings) and
roofs as well as interiors of houses. External application in
houses is best represented along the coastal strip, where carvings, alone or in association with brick inlays, may cover entire
facades. In the mountains, external decoration of houses with
lime-plaster, in skirtings and about the openings, is concentrated in the Southern Uplands (Ibb, Al Hujariyah). Internally,
simple motifs may appear on the main rooms, along the ceilings or in wall panels. The most generalized application, however, is the waterproofing of the skirting of bathrooms and
hallways, with simple figurations of birds, flowers, kettles and
fretted panels.

Gypsum-plaster (juss or guss) is less laborious to produce
butbecause ofits lesser resistance to weather is mainly used for
full rendering of interiors. In exterior walls it is used to emphasize openings and inlaid work and form the tracery offanlights.

EpUEIER OPUELIS. CH

305

## S. 103

|

1.V PRODUCTION

Total plastering of the outside walls is rare although in the
central highlands, where gypsum is abundant, cases occur
which appear to be justified as an expedient way of restoring
decaying mud or brick exteriors. In the area of Al Baydha
(in the southeast) applications of gypsum-plaster in geometric
Patterns over grey stone walls were a manifestation of modernity in the 19708. This plaster has a drying time that allows itto
be worked with such simple tools as a penknife to produce
elaborate ornamentation. At their simplest, interior carvings
consist of ogee-arches around openings, and stylized birds
formingthe brackets ofthe built-in plaster shelves (safif) featured
in most rooms. Enriched work is done around themes such as
calligraphy and stylizations of birds, leaves and flowers, the
fleur-de-Iys being recurrent, and can cover everything possible,
including shelves, cornices and wall panels surrounding openings and niches. Patterns may be created using stencils,
although skilled craftsmen often do without them.
FERNANDO VARANDA
1V.2.j-i Plaster: moulded (British Isles)
Plaster has been used from Roman times to the present day. In its simplest form it has acted asa draught-stopping
and protective internal covering for stone, timber or earth constructions; in its most developed periods it has provided the
means for complicated and dramatic applied sculpture and
imitated stone, timber, drapery and natural forms.

In Britain the Worshipful Company ofPlasterers was granted
its charter in 1501, by which time there were already skills and
traditions well established in the craft. Although plain limeplaster is extremely difficult to date, there are undoubtedly
ancient fragments existing in many buildings. Lime-plaster (a
mixture oflime, earth or sand and animal hair) is a robust and
long-lasting material. It tolerates changes in temperature,
damp and even a small amount of structural movement. The
plaster itselfis notattacked by pests or broken down by rot, but
is very dependent upon its supporting structure.

Plaster was applied directly to masonry and other solid surfaces (e.g. cob) where it improved the thermal properties and
also provided asmooth clean finish. As lime-plaster evaporates
away any ambient moisture, problems such as rising damp and
condensation are largely eliminated. On internal partition
walls and in timber-framed buildings, plaster would be supported on some form of framework, usually wooden. Laths of
riven (split) oak, chestnut and, later, softwood were nailed
across joists, wallframes and lintels. Hazel twigs, bundles of
reed or brushwood were also used. Anything which provided a
key for the plaster to squeeze itselfonto would be suitable.

As lime-plaster requires contact with the air to harden,
several thin coats were applied, with drying time between
coats. The first coat would contain proportionately more earth
and was roughly reinforced with hair, straw or even grass and
dung; it was applied with a confident single action, especially
on lath. Overworking the first coat caused the plaster key to
break and the plaster to drop off again. When starting to
harden this coat would be roughly scored to provide a key for
the subsequent layers, and also to distribute the inevitable
shrinkage and cracking evenly. Whilst this cracking was

306

acceptable in the backing layers, the following layers contained
a higher proportion of lime and were reworked a number of
times to provide a smooth white surface, although the larger
irregularities ofthe wall would still be apparent.

Plastering tools have changed very little. A trowel with a rectangular metal face and a wooden float, both with their handles
mounted behind (like a flat-iron), were used in conjunction
with a hawk, or mortar board, to convey the material to the
wall. Other trowels of varying sizes with cranked handles to
keep the knuckles away from the smooth plaster were necessary both to keep the plaster well mixed before use and also to
reach into awkward corners to smooth it out.

By the ı6th century in Britain it became apparent that the
‘plastic’ quality of the plaster lent itself to marking in patterns
by tools and fingers, and that the slow dıying time allowed
for alterations and refinements to these designs to be made.
Ornamental finishes could be achieved relatively cheaply and
this type of decoration became particularly popular where the
local stone was difficult to carve and wood scarce. The work
shows strong regional styles, sometimes derived from designs
seen in pattern books, though the craftsmen were very probably
local. Much oftheornament was modelled in situ: ribs, cornices
and mouldings run out with simple profile tools. Some casting
was employed for repeat motifs, but these were still slightly
irregular as they were turned out of the mould when still pliable. Plaster was invariably leftwhite, being painted with a limewash or distemper only after years of soot and dust had
discoloured the surface.

Plaster in Europe at this time was imitating the elaborate clas-

England.

sical work uncovered in Roman excayations. In the grandest
houses fabulously rich interiors were modelled by stuccoists,
and usually painted. However, the majority of small dwellings
probably had the plain lime, sand and hair plaster detailed above.

Towards the end of the 17th century, classical plaster had
spread throughout Europe, and in addition to lime, gypsum
(plaster of Paris) was in common use - making plaster quicker
setting and easier to use. This led to more ambitious modelling, butalso to an increase in casting techniques using flexible
gelatin moulds as well as carved wooden ones, resulting in
much more mechanical designs. Sawn softwood and eventually
expanded metal lath were introduced.

Ultimately the use of gypsum completely overtook lime.
Ornamental work became preformed off-site as fibrous plaster,
where plaster and linen scrim is built up off a mould, then
screwed into place. Plain plaster is manufactured as papercoated plasterboard which is nailed in place, the plasterer’s
craft being reduced to spreading on a single coat to cover the
joins. JANE SCHOFIELD
1.V2.k Raddle
‘Raddle’ is defined in The English Dialect Dictionary of
1904 as ‘red ochre or oxide of iron used in marking sheep,
colouring tiles, window sills etc’. The Dictionary of the English
Language by Samuel Johnson (1827 edition) describes ‘reddle’
as ‘an earth ofthe metal kind, ofa tolerably close and even texture; its surface is smooth and somewhat glossy, and it is soft
and unctuous to the touch, staining the fingers very much;
in England we have the finest in the world’. ‘Ruddle’ ‘“(rudul,
Icelandick) red earth’ is, he notes, similar to reddle but he
refers to raddle only as an alternative term for wattle.

There are good sources of red ochre in Britain, France,
Germany, Italy and elsewhere throughout the world. In the
past, where red ochres were not available yellow ochres were
baked until red and it is said that it was not possible to distinguish between the two. Most ochres were used direct from the
ground and sieved through a mesh. Some needed grinding.
Mills at Wheatley and Milton in Oxfordshire had kilns for burning the raw materials to a high temperature to convert yellow
ochre to red. Wilfred Foreman in his book Oxfordshire Mills
describes how grinding was done from outside the mills with
edge-runner stone grinders driven by a belt from a vertical shaft
driven from the spur.

Raddle was a cheap and effective means of colouring in the
building trade. It was used by masons in marking stones, by
carpenters on timber and by slaters, some ofwhom still follow
the practice of dipping string into raddle in battening out slates.

Exposed timbers of framed buildings in Britain were sometimes coated with a mix ofbullock’s blood and ‘rudd’. Weatherboarding was protected with a mixture ofred ochreand pitch in
Oxford and other centres. Whole villages were rendered with
limewash pigmented in shades of pink or yellow according
to the local ochre and the tradition continues in many areas.
During the ancient Egyptian and early Minoan periods ochre
was added to newly slaked lime and applied while still hot
to lime-plaster just before the initial set had taken place. The
raddle provided a degree of protection to the wall and as a

Cladding and Finishes 1v.2

colour wash has withstood the test of time. The custom has
continued until the present day.

‘Levigation’, the age-old means of grading powder, was
carried out for fine work. The ochre was added to a tank of
water and allowed to flow into tanks of increasing sizes which
were placed at descending levels. The resulting slurry was dried
at a low temperature. ADELA WRIGHT
1.v.2.| Render
Render is applied to the external face of a wall
both to weather-proof it and to enhance the visual appeal of a
building. Although render can be a simple slurry of mud, it is
more usually composed of two basic ingredients - a filler and
a binder, in the same way as mortar. It is often coated with a
protective paint although self-coloured renders and some
roughcasts are left uncovered.

On walls of stone, brick or earth render is applied directly to
the wall surface. Traditionally a mix of lime, sand and aggregate, the proportions would be chosen to be compatible with
the walling material and the mortar used in its construction.
Thus, a gritty earth wall would need a soft, coarse render which
was extremely permeable, whilst a wall of hard, high-fired
bricks in cement mortar would be compatible with a finer, hard
cement render. A hard impermeable render on a soft wall will
eventually be heaved off by the action of frost on the damp
trapped behind it.

Timber-framed constructions are rendered in different ways.
Where wattle and daub panels infill the framing, a final coat of
daub (often consisting of just earth) is smoothed between the
timbers, supported on the hazel wattles. This requires the protection of limewash. More often, timber frames are completely
covered externally, either at the time of construction, or when
shrinking timbers have allowed damp and draughts inside the
building. Laths, traditionally of riven (split) oak, are nailed
across the frames and panels and the render is applied to these,
usually in two or more thin coats.

Fashions in render have included roughcast (where therender
is flicked on, leavinga gritty surface), smooth stucco (often lined
outin imitation ofdressed stone) and pebble dash (where clean
gravel is cast onto wet cement render).

307

## S. 104

1.v PRODUCTION

Hard cement renders are inappropriate on old buildings
built in the traditional way using lime. Because these incompatible materials do not stick together easily, metal lathing is
nailed to the wall first to support the render. This results in
additional problems of rusting nails or staples and eventually
of render falling off in sheets. It is essential that new renders
which are applied to old buildings be composed ofsympathetic
materials. JANE SCHOFIELD
1.v.2.m Shingles
A shingle is a small thin piece of timber, traditionally cleft from a log but latterly sawn, which is used in a similar
manner to slates and tiles, and at a pitch of no less than 45°. It
provides a weatherproof surface, capable ofshedding rain and
snow and may be used on the roofor on vertical surfaces, such
as walls or church steeples.

The artofeleavingwood to produce shingles, planks or paling
without using a saw has its roots in Roman times. A skilled
craftsman can cleave a log ftom end to end by driving an axe
blade or wedge into the end grain, on the line ofone ofthe rays
which radiate from the centre in the heartwood. Because this
method was fastand convenientand required only the simplest
oftools, it was used widely by the early settlers and colonists
across the world. The rough finish of at least one surface
prevented the capillary transfer of water that can arise when
shingles are laid without an airgap.

Today, traditional cleft oak shingles are virtually unobtainable in England and have yielded to the production of sawn
shingles. These sawn shingles have halfthe 80-100 years lifespan of cleft oak as they may be cut across the grain, which
leads to splitting. The appearance of sawn shingles is also
markedly smoother and less textured.

The use of shingles is common where trees are a ubiquitous
source of building material and has been noted in such diverse
cultures as Padju Epat in SoutheastAsiaand the northern slopes
ofthe Alps, where the use ofshingles has been correlated with a
climate suited to the production of fast-growing tree cover
(Cereghini). More surprising is the use of shingles in Sherpa
houses, where trees are byno means a readily available resource.

In America the species favoured for shingle cleaving was
white cedar, while in Australia the wood from the stringy bark
tree provided, in the words of Alfred Joyce, a settler of Port
Phillip, ‘slabs and shingles of a most superior quality, all
perfectiy straight and flat and of even thickness’. In England
shingles were of oak or sweet chestnut.

Morse, in his description ofJapanese houses around the end
ofthe ıgth century, notes that the cost ofa shingled roof was
less than that of a tiled roof, although shingles were by no
means confined to poorer houses. He describes the shingles as
being the thickness of ‘an ordinary octavo book cover and not
much larger in size, and having the same thickness throughout. They come in bunches, each bunch containing about two
hundred and twenty shingles and costing about forty cents.’

The shingles were secured with bamboo pegs in close
courses over and onto an underlay oflight boarding. A special
hammer, which hada brass strip on the underside ofthe handle
was used, the strip being pressed against the top of the peg,

308

forcing it into the single; the remaining peg was broken down
against the surface ofthe shingle, without the peg fibres being
separated. The handle ofthe hammer was marked as a carpenter’s measure, so thatthe courses were properly aligned. Morse
also notes that this flimsy means of fixing led to the common
practice of fixing narrow strips ofbamboo obliquely across the
roof from the ridge-pole to the eaves at centres of 30-45 cm
(12-18 in). Despite this, in violent storms, shingles often gl
the air at such times like autumn leaves’. In better shingled
houses, a wedge-shaped tilting piece was secured parallel to
the eaves onto which the first four courses of shingles were
fixed. Morse has also described other methods of shingling
used for instance on temple roofs, where shingles were laid in
thicknesses of 30 cm (12 in) or more, and trimmed to give a
rounded effect, in the manner ofthatch.

DEIRDRE MCDERMOTT

1.v.2.m-i Shingles: tejuelas (Chile)

South of the Bio-Bio river in southern Chile wood
is the most abundant building material and the traditional
exterior cladding for all sizes of constructions are shingles
(tejuelas). The most suitable wood for making shingles is alerce
(Fitzroya cupressoides) owing to the easewith which itcan besplit,
its resistance to rot and its light weight. The standard size ofa
shingle is 61x 1oxıcm (2 fix4xo0.4 in) although shorter ones
(afil) are used alongthetop row ofa wall or roof. Roofshingles
(partidas) are ofan even thickness, whereas those used on walls
(conicas) are thinner towards the bottom.

"The bottom third ofshingles are exposed and, when used on
the walls of houses, they are normally shaped, creating a
repeated geometrical pattern which produces a heavily textured
appearance that constantly changes with the direction and
length of the shadows. The most common shapes seen are
those with angled corners or rounded, triangular, lanceolate
and concave ends. The specialist manufacturer of shingles
(maestro tejuelero) has a favourite design which is much more
elaborate and is only repeated every three or four shingles.

sringles (tejuela)
house in
quinchao Island,

with shingle roo
