°

The commercial value of alerce has endangered the species
and living trees are now protected. A mature tree may reach a
height of45 m (150 ft) and live for 4000 years. In the Llanquihue
and Chilo& regions alerce shingles were widely used as a form
of currency and also as writing slates in schools. A barn or
church roof requires 60 ooo shingles and, with the large number of wooden buildings which are protected national monuments, other trees, particularly roble (Nothofagus obliqua) and
canelo (Drimys winteri), are now used for producing shingles.

The cost of shingles and their natural durability depend on
how they are made; the most valuable are split by hand with an
axe and are easily recognizable by the longitudinal ridges
formed by the annual growth rings. The least durable are manufactured with power saws and require treating, which has
altered the general appearance of the shingle buildings in the
region.

The old alerce shingles which become silvery white with
weathering were fixed with canelo pegs (tarugos) as the wood

corroded iron nails. CHRISTOPHER SAINSBURY

Cladding and Finishes

1.v.2.n

slate hanging and roofing
Slate as a roof cladding has many advantages over
other materials; it is more durable than thatch, it involves less
processing than clay tiles, it is relatively light when used in thin
slabs and is impervious to rain when well laid. Because of
its composition as ametamorphosed rock it can be split along
natural planes of cleavage. See 1.1V.4. Though sometimes grey,
many slates have a muted colour ranging from green to purple,
which imparts a uniform hue to a roof.
Slate quarrymen in England often obtained the raw material
by blasting, a process which caused the material to cleave in
large slabs, but which could also be very wasteful. When the
slabs were stacked to similar sizes the slate was available for the
craftsman who cleft the rock into thin layers. These were trimmed into long, thin slabs, often up to 40 cm (16 in) in width but
up to more than 60 cm (2 ft) in length, which could then be laid
on.aroof. The slate was ‘picked’ to create a hole through which
a wooden peg could be inserted. This was hooked over battens
attached to the roof rafters. Oak pegs were used rather than
nails which could slowly deteriorate with ‘nail sickness’ or rust.
Copper or galvanized iron nails with large heads could be used
though formerly ‘peggies’ and even sheep bones were preferred.
Commenceing at the eaves the slates are laid one above
another in staggered order so as to cover effectively all joints.
With the best laid roofs it was customary to place the slates so
that each succeeding layer overlapped the layer below by twothirds its length, thus producing a double thickness over the
entire roof. A pitch as shallow as 30° would be acceptable and
slate could be laid at much steeper pitches without problems.
Thesize oftheslates is graded so thatthose atthe eaves are considerably larger, both in width and depth, than the slates close
to theridge.

The need for slaters to call for stones of specific dimensions
resulted in a variety of terms being used to identify them.
Though some of these were curious, a degree of standardization resulted in a range based on the wives of the aristocracy,
Princesses (24x 14 in [61x 36 cm]), Small Duchesses (22x 12 in
[56 x 30 cm]), Marchionesses (22 x ır in [56 x 28 cm]) and
Viscountesses (18 x g in [46 x 23 cm]) among them. Smaller
sizes were less gallantly known as Broad Ladies, Narrow Ladies
and so on. The quality of slates was also differentiated; in
Cumbria the finer quality ‘Londons’ were sold for use outside
the region, the thicker and rough-edged ‘Toms’ being used
locally. By the ıgth century the thinner, lighter but less visually

309

1.v.2

Kinasujes veugois1yg@

## S. 105

5
e

1.V PRODUCTION

attractive Welsh slates had become increasingly common, and
were eventually to dominate the trade; slates were exported,
often in batches of only one size by boat or train, thus reducing
the practice of grading.

At the crest, shaped ‘wrastling’ (wrestling) slates cut with
slots that enabled them to interlock, spanned the ridge-piece;
alternatively, lengths of sandstone were chiselled to shape and
bedded on mortar. Slate roofs are not resistant to lichens,
though the smooth surface makes them less vulnerable than
stone. The joints were not proof against draught or driving
wind and snow, so that ‘mossing’, or the use ofmosses such as
the compressible sphagnum as an insulation was common in
Cumbria. Torching or tiering with a mixture oflime, sand and
horsehair anchored the slates in position, though, like painting
the slates with tar as was common in Cornwall, the practice did
not allow the timbers to breathe and encouraged wood beetle.

In regions with a slate tradition where walls are persistently
exposed tothedamp and rain driven bywinds, slate hanging of
walls is not uncommon. The method of cladding is similar to
that of slate roofing, with the pegs through the slates being
hooked over battens pinned to relatively closely placed studs.
The slates are generally small and thin to reduce the weight on
the pegs or nails, and they may be decoratively shaped to produce scale-like textures such as may be seen in Devon and
Cornwall. The use of slates of different coloration arranged in
patterns also enlivens walls, decorative slate cladding being
especially remarkable in Hesse and other parts of central
Germany. PAUL OLIVER
1.V.2.n-i Slate roofing (France)

Slate is a schistose rock and is easy to split into
plates between 5 mm (0.2 in) and to mm (0.4 in) thick. Itcomes
from quarries in blocks which are then cut, the larger slates
being placed lower on the roof and the smaller slates higher.
Hardwood pegs driven into thick, horizontal sleepers, prefer-

310

ablyofchestnutwood, hold them in place. The slates are bevelled
to enable rainwater to flow off. The stacking oftiles and slates
on the opposite side of a roof that is being slated is crucial,
so that the weight is kept in balance. On facades, slates 25 x 18
x 0.5 cm (I0OX7 X 0.2 in) are nailed into panelling and overlap
by two-thirds. In windy regions the slates are sometimes held in
place with lime-mortar. Slates can be cut rounded or straight
and can be laid in different patterns, but they must always overlap. In Brittany, the roof-ridge is formed with slates interlocking sideways in pairs (en lignolet) with different animal or horn
shapes, through the holes ofwhich the wind can whistle. Slate
roofs are used in western Europe and Canada but notin Asia or
Africa. FREDERIC AUBRYand PLEMENKA SUPIC
1.V.2.0 Stone roofing

Stone is only usable as a roofing material where it
naturally cleaves or can be riven into suitably thin layers. Where
this is possible stone may be used in a manner similar to the
laying oftiles of slates. Sandstones were widely used for roofs,
but the oolitic zones yielded the best material for the purpose.
The Cotswold region ofthe British Isles produced some ofthe
finest oolitic stones. Here the stone ‘tiles’ could be obtained by
natural processes. Slabs or ‘potlids’ of stone were laid on the
ground after quarrying and drenched with water daily in the
winter months. A quick thaw after freezing temperatures
cracked the stone into layers, requiring the minimum of cleavingand trimming to produce slates ofthe desired dimensions.
These were, however, notas regular or as standardized as those

farleft Slate-huj h
storey, Harbe;

covering, Lugurla, Ita}

See also
2.11.5.0/f Bril
‚Aquitaine

References

Ireson, A.
Denyer, Susan)

E
el

for slate; the skill ofthe craftsmen lay as much in the selection
and matching ofadjacent slabs, which varied in thickness and
shape, as in the laying and fixing ofthem.

Foratleast 300 years the village of Stonesfield in Oxfordshire
was the centre ofthe largest quarry system for themining ofthe
stone, which occurred in some five overlaid beds. Deep shafts
were dug in the late-ı8th century following a period of opencast mining. When the stones were dressed the slate-makers
or ‘crappers’ worked under corbelled stone or wickerwork
shelters. The ‘slates’ were trimmed to sizes recorded on sticks
or ‘talleys’, and identified as Cocks, Cuttings, Muffetts, Becks,
Bachelors or Wippets, and by numbers.

When laid the sequence was similar to that of slates, but
with a slight flare at the eaves, while the reduction in size as
the slates were graded to the roof-ridge was often more
marked. The roof could be difficult to work on, the angle frequently being 50° or steeper. With a load ofapproximately one
ton to every 9 sq m (Ioo sq ft) the weight on a roof was considerable. During slating it was necessary to stack slates on the
opposite side to preventundue thrustand unequal loading. The
greatest skill ofthe slater was demonstrated in the shaping of
the ‘swept valleys’ and curving roofs of the Cotswold houses,
especially between gables and dormers, for which specially trimmed ‘wings’, or polygonal slats were made. Asmall house could
be roofed by four men over four days. Though the Stonesfield
quarries closed early in the 2oth century others continued production, while stone roofing continued into the 19908.

Stone roofs in France are numerous, with Perigord having
many fine examples, as in the canton of Sarlat or the village of
Autoire. Often the stone tiles (lauzes) are laid almost horizontally, being stacked from the wall plate and slightly packed with
small pieces ofstone to give asslight inclination. Split branches
are nailed as battens to the rafters at intervals ofapproximately
four layers of stones. The ridges may be crested with tilesoron
barns simply with flat stones, but finials on the ridge-ends of
houses may be carved with great virtuosity in polyhedral and
other geometric forms.

Finally, mention should be made of the massive flagstones
used to cover the roofs of houses, boat-sheds and farm buildings, as in Sogn and Gudbrandsdalen, Norway. Some of the
stones are of remarkable size, being as much as 1.5 m (5 ft)
square; roofs entirely covered with flags 75 cm (29 in) wide are

Cladding and Finishes 1.02
frequently found. Many have a further layer extending in tapering stone fingers from the ridge. Their form suggests a possible derivation from the wooden planks that once were used to
hold down thatch, but their primary purpose appears to be to
deflect rainwater from the joins between the larger slabs.
PAUL OLIVER
1.V.2.p Thatching
‘Thatch’ is the generic name for any roofcovering
made of dead plant material other than wood. Grasses and
palm leaves are the most widely used traditional materials; many
others, such as seaweed, herbaceous fibres and large leaves,
also provide roofing material for traditional building in some
countries. Historically speaking, thatch, in conjunction with
rough pole timber, is the original building material. Since
thatch, at best, lasts no more than 100 years, precise information about vernacular methods before 1900 is only indirectly
available. Archaeological reconstructions, written evidence
and old photographs and drawings indicate thatthe vernacular
styles and skills of many countries are still used and in places
where change can be noticed, particularly in northern Europe,
the fundamentals ofthe craft are still wholly intact.

In rural areas of Africa and Asia thatch is still the only available economic roofing material. Elsewhere it has largely been
replaced by inorganic coverings like tiles or sheet metal, save
in a few northern European countries where, despite its combustibility, it is still used because it is a weathertight, durable
covering and looks good.

For thatch to be effective and durable it must be laid so that
water runs offthe entire surface consistentlyand quickly and in
a way thatallows itto dry outas soon after rain as possible. The
thatching material should be able to withstand the degrading
effects ofmechanicaland chemical damage, and, ofcourse, must
be both available and affordable. Thus, in Europe, stiff stemmed grasses, particularly water reed (Phragmites australis), have
long been recognized as the best material. Reed can last up to
Ioo years. Softstemmed grasses and straw do not lastas well, as
Palsgrave (1530) was aware when he wrote, Tam butapoor man,
sythe Ican notafford to tyle my house I must fayne to thacke it.

And, though there are differences in style which are most
apparent in the finishing details, all thatchers who use stiffstem grasses lay and fix their material in fundamentally the

same way. NICOLAS HALL

## S. 106

1 PRODUCTION
1.V.2.pP-i Grass and reed thatch

Like tiling, thatching involves placing the material
in ascending and overlapping courses so that rainwater flow is
directed as rapidly as possible to the ground. In place ofthe tile
the thatcher works with an armful of grass and his skill lies in
an ability to secure each armful, which may comprise several
thousand grass stems, so that each and every stem lies parallel
to the next one. The join between each armful and each course
should be invisible on the surface, and the fixing which holds
each armful must be at the same depth within the thatch.

Each armful ofgrass, wedge-shaped as grass stems are tapered,
once tied in place, is then driven under its fixing so that itis held
more and more tightly. The thatcher uses a legget, which is atool
like a large, flat mallet but with a ridged working surface to catch
and drive each grass stem upwards, wedging them into the
fixings. The legget (in Wales it was called a ‘dobren’, in Devon a
‘drift’) also allows the thatcher to produce a smooth surface with
an even thickness - approximately 30 cm (1 ft) is considered ideal
for economy and durabilityon aroofpitched at 45°.

Thatch is traditionally stitched to the roof battens using
tarred twine. With a straight needle two people are required,
one inside the roof space and the thatcher on the outside. Various labour-saving devices are used in different places. In Denmark curved needles are still common though copper wire has
replaced twine; in Holland thatchers use two needles-one with
ahook, the other with an eye. In England thatchers abandoned
stitching in favour ofblacksmith-made crooks. These are long
iron nails with a hook which holds a horizontal, traditionally
hazelwood sway which is laid over each course of grass.

Regionally distinct styles which survive in Europe are often
mostvisible in the finishing-offwork, particularlytheridge. The
ridge-capping is essential as it prevents water from percolating
into the body of the thatch through the otherwise exposed
fixings ofthe uppermost course ofreed. The distinctive, scallopcut patterned ridges which are traditional in the eastern counties of England, are made of sedge. This type ofridge has to be
replaced every 12-15 years whereas across the North Sea Dutch
thatchers, also using water reed, place large half-round fired
clay tiles to serveasaridge which can bere-used when thethatch
is replaced. Danish water-reed thatchers continue to ridge with
straw weighted down with a row ofrough-hewn wooden yokes.
This random-straw ridge is replaced every three or four years.

The different thatching terms of each region are another
indication ofretained tradition. In England thatchers (formerly
called ‘culminars’ ‘theykers’ or ‘reders’) secured the ridge with
sharpened hazelwood spikes, each bent double into the shape
ofa hairpin. Today these are generally called ‘spars’; in the past
they were broaches, buckles, scobs spiks, sparrows, sparrods,
speets or, quite simply, thacke-pegs. In Devon, the thatcher
carried his straw in a frail, a strood, a groom or a yoke. The
first layer ofthatch was made up of small, tightly tied bundles
variously called bottles (Berkshire), wadds (Devon), gavels
(Norfolk), batts (Nottinghamshire) or simply bunches (East
Lothian). In Norfolk, reed was cut by marshmen who sold it by
the fathom, which is a bundle of 2 m (6.5 ft) circumference.
Devon thatchers bought combed wheat straw by the nitche
(trkg [24 1b]), orthetruss (6.4kg [14 1b]). NıcoLAs HALL

312

1.V.2.p-i Palm leaf and atap thatch

Palm leaves are naturally occurring preformed panels ofthatch. Most ofthe work ofthatching is its preparation on
the ground. Coconut palm leaves are traditionally processed in
two different ways. In India they are split down the central rib
and the leaflets are then plaited together. The resulting thatch,
made up of panels tied to the rafters, lasts no more than two
years. On the East African coast a different process was developed which produced a thatch (makuti) with an eight-year life.
The leaf blades are stripped from the stem and then wrapped
over and stitched tightly to a batten so thatthey hang vertically.
Water is thus able to discharge rapidly offthe roof, rather than

VAL

‚gclay tiles tied
Nhmboo battens
Aapaim stems,

Outhern sudan.

Grass andreedi
being appliedto;
Wewewa hou:

(See alsofig, 2.

1, Connaught

1984

jean Roman

N palms/Pandanus

zigzagging slowly down a woven leaf surface. Southeast Asian
thatching is similar to the East African technique. Instead
of palm blades the panels (ataps) are made of long, pliable,
flat-stemmed grasses lashed to a batten. On the island ofBali a
traditionally made atap rooflasts about 30 years.

NICOLAS HALL

1.V.2.p-ii Roped thatch

Roped thatch is the usual thatched roof form along
the westand north coasts of Ireland, and its distribution is part
ofa wider coastal distribution on the northwest Atlantic fringe,
particularly characteristic of northwest Scotland and the Isle of
Man. There is some evidence that roped thatch was formerly
known down the eastern coast of Ireland as far south as the
Mourne Mountains in southeast Ulster.

The thatching technique involves the material being placed
fairly loosely on the roof, with a close network ofropes over the
top to secure the thatch. In general a close grid of horizontal
and vertical ropes were used, although in the northwest there
were usually only a few horizontal ropes. A particular problem
was to avoid the ropes cutting into the roof and eaves. This
could be countered at the eaves by introducing laths placed on
the thatched surface over which the ropes passed before being
tied to the pegs. The rope ends were usually fixed to a string
course of projecting pegs built into the wall heads, and a few
pegs on the gables. On the west coast of Ireland, the rope ends
were sometimes weighted down by beingtied to large, hanging
stones rather than pegs.

The occurrence ofroped thatch in exposed coastal locations
is usually associated with the ‘whaleback’ or rounded ridge
form achieved by using a saddle piece to separate the roofapex
or upper truss ends of the principal rafters. The traditional
thatching material associated with roped thatch is the naturally
occurring marram grass that grows on coastal sand-dunes.
This material contrasts with the grain-crop straw normally
associated with the alternative ‘scollop’ thatch technique of
inland areas. The ropes themselves were generally of twisted
straw-rope until the introduction in more recent times of an
imported sisal two-ply cord, widely known as ‘Hairy Ned’.

PHILIP ROBINSON

1Vv2.4 Tile

Originating in China, the Mediterranean and northern Europe, the use of clay roofing tiles spread, particularly
during the period of European colonization, to other regions
where suitable clays, fuel and roofing timbers were available.
Uplands clays in Africa, for example, are less suitable and the
relatively few examples of clay tile manufacture date only from
the colonial period. Adaptations ofthe tile types described have
been made to accord with local conditions. In the typhoonvulnerable Thanh Hoa province of Vietnam, interlocking
Marseilles tiles, introduced by the French, were wired on to the
bamboo roof structure to prevent uplift.

The shape of roofing tiles is governed by the need to shed
rainwater by overlapping or interlocking the tiles on all four
edges. This is in turn related to the pitch ofthe roof on which
the tiles are laid. The simplest tile, the plain tile, is rectangular,

Cladding and Finishes 1V.2

approximately 165 x 265 x 12.5 mm (6.5 X 10.5 x 0.5 in), and
includes two nail holes and usually two lugs on its upper edge
to allow it to be fixed to the roofing battens. Weatherproofing
requires a double lap and a roof pitch of 45° or more, makinga
plain tiled roofheavy and steep compared to other types. This is
appropriate in northern Europe where snow loads are shed at
these steeper pitches and where plentiful supplies of sound
roofing timber have been available. The larger S-shaped pantile
originated in Holland. Its use has since spread throughout
northern Europe and, during the period ofcolonial expansion,
to Indonesia. Requiring onlya single lap, lower pitches are possible, reducing the cost ofthe roof structure.

In the Mediterranean region, where there is little snow
and timber is more scarce, pitches as low as 30° are achieved
using Spanish or Roman tiles, which are shaped in the form of
a half-truncated cone; they are laid with a single lap, the curved
surface facing alternately up or down. Sometimes the lower
upturned tile is replaced by a broader, flat, rectangular tile with
upturned edges.

The Chinese tradition, which is perhaps the oldest ofall, uses
roofing tiles of a similar half-conical shape, but the roofs are
steeper and the ridges, hips and eaves more decorated and
prominent. Often the radius of curvature of the upper tile is
smaller than thatofthe lower, producing a strongly defined roof
line which is often curved, becoming less steep at the eaves.

With industrialization, interlocking tiles were formed by
Pressing out a more complex series of indentations on a rectangular, extruded clay slab before firing. Named ‘Marseilles

Hoya zalımen o

313

## S. 107

1.V PRODUCTION

tiles’ in France and ‘Bangalore tiles’ in India, they interlock
sideways with each other and lap singly in the vertical direction.
In this way both the pitch and weight of the roofcovering are
lowered and thetimberorbambooroof structure is reduced to its

simplestand most economical form. MAURICE MITCHELL
See also 1.1V.2 Earths and Clays

1.V.2.9-1 Tiling

Tiling is a very old method of covering the roof
in Asia and Europe. There are three principal forms - Roman,
gutter and Flemish. The oldest is the Roman tile with 2 types:
the flat, rectangular tegula, which has raised edges and measures 36x 26x 2.5 cm (14x 0x Lin), and the curved imbrex,
tapered to overlap. The 'leadingedge’, orexposed edge, is filled
with lime-mortar to make itwind- and watertight. The tiles are
placed on sleepers or panelling. Gutter tiles are frequentlyused
and are always half-cylindrical, tapering and overlapping, with
the ‘canal’ and ‘cover-joints’ alternating. The roof slope can
only be up to 35°. The firing must be perfect so the tiles are
sound, non-porous and can resist the weight ofa person. The
dimensions vary from 35 cm (14 in) to 65 cm (26 in) and the
thickness is 1.5 cm (0.6 in). These gutter tiles are sometimes
placed on triangular battens to enable the tile and batten to
abut, thus holding the tiles in place. Lime-mortar is again used
on the ‘leading edge’. Flemish or ‘$’ tiles are used in Picardy
and Flanders. They also overlap.

FREDERIC AUBRYand PLEMENKA SUPIC

ıus Integral Furniture and Fixtures

Abuilding is ahighlyarticulated shell, designed and
built to meet the physical and social demands of members ofa
society. As such it is a setting, a complex artefact which is
essentially inert. In some societies the building requires comparatively little to meet basic needs: a single-cell structure may
have a hearth for cooking and heating, perhaps a simple partition to separate the sleepingareas, orto providea pen foryoung
animals who may be protected within the dwelling unit.
Though simple, the habitual use of such basic elements and,
very frequently, their similar location in relation to the entrance
orthe centre, indicates thatthese are not chosen spontaneously
butare related to the norms ofthe cultural group to which they
belong.

The dwelling as a container awaits the occupants to animate
it with their presence and with the articles of furniture which
enable the building to function according to their specific
requirements. Furniture is therefore customarily regarded as
independent and freestanding, capable of being placed at the
owner’s discretion. This particularly applies to chairs or stools
which are usually relatively light and capable of being moved
without difficulty to the fireside or to atable.

The stool is probably the most ubiquitous item of freely
mobile furniture: it is a backless seat, often lower than the
height ofthe tibia, which gives support to a squatting position.
Most stools are three-legged so that they can be stood without
rocking on an irregular surface: this is the reason for the three
legs of the milking stool or ‘cricket’ table. Others, like the
Asante stool, may have a sufficiently large, flat base to distribute the load ofthe sitter to points of contact. An Asante stool is

I

personal to its owner, and its curving sides, each terminating in
an arris, ensure that only one person can sit on it; when notin
use itis laid on one side. The example emphasizes the possessive and territorial connotations thatare frequently claimed for
stools and chairs. In spite of their being unattached they are
often associated with a particular location within theroom, and
frequently with a particular user- thus, ‘father’s chair’. Even in
sophisticated societies visitors will wait for the invitation to sit
and may be directed to a specific chair.

Items of furniture within a dwelling often embody cultural
expectations and customs. So, for instance, the wall-bench
which still survived in use in British farms until the mid-2oth
century, was used by a farmer’s labourers, while members of
his family satata long table, with himself seated in the ‘carver’
atthe ‘head’ ofthe table.

The relative positions of the items of furniture were not
changed, and the farm labourer’s bench was often fixed to the
wall. Many peasant homes have few items of furniture, and in
Mediterranean climates, daily activities are largely undertaken
outside, where permanent stone, gypsum or tiled benches are
built against the walls. A matrimonial bed, a simple table, a
number of upright chairs and pegs in the walls to take clothes
are still the principal furniture of millions ofrural dwellings.

Where cultures still recollect the traditions of a nomadic
heritage, as in some Middle Eastern and North African regions,
couches and cushions thatline the walls around low tables may
constitute the principal furnishings. The Anatolian equivalent
is more substantial; a combined bench and chest which
provides a seating or reclining level for the male members of
the household, which continues unbroken round three sides
ofa room. Elsewhere in Turkey, in the pinnacled landscape of
Cappadocia, the excavators of dwellings, churches and monasteries also carved certain items oftheir furniture out ofthe tufa
rock, planning the sculpting process so that the required table,
bench or sleeping platform emerged as the waste material was
removed. Further east, in China, light couches, tables and
chairs were placed formally against the walls. They were also
disposed in relation to the built-in and often heated kang
or sleeping and reclining platform, which was already in use
in the Han dynasty. West African cultures use few items of
movable furniture apart from stools; instead, the laterite mud
that is employed for building is also moulded to provide levels
for seating, sleeping and other domestic functions.

While European furniture was theoretically freestandingand
designed to be placed at will, most pieces of furniture soon
acquired fixed positions. Often these were conditioned by
doors, passages and service rooms, by the screen walls that

315

## S. 108

EEG

1V PRODUCTION

separated human from animal occupants, or by the massive
stoves and fireplaces which, in different traditions, were
located on the crosswalls or the end walls, in the room corners
or beside the stairs. These and other significant structures
could dominate the rooms, influencing the locations of furniture over generations. They also created recesses and articulated space in ways that invited closure; innumerable settles,
cupboards and dressers were built into them.

Spiral stairs that rose around a central newel post were economical on floor space though their wedge-shaped steps could
be inconvenient in use. The inclination of straight flights of
timber stairs around a newel frame or in dog-legform occupied
more floor area, which invited closingto provide storage space.
Understair closets were often installed in British, North American and South African homes. New England houses sometimes had doors in the panelling of stairs beside chimney flues
that gave access to ham-smoking chambers. The potential of
the steps to be used as drawers was seldom exploited, but
examples are to be found from Switzerland to Japan.

Tables, couches, beds, chests — the articles of furniture
which have been found in European village homes and farms
since Roman times — were slowly augmented by sideboards,
cupboards and closets, many ofthese becoming integral to the
farmhouse or town house. Like the door-cases, overmantels
and staircases they were accepted as partofthe total fabric. The
acquisition of furniture related in part to the desire for greater
comfort, which seems to have arisen with the growth of
the merchant classes in the 17th century. It also related to the
growing use of articles of domestic ware which required
storage. In parts of Europe the dowty chest was an important
item in which to place a young woman’s possessions in preparation for marriage, while full and elaborately embroidered

© Pauloliver

316

clothes required additional closet space. It was an affluent
home which owned, or builtin, all the range of furniture which
was available. But successive generations inherited them with
the house and land from family patriarchs and yeomen forebears, acquiring others over time from rural craftsmen.

In cottages and other small buildings of farm labourers and
artisans the advantages of furniture that was integral to the
structure were soon recognized. A frame and doors in a recess
could provide a cupboard without the necessity of constructing, or paying for, the back and sides. Sometimes a built-in
structural element could function also as an item of furniture,
like the Welsh oak room divider which served as a screen on one
side and as a dresser on the other. But the greatest advantage
was the saving on room space that a folding, or built-in item of
furniture could provide.

Some furniture went overseas; the styles and forms of European furniture spread worldwide with colonization in the
17th-2oth centuries, not only with the exported households of
the gentry but in the memories and skills of craftsmen seeking
new lives as settlers. Among the emigrants were the Mennonites of Germany and Czechoslovakia, and the Quakers from
Britain. Committed to an industrious but austere life, the
religious emigrants saw godliness in cleanliness and virtue in
simplicity. None more so than the Shakers, whose desire for
uncluttered spaces within which to hold their meetings and
engage their hands led them to create an aesthetic ofeconomy
in design and craft which achieved some of its most notable
work in built-in furniture. Even freestanding chairs were
removed to keep them from obstructing the Shaker floors. The
chairs were hung from specially made fixtures - rows of
wooden pegs on battens at frieze level.

Fixtures are not furniture, capable of being moved from
room to room and place to place as the need arises; norare they
items of furniture which have been built in to the structure of
the building where they serve the same purposes as do the
mobile items. Insofar as there is a distinction fixtures are, inan
18th-century legal definition which still stands, ‘things ofan
accessory character annexed to houses or lands, which
become, immediately on annexation, part of the realty itself”.
They are often made for a specific function. Such fixtures as the
stalls in a stable, or mangers in a cowshed will differ in many
respects from the fixtures of a boat-house. As every craft or
skill, workshop or service building will have components
specific to the purpose for which it was built and the functions
it accommodates over time, it is evident that the range of
fixtures is considerable, and different in diverse cultures.

Nevertheless, certain fixtures are widely used throughout the
world though their type, size and means of attachment may
vary. The majority ofthese have domestic applications; though
they may frequently be utilized in a variety of contexts, they
share a number of characteristics. For instance, peg rows on a
batten affıxed to a wall bear loads which are temporarily
applied. Not only do they clear space, they also relieve the
weights from being exerted on the floor, and supplement the
inherent strength of a mass wall with its loads transmitted
gravitationally to the ground. Broadly these may be classified
with hooks and hangers, which may be used for the storing of

Kitchen inte
dresser, hol
Sachseln, Bi
centralswi

‚noldLamu

© Michael Lioyd

tools and implements, the keeping ofoutdoor garments, or the
display of trophies or weapons. Because they are affıxed they,
are technically integral to the building, but fixtures can generally be detached, to be found eventually in antique emporia and
public or private collections of crafts. Built-in furniture is less
readily disengaged from the buildings into which it is inserted,
while furniture which was integrated in the building process
cannot be removed without damage to the fabric.

Modernist interest in function and form gained inspiration
from the integral furniture ofthe Japanese tradition and ofthe
Shaker communities, but this did not lead to serious study of
the subject. It has been virtually ignored in the literature on the
history of furniture while fixtures are regarded as appendages
oflittle interestand no worth. Commercial worth is atthe heart
of the matter, for the commodification of furniture by historians largely serves the interests ofthe furniture collector and the
antiques trade. It is an aspect of vernacular architecture which
is inadequately researched, butwhich has a longand important
role in its complex history. PAUL OLIVER

1.V.3.a

Alcoves and niches

Usually found in buildings with thick stone walls,
alcoves are recesses set back from the inner wall surface. They
may rise from floor level to above head height, and be wide
enough to take pieces of furniture, even a bed or couches.
Alcoves, beingwide and large, require an appropriate spanning
system. For structural and aesthetic reasons this is customarily

Integral Furniture and Fixtures

with the aid ofan arch, which may be covered with plaster and
coved back to the walls at side and rear to produce a shell-like
enclosure. Alcoves that are set into a wall can expose a weakness, for the wall is made thinner by their insertion. This often
necessitates some protective or damp-resisting surface, such
as ceramic tiles, as is to be seen in many examples in North
Africa and the Middle East.

Tunisian houses with alcoves in the walls oftthe inner courtyards sometimes have tiled shelf surfaces in the recesses on
which a bucket may be placed. A small well cover enables
access to an underfloor or courtyard cistern (majin). Off the
courtyard are the principal rooms, in the back wall of each of
which is recessed the deep alcove (majlis), spanned with astone
arch (kus). Lined with couches for entertaining visitors, the
walls ofthe lower partofthe majlismay be covered with Spanish
tiles, while the upper third may have wooden shelves, gilt mirrors, paintings and decorative floral motifs. In more modest
houses the majlis has an upper storage space (mustrak). Larger
and bourgeois houses have a large majlis (kbu) which may have
a ceiling of painted wood and a Venetian chandelier hanging in
the centre (Santelli).

Niches are smaller than alcoves, frequently at eye level or
below, and do not descend to floor level. A niche may be
spanned with a single stone, or two stones inclined to meet in
pitched roof fashion. Sometimes niches will be found on the
exterior of.a building, in which case they are above head level.
They may berecessed overa doorway or even setinto acorner of

37

1.0.3

Lisl 3ono Ined®

## S. 109

1V PRODUCTION

adjacent walls. Many niches, both interior and exterior, May
have a votive object, such as the image ofa saint, placed within
them. Others, found beside open fireplaces in western European houses, accommodated a salt-box or salt-jar, the warmth
ofthe fire preventing the salt from getting damp. Occasionally
a family Bible would be kept in the niche for the same reason.
Among the many instances where generous use is made of
niches, mention should be made of the ndani, the innermost
room and ritual space of the Swahili house. Its rear wall has
rows ofniches (zidaka) in which were placed Chinese porcelain
plates, regarded as protective charms against malevolent
spirits (jinni), but also displayed as status symbols (Donley-
Reid). Now in an advanced state ofdeterioration but ofnotable
quality are the many niches in the walls ofhouses in the island
town ofSuakin, offthe Sudan coastofthe Red Sea. Some niches
and narrow alcoves had two-leafed cupboard doors and were
fitted with shelves. The upper section was usually pierced orleft
open, to permit adequate ventilation. All had stone-built
arched spans carved in scallops, cusps and ogee shapes, of
which ‘like all decorative features in Suakin, there were never
two alike’ (Greenlaw). PAUL OLIVER
1.V.3.2-i Lighting niche (Central Europe)
Lighting niches (German: Wandherd; Czech: kb;
Lusatian Sorb: pjecak; Slovenien: leva) are a central European
cultural phenomenon that was widespread in the German-Slav
contactregion. A lighting niche is an alcove in the living-room
wall by the doorway leading towards the bake oven-orin some
cases inside the room and elsewhere - or a pedestal that
is approximately ı m (3 ft) high and 50 cm (20 in) wide built by
the wall. The alcove is oblong-shaped, measuring 60 x 80 cm
(2 x 2.5 fi) and served to light up the rooms and heat up the
meals. During the winter months, people would warn their
hands there while doing the housework. Open fires were made
in the lower areas and the smoke would go through a cylinder
opening in the upper walls into the chimney. The creation
of niches obviously led to the removal of open fires from the
living-room during the late Middle Ages and the early modern
period. Not only were the walled niches lit, but so were: the
chips of pine, birch and beech that were held by metal ‚pliers’
which were frequently placed on a wooden stand. The chips
could also be found in a metal basket that hung under the claycovered smoke catcher, as ‘a hanging fireplace’. Lighting

SI]
—N|

III

SI

© Josef Scheybal

niches were often the cause of fires. For this reason, building
regulations from the beginning ofthe 19th century forbade the
use ofopen fire especially in the cowsheds; in the living-rooms
where the cooking was done in the lighting niches-— particularly
during summer months - their construction had to complywith
fire regulations.

The introduction of oil, paraffin or kerosene lamps and

candles at a later stage caused the lighting niches gradually to
disappear from about the mid-ıgth century onwards. Farmers
would often turn them into alcove shelves for storing kitchen
utensils and tools; some of these shelves have remained in
certain old homes to this day. JOSEF VAREKA
1.v.3.b Bed outshots (Ireland)
Many vernacular houses in northwest Ireland are
distinguished by a projection, which is part ofthe main structure, large enough to contain a built-in bed. This outshot projects to the rear of the house and is located near the cooking
hearth in a corner ofthe kitchen. Its distribution in Ireland is
consistent with its more general distribution on the northwestern Atlantic fringe of Europe (it is also known in Brittany,
northwest Scotland and in parts ofScandinavia), and as such is
interpreted as an extremely archaic feature related to the longhouse or byre-dwelling tradition where cattle and humans
shared a single, unpartitioned space.

In surviving houses this feature only occurs where the house
has a gable-hearth or direct-entry plan (i.e. where the doorand
hearth are at opposite ends of the kitchen). It is unlikely that
bed outshots were formerly common to the south and east of
their present distribution in Ireland, as the hearth-lobby plan
type has been the traditional form in those regions.

The bed outshot is expressed externally as a projection outside the rectangle ofthe dwellingat the rear gable corner. Internally it enlarged the living space so thatthe bedaccommodated
impinged as little as possible on the kitchen floor area.
Its length was simply that ofthe bed accommodated, in which
traditionally the senior occupants of the house slept. Most
outshots were roofed externally by continuing the house roof
outwards. It is rare for outshots to have been retained when a
single-storey house was ‘raised and slated’ to two storeys. The
purpose of such enlargement and ‘improvement’ ofthe house
was to provide separate rooms for different domestic functions, including ofcourse bedrooms.

NOW AIDNa0

References

Frolec, Väclay, and.

houses.

Internally, the bed outshot is a legacy ofthe traditional use of
the kitchen as a multipurpose communal space. The bed itself
was usually screened from view by means of curtains draped
across its kitchen side, thus enclosing the bed on all sides. In
some cases, along the north coast of Ulster, the outshot was
encased behind timber doors, while documentary evidence in
the west oflreland shows thatthe screening was there achieved
by means ofstraw mats. Windows in bed outshots are rare, and
when they do occur, are often found to be later insertions.

PHILIP ROBINSON

1.v.3.c Bedding piles (Syria)

In most households of northeastern Syria large bedding piles (Arabic: nadidah, firsh, hudum; Kurdish: levin, geha
dusek) cover a wall of the family room. The upper part of the
bedding pile consists of mattresses, quilts and big pillows.
Each ofthem has a different cover colourful or golden materials are preferred. Some mattresses weigh up to 50 kg (110 Ib).
The undersides ofthe quilts are of white cotton and the pillows
are partly embroidered. The bedding piles form a large-sized,
aesthetic, mosaic decoration in a mostly undecorated house.
Storing them is an operation requiring careful forethought,
and needs at least two strong women and a ladder. Everyday
bedding is stored separately in a place more easily accessible to
the women.

As a dowry young girls and their mothers sew and fill up this
bedding, using the best raw wool or cotton, often products of
their own agriculture or sheep-rearing. If they can afford it,
every year after the shearing of the sheep or the cotton harvest
new pieces ofthe bedding are made, so that during a lifetime a
large bedding pile develops amounting to about ten sets. The
bedding pile decreases when children leave their parents’
house and take about three new pieces of bedding with them.

Allthe bedding has to be aired in the sun at least once a year.
Every two or three years the mattresses and quilts have to be
opened and plucked, even if they have not been used during
that period, because they are reserved for guests. Although a
normal household quite often has some guests, on only a very
few occasions, such as wedding feasts or funerals, is all the
bedding needed.

To keep it clean and away from dirt, insects and mice, the
bedding is removed from the ground. Traditionally, small wall

Integral Furniture and Fixtures 1.0.3

posts, wooden horses or camel litters support wooden beams
for the bedding pile. In the space between this construction
grain sacks, food provisions and suitcases ofclothes are stored.
Special handwoven tapestry hides the objects underneath the
bedding. These rugs (Arabic: äjim, sahah; Kurdish: bar) are also
used at the big feasts inside the tents. They are either spread as
carpets or used as tapestry partitions between the men’s and
the women’s part. This partition is the traditional place for
storing the bedding pile in the Bedouin tent.

For several years wooden or metal sideboards have been
replacing traditional frames for the bedding.

The sedentary population prefers to store bedding in niches,
which limits the amount of bedding. Until some decades ago
(and in other parts ofthe Middle East until today) every family
possessed only very little bedding. Nowadays women present
their wealth in beddingpiles. This isa resultofthe prosperity of
former sheep-rearing nomads.

The size ofthe bedding pile sometimes affects the choice of
the housetype and its dimensions. Moreover it gives a thermal
insulation for the exterior wall facing the weather on the eastern or northern side. KARIN PÜTT
1.v.3.d Beds (sleeping platforms): Inuit, Maasai
The bed is second only to the cooking-place as

a requirement of the domestic dwelling. Both may be accommodated outside the building itself; food may be prepared
and cooked on an open hearth or in a cloam oven within
the yard, while bedding may be spread in a veranda or on a
flat roof. But in much of the world the nights are cool or
cold, and the bed must be made within the house. At the
environmental extreme are the regions north of the Arctic
Circle, where the bed is always inside the shelter. The snow
house of the Inuit remains the archetype of architectural
adaptation to an inimical climate, and this applies particularly
to the sleeping platform (iglig).

Alevel plinth constructed of snowblocks, the igliq was built
within the Inuit iglu when the construction of the snow dome
was completed. Over 2 m (7 fü), the iglig occupied two-thirds of
the iglu space and spanned its width. The platform was used for
daily activities, such as sewing skins, or eating, when occupants
would sit on the platform with their legs over the front. Used
at night for communal sleeping under furs and skins, it was

Interiorofal
cottage will
outshot on
Isles.

© Karin pütt

## S. 110

1V PRODUCTION

built 75 cm (2.5 fü) in height to keep the occupants above the
cold at floor (natig) level. Insulated with a layer ofsticks, bones
or broken paddles, over which was laid moss and seal — or,
preferably, deerskins, itwas an essential structure which functioned as chairs, table and beds.

Such an internal construction was customary and its general
size and position established by the collective experience of
tradition in meeting the harsh Arctic conditions. In contrast,
the Maasai ofthe African Rift Valley also use an integral sleeping platform, though its design, form and construction in a
temperate climate are very different. .

Construction of a new dwelling within a Maasai enkang in

terms of its site, overall size and orientation is determined by
custom and the direction ofthe male household head. If there
is more than one wife each is appointed her position in the
enkang circle, but decisions related to the construction and
internal design arrangements ofthe dwelling are her own. She
is notleft to solve her own structural problems, nor is she lackingadvice on how the hutshould be planned for herselfand her
children. In consultation with other women in the enkang she
lays long canes on the site outlining the plan and the division of
spaces within. These are adjusted in discussion until the main
walls and structural supports are determined. The latter not
only assist in supporting the roof shell ofwoven branches; they
also provide the fixings for the poles ofthe bedframes and the
woven wicker screen walls. As the structure ofthe hut is completed so the wattles are woven across the bedframes, which ae
each covered with skins. The snail-shell plan of the Maasai
house prevents daylight from entering, but during the day tbe
platform level is illuminated at bed-coverlevelbya smallholein
the wicker and dung-plastered wall.

The Maasai sleeping platforms are integral to the structure,
but unlike the Inuit iqliq they are built and occupied separately,
and function differently. The wicker platform ofthe Maasai bed
raises the sleeper above the damp and chill ofthe ground, and
permits warm air from the hearth to circulate beneath.

PAUL OLIVER

1.V.3.d-i Beds (Ghana, n)

The activity of sleep and the places designated for
sleeping among the Gurunsi and Tallensi are directly connected to the design of the traditional compound and its
definition of private, semi-private, communal and public
space. The most important and communal element of Ghanaian traditional homes is the courtyard, the area around which
the entire family’s daily activity is oriented. The courtyard area
is an open, yet semi-private, area, used for bathing, cooking
and food preparation, dtying and sorting of foodstuffs and
grains and the housing of small family animals or fowl. The
most ‘public’ land is outside each compound wall and is used
for subsistence farming.

Bedrooms in a compound are not a collection of rooms
within a single building, butare one-room buildings connected
by open spaces and linking walls. Privatization ofspace teflerts
segregation of gender, sexual relations and age dependencies.
Often in traditional society (owing to Islamic and/or polygamous customs) where men can have more than one wife, there

320

PF6L JanIOINEd

develops an acute awareness of women’s work, women’s
cycles, women’s rituals and women’s possessions. Several
wives in a compound necessitate separate sleeping arrangements and thus separate sleeping quarters. The head of household will have a room and another room may be shared by
unmarried boys or unmarried girls. Small children sleep with
their mothers. Sleeping can also be an outdoor activity, weather
and semi-privacy permitting; private bedrooms then become
no more than storage places where closed room territoriality
defines the sacredness and safety of one’s possessions.

In closed private spaces the design ofthe bed is integral with
the design and material ofthe dwelling. In many instances the
built-in bed is constructed of mud that is moulded into an
anthropomorphic sleeping platform, 15-30 cm (6-12 in) high
and about 1.2 m (4 ft) in width. Such beds are moulded from
laterite clay and become the interior extensions of red clay
housing forms. The walls of these moulded beds are often
decorated with the characteristic red, black and white geometric designs in natural pigment. In many women’s quarters just
above these in-situ beds are private storage areas for hip beads
and other erotic jewellery. This is especially true in the northern
Nabdam region, near Bolgatanga and Navrongo. Here, the
Tendaana, or earth guardian, blesses the soil that makes the
house and also blesses the soil that makes the bed for future
generations.

Temporary or mobile beds made ofwoven jute, cloth or dried
grass are used both indoors and out either on the earthen
ground or on the flatroof ofthe buildings.

The use ofthe square, ‘manufactured’ bed in the traditional
northern round home guarantees immediate culture clash.
Because of‘standardized’ European bed sizings, beds imported
from southern seacoasts to the northern hinterland, upon
arrival can not be fitted into the small ritual-oriented doorways.
Imported beds often become status symbols rather than functional items in traditional homes. When imported beds begin
to define standard measurements for distinct spaces, it most

often occurs in the non-African context of the single family

structure, Nabkan
northern Shana,

See also for ad
3.v1.1.gngallk
3.V11.1.K Maasall

‚Andersen,
1977

oliver, Paul, %

dwelling. The notion ofsingle activity rooms with single activity

furniture is particularly evident where the notion of the multi-

use compound/courtyard scheme has been abolished.
RENEE KEMP-ROTAN
1.V.3.e _ Benches (Burkina Faso)

The central room (tutuini) ofa Lela house is used for
several domestic activities, such as eating, sleeping and grinding, and is also the place where visitors are received. 6-12 m
(20-40 ft) long and 2.5-3.5 m (8-11.5 ft) wide, this room is
formed by two curved walls, which join lateral rooms at either
side. The centre ofthe room is kept clear. Tables, principally
placed near the entry, are made from earthen benches 20-35
cm (8-14 in) high and wide. The dirt floor, sunk 25-50 cm
(8-20 in), is packed down and smooth. Earth dug out to form
the floor is used to make the terrace roof and the wall-benches
which are often built around the vertical posts for supporting
the roof. Various items, notably braided sleeping mats, are
hung from the horizontal ceiling beams.

Ateither side ofthe entrance are benches which extend along
the curved walls in a convex circular arc. People often sit and
talk on the benches near the entrance, in which seats are sometimes modelled; they also sit on the floor and on smallwooden
stools. Pots, calabashes and the like are often placed on the
benches which extend further into the room. A clay pot containing drinking water is placed on the bench close to the door,
usually to the right. Itrests against the wall, and on amound of
earth ora pot filled with sand affixed to the bench.

The back ofthe room is occupied by a platform which stands
against the concave wall. It generally forms a straight line with
the doors of the lateral rooms, and may be up to 2 m (6.5 ft)
wide at its centre. A large conic pot (ja) with a braided cover, in
which liquids or solids are placed pending utilization or transformation, is fixed onto the platform.

Granaries identical to those found in food preparation
rooms may sometimes also be placed on the platform, as are
baskets, pottery moulds and other objects of occasional use. A

pedestal may stand on one side ofthe platform, on which two
or three millstones are placed. The pedestal (50 cm [20 in]

Eikonostasi

wide, 0.9-1.3 m [3-4 ft] long and 40-60 cm [16-23 in] high) is

fixed perpendicularly to the wall, ahollowed part ofitservingas

an oil lamp. Grinding is done facing the door in the area
between the back walland the pedestal. This area is enclosed by

a small wall, and two or three sheep are often kept behind this

enclosure at night. The flour is milled directly onto the floor,

and walking is not allowed here. LUC PECQUET
1.v.3.f Benches and platforms (Dodecanese)

The use of a system of elevated wooden sleeping
platforms reached by various benches, with the space beneath
being used for storage, is found with manyand importantlocal
variations in the houses of the islands ofthe Dodecanese, and
might have also existed in several other areas ofthe Aegean.

A common forerunner might be sought in the small agricultural establishments of these islands. It consisted of a simple
platform (in Karpathos: kreatsoula) and the storage space
beneath (ambari).

In the more developed form, the sleeping platform was normally placed opposite the entrance ofthe single-space nucleus
and could occupy the narrow side of it (as found in Astipalaia,
Kalimnos, Leros, Patmos and Simi), the long side (as in
Karpathos), or one of the two parts into which the unit was
divided (as in Rhodes). The platform was called moni, krevvatos,
apokrevvatos, sofas, pataros, apataros, ampataros or mousandra, and
was placed at a height varying from ı m (3 ft) to 2 m (6.5 ft). A
carved railing prevented the sleepers from falling down. The
platform was sometimes divided into two, providing a separate
sleeping space for the children, at a different level. It could be
open towards the room, as in Karpathos, where it was called
panossoufi, or separated by wooden partitions with a door
towards the first part and shelves towards the main room, as in

Patmos, where it was called the panokravvatos.

The storage space underneath - called like the platform, krevvatos, apokrevvatos or ampataros, or otherwise, apokratos or apokriatos- could be subdivided and had the respective openings or
doors placed in convenient places. In the single-space houses it
was used for storing provisions, oil, wine, cheese, olives and
dried fruit. When the houses grew and had additional storage
rooms it was used for securing the valuables ofthe family.

The approach to the platform was by means of. bench (pan-
‚90s) with the help ofintermediate chests. The bench sometimes
extended around the walls ofthe room providinga sitting area.

321

o

## S. 111

[> Do 0

1V PRODUCTION

In many cases there were proper wooden steps or a staircase
leading to the various levels ofthe platform.

The whole of the construction was richly decorated. The
islands had carpenters, who, owing to their naval tradition,
were experienced, so the wooden parts of the benches and
platforms, the chests, as well as the wooden supports ofthe flat
roof, the various shelves and other equipment were richly
carved with various patterns that originally were oiled and later
painted. Part ofthe decoration was formed by the woven and
embroidered sheets, pillows, covers and blankets, which were
piled on the platform or hung from the railing. In many cases
an embroidered curtain, (Rhodes: sperveri) provided a visual
separation between the platform and theroom. The decoration
was completed by the sets of plates exposed on the shelves as
well as by other utensils, mirrors and portraits.

Sometimes a second sleeping platform (called specifically
mousandra) was constructed opposite the first, placedata much
higher level than the sofas. The mousandra could be placed above
one ofthe auxiliary rooms in cases where the original nucleus

had been enlarged. ALEXANDER G. KALLIGAS
and HARIS A. KALLIGAS
1.1.3.8 Benches and settles (British Isles)

Fixed seatingwas commonly provided from the 16th
century onwards. The earliest was the fixed bench at the upper
end ofan open hall, which would have had a long table placed
in front of it. Very few survive, but particularly in Devon the
evidence of peg holes in timber partitions shows their former
existence. Occasionally a carved bench-end remains in situ, as
in two early-rzth-century houses in mid-Wales. One also
retains the seat, a heavy oak plank fixed to the timber partition
by large wooden pegs. Benches with similarly shaped ends
survive in the chapel of Somerset alms-houses of 1635 and the
schoolroom of Wiltshire alms-houses 0f 1668. In 17th-century
Yorkshire timber firehoods were common instead of brick or
stone fireplaces. The partition atone side, calleda ‘heck’, often
includes a fixed bench. Most were wooden, but one house has
an unusual stone heck and bench. A simple version can be seen
inamid-Wales farmhouserre-erected atthe Welsh Folk Museum
atStFagan’s, Cardiff. The partition base isabeam wide enough
to serve as a seat, with a forked timber making a primitive arm
rest. A Guernsey house has a wooden bench built into the side
of the stone-walled fireplace, while many English fireplaces
have small rounded ‘inglenook’ seats built into the solid

OLinda Hall
J
N

stonework. Some were made more comfortable with awooden
seat, and even wooden arms in one Gloucestershire example,
and many have an elbow rest hollowed out ofthe stone.

In the ı8th and ıgth centuries settles, movable or built-in,
were commonly placed at right angles to the fireplace; the
settle back wentright down to the floor, and with the projecting
ends protected the occupants from draughts. Settles may
include storage space either in the seat (for blankets, linen and
so on) or in the back (for hanging sides of bacon). Some
include a shelfnear the top for crockery, pewter, or even books
(as in asouth Welsh house at St Fagan’s). Sometimes a built-in
settle consists simply ofone or two end-pieces and a seat, with
the wall forming the back, as in a Guernsey house of 1729. In
this case the settle faced the fireplace.

Other fixed seating was provided in stone-walled houses in
the form of window seats. In the better-quality houses these
were panelled, and some 18th-century examples include storage
with lift-up seats. LINDA HALL

Settles and seats (Devon)

Settles are benches which can accommodate from
two to six or seven people sitting side by side. Their essential
characteristics are the straight back, which in some cases may
be 1.8 m (6 fi) or more, the box seat, which may be opened from
the top or the front, and the shaped bench ends, curved to take
the elbows ofthesitters. The latter are often attached to ‘wings’
or extending board sides which offer some protection from
draughts or the heat ofa fire on the face.

Settles were not built for comfort, but some large examples,
as are found in the Devonian region of England, were constructed in a slight arc so that conversation along the length of
the seat was more convenient. Efficient as draught excluders,
many Dartmoor settles hada raked top which protected the sitters from falling plaster or thatch, and projected the pieces
behind the seat. When a number of people occupied a freestanding settle their weight was thrust forward. An inclined
back would have created the risk of its tipping backwards; to
counter this and the forward weight most large settles of the
Devon type would have a ‘skirt’ or flared base atthe rear.

Sometimes over 2 m (6.5 ft) in height, Devonian settles were
bulky and rarely moved. They were often built into the fabric of
the house, either with the back as part ofthe wall, on two sides
or even three ofa ‘nook’ or partially enclosed corner space, or
with one end attached to the wall and the sitting position at
right angles to the hearth. All types are to be found in “public
houses’, inns and taverns throughout Devon and Cornwall.

Related to the fireplace settle is what is popularly termed the
“inglenook’, a seat within the depth ofa large hearth chimney,
behind and perpendicular to the breastsummer that spans the
opening. Probably derived from the alcove seat of the ‘turnspit’ ofthe manorial houses, the inglenook seat provided both
warmth and protection from the draughts that the large fires
generated. But winter does not last all year, and among the
most popular ofbuilt-in features are window seats. Lower than
the window sill, these seats make use ofthe thickness of stone
walls, spanning between the splays, and are usually supported
by stonework. Because the wallingwould otherwise be thinand

vulnerable to risingdamp, and because thelid would besplayed
or cutoutofthe plane ofthe seat the volume below the window
seat is rarely used for storage. In cottages and farmhouses the
window seat obtained the last of the natural light of the
evening, making it an attractive place for sewing.
PAUL OLIVER
1.v3.h Boxbeds
Probably Scottish in origin, the term ‘box bed’ refers
to a bed structure which is integral to the building. Virtually a
room within a room, the box bed is, as its popular name
implies, encased at the top, base and sides, though there are
variants in the degree to which the side panels are fixtures. The
origin of the box bed is uncertain and it has been noted that
they seldom appear in inventories before the ıgth century. This
may simply reflect the fact that they were built into the domestic dwelling and were therefore not described as an independent possession or item of furniture. Several Breton examples
dating from the 17th century are known and classified. Box
beds are principally associated with northern Europe, but their
distribution does not directly correspond with cold climates;
they have been recorded as far south as Portugal.

Typically, the box bed was roofed, the planking preventing
fragments of plaster and thatch from falling on the sleepers
below, performing the same function as the ‘tester’, or fabric
cover, to the ‘four-poster’ tester bed. An Alsatian example is
clearly a tester bed which has been placed within a built-in box
frame, closed by curtains. Though curtains were often used to
close the box, especially the wood-vaulted canopy beds unique
to Ireland, the front of the box bed frequently consisted of
two to four panels fitted into rebated channels which made it
possible for them to be slid aside for access. The slats ofthe bed
were raised approximately 50 cm (20 in) from the floor, though
various heights have been noted. The height ofthe box seldom
permitted the occupant to stand, but this was less restricting
than the internal dimensions which were often little more than
1.25 m (4ft) wide, and less in depth. This has suggested thatthe
occupants were shorter than the average height of 2oth-century
adults, but it is possible that it acknowledged the common
foetal sleeping position. As built-in furniture was partly
designed to economize on space, the restricted dimensions
may simply reflect this.

In some cases, including Scottish and Ulster farmhouses,
the bed was set in an outshut (sometimes called an ‘outshot’).
The advantage was the shallow intrusion on internal space; the
disadvantage was the increase in exposed surfaces ofthe sleeping area. Thermally more efficient were the box beds that were
fitted on internal walls, or even served as a substitute for them.
Notable examples survive in the Hanseatic merchant’s house,
Finnegarden, on Bergen’s Bryggen (Norway); they range from
those of the master’s bedroom (hosbondsklaven) to the bank of
crudely constructed and shuttered box beds ofthe apprentices’
room (drengekleve). These could be accessed from either side.

While most box beds were sturdily built they often carried
carved details. These included a valance along the top rail,
sometimes an architrave, and decorated sliding panels. These
panels were often pierced for ventilation, with turned wood

Integral Furniture and Fixtures 1.V.3

bars, or shaped and carved motifs including the religious
monogram IHS; even, at /’Ile Sein (Brittany), with a small porte
du malade for a bedridden occupant. Among the finest in their
combination of peasant workmanship and decorative detail
were the Breton farmhouse versions ofthe French lit-clos. These
were frequently positioned between the rear wall and the transverse open hearth. Between the lit-closand the hearth a coffer of
the same length afforded a seat during the daytime, with the
closed shutters of the box bed providing a back rest. A small
shrine figure of the Virgin Mary or a local saint, was often
mounted over the opening. In larger farms arow ofintegral box
beds would beranged along the rear wall, each with a banc-coffre
delit before it containing bedding, clothes or grain. Inthe1g5os
such efficient ensembles of lits-clos were still in use in Morbihan, Brittany. PAUL OLIVER
1.03. Closets (Anatolia)

Inan Anatolian town house, aroom will haveatleast
one wall lined with a strip of paired windows and an inner core
wall in masonry in which the fireplace (ocak) and its chimney
are centrally placed.

In this room there are two major types of closet: one for storing the bedding (yüklük), the other for storing clothes (dolap).
The yüklükis 75-90 cm (31-35 in) deep and 120-150 cm (4-5 ft)
wide with a pair ofdoors. The bedding is folded three times and
weighs about 10-12 kg (22-26.5 lb). These closets are 65-80
cm (25-31 in) from the floor to make it easy to take the bedding
out. Often the space below the yüklük is used for ablutions
(gusulhane). These bedding closets are often found along either
side ofthe fireplace. The closet for clothes is less deep and less
wide, and may have one or two doors. It, too, is 65-80 cm
(25-31 in) above the floor. The space underneath the dolap is
used to store dailywood for the fireplace. There areanumber of
shelves inside the closet where various articles of clothing are
kept in ornate textile wrappings. The doors are ornamented
either by geometric patterns, different kinds ofwood, colour or
paintings.

On either side ofthe fireplace or ofthe closets there are three
small, vertically placed, ornamented openings (oyma). The
original reason for their being made is lost today; nowadays
they are used for displaying small items of either functional
(matches, candles) or aesthetic (vases and other objects) value.
Over the top of the row of windows and closet doors a small

323

BENNO MeaI109 0

## S. 112

EREBE

1.V PRODUCTION

OBilgiDenel

shelf (sergen) about 15 cm (6 in) deep runs all the way around
the room and is used for displaying and subsequent storing
copperware and ceramics. The fixed seats meet at a corner
where thewomen’s embroideries and other crafts are displayed.
Delicate handmade curtains for the windows are used predominantly for aesthetic reasons and fine wooden movable screens
provide privacy and ventilation. The floors are covered with
handwoven rugs and the ceilings match the craftsmanship of
the closets. In addition a fixed seat (sedir) runs continuously
under the windows. The seat is 27-30 cm (10.5-12 in) from the
floor and 90-100 cm (35-39 in) deep. The back of the seat is
45-65 cm (17.5-25 in) from the bottom ofthe windows. Since
these are dimensions used uniformly, they set a series ofvisual
bands as standards inside and outside. Often the space under
the seat is used for additional storage. Both the seat and the
back have long hard cushions (yastik) made of handwoven rugs

D1an Davis

324

with plain backings filled with straw. The patterns of these
cushions would traditionally match the patterns of the rugs
used on the floors. The relationship of the seat to the window
allows people to see out by twisting their necks.

BiLGI DENEL

Cupboards: bread (England)

Lake District farmhouses are known for their wealth
of oak fixtures and fittings. Of particular note are two-storey
oak cupboards built into one wall of the living-room opposite
the fire. Most were made in the hundred years following 1620.
They are of panel construction with the upper storey recessed
and often decorated with finely carved running-scroll and interlaced motifs, specific to the locality. The top rail of the cupboards usually carries the initials ofthe original owners and the
date of construction - often commemorating a marriage or the
rebuilding of the house. The cupboards were used for storing
clapbread, an unleavened oatbread, the staple food ofthe area.
SUSAN DENYER

13)

Cupboards: spice

Small built-in wall cupboards are commonly called
spice cupboards, but may have had a variety of uses. Most are
lockable, suggesting that the contents were valuable, and many
have a single shelf inside. They are usually sited in the hall or
kitchen, close to or even within the fireplace; this position
would keep costly salt and/or spices dry. One such cupboard in
Yorkshire, dated 1698, has a smaller cupboard above the main
one, possibly to hold the family Bible (Alcock and Hall). ‘Spice
cupboards’ are also found in bedrooms, where a more likely
use would be as a safe for documents, money orjewellery. Many

‚nd cupboard in
"oom, Hancock,
