11991

akers (MA, nv)

„Dert, 1979

date from the period 1670-1750, but some earlier ones are
known. A Wiltshire house of 1631 has two, decorated with
incised lozenges on the harr-hung doors. Harr-hung doors
may have been a common feature, but most spice cupboards
now have butterfly hinges.

The structure consists of a frame, usually 7-10 cm (3-4 in)
wide, set into the wall between 1.25 m (4 ft) and 1.5 m (5 fi)
above the floor, and a door which comprises a wide frame
arounda central panel. 17th-century frames and doors are often
decorated with scratch moulding, while in the 18th century the
frame may have a simple ogee moulding around the outer edge.
Some late-ı7th-century cupboards are elaborately carved on both
door and frame. A particularly fine Gloucestershire example of
1674 has a central acanthus design, scrolls, strapwork and stylized flowers, including the tulip that was so popular atthe time
(Alcock and Hall). Its size is typical of the average spice cupboard, withaıocm (4 in) frameandadoor measuring33x46cm
(13 x 18 in). A large central lozenge was another favoured
design, while in northern England itwas common to include the
date in the design carved on the door panel. This practice continued well into the 18th century in the Lake District. Elsewhere
18th-century cupboards are plainer; the central panel might be
surrounded bya projectingmoulding or by asmall ovolo moulding, with either a recessed or araised and fielded panel.

The Lake District is also home to the built-in ‘court cupboard’, a much larger feature but similar in its decoration to the
local spice cupboards. Itwas used to store bread and probably
also such items as pewter and silverware (Denyer).

LINDA HALL
1.V.3.K Drawers and cupboards: Shaker
‘All work ought to be faithfully and well done, but
plain and without superfluity. All things ought to be made
according to their order and use’, wrote Father Joseph
Meacham in the 1790s. The built-in furniture made by the
United Society of Believers in Christ’s Second Appearing, popularly called the ‘Shakers’, fully expressed this philosophy.
Much ofthe Shakers’ considerable output offurniture and artefacts was built for sale, and their marketing strategies, while
not unduly compromising their values in design, nevertheless
meanta concentrated outputofchairs, brooms, oval boxes and
kitchen industries. The quality was ofa high order butitwas the
integral furniture which was not for sale and which was made
wholly for their use that embodied their design principles
unaffected by the ‘world’.

When the United Society was poor and new the rooms were
‘very contracted’ and passages were narrow. The advantages of
built-in furniture were soon realized and, as chests, drawers
and cupboards were prohibited in lower rooms, they were necessary for the accommodation of the Shakers’ considerable
quantities of linen. Built-in banks of drawers and cupboards
were often used to define a corner, and ran from polished floor
to white ceiling. In the corner of Hancock’s Church Family
meeting room, dating from 1830, a stack of six wide drawers
was surmounted by a group offour small ones and a cupboard,
the whole assembly flanked by three tall cupboards, one above
another. Cupboards and drawers were made of pine and

Integral Furniture and Fixtures 1.0.3

varnished; others were made in butternut wood, like the large
case of ten wide drawers topped by two cupboards in the
Brethren’s retiring room ofthe South family, New Lebanon.

As cupboards and chests of drawers were deeper than the 23
cm (9 in) internal walls they were permitted to project into the
adjacent room, either to alternate and thus retain a continuous
plane, or to be capable of being opened from each side. Shaker
carpentry was precise, the joints were close-fitted but not concealed; such artifice, including the use of veneers was contrary
to their belief in truth in workmanship. Deliberate beautification was shunned in the belief that it was a vanity; occasionally
their aesthetic was stretched to the limit, as in the Church
Family attic battery of 28 drawers in four stacks terminating
with cupboards. But almost without exception Shaker discrimination in design and form, was unerring, whether itwas applied
to the installation of settles at Sabbathday Lake, sliding dıying
racks for the entire laundry of Canterbury, the chests for medicines in the nurse-rooms, or the wall racks to take the shoes
produced in the cobblers’ workshops. Even the woodstores
beside the neat, black Shaker stoves were ‘built into the walls
like closets ... so that no wood, splinters or dirt is seen’, wrote
a visitor, H. M. Pomeroy, as the 2oth century opened. By this
time the Shakers had reduced to 1350 members; a quarter
ofa century later all but six Societies had closed and the sect
went into slow but inexorable decline. Its built-in furniture is
one testimony to the Believers who had ‘the giftto be simple’.

PAUL OLIVER

325

EITTST

## S. 113

ER EEEERREFESEGEEETSESISeLGEERLnI an umEssnmusE mare

1.V PRODUCTION

1.3. Dressers
Built-in dressers developed in the ızth century from
a set of wall-shelves to hold plates or wooden trenchers (a
“trencher rack’ in contemporary documents) and a wider ‘dresser
board’, originally a place where food was prepared or ‘dressed’.
The simplest form had a wide bottom shelf and two or three
narrow plate shelves above. The ends might extend only from
the ceilingto the bottom shelf, but more commonly continue to
the floor. Their design varies from the simplest curves to elaborate pierced decoration, as in aGloucestershire example 0f1676
and a Monmouthshire one of 1690 (Alcock and Hall). Carved
brackets or wooden rods might provide additional shelf support. 1zth-century dressers are rare, butamong those surviving
are simple, three-shelf dressers in a Gloucestershire house of
1636 and in Wiltshire alms-houses of1668 (Alcock and Hall).
In the western Cotswolds and probably also elsewhere,
17th-century dresser ends continue to the ceiling; in the 18th
century they were more likely to stop short ofthe ceilingand be
joined by a moulded cornice. Dimensions vary, but most
dressers are between 1.8 m (6 ft) to 2.1 m (7 fi) long and are
2.1-2.45 m (7-8 fi) tall. The bottom shelfis usuallyaboutgocm
(3 fi) from the floor and 45-55 em (18-21 in) wide. Larger
examples, especially in the ıgth century, may extend along a
whole wall or even turn a corner to form an L-shaped dresser.
One such is in a Guernsey house dated 1805. Bythe 18th century
most dressers included various arrangements of drawers and
cupboards below the bottom shelf. Certain regional variations
have been noted, although there is more work to be done.
Gloucestershire dressers commonly have three drawers and
three cupboards. Dressers from South Wales have an open potboard below the drawers, with turned supports, while some
from County Durham have a mixture ofdrawers and cupboards
andan open area with a splat baluster to supportthe wide shelf
above. The hen-coop dresser is an Irish feature, with slatted
doors to the cupboards where the hens were kept. The same
feature is known in the United States and in the Philippines.
By the later ı8th century decoration on dressers was minimal; a Guernsey example has simple scratch mouldings on the
shelves and on the otherwise plain uprights, and is simply a set
of shelves for the display of china. The traditional built-in
dresser with drawers and cupboards continued to bea popular
and practical feature as late as the 1930, such as the one which
survived until 1992 in an Edwardian terraced house in Sussex.
LINDA HALL

1.v.3.m Folding furniture (Ireland)

Vernacular homesteads are frequently small and
governed by the materials used in their fabrication. In asimilar
way hinged furniture is economic ofboth space and the materials from which it is made.

The traditional Irish rural homestead was small, indeed, in
the 184 census, nearly halfofthe rural population was livingin
single-room mud cabins, although by the start ofthe 2oth century, the size and standard ofliving accommodation had risen
considerably and the one-room cottage had become a rarity.

By the 17th century timber in rural Ireland had become so
scarce thatin seaboard counties driftwood and timber salvaged

326

Le le IL oe |

from shipwrecks were a valuable source ofextra income and of
building materials. Driftwood was a common element in furniture, which was traditionally painted, thus concealing any
differences in the underlying timber.

The bed press was an article offolding furniture well adapted
to suit the space constraints of the household. This was a bed
hinged into a cupboard in a vertical position when not in use
and concealed by a pair of doors resembling a wardrobe. A
common piece of furniture in the ‘room’ or parlour, it was used
to receive guests and enabled them to be provided with full hospitality, whilst the journeyman was accommodated in the settle
bed in the kitchen.

Press beds were not as common as the settle bed described
elsewhere, but none the less were widespread. They are believed
to have originated from beds folded up into wall niches. Amore
common, and arguably more useful, form of fold-up bed was
the chiffonier, or low fold-up bed, where the bed was jointed in
anumber ofplaces, normallythree, and concealed in alow cupboard disguised to look like a bureau or chest of drawers and
which could be used like a sideboard. The apparent lack of surviving examples may bearesultofthe success oftheir disguise,
or itmay have been due to the difficulty ofstoring a mattress in
the chiffonier, which was more easily achieved in the bed press.

Other fixed forms of folding furniture were the falling-leg
tables, which were fixed to the wall and hinged upward when
closed. Theywere economical ofboth space and material, requiring a single support and being folded away when not in use.
Most falling-leg tables were fixed in asingle position to the wall.
However, in some instances a metal rod was fixed to the wall,
which allowed the table to be moved along the wall to different
positions, closertothefire, ortoactasa window shutter. This also
allowed its occupants to avail themselves of better light during
daytime and warmth during the evening or winter months.

Furniture in the rural house was frequently designed to serve

more than one function, as with the settle bed, which hinged
down into a bed from its normal function as a seat, Neither the
settle bed, the chair or hutch table, nor the settle table are built
into the fabric ofthe house but they are so ubiquitous and integral to the vernacular household as to warrant mention. The
importance of these items of furniture to the occupants ofthe
houses is endorsed by the fact that their transference can be
traced into countries which have been colonized from the old
country, such as North America and Australia.

Drawers &
B> —=| cupboards

A high-backed settle was converted into a kitchen table by
tipping the back, normally on long strap hinges, through go° to
rest on the arms ofthe settle, the seat of which was frequently
widened to facilitate its use as an occasional bed. A more ingenious hinging arrangement, where pegs attached to the arm
rests fitted into slots in the pine langes ofthe back, enabled the
back or the table top to be centred over the base and thus provided a more stable surface, less inclined to tip over.

Chair tables, often known as ‘hutch tables’, named for the
storage compartment formed in therelatively deep base, werea
variant on the settle table, formed by hinging the back ofa single
chair. These were not widespread and their lack of popularity
was attributed to discomfort. They also occur in New England
and in those parts ofEngland closest to Ireland.

A variation ofthe settle table named after a barony in Cork, a
county on the southern coast of Ireland, was the carberry settle,
with a hinged flap fixed to the back ofthe settle, which could be

let down to form a table. DEIRDRE MCDERMOTT

Integral Furniture and Fixtures 1.0.3

1.1.3.n Racks

Storage racks of various types survive in vernacular
buildings from the 17th century on; some are widespread, others restricted to certain areas. Spit racks are found in many
parts ofthe country; many houses had more than one spit, and
the rack kept them out ofthe way on the wall above the main
cooking fireplace. A spit rack consists ofthree or four wooden
uprights, usually carved in a decorative design to include slots
for three or four spits. The popular notion that they are gun
racks is false as the slots are too narrow (although some have
been damaged in order to hold guns ata later date). An unusual
Gloucestershire example of the early-ı8th century has four
upright battens each carrying three iron hooks, while another
has large wooden pegs similar to coat pegs. Spit racks sometimes incorporate a mantelshelf carried near the base of the
uprights, but often the mantelshelf is separate, supported on
its own decorative brackets.

Bacon racks were suspended from the kitchen ceiling by
large iron hooks and were used to store flitches ofbacon as well
as to hang bunches ofhherbs to dry. They were square or rectangular wooden frames with horizontal slats and could be as
much as 1x 1.5 m (3 X 5ft). Often only the hooks remain to
show the former presence ofa bacon rack. Guernsey has some
very large examples, as well as bread racks, which appear to be
unique to the island. These are narrow racks with horizontal
diamond rungs, and were used to store freshly cooked bread
while it cooled. Cheese racks are particularly common in
Wiltshire and Gloucestershire, where cheese was made in vast
quantities in the 17th and 18th centuries. They consist of fixed
supports for loose shelves, which were removable for cleaning.
The supports had a variety of forms, such as freestanding
ladder ends, freestanding post and bracket ends, or brackets
fixed to the wall. They are situated in dairies, cheese-rooms and
cheese-lofts. LINDA HALL

References

Alcock, N. W., and Hall, Linda, 1994

Hall, Linda,1983

Jekyli, Gertrude, and Jones, Sydney R.,1944-5

## S. 114

Openings and Fittings

1V.4

1v4A Openings

‘Openings’ is the general term used for voids in the
external envelope ofbuildings, most often in the walls, but sometimes in the roof; included in the term are doorways, windows
and vents. Openings provide access, admit light and allow ventilation, sometimes combining all three functions. They are
associated with a variety of means of closure, including shutters, doors, sashes and screens which provide security, protection from the weather and an element of privacy.

Fixed glazing to window openings or to their means of
closure, in a range of materials such as shell, mica, obsidian,
animal gut and even paper, all of which were used before the
invention of glass and continue to be so, allows light to enter
and helps to reduce draughts.

Openings as a means ofaccess are found in any construction
with an external wall and are frequently given great symbolic
significance. Many cultures in hot climates do not build walls
to their shelters and may have other means of defining the
social space attached to their dwelling unit, such as raising the
entire construction on a platform. In this instance, the stair
access may assume an importance akin to that of the doorway
orentrance.

The materials and form of the basic construction influence
the type of opening, its size, shape, location and incidence. In
framed construction, comprising regularly spaced uprights
joined by horizontal members at predetermined levels, such as
attheeaves or wall plate, atsuspended floor level oratsole plate
level, openings can be inserted with ease and framed by an
additional horizontal door or window ‘head’ orsill; or they may
have subsidiary shaped horizontal or vertical members set in
the timber framing to form a variety of door-opening shapes.
Examples of these, still identifiable in older English houses,
include shouldered or cranked, four-centred, round, ogee,
pointed or segmental door-frames.

Openings are less easily made in solid, load-bearing walls,
without compromising the inherent strength of the structure.
In banco or puddled mud construction, likened to coil pottery,
where walls derive their strength from the continuity of their
circular form, there are few openings. Those that exist are
restricted in size, are commonly round or oval and are cut into
the wall after its completion. The doorway may be strengthened
byathickening ofthe mud to express the opening and to form
a cowl, which has the additional function of shedding the rain
and diverting drips.

Where openings are formed during construction of mud
walls, alintel, or other means ofspanning the opening is introduced. This may comprise a single piece of timber or even
stone, or multiple scantlings which derive their strength from

328

spreading the load between them. The lintel must conduct the
gravitational load ofthe building, which in the case ofmasonry
construction may be great. Techniques have been developed to
spread this, as where lintels ofsoft stone are arched by a pair of
stones supported on a short oak billet in the Quercy region of
France.

There is an inherentcompromise in the concept ofopenings.
As well as a loss of strength, larger openings created to admit
more light result in a loss of privacy or a reduction of security,
which may restrict or determine the location or incidence of
such openings or their configuration.

Many cultures have developed dwelling types in which the enclosing external wall is breached only byasingle door, for defensive or environmental reasons. In parts of New Mexico, often
only a single entrance breached the compound wall, generally
taking the form ofa double gate through which awagon could
be driven into the yard beyond. Windows were small and were
barred, ifthey existed at all. This almost windowless form was
typical ofthe shifting frontier circumstances where settlements
were formed in the face ofa hostile indigenous population.

In other cultures, the plan configuration of the entrance as
well as the shape of the door itself have been developed for
defensive reasons. The plan has been contorted so that the person entering cannot see directly into the house, but must make
a series ofturns through a restricted space. The height ofthe
door may be reduced so thatthe person entering must stoop or
practically crawl, making itimpossible to enter on the offensive.

Mica windows,
pueblo, New
United State:

for 1.V.4-8
‚and Silcock, A.,

In other instances, high sills have to be stepped over, a practice
which has an additional function of excluding animals.

Defence, which was such a significant influence on the
arrangement of the house, is now rarely a consideration in
house form, and other issues such as daylight and ventilation
have become paramount.

In much ofthe Middle Bastand many other parts ofthe world
influenced by Islam, the rooms of the house are grouped
around a courtyard, and doors and windows only occur facing
onto this internal space. The external, enclosing walls are
blank and access is through a single door. This plan form, with
limited openings in the external walls, enables a very dense settlement pattern to develop.

The introverted plan form developed as a result of harsh
climate is also suited to cultures where women are notexpected
to mingle or even to be visible to the public. Entrances
and guest-rooms are arranged so that the visitor does not have
to enter the private part ofthe house, but can be entertained in
rooms close to the entrance, with no other physical connection
to the remainder of the house and with no direct view to the
interior. Openings at the upper levels overlooking the street,
particularly the rooms where women sit or work, are screened
heavily by an intricate latticework which permits ventilation
and may provide a vantage point to the outside for the women,
whilstshielding them from view, as in the mashrabiyyat ofEgypt.

Larger openings are appropriate in hot, humid climates,
where the diurnal variation is minimal and where cross-ventilation must be maximized to ensure body comfort. The West
African indigenous coastal builder developed a wall system
frequently ofbamboo, echoed by the matting systems ofmuch of
Oceania, which allowed the passage of cooling breezes. Early
colonial settlers to West Africa adapted these solutions byraising
their mansions high above the ground and incorporating walls
comprisinga series oflouvered doors and screened verandas.

Where diurnal variations are extreme, as in the savanna belt,
earth walls can accumulate and store daytime heat to temper the
cold ofthe evenings. Openings are keptto aminimum to exploit
the thermal properties ofthe mud walls and to restricteither the
loss ofheatatnightorthe incursion ofheated air duringthe day.

In hot climates, the resulting dark interiors are a relief from
the often brilliant sunshine outside. The houses themselves are
used only for storage or sleeping and the compound or private
space surrounding the house or houses is used as kitchen,
living-room, dining room and workroom.

In colder, more temperate climes, the major function of
window openings is to admit light. The level of illumination
achieved internally is dependent upon a combination of free
window opening size, surface reflectivityand depth ofroom.

Window openings with splayed reveals were developed to
maximize the amount oflight thatreached the interior and diamond-section mullions were placed in the frame to similar
effect. Certain types of building, such as weavers’ shops, were
identifiable by rows of windows almost uninterrupted by wall.
Thinner mullions and glazing bars and the tradition of painting the window reveal white, which became standard and
enshrined in city codes in Georgian times, developed to maxi-

mize the light entering. DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

Openings and Fittings 1.V.4

1v.4-B Fittings

Fittings are artefacts that are attached to other
elements ofa building, such as window catches, to assist their
function. It follows that they are subservient to the building or
to apart ofit, and therefore they are not determinants ofits form.
The majority of fittings are subject to heavy wear and use and
often receive unequal and sometimes unpredictable stresses.
For this reason, materials that are low in tensile strength and
are easily fractured, including stone and ceramics, are seldom
used. Until the middle of the 2oth century the majority of
fittings were fashioned in wood or wrought in iron.

Many fittings were and are made to cope with live loads in a
building. Some live loads are created by the movement of people and animals, and others are generated by natural forces
such as wind pressure or accumulated precipitation. Some
fittings, from weathervanes to gutters, are designed to utilize
these forces or channel and deflectthem. Butthe live loads that
fittings are principally made to bear or control are the outcome
of purpose-made elements in the building, most notably those
that assist the swinging and fastening of doors and windows.
Hinges, draw-bolts, latches and locks are all fittings ofthis kind,
sometimes referred to as ‘door furniture’. Related to these are
fittings that have been made for purposes of security and safety,
such as balcony railings and handrails, which remain inert but
which are intended to constrain live loads and give support.
Window grilles are similar, not subjectto loads for long periods,

but made to resist forces suddenly applied. PAUL OLIVER

1V.4.b Doors (Europe) 1.V.4.b-i Doors: Cape Dutch and British ne

17th-century type of false panelled door had three planks, the Another common group ofdoors had upper leaves with thetop enkouce weather protection options ofa sash window. Cylinder hinges a uw —

central one thinner than the others, with moulded horizontal
timbers applied to the central plank to mimic true panelling.
Truly panelled doors followed the same designs as wall
panelling. From the late-ı6th century doors with six or eight
equal-sized panels were used either on their own or as part ofa
panelled room. Later in the 17th century larger panels became
fashionable and two-panelled doors were popular. Sometimes
there were one, two or three small panels between the two large
ones. These designs occur in doors that are truly panelled, but
were also created by applying mouldings to plank and batten
doors. Projecting mouldings were popular in the late-ı7th and
early-ı8th centuries; alternatively, a plain recessed panel could
be surrounded by a small ovolo or ogee moulding. From about
1670 the panels could be raised and fielded, and in the 18th
century the door with six panels of unequal size became the

norım in better-quality houses. LINDA HALL

330

bolection moulding stepped upwards, often with combinations of convex and concave curves that echoed the curvilinear
forms ofplaster gables. Doors ofthis type usually had plain rectangular bottom panels, sometimes with rounded corners.
One particularly interesting autochthonous type of door also
found in Cape Dutch houses was sometimes used as the front
door, but more commonly led out from the agtherhuisatthe back
ofthe house. The top leaves of Cape Dutch external doors were
almost never glazed, but this type of door was, in effect, acombination ofan inward opening, horizontally divided door and a
vertical slidingsash window, setwithin asingle frame. The upper,
fixed sash became the fanlight, and the lower sash covered the
top leafofthe door and closed on a projecting moulding on the
lower door-leaf. With this sash fully raised, the door, whether
open or closed, looked and functioned like a normal door; with
only the top leaf open, it gave all the usual light, ventilation and

were occasionally used but doors were usually hung on pintles.
Strap hinges with nailing pads were the most common, but
cockscomb hinges were also sometimes used.

After the Cape became a British colony, one of the most
distinctive influences ofBritish taste was on joinery patterns. At
first this influence was restricted to the vicinity of Cape Town
and to areas ofspecifically British settlement, such as the Eastern Cape, but after about 1845 it spread throughout the rural
Cape, where Georgian joinery patterns remained popular until
about 1875. Even in buildings where traditional Cape features
such as gables were retained, front doors almost invariably
took on Georgian forms. The doors ofhigh-status houses were
almost universally divided vertically, with two leaves, and were
sometimes framed by pilasters or other mouldings in plaster.
Earlier doors usually had four panels per leaf, but after about
1860 often only three panels per leafwere used. The lower panel

€

© DerekandVivieneTapha
was usually flush and was surrounded by a delicate groove
moulding. The upper panels were recessed, sometimes with
fielded centres, and were surrounded by bolection mouldings.
The doors always had decorative fanlights.

Very little is known of the craftsmen who made the doors of
Cape Dutch homesteads — both immigrant and slave artisans
were probably responsible for particular cases. During the
British period, many British artisans emigrated to South Africa.
Some are known to have lived in small country towns, so it
can be assumed that some of this joinery was made by them.
However, local craftsmen were probably also involved in their

manufacture. DEREK JAPHA and VIVIENNE JAPHA

331

## S. 116

TG

1.v PRODUCTION

1.V.4.b-ii Doors (Yemen)

Entrance doors in Yemen are the subject of special
treatment, in terms of both the surrounding surfaces and the
material from which the door is made. In the mountains, earth
and stone buildings differ in detail. In earth construction,
particularly when the coursed clay method is used, the door
may be set between buttresses (e.g. in the Mashrig where red
ochre stripes are a further enhancement) or within a projecting
box made of flanking buttresses supporting a wide lintel
(characteristic ofthe Jabal northern highlands). More often in
transition areas or in areas of mixed technologies, a space in
the form ofan inverted trapeze may be set in the earth walland
filled with stone to follow a technique developed in stone buildings. In these situations the dooristopped byan arched tympanum, which may be blind but generally has one or multiple
perforations, made with staggered stone blocks, to ventilate
the entrance hall. The treatment of this area may identify
regional variations. In the Tihama this tympanum may either
not exist or else be rectangular and made by trellised woodwork. Itis in the Tihama, in fact, that woodwork supplies most
of the individual character of entrances, with typical carved
brackets on either side and laborious carvings on the panels
and muntins with elements seen elsewhere in the Muslim and
Indian tradition. In the mountains, decoration ofdoors may be
reduced to the block for the metal knocker, the door panels
themselves being plain. Otherwise decoration either enhances
structural components (door-jambs and particularly mullions)
or covers the whole surface. Linear or cuneiform incisions
made along determined conventions of form - including arch,
cirele, foliated circle, ogee and cusped arches- and carvings of
stylized floral motifs and Koranic inscriptions, are occasionally
associated with more spontaneous expressions, such as figurative representations of trees and birds. Mother-of-pearl and
brass inlays appear in the best examples, especially in interior
doors. Normally, however, the only non-wood elements ofthe
doors are the metal roses and rings, in the middle ofthe panels,
and the fixtures — hinges, catches, handles - ofa simple kind,
although a degree of fantasy may be applied even in these.

As in the case of windows, and for the same reasons, door
carpentry declined rapidly from 1948 and 20 years later was reduced to the bare essentials as is evident from the unadorned
entrance doors ofthis period. By the early 19708, house doors
and the wide gates required by motor cars and shops were
being made of metal which was painted and decorated with
welded volutes. Throughout the decade these doors developed
remarkable approaches both in design and colour combinations, but eventually they settled on a pattern similar to the first
models: uniform colour and juxtaposed, forged work with
repeated stereotypes, such as volutes and rosettes.

FERNANDO VARANDA

1.V.4.C Doorways

Doorways are a feature common to all buildings
which are enclosed or defined by an external wall. The doorway
is avoid in the enclosing wall, through which people enter the
space orbuilding beyond and which can be barred orclosedbya
door, the weight of which must be supported by the doorway.

332

Whilstall doorways are entrances, notallentrances are doorways.

The functions of doorways are many and varied and are frequently reflected in the form ofthe doorway and its door. Atthe
most basic level, doorways are simply entrances which can be
closed and which are shaped by the nature ofthe materials from
which they are made. Amongst both the Zulus and the Xhosa
they are low, formed by arched saplings and woven grasses.
The size and shape ofthe doorway are dictated by the length of
the saplings which are bent over to form the framework for the
huts. The doors were very often little more than a framework of
saplings pulled into position across the doorway and wedged
into place from the inside to provide protection ftom the
weather or to provide privacy or security.

The construction of doorways may be simple, as when a
section of wall is omitted and the resultant void or opening is
topped by a lintel ofmud or timber. In circular mud construction the inherent strength of the cylinder is severely compromised by the insertion of a large opening, and thickened
surrounds may have more than the purely decorative or symbolic
function of accentuating the entrance, acting as buttresses to
the wall. The Hausa gemu, which translates as ‘beard’, and
which is ascribed to all cantilevered or corbelled parts of the
construction, provides a coping above the outer doorway and
shelters it from rain. This coping projects out from the walland
is supported by kahwan karo which, in the tailor’s vocabulary,
means ‘double thickness material either side of the back and

front centrepieces in the upper part ofthe gown’.

and Paul,
Varanda, Fel

BEREDEREER

The development ofstone quoins, sometimes usinga harder
and more durable stone, both enabled the doorway to support
the weightoflarge and heavy doors and allowed the doorway to
be given greater emphasis in the wall. Mollison describes how,
in Quercy, the re-use in newer buildings ofthese doorways salvaged from older, decaying structures is acommon feature.

Orientation as well as shape and dimensions of doorways are
frequently influenced by defensive considerations. Doorways
may be aligned in the wall so as to prevent those outside from
havinga clear view to the space beyond. Or, as in many cultures,
from the Cross River peoples to the Maasai, the doorways are
aligned facing inwards towards the centre of a compound
formed by a continuous and circular grouping of huts, and
which itself has only three or four entrances.

On the Malaysian peninsula, doorways can still be seen set
into the facade of the houses ofthe wealthy Baba Nyonya merchants, with the first floor jettied above, into which a peep-hole
was set through which to observe and even to assault those
below.

The Igbo of Nigeria constructed doorways which were often
trapezoidal, with a high threshold of up to 50 cm (20 in) of
moulded loam. The bottom of the opening was often narrowed, so that people had to squeeze their feet through, and in
such cases a special support was provided for the elbows to
assist in this manceuvre.

Lowering the lintel so that those entering are forced to bend
almost double or even crawl was not always a response to the

Openings and Fittings 1.V.4

need for security. In the snow houses of the Inuit a long low
passage, dipping down to below floor level was used as a device
to prevent warmer internal air from escaping and to exclude
cold draughts.

Amongstthe Hausa doorways were oftwo kinds: outer doorways closed bywooden doors hung on pivots, and inner doorless
entrances allowing passage between rooms. External doorways
were rectangular, bridged by flatwooden lintels formed of several slender timbers (azara) supported either on the mud walls
or on abutments formed by a slightthickening ofthe walls.

Internal doorways - or more properly entrances - were commonly oftwo parts, the lower part being a plain rectangle, the
upper parta semicircular arch ofa diameter some 30 cm (1 in)
greater than the width ofthe lower part. The shelfthus formed
was used to place lamps on. The arch was supported by a hidden internal frame made from two wooden azara slabs setatan
angle in grooves hacked out of the bottom of the arch and
reaching the height of the apex. After the adjacent walls were
built up to the height ofthe apex, a horizontal beam was laid
across the gapand the diagonal azaras and this trapezoidal form
was then plastered to a semicircular shape. These doorways were
sometimes screened by hanginga mat from a hook moulded in
the mud above the door.

As with any other part ofa building, the material used in the
construction ofthe doorway influences the nature ofthe decoration. Thus, where construction is in mud, decoration may be
incised or moulded and, as amongst the Hausa, may be
expressed three dimensionally through the gemu or canopy
above the door, right up to the parapet, terminating in finials
(zankos), all ofwhich may be lavishly moulded or carved.

Where construction is in reed or grass, the binding lends
itself to decorative treatment whilst serving the more prosaic
function of strengthening the building at its most vulnerable
and heavily used entrance. The Baganda of Central Africa form
the doorways to their huts in coloured cord made from papyrus
fibre; the finest reed and the most expert stitching are used
closest to the door.

The emphasis given to the doorway is an expression of its
importance as an entrance from the outside world to the privacy
of the enclosed space within. It may also denote status, as
amongst the Zulu, where the huts ofthe priest-diviners are distinguishable from those of ordinary people only by the horns of
ritually sacrificed animals above the doorways.

The doorway may also carry symbolism not immediately
apparent to the uninitiated. Amongst the Tetum of Timor the
front of the house is called the ‘eye’ and may be used only by
men and boys who have achieved puberty. The rear door is
known as the ‘house vagina’ and, in common with many other
cultures who also practise body and gender symbolism, is the
preserve ofthe women, along with the working area to the rear
ofthe house. DEIRDRE MCDERMOTT
1.V.4.d Fanlights (Southern Africa)

Above the entrance doors of Cape Dutch houses
there was usually a fanlight, divided into small panes. Circular,
radial and other complex curvilinear patterns were all used in the
fanlights ofimportant buildings, in some cases with mullions

333

## S. 117

——— —

1V PRODUCTION

elaborately carved into leaf forms. More commonly, however,
particularly in simple buildings, orthogonal geometry was
used. Some fanlights were no more than simple, horizontally
proportioned rectangles, two or three panes high and five
panes wide. More commonly they were square in proportion,
four panes high by five panes wide, with heavy mullions forming a simple rectangular grid. In some ofthe most interesting
Cape Dutch fanlights this basic rectangular grid was deformed
by bowing the mullions, using combinations of convex and
concave curves for the horizontal mullions, or by stepping
them upwards towards the centre ofthe fanlight. The transom
and door head were sometimes stepped or curvilinear as well,
following the profile ofthe horizontal fanlight mullions above.
Cape Dutch fanlights often incorporated a lantern.

The fanlights in houses with British-influenced joinery
patterns took one of several typical forms. Some were semicircular, with radiating mullions with or without concentric
intermediate mullions. Sometimes the semieircle was set
within a rectangle, again with radiating mullions. Various geometrical designs based on combinations ofcurved and straight
mullions were set within a rectangle; for instance, circles,
lozenges, segments making floral patterns and Gothic pointed
arch forms were often used. Curved mullions were often
employed for decorative effect only, the wood being placed over
the glass, which was cut into rectangular panes behind the
curved mullions. Finally, various simple patterns of intersecting straight mullions within rectangles were employed.

Some of these forms existed in Cape Dutch houses as well.
The decisive difference, however, was in the character of the
detailing. In British-inspired joinery the mullions were cut
much thinner and deeper than those in typical Cape Dutch fanlights to provide the necessary structural rigidity but also to
give the elegance that had become an aesthetic objective.

Various types ofwood were used. In Cape Dutch houses high
quality hardwood, such as oriental teak, was often used for
front doors and fanlights, but local timbers were used as well,
particularly in remote locations. Joiners in the British period
also often used teak, but other wood such as Baltic deal or
Oregon pine was often used. Cape Dutch joinery was sometimes oiled but was often painted. In the British period high
quality timber was usually oiled so that the natural wood
remained visible, but softwood was always painted.

DEREK JAPHA and VIVIENNE JAPHA

11.4.e Harrs and pivots
A predecessor and alternative to the hinge, the harr
(or harre, har) has an ancient ancestry, Etruscan and Iron Age
sites frequently showing clear evidence of its use. Unlike the
hinge, which is fixed to an upright side post ofa door-frame,
the harr is customarily socketed into the lintel and sill. It consists ofa projection from the top and the bottom of the door
itself, usually extending from a stile at one side. The projections, or pins, are inserted into corresponding holes which
have to be made in at least one element in the frame, normally
the lintel, so that this can be lowered on to the door which is
therefore placed in position atan early stage in assembly.
Where this is inconvenient, the lintel may be grooved to receive

334

the harr, or pivotingpin, and closed with a metal plate or wooden

plug, though itweakens awooden frame and makes it vulnerable

to damage. Harr-hung doors are extensively used in Africa. Ona

heavy door from Kawimbe, Zambia, ‘the lower harr rotated ina

socket in a roughly trimmed wooden threshold which was

provided with a drainage hole to permit the escape of rainwater.

The upper hart passed through a hole in the lintel (Iyema) which

was slotted at each end to fit the hut posts’ (Walton, 1956). Other

examples were of lighter construction, including Bemba doors

made of poles, notched to interlock and having rods threaded to

secure them. The ends ofthe poles were trimmed ‘with the last
pole left about two inches [5 cm] higher than the rest in order to
pivotintoasocketin the door frame’, which was made ofbamboo
rods lashed together. William Fagg reported extensive use of
harr-hung doors throughoutthe savanna grasslands region, and
Dmochowski illustrates a number oflarge examples used by the
Igbo (Walton, 1954; Dmochowski).

Some Yoruba harrs are inserted into a lintel above the door,
but into the hardened earth floors below instead ofthesill. Mud
walls can take hart-hung doors if they are of sufficient thickness to give support to the harr pins as they revolve within a
hole in the compacted mud doorway. Exerting less strain on the
wallwhen theyare small, the carved shutters used by theDogon
for their granaries are notable examples.

Harrs are sometimes found in small door panels in European
integral furniture, sometimes with a metal pin projecting from
a plate which is nailed to the top edge ofthe door. The most
common surviving use of the harr principle is the gate-hinge.
Short iron bars with vertical pins are driven into a gatepost or
timber barn door-frame to receive metal collars projecting

from the side stile ofthe gate or door. They can be lifted offthe
harrs when there is a risk ofdamage from a vehicle.
PAUL OLIVER

1vAf Hinges (British Isles)
Iron hinges fall into two main groups, long strap

hinges and smaller hinges, most of which are symmetrical
about the vertical as well as the horizontal axis. Heavy-duty
strap hinges on external doors are often nearly as long as the
door is wide. Some have plain ends, but particularly in the ızth
century a variety of decorative forms was used, such as
lozenges, spearhead or leaf-shaped, and double scroll. These
heavy hinges have a loop at the inner end which is hung on a
strong iron pintle set into the door-jamb or the wooden doorframe. The loop is formed either by folding the end ofthe hinge
back on itself, or by carrying the hinge right round the door,
leaving a gap between the door and the end ofthe hinge. This
method is commonly but not exclusively used on double thickness external doors. Lighter internal doors may have strap
hinges 0.5-ı m (1.5-3 ft) longand usually about 0.8 cm (0.3 in)

attheir widest, taperingto 0.4 cm (0.16 in); these often hangon

pintles butare sometimes pivoted from a base-plate fixed to the

frame. 17th-century base-plates were often decorative, but in

the 18th century a standard type ofhinge took over in which the

base-plate was along, thin rectangle, set vertically. The accompanying hinge had a very pronounced taper, reducing to about
a centimetre in width before a small circular end.

The second type of hinge was developed for use on panelled
doors where a strap hinge would interfere with the structure
and/or the decoration. The most elaborate is the cockshead
hinge 20-30 cm (8-12 in) long, found from the late-ı6th century
in the better-quality houses; its name comes from the distinctive shape ofthe ends, said to resemble cocks’ heads. The type
degenerated through cruder ı8th-century forms to the massproduced neovernacular forms still available today. Butterfiy
hinges about 1.2 cm (0.6 in) deep by 1.4 cm (0.5 in) wide appeared in the 17th centuryand were especially common on small
cupboard doors in the later-ı7th and early-ı8th centuries. H-
hinges were used in vernacular houses from the 16708; the earlier examples had decorative ends, but most 18th-century ones
are plain. So are the very common L-hinges, which occur

almost exclusively in ı8th-century building. LINDA HALL

Openings and Fittings 1.0.4

1.V.4.f-i Hinges (Scandinavia)

The simplest form ofiron hinges were rings forged
with spikes, hammered into the doorpost and the sides ofthe
door, and interlocking with each other. However, the best
known and most interesting type ofhinges are the strap hinges
which were used throughout Europe. These helped to brace the
door-leaf and also the corners of window-frames. They were
often richly decorated. Sometimes they were forged round the
thickness of the door at both ends and fastened with rivets
going right through the door. On the inside of the door they
would normally extend only beyond the first plank. Soemetimes
the iron forming the hinge would double back again to the
front with the end forged as asnake or dragon’s head lying just
in front ofthe rivet. The rivets themselves were forged as foursided high pyramids, flower heads or other motifs, and formed
astrong part ofthe ornamentation.

Other examples of strap hinges that are still to be seen in
Scandinavia were divided into branches with leaves and formed
tree-like ornaments covering much of the door. The divisions
were cut with a chisel from the main strap and forged hot to
form the leaves; alternatively, the extensions were welded on in
the fire.

The pintles were forged with a collar that ended in a spike
hammered into the doorpost. An extension was added to the
collar and was forged into some ornament which could be
fastened to the doorpost with a nail or spike.

1. PRODUCTION
1.V.4.h-i Latches (Cyprus)

Alatch is a device for fastening a door, window or
shutter, without locking it but simply to prevent its accidental
opening. The mechanism consists ofa rockingrod, screwed on
the door, which falls down on to a stop piece fixed on the doorframe. Itcan be made ofbronze or iron, ofwood, or ofboth.

The simplest type oflatch can be used only from theside on
which it is fixed. The improved type is set up on the inside and
can be made to work from the outside by a mechanism which
traverses the door, either with soldered pieces or witha rocking
piece which raises up the bar situated to the rear of the door.
Such devices are usually made of metal and are often set in a
piece ofwrought ironwork on the outer side, becoming an element ofdecoration.

The fastening device ofa door in vernacular architecture can
also combine various fastening types. The traditional Cypriot
type, forexample, isacombination ofavertical boltwithalatch
and an outside handle. Two notched and rocking wooden
latches hang under the upper bar ofthe door-frame keepingthe
two door leaves closed, while two others are fixed to the rear of
thesame bar for retainingthe leaves open. The latches are lifted
up by two vertical sliding wooden bolts set up on the inside of
the door leaves and which are moved upwards from the outer
side by an iron handle. This hangs from a round piece ofiron

adorned with repousse work. IOANNIS IONAS

See also
2.1V.6.h Cyprus

4

Traditional latches on a Cypriot door.

338

Locks

The earliest form of lock was simply a bar or bolt
which was attached to the inside ofthe door and which could be
slid across into a hole in the doorpost when the door was
closed, preventing it from being opened. A peg fixed in the
doorpost and inserted into a lengthwise slot in the bolt, in turn
prevented the bolt from being drawn back, effectively lockingit.

This was developed so thatitcould be opened with a key from
the outside as well. Two springs sit on the doorpost behind the
lock pressing against the stopper ofthe boltand preventingthe
bolt from being pushed back except by a key which fits into the
two holes and pushes the springs back to the doorpost.

As the wish to keep belongings safe became important, locks
developed into complicated small machines. The lever-latch
lock is one ofthe early locks where a key was used from the outside. Inside, the lever holding the latch was lifted, thus pulling
the latch back. This lock is still in use today.

The heart of the lock is the key. The more complicated the
‘bit’ on the key, the more difficult it is to pick the lock. So the
key is made first and all the measurements for the wards are
taken from it, The pattern of cuts on the bit ofthe key can be
very complicated and the box of wards of course is equally so.
As a guide for the key there is usually a thin round bar fitted; it
goes down the middle of the wardcase and is riveted to the
outer casing. The shaft ofthe key is hollow from the bottom to
beyond the bit in order thatit can take the bar.

In a lock where a key is used from both sides of a door the
construction of the wardcase is somewhat different, but the
principle with the latch and lever is the same. The key bit must
be cut symmettrically, and in order to ensure thatthe key enters

the precise amount required, a collar or shoulder is provided
above the bit. The stem ofthe key is solid.

- The desire to be able to open the door with handles from
both sides and to be able to lock it from both sides developed
the lock further, and the two functions were often put into the
same casing. The wardcase in the lever-latch is exchanged for

1.VA.i

c Wardandkey
D Mortise lock

inge and lock

oorsat Augila,

SBuag ars

four or more separate levers with springs. Each lever has its
specific curve on the bottom where the cut ofthe key bit lifts it
up. In the middle of each lever a three-part pattern is cut, two
concentrically cut holes which are the same on all levers and a
slot connecting them. The position of this slot differs on each
lever according to the bottom curve, so that when the key has
lifted all the levers to the right heightaa peg on the bolt that lies
underneath the levers can pass through the slot. This pushes
the bolt into the desired position. CATHARINE LLOYD
1.V.4.-1 Locks (North Africa)

Many doors of northern Libya (Augila) are located
on the inside of the gateway. The door is made of palm tree
planks, about 8 cm (3 in) thick, stabilized by two bunches of
palm tree branches and kept together by camel tendons. The
upper hinge is anchored through the wall and the lower one is
the pole-shaped last plank, driven into the earth.

The lock ofthe door is attached to a pole inside the gateway
and can bereached byaholein the wall. The pole is anchored to
the wall bya camel tendon. When the lock is closed the pegs of
a cube fall down into the corresponding holes ofthe bolt. When
opened, the corresponding pegs of the key push the locking
cube up and the bolt is released. The lock is carved from wood,
except for the metal pegs and the metal plate with the holes.
The key is carved from wood as well, except for its metal pegs.

Openings and Fittings 1.0.4

1.Vv.4.-ii Locks (Yemen)

Locks of Yemeni houses consist of wood blocks
attached to the door panel; they have a hollow for the cogs or
pegs that act over a horizontal latch set in a notch and sliding
into a mortise in the door-jamb. In archaic examples, seen
mostly in the houses of the mountainous countryside, the
rabbet for the latch is separated from a hollow above bya thin
segment of wood with perforations regularly disposed. The
latch itself is hollow and has on the upper part the same perforations in exact coincidence. Wood pins, working in the hollow
ofthe block, go across the coinciding perforations to the exact
thickness of both segments, securing the latch in the closed
position. A key piece, reminiscent ofa toothbrush in which the
bristles are replaced by as many wood pins as the perforations,
is introduced through the hollow end ofthe latch. The key pins
push the mobile pegs up and the latch can slide. There are versions ofthis with metal pins, or with a metal key piece shaped
like a twisted fork, introduced through a slit in the door panel.
Most common are the locks in which a large metal turnkey operates a notched wood latch and a wooden cog secutes it in the
open or closed position. In interior doors, where smaller blocks
may be desirable, iron may replace some orall the moving parts.
In the mountain areas moderate ornamentation may appear on
the blocks of both interior and exterior locks; in the Tihama
these are often as densely decorated as the rest ofthe door.

Metal padlocks are frequently used for the doors of, for
instance, market stalls. They are variations of a common
principle of a cylinder containing the works needed to open
or close an attached ring, which may be circular, semicircular
or elliptical. The key may be a flat strip of metal with a fairly
complex cut-out pattern that is introduced sideways and slides
along the cylinder, or a thin, hollow cylinder, with an annular
handle, which acts by being threaded into the lock from one of
the bases. Imported industrialized turnkey padlocks became
common after the ı960s and have progressively replaced these
models. FERNANDO VARANDA
1.V.4.j Shutters and louvers
Unglazed means ofclosing wall openings or ‘winddoors’ (windows), shutters are likely to be the oldest form of
fixture used to regulate the brightness of light, the strength of
draughtand the degree of ventilation that they admit. They are
most commonly constructed of wood and act as small-scale
doors, being either harr-hung or hung on hinges. There are
other methods of closure by shutter, however, the horizontally
sliding shutter being the most effective. A version of this was
commonly used in Devonian farmbuildings, the shutter consisting of vertical battens with spaces between, which could
slide overa corresponding shutter in a parallel grove, thus either
admitting light and air or completely closing the aperture.

Most shutters are side-hung in pairs and are similar in form
to casement windows, though often they are taller and narrower. Typically, a pair of French shutters will be hung on the
outside of the window and folded back against the wall when
not in use. An iron catch, sometimes cast or shaped by the
smith with a figure or lion’s head, holds the shutter to the wall.
When there are high winds, when morning lightis too bright or

339

## S. 120

- "

1.V PRODUCTION

ernando varanda

or

when additional security is required, the shutters are folded
inwards and secured with a pivoted drop-catch. This system,
widely used in Francophone countries, requires the casement
windows to open into the room. To keep the width ofthe shutters narrow and to avoid collision with those of an adjacent
window, each shutter may be divided and hinged, to be opened
out bookwise when the entirewindow opening is to be covered.
In England, where theyare mainly ofr7th- to 18th-century date,
shutters are hung in the internal splay of the window, often
divided and hinged book-fashion, and folded back into wooden
casework when not in use. They are most often to be found with
sash windows, or more rarely, with casement windows which
open outwards.

The simplest shutters are constructed like door panels, as
can be seen in many towns and villages in the Netherlands.
Customarily braced, they provide effective security and, as in

340

Goes or Gorinchem, may be used to close the lower, more vulnerable half of the windows only. Presenting a flat external
plane on which are affixed strap hinges, the shutter panels are
frequently painted inasingle colour. Butwhen theyare opened,
a pattern of horizontal battens or Z-bracing is sometimes
exposed, and painted in contrasting colours. The surface of
heavy shutters can offer a field for an inventive wood carver.
Few can compare with the variety ofsolar, foliated, blade, comb
and other motifs set in fields defined by strapwork, diamonds,
crossed diagonals and similar shapes, carved in low relief on
continuous rows of shutters in the mountain terrace houses of
the Pashai ofeastern Afghanistan. Occasionally, window shutters may be decoratively painted as in parts ofMittelmark, eastern Germany, where floral arrangements were painted against
adark green ground. Long supptessed, the tradition is slowly
reviving. j
Shutters that provide security can also reduce ventilation; in
Germany and Switzerland some shutters are pierced near the
top with a heart or other motifwhich permits some ventilation
when the shutters are closed. Shutters that are effective in providing both ventilation and shade as well as a measure ofsecurity are achieved by the use oflouvers or transverse slats ofwood.
Setin rows down part orall ofthe shutter leaf, they are inclined
at an angle so that reflected light and air may enter, but views
into private rooms are inhibited. The origin oflouvered window
shutters is uncertain, though they are probably French, as the
term ‘louver’ (from l’ouvert?), used for both individual strips and
for the whole assembly, suggests. However, the word has been
in use in England since the mid-ı6th century, when louvered
ventilation assemblies on roofs were first used.

Louvered shutters were introduced throughout the tropical
world by western powers, especially the Dutch, French and
English engaged in trade and colonization during the ı8th and
ıgth centuries. Glazing was seldom necessary and louvered
windows were often substituted for glazed windows, particularly in Caribbean islands such as Jamaica, where solid wooden
shutters could be closed over the louvered windows. Louvers,
orjalousies, were often top-hung, with a simple iron hook and
rod atthe base ofthe frame used to hold the louver open. Where
glazing was used, sash windows were common, flanked by permanently fixed narrow strip jalousies which now performed no
shutter function but were installed for ventilation. All forms of
shutter and louver are used in the variations ofthe Malay house,
whose strong framing system permits entire walls to beopened
up save for grilles over the window openings, or closed as the
occupiers wish, with solid wooden shutters or with folding
shutters, halfor fully louvered. PAUL OLIVER
1.V.4.k Smoke-holes
Asmoke-hole, as the name implies, is a hole or vent
generally, but not always, in the roof, which serves to enable the
evacuation ofsmoke build-up within the house. In many cases it
may have been a precursor to a chimney, but this is notthe case
in at least one of the examples cited, namely Japan, where the
development of smoke outlets, in the form oftthatched gables,
frequently infilled with a lattice ofwood or in other cases dormers
akin to eyebrow windows, is asophisticated and well developed
response to the need to ventilate internal hearths.

The smoke-hole has been described as universal in the most
basic ofdwellings, with its absence amongst the Pima of North
America taken as an indication that the Pima originated from a
habitually warm climate.

Openings and Fittings 1.0.4

The distribution worldwide of smoke-holes in vernacular
buildings is clearly climatically related, as is borne out by the
apparent absence of the feature in Africa as a whole, where
indeed, as elsewhere in the world, smoke functions as an insect
repellent. Dmochowski makes reference to smoke vents occurring not in the roof, but low down in the walls, to ventilate the
furnaces ofthe Nupe blacksmiths, brass and silversmiths, and
glassmakers, which have made a notable contribution to this
central Nigerian culture. These vents, whose location suggests
that their purpose is the provision of oxygen to the furnaces
and notthe extraction ofsmoke, are not, therefore, true smokeholes.

The double-skinned roofofthe Nok, the inside layer ofwhich
is built up in mud almost in the manner ofa coil pot, with the
outside layer of thatch fastened to a structural frame (rintang)
made up of.a cone of bamboo held in place with horizontal
rings (fab) of cornstalks, sometimes provides a ventto the roof
space. It has been suggested, however, that this vent serves
purely as an access to the roof space. A feature of these houses
is their smoke-blackened interior surfaces.

Nor were smoke-holes the inevitable result ofa cold climate,
where the internal fire was required for heating purposes. The
Hebridean ‘black houses’ were purposely built without smokeholes to encourage the deposit ofsoot, an excellent fertilizer. It
is also noted that in such houses furniture for the occupants was
low slung, enabling the family to sit with their heads below the
level ofthe smoke which accumulated in the roof space, whilst
visitors suffered the discomfort ofthe more formal furniture.

The Nuristani, Japanese, Nepalese and the North American
Indians all provide examples of smoke-holes. In none ofthese
cultures was the collection ofsoot an agricultural requirement
as fertilizer.

Amongst the Nuristani houses were built of stone and timber, with flatroofs constructed from poplar rafters, supporting
reeds or planks covered with layers of woodshavings, crushed
stone and processed clay; smoke-holes were formed by trimmers ofrough laths or planks spanning between rafters to form
a frame. Successive frames made of planks and diminishing in
size were then added, each layer being rotated through 45° into
a coffer-like form.

Smoke-holes were a feature of both types of construction
used amongst the Omaha. They built earth lodges in exposed
situations for comfort, for summer occupation when they were
not migrating, and favoured skin lodges for the winter season,
because they could be pitched in timber or brush or in secluded
ravines.

In the earth lodges theroofwas supported by two series ofvertical posts forked to receive transverse poles. The walls described
a circle of planks 1.8 m (6 ft) in length, with their tops meeting
the crosstimbers. Hard willow stocks were then set, with their
butts resting on the tops ofthe uprightslabs, radiatingto form a
conical roof. These willow rafters were covered with about 30 cm
G fü) ofgrass and the whole was covered with up to 60 cm (2 ft)
ofearth. There was a smoke-hole in the middle.

Their tent was made from 10-12 dressed or tanned buffalo
skins and was about 3-3.7 m (10-12 ft) high, and 3-4.5 m
(10-15 ft) in diameter at the base and 45 cm (18 in) at the top,

341

## S. 121

|

1.V PRODUCTION

which served as asmoke-hole. This was formed by two triangular skin ends immediately above the entrance. When the wind
blew from a certain direction, one ofthe skin ends was raised to
the windward and the other lowered, leaving a hole for the
escape ofsmoke. Ifthe wind blew directly against the entrance,
both flaps were raised.

Amongst the Menomini, although the majority bytheend of
the ıgth century lived in log cabins (Hoffman), wigwams were
still used, formed by planting 5 cm (2 in) oak or other sturdy
poles in an elliptical outline. These were then bent inward and
bound in position with strips of basswood bark. Horizontal
poles were then lashed to the hoops along the axis ofthe frame
and overlapping mats fastened to the frame from the bottom
up. The whole was topped by strips ofbark, exceptatthe centre,
where a smoke-hole was left.

The Algonquin, who also lived in bark wigwams, used an
adjustable smoke guard to draw out the smoke and to exclude
the weather.

Amongst the Sherpas houses are basically two-storey rectangles with the lower floor built into the slope ofthehillatthe
rear; the animals are kept on the ground floor and the livingroom is upstairs. The orientation of the houses is generally
southeast to maximize the ingress of sunlight. They are builtof
a solid, outer protective wall, around an inner and separate
wooden frame. The focal point ofthe Sherpa living-room is the
open hearth for cooking and heating, around which the family
gathers. This is made of high slabs of stone, above which are
suspended slats for smoking yak meat. Smoke from the fire
escapes through an opening in the roof.

DEIRDRE MCDERMOTT

1..4.k-i Smoke-holes: vent (West Africa)

Of importance in many West African earth dwellings is the location of light sources other than the doorway.
Apertures in the roof formed at the time of construction from
pots with broken bases, or moulded at the very top, as in the
domical Mosgoum houses in Cameroon, function both as skylights and as smoke-holes. The houses of the Batammaliba]
Tamberma in Togo are built as sanctuaries dedicated to thesun
(kouiye). Each house is oriented towards the setting sun; at
noon the sun strikes through the tabote, or ‘house hole’, located
ontheterraceroofofthe entrance or cattleroom, andat dusk, the
sun enters the house portal to communicate with the family
ancestors. JEAN-PAUL BOURDIER
1.1.4. window framing: casement and sash

There is a great diversity of window types throughoutthe world, ranging from a short section being omitted from
a single log in a Nordic log house to the triple-layered sliding
windows that virtually constitute the entire walls of houses in
parts of the Philippines. In terms of their operating systems
rather than their dimensions, casement windows are probably
the most widely distributed. The term ‘casement’ window has
been applied at least since the mid-ı6th century to windowframes that are suspended on hinges along one vertical side,
and which may be opened through go° or more. Glazed windows
are known to have been used by the Romans, and there is evi-

342

dence of Saxon glass having been used at a palace in Windsor.
Glass was used within lead and iron framing in churches in
many parts ofmedieyal Europe, butits secular use was confined
to the aristocracy.

The weight of the thick glass in iron frames was such
that leaded and glazed windows were fixed, though by the ıs5th
century, small single ‘leafs’ were made to open in larger glazed
windows. The lower half of many Dutch windows were solid
timber shutters, while the upper part was fixed and glazed, a
system which survives to the present in some parts of The
Netherlands. Peasant houses often had window openings which
were covered with bowed wicker screens sprung into place
when winds had to be kept out. Others had a small horizontal
opening divided into ‘lights’ by a series of vertical mullions or
diamond-sectioned posts. The use of thin parchment made
ftom the skin of sheep or goats, thin shavings of horn, paper
oiled with linseed and other translucent membranes were
employed in windows to admit light, ifnotto see through. The
growth of the glassmaking industry by the mid-16th century
enabled a large proportion ofthe peasant houses of England,
France and northern Germany to have glazed casement
windows, which were made to open on side hinges.

South ofthe Loire glass was still not in use in domestic houses
nearlya century later, and the use ofglass in other parts ofEurope
was delayed or unevenly distributed. European trade and expansionism introduced it to many parts of the world where there
were no local industries. In China glass had long been made for
many purposes, butits introduction for windows may have been
as late as the 17th century; the use ofoiled paper for the windows
of peasant houses, adhered to the intricate patterns of mortise
and tenoned wood lattices, persists to the present. In the coastal
provinces of China the translucent shell of a bivalve, Placuna
placentae, is used in overlapping squares, held in place by bamboo

strips wired to the lattice. Other materials of known early use,
such as mica-thin layers oftalc, selenite or other silicates— were
still employed in the 2oth century: mica was used for windows

1

see also for 1.VdK
2.1.9.1 Nuristanl

Jin, and Cengiz,

on domestic stoves before the 1940s, and one or two fixed mica
windows survived at Acoma pueblo in the North American
South-West (see 3.v1.7-A: Keres) until the 19708.

Nevertheless, it was the manufacture of thin glass sheets
which facilitated the dissemination of the casement window,
and subsequently, the window sash. ‘Sash’ (from the French
chassis) windows may have been invented in Holland, though
it is confirmed that they were installed at Chatsworth House
in England in the 16708. The sash window opened by sliding
vertically, thus causing less spatial interruption and providing
a larger yet more secure glazed area. At first the frames and
glazing bars were carved from solid wood, but improved
techniques of planing led to lighter window construction. Sash
cords over pulleys- introduced perhaps, from France- enabled
the movement of the window, while weights hung in grooves
within the casing facilitated its positioning. In less complicated
windows wedges or hooks and catches were used instead.
A horizontal variant, reputedly invented in Yorkshire, though
distributed as far south as Cornwall, slid laterally, thus consuming more wall space, but being appropriate for low farmhouse rooms, and obviating the need for cords and weights.

Originally placed flush with the outside wall, sash windows
were required by law in the early-ı8th century to be set back
10-15 cm (4-6 in) from the external plane ofthe wall to reduce
the risk of chain ignition in the event offire. Early sash windows
often had a moving lower half and a fixed upper half, a type
which was widely used in Holland. The proportions of British

windows as Georgian neoclassicism evolved were in 2:1,
Golden Mean, and other rectangles, expressed in combinations 0f3 X 3 Over 3X3,4X2 0ver4X2,3X20ver3X3,3%x3 over

Openings and Fittings 1v.4

3x2 and other arrangements of lights or panes. As Georgian
style ‘trickled down’, its influence was extensive in vernacular
building ofthe 18th and ıgth centuries. Sash windows became
popular in many parts of Europe during the course ofthese two
centuries, to be adopted in Greece, where 2 x 3 over 2x 3 and
2X 2 over 2x 2 arrangements were often found, and enthusiastically installed as far east as Istanbul and northern Anatolia.
Sash wındows were widely used in North America, the arrangement of3 x 2 over 3 x 2 becoming standard. Extensive though
the dispersal ofthe sash window was, elsewhere it did not displace the popularity ofthe casementin vernacular architecture.

PAUL OLIVER

1.v.4.-1 Window framing: dormer

Usually constructed to bring light to an unlit space,
dormers take their name from the French dormir, to sleep.
Dormer windows are inserted into the slope ofa roof when the
loft is being made into a habitable room or rooms. They take
many forms, but these are all conditioned by the desire to have
thewindows in a vertical plane, when the roof slope is inclined.
Among the simplest of dormers are the popularly termed ‘eyebrow’ windows, over which the roof cladding of thatch, slate,
shingle or tile is raised in a curve over a small window frame,
perhaps 50 cm (20 in) in height. This obviates the necessity of
building triangular side walls to the window projection and
permits the thatch or slab covering to continue withouta break
in its surface, reminiscentofan eyebrow, or more accurately, an
eyelid. The term ‘eyebrow window’ is also used in North America to identify a window directly below the eaves and shaded by
them; hence itis nota dormer.

Aeno neda

## S. 122

———

1.V PRODUCTION

Eyebrow windows ofthe dormer kind are to be found in many
parts of Europe, from the Balkans to the British Isles. Those in
Slovakia, for example, are sometimes very small, only permittinga limited amount oflight but causing theleast interruption
to the slope ofthe roof. In some instances the window is curved
as a lunette or fanlight, further facilitating the flow ofthe roof
cladding and redueing the risk ofrain penetration. An alternative to the eyebrow but offering some ofthe same advantages is
the catslide’or ramp type, in which astrip ofeladdingabove the
window is apparently lifted so as to producea slightly flattened
angle but still reflecting the plane ofthe roof. These appear to
be particularly effective on buildings with high, steeply pitched
toofs. Exceptional examples are to be found in Baden-Württemberg, southwest Germany, and in neighbouring Strasbourg.
Immense timber-framed town buildings rising to eight storeys
in height were built in the ısth and 16th centuries, many of
which had four or more storeys in the steep roofs, illuminated
byramp-type dormers. Itappears thatthe eyebrow dormers are
mostsuited to relatively shallow (30°) roofpitches, whereas the
ramp-roofed dormers are effective on 50° and 60° roofs.

Some dormers are constructed by carving out a section of

the roof, and permitting the fall of the slope to accommodate

the desired height ofthe window; this method requires a shal-

low-pitched apron before the window and to the eaves, which

can be subject to rain and snow damage. More often, dormer

windows are constructed as projections from the roof, gener-

ally to the width ofthe framing or with a narrow surround. The

structure is itself covered by a small roof, which may be flat but
which is frequently gabled, and sometimes hipped or ‘bon-

neted’. In certain regions, for example southern Romania, the

shallow-pitched roofs of the dormers may be permitted to

extend well in front ofthe window, protecting it from weather
though reducing the light admitted by it. Usually the width of
the gable is determined by the interval between the principal roof
rafters. Triangular side walls formed by the window-frame, the
eaves ofthe roofto the dormer and the slope ofthe roof structure may be clad with shingles or simply plastered. This form of
dormer is common on 45° roofs but, as the supporting structure can be weakened by the insertion ofthe dormer, especially
ifthe common rafters are cut away, the window-frame may be
built in the plane ofthe main wall or facade. The wall may simply be raised in front ofthe wall plate to provide support forthe
framing ofthe dormer, or flanking piers may be supported on
corbels. A further solution is the insertion of a third of the
dormer height into the wall plane, breakingtheline ofthe eaves
but gaining from the structural stability ofthe wall.

Paintings by ısth-century Flemish and French artists (for
example, Robert Campin) confirm that by this time dormer
windows were used on many domestic buildings; even where
the vernacular tradition ofbuilding houses declined in the 19th
and 2oth centuries the custom of gaining living space in the
roof illuminated and ventilated by dormers has continued to
the late-2oth century. PAUL OLIVER

See also
2.111.6-A Germanic

References

Cuisenier, Jean, Raulin, Henry, and Calame, Frangois,1988
Hansen, Hans Jurgen (ed.),1971

Stanculescu, Florian, et al.,1958

344

Window framing: shell (Philippines)

Windows in traditional urban houses in the Philippines encourage maximum circulation of wind and light.
Moreover, they have safeguards for children curious about the
outdoors. These windows have two sections: the main window
(ventana, bintana) and the small window (ventanilla). The main
window is a wooden frame that extends from one wooden
house-post to the other and reaches up to the transom. On the
wide window-sill are grooves where two sets of panels slide:
jalousies (persianas) and shell panels (conchas). The latter are lattice-work panels which hold up flat, translucent capiz shells
(Placuna placentae) harvested from shallow waters. Bach shell
frame in the latticework measures 6 cm (2.3 in) square. This
type ofwindow dates back to the 17th century.

Startingin the18gos, glass became more common; frames in
the sliding panels became larger. Butshellpanes have remained
popular in many areas because they do not easily shatter when
buffeted by strong typhoon winds. While shell panels face the
street, jalousies face the interior. These can be opened or closed
with herringbone-shaped wooden spines. At noon, the shell
panels are pushed aside while the jalousies are drawn together
to lessen the radiation. At other times, while thejalousies are at
the window sides, the shell panels are partially closed to provide both protection and a soft light. Below the window sill is a
small window that extends to the floor. It is protected either
with turned wooden balusters or with iron grilles in a variety of
floral patterns. Plain wooden panels, sliding in floor sills, shut
this window. When it is open, children playing on the floor can
peer into the street without falling out.

For more light, an immovable panel (espejo), with sheets ofglass
or shells in latticework, opens up the space between the window
proper and the ceiling. The upper storey of Philippine urban
houses ofthe 1890s-1930s is thus almostaall windows. However,
openings on the stone ground storey are narrower. Their main
windows are enclosed with protective grilles, sometimes with
floral designs. The shell panels in both storeys give the house

FERNANDO N.ZIALCITA

1.V.4.1-ii

exterior a unique shimmer.
