1.v.4.m Window types (Nepal)

Elaborately carved wooden window and door elements placed within brick facades are the most prominent
ordering element of Newar architecture in the Kathmandu
valley, Nepal. Newar windows are typified by a complex structural and symbolic framework. According to Gutschow,
although Newar carving dates from as early as the ı3th to ı6th
centuries, it was during the ı8th century that woodwork
reached the levels of sophisticated design for which Newar
architecture has become famous.

Newar windows are arranged hierarchically in the building
facade such that the higher the window, the more open it is.
Windows at the ground floor are set into brick and are often
‘blind’ or false. Small latticed holes, or ghost holes (nasapvah),
provide unobstructed exit for evil spirits. In cases where groundfloor windows do admitlight, they are similar to the lattice window (tikijhya) of upper floors, or middle layer mätä. On the
ground floor, the latticed tikijhya frame is placed flush with the
exterior while on the interior it allows maximum light infiltration to storerooms beyond. Top-floor windows are largest in
size, called ‘window-to-be-opened’ (säjhya). Säjhya frames are
angled and contain movable lattices which swing up to form a
canopy over an interior window seat, usually intricately carved
with decorations. Top windows contain three to five or more
sections and are usually centred on the facade ofa house as its
primary design element, although they may be flanked by
smaller windows on longer houses. Top window placement in
the facade determines its type as deep (gä-), roofed (pakhä) or
bent (kochujhya). Special corner windows are known as küjhya.

The structure of Newar windows is complex, consisting ofa
primary and a secondary frame which interdependently connect. Constructed from multiple prefabricated units of varied
shapes and sizes, frames are assembled without metal connectors or glue. The main components of the primary bearing
frame ofthe window are the sill (kvakhalu), the lintel (mutanga)
anda pair ofjambs (mubaha). Ofthem only the projecting ends
(lapu) are visible: a secondary frame hides the rest. The sec-

Openings and Fittings 1.v.4

ondary frame lintel (chvakula) and sill (asan) are fastened by
tenons to the primary frame. Secondary jambs (hache) stretch
and project into the primary frame. Additional jambs (torana
tha), shaped as freestanding pillars, stretch between the secondary sill and lintel. Lattice is placed behind the surface ofthe
bearing frame. Lattice is constructed by combining three
different battens: perforated, serrated and key. Latticework is
pressed into the prepared frame and cannot be disassembled
without dismantling the entire frame. A tertiary frame surrounds the lattice opening. Its horizontal sill and lintel (ma),
are considered female, while its pair ofjambs (ba) are considered male.

Gutschow refers to an ancient Newari manuscript, the vastu
vidhi-vastu lakshana vishuddhi, which ‘describes the complex
associations ofstructural parts with various gods’. The boundary
between enclosed ordered space and the outer world is experienced as highly vulnerable: iconographic carvings representing gods or animal spirits serve to guard against evil spirits.

Gutschow further describes that Newar wood carvers liken

windows to a face, similar to universal anthropomorphic
connotations of the primordial house. In English, ‘window’
derives from the old Icelandic term vindauga (‘wind eye’),
whereas in ancient India, gavaksha (‘cow eye’) for lattice window references similar origins. Local craftsmen interpret
design details of primary jambs as hair curls (saethu). The frieze
over the opening as teeth (va) is often painted beside openings
or on doors. Initiated Newars, such as a bajracharya (Buddhist
priest), who interpret the world through the dharma, or religion, may read the window as a division ofthe world into three
realms, the heaven, the middle and the underworld. The sill is
the underworld (patal), the lintel the upperworld (svarga) and
the opening the middle world (madhya). Further divisions
define the remaining structural components of the window
including design motifs about the opening. These divisions are
perceived to relate to the essence of life, the cycle of life and
rebirth, and the existence of suffering. The five opening pasukajhyah is defined as the eyes of knowledge (jnana chakshu)
(Gutschow). Even the Newari word for window, jhyä, or the
Nepali jyaal, often translate as ‘eye’, further imbuing symbolic
significance to building fenestration within the complexly
developed Newar window. TARA MICHELE CAHN
1.V.4.m-i Window types (Yemen)

The most elementary houses of the Yemen mountains have no windows. Simple, small, rectangular windows
appear in more developed types such as the tower houses of
the hinterland. The most common form of window, however,
consists of two sections usually enclosed within a stilted arch
and separated by a lintel: a lower rectangular window (taga),
provided with wood shutters or fixed wooden bars for letting in
airand light, and above itan opening fitted with a fixed translucent element for additional light, variously designated as ‘agd
(arch), gamaria (from gamar, ‘the moon’) and takhrim (tracery).
This element may be no more than a small (ro-ı5 cm [4-6 in]
across) plate ofalabaster, round, rectangular or arched, setina
thinner, recessed spandrel with the same width as the lower
part, allowing enlargement without affecting the structure of

345

## S. 123

1.V PRODUCTION

the window-frame. Complex forms have multiple perforations
in compositions of rounds, arches and rectangles in stone or
brick tracery and are a major element ofthe decorative characterization ofthe buildings. .

Single fanlights made of alabaster plates ofa standard size
(up to about 60 x go cm [2 x 3 ft]) are common. In the area of
Radä’ the fanlight area is increased with twin or triple arches
over the shuttered part, each arch dimensioned according to
the size of the alabaster plates. Elsewhere fanlights may be
made wider by joining two plates side by side but in general
large single fanlights use coloured glass and gypsum wacety.
Ventilation slits (shaqus) or additional fanlights on either side of
the window are frequent additions.

Some compositions have a wooden canopy between the
shutter and the fanlight (kunna), sometimes rather elaborated
(e.g. in San’ä). In the southern uplands this canopy is made of
plaster. With the widespread use of reinforced conerete, ehe
kunna is being replaced by a projecting lintel at the springing
line ofthe arches.

The Tihama fanlights, in the form ofa coloured glass circle,
appear only in the larger urban buildings, particularly of the
‘Red Sea’ type. Multiple colours and tracery of the mountain
type were introduced only in the 19708.

The traditional carpentry of windows and doors favoured
hardwood of a local variety of acacia (tunub) finished with
mustard seed oil. A most common size of window - in the
75X100cm (30X4oin)range-hasa two-leafpanelled shürter,
seton the outer face ofthe wall and opening outwards. Awicket
with the size and shape oftthe shutters’ lower panel (usually a

cusped arch) may be fitted in either or both leaves. Four to SR
leaves are common for wide windows. Glass windows opening
inwards came later. In some cases they coexistwith shutters but
they may rely only on gratings or metal bars for security and
curtains for privacy and light control. Their frames and sashes
are very slender - 2 cm (0.8 in) across on an average size leafdiscreetly adorned with straight grooves.

Shutters may be decorated, although seldom as elaborately
as doors, with carvings, inlays in other materials and, more

346

EpURJen OPLEULAIO

recently, coloured paint. Except for a few provincial examples,
carpentry was a craft controlled or influenced by Jewish artisans, predominantly in the mountains, although in the Tihama
a comparable Indian influence is also evident. The Jewish exodus 0f1948, together with increasing scareity ofwood and the
troubles ofthe 1960, made carpentry a moribund craft in the
mid-1970s. Mechanized production which followed concentrated on glazed window-frames, with thicker sections and

heavier ornaments. FERNANDO VARANDA

Rada, Yemen,

See also
2.V.1-B Yem

References

Bonnenfant, GU
and Paul!‘

Varanda, Fe!

ıvs-A Roofs and Spans

It is a truism of vernacular architecture that its primary function is to provide shelter, irrespective ofthe many factors that may influence the forms that it may assume, or the
social and symbolic values that it may express. Places may be
cleared in the forest, raised as earthworks, defined by walls,
marked by monoliths or in many other ways given identity and
distinction by human interventions. But the essence of shelter
is that it encloses space by covering. The qualities identified
with the structures that function as shelter are multitudinous,
but they share in common protection from the elements and
the creation of a microenvironment within the building envelope. Such provision may range from the simplest ramada or
shade shelter to the immense and multifunctional hall-houses
of northern Europe, or the tower houses of the High Atlas,
Morocco. Small or large, structurally simple or complex, such
shelter both occupies volume in space and creates a defined
space within.

These fundamental characteristics ofvernacular architecture
distinguish it from mankind’s other place-making pro-pensities and also provide its builders with some oftheir most challenging problems: for volume demands the means to span
space. In categories in Section 1.1V the diverse materials for the
making of structural elements for spans are summarized and
the means of preparing or transforming them for these building functions are described. Other categories in the present
section, notably 1.v.ı Assembly, explain how components are
raised, combined and joined to achieve built structures.

Most spanning systems require supporting structures and

are rarely self-supporting. Builders within societies thatusethe
entire internally defined space oftheir buildings, orwhich have
a social system necessitating that a number of related nuclear
family units share the same volume, face considerable structural difficulties in supporting the roofs that will achieve the
required spans. Many magnificent vernacular buildings, such
as Amazonian malocas, demonstrate great structural ingenuity,
understanding of the forces exerted on the spanning elements
and skills in construction within restricted means. When the
additional problems of aging of materials, of animal or insect
damage or ofrotand decay are taken into consideration, along
with the limitations of available building resources and the
tools with which to work them, the achievements ofvernacular
builders are frequently remarkable. Others are modest, but
adequate for the demands on them.

Flat roofs are the simplest roofs to construct, with beams
of timber or palm laid with their ends on supporting walls.
Frequently a wall plate, or timber beam, runs the length ofthe
wall to distribute the loads exerted by the crossbeams. With the
beam ends inserted into the wall and with some measure of
wall above them, the transverse beams are keptin tension, and
some gain a measure of structural advantage by extending
through the wall, as is the case among the Pueblos. Atthe point
of insertion the beam is subject to shear forces, though the
vertical gravitational pull is exerted to the greatest extenton the
middle ofthe beam. This creates unequal stresses, the top of
the beam being in compression, the lower side, in effect
stretched, being in tension. A slight arching of the beam can
counteract this to a degree.

In some cases where the span is great, some form of brace
may be used to help transfer the load to the wall. A number of
beams, overlaid with flooring joists and planking, or a layer of
earth on the roof top may considerably increase the weight
borne by the beams. This is partially compensated for by the
comparative elasticity of the wood, but eventually permanent
deformation or bending in the form of hinging action and
fracture may occur. Should the length ofthe beams be doubled
the bending forces are likewise doubled so that the beam can
only bear halfthe load. If the vertical thickness of the beam is
doubled, however, its strength is quadrupled, which accounts
for the massive beams found in some European buildings;
nevertheless, the weight increases Proportionately, exerting
extra loads upon the supporting walls.

Flat roofs are suitable in regions where precipitation ofrain
or snow is low and where as few surfaces as possible face
the sun, to reduce the temperature increase from solar gain.
Flat roofs are therefore common in Middle Eastern, sayanna

347

## S. 124

1.V PRODUCTION

and semi-desert regions. In areas exposed to rains, and where
independent buildings may be dispersed, conical roofs may be
constructed of radial poles raised on a cylindrical wall structure. Such roofs are limited partly by the weight and length of
the beams or poles used: bamboo, for instance, is light and
obtainable in considerable lengths. Its elasticity permits structural members to be kept in tension but it is less successful
under compression. An external sheath and a hollow core hinder the use of carpentered jointing systems, but it is ideal for
lashing. Rings of twisted vines may be used to restrain undue
buckling or deformation ofpoles. Such conical roofs were once
common in northern Europe; in the 2oth century they are principally found in Africa.

Throughout Europe and Europeanized territories, allofAsia
and much ofLatin America the use of ‘pitched’ roofs, sloping
on twosides and with gables ateach end, is common. Theinclination oftherrafters to the centre, which may have a ridge-pole,
may be from 35° to 45°. Much steeper roofs have been recorded
- as among the Sre of Vietnam - but with a consequent loss of
internal width in relationship to the height of the roof. It is
often assumed that the ‘roof-tree’ ortheridge ofthe roofsystem
takes the weight ofthe roof but this is fallacious. The ridge will
take some ofthe burden ofthe rafters which, however, will also
be shared by the opposing rafters. All rafters will transmit the
weight ofthe roofto the walls. Such a roof may carıy a burden
of cladding such as thatch, tile or stone, necessitating the use
of purlins or intermediary horizontal members to support the
rafters. In more developed forms ofvernacular timber technology, the principle of rigid frames by the use of triangulation
with tie and collar beams was employed. At the junctions of
beams and rafters carpenters devised many ingenious timber
joints which ensured that the frames were securely interlocked
and pegged. Resistance to high winds and other loads was
sometimes introduced with ‘wind-braces’ or angle members
which gave lateral triangulation to the principal rafters.

An alternative to the pitched roofwas the hipped roof, which
had a shorter ridge and, instead ofa gable at each end, sloped
back from each wall. Pyramidal on the relatively rare square
plan, this roof system was generally found with a rectangular
plan. It had the advantage of a shorter ridge and could be
thatched ortiled over the whole roof surface, though it necessitated more complicated joints. Amonopitch roof, orone where
a single inclination was achieved by having one side of the
building higher than the opposing side it was simply made and
had the advantage ofaslope to throw offrainwater. It avoided the
necessity ofbuilding a framed roof structure, though it exerted
athrust on the lower wall. The system was also used in association with a larger building, as a ‘lean-to’ with a “pentice’ roof.

While these trussed roof systems are characteristic of many
parts ofthe world they were not developed in East Asia—-China
and Japan evolved totally different systems ofroof construction
which permitted the curving profiles and rising eaves favoured
in their cultures. These systems relied on rows of posts and
brackets which are discussed under 1.V.6 Supports and Floors.

In regions where there was a shortage ofwood or where such
wood and palms as were present were valued for their shade
and fruit, arches, domes and vaults were common. The arch

348

consists of wedges, or ‘voussoirs’ of masonty which are
bonded in compression by gravitational forces. Theoretically
there are no bending stresses in a correctly constructed
masonry arch, whose angular forces meet the perpendicular
line offorce ofthe adjacent stones and are transmitted successively to the supportingabutments. An arch can take considerable loads of masonry above, gravitation forces compressing
the masonıy bond of the arch yet tighter. But if stability is
threatened a large ‘relieving arch’ may be constructed above the
span. Simple barrel vaults are, in effect, a succession ofarches,
but these become complex when such vaults meet or cross each
other. Highly original and sophisticated forms of masonry in
brick or stone were invented by builders in the Iranian plateau
and the Arab world.

Less frequently used in the domestic vernacular, but to be
seen in rural mosques and churches in the Mediterranean and
adjacent regions, the dome might be considered as a rotated
arch. The resultant form, however, is arched both in the vertical
änd the horizontal directions, the horizontal compression
forces permitting the opening ofthe top ofthe dome to admit
light, as is the case in the domed bazaar routes of many Arab
cities. To cover a cubic space with a dome requires a difficult
construction, which is often achieved with the use of the
squinch, amasonryarch across each ofthe comers ofthe cube.
Sometimes two or three arches may be placed above each other,
increasing in span as the top of the cube and the rim of the
dome are reached. An alternative system, the pendentive, may
be conceptualized as a concave masonty triangle rising from
each corner to achieve the same result.

Such abriefaccount can do no more than summarize the principal forms of roofs and spans which have been widely used
throughout the world; the many variants and singular systems
cannot be included here, but where possible they are described
in Part Two. Further discussion will be found under Assembly
in this section, and in categories in Section ı.vII Forms and
Structural Types. The spanning systems summarized above,
while being subject to compression and tension, are essentially
constructed ofrelatively rigid members. In thevernacular traditions among the oldest spanning systems are tents, which are
subject to similar forces but which meet and exploit them by
different means, as outlined below. In this category examples
of different spanning systems, both widespread and localized,
are described. PAUL OLIVER

1v5-B Membrane Tents

The tent is infinitely variable. Its very great flexibility
and the ease with which it can be changed result in a proliferation ofvariations within each ofthe main types and subtypes that
is confusing to say the least, and resists any neat division into a
consistent tent taxonoıiny. Despite so much variation, however,
there is often a basic shape or some fundamental feature (such
as the foundation frame of.a reed tipi) common to the conical
skin-covered tent ofthe boreal forestregions ofnorthern Eurasia and North America, and which reveals its membership ofa
type. This fundamental feature has been preserved through the
tent type’s development, giving it its special identity, however
much it has been modified from locality to locality and between
tribal groups. The essence of the tent is its repeatability and
standardization, but it is a standardization that allows great
flexibility to adjust itto each new change of circumstance.
Nomadism is an extreme case ofhuman society’s adaptation
to an unfriendly environment in lands where the rainfall is
scanty and irregular and far below the minimum required for
growing cereals by dry farming. The inhabitants, if they are
pastoralists, must be on the move in search ofnew pastures for
their herds and flocks. Because the nomad occupies land which
has a low precipitation (usually less than 300 mm [12 in] annually), there is usually no wood for building, hence the nomad
must bring materials for building his house with him.
Tents did notspring into existence fully developed; they arose

Roofs and Spans 1.5

gradually from existing fixed dwellings and huts, and over time
their framework was simplified and lightened by the tent
awning or membrane taking on a more significant structural
role in resisting wind, snow and rain loads. Sometimes an echo
of the original hut form is preserved in the shape of the tent.
Many nomads were previously settled farmers before they were
driven to nomadism by the increasing desiccation ofthe environment which forced them to adapt their fixed dwelling so it
could be transported.
There are two basic plan shapes: circular, and square or rectangular. Structurally, the division is also quite simple: there is
the frame tenthavingalimp, unstressed membrane that is wrapped around or over a structural frame which carries the loads
and is secured with thongs or straps to prevent it flying off; and
there are tents which have no frame and whose covering membrane is the primary load-carrying structure carried on compression elements disposed as linear or point systems of support.
Few traditional nomad tents are true pre-stressed membranes.
The membrane of the black goat hair tent of the Middle East
and North Africa is very heavy; its weight, especially when wet,
helps to stabilize it, butitis also stretched and deformed by ropes
around its edges to increase its stability under lateral wind
loads. Hence, itis a partially pre-stressed and weight-stabilized
membrane. In structural terms, traditional tents can be divided
into unstressed frame tents and stressed frameless tents supported on poles, arches and horizontal bars or tensile filaments,
ora combination ofthese.

Frame tents

The use of the conical tent is a circumpolar trait found in the
boreal areas of both North America and Eurasia. It is retained
as a summer dwelling by many nomadic and semi-nomadic
herdsmen and even by some sedentary agricultural tribes. The
tent consists of a conical framework of radial inclined poles,
arranged in a circle with their slim upper ends secured at the
peak. The pattern of the pole frame and the material used to
cover it, but not the basic form, are remarkably uniform across
Siberia. Two, three or four poles usually make up the foundation or main support for the secondary poles which are laid in
the V-fork of the crossing. In the north of Siberia the frame is
covered with separate strips of tanned reindeer skins fastened
to the poles with leather thongs, butin the south itis covered by
birchbark softened by boiling before it is sewn together. The
conical frame tent belongs to the region of northern ‘taiga’ forestand is found among the Samoyed, the Northern and Evenki
Tungus, Nganasani, and the Lapps. Even the Kalmuk of
Astrakhan and the Kirghiz use felt-covered conical tents.

The tipi ofthe Plains Indians is a late modification ofthis circumpolar type which dispersed over the Great Plains. The tipi
displays all the essential features ofthe Eurasian conical frame
tent; it has the central fire, asmoke-hole centring on the crossing ofthe poles atthe top, an eastern entrance and the place of
honour opposite the doorway. In many respects it is more
advanced, for example, the vertical axis ofthe tipi is tilted backwards to leave the smoke-hole unobstructed and there are
‘ears’ to keep outrain. Itsimilarly has a two- three- or four-pole
foundation like the Eurasian conical frame tent, but, unlike the

349

## S. 125

u

1.V PRODUCTION

Eurasian example, the two-pole foundation is rare. A line seems
to run directly from the American to the Asiatic forms. In the
19th century it was distributed widely throughout North America except southeast ofthe Great Lakes and Mississippi, and it
was largely absentalong the Pacific coast. The developed American tipi was directly connected with the vast expanse of prairie
plains grasslands west of the Mississippi which stretches
unbroken from northern Alberta and Saskatchewan in Canada
to the Rio Grande of Texas. South and east of Hudson Bay the
conical frame tent extended right up to the Atlantic Ocean and
Newfoundland.

The cylindrical-shaped trellis tent covered with felt is another
highly developed frame tent that is found in the vast area
extending from the Volga river and the Anatolian plateau in the
westto the Khingan mountains in the east. The shape and details
of the trellis tent are surprisingly uniform and have remained
much the same over the past four centuries. There are two main
types: afelt-covered cylindrical-walled trellis tentwith.a conical
ribbed rooffound amongst Mongol peoples and some Turkishspeaking tribes of northern Central Asia and the cylindricalwalled trellis tent with a convex, domical-shaped, ribbed roof
which is found amongst the Turkish-speaking tribes ofwestern
and southern Siberia such as the Kirghiz, Uzbek and Turkomen.
The conical rooffelt trellis tent is customarily referred to as the
Kalmuk or Mongolian type, while the domical roof felt trellis
tentis known as the Kirghiz or Turkic type. Besides the Kalmuk,
the Mongolian type, is used by the Northern and Transbaikalia
Buryat and the Mongols proper. Some of the Turkic tribes of
western Mongolia which have been exposed to Mongolian influences, such as the Altai-Tartar and the Sarts ofnorthwestern
Mongolia, have also adopted the conicalrooftype. The domical
roofversion is employed by both branches ofthe Kirghiz, Kara-
Kalpak Uzbek, Turkomen, Nogais and the Bashkir.

Amuch less developed type oftent that is either polygonal or
cylindrical in shape is found in southwestern and northwestern
Siberia. The polygonal version is scattered along the northern
border with Mongolia, stretching from the Sayan mountains to
the Argun river. These tents appear to be related to the Mongolian trellis tent. The second cylindrical type is restricted to the
extreme northeastern portion of Siberia and extends across
into the Chukchi peninsula and into northern Kamchatka.

Frameless tents with stressed membranes

The black tent has a stressed membrane with an anticlastic, or
flat saddle-like, surface supported by ridges, arches or high
points and anchored to the ground by long stays. Itcomes closest ofall to the modern concept of pre-stressed tensile surface
structures in using the least amount of wood for its supports.
Reliefs from the 7th century Bc depict Assyrian cavalıy setting
fire to black tents belonging to Bedouin tribesmen. Black tents
are found in a nearly continuous band stretching from Mauritania in northwestern Africa to Afghanistan, with an isolated
Tibetan group on its own. The principal zone is confined to a
narrow belt between latitudes 25 °N and 40 °N, but the black
tent is the dominant type in a zone between 30 °N and 35 °N.
Their distribution is approximately within the region of desert
vegetation running across North Africa and the Middle East.

350

The distribution of the black tent is related to two animals:
the tent membrane is woven from goat hair and, in the main, it
is transported using the dromedary, except in Tibet where the
yak is used. The distribution of the dromedary is roughly the
same as that oftthe black tent which gives rise to the supposition that both belong to the same stage of cultural evolution
among pastoral nomads.

There are notable differences between the pattern of Arabian
black tents and tents on the northern fringes of Arabia and
North Africa. The roof membrane of the Arabian tent, with
slight variations between the Palestinian tents and desert
Bedouin, the Sba’a and Rwala, has three longitudinal rows of
poles supporting a rectangular membrane, with the poles of
the central row higher than those at the front and back which
gives it its characteristic ridge profile: this is the ridge-tent. The
tent ofthe north Arabs differs significantly in having only two
rows ofpoles-a frontrow anda central row but no back row.

The membrane of the Bedouin ridge-tent is made of 60-
70 cm (23-27 in) widths of black goat hair sewn together and
reinforced by straps (tarige) which spread the forces from the
supporting poles more evenly throughout the tentawning. The
restraining cables, more commonly guy ropes, are extremely
long and this increases their effectiveness as anchor points
in resisting horizontal loads from the wind. The ridge-tent
is primarily a shade shelter and wind-break. Structurally, it
consists of a series of flat saddle surfaces between multiple
internal and edge point supports with acute heads.

The North African black tent, which was brought to the
region by the Arab expansion ofthe ııth century AD, hasastriped
awning, a prominent keel-like transverse ridge supported by
crossed poles and a membrane which falls obliquely almost to
the ground along the short sides. There is greater regional
-diversity in North Africa than in Arabia. The archetypal North
African black tent with its short ridge-piece closely resembles
thetentofthe Moroccan hillnomads. Tents with T-shaped posts
are confined to Arabs living in Tunisia and Egypt, and to the
Uläd Nail. East of Benghazi, the distinctive traits ofthe North
African type are suppressed and Arabian features predominate.
The tents of the Uläd Nail mark the eastern periphery of the
North African short ridge-piece type.

In southwestern Asia the black tent is divided into a western or
Arabian group and aeastern group comprising Iranian, Afghan

uränni barrellacktent, near

Ridge tent, Ghll zal,
‚Afghanistan.

and Tibetan peoples. The break occurs along the border ofthe
plains ofMesopotamia. The eastern group may be further divided
into two subgroupings: the tents of western Iran and those of
Tibet. These two subgroups are separated by awedge of dry tropical forest. To the west ofIran, the Yurruks and the Kizilbash of
Anatolia also have the black tent. In Iran it is found amongst
Kurd, Lur, Bakhtiari, Bassari, Qashgai and Baluch nomads in an
area extending from the Transcaucasus and the Caspian Sea in
the north to the Zargos mountains in the south. The Fars, Lurs
and Qashgai also have a type ofbox tent that is rectangular with
vertical walls, short stays and a relatively flatroofwith a suppressed ridge. In Afghanistan, south ofthe Paropismus and Hindu
Kush mountains, itis found amongthe Moghöl, Brahui and Sistan Baluch nomads. The eastern limitis setby the Sulaiman range.

The mostdense expansion ofthe Tibetan black tent occurs in
a zone north ofthe Indus and Tsangpo rivers in northeastern
Tibet where it is found among both Mongol and Tibetan
nomads. Their structural arrangement varies considerably,
from the box tent ofthe Kurds in western Iran with its central
row of poles to the ridge-tent of the Lurs with its prominent
transverse T-pole in the centre. The Ghilzai tent near Kabul is
stretched over three rows offour poles and the peaks above the
two middle poles are higher than the rest. The Brahui tent
membrane consists of a nartow middle width of goat hair
which is led down to the ground at the sides over two lower
poles on either side ofthe main pole. The Sistan tent is similar
to the Brahui tent but it has middle poles with small wooden
discs on top and a system of diagonal stays.

A hoop-tent type is encountered in Afghanistan amongst the
Baluch (for summer and winter), Duranni and Moghöl nomads.
Hoop or barrel-vaulted black tents have the tent membrane
stretched over a central or two to three parallel arches placed at
right angles to the longitudinal direction ofthe tent. This archsupported membrane type of tent probably originated from a
Primitive hooped reed hut belonging to the Guador people of
Human in Sistan.

Roofs and Spans 1V.5

In all black tents, the main pole or arch supports are internal.
Called ba-nag by the pastoralists who inhabit the high plateau of
central Tibet, the spider-like Tibetan tent is the exception. Itis a
box tentnotunlike the Kurd and Qashgai tentwith aroofawning
that is extended almost horizontally, but in this instance, the
unusual system ofrestraining stays passes over poles placed well
outside the walls ofthe tent. This frees the interior ofobstructing
poles. The shared origin of the Tibetan and other black tents is
attested by the emphasis on the longitudinal axis and the placement ofthe ridge-bar underneath the ridge. It is, nevertheless, a
well-defined and distinct regional type which differs in some
important respects from its Afghan and Iranian neighbours.
These include an awning and walls formed from the one piece,
anda pole provided in the longitudinal axis ofthe awning, above
theridge itself, to letthe smoke escape; stay ropes are attached to
the awning by ‘crow’s feet’ roots and pass over outside poles
placed some distance away from the tent itself.

With reference to the black tenttype, skin tents and mattents
were previously much more widely distributed, but they have
either been pushed back or absorbed by the expansion of the
black tent across North Africa. PHILIP DREW
1.V.5.a Arch
The mostcommon arches in vernacular architecture
are the corbelled arch, the semicircular arch, the segmental
arch and the pointed arch.

The corbelled arch is built with successive overhanging horizontal courses which, theoretically, create no lateral thrust.
Each inward projecting stone mustbessetinsuch a position that
the weight of the next course can balance the vertical pressure
exerted on its projectingend. This arch needs no formwork and
is the easiest to build. However, it is always high, of reduced
span, and must be buttressed by heavy masonry.

The semicircular arch, built with voussoirs, is lower than
the corbelled arch and is easy to draw with a cord or a stick.
However, its height and its span are still interdependent and its
construction needs a formwork. With the effect of the thrust,
which is exerted downward and outward from the crown, the
supports of the arch tend to spread apart, the haunches crack
and the keystone slips downward. For this reason, itis an arch
which does not offer good resistance to heavy loads and which
must be carefully buttressed.

The segmental arch is a part of a semicircular arch, the
centre of which is situated below the spring. Its height and its
span are consequently independent of each other. Its curve is
theoretically ideal butthe thrustttends to the horizontal and the
supports must be massive or well buttressed.

The pointed arch is the most difficult to build, but presents
many advantages. Because of the relation between the line of
pressure and the curve of the arch, the breaking points are
inverse to those of the semicircular arch. Especially when it is
strongly pointed, the arch tends to rise atits crown and to crack
at the haunches, whereas the joints tend to open inward below
the crown. This arch, consequently, offers a better resistance to
vertical loads than the semicircular one. Its structure and its
buttresses are the lightest that can be found in vernacular architecture.

Ru Sean mas

Howeyer, arch construction by traditional and often illiterate
masons is never based on mathematical considerations and is
only a matter oftradition and practical experience. This can be
illustrated by the manner ofconstruction oftheCypriotpointed
arch. The curve is first drawn on the ground, under its future
location. A line represents its width, whereas the height is
figured by a perpendicular projecting from the middle of this
line. Then the mason approximately divides the width in three
portions, explaining: “for each three feet [rm], Ikeep back ı foot
[30 cm]’. Two-thirds ofthe width represents the radius length
which is expressed in concrete form byalength ofbamboo. The
centre of one side of the arch is gropingly found with the
bamboo from one extremity ofthe base and the extremity ofthe
perpendicular, and the intrados is traced from this centre, again
with the bamboo. The same is done for the other side. The
voussoirs are then dressed and thewooden formwork is done in
situ, on the drawing. If, finally, it happens that the whole structure falls down, the mason tries again. IOANNIS TONAS
1.V.5.b Brick slab (Gujarat)
The brick slab is a rarely used but ingenious technique found in Gujarat, India, for makinga floor orceiling slab
entirely out of brick. A typical slab is square and measures
approximately 2.5 m (8 ft) on a side. Each edge of the square
rests on a brick arch, the base of each arch on a brick column.
The bricks employed are oversized and measure approximately
30Xx30x5cm(12X12x2in). Although it is almost certain to
have been invented independently of the Nubian vault, the
brick slab works in principle like a combination ofthe Nubian
vault and a shallow dome. It can also be seen as extending the
principle ofthe flat arch to three dimensions. Beginning at the
four corners ofthe square, the bricks are laid and mortared at
an angle, each brick resting slightly on the one immediately
precedingit. (A flat piece of: formwork may be necessary to support the slab until the mortar dries.) Approximately 20 courses
ofbricks are needed to reach the centre. When the space at the

© Peter engel

35

m

centre becomes too small to fit entire bricks, it is filled with
brickbat and mortar, though it may also be left open as a skylight without disturbing the structural integrity of the slab.
Brick pavers are laid on top of the finished structure to
distribute live loads and to prevent individual bricks from dislodging. The top and bottom surfaces are then plastered.

The completed slab works like a polar cap: adome flattened
to such as degree that it could be contained entirely within the
30 cm (12 in) thick slab. The vertical load on a given brick is
transferred as a compressive force from brick to brick until it
reaches the perimeter ofthe slab. Because ofthe slab’s roughly
radial symmetry, the arc travelled by the compressive forcetakes
the form ofa shallow dome and the resulting thrust is taken at
the periphery by the arches and columns. It is not known
whether this technique is still in use.

1.V1.5.C Ceiling: date-palm

Surveys and studies made of the traditional dwellings in several ksour in the desert areas of North Africa have
shown a variety of different forms ofthe dwelling, but usually
no orthogonal one existed. In some instances, up to five major
forms of housing were achieved, based on the variation in the
number of pillars - from none to four - used in the central hall.
The central hall was chosen because it is usually the largest
space in the dwelling, and the covering of its roof and ceiling
were all-important.

This variety offorms and number of pillars in the central hall
appeared to be linked, to some extent, to the dimensions ofthe
hall and the span that can be covered by the date-palm trunk
beams used. This span rarely exceeds 3 m (10 ft). Beyond this
length, the date-palm trunk beam bends under the heavy
weight ofthe roof. The limited span covered by the beams also
influenced the form of the other rooms of the dwelling. For
instance, in the case ofnarrow corridor-like rooms, to coverthe
ceilingthe beams were laid transversally on the long wall parallel to the short dimension ofthe room.

In the central hall usually two to three date-palm trunk
beams were joined together and supported by the pillars. These
have the function ofthe main beams which support secondary
beams ofdate-palm trunks. In addition, where rooms had large
dimensions, an indigenous technique of setting the beams
in the ceiling was used, whereby the ceiling was divided into
parts, and two joined date-palm trunk beams were used as the
main beams; sometimes single strong beams were used for
this purpose. These beams were used for division transversally
and then single palm trunk beams were laid in the gaps,
supported either by the two main beams or by one main beam
and a nearby wall.

To cover the roofs and ceilings, joists made of date-palm
trunks were usually seton the ceilings, spaced atapproximately

Roofs and Spans 1V.5

60 cm (2 ft) intervals. In most cases, these palm trunk joists
supported a layer of palm sheaths, which had the function of
infilling units between thejoists and, because oftheir rough triangular shape, were laid in a juxtaposed manner on the joists,
giving the ceiling a harmonious pattern. In addition, since the
large parts of the sheaths bulge, they were flattened by being
cut before they were laid on thejoists; this prevented them from
rocking on thejoists.

In some ceilings, or in parts ofthem, a layer ofpalm leaves or
a lattice-work of palm ribs replaces the palm sheaths. It is
interesting to note that in some cases the lattice-work of palm
ribs covering the joists has been laid in an aesthetic pattern. In
some cases short palm trunk bars have replaced the palm
sheaths to cover partorallofthe ceiling. Then a layer ofapproximately 10-20 cm (4-8 in) of earth has been rammed on the
date-palm elements supported by thejoists.

BOUSMAHA BAICHE

1.v5.d Dome

Whilstthe image ofdomed roofs may often be associated with the roofs of important structures such as churches,
mosques and temples, they are just as widely used for making
the roof over homes, granaries, shepherd’s and nomad huts,
and other small and large domestic and public buildings. The
basic dome form is a structure with double curvature, generally
hemispherical in its upper parts.

As well as the basic dome form, domes can be quite pointed,
as in the case of the parabola-shaped earth domes of Syria or
Cameroon, or almost flat, as in the case of some fired brick
structures in Tunisia or Iran (Nä’In). Domes can be built over
circular rooms, butalso over rectangular or multi-sided spaces.
Skilled dome builders in Iran are able to cover almost any
shaped room with domes built of fired or earth bricks.

Domes can be built springing straight from ground level. An
Eskimo snowblock iglu is a good example, although now very
rare. Very similar shaped but larger organic domed structures
are built by fishermen and nomads in the interior delta of the
Niger river in Mali, using a frame of sticks curved up from the
ground to a central point, held in place by a series of concentric
horizontal rings and covered with thatch.

More often, domes are built on supporting walls or arches.
The simplest form is the round room, covered by a hemispherical or parabolic dome. For example, thin shell domes built up
with handmoulded mud are constructed for grain stores in
Nigeria and Niger, and a few are still used for houses. Some of
these structures span over 8 m (26 ft).

Building round domes on rectangular rooms poses problems at the corners. A common solution has been to build
squinch arches or corbelled-out courses of bricks to straddle
the corners and turn a rectangle into a shape closer to a circle.

This transition is often used as a fine architectural feature.
Another method is to finish the supporting walls with an arch
shape that allows the lower part ofthe dome to be built up with
curved triangular pendentives in each corner.

The dome shape can be built supported by ribs made of
stone, as in many churches in Europe, with an infill ofdressed
stone between the ribs, or made of branches and plaster ribs

353

## S. 127

1.V PRODUCTION

with wood and earth making the infill. The ribbed stone roof
requires shuttering, or temporary support, to hold it up during
construction, as do other dressed stone domes, and the technique is expensive. An alternative is the construction of corbelled domes, which have been used to build church roofs (as
in Perigueux, Souillac and Cahors in southwest France), but
also used by shepherds in the same area to build one-room, tiny
stone structures (gariottes) with corbelled roofs. Each progressive course of stone is cantilevered in towards the centre ofthe
dome in smaller and smaller rings. The same technique can be
seen in southern Italy. In both areas there are examples of several corbelled domes linked to make homes. Corbelled domes
do not.need shuttering during construction, and the technique
has been adapted for use with fired bricks in Angola and Senegal. Another dome building method built without shuttering as
the Nubian dome, developed several thousand years ago in
upper Egypt in response to the shortage ofwood. These hemispherical domes are traditionally built up with horizontal concentric rings of unfired mud-bricks. Bricks incline towards the
centre. The position and angle ofeach brick is determined with
the help ofa wire or radial arm rotating round a central pivot.
The profile ofthedome can beadjusted by changingthe form of
the radial arm, and a more parabolic form can be achieved.
These domes are easy to build and require no wood, and in
recent years have been successfully taken up in areas where
ecological degradation has made wooden roofs unviable, and
notably in the sub-Saharan belt of West Africa. The same
domes can be built with fired bricks, stabilized blocks or stone.

In Iran, masons also build domes without shuttering, and in
addition without the use ofa guiding string, using fired bricks
and gypsum plaster mortar, or for smaller rural structures,
earth blocks. There are many different forms and techniques
used. One technique, starting from a flat rectangular base, is to
build up a sloping vault out from each corner, so that when the
four vaults meet the bricks form a diamond pattern when seen
from below. The same technique can be applied to six- oreightsided structures, creating intricate interlocking patterns of
bricks, butrequiring great skill.

In the structure ofa dome, the upper part is in compression
but, depending on the form, the lower part can be in tension,
with a thrust being exerted outwards. In some instances this

thrust has been countered with ties made ofwood or metal (as

in some ofthe very flat domes in the Tunisian town of Korba),
but most often the thrust is resolved by building up extra layers
of material on the outside ofthe dome. Buttresses are another
solution. These external supports in turn give many domes a
shallow profile in their lower levels, which can also be used
advantageously to assurea quick runoffofrainwater, an important factor with earth domes. JOHN NORTON

1.v.5.d-i Dome: ribbed (Sidama)

Living in the southwestern region ofEthiopia on the
southern margins ofthe plateau between Lake Abaya and Lake
Awassa, the Sidamo number approximately 1.5 million people.
The tukul is among the most widely distributed and best-known
types of African ribbed domes, which has represented Ethiopia
inanumber ofinternational exhibitions. Thetukulofthe Sidamo,
a people of southwest Ethiopia, has a slightly ogee section,
which is structurally differentiated but which, in the course of
erection and cladding, is a continuous form. A circlewhich may
beupto 6m (20 ft) in diameter, isscribed on theground, andas
many as sixty poles, about ım (3 ft) in height, are driven into
the earth in groups of three. Longer and more substantial
poles, two on either side, define the entrance and are spanned
with pole lintels, lashed into position. Large quantities ofbamboo are splitand interwoven between the groups ofthree poles,

Isfahan, Iran,

approximately six lengths ofbamboo being woven in a vertical

band before an alternating band is woven above; four or more

alternating bands follow.

After the side walls have been woven a roof-frame is assembled within the eircular pen thus constructed. This roof-frame
consists ofradial bamboo len gths, which converge atthe centre
where they are bound with ensete cord, each radial having a
sleeve ofthis material, and bein gtied to its neighbours. Acapof
bamboo rings, 60-90 cm (2-3 fi) in diameter, is tightly woven,
basket-fashion, round the ends of radials to form a crest that
determines the roof shape and holds the frame at an angle of
approximately 30°. Further rings ofbamboo are bound around
the radials at intervals ofabout 30cm (1fl). Athird central pole
3-3.6. m (10-12 ft) in height is then raised in the centre ofthe
eircle, with a small cruciform arrangement of short lengths of
bamboo lashed firmly at the top. A collar of ensete cordage prevents itslipping and a ring bound in cord, the size and form of
the rings used in carrying water vessels on the head, is placed
over the end ofthe pole. This acts as a cushion for the peak of
the roof-frame and prevents the poleand crossed bamboo from
penetrating the roofcap. Eventually itmay be surmounted with
Be. alarge grass and bamboo finial.

1991 With the pole and the roof-frame in place the ends ofthe split
onald,1978 bamboo radials are fed into the bands ofthe woven walls, their
flexibility permitting this process and allowing adjustments to

va be made until the desired form is achieved, Further rings are
(unporting dome. woven round the lower ends of the radials until wall and roof

are effectively one. Over the completed frame is now placed a
layer ofensete or bamboo leaves which are pegged into therings
with short lengths of bamboo stalk; finally, the entire roof is
covered with slivers ofbamboo which cover the leaves to form
both an insulating layer and a water-deflecting outer layer;
these are insertedatboth ends, givingthe impression ofa finely
woven basket of great size. Where the doorway opening has
been left the radials are shorter and inserted between the two
lintels. A projecting porch in the form ofa ‘nose’ oran inclined
plane shades the doorway.

PAUL OLIVER

1.V.5.d-ii Dome: self-supporting (Rajasthan)

A technique for building a masonry dome without
using wooden scaffolding is employed in the Indian state of
Rajasthan. In ordinary dome construction the bricks or blocks of
Cut stone that make up each ring will slide into the cavity ofthe
dome unless they are supported by aa wooden scaffold. Once all
the blocks in a given ring have been placed and the mortar has
dried, the ring supports itself and the scaffolding can be
removed. The self-supportingdome, thatis, adomebuilt without
formwork, uses an external force, the pressure from a weighted
rope, to hold the bricks in place until the zing can support itself.

When such a dome is to be built, the masons erect halfa
dozen to a dozen poles, each slightly taller than the peak ofthe
intended dome, inaring surrounding the base ofthe building.
Nearby trees may be used in place of some of the poles. The
masons attach three or four ropes to the top ofeach pole and
counterweight the other end ofthe rope with a loose stone or
brick. The two orthreedozen ropes are then swung overthe top
ofthe ring under construction so thatthe weights dangle inside
the incomplete dome. When the masons lay the blocks or
bricks for an unfinished ring, the Pressure from a rope holds
each piece in place. As soon as one ting is complete, they can
begin the next, even without waiting for the mortar to dıy.

PETER ENGEL

1v.5.e Earth roof

The house with the earth roof (or flat roof, orterrace)
can be found in a zone around the world, from California to
Central America, and from West Aftica and the periphery ofthe
Mediterranean to China, including North Africa, the Caucasus,
the Iranian Plateau and India. This type ofroof maintains the
freshness ofthe house and is well-adapted to hot climates.

Although it is called flat, the earth roof is always slightly
sloped. It rests either on small wooden beams (joists), 30-40 cm
(11.9-15.7 in) apart in the Near East, or on stone arcades when
no wood is available. The space between these supports can be
closed in various ways according to the available materials and
the financial conditions: branches laid across joists, blades of
reeds, bound latticework of palmribs or reeds, matting of
reeds, boards, stone slabs or even small segmental vaults built
with small stones.

Ifthe infrastructure is ofstone, itis directly covered byalayer
oframmed earth. Otherwise, itis often protected bya bed ofdry
plants (algae, scrubs, pine-needles, leaves), which allows it to
be ventilated and preserves it from humidity. Then comes the

355

## S. 128

——

1V PRODUCTION

earth, mud, composted earth mixed with ashes, or mud or
earth from pond beds mixed with organic matters and allowed
to ferment so as to become waterproof. In the Near Bast and
from antiquity, when the earth is not sticky, itis rammed with a
stone roller which stays permanently off the roof. In some
areas, a thin layer of clay is spread on the earth and renewed
every year. Salt can also be thrown on the roof to prevent the
growing of grass whose roots fissure the rammed earth. The
total thickness varies between 20 and 40 cm (7.9-15.7 in).

The earth roof must be permanently kept in repair. Every
year, the grass must be pulled up, the cracks filled in and some
earth added and rammed. IOANNIS IONAS

Gable roof/pitched roof
The simplest kind of gable roof has two sloping
sidesthatmeetataridge and terminate atthe gable-end, which
is atriangular piece ofwall, often inacontinuous vertical plane
with the wall below. The angle of incline of the two sides is
often the same, providing symmetty ofthe roof around a vertical plane that contains the ridge. The ridge may be horizontal,
as itis all over Europe and America, or it may be curved, lower
in the centre and sometimes with ends that project beyond the
end ofthe building, the most pronounced examples being in
Indonesia. Gable roofs, which most often have a timber structure, are found in most parts ofthe world where wood is plentiful and where the sloping roof helps to shed rainwater and/or
where a continuation oftheroofbeyond the wall, inthe form of
an overhanging eave, can help provide shade to the exterior
wall ofthe building. Buildings may have more than one gable
roof, and the roofs may be of different sizes, different ridge
heights, and with ridges that are either parallel or perpendieular to each other. Gable dormers are small building volumes,
with their own gable roofs, that project from a main roof,
usually in a direction perpendicular to the ridge, and allow the
space under the main roofto be useful and to receive light. .
Agableroofcan bevery efficientfrom astructural point ofview
since the principal spanning members need only span between
thewall and theridge, rather than from wall to wall. This reduces
the depth required of these members to support the load of
the roof- a load that may include snow, and in some northern
culture, turf. The angle of the sides of a gable roof takes on
a characteristic value in a particular region. In places where
snow is used as an insulating material, and where the volume
ofthe building interior is to be kept low in order to keep heating
demand low, the roof pitch may be kept low; this is the case, for
example, in many rural buildings in Scandinavia. In rainy Diates,
or where a high interior volume is desired to allow heat to rise,
the roof pitch may be much higher, as in south and southeast
Asia. In these cases there may be openingsatthetop ofthe gableends to allow heatto escape. A high-pitched roof may also allow
for efficiency of space utilization within the building; extreme
examples are found in large Swiss farm structures, which may
include several floors within the sides ofa gable roof.

Gable toofs may be composed ofdifferent structural systems.
Early forms, examples of which may still be found in Scandinavia, Russia and eastern Europe, consist of whole logs that
span parallel to the ridge, from gable-end to gable-end. These

1.v.5.f

356

may be finished with boards thatrun perpendicular to the ridge
or with living turf. More structurally efficient systems — that
is, those that use less material to achieve the same span - use a
hierarchy of framing members that distribute roofloads to relatively few points of support. These framing members include
rafters, thatspan the rooffrom theridge to the wall, and purlins,
thatspan the roof parallel to the ridge, from gable-end to gableend. Gable roof systems may be composed of combinations of
these elements, with major purlins supporting rafters, or major
rafters supporting purlins, and various other hybrid forms.
Some systems that use rafters as their primary structural
componentrelyona ridge-beam, astructural member thatsupports rafters that span between the ridge and a parallel wall.
The ridge-beam may be supported by the gable wall and/or by
columns along its length. In other systems rafters essentially
lean against each other at the ridge, resulting in an outward
thrustatthewallthat mustbe resisted with the use oftie-beams
or by other means. The use of a tie-beam also zeduces the
necessary depth ofa rafter. More elaborate three-dimensional
systems were highly developed in Europe, South and Southeast
Asia and Japan. Some ofthese systems rely on trusses that both
support the roofand stabilize the structure, or on purlins that
are supported on vertical posts that themselves rest on hör:
zontal spanning members thatare at the level ofthe eaves. This

last system is most typical ofJapanese construction.
HOWARD DAVIS
