1.1.5.8 Hipped roof

Asimple hipped roofis one that has a plane sloping
down to an eave on all sides, in contrast to a gable roofthat has
an eave on two sides and a vertical gable-end on the other two
sides. A hipped roof may be pyramidal, in which case there
is no ridge, and where the roof planes meet in a single point;
or itmay have a ridge, in which case the two end planes ofthe
roof each meet one end ofthe ridge. A pyramidal hipped roof
is often associated with a square plan. A hipped roof allows all
the walls ofa building to be built to the same height before the
construction of the roof framework without the necessity of
shaping a gable-end wall. In general the apparent symmetries
ofa hipped roof may give a centralizing aspect to the building.
Hipped roofs are common in many areas ofthe world, including eastern and southeastern Europe, Russia and various
wooded regions around the Mediterranean.

Variations on the simple hipped roof form include a roof on
which the slope changes and becomes shallower where the
roof projects to form an overhanging eave orto shelter a porch.
This allows for a relatively steep roof over the main part ofthe
building, with the exterior wall not as high as it would otherwise have to be for the roof to project over a habitable porch.
This is typical, for example, in some houses ofthe Mississippi
river valley, influenced by both French and Caribbean traditions. Other variations include hybrids between gableroofs and
hipped roofs, in which the main part ofthe building is covered
with a gable roof, but where a hipped roof, two sides of which
are extensions of the gable roof, forms a continuous eave all
around the building. Such roofs are used, for example, in
places where a continuous open porch surrounds the building
- often hot, humid climates. Such hybrid forms also allow for
openings in the tops ofthe gable-ends, providing for the warm
rising air to pull in after itthe cooler air from the shaded porch.

The simplest structure of a hipped roof consists of wooden
members of equal section, rising out of the top of the walls,
leaning inwards and meeting either the peak or the ridge to
form the four planes ofthe roof. In this case, the rafters are not

necessarily parallel with each other and the hip rafters - at the

Roofs and Spans 1.5

intersection ofthe roof planes, above the corners ofthe building
—do notreceive the load ofany ofthe other rafters. Such asystem
can be seen, for example, in houses in Kerala State, India. In systems with a greater structural hierarchy, the hip rafter is amore
critical element, receivingthe load ofthe secondary rafters. In the
case ofa square pyramidal hipped roof, the hip rafters support
halfthe load ofall the other rafters, which span between the top
ofthe wall and the hip rafters. HOWARD DAVIS
1.v.5.h Lantern roof (Asia Minor and Caucasus)

The lantern roof house derives its name from its
roofform and has stemmed from the need for a large singlevolume which can house the whole family and livestock and where
food for everybody and firewood can be stored for the eightmonth-long winters in the mountainous regions of Asia Minor
and the Caucasus when temperatures may fall to as low as-40 °C
(-40 °F). Generally, in this type ofconstruction diagonal beams
are placed at the corners ofthe opening of a square volume to
decrease the span. More beams ofdecreasing length are placed
on these in the same fashion forming squares turning ata 45°
angle, or an octagon, and thus the opening gradually decreases
forminga pyramidal or dome-shaped roof.

Because of firewood shortage, a single fireplace is used both
for heatingand cooking and the bodyheat ofthe livestock helps
keep the interior warm. The most interesting examples of
lantern roof houses are found in east Asia Minor, in the vicinity
of Mus. This type ofhouse can be semi-subterranean, dug into
aslope, or completely above ground.

The urban house examples in settlements like Erzurum and
Tbilisi have an unusual spiral effect on the interior volume. The

2

## S. 129

1v PRODUCTION

lantern roof houses, called tandir ev in Asia Minor, are called
karadam or sakli in Azerbaijan, darbazi in Georgia, and tun or
‚glhatun in Armenia.

Rooms in a lantern roof house get light only from a hole at
thetop ofthe overlappingroofconstruction and this ray oflight
travels round the room throughout the day in a spectacular
fashion. The fact thatthese houses, which date back averylong
way, are often buried in the ground and the fact that they owe
their existence to an extremely cold and snowy climate has led
to their being perceived as an individual mierocosm. \

These types of construction are also used for religious purposes in all the cultures where they are found which is proof of
their symbolic characteristic. The religious buildings that the
North American Pueblo Indians call kiva, the Indian and Central Asian Buddhists call mandapa, the heterodox Georgians call
darbazi, the non-Muslim people of Afghanistan call jestak-han
and the heterodox Turks of Asia Minor call cemevi, all have

5 KIN
lantern roofs. GÜNKUTA

Lintel

A horizontal element which spans the void of an
opening in a wall, such as a window or doorway, is termed a
‘inte’. Unlike the arch, which is a structural system which
gains strength from the compression of the youssoits or
wedge-shaped pieces from which it is constructed, die lintel is
aload-bearing member. Itis subject to the forces acting upon a
beam with compressive loads on the upper face while the lower
face is in tension. For this reason, timber is frequently used for
a lintel instead of stone, as stone is in the main a compressive
rather than atensile material, whereas timber has greater tensile
properties though it may not bear the compressive weights that

1.v.5.i

stone can support.

Alintel across a window opening or doorway can be ofstone
ifitis substantial enough and ifthe loads borneatthe centre are
notofa scale that may cause fracture. Shaping the stone so that
it is higher at the centre helps to transmit the loads to the
supporting walls or piers, though it increases the unsupported
weight; carved mouldings as for a low pediment help to counteract any visual discomfort. Where a stone lintel has to be
made in more than one piece a ‘joggled joint’ -ajointofashape
and nature that it resists shear action and cannot be separated
by vertical pressure — is sometimes used. Alternatively, a flat
arch of trapezoid voussoirs and a central ‘keystone’ is sometimes employed in vernacular houses.

Timber lintels may be subjected to considerable compression ifthe loads they bear are ofstone. In northern Europe wellseasoned oak was considered to be the strongest timber for this
purpose. Generally the ends of the timber lintel extend well
beyond the opening and the supporting frame, to be bedded
into the stonework of the wall. In effect, this produces a can-

tilever at each end which contributes to the resistance of the
beam to vertical forces.

Stone lintels have also been used extensively to span fireplace
openings. On Dartmoor (British Isles) massive granite Intel
slabs are placed so that they rest on the narrower dimension,
presenting a large surface above the opening. Certain.of these
are reputed to have been robbed from Bronze Age granite burial
boxes (kistavens), once found in numbers on the moor. Supportingjambs may also have been former ‘standing stones’, or early
waymarkers, though some bear the marks ofthe stone-cutter’s
drills with which the slabs were split. Known as ‘bressummers’
or ‘breastsummers’, oak lintels are also widely used to span
fireplace openings, the inner face often being chamfered to

help direct smoke up the chimney. PAUL OLIVER
1V.5J Squinch and pendentive

Amasonry dome springs from acircular plan, which
presents problems for the mason who wishes to span = cubie
space with adome. As the circle will only make contact with the
square plan atthe centre points ofeach side, there is no support
for the main structure. A dome set further over a wall so thata
larger surface makes contact with the supporting masonıy will
either overhang, which is clearly not an acceptable solution, or
itrequires a wall thick enough on all four sides to take the full
curvature ofthe base ofthe dome. This produces a deep semicircular lip or shelf at the top ofthe masonıy walls, which is
sometimes used but which diminishes the effect ofthe domed
space. The most common solution to the problem accepts that
the thickness ofthe dome will only be supported at the centres
ofthe cube to the depth ofthe wall.

Each of the four uncovered spaces remaining is defined by
the two sides ofa corner perpendicular to each other, and the
arc of the segment ofthe dome that spans across them. This
space is closed either by a ‘squinch’ or a ‘pendentive’, the putpose of both being to continue the curvature ofthe dome until
this meets the corner of the cube. The squinch normally consists ofa succession ofarches spanning the corner, commencing with a short arch and continuing with three or four arches
increasing in span, one above the other until the lip ofthedome

See also for,
2.1V.2-A Asii
Caucasus, South

Reference forqy,
Akin, Günkut, 1985

See also
2.111.3.e-i Devol

Granite lintel
entrancetoal
Babeny, Dartmi

is supported. As the arch is a strong structure the void behind
the arches may be closed with masonty, which is not seen from
inside. As a succession of projecting arches, squinches may be
used to support other superimposed masses, such as the faces
of.an octagonal drum, which may in turn support a dome. A
single arch across a corner, which is normally insufficient to
provide the support for a segment ofa dome but which may
bear a small one, or another form of corner. load, is termed a
‘sconce’.

A pendentive performs a similar function to a squinch but is
laid in arcs of masonry across the corner, each successive and
broader span of masonıy being bonded in a continuous curve.
The lowest point ofthe pendentive may be supported bya corbel
ora corner pier. Variants ofboth systems are numerous, including pendentive bracketing, or the use ofa single pendentive
which supports a double pendentive which, in turn, supports
the dome rim, the masonry ofthe pendentive arcs to be subtly
trimmed and coved within so that a continuous curved plane is
achieved. Examples ofeach method areto be found inall regions,
butespecially in the Middle East where masonry domes are built.

PAUL OLIVER

1.V5.k Truss roof

The truss roof is essentially a structural system in
which ‘principal’ rafters support horizontal purlins, which in
their turn support ‘common’ rafters. The trusses are spaced at
regular intervals and transfer the load ofthe roof down generally to the head ofthe main posts or to the walls. The system is
distinguished from untrussed roofs in which loads are distributed evenly to the wall plate by the common rafters, in a form
best described as a “uniform scantling roof”.

The uniform scantling roofis common to framed buildings
in the Weald of England which pre-date the 16th century and
was known generally as the ‘erownpost’ or the ‘kingpost’ roof.
These roofs consisted of simple pairs ofrafters, connected ata
high level by collars, with tie-beams serving solely to connect
the heads ofthe main posts. Principal rafters and side purlins
were notably absent and the only lateral beam in the assembly,
omitted in early examples, was the collar purlin which connected
the heads ofthe crownposts and which served to stabilize the
otherwise weak construction. The early forms of ‘sans-purlin’

Roofs and Spans AV5

roofs were liable to overturning and bucking of the wall plates
and longitudinal collapse ofthe rafters,
With the introduction of collar Purlins and cerownposts, a
perfect equilibrium was created. This was further improved
with the addition of struts, which reduced the distance of. span
ofthe purlin and provided longitudinal stiffening by triangulating the roofin all directions. This system was popular well into
the 15th century, when it gradually gave way to truss roofs with
side purlins and wind-braces. These prineipals were coupled by
tie-beams to form a truss, They were very economic ofmaterial
and, by using enlarged Principals at intervals to coincide with
the main frame ofthe building, enabled larger distances to be
spanned cheaply. It is likely that cost, rather than structural
Preference, was the factor governing the decline ofthe uniform
scantling roof, as timber became more and more scarce in
medieval England.
The use ofthe truss roofwas regarded as one ofthe mostrrevolutionary changes in medieval housebuilding in The Weald
and it is believed to have spread from the northwest and west
Midlands, where it was in common usage in the 14th century.
Elsewhere, in Essex, the kingpost roof gave way in the 16th century largely to the queenpost roof, which was based on a series
of framed trusses, each consisting ofa braced tie-beam, from
which a pair of upright queenposts rose, equidistant from the
centre ofthe ties to form principal rafters connected byacollar
about halfway up the truss. Struts sprang from the queenposts
to the principalrrafter and to the collar and carried a ridge-piece
and side purlins, which in turn supported common rafters.
Both the queenpost and the purlin and wind-braced roof
enabled an attic storey to be utilized as living space more easily
than in a crownpostroof, buteven the crosspiece in the queenpost roof had to be accommodated and restricted flexibility. In
the 17th century the wind-braces ofthe Purlin and wind-braced
roof disappeared and the rafters, purlins and principals were
made to act asjoists, girders and main girders in the butt purlin
toof, in a system which lasted until exposed roofs became
unfashionable and the roof truss declined into nothing more
than a rough method of purlin strutted into the tie-beam. The
toof trusses which pre-date this decline are testimony to the
complex system ofjointing used in the connections.

DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

1.v.5.1 Turf roofs (Scandinavia)

Turf roofs were widely used in the medieval period
on farm buildings throughout Scandinavia, and still have alimited use on contemporary new buildings.

The technique produced a roofthatwas heavy butwhich had
good insulation qualities compared to other roofing methods
in use atthat time. This type ofroof has to be renewed at regular intervals when the turf degenerates or when small bushes
begin to grow. The roof has to have a Pitch which allows it to
drain well, where the turfcan be held in place, and where snow
can settle in winter; this is often around 27

Robust boarding is necessary on which is laid a layer of
birchbark, with all the pieces having generous overlaps. Ideally
this should be followed by a network ofbirch twigs and small
branches, and gravel or small stones spread in a thin layer.

359

## S. 130

1.V PRODUCTION

© Michael Lloye

Then two layers of turf are laid, the first upside down. It is
possible to omit the twig network, gravel and the lower layer
ofturf, but the roof will not perform as well or last as long. A
modern variant uses a corrugated waterproof layer of tough
plastic in place ofbirchbark.

The eaves at the bottom of the roof are finished by a solid
board held in place by hooks which extend some distance up
the roof under the birchbark. Both board and hooks were
preferably made from oak. The birchbark/waterproof layer
must continue under the eaves board and form a water drip
nosing clear of the roof boarding. A form of barge-board was
often used on the gable eaves; this would also be a substantial
piece of timber and was held in place by long wooden pins
bored through the board and dug well into the turf.

MICHAEL LLOYD

1.v.5.m Vaults

Vaults can cover rectangular, usually oblong, spaces.
The basic vault form is a horizontal curved roof. Apart from
rectangular spaces, skilled masons can cover slightly irregularshaped rooms which narrow towards one end, and of which
there are good examples in Iran. Complex configurations result
byjoining two vaults atan angle. In Europe the angle formed by
intersecting vaults is called a ‘groin’, hence the term ‘groined
vaults’ used in Romanesque and Gothic architecture. Early versions had a plain groin, as do contemporary domestic examples in Tunisia and Iran. In Gothic architecture ribs were added
to the groin, used as a structural and decorative element. Ribs
forming arches are used to make straight vaults, as in the case
of the vaulted Iraqi mudhif, where curved reed bundles form
ribs which support a reed covering. An example of ribs made
of earth reinforced with sticks which have been used to form
arches, covered by short sticks and earth to form a vaulted roof,
can be found in Bancoucou, Niger.

More commonly, vaults are builtwith masonry. Most masonryvaults (and indeed domes) are in areas where wood is scarce,
and where stones, earth bricks or fired bricks are available for
roofing: vaults are common in North Africa, in southern
Italy and in arid areas such as Iran. Vaults are useful where a
solid upper floor is needed, and vaulted cellars covered with
tiled Aoors are common in southwest France. Masonry cellars
have good thermal qualities and keep cool; likewise, in hotarid
countries the thermal properties ofmasonry vaults are appreciated. The high roofspace often achieved with avault is also used
advantageously for letting warm air rise and escape through

360

roof vents or high openings in end walls. Multi-storey vaulted
structures are possible, and some flatroofs for sleeping on, but
thevault has to be levelled offwith extra material, or bybuilding
secondary small vaults on each side of main vaults, which
works where several vaults are side by side. Parallel rows of
vaults enable the outward thrust of each vault to be countered.
Without this counterthrust side walls must be thick to transmit
the thrust to the ground. Walls can be reinforced with buttresses. Wooden or metal tie bars are also used.

A vault’s shape influences its strength and stability, and the
amount of thrust exerted. Natural forces in a vault descend to
the ground following the curve of an inverted catenary - the
form made by a chain when suspended from both ends. These
lines of thrust do not, however, conform to the semicircular
shape ofthe classic barrel vault and to overcome this the lower
partofthevaultneeds additional thickness, sometimes builtup
as a parapet, or buttresses, to actas a counterforce. The pointed
Gothic vault corresponds better to the catenary, but there are
also much shallower vaults that follow quite closely this same
curve. However, lower vaults exert greater outward thruston the
walls, and one approach has been to build partly underground.

The vaults that correspond most closely to the catenary are
earth brick Nubian vaults. These vaults have existed for at least
5600 years, with standing examples in Egypt, where they are
still used. Similar vaults are used in Iran, where there is an
ancient tradition of vault building to cover anything from a
store to a palace. Vaults from Nubia and Iran have another
important quality: they can be built without shuttering or temporary support. European vault builders have had to support
the roof during construction, using wood or piled up material
for shuttering. In arid, treeless countries techniques evolved to
eliminate the shuttering. For the Nubian vault almost vertical
courses are built up following the catenary curve, with each
course built inclined against an end wall. A sticky mud or
plaster mortar is used. The slope ofthe brick and the adhesion
ofthe mortar prevent the brick falling down during construc-

tion, and the catenary form of the vault means that as soon as
each course is complete it is structurally stable: itis possible to
stand on a vault whilst the mortar is still wet.

In Iran the traditional techniques have been adapted to build
low, ı m (3 ft) wide, jack arch vaults between I-beams, and
similar larger span, brick, low-rise vaults are built in Tunisia.
Both used fired bricks and usually a gypsum-based mortar
which is very adhesive.

The advantage of building earth vaults without shuttering
has led to their contemporary introduction in parts ofthe world
where trees are becoming scarce. JOHN NORTON

1.V.5.m-i Vault: corbelled

Dry stone walling has been the preferred method of
construction for the field walls and structures that characterize
the agricultural landscape of stony regions. By combining with
specific roofing techniques, mainly the vault of corbelled and
outward-inclining stones and, to a lesser extent, the vault of
stone voussoirs or keystone vault, it has also enabled a genuine
peasantarchitecture to materialize in the form ofdry stone huts
(cabanes).

ngelevation
perspective
dry-stone

See also for 1.V:
2.1.2. Baltic anı
2.111.8-A Nordic

Referenee
Fathy, Hu 19707

© © Michel Rouviere

As shown by the French cabanes (e.g. the caselas of Aveyron,
oustalets of the Cevenol Gard, cabordes of Doubs, barracuns of
Corse-du-Sud) or the dry stone huts of other European countries (e.g. the barraques of Spain’s Valencia province, casedde of
Southern Italy’s Apulia, girnas of the Maltese islands, booley
huts ofwestern Ireland’s Dingle peninsula), the corbelled vault
has been most popular with the self-building peasants or dry
stone craftsmen of the last two or three centuries for roofing
space at the least possible cost.

This type of vault, when constructed on a circular basis,
obeys two principles: the corbelling method and the method of
the outward inclination. The principle ofcorbelling consists of
laying the stones in each circular course with a slight overhang
over the stones underneath. It is imperative that the centre of
gravity of each stone should be kept within the depth of the
underlying stone. To this effect, each corbel is laid with its long
edges into the wall for counterweight, while its overhanging
end is trimmed. It is also necessary for the corbels to betapered
so that their interfaces should radiate towards the centre ofthe
eircle.

The principle of the outward inclination consists of giving
the stones ofeach course a slight outward slope (ofabout 15°).
(If the stones were laid flat, they would form, to be precise, a
“tas-de-charge’ vault made up of horizontal courses.) This inclination causes the slabs inside each course to buttress each
other in a horizontal plane, resulting in a polygon of forces
being closed: each course thus becomes self-binding and
autonomous while being borne by the one below. The outward
thrustexerted by each course is cancelled by piling up on top of
it material that serves as abutment.

Roofs and Spans 1.5

The succeeding layers curve inwards gradually until they
almost meet, the final layer being topped by a single capstone.
No formwork or framework is required in this vault with horizontal stress (as opposed to the conventional keystone vault
which is with vertical stress). While the removal ofthe keystone
from a vault of voussoirs will bring about the fall ofthe whole
vault, the final capstone in a corbelled vault can be removed
without causing the vault to collapse.

It should be borne in mind that a dry stone vault, whether
corbelled or keystone, is in its turn concealed by an outside covering of slabs or flat stones after the technique of the ‘double
skin’.

Attempts have been made to contrast the corbelled vaultand
the keystone vault by calling the former a “false vault’, as
though the keystone vault were the only ‘true’ vault. In fact, the
expression “false vault’ is to be reserved for a covering other
than of stone (for example, of painted wood), imitating the
form and appearance of a stone vault. The corbelled vault
should be regarded as a vault in its own right, buta ‘horizontal’
one as opposed to the 'vertical’ one. CHRISTIAN LASSURE
1.v.5.m-ii Vault: gypsum (Algeria)

Gypsum is classified as sedimentary rock. It occurs
in nature in different forms: rock gypsum, and gypsum sand or
aeolian gypsum. The former is formed through the evaporation
of salt water, while the latter consists of beds or sand dunes of
wind-blown powder which can form a harsh solid crust in arid
lands such as the Algerian Sahara (Coburn and Spence).

Gypsum is used as a traditional local building material, constructing and defining the shape ofthe traditional buildings in
the region of Souf, located in the sand dunes ofthe Grand Erg
Oriental in the northeast of the Algerian Sahara. To prevent
wind-blown sand accumulation on the roofs, ten thousand
cupolas - elliptical domes - and barrel vaults crown El Oued,
the capital of Souf, and its surrounding settlements, Guemar,
Kounien and Zquem. They are all typical in their form of the
courtyard houses and their method ofconstruction.

One type of gypsum used in the region is loussa, in which the
gypsum become encrusted with quartz sand to form rose du
sable; this is formed overlying a sand bed and underlying sand
dunes. Owing to its quality and hardness it is used in construction of the vaults and domes. Tafza and sallage stones are also

## S. 131

1.V PRODUCTION

used in the region. Characterized by being softer and ofpoorer
quality, they are burnt to produce gypsum powder. When
hydrated with water this is used as a binding agent for the construction ofarches, vaults and domes.

The traditional house is typified by its large interior open
courtyard, thick plastered domes with a diameter of2.2m (7 fü
and a height not exceeding 2.5 m (8 fD, and vaults which cover
its rectangular surrounding rooms to prevent sand settling on
the roof. For protection from the occasional flash rain storms
and for insulation a gypsum plaster paste with a high water:
gypsum ratio by weight ofapproximately 1:2.5 is poured on top
oftheroof (Cherif).

Domes were the common form of roof on a square room;
there can be two or three domes covering a rectangular room,
placed side by side; thus, several small domes supported by
transverse ribs cover a rectangular room.

The construction ofa vault was improved by the French who
taughtthe Soufies thevaulttechniqueand their use has become
widespread in theregion. The barrel vaultis used to build much
more spacious rooms, particularly in El Oued.

To seta dome, a pendentive is constructed at each corner of
the square room to form the ringor circumference ofthe dome.
By pulling a string which is equal in length to the radius ofthe
dome ring, and attached to the top ofa stick (set in the middle
of the room) at one end and his hand at the other end, the
mason traces a perfect hemisphere. He then sets diminishing
circular courses ofplaster blocks laid one on top ofthe other.

MOHAMMED SHERZAD

1.V.5.m-iii Vault: timber barrel (Central Europe)

A corner-timbered construction still exists in Central Europe, in which the horizontal beams of the narrower
main walls as they progress upwards gradually become shorter.
This means that the side corner-jointed walls incline towards
the centre as a barrel vault. Should the main walls also incline,
the construction is shaped like a monastic vault. This alternative was described in Roman literature by Vitruvius. With
regard to central Europe, the oldest evidence points to the use
oftimber barrel vaults in medieval castles in which rooms with
such vaults served as warm bedrooms, as well as rooms for the
women and children. Iconographic evidence from 14th-century
Bohemia shows that this form oftimbering likewise applied to
the various bed-sitting rooms in the town houses. It was also
known in thevillages. A typical concomitantemblem consisted
ofa triangular arrangement of small windows in the main wall.

Atthat time, the entire assembly undoubtedly complied with
the customary way of heating a medieval living-room with the
open hearth and bake-oven; as the result ofsuch an operation,
some ofthe polluted hotairthatremained in the medieval livingroom accumulated in the upper vaulted part of the high space
and was therefore ventilated through the upper ventin the main
wall. The abnormal height of this space, including the upper
vents, disappeared with the gradual development of heating
equipment, and the transformation ofthe medieval hall into a
domestic living-room at the close ofthe Middle Ages.

As for rural construction methods, the timber barrel vaults
remained, especially in the granary buildings from the Alps

362

through Bohemia and Silesia right into Slovakia and Poland.
Until recently the timber barrel vaults also remained in rare in-
$tances in thesittingrooms ofold 16th-century buildings. Also to
be found in the counttyside are similar wood and clay constructs.
For fire prevention purposes, the timber barrel vaults are
smeared with layers of clay. Also, for further protection, the
whole building is covered with gable roofs that are structurally

separate from the vaults and easily removable in case ofa fire.
JOSEF VAREKA

4.4.5.m-iv Vault: tube (Hyderäbäd)

The burnt clay tube vault, known in India alternatively as a Guna- or Hyderäbäd-style roof, is a parabolic or
catenary-shaped roof vault made of cylindrical clay tiles. The
origins of the technique’s use in India are not well known; it
was used by early Romans and Mesopotamians and may have
been invented independently in India. In this century it has
fallen out of use, although it is currently being revived by a
number of Gandhian institutions as a solution to the country’s
shortage ofaffordable housing.

The clay tubes are fabricated on a potter’s wheel and take the
shape ofa hollow cylinder with one end slightly larger than the
other. While the tubes are fired in an oven and cooled, atemporary scaffolding is constructed of wood or bamboo with the
longitudinal members at approximately 30 cm (1 ft) centres.
The scaffolding terminates at the top ofthe walls atthe springingline ofthe arch; tie-beams must be laid on top ofthe wallto

1.1.4.1 Gypsum

References for.
Cherif, Emad,1
Coburn, Andrew, and’
Spence, Robin
Etherton, Davi
Tindert, Massa

pöttler, Viktof
Tioczek, Ignat)

illai, 5. U.,1990

take the first tube on either side. One by one, tubes are placed
on the scaffolding to form an arch by inserting the small end of
one tube into the large end of another. Each successive arch

reverses the direction ofthe tubes. A typical arch spanning4 m

(13 ft) uses 20 to 30 20-cm (8 in) tubes. Thick walls, buttresses

or tie rods are required to withstand the thrust. Small vents at

the peak allow hotair to escape.

When all the tubes have been placed, they are covered with
mud phuska, mud and cow dung mixed with hay as a stabilizer,
or are wet thoroughly and then mortared with a mixture of
lime, cementand sand. A final coat oflime-plaster is applied to
the inside. The resulting roofresists rain, rodents and insects,
insulates well against heat (theair gap inside the tubes provides
cost-free insulation), uses no non-renewable wood resources
and provides jobs by employing the traditional potter’s craft.
Surviving examples of traditional vaults can be seen near the

Golconda tombs in Hyderäbäd, Andhra Pradesh.

PETER ENGEL

Roofs and Spans

1V.5

© Potor Engol

363

## S. 132

ıvsa Supports and Floors

Itcould beargued that some buildings are ‘all xoof”;
they might equally be considered ‘all wall’. The A-frame isan
example; being based on the principle of triangulation, itisa
most stable structural element. The term can be appropriately
used to describe a pair of opposing rafters converging and
jointed atan apex, witha tie-beam connecting them atthe base.
This is customarily identified as a ‘truss‘, and the term ‘A-frame’
is more commonly applied to a simple triangular structure
formed by one inclined side plane leaningagainstanotheralong
their top edges, the lower edges resting on the ground which
completes the triangular section. Yeteven these frequently have
asupporting skeleton, as is often the case with temporary shelters and wind-breaks. Such shelters offer minimal accommodation, and standing room may only be possible in the centre;
the limited space at the meeting ofside plane and ground is virtually unusable. The immense A-frame barns constructed by
farmers in the Revelstoke region of British Columbia are sometimes 15 m (50 ft), or more in height, and by keeping the base
narrow in relation to the height, with the inclined roof planes
being as steep as 70°, most ofthe internal space can be used.

Large or small, such A-frame structures are neither roofnor
wall- or they are both. They can be said to be “undifferentiated’
as no distinction can be made between the side walls and the

toof. If, however, one or both ends are closed then the building
is in part differentiated, having one or two walls that are constructed independently ofthe roof. Undifferentiated buildings
are principally domes, or unclosed vaults.Theigluisan undifferentiated domical structure, though it is constructed from blocks
laid in a spiral and is structurally different from most domes
built in the vernacular or in the elite traditions. Curiously, its
construction is akin to that ofthe traditional bee-skep which is
made oftwisted straw rope, laid in a continuous and tapering
spiral. The mostfrequently usedand misleading of popular form
descriptions, the ‘beehive shape’, or an equivalent phrase, is
applied indiscriminately to shallow, pointedandribbed domes,
the imprecision oftheterm renderingituseless for form deseription (see 1.vır1.2 Typology of Forms: Domical). However, it does
identify their common undifferentiated character.

In a small number of instances, such as the dwelling of the
Sidamo of Ethiopia, the building may be constructed with separate walls and roofwhich are subsequentlyjoined to produce a
continuous cutved profile and an apparently undifferentiated
structure. Such cases are rare: differentiated buildings constitute themajority oftheworld’s vernacular traditions and are built
with a clear distinction between the roofspanning systems and
the supporting structures. This distinction is often marked not
only byachange ofform butalso by one ofmaterials: stone walls
and a thatched timber-framed roof, for example. Even where a
‘stone roof? is used, this is customarily on a timber frame and
differs from the wall construction. The example illustrates the
essential difference between the roof structure and the supports,
for the roof depends on the distribution of gravitational forces
to the supporting system which has to cope both with its own
load and that ofthe roof.

All loads in a building are transmitted to the ground and, in
theory, to the centre of the earth; the structural problem is to
transfer the vertical loads exerted on theroofhorizontally to the
walls and vertically to the ground. The resultant of the vertical
force atthe centre orridge oftheroofis transferred to the walls,
but the direction ofthe force continues as a thrust; it is part of
the purpose ofthe wall to resist this thrust. When this is failing,
bulging, lurching or buckling may occur, which can be countered by piers or buttresses.

Most load-bearing walls are subject principally to compression and a well-built wall that is thick in relation to its heightis
likely to be sufficient to counter both the vertical loads and the
lateral loads exerted upon it, as is the case in grain stores or
malt-houses. For this reason some walls may beas muchasım
(3 fü) thick; as such they may be technically ‘over built’, having
more mass than is necessary to cope with customary forces.

An A-frame

(See also Fig. ulIS)

system of pillars

nised tie beams

Nevertheless, the survival of vernacular buildings over many
centuries, sometimes in spite of extreme natural hazards, is
often due to the intuitive precautions taken by their builders not
to ‘take chances’.

Additional to the weight ofthe roof-frame is that ofthe materials used for cladding, such as thatch. Butthere are otherloads
which a building has to withstand and which can be predicted
only with uncertainty. These range from the ‘live loads’ of the
dynamic forces of hurricanes, to the ‘dead loads’ of snow,
which can become live as they slip: the A-frames of Revelstoke
are builtto deflect the accumulation on the building ofsome of
the heaviest snowfall rates in the world. On rare occasions
there are ‘impact’ loads generated, for example, by a runaway
team ofanimals orbyarock fall.

Mass walls differ with regard to the respective inherent
weights ofthe materials of which they are constructed, and in
which there may be a considerable range, as any comparison
between tufaand granite blocks ofequal dimensions, or ofbalsa
and teak will rapidly demonstrate. Similarly, the methods of
bonding the materials by the use of mud or mortar may also
affect the structural strength ofthe wall, which may not necessarily be greater than that of a skilfully built ‘dry’ stone wall
without a bonding agent. Where walls are subject to excessive
loads buttresses may be used to stabilize the structure. An alternative method has been to place an additional load on the wall
above therroof-frame, such asa parapet, thus increasing therrelative vertical forces to counter the thrust. The widely dispersed
systems of block-bau horizontal timber construction are found
in Siberia, eastern and central Europe, the Balkans, Alpine and
Nordic areas, and in North America but not in the British Isles.
Whether oftrimmed or round section timber, they counter the
rolling action which is inherent in the use of logs by joints
designed to interlock the components where they intersect at
the corners.

Notall enclosing constructions act as supports except insofar as they support themselves. Some in fact, require independent support: ‘curtain’ walls, for instance, made of palm leaf
thatch over liana nets require a frame of posts that will provide
fixings for the lianas; they are not self-supporting. Nor are the
many buildings of Oceania which have walls or screens of
woven mats, or which are simply open-sided. These buildings
are frequently dependent on the ‘post-and-beam’ (or ‘post-andlinteP) system, or the use ofhorizontal members which take and
distribute the loads ofthe roofby the flow offorces to the ground
through the supporting posts. These posts may be ‘earthfast’ or
partially buried, which focuses the ‘point-load’, the concentration ofthe weightexerted on each post below the ground surface.

The loads may be distributed above ground by “footings’.
Earthfast posts act in principle as vertical cantilevers, being
subjectto shearatthe point where the postis exposed above the
earth, butbenefiting from the lateral forces below ground which
prevent slippage. Barthfast posts are subject to rotting and
posts thatstand upon a sill may be more protected, whether the
sill is a timber baulk or built ofstone.

Shear may be counteracted by the bulk ofthe vertical timbers
alone, but customarily corner-posts are connected by intermediate supporting posts and beams to construct a timber frame.

Supports and Floors 1.1.6

Aulanbneo aueısor @&

This may be further reinforced by the use of angled braces
which again exploit the triangulation principle. Remarkable
regional traditions ofsuch framing are to be found in Germanic
and Gallic regions, and in Britain.

Much thoughtand print over the centuries has been expended
on the origins ofmankind’s buildings and the generative structural systems from which others may have evolved. The theme
of the ‘primitive hut’ obsessed writers in the Renaissance and
has persisted to recent times. Part ofthe fascination ofthe subject is the clear evidence on classical buildings in Greece ofthe
forms and details ofa previous timber technology on its marble
temples. Bases, columns, capitals and architraves may have
timber precedents but they have their own rules, governed by
the different behaviour ofthe material: the great compression
strength ofthe marble or stone buttheir limitations in tension.
All post-and-lintel or framing systems offer greater freedom in
the location of openings, permitting the curtain wall to be
punctured ata point and at a scale which may weaken a mass
wall-a practical reason for the larger windows often found in
timber-framed, or post-and-beam buildings compared with
the small ones encountered in mass walling.

As discussed in the Openings category in this section (1.V.4-A),
doorways and windows can weaken wall structures and opening
doors can exertlive loads. Yetaccess, lightand ventilation are necessary for human occupancy, as are the horizontal surfaces that
are enclosed by the periphery of walls. Floors may simply be of
sand or beaten earth butthey are essential for living. With accommodation for eating and sleeping, the demands ofcirculation and

365

## S. 133

1.V PRODUCTION

cleaning, and the growing use of furniture for seating or storage,
level floors with smooth but durable surfaces become necessary.
Floors, of course, are often spanning systems inserted between
ground and roof which contribute further loads to the supporting
walls or frame, and which are subject to variable live loads in
everyday use. By far the majority of the world’s vernacular buildings are not more than two storeys in height; perhaps two-thirds
of the total are single-storeyed and with three rooms or less. A
large proportion have a single chamber only, calculated by Gluck
as averagingin Africasome 20sq m (215 sq ft) offloor space. Varying use patterns in different cultural and climatic conditions can
place considerable demands upon the flooring systems used;
some ofthe solutions are discussed below.

Enclosure by building is achieved through the combination of
supports and spans, assembled and connected by various
means appropriate to the materials, dimensions, conditions
and functions applicable in specific circumstances. The general
principles outlined here and in other categories within the Production section are subject to the resources available to every
society, and the structural system that each employs. These are
further tempered by environmental and social factors, and by
cultural influences. To illustrate this the support and spanning
systems used by a number of cultures in the South China

province of Yunnan are outlined below. PAUL OLIVER

1v6-B Structural Systems (Yunnan)
Locatedatthe southern border ofChina, the province
of Yunnan covers an area of 380.000 sq km (147 000 sq mi) and
has 34 million inhabitants. In addition to the Han, 26 other
ethnic groups are represented in the province, among which the
Yi, Bai, Naxi, Zhuang, Dai, and Hani are the most important.
Mountains cover 93 per cent oftheterritory. Theaverageannual
temperature is 15 °C (59 °F) and rain is frequent. Each ethnic
group has its specific type of dwelling but the influences have
been mixed over the centuries.

The Han ofthe Kunming region have adopted the Northern

366

Han siheyuan model, a courtyard dwelling enclosed by buildings
on four sides. This dwelling, called yikeyin, is square-shaped with
one floor. The entrance door is located to the south. The base of
the buildings is made of rough stone, and the walls of compressed clay or ofunbaked bricks. The load-bearing system is
made of wood and tiles are used for the roof. The use of lightweight materials like earth, particularly near Kunming, allows
the dwellings to withstand earthquakes.

The Han influence is evident in the Bai dwelling, found in the
west of the province, in the Dali region. It has three U-shaped
buildings (sanheyuan) which are organized around a courtyard
enclosed by a wall (zhaobi) decorated with floral painted motifs.
Facing the wall is the main house, two storeys high. The ground
floor serves as living space while the upper level is for storage.
The courtyard, which contains plants and bushes, varies in size
and is the centre offamily activity. The main entrance is located at
the northeastangle. Its door, richly ornate, forms a complex unit
similar to a house, either havinga protruding roof or being withouta roof but having rich decorations made of stone or brick.

Found in the east ofthe province in a mountainous region,
the Yi dwelling (tuzhangfang) is made entirely out of earth and
covered with a flatroof. Wooden beams supportthe flatroofing
which is made of a mixture of earth and straw. This mixture
serves as thermal insulation, keeping the room cool in summer, while retaining the heat in winter. The dwelling is made
up ofa ground-level building and another with one storey that
is adapted to the mountain’s slope, disposed around a courtyard which is sometimes covered.

Located in south Yunnan, the Dai build their homes on stilts
(ganlan) made of bamboo or wood. The frame is covered in
bamboo and straw forming a large roof which facilitates the
easy evacuation of rainwater and provides protection from the
sun. The animals (chickens, pigs and cows) are kept under the
dwelling. The upper level, with a square layout, has a large
main room centred around the fireplace, a common bedroom
andashed for drying cereals and reserves.

The Naxi live in the northwest ofthe province, in the moun-

Pa9 asıodues4 pue Sueibana Suado

tains where forests are numerous. The men live separately from
the women and children. The dwelling, the jinggan, is a composite of Han, Bai or Yi influences and is similar to the sanheyuan
model. The load-bearing structure is made of wood and the
roof and the walls are made ofa double thickness oflogs. The
interstices between the logs are blocked with earth.

Another type of Naxi dwelling adopts the shape of the Bai
dwelling with the same characteristics of the main house.
However, one of the two side buildings is used to house the
animals, and has one less floor than the other buildings. The
Hani dwelling is a composite of Yiand Han influences.

Supports
Five different structures are found in the region: tailiang, chuandou, ganjing, renzi and miliang pingting. In tailiang systems the loadbearing structure is composed of superimposed crossbeams —
wooden beams and pillars - which form the non-triangulated
frame; itis assembled with mortiseand tenon joints. The weight
ofthe structure rests on the pillars by the way ofthe beams.

The load-bearing structure of chuandou systems has a central
pillar and four or six other pillars, according to the size of the

Supports and Floors 1.V.6

house. The beams and pillars are embedded with the beams protruding. The weight rests directly on the pillars, and the beams
balance the whole structure. The disadvantage ofthis structure is
that the pillars are in the middle ofthe rooms, in contrast to the
tailiang structure whose floor space is unencumbered. Chuandou
structures are prevalentin the neighbouring province ofSichuan.

For the ganjing load-bearing structures, the walls, made of
roughly squared timber, are entirelywooden and form a bearing
envelope. This structure is often a small, one-room dwelling.
Sometimes more than one of these structures is juxtaposed to
provide more living space.

Renzi structures have a simple truss frame restingon wood or
bamboo bearing pillars. This structure takes its name from the
triangular shape ofits roofwhich evokes the Chinese character
ren, A, which means ‘man’. It is used in homes built on piles
(ganlan) in humid regions which are often flooded. The frameis
covered with bamboo and different kinds of straw, forming a
large roof which facilitates the easy run-off of rainwater and
protects against the sun.

In miliang pingting systems the load-bearing structure is
entirely made oframmed earth, has a flatroofand is associated
with the tuzhangfang dwelling; wooden beams support the flat
roofing, made of a thermally insulating mixture of earth and
straw. The flatroof can be used to dıy cereals.

The first two structures, sometimes composite, are mostly
used by the Bai, the Yiand the Naxi. The third is found in cold
mountainous regions used by the Naxi, the Lisu, the Pumi, the
Drung and the Yi. The fourth is peculiar to the Dai, in the south
ofthe province, near Burma. The fifth is frequently found in the
temperate mountainous regions in the south of the province
among the Yi, the Hani, and sometimes the Dai andthe Han.

Common characteristics existamong the five categories. The
load-bearing structure and the partitions always form two separate entities. As a preventive measure against earthquakes,
mortise and tenon joints are often used or the ends of the
beams and pillars are held in place with sheet metal, copper or
steel. Furthermore, local materials - wood, bamboo or earth —
are systematically used for their adaptability to the climate. The
walls can be made of rammed earth by pouring soil between
two wooden boards; they can also be made by using clay bricks,
cooked or not, or even with a mixture of gravel and earth, or
pebbles and earth. Lastly, the walls can be made of wood or
woven bamboo.

Two main types emerge: one based on the Han courtyard
house, the other based on the individual home. The former is
often used by the Bai, the Yiand the Hani. In each ofthe buildings there are three jian —- empty, unbuilt spaces between the
two load-bearing pillars - linked by outside walkways, and
these form the dwelling. The dwelling can take on different
shapes: the siheyuan has a courtyard enclosed by four buildings,
while the sanheyuan is a U-shaped dwelling situated around
a courtyard and enclosed by a wall on the fourth side, or an

L-shaped building. The individual home can be rectangular,
square or ovaland may or may not have a courtyard; the entire
property is enclosed with a wall. This type of dwelling is mostly
found among the Bai, the Jingpo, the De’ang, the Blang, the
Jino and some ofthe Hani, the Lahu, the Va, theLisuand theNu.

1.V PRODUCTION

Architectural style
"The Han influence among the Bai, the Yi, the Naxiand the Hani
is considerable, but the roof of the Naxi dwellings extends
beyond the facade and forms a contrastwith itbythe shadow of
the protrusion. The roof is distinguished from the rest ofthe
structure by its mild contours, in stark contrast to the massive
stone and earth base ofthe house.

The Han influence is much less among the Dai, the Jingpo,
the De’ang, the Blang and the Jino. The Dai often use bamboo

2. Chuandou 1. Taillang (or liangjia)

3. Ganjing

DFeng Yueglang and Frangolse Ged

in their homes built on piles and make no use ofthe entrance
level. TheJingpo build their entrance on theside ofthe dwelling;
the building is massive. Contrary to the Dai, the De’ang use the
ground level oftheir dwellings, which is square or rectangular,
covered by a circular or semicircular roof.

Both the Yi and the Hani build flat roofs, with tamped earth,
in the mountains, by adapting their storeys to the slope ofthe
mountain. Unfinished stone or rammed earth are the most frequently used building materials. In the northwestern mountains, where the climate is coldest, the Naxi, theLisu, the Pumi,
the Drung and the Yi have adopted the ganjinh structure, with
one ortwo storeys, usingwood from nearby forests. The Vaand
the Lahu, in the south ofthe province, near Burma, have onelevel homes, made of rammed earth or woven bamboo with
straw roofing. The building materials are used in their natural
state. FENG YUEQIANGand FRANGOISE GED

Balloon frame

Balloon-frame construction is amethod ofbuilding

with standardized sizes of milled lumber fastened together

with mass-produced nails to form an integrated network of
structural members. A distinguishing feature ofballoon-frame

buildings is the uninterrupted rise ofthe 5.08x 10.16 cm (2x4

in) vertical studs nailed to the sill at the foundation and to the

plate atthe top. Studs are doubledatthe corners ofthe building

and at door and window openings for greater strength at these

points. The studs are placed at 40.64 cm (16 in) on centres

around the perimeter ofthe building and throughout the inte-

rior walls in order to receive at regular intervals the 121.92 cm

(48 in) long lath used as support for the interior plaster walls

and ceilings. The 81.28 cm (32 in) width of door and window

openings is easily determined by doubling this 40.64 cm (16 in)

module in the spacing ofthe studs. In order to provide a span

for floors, carpenters nail5.8x 20.32 cm (2x8 in) or 5.8%25.4
cm (2 x 10 in) horizontal joists to the studs along the exterior
side walls. First-storey joists are positioned on the sill; second-

storeyjoists are supported by a horizontal ledger or ribbon that
is letin or nailed to the vertical studs. Diagonal bridging nailed
between each joist stiffens the members that receive a subfloor
layer ofrough boards 0f2.54x 20.32 cm (1x 8in) surfaced with
2.54% 15.24. cm (1x 6 in) clear pine boards or narrow strips of
hardwood as the flooring. The exterior ofthe frame is enclosed
with a layer of sheathing boards that is covered with shiplap
siding. Rafters of5.8x 15.24 cm (2x 6 in) are nailed to the plate
at 60.96 cm (24 in) intervals, then covered with roofboards and
surfaced with shingles made ofwood or asphalt. Builders cut
rafters at angles of 30° to 90° to determine the height and the
slope ofthe roof. The completed frame is like a basket in which
all parts ofthe structure are tightly knit or fastened together to
form a secure container ofinterior space.

Basic shapes ofballoon-frame structures are based upon the
rectangle, square and triangle. The planar wall and roof surfaces enclose a rectangular space covered by a saddle roof or a
square space spanned by a pyramidal roof. Customary scale of
balloon-frame structures varies from one to two-and-a-half
storey elevations and from simple one- or two-room structures
to elaborate asymmetrical extensions of enclosed spaces.

1.V.6.a

Although relatively few housetypes and commercial building
types result from the inherent geometric order ofthe building
system, vernacular builders realize an amazing variety ofdesigns
in actual structures. Differences in elevation and extension of
the primary section ofthe structure in relation to the scale and
proportion of wings, the placement of doors and windows on
facades, the addition of porches, roof dormers, bay windows
and surface decoration are means used to individualize
designs. Balloon-frame commercial structures that fit narrow
business lots can rise from one to three storeys and are shaped
as extended rectangles spanned by a saddle roof. This sloping
roof line is usually concealed by a false-front street facade that
rises to the full height ofthe structure and crowned with a horizontal cornice the full width ofthe facade to give the impression ofa building constructed according to traditional classical
design.

Generations ofbuilders in America, seekingto conserve time
and labour in constructing heavy wooden-framed structures
joined by mortise and tenon, led to the creation ofthis economical and efficient method of building. Balloon-frame construction originated during the ı830s in newly settled, rapidly
developing areas on the American frontier. Sawmills cut abundant sources of native pine. Industrial machine processes
produced inexpensive nails. Railroad lines delivered these
materials near building sites. Experienced carpenters and practical vernacular builders used the dimensions ofmass-produced
milled lumber and machine-cut nails gradually to establish
balloon-frame construction as the most popular and widely
used building method in ıgth- and 2oth-century America. Less
labour, materials, and money were needed to build in this
efficient way with wood. Further, the adaptability of balloonframe construction meant that specifications for buildings
could be suited to the comfort and convenience of the individuals who planned and built them. The system of construction
was sufficiently simple to allow virtually everyone the opportu-

nity to bea designer and builder. FRED W. PETERSON

Supports and Floors 1.1.6
Braced frame

Carpenters built braced-frame structures during a
period oftransition from traditional mortise and tenon frames
to alightweight, all-nail balloon-frame construction during the
second half of the ıgth century in America. A braced-frame
structure is similar to a balloon-frame building in the way the
milled lumber components are placed and spaced, but bracedframe members are heavier than those ofa balloon frame. Corner-posts ofa braced frame usually measure 15.24 x 15.24 cm
(6x 6 in) and studs are 5.08 x 15.24 cm (2x 6 in) instead ofthe
10.16 x 10.16 cm (4x 4 in) posts and 5.08 x 10.16 cm (2x4 in)
studs of balloon-frame construction. Studs, corner-posts and
sometimes joists are mortised as well as nailed into thessill. The
frame is further strengthened by diagonal 5.08 x 15.24 cm
(2x 6 in) braces let in and nailed to the posts and studs at the
corners of the structure on both first and second storeys. This
method of framing seemed to satisfy conservative builders’
preferences for an apparent strength of a heavy frame while
taking advantage of some ofthe economies and efficiencies of
building with milled lumber and nails.

1.V.6.8-i

FRED W.PETERSON

Platform frame

Platform-frame construction is virtually identical to
balloon-frame construction except for one significant feature.
Instead of the uninterrupted rise of vertical studs from sill to
plate of balloon-frame construction, studs in platform-frame
construction rise to the height of only one storey. After the
carpenter has nailed a plate to the top ofthe studs and fastened
joists to the studs and plate, he nails subfloor boards to the top
of the joists, creating the platform on which he constructs the
second storey in the same manner as the first. Platform-frame
buildings originated during the late-ıgth century in the United
States as an economical and efficient building method; they
used shorter studs that were convenient to handle in construction and easy to package for shipment on railroad cars as parts
ofpre-cut, ready-made, mass-produced frame houses designed
and supplied by companies such as Sears, Roebuck and Company ofChicago, or Aladdin Homes of Bay City, Michigan.

1.V.6.2-ii

FRED W. PETERSON
1.V.6.b Buttress
A buttress is vertical mass of stone masonry generally built to project from a wall to support the side and give
additional strength and support. In the case of a massive wall
the buttress may be no more than an appropriate thickening.
The buttress is usually at right angles to the general alignment
and is acommon device to resist the thrust ofvaulting.

Buttresses are often subdivided into stages or steps. The
steps can be staged with reduction in width and projection,
with each stage being marked by set-offs.

There are wide (Norman and Saxon) buttresses, and internal
and external buttresses, the latter with a major function to
brace the thick walls of Norman architecture. In formal building buttresses are often elaborately decorated, with some ornamental elements serving to act as counterweights. A corner
buttress is an example of an internal buttress and does not

369

## S. 135

——n

1V PRODUCTION

© Asher Shadmon

essentially project from the wall, being nestled at the intersection. It may be constructed by using vertical, restraining stone
slabs with double corbels to contain a mortared or even a dry
wall structure.

Angle buttresses are external and meet perpendicularlyatthe
corner of the building. They are the most common type for
bracing constructions which require additional support after
the building has been finished. The large square buttress encasing the corner is known as a ‘clasping buttress’. The setback
buttress allows the corner or angle ofthe building to be freely
expressed. The diagonal buttress meets the corner at a 135°
angle. Capping with a plain slope or a triangular head or pediment when atthe angle ofa building is common.

Apier can beusedto receive the lateral thrustand is known as
a "buttress pier. The outward thrust of a vaulted roof can be
countered by heavy pinnacles. A freestanding vertical rib, supported from a wall by a corbel rather than by its own foundation, is known as a ‘hanging buttress’.

Buttresses may be shaped as scrolls or constructed as aqueducts to channel offthe water. Those in the form ofreduced detailsareadeparture from the traditional shape forms, forexample
selfbuttressed, inclined columns. ASHER SHADMON

column
Normally circular on plan, often conforming to the
classical orders, a column is a vertical pillar which may be
either freestanding or supporting a lintel. When it is eircular,
polygonal (canted) or similar, the individual units forming the
shafts are called ‘drums’ and when rectangular, ‘blocks’.
Columns usually stand on a base.

A row of columns forms a colonnade, with the function to
provide shade and shelter. Formed by supporting a roof and
parapet with stone columns, they are usually mortared and of
square section.

A small column with the shaft in one piece, usually not more
than 2-3 m (6.5-10 ft) in height, is a ‘monolith’, and is preferred where possible, as it requires fewer adjustments when
fixing the drums, is less vulnerable to weathering and needs no

1.V1.6.C

379

repointing. Supporting columns with a downtaper are the
exception as is the use ofsculptured figures as columns. When
the surface is worked into a regular series of concave grooves
the column is said to be “fluted’, and a swelling in the shaft is
known as ‘entasis’. As confidence in the strength of stone
increased, freestanding piers replaced half-columns attached
to engaged piers, pilasters and walls. ASHER SHADMON
1.V.6.d Floors

A floor is defined as the lower surface of a space

used for human or animal activity or storage. For the sake of
convenience it is normally horizontal but on some occasions a

floor may be slightly dished. The functions ofa floor comprise

support for human or animal activity or storage, distribution of
loads and insulation from cold and rising damp. It may rest

directly on the ground or it may be supported above a space or

room, in which case it is referred to as a ‘suspended floor’.

The formation and construction ofa ground floor depends
on whether the structure is occupied on a permanent or temporary basis. Iftemporary, a floor may be obtained by clearance of
the ground and the laying of mats or carpets as in the case ofthe
tent ofthe Bedu. In contrast, the Inuit sleep inside the iglu on
furs laid over a platform ofcompacted snow (iglig). Ifthe structure is intended to be permanent the ground-floor construction
can vary a great deal, depending on situation, ground conditions and climate. Acommon method where the soil is suitable
— tropical laterites are especially so - is to remove the surface
layer and replace this with selected soil which may be stabilized
with lime or cement. Itis then compacted with hand tools and
sometimes hardened with ash, small stones, gravel or seashells. Often this is plastered with animal dung and fine clay
which is carefully polished to form asmooth, durable surface. In
northern Ghanaaresin from local plants is mixed with the clay to
provide resistance against termite attack and prevent cracking.

In areas where there is high rainfall and/or wet ground conditions or if the floor is at basement level, rising damp may
occur in which case a waterproof membrane is laid under the
floor finish. Often asphalt or bitumen is used for this purpose.

Buttresses su,

pı
wallsofa mon; da}
Valley ofthe er
Jerusalem,

jorthern Ghana.

Damp can easily cause timber to rot and for this reason timber
floors are often suspended and raised above ground level to create a ventilated space between the floor and the ground.

The loads received by a floor are considered to be ‘live’ in that
they are applied to the floor and can vary with the activity and
usage taking place. A ‘dead’ load, on the other hand, is the
weight of the construction itself and is therefore constant. A
suspended floor must cope with both ‘live’ and ‘dead’ loads.

A suspended floor normally combines a finish that is both

durable and pleasing, with a stable supporting structure. The
materials and type of structure used depend on the ‘live’ load,
span, degree of acoustic insulation and fireproofing required
and availability of materials. The construction of a suspended
floor mustresist the tendency to bend which creates stresses of
tension in the lower section and compression in the upper.
A ground floor, on the other hand, because it has consistent
support must resist predominantly compressive stresses. The
most common material to be used for a suspended structure is
timber in the form ofregularly spaced beams usually spanning
the shortest distances between the supporting walls or columns.
The timber beams are sometimes used in their natural state in
circular section with the bark removed or sawn to arectangular
section with the longest dimension placed vertically in order to
gain maximum resistance to bending. The spaces between the
beams must be spanned to form a continuous surface above. At
Suakin in Sudan on the Red Sea, circular-section timber beams
were supported on the walls at 30-40 cm (12-16 in) and thin
canes or sticks were laid diagonally across the beams in one
layer, while another layer was placed over itata different angle
to form a diamond pattern. Palm matting was then applied over
the whole surface and a ıs cm (6 in) layer of cement and sand
was added to receive the final finish of mats or carpets. Sometimes in other parts of the world where a similar type of suspended construction is found a layer of compacted soil is used
to receive stone flags or large ceramic tiles which produces a
heavy ‘dead’ load and thereby good acoustic insulation.

In hothumid climates where there are ample supplies oftimber and bamboo itis common for houses to be builtas a timber
or bamboo framework with the floor beams tied in to brace the
total structure.

The qualities of the visible surface of floor construction are
related to aesthetics, thermal properties, durability and ease of
cleaning, especially where food is prepared. Animal accommodation is normally provided at ground-floor level and the
surface is either compacted soil or stone flags. Elsewhere,
colour, pattern and texture are used to give visual delight,
whereas thermal and tactile properties combine to give the
effect ofcold or warmth. The latter is importantin communities
where raised furniture is rare and the floor is used for sittingand
sleeping. Resistance to wear is often improved by the application of wax polish or varnish to timber flooring, whereas
ceramic tiles and hardwood blocks are frequently used for both
their appearance and hardwearing properties. Fire-resistant
materials like stone, brick and concrete are used to form protective inserts surrounding the hearth and stoves in both ground
and suspended floors where timber is the main material.

MILES DANBY

Supports and Floors 1.1.6

1.V.6.d-i Floor: laid - compacted earth
Specially selected earth is used to make floors at
ground level and, in some cases, suspended floor construction.
It is selected for durability, strength, density and capability of
compaction. In most parts oftheworldwhere compacted earth is
used there is traditional knowledge of how to make the selection
and where to find a suitable soil. Lateritic soils found in tropical

areas are very suitable and commonly used for ground floors.

The procedure is that the ground surface is first cleared of

topsoil and then a layer ofselected earth is spread over the floor
area between the walls and tamped with wooden rams, flails or
flat stones. This may be repeated until the necessary thickness
and stability has been achieved. The surface is sometimes hardened with the addition ofsmall stones, ash, sea-shells, or hard
shells of nuts. The earth is sometimes stabilized by mixing it
withasmallamountoflime, cementor bitumen. The final finish
is usually a mixture ofrefined soil mixed with animal dung or
resin obtained from boiling local plants or chopped plant fibres
or straw. This finish is applied like a plaster and in northern
Ghana the women of the Nankansi people sprinkle an extract
made from the pods or bark ofthe locust bean tree (dawadawa)
over the floor daily for a period oftwo weeks. This further hardens the surface and imparts a pleasantred colour.

When a well-ventilated covered area is needed for cooking or
food preparation in a tropical rural community, the floor slab is
raised above the general ground level byretainingthe compacted
earth within timber formwork fixed outside or inside the vertical

Aauea salın a

## S. 136

en

1V PRODUCTION

posts which supporttheroof. The surface is hardenedand coated
as described before and the formwork replaced with stones.
Compacted earth is sometimes used in the construction of
suspended floors when a layer forms the base for a surface of
burnt clay tiles or stone flags. It is usually supported on small
sticks or canes spanning across timber beams which are inturn
supported on thewalls. The use of‘ compacted earth in vernacular
buildings has tended, particularly in urban areas, to be superseded by concrete for at least halfa century but, because ofthe
increasing cost of energy needed for the production of cement,
the advantages of earth construction are being reassessed,
especially in those countries which must import oil or coal for
hard currency. MILES DANBY
Floor: laid - concrete slab
Arelatively modern method of ground-floor construction is to spread a layer ofhardcore composed of] broken bricks,
sand or stones after the topsoil has been removed. A slab ofconcrete is then poured over the hardcore and steel reinforcement
is added ifconsidered necessary to prevent cracking which could
be caused by later subsidence. The concrete is smoothed with a
trowel before it sets if no further finish is needed. Alternatively,
ascreed ofcementand sand may be spread and levelled over the
rough concrete slab to receive a finish of stone slabs, brick,
ceramictilesormosaics, terrazzo in tile form or in situ, hardwood
strips or blocks, mats and carpets, linoleum or plastic tiles or
other material considered to be durable and attractive. These finishes, especially stone flags, may also be applied to a subfloor of
compacted soil. In some parts ofSpain ground floors are finished with elaborate patterns ofriver pebbles setin cement mortar.
Reinforced concrete is now frequently used for suspended
Aoors in frame construction with slabs, beams and columns
made ofthe same material. An alternative is an in situ slab spanning from wall to wall which may sometimes incorporate beams,
in which case the slab spans between the beams. In most vernacular situations the concrete is poured into formwork usually
made from timber which is used repeatedly for the same purpose. In some Mediterranean countries preformed floor elements are widely available. A typical system embodies the use of
precast, pre-stressed beams with precast hollow pots supported on the lower web ofthe I-section beams. Stone, however, is
weak in tension and when used to supporta suspended floor is
either in the form ofa dome, where every piece is in compression, orin thick slabs with ashort span between beams. Acomposite structure occasionally found is a continuous short-span

brickarch resting on the lower flange of steel beams.
MILES DANBY

1.V.6.d-ii

1.v.6.d-iii Floor: laid - mud (Tswana)

Amongthe Tswana the floor, like the house, is made
of mud and is raised about to cm (4 in) above the ground level
to prevent rainwater from flooding indoors. The mud layer is
topped bya thin layer ofplaster which is smoothed andcoloured
black with a piece ofslate. The floor maybe regularly covered by
a thin coating of cow dung to give ita hard protecting surface.
Byspreadinga mixture ofcow dung and water using the side or
fingers ofthe hand a pattern is created on the floor. Once dry,
the floor is swept to remove loose particles ofthe cow dung.

372

e

Theinnercourtyard (lolwapa), amain characteristicofTswana
dwellings, also has a mud floor raised above the surrounding
ground. A coatingofcow dung is regularly puton the mud floor
to harden the surface and a pattern is drawn with the side ofthe
hand or the fingers. Like all other work in mud, floors are built
and maintained by thewomen in the household.

ANITA LARSSON

1.V.6.d-iv Floor: laid - tiled

Tiles applied over a supporting structure are used to
produce an attractive and hardwearing floor finish. The tiles jare
usually square or rectangular in shape and made from a variety
of materials. The most common is burnt clay which can be
unrefined in the case of quarty tiles or refined when the tiles
may be coated with a glaze before being fired. The colour of
unglazed burnt clay tiles, usually known as terracotta, depends
on the colour ofthe original clay, either red or ochre. Clay tiles
are formed in trays or moulds and another variation that can be
produced is the inlaid or encaustic tile whereby a design is
stamped into the clay before firing and the indentations so
obtained are filled with white liquid clay. The availability of
suitable clays as well as the fuel for firing the kilns limits the
areas where clay tiles are found.

An infinite range of sophisticated designs can be made with
the addition of transparent and coloured glazes to the terracotta, which is then often known as “faience’. The nature ofthe
finish depends on the material (e.g. silica, soda ash and metallic oxides) used to produce the glaze. The metallic oxides
produce an extensive colour range. Coloured designs can also
be printed on to the body ofthe tile before glazing and firing.
These techniques date from Egyptian and Assyrian times and

Patterned fiogrg; 3
inner Courtyard
