e
&

spread from India and the Middle East to Europe, Asia and the
Americas. Geometric and floral patterns were favoured in communities where human and animal images were discouraged.
In Islamic countries, in particular, geometric patterning was
taken to a high degree of complexity, often with the use of
hexagonal, octagonal and triangular shapes in addition to
square and rectangular tiles.

Tiles are also made from cut stone usually from rocks thatare
found naturally in alaminated form, such as slate and quartzite.
Some marbles, however, are cut and polished for use as floor
tiles. Other materials used include cement and sand often with
coloured pigments, terrazzo (a mixture of crushed marble
aggregate and coloured cement), cork, linoleum and other
manmade compositions.

All tiles need to be fixed firmly in position and this is normally achieved by setting them in a bed of mortar or similar
fixative. The joints between the tiles can be practically invisible
ifthe tiles are of consistent dimensions but some tiles can vary
in size, particularly if made from unrefined clay which may
distort under firing. A wider joint is necessary to take up the
discrepancy and this becomes visually obvious and part ofthe
original design. Grog, thatis claythathas been firedand ground,
is sometimes added to the original clay to avoid undue distortion.

Tiles are often used because of their resistance to wear and
ease of cleaning but unglazed clay, cork and manmade materials need to be sealed and should receive regular applications of
wax polish to maintain the surface. MILES DANBY
1.V.6.e Floor: suspended - planked
Timber, because of its resilience, pleasing appearance, qualities of insulation and availability is widely used for
flooring in the form of planks and boards. A plank is normally
defined as a length of sawn timber whose widest sectional
dimension is at least 23 cm (9 in). The shortest dimension is a
maximum of5 cm (2 in). A board is a similar length oftimber
but with smaller cross-sectional dimensions.

Both planks and boards span across supporting beams which
are also usually oftimber. The dimensions ofthe beams orjoists

Supports and Floors 1.6

are determined by the span. Traditionally, the depth would be
one twenty-fourth ofthe span plus 5 cm (2 in), with aminimum
width of5 cm (2 in). To avoid bending, a spacing 0f 35.5-40.5
cm (14-16 in) is normally maintained between the beams or
other supports anda minimum depth ofıg mm (0.75 in) forthe
planks or boards, which are fixed to the beams bywooden pegs,
nails, brads, or metal screws. At the side they are often butt
jointed but because timber, especially ifithas notbeen properly
seasoned, shrinks if the humidity of the air varies small gaps
appear between them. In agricultural buildings this does not
matter but in a domestic or other situations it can result in loss
of acoustic privacy and the falling of dust and small objects
through the gap. The gap can be plugged with hemp or similar
fibres. The shrinkage can be accommodated with the use of
splayed, rebated or tongued and grooved joints. The latter system ofjointing can be shaped to allow for secret nailing.

There are innumerable species oftimber, both softwood and
hardwood. Most hardwoods are more difficult to work and are
therefore less commonly used and usually with smaller dimensions than softwood. The texture of wooden floors can vary
from rough unpolished planks to highly polished hardwood
finished with wax or clear varnish. Plain softwood without an
attractive grain is sometimes painted but more often covered
with carpet or mats. A decorative effect may also be obtained
with the use ofparquet or wood blocks.

If timber flooring is used at ground level it is either laid on
battens or fillets in a cement and sand screed applied to a conerete slab or on timber beams fixed to a timber plate supported
on brick or concrete foundation walls. As timber is susceptible
to wetand dıy rot, awaterproofmembrane ofbitumen orasphalt
is often applied to the concrete. In the case ofthe suspended floor
the space between the ground surface and the underside ofthe
floor is ventilated. The base of the vertical surface ofthe wall
where itjoins a timber floor is normally protected from damage
while cleaning by a timber skirting. As timber is combustible,
hearths and stoves are placed on inserts ofstone, brick or concrete, MILES DANBY
1.V.6.e-1 Floor: suspended - slatted
A slat is a strip of timber or bamboo which is narrower and smaller in section than a board. Slats are used to
form a floor surface either by being butted together or, more
usually, with tongued and grooved joints or with the spaces
between them being close enough to produce a stable and comfortable support for the human foot. They span between supporting beams which may be sawn timber, bamboo ortimberin
a natural state with a circular section. In the humid tropics
where there are ample supplies of a variety of species of bamboos and hardwoods, suspended and spaced slatted floors are
commonly used to help through-ventilation and to allow food
scraps and other rubbish to fall into the space below for the
domestic animals. Slatted floors of this kind are normally
found in single-storey framed structures oftimber and/or bamboo lashed together with split bamboo or natural fibres. There
is an ancientand sophisticated tradition ofbuilding with bamboo in South Asia where slatted foors are made with bamboo
strips or boards fixed by thongs ofvine or wire.

373

## S. 137

1.V PRODUCTION

When used in a suspended floor the slats mustbe sufficientin
depth to prevent bending and therefore the depth is related to
the spacing ofthe beams. This is notsso vital ifthe slats are supported on a cement and sand screed and fixed to timber fillets
embedded in the screed resting on a concrete slab; in this case
the depth relates only to the need to maintain stability and preserve space for the jointing.

Short hardwood slats, usually known as ‘blocks’, are assembled in a wide variety of decorative patterns, including herringbone, basket weave or brick bond. This type of floor, widely
known as ‘parquet’, requiring a high standard of craftsmanship and well-seasoned timber, is relatively costly. Because of
the risk of rot or other damage from rising damp in groundfloor or basement situations it is laid over a damp-proof membrane ofbitumen or similar material.

Slatted floors in a variety of forms are to be found in basic but
ecologically sophisticated rural communities and materially
richer urban districts. MILES DANBY
1.1.6.f Footing
The footing is the lowest part ofa wall’s foundation
which can be rock, hardpan, sand and gravel, clay, loam and
other soils, the softer earth materials often being called ‘subsoils’. The object ofthe footing is to distribute the weight of.a
building or other construction equally over the substratum and
to prevent unequal settlement. The bases of structures are invariably made wider than the superincumbent mass to increase
the stability by distributing the load over an area sufficiently
large to safely withstand the pressure and to counteract the
damaging forces thattend to cause building failure or collapse.

There are three basic types of foundation. Those in trenches
30-80 cm (12-31 in) deep and wider than the planned wall are
sufficient where the unit load is less than the practical resistance ofthe soil. Foundations with a wide trench enable the use
of footings to spread the load over a greater area, and thirdly,
foundations in trenches where the soil is problematic require
the use ofsteps or tiers.

With the exception of solid rock and gravel, nearly all soils
are compressible under pressure and itis notalways possible to
avoid settlement. This is no problem provided that the settlement is uniform and of no great depth, but, where the resistance of the soil is not uniform throughout, there is a risk of
irregular settlement. The weight of the walls and columns
includes the ‘dead’ loads that are transmitted from the floor
and roof of the building. In addition ‘live’ loads, such as the
movement of occupants and wind or snow loads are transferred to the footings ofa structure.

No structure is better than the quality of its substructure.
This consists ofthe subsoil footing and the foundation laid for
the building. The selection and the depth ofthe seating for the
foundations depends partly on the presence of a resistant soil
and the need to protect the seating from harmful climatic
effects due to variations in seasonal humidity.

Squared masonry when used in footings is usually plain and
consists oflarge flat stones that are at least 30 cm (12 in) wider
than the wall. The mason must ensure that the thickness ofthe
stones is sufficient to prevent the projecting part from breaking

374

„Which the first stone courses are laid.

off and the stone is carefully bedded or stacked, in the case of
rounded stones or boulders. A bed of mortar is sometimes
placed to ensure an even bearing on the substrata or soil.

When stone constructions are built on footings which do not
always reach the rock, the foundations are wider than the walls
which they support. On stony ground the building is usually
constructed on or against the rock after it has been made certain that the rock is sound.

When the ground is sound and the rock uniform, the lowest
stone course of large cut blocks, perhaps ı m (3 ft) long and
30 cm (12 in) thick, can act as an initial foundation. However,
since stone building is most economical from locally quarried
stone, the mason usually takes advantage ofthe sizes available.

Today, foundations are often constructed by a trench being
dug in the shape ofthe house and tamped down, then concrete
and any stone material available in the area being thrown or
poured in. Strength can be increased by selecting and stacking
the stone with a minimum of concrete or mortar and applying
the principles of dry-wall building. For a compacted base of
clayey soil or clay, with or without stone, a footing course may
be sufficient for a one- or two-storey building. For sandy, marl
and other light soils, a concrete belt of about 20 cm (8 in) is
sometimes used, again wider than the wall which is to be built
on it.

Stone courses below ground level require as much attention
as visible courses, since the substructure is justas important as
the rest ofthe construction. Indeed, it determines the level of
the building and carries more weight than any other course.

To complete the foundation the top ofthe foundation wall is
levelled with long stone slabs about 30 cm (12 in) thick. Assuch

slabs are not always available, or may be complicated to square
with basic tools, a continuous concrete slab may be cast, on to
ASHER SHADMON
1.V.6.f-1 Failure of footings
The principal causes of failure of footings are those
which induce settlement, such as inequalities of earth resistance, lateral escape of soft soil, sliding of the substratum on
sloping ground, shrinking due to the withdrawal of water,
atmospheric action, distributive lateral pressures causing overturn (e.g. wind pressure, the thrust of barrel vaulting or an
untied couple raftered roof) and, lastly, concentrated lateral
pressure which induces settlementand overturn (e.g. thethrust
of framed floors, trussed roofs and groined vaults, subjecting
small areas of support to great pressures). Inequality of settlement in foundations has two causes: the unequal resistance of
the soiland the unequal loading ofthe substratum. Special precautions are usually taken in both cases either by distributing
the pressure over a sufficient bearing area or by piling.

A damp-proof course is always desirable, but not always
used. The most common, traditional damp-proof courses are
made of two or three layers ofthin slate where this material is
available, and are widely used in Nepal, Bhutan and the Indian
subcontinent.

Protection against running water is important, too, and drainage channels around the house, filled with pebbles or gravel,
are usually effective. In some parts of Africa, where splashing

Reference
Janssen, I.J.A, 1985

1.V.1.b Excavating

2
°

rains may cause trenches to cave in, flat stones or boulders are
required to protect against the erosion ofthe foundations.

Precautionary measures against earthquakes, for example in
Anatolia and Kashmir, are not only confined to the foundations,
although these are an important part of the total structure.
Basic precautions in earthquake zones require thatfoundations
should preferably be continuous on a flat surface.

In mountainous regions, such as Serbia or the Alps, excavation of the slope at the back of the house to form a terrace is
preferred to building on infill (however well supported or contained), for when the slope is steep, sliding on the foundations
may take place. ASHERSHADMON
1.1.6.8 Forked post: paufourche
The load-bearing forked post is a type of combined

support and joint which, originating with man’s earliest constructional attempts, has been part of his technological heritage ever since. Employed as early as neolithic times both in
dwellings and dependencies, construction with forked posts —
or pauxfourches in the words of 15th-century building contracts
in the French Sologne region - is still extant today in Africa,
South America and Asia. In Europe itself the technique has
been relegated, since the late Middle Ages, to the building of
small agricultural and pastoral dependencies, with a stigma of
inferior technology attached to it. Its survival throughout the
ıgth and 2oth centuries can be ascribed to its being simple and
cheap to implement.

Pauxfourches construction falls within the realm of construction with bearer posts, ofwhich itis only one particular aspect,
marked by the use of natural or artificial forks as joints. The
natural fork or crutch ofa paufourche may result from either the
top ofa tree-trunk dividing into two equal prongs (bifurcation),
or a smaller bough branching off the trunk (ramification). A
substitute for the natural fork is obtained by nailing two small
wooden planks or bolting down two flat iron bars on both sides
ofthe top part ofa post.

Pauxfourches structures can be divided into two main families
according to the plan marked out on the ground by forked sup-

Supports and Floors 1.V6

ports: rectilinear structures (e.g. single row, square plan, rectangular plan); and non-rectilinear structures (e.g. circular
plan, elliptical plan).

The basic component of rectilinear structures is the single
row of forked uprights bearing a horizontal member. In this
configuration the plane of each fork is Perpendicular to the
dirertion ofthe row of uprights. A row of only two pauxfourches
makes upa ‘gantry’.

The single row of pauxfourches serves as a basic component
for structures with a saddle-backed roof, consisting ofone tall
centre row between two lower side rows. In this configuration
the upper ends ofthe roof rafters test on a ridge purlin while
their lower ends rest on base Purlins or plates. Seyeral paux-

ourches placed on the same segment ofa straight line (but with

the planes oftheir forks running parallel to it) make upa bay.
The single row of pauxfourches also serves as a basic component
for the frame-bearing structure consisting oftwo parallel rows
of equal height. In this configuration the bearer uprights in
both rows carıy crossbeams which form a horizontal frame by
combining with joists running from one crosspiece to the other
straightabove a bay.

If several joists are laid across the horizontal frame in the
interval between two successive bays, then this structure
becomes a platform-bearing structure.

Non-rectilinear structures consist ofbearer uprights planted
on the boundary of are circular or a near-circular area. The
planes of the forks either converge towards the centre of the
resulting circumference or areata tangent to the latter. Within
either ofthese subtypes, two structures can be made out: those
bearinga conical roof ofrafters (i.e. acone) and those bearing
aflatroofofjoists (i.e. a frame or a platform).

While rectilinear structures are still to be found in 2o0thcentury Europe as small agricultural or pastoral buildings (e.g.
charcoal-burner’s hut, shed for splitting logs, sheep shelter,
shed for milking ewes, vine-arbour, cart shed, shelter for agri-

cultural machinery), circular structures have become quite
Scarce, in contrast to Africa where the full range ofthem exists.

Various uses are made ofthe forked post in fields other than
architecture, as in a gantıy carıying.a pulley over a water well or
forming part ofa field fence, orasingle paufourche being used as
a prop or acting as the fulcrum for a water-raising sweep.

CHRISTIAN LASSURE

1V.6.h Jalous (Sudan, central)
Traditional earthen building in Sudan is called Ljalous,
after the moist soil used directly in mass wall construction. The
common dwelling comprises freestanding rooms, and has a
square plan with a flat roof (agoba or muraba’a). A eircular plan
building (khuf) is sometimes found.

Houses are organized informally as cellular spaces, perhaps
connected byaveranda, within ahigh courtyard (hosh) wall providing secure, private, open living space. The baladi roofis constructed of a main timber beam (mirig), smaller pole rafters
(shagig), bearers and crossbearers (javeed), matting (birish),
straw (nal), a layer of earth, and mud, straw and dung render
(zibala). Material properties, wall mass and roof insulation,
plus night reradiation and ventilation, mitigate solar exposure

375

## S. 138

1V PRODUCTION

[ E 2
and high temperature ranges. The great vulnerability is to water
damage. j

Such shelter is mainly built by direct artisanal labour, with

limited self-help or co-operative (nafeer) inputs. In typical con:
struction the owner acts as general contractor, arranging delivery of the materials, contracting a builder (osta) with three
assistants for walls and roof, and separately hiring a zenderer wo
finish the building. Water for construction is the responsibility
ofsubcontractors.

"The soil is mixed as is and used immediately, requiring a day
to set. Ideally, the builder alternates days between two or Mate
projects. Damp jalous masses are packed in a 50 cm (20 in)
trench along the wall lines, with buttresses every 3 m vo fü).
Bach 25 cm (10 in) rise (suf) is approximately 30 cm (12 in) wide,
slightly tapered and rounded, with the top indented for better
bonding. Openings are left free but for timber lintels, and rarely
arches, or subsequently cut for fitting. The roofis builtat 3.5 m
(1.5 ft), historicallytopped byaparapettoa heightofs m (6 fi).

Roofconstruction involves settinga 10 cm (4 in) beam across
the centre ofthe room, crosslaying 7 cm (3 in) joists, followed
bytied 2 cm (tin) bamboo splits and woven matting. Traditionally four camel loads of straw insulation would be topped with
15 cm (6 in) ofpacked soil. Finally, three layers (5 cm L in]) of
a fermented mixture ofstraw, dung, clay (rigaita) and sand are
applied.

Rates ofconstruction are measured by dura'a (cubit), and tools
are limited to aladder, hod carrier, hoe, metal spikes andaline.
A master-builder stated that 00 m (30 ft) wall length could be
laid in 6-10 hr, and thata house on 300 sq m (3230 sq ft) with a
boundary wall and three rooms can be builtinıs days using 118
tons ofsoil. He estimated that only 10 per cent ofsuch housing
was self-built. Relations between builders are very strong,
involving referral and sharing ofwork to benefit one another.

A breakdown with 1988 materials costs shows that walls
account for 33 per cent, roofs for 55 per cent and render för 12
per cent, waterand labour notincluded. Openings are typically
fitted with doors and windows ofblack iron sheets.

Rendering, if good, is renewed every 5-15 years ata cost of
two-fifths ofnew construction. For a single room this requires
seven sacks of zibala and onetonne of rigaita. Zibala render may
use straw, fermented for 10-15 days, or dung, fermented only
for three to four days. .

Though this archetype is attributed to the Manasirr and
Shaigiyya tribes of the lower Nile valley, changes in northern

376

Sudan, with respect to migration, trade, expansion of Arab culture, desertification and urbanization, have led to its de facto
position as popular urban housing archetype.
Minimum standards for third-class residential areas permit
jalous, a rare example of modern urban development accepting
traditional construction. While this perpetuates sociocultural
norms and suits the economic position of most poor people,
several problems arise. Increased urban exposure to flooding
and ground heaving significantly reduces durability, as shown by
the destruction of200 000 homes in Khartoum’s floods of1988.
High demand, devegetation and transport costs have sharply
inflated the prices ofbuilding materials, and poverty results in
neglect ofrendering and maintenance, with obvious risks.
PATRIK SCHUMANN

Piles and stilts

Extreme drought conditions in 1853-4 caused lakes
in Europe to fall to unprecedented low levels, revealingon Lake
Zurich the evidence of neolithic dwellings on the borders of
lakes in Switzerland and Germany. Several such sites were in
Württemberg, notably on theshores ofthe Federsee, where two
large pile dwellings were discovered beneath layers of Jatet log
houses; they had large front platforms, gabled roofs and interior division into two rooms. Baking ovens and clay hearths,
cooking utensils and waste food products were found ‘in perfect condition’. The discoveries in the ancient lake beds were
remarkably preserved in waterlogged deposits (Keller). Apart
from the extensive finds of artefacts the sites indicated the
ancient history of pile dwellings.

Many timber-framed buildings have wooden posts which are
“earthfast’, or driven into the earth to make avertical cantilever.
While they share this feature, pile structures support a raised
platform, creating a void beneath the occupiable structure and
the ground. The methods of driving the piles are varied, butthe
soil conditions largely determine the system employed. They
range from the use of sharpened stakes, which are hammered
into the ground with heavy mallets, to logs dropped vertically
between guide posts or a trestle to aid the direction of force,

using team labour or horse power. Roman engineers developed
techniques of pile-driving which were used on major civic
works such as bridges and wharfs for many centuries, though
they were seldom applied to domestic building. An important
exception was the construction of the merchants’ houses of
Amsterdam on literally thousands of piles that had to be driven
some 12 m (40 ft) through peatto reach a sand layer. This was
part ofthe building programme in the major planning scheme
designed by Hendrik ]z Staets and commenced in 1610. Likethe
development of pile construction within caissons and coffer
dams and ofsteam power for driving piles, itmay be considered
as professional engineering. i
Within the many vernacular contexts where piles are used in
building, post-holes are often dug first, sometimes enlarged by
the rotation ofa stout branch, or jumped’ by the repeated driving ofa pointed guiding pole which is withdrawn before the
post is inserted. Heavy posts may have to be manhandled or
drawn up an incline before being dropped into the hole, which
is then 'back-filled’ with earth or stones to close the surround-

1.6.

Urban jafous houser,
Omdurman Showin, =
renewal ofrenge,
Sudan,

See also
1.11.3.c Flood
1.VI.5.d Mass wall.
3.VI.7-A Nile and syg n
References

el Agraa, Omer M,

1985

Kidd, W.J.,1980
Schumann, P.K,,

ing space, and ‘tamped’ hard with a blunt pole, maul or mallet.
An independent floor on short piles was used by men in the
Purari delta, New Guinea, to support shear legs which were
advanced as the main house-post they carried was lowered into
a prepared hole, edged with guide posts. The raising ofthe first
postis often perceived asasymbolic actwhich requires propitiation of land spirits or ancestors, sometimes by sacrifice and
with appropriate ritual.

Pile supports are built in diverse environmental conditions

and are among the most widely distributed ofall structural systems, being used in open plains and forest sites, hilltops,
slopes and valley locations, by lakes and rivers, on coastal
shorelines and also offshore. In some cases the exposed length
ofthe post is short- 50 cm (20 in) - but ftequently house piles
are raised above head height, and examples of pile supports of‘
Io m (33 ft) or more have been recorded. The reasons for raising buildings on piles are various, and reflect the nature ofthe
culture concerned, and often, its economy. In hot and humid
tropical climates pile dwellings have been builtto gain theadvantage of any air movement or breeze. They also raise houses
above the level oftroublesome snakes, rodents and insects. Pile
dwellings built over the sea can be free of mosquitoes, while in
the central Amazon piles raise houses above seasonal flood levels. Sometimes the supporting poles rise to rooflevel, the loor
frame being lashed or bracketed to them. Such piles are sometimes referred to as ‘stilts’. The isolation of individual house
units by raising them on stilts is evident among some peoples,
but the longhouses on piles in Southeast Asia and Indonesia
accommodate whole villages, and perform both social and
defensive functions.

Many highland peoples in Southeast Asia use pile and stilt
structures to produce level platforms for their houses, with tall
poles supporting one end of the structure, and short ones
inserted on the upper level ofaslope. Butthis is also ameans of
making valley and cliffsites suitable for housing, as in Colombia and Alaska. In northern climates floor levels may be raised
above cold ground, butthe houses are customarilybuilt.directly
on firm and continuous foundations. An exception is made in
the case of granaries, which are generally raised on piles by
sedentary agrarian peoples the world over. Where cereals are

Supports and Floors 1.1.6
stored this is usually to keep the food from attack by rodents,
the piles being encircled with ‘ratguard’ discs. Inclined downwards, they serve the same purpose as the mushroom-shaped
‘staddle stones’ of British granaries, or hewn granary posts in
the Nordic countries.

Lake, lagoon and offshore settlements of pile dwellings or
palafitos are found extensively in places as far apart as Venezuela,
New Guinea and Dahomey, and many examples are discussed
in this work. Reputedly among the largest lagoon settlements
is Kampung Ayer, Brunei, which has been transformed from its
thatched pile dwellings ofthe early-2oth century to the contemporary town oftimber-framed houses covered with corrugated
sheet metaland lamina, connected byanetwork ofwalkways and
bridges; even the school is built on piles. Since World War II
rapid urbanization has resulted in increasing use of pile dwellings to house the homeless, such as at Guayaquil, Ecuador,
where piles and stilts were driven at low tide and stabilization
ofthe mud flats was traded for votes. PAUL OLIVER

1.V.6.j wall: brick
Brick walling is a strong form ofmass walling, comprising innumerable small units or bricks, bonded together with
mortar to form a monolithic load-bearing structure. Mortar is
made from a range of materials, from mixtures of earth and
sand, lime, burntshelland more recently, cement, The strength
of the mortar should not exceed the strength of the brick.
Excessively strong mortars such as are formed from modern
cements frequently lead to cracking, not across the joints but
through the actual bricks themselves.
The thickness of mortar joints has varied through time
according to fashion and the quality ofthe bricks themselves.
Unevenness ofthe bricks was often concealed in thick mortar
joints. First- and second-century Roman mortar joints were no
more than halfthe thickness ofthe bricks, but by the third century this had increased to joints of equal thicknesses to the
bricks themselves. Mortarjoints were used notonlytobond the
bricks together, butalso to space them, and deceits or practices
developed whereby thick mortar joints similar in colour to the
surrounding bricks were ‘tucked’ by striking a horizontal line
through the mortar which was neatly filled with a thin tuck of
white putty.
The strength ofbricks is greatly affected by the method used
to dry them. To achieve the properties characteristic of fired
brick, they must be fired at over 900 °C (1650 °F) for a period of‘
days. Engineering bricks will achieve crushing strengths of up
to 10000 Ib/sq in (69 N/sq mm) through pushing the vitrification process. Ordinary London stock bricks, which typically
have a high ash content, leading to high combustion temperatures within the bricks themselves, can achieve strengths of
750-1500 (5.2-10.5 N/sq mm) and even unfired or green bricks
can achieve strengths of350 N/sq mm. In fact, the load exerted
on each brick by those above it is relatively small and unfired
brick can be used safely in the construction ofbuildings ofup to
three storeys, It has been calculated that no fewer than 8000
bricks would be needed to crush a brick in the lowest range of
erushingstrengths, which when piled on top ofeach otherwould
reach a height of535 m (1750 ft).

377

## S. 139

1.V PRODUCTION

The strength ofa brick wall is also dictated by its slenderness
ratio, or the wall thickness relative to its height. An approximate rule ofthumb suggests that a ratio of 1:20 should not be
exceeded withoutthe use ofthickenings such as piers, pilasters
and even buttresses to absorb lateral distortion. In most
domestic work brick walls were no more than 225 mm (9 in)
thick before the advent of cavity walls and building regulations
this century. However, in non-domestic work, such as mightbe
required for mills or malting houses, thicker walls one and a
halfor even two bricks thick were required.

Bricks in England vary in size according to their age. In
Roman times several different sizes were in use, each with its
own name and each with a half brick to go with it. The usual
size however was the great brick, some 30x 30x 4. cm (122x12x
1.5 in) thick which eventually gave way to smaller units of
28-23 X I1.5-I4X5 cm (H-9 X 4.5-5.5x 2 in) and consequently
to thinner walls. Only in Georgian times was there arecurrence
ofa great brick, when a brick tax introduced in 1784 on every
brick produced led to their increase in size up to 30 cm (12 in)x
15 cm (6 in) x7.5 cm (3 in). Whilstbrick size has varied through
time, the ease with which bricks can be held in one hand whilst
the mortar to bond them is picked up in the other has kept the
critical dimension, the width, remarkably constant.

A bond has two purposes: the achievement of firmness by
overlapping the bricks and avoiding the creation ofccavities and
vertical joints through the wall, and the decorative appearance
formed by the pattern ofjoints in the facework. The use of a
‘double square’ or rectangular brick shape has enabled the
development ofa range of patterns. A header is a brick which is

378

laid across a wall which is made up otherwise of two stretcher
rows or leaves of brick, laid end to end. The headers in a wall
bind the two leaves together.

In England bonding can be traced back to the ızth century,
before which the bond was unclear. Thereafter, systems or
patterns developed, the most common being English bond,
which consists ofalternate rows ofstretchers and headers, with
the headers centred overajointorastretcher and vice versa. Cut
bricks or queen closers are used with a header to terminate each
header row. Itis considered to be the strongestofbonds, owing
to the absence of cut bricks in the main area of work; itwas the
earliestand remains the most widely used bond both in Europe
and in the developing world. DEIRDRE McDERMOTT
1V.6.k Wall: earth
Of all materials used for walling, earth is probably
the most widespread. In India alone the National Building
Organization reported that over 43 per centofthe nation’srural
population lived in katcha houses of mud walls and thatched
roofs accommodatingin the order of 200 million people. Butas
soils differ so does their suitability for earth construction. Laterites, which cover much ofIndia and Africa, are low in fertility
and leach out when forest cover is cleared, hardening when
they are exposed to the sun and air. They are frequently used in
piling, packing and modelling techniques. The sandy desertic
soils are found in North Africa and the Sahara, Arabian peninsula and the Middle East, much of Central Asia, and the southwest of North America; they lend themselves to methods of
compression such as rammed earth or adobe blocks. Podzolic
soils, such as are found throughout northern Europe and much
of Russia, are suitable for mass walling; those beneath fir
forests are too acidic for this purpose, though the firs provide
an abundantalternative building material.

Frequently, earth is used in combination with other materials
— bamboo to act as a reinforeing frame, chopped grasses as a
binding material, small rocks as an aggregate, or timber as framing to panels of wattle daubed with clay. These, and literally
scores of other techniques in the use of soils, earths, clay and
mud, are responses to the physical and chemical properties,
such as particle size, ofthe material to hand. Allthe techniques
used are means of rendering the local soil suitable for wall
building.

Earth can only be used as a mass material; it has no tensile
strength and cannot be used for spans. Though mud roofs are
an apparent commonplace, in much ofthe lateritic, brown soil
and chernozem regions they are, in fact, palm or timber
spanned roofs, augmented with branches, palm-ribs or reeds,
and covered with mud, compressed with tamps or rollers. Only
in rare and regional circumstances can earth blocks be used

for domes or vaults; the celebrated parabolic domes of the
Mousgoum ofCameroon are only made possible by the unusual
plasticity of the local soil; only a few miles away the soil is not
suitable for making these remarkable but singular structures.
In regions which have sufficient trees of adequate length for
pole construction, earth is commonly combined with an internal armature or frame, which exploits both the elasticity ofthe
wood and the density ofthe earth.

Palouse, Wash z
United States,

See also
1.1V.2.b Brick‘

References
Brian, Anthea,]ı
Smith, Lance,19
Spencg, R.
D.1.,1983

NS.Clough,1920

Advocates of earth building as a practical material to solve
the world’s housing problems are numerous, and the evidence
of its effectiveness for mass walling that takes compressive
loads is evident in the dramatic towers of Yemen, the ksourofthe
High Atlas, or the cubes and terraces of the Pueblos. But the
majority of earth buildings are single-storeyed, rectangular
prisms, low cylinders and linked compounds, shaped to
enclose volumes while countering the disadvantages of the
material. The majority ofcultures that use earth for building are
agricultural; as soil is the basis oftheir economy, the use oftopsoils has to be avoided. Excavation of the ground for soils
requires appropriate tools and consumes land; the urbanized
Hausa, with limited space, are obliged to dig deep and disfiguring ‘borrow pits’. Yet earth is a heavy material which does not
transport well in block form. In consequence, itis obtainedand
moulded close to the construction site with an inevitable
confliet ofbuilding and land use interests.

Though ramming produces solid walls, in many regions
walls are built of mud balls or coiled clay. Many African and
some Indian buildings have walls that are constructed as ifthey
were pots, as indeed some granaries are. The rounded walls
gain in their resistance to lateral forces, and the linking of hut
cylinders with moulded compound walls helps disperse them.
Many such dwellings have built-in platforms, cooking and
storage spaces moulded also in clay, which, apart from their
practical and sculptural qualities, act as buttresses to the walls.

Earth walls are extremely vulnerable to damage by heavy
rains, and though these may be infrequent or seasonal, the
effect of water pouring down the surface can result in loss of
material, liquefaction or partial collapse of the structure; in
heavy rains and floods earth buildings can literally dissolve.
Deep eaves and thick thatch, itself prone to water damage,
are necessary to throw rainwater away from the walls, while
mud-plaster is often laid to shed surface water. Sometimes this
is finger-moulded to channel water away from weak points, but
splashback, or the rebounding effect ofrain on laterite ground,
can cause serious erosion. Spalling, the sheet erosion of mudplaster which exposes the unprotected inner mud layers, is very
damaging. The high absorption of water by mud walls can lead
to slow disintegration, while the poor adhesion of mud to timber may result in sagging or collapsing roofs.

Some regions where earth is used are also prone to earthquakes. The considerable mass and weight ofearth blocks and
the relative weakness of mud-mortar combine to make it a
lethal material when subjected to seismic shock. ‘Humping
action’, orthe combination ofshock currents in different directions, causes cracks to appear and walls to part, with the result
that roof timbers and mud layers fall on the occupants, frequently with fatal consequences. Extensive research on the
behaviour ofearthen walls in seismic conditions has resulted in
improvements in building practice and economic systems of
reinforcement, such as the use of chicken wire or internal bracing. Similarly, methods of soil stabilization, from the use of
asphalt with the mix, to combining laterites and cementto produce ‘land-crete’, are being introduced to assist the builders of‘
earthen walls in vulnerable regions.

PAUL OLIVER

Supports and Floors 1.1.6

1.1.6.1 wall: timber (Appalachian)
Log wall construction, of timbers laid horizontally
and connected by corner notching, is widely distributed in
Eastern and Central Europe and in the United States. In rough
or temporary buildings, the timbers might be left round and
saddle-notched at the corners, in which case they are called
‘poles’, not ‘logs’. In more finished work, in mosthouses andin
the majority of other buildings, the logs are ‘faced’, hewn on
the front and rear to create smooth walls inside and out. Much
less commonly, especially toward the south, logs are split or
sawn lengthwise, yielding matched pairs. The tops and bottoms oflogs are not hewn square; the timber retains its sinuous
natural contour, and logs are set in the wall so that they arch
upward. Spaces remain between the undulating tops and bottoms ofthe logs. Left open for ventilation in barns, these interstices in dwellings are ‘chinked’, packed with stones or split
wood, bedded in clay, and theman who can mixa good ‘mud’ to
chink the wall is a craftsman as valued as the one with the
broad-axe who hews the logs to a snapped line. The ends ofthe
logs do not protrude beyond the plane of the wall; they are
hewn, or more often sawn off flush to create a trim box corner.
Within this general technology, two distinct handlings -
both usually neater in houses than in outbuildings — can be distinguished, though they mix in real buildings in accord with
the wide variety of available timber. In one the log is ‘planked’,
hhewn so thatitis talland narrow in section, two to four times as
high as itis deep, and then joined at the corner in a half-dove-

379

asse1D AuaH ©

## S. 140

|
|
|

1.V PRODUCTION

© Henry Glassie

tail notch. The timber is characteristically poplar, the narrow
spaces between the logs are packed with clay, and the exterior
face is usually covered with clapboarding. In the other the logs
are squarer in section, and they are joined atthe corner in a V-
notch, called ‘gable-topping’ by the old carpenters; the end of
the log resembles the gable-end ofa building. Oak is the characteristic wood. The wide spaces between the logs are chinked
and then coated with white lime plaster, moulded to throw
water away from the joints. The log wall is exposed to the
weather or whitewashed. Through time, as log construction,
associated with rustic pioneer days in the nation, became associated with an impoverished past in the region, many houses
were sheathed with clapboarding which obviated the need for
annual renewal ofthe chinking and gave the neater appearance
ofa framed house. HENRY GLASSIE
1\V.6.|-1 Single-wall (America, w)

Awooden framing system locally called ‘single-wall?
or‘batten board’ construction flourished in the late-ıgth century
during the first phase of settlement of mining camps in the
mountains ofthe West. With enough boards and nails a rancher
or miner could lay out and erect a modest building using this
efficient and relatively economical framing tradition which
called for nothing more than two layers ofboards. The tradition
has antecedents in medieval Europe and in other regions ofthe
United States and it is a widespread if comparatively seldom
documented form of vernacular construction technology,

380

adopted where speed and simplieity were concerns and where
portable, even prefabricated, structures were desirable.

Asingle-wall building features walls made of one or two layers ofthin sawn boards and without benefit of posts and beams
or ofstuds for bracing. The sawn boards were placed vertically
(or with two layers, vertically and horizontally); since a wall
made of a single layer of boards aligned horizontally or vertically was not strong, a second layer was usually added. The second layer of boards was nailed at right angles to the first layer;
if the outside of a building was composed of vertical Aush
boards, the interior layer would be ofhorizontal boards. Often
the interior layer was neither substantial nor, in some cases,
fully covering. It could be wainscoting or horizontal boards
applied ı m (3 ft) up the side of the wall with the space above
that left bare and unfortified. The rigidity of the structure is
aided by the horizontal bearing members nailed to the finished
panels of single-wall construction, to which are nailed themud
sills, floor joists, plates, ceilingjoists and roofsystem. Whether
the structure has just one or two layers of boards, it is considered to be a single-wall or batten board building.

Window openings are located, measured and sawn outofthe
wall, with ‘two-by-four’ (2x 4 in [5 x 10 cm]) studs added to
support the window-frame. One indication that this method
has been used is in the boxed window-frames where the
window-frame is thicker than the wall itself in single-wall construction. The wall may be only 2.5-5 cm (1-2 in) thick. So the
window-frame protrudes through the wall several inches, usually
to the inside. This protrusion is sometimes mitigated or hidden
byadding more layers ofinsulation or decorative wall coverings
inside — creating thicker walls to make up the difference. The
individual strata ofwood sometimes have a layer ofcheesecloth
or newspapers between them for added insulation. The inside

„walls are usually nicely finished with a layer of cheesecloth,

over which is added wallpaper or composition board.
In its crudest form, a single-wall building could be a
hardrock miner’s shack, a cabin ofslapped together pine slabs

Glassie, Hent
Jordan, Terry

‚composition
sheathingins

bunkhouse,

United States.

‚dstrip
and batten

bought cheaply at the sawmill. In form, these were single-pen
(single-cell) or double-pen houses - basic British Isles-American folk housetypes. Many such buildings, thought of as
impermanent structures and temporary lodgings, were planted
on the Nevada mining landscape in the bonanza years between
1859 and about 1910. However, in its most expansive mode, as
in the home ofa relatively wealthy banker, single-wall technology could provide a handsome two-storey Gothic Revival house
teflecting late-Victorian domestic vernacular architecture.

A building that was simple to make was not necessarily simple in shape or ornament. The inexpensive, efficient carpentry
technique behind single-wall construction was known across
the nation through a variety ofpublications. Sketches and plans
for ‘cottages’ in the ‘rural Gothic style’ but built ofthe singlewall construction method were published in builders’ guides.

HOWARD WIGHT MARSHALL
1V,6.m Wall: stone
Stone walling is associated with craftsmen. Whereas
a wall of plant-processed stone is predetermined by the shape
and dressing of the stone, the appearance of locally dressed
stone depends on the skills and the taste ofthe waller. The term
‘stone walling’, used rather loosely, is to emphasize the mortared
versions as opposed to dıy stone walls.

There are two basic constructional types of stone wall —
the skeleton wall and the massive wall. The skeleton wall may
consist of wooden poles and beams which form a framework
and are then filled in with stone, also known in the Caribbean
as ‘Spanish walling’, for example in Jamaica. This type ofwallis
an indigenous type of stone nogging, reputed to have been
introduced by the Spaniards. The stones are keyed in to form a
tight knit, mortared with earth and lime. Such constructions
are more flexible than massive walls and more suitable for use
in earthquake and hurricane areas. Skeleton frames in Haiti,
common between Miragoane and Fond des Negres in the
south, use vertical uprights and are not braced by diagonal tim-

Supports and Floors 1.V.6

bers. The massive wall is a monolithic wall and is more common in areas with high seasonal temperature difference.

Both types ofwall can be ofdty stone or builtwith mortarand
can be coursed rubble, random rubble, snecked rubble, polygonal or flint walling.

The straight run of the wall is normally turned through a
right angle by cornerstones or quoins to enclose space or to
form a framework which is then filled with stones or bricks.
In stone rondavels, for instance at Maseru in Lesotho, the individual stones are gradually turned until a circle or oval shape is
attained.

The thickness ofthe beds in the quarry is of primary importance and determines the position ofthe stones in the wall. The
height ofthe mullions usually exceeds the thickness ofthe bed:
hence, long slender mullions are placed edge-bedded, rather
than small pieces of stone being piled precariously above one
another.

Edge-bedding is also used for cornices, copings and string
courses to avoid face-bedding. To achieve a tall, slender
masonry detail, the quarry bed may be cut diagonally across in
order to obtain maximum height.

Stones are laid in their natural on-bed position. Inthe case of
soft, medium and hard stones the underside of lintels, arches
and some types of projection can be subject to attack by atmospheric elements, such as rain, in which case the bedding plane
is contrary.

Walls tend to support one another and when enclosing a
space give mutual support at their ends. Aminimum thickness
is required for their stability and thickness increases with length.
The nearer walls are to each other, the greater the strength of
the connecting wall. Various tying and bonding devices compensate for inadequacies in stonework, serving to increase
stress resistance and to imparta certain amount of pliability.

These devices, besides quoins, include corner-ties, jambs or
piers made of cut stone, hard-stone cupola ties, and iron or
wooden ties which bind the corners ofa building. Floors act as
braces or stays in a structure, as do partition walls, cornerreturns and buttresses.

Solid or massive walls are constructed from one side ofthe
wall. When two faces are constructed with the cladding acting
as shuttering and with infill in between, one face at a time

381

o
3
Ei

## S. 141

u

1.V PRODUCTION

is worked up to convenient height. When the second face
has reached the same height infilling takes place. Infill is a
packing material, and not only does it not bind the wall, it also
contributes to pushing the faces apart. The two faces are
bonded together with long, narrow ‘bond’ stones, setaside duringsorting.

Composite walls may contain several materials across their
width, for example, stone and concrete, various stone materials
such as pebbles and broken slabs, and a sand and lime mortar.
This is called the ‘banked stone system’; to pour in the conerete, therough or cutblocks or slabs ofstone are used as ‘form
boards’ in place of shuttering on the outer face. This system is
common in the Middle East.

In massive walls, dry or mortared, the principle of two-overone and one-over-two is employed, thus preventing lines of
weakness. The topping requires a stronger mortar to ensure
that the stones remain in place, especially in dry walling and
where coping is not used.

There are several kinds of load-bearing stone wall. The
uncoated single wall is a simple structure consisting solely of
the stone material used to build it. Water permeates through to
the inside face, as there is no impervious facing on the outer
surface and no damp-proof membrane in the thickness ofthe
wall, unless a damp-proof course is provided.

The coated single wall does not usually have an impervious
facing on the outside, but across the width ofthe walla continuousnon-capillary damp-proofcourseis essential. InIndiaand
Nepal slates are commonly used for this purpose.

Massive stone walling usually consists of courses held
together with mortar and containing various stone elements.
The face is the portion ofthe wall to view and the back is the
inner portion. Major elements in mortared stone walling are
stone, mortar, joints and pointing, and to a certain extent
infillings, especially in vernacular construction.

Courses have particular names like plinth, a projecting
course near the ground line, belt courses or string courses
placed at intervals for the purpose ofornament. A course from
which an arch springs is called a springing course and a
stretching course is composed entirely of stretchers; likewise a
header course is solely of headers. A bonding course is composed of transverse ‘bond stones’ and a corbel course is composed of pieces ofstone projecting from the face ofthe wall for
supportinga course that projects still further. The cornice is the
course generally setat or near the top ofthe wall.

The mortar layers are called joints; bed joints are horizontal
and vertical joints at the end. There are flush joints, struck
joints, weathered joints, key joints, raised, ruled and eroded
joints, each with a characteristic appearance. Pointing is used
for special protection and consists offilling the groove made by
scraping out the mortar and may be coloured by adding pigments. Itis used likewise to give the wall a superior appearance
and conceal the effects ofinferior work.

The two principal types ofwalls are retaining or free-standing
walls. Retaining walls are used to keep back earth, soil, infill or
terracing, or to preserve contour levels, to act as road-cuttings
or to control erosion; in all ofthese uses one face is exposed.

ASHER SHADMON

382

1.V.6.m-i Wall: stone - cavity

Cavity walls are double, separate-leaf walls consisting oftwo distinctly separate wall structures and are seldom used
in vernacular constructions. The damp-proofing is achieved by
acontinuous air gap ofs5-15 cm (2-6 in). The outside stone wall is
usually non-loadbearing, but it must be resistant to external
pressures, such as wind, depending on its topographical position.

Thetypeofwall, referred to as a ‘self-supporting facing wall’,
is usually builtwith cutashlar or squared stone. Expansion joints
are required for larger-sized structures. This wall either extends
to each floor level or is continuous for a number of floors,
avoiding contacts where heat or dampness may be conducted.

Mechanical stability is ensured by connecting the exterior
wall to the load-bearing wall by ties made in non-corrosive
metal and used in varying quantities. The thickness ofeach wall
varies from 7.5 cm (3 in) to o cm (4 in). The application ofties
is made easier if the stone blocks used for the outer walls have
the same dimensions as those used for the inner walls, so that
their courses correspond.

Movements between the two walls are prevented by providing flexible joints across the air gap. Roof projections are
usually adequate to decrease the amount of water reaching the
facade immediately below; small projections at a lower levelon
the wall, such as below windows, serve a similar purpose.

ASHER SHADMON

1.V.6.m-ii Wall: stone - dry stone

A dry stone wall is constructed without mortar and
requires great skill. The stone used can be dressed, non-dressed
orshaped, or can consist ofany type ofrubblestones; usually it
is built of fieldstones as this type of wall is mainly used where
local stones are easily available. Direct stone-to-stone gives the
wall its structural stability. A properly constructed dry stone wall
expands and contracts with changes in temperature, unlike a
mortared wall which, beingrigid, breaks and cracks under stress.

ult: corbelled

Shadmon, Asher,iggb

‚Argentina.

Üfetaining walls,
Aalearic Islands.

see also for!
1.1v.4 Rocks al
11v.a.e coutsEd

References/d"

shadmon, ABl
1988D

The lowest courses, known as “footing’, rest on subsoil,
rather than directly on the ground. The soil is raked to ensure a
flattish base. Stakes higher than the planned wall are hammered in to mark the outline. Four more stakes are placed
within this area, with string touching the inner facing of each
stake and fastened to prevent curving by the wind.

The choice of stone for the foundation, that is, whether to
use small, odd-shaped stones and save the biggest flat stones
for the top course, depends on the nature of the foundation;
soft, irregular footings may provide better anchorage than aflat
underside. Open spaces filled with smaller stones provide good
drainage especially when used in work below ground level.
Angling the stones slightly inwards towards the centre causes
the outer walls of the structure to lean in against themselves,
increasing stability.

The higher the wall, the wider and deeper the footings. The
top face ofeach stone has a slight downward slanton which the
next course rests. Splinters and wedge-shaped stones keep the
main stone units stable. Wedge-shaped stones are placed with
the widestend in the core ofthe wall and the thin edge pointing
out. The uppermost course consists of good flats.

If a wall is built on an incline, a ditch is dug on the upper
slope ofthe wall and a hole or culvert in the first course aboveground is constructed to control drainage. Batter, or the narrowing ofa wall from a broad base, is detailed for retaining
walls, especially for the backward slope, the angle increasing
with the height required.

Although different principles may apply to dry walling, batter
is essential for stones which are not flat; with regular and evenly
laid stones the moment of resistance (weight x volume x leverage) is important and depends on the form and disposition of
the retaining wall. The leverage is calculated by determining
the location ofthe centre of pressure by defining a vertical line
through the centre ofgravity ofthe walland a further line transversely along the joint at which failure would be most likely to
occur. This is usually atthe ground line.

Usually freestanding dry walling does not exceed a height of
80-90 cm (30-35 in); even then, a base of 50-60 cm (20-23 in)
width is required. However, higher walls can be built, widening
the base accordingly (20 cm [8 in] for each 30 cm [1 ft] ofadditional height), and most importantly, the footing depth is well
below any sensitive ground level (e.g. frost level) down to about
6ocm (2 fi).

Freestanding walls, such as stone fences, have both faces
exposed. When dealing with ashlar, slabby or squared stones, the
construction ofthe first course is fairly straightforward, butrandom rubble requires special techniques. A rubble filling between
the retainingwalland the earth bank enables water to flow freely
behind the walland prevents a build-up of pressure atany point.

Adry stone lattice fence consists of alternate squarish blocks
and thin slabs, with a support block where the slabs in the
course above meet. Gaps are angled in order to keep out cattle.
Stiles are often cantilevered into the walls and have rises of
about 20 cm (8 in) and the same distance forward per step.
Gates or door hangers are fixed into the facing ends of the
opening, holes being formed with a hammer and chisel, with
the stone in a horizontal position to ensure stability.

Supports and Floors 1.V.6

In addition to dry stone walls per se, in many parts oftheworld
such walls are used in housing, shelters and other structures.
In the Khota district of Rajasthan practically all dwellings are
constructed from smooth, fine-grained flagstones thatabound
in the area. The stones are trimmed to sizeand builtup between
uprightslabs which then stay in position to form the door, windows, cupboards, shelves and niche surrounds. The parts
which take most ofthe load in these all-stone structures -sills,
beams, roof slabs - are sometimes constructed of sandstone
which has a higher bending strength.

This type ofconstruction is ‘vernacular modular’ par excellence
and, besides some rudimentary inclines needed to haul the
material, practically no scaffolding is used by the Rajasthan
masons. ASHER SHADMON
1.V.6.m-iii Wall: stone - retaining

When built dry, a retaining wall does not usually
exceed a height ofı m (3 ft) or a thickness of about 45-50 cm
(18-20 in). Retaining walls are usually started some 3ocm(ıft)
from the site and then back-filled. Dry walling is less common
in the southern part ofthe world and the mortar is notas critical as it is in the northern climate, where it is subjected to a
range oftemperatures and frost.

There is perhaps no other type of walling in which stone is
as frequently used as in retaining walls. Generally dıy or
mortared, there are several types of retaining wall; some basic
structures have a vertical rectangular section which, though
effective, is not economical. Because pressure increases with
the height ofthe wall, it is advantageous to increase the width
of the base. With a vertical face with a sloping back, the
increased area ofthe base gives more stabilityanda greater area
over which to distribute the pressure. An additional advantage
is that the weight ofthe earth on the side of'the wall acts with
the weight ofthe wall.

A section with a sloping back and battered face offers no
practical advantages over the previous example, except thatthe
battered face gives the appearance of greater stability. There is
no advantage ofa curved, battered face over a straight one, and
ittakes considerably more time to construct. A sloping rectangular section is wasteful as the portion ofthe wall on the earth
side ofa vertical line drawn from the inside corner ofthe base
does notact with the wall.

ASHER SHADMON

## S. 142

ıv-a Techniques and Skills

All vernacular processes of production, from initial
preparations to assembly and final finishing, require techniques of execution: methods by which the desired results are
achieved and the edifice is constructed to the satisfaction ofthe
builders and to the normative standards within the culture.
Techniques are in essence mechanical, to be learned and
applied as means to an end. They are generally taught by the
experienced to novices, and to those who will assume part or
the whole ofthe responsibility for building in the future. This is
not to minimize their importance; techniques demand the
attention ofthe builders and understanding oftheir purposes.
They also require the development ofthe necessary abilities to
achieve the desired intentions, whether they involve the testing
of the strength of materials, ensuring the consistent use of
specific joints, or the applying of surface treatments to create
an impermeable skin.

Such techniques — and there are literally thousands of them
within the vast compass ofvernacular traditions— have been little documented; the greater emphasis of research is on the
finished product rather than the process. As has been noted in
1.11.18. Tradition and Transmission, there are a great many
ways by which techniques are passed on, from mimicry to
‘wrinkles’, and the study of these in practice is fundamental to
the understanding of the handing down of vernacular traditions in architecture. Unlike many monumental and elite forms
ofarchitecture in literate societies ofthe world, they are seldom
backed with scientific research or supported by codes of practice and building regulations. Techniques in the vernacular are
refined over time; their durability is testimony to their importance.

To implement the appropriate techniques for a particular
aspect of building production requires a thorough knowledge
ofthe craft or the application of the technique in practice. The
craft demands not only the capacity to comprehend an entire
process and its objectives, but also a knowledge of its variables
and the capacity to deal with them by adaptation, flexibility
or substitution. Differences in dimensions or thicknesses or
consistency of materials, forces of weather or other natural
phenomena special to a district, limitations or irregularities in
asite and innumerable other factors mustbeanticipated or dealt
with as they arise. Whereas the technique is inert and passive,
the craft is active, though dependent upon the technique.

Yet there are clearly degrees of craft abilities in applying techniques. In many cultures there isan expectation thatall members
have sufficient command of technique and craft to build their
own houses; in others that specific members of a family unit
have defined responsibilities for roof construction, thatching

384

orlaying the floors. Sometimes these are age or gender differentiated. It is evident to any observer in such conditions that
abilities vary, as they do in all societies; some builders are impatient, some clumsy or easily satisfied, while there are generally
a few who have more exacting standards. At a more abstract
level it is apparent that the spatial conceptualization necessary
to make components and assemble them with accuracy is a
mental capacity that is not shared by everyone. In any cultural
group itappears thatsome members have advanced spatial perception, frequently accompanied with advanced skills.

Skills are more than the combination of technique and
crafts; theyare personallyacquired and developed. They involve
diserimination in the materials used, which includes an understanding of their suitability and potential for a structural task,
and the ability to exploit these to the full. But skills also require
a command of the technical resources and means to work
the material, in particular the mastery ofthe tools made for the
purpose. Sometimes the skilled craftsperson makes his or her

ned

Janı

ihetic of
‚with clearly

Bodger‘slathe
La Granja, Ma
Balearic Islanı

own tools, and many will modify or augment them to suit their
hands or the task. Skills imply the synthesis of knowledge and
expertise which may be directed or focused on a specific craft.

Techniques, craft, skills, expertise and tools, nevertheless,
are not always readily comparable. The Punan hunter who
makes atemporary shelter that is substantial, protective and yet
easily concealed, has knowledge and skills tuned to the special
eircumstances of the Sarawak jungle that are not comparable
with those ofthe Apulian builder ofcorbelled tufa-domed trulli.
Environment, climate, economy, resources, tools, duration of
occupancy are all so differentiated between such cultures as to
be irreconcilable and to render any attempt at comparable criteria oflittle value.

This does not mean that criteria of quality and skills are not
applied: Wilson reported that the Nyakyusa took ‘immense
care’ in building, ‘essential to comfort, and indeed a condition
of survival, in this climate of torrential rains and cold winter
mists’. They also took ‘great pride in the appearance of their
homesteads,’ in Lord Lugard’s terms (1893), ‘the neatest and
most wonderfully built ofany I have seen in Africa’ (Wilson). It
seems that craft skills, even ifthey are partly intuitive, are also
acquired through practice and competition within a skilled culture. The villages ofthe Gediz region in Turkey varied greatly in
the degree oftime and attention given to building. In part this
reflected their relative poverty, but certain villages, such as
Erdogmus, were locally celebrated for the high standard of
building but, rather than attempt to acquire the skills, or
improve their own, members of satellite villages employed
Erdogmus builders for their own houses when they could
afford to do so.

When the early medieval lodges (oeuvres or bauhuettes) were
established, partly as a response to the demand for craftsmen
and journeymen to work on the great cathedrals of Europe, a
hierarchy of skilled journeymen craftsmen under a magister
operis (manager) and a magister lapidum (master-mason) operated. Daily wages were regulated and differed with the seasons.
Around 30 places in the British Isles had guilds before the rath
century but it was another two centuries before builders’ guilds

Techniques and Skills 107

emerged. Their members were theoretically equal, and existed
both to regulate the quality ofworkmanship and to protect the
interests ofthe craftsmen. But the freedom ofthe journeymen
was curtailed by the guilds: a craftsman served a long apprenticeship, had to accept fixed rates of pay and could not easily
transfer his services. Nevertheless, what became a system of
restrictive practices did ensure high standards of skill; the
progress from apprentice to journeyman to mason to mastermason was one of increasing merit (Hauser). Though the
guilds declined and a working class slowly emerged in the ızth
century in northern Europe, in some parts of central Europe
and the Balkans they survived until late into the 20th century.
Observed Giedion, idealistically, ‘guild ethics centred around
one fundamental: maintenance ofquality. The European countries whose sense ofquality has remained vigorousalon: gside of
mechanical production are those where Gothic modes of living
have survived’ (Giedion).

Similar hierarchies of skills were evident in the period of
Islamic expansion; the builders of the Moghul mosques and
forts were classified in grades of craftsmen working below the
master-mason (banna), the higher-ranking me'mar working
under the ustad me'mar, virtually the architeet. Prodigious numbers of craftsmen were employed. For the r6th-century Agra
Fortalone 2000 stone-cutters and 2000 mortar-makers worked
daily while 8000 labourers supplied materials. Some workers
were employed by the job (ijara) while others were daily wage
earners (rozinadar), with distinctions made between artisans—a
naggash was a skilled stone carver, but a sadakar was a stone-cutter (Ahsan Jan Qaisa).

In Persia the censor of morals (muhtasib) supervised construction with regular checks on quality and good practice in
measurement, care of tools, preparation of materials, construction, timekeepingand allaspects ofbuilding (al-Hassan et
al.). Comparative documentation on the workers who built vernacular structures is not available, but the evidence ofthe existence of immense numbers of skilled builder-craftsmen is
substantial; guilds continued through the 20th century in Iran,
each centred on amosque and workshops.

Systems comparable to the guilds exist in many societies;
Nadel described the blacksmiths’ guilds ofthe Nupe, Nigeria,
which showed ‘the characteristic features ofthe guild system —
the closed profession, centralized control, the hierarchy of
ranks and grades’. Though not detailed, the builders, woodworkers and carpenters wereamongthe industries in Nupe organized in craft-guilds, which were subdivided into co-operative
efako labour units. These kinship groups (dengi) were exclusive,
working under a family head, with young boys employed in
‘easy auxiliary work’ until they were old enough to carry out
work under tuition, eventually to be full members of the unit.
Deeply embedded in the political system, with the guild-head
(dokodza) enjoying a privileged position in the Nupe kingdom
as a member ofthe King’s Council, the Nupe favoured ‘group
efficiency rather than progress and individual initiative’
(Nadel). But the system also assured steady and controlled
employment.

Family-based industries who have rights to their crafts are
not unusual in vernacular communities. On the island of Truk

385

## S. 143

1.V PRODUCTION

all crafts were the private property of specialist families, a craft
(rong) being handed down by a father to his eldest son who
became the popinrong, the master or ‘trunk’, the members ofthe
family being the ‘branches’ (panenrong) of the craft. A building
craftsman (sowrong) had to learn not only the skilled work of
house construction, but also the prayers, charms, divinations,
ritual practices and craft secrets. The principal craft masters
(idang) spoke secret languages and collectively exercised great
political power and influence. Family-based specialization in
Thurnwald’s view resulted in ‘the special dexterity of some
individuals’, the practice becoming the secret ofa clan’. The
‘secret and incomprehensible’ work of the craftsman could
gain him a reputation for beneficent magic and a respected
position, he concluded, butifhis work was an emergency expedient to gain food by exchange he was reduced to servitude. Yet
craft that was ‘not primarily dependent on profits permits of
entire devotion to the work’, accounting for ‘the perfect finish
of handmade articles ... made with the most primitive implements’ (Thurnwald).

Analysis ofthe work of past individual building craftsmen is
largely based on the identification of personal styles. Africanist
Fagg was ofthe opinion that ‘therigidity under which the tribal
artist worked was not appreciably greater’ than it was for the
‘Renaissance Italian painters of religious subjects’ (Fagg,
1961). He compared the work ofmany Yoruba carvers ofhouseposts, including Arowogun of Osi, a sculptor ofstrong forms,
with Agunna ofOke Igbira, distinguishable for his use ofsharp
features and his triangulation of forms, and Agbonbiofe, a

386

member ofa famous family of carvers. He had been loaned by
his ‘patron and master’, the Arinjale ofIsa, to several kings, and
his lively doors and house-posts were to be found over a large
area of Yorubaland (Fagg, 1969).

Such work was highly visible, but many of the techniques
and skills ofthe craftsperson are hidden from view, claspedina
roofingjoint, covered by plaster, enveloped in paint, often overlaid by the craft skills of others in the building team. Similarly,
the skills may be intentionally blended, as in the maintenance
of buildings or, more recently, in their conservation. This
anonymity the guilds trained the craftsman to accept, while
affording opportunities for work of quality in the building
production as a whole. When it is evident, skill in the craft
is applauded for its refinement, its Auidity of line or subtlety
ofform. Yet some building crafts gain by their ruggedness and
the correspondence between the materials and the techniques
with which they are worked. As Pye observed there is a ‘workmanship of certainty’ where the result of every operation is
predetermined and a ‘workmanship of risk’ which arises outof
every operation and is ‘determined by the workman as heworks
and its outcome depends wholly or largely on his care, judgementand dexterity'. Rough work, heargued, was the workmanship ofrisk and is as necessary ‘as the sketch is ofthe picture’;
in the workmanship of certainty there is no rough work (Pye).

Rough, unfinished, rugged work can have its own aesthetic,
and can be more indicative ofthe technique and skill ofa craftperson than perfect execution and immaculate finish. The aes-

thetic appeal of vernacular architecture often resides in the
evidence ofeye, hand and tool, matched without pretensions or
self-conscious artifice to the material worked and the purpose
for which itis made. As an example oftechniques and skills in
the working ofa material, stone is cited in this category.

PAUL OLIVER

ıv7-3 Building Guilds

Building guilds were self-regulating societies that
controlled much of the craft and process of building in many
post-feudal and pre-industrial societies. They were part ofa
wider system ofguilds thatincluded the professionals, artisans
and merchants who were central to the production of goods
and services in developing urban centres. In Venice of AD 1300
there were 52 such guilds, covering nearly all aspects of economic and social life. In some cities, there were even guilds of
beggars, prostitutes and thieves-a testament to the awareness
ofthe benefits of corporate association. In the emerging nonagricultural economies the guilds ensured thatasocialand eultural value was an integral part oflabour.

Guilds were known in ancient Egypt and in the early Semitic
world and were well established in India by 800 sc. The Collegia
and Scholae of classical Greece and Rome were guilds, and the
Byzantine world sustained an extensive guild system with the
building guilds continuing under the Ottomans in the 2oth
century. In China and Africa elaborate and functioning guild

Constructiong;
in Kampung na
Java.

1.11.18 Tradition and.
Transmission
