al-Hassan, Ahı

gu

Hauser, Arnold

Pye, David, 1968"
Reynolds, Su:
Thurnwald, Ric
Wilson, Monic:

networks are within living memory. Guilds seldom extended
beyond their local boundaries, and within towns and cities
their members often crowded together in one locale. Essential
to a guild was its open and voluntary association. Not tied to
family or caste, guilds were societies of brotherhood, friendship and mutual aid.

For the guilds involved in building, jurisdiction often shifted
as alliances changed over time. Sawyers, painters, stainers, tilers,
daubers, plasterers, thatchers, masons, marblers, carpenters,
plumbers, glazers, pavers, joiners and so on would stand alone
at one time and later be united with other trades. In early-ı5thcentury Venice, more than 64 service trades were involved in
building. Much of the great building from the Middle Ages of
many cultures was the work ofthe building guilds. Their legacy
includes their organization, their contributions to society and
culture, and their development ofthe craftof building.

The initial function of the guild was to determine the rules
for membership. In Florence every citizen was obliged to be a
member ofa guild. In England it was thought thatmembership
would ensure againstidleness - both an economic and a political liability. In India guilds relied more heavily on hereditary
membership than did those of Europe. Central to a guild’s
influence was its ability to negotiate and enforce equitable
hourly wages and good working conditions (six pence a day
plus an after-dinner drink in England in 1339). Also delineated
were hours of work, varying with the seasonal amount of daylight, use of seals and marks, prescribed dress code, meeting
times, banquet times, holidays, lunch hours and break times.

m

IM

Section

Winter
kitchen

Groundfloor „

Techniques and Skills 1.v7
The writing of contract rules and their enforcement were handled much as in contemporary practice. Usually payments were
made to the guild and individual working members were compensated according to their rank. Guilds attempted to reduce
competition between their members, and to bind them
together to avoid the emergence of cliques or combines that
would threaten the livelihood ofindividual members. The yearly
election of elders sustained a working reality ofdemocracy.

Everywhere the guilds had three levels of membership: master, journeyman and apprentice. This training ofnovice by master ensured that knowledge and skill were passed on in a
practical, non-theoretical way. Apprenticeship usually lasted
seven years, so that by 20-23 years of age an apprentice could
apply for master status. Each ofthe guilds had its meeting hall
and usually there were a number of workyards and buildings
where members could work together. They also facilitated bulk
purchases of material and could, by their name, secure future
deliveries. Tools were often handed down within the guild.

The relationship ofthe building guilds to the society and state
varied with place and time. In some instances, as in India, there
was a direct extension of the state bureaucracy with appointed
local power that included banking and judicial rights. Elsewhere, as in Florence, the guilds were an organization that
found itselfbetween the aristocracy and the general populace, a
position that made neutrality and stability difficult. In both
these instances, guild members were required by the state to
provide an armed militia in times ofneed. In Venice the guilds
were removed from the paths ofpower and there they developed
with less intrigue and disruption into the famed scuole.

Guilds were an interesting mix of secular practicality, religious fervour and egalitarian camaraderie. Saints, banquets
and parades were as much a part of guild life as were the materials they worked with. The origins of most guilds as religious
fraternities added an aspect of sanctity and mystery. The
builders guild of Beijing was commonly known as the ‘The
Sacred Society ofLu Pan’.

The brotherhood also looked after its members’ social welfare. The provision of pensions in old age, stipends for the
infirm, plus funds for proper burial were common. The building guilds undertook officially to monitor the work of members, thereby acting as their own building inspectors. Initiated
to maintain the standards of the craft, the inspection process
was open to irregular and self-serving judgements that weakened the reputation ofthe guild.

The craft defined by a building guild emerged from an earlier process of general popular building and this opened the
opportunity for refinement through specialization. Bound in
his oath to further the craft, a guild member was obliged to
advise a colleague ifan improvement could be suggested.

As skills became more complex through this system of
mutual aid, a strong sense of both the mastery and the mystery
of the craft emerged. Complex structural details were often
concealed and most guilds developed a dialect of their own to
keep craft secrets amongst themselves. Usually guilds discouraged emigration to protect their advantage, yet travel, often to
distantsites, was one ofthe important ways new ideas were disseminated. English carpenters travelling in Anatolia with the

387

## S. 144

— ELaOSTET

1.V PRODUCTION

Crusades and Greek masons rebuilding Jerusalem’s Church of
the Holy Sepulchre in 1801 are examples ofthis.

The reasons for the demise ofthis important social structure
are complex. At the root may be the fact that guilds were inherently conservative: in protecting their skills and their members,
they were unable to adapt to new social and technological realities. Mobility, industrialization, standardization, new materials and corporate entities were seen as threats to the existing
order. Inaddition, therise ofthe cultofindividual creativity was
difficult to accommodate in an order that championed collective well-being. Although almost universally replaced by new
building delivery systems, the echoes ofthe old building guilds
can still be found in some countries. In others, new guilds, like
the American Timber Framers, have recently formed to explore

opportunities in a post-industrial age. R.W. WALKEY

ıv-c Guilds: Urban (Belarus)

As far back as the 18th century all major buildings in
towns were designed in full, with the designs being passed to
local bodies for approval, and then they were constructed by
professional builders and designers. The higher the administrative level ofa town, the narrower was the sphere ofactivity of
folk builders. In the 16th to rgth centuries there were guilds of
builders in towns that organized the building craft and the
training in it, regulated the relations between builders and
defended the interests oflocal builders. For example, one ofthe
articles in the regulations ofthe craft union of masons and carpenters in Minsk (1636) specified big fines for those builders
coming either from foreign lands or from other, even neighbouring, towns who began working in the city and its environs
without permission. City dwellers who placed an order with
builders from elsewhere were also punished with a fine. Since in
big towns there lived many craftsmen in the building trade, it
was possible to organize the making of separate parts and elements ofa building, such as beams, boards, windows, doors and
even block houses. These prefabricated units would be bought,
removed to the intended site and put together, the finishing
work being carried out in accordance with the owner’s wishes.

The main types oftown building were a town hall, temples, a
row of stalls and detached shops, a special building where
standards for weight and linear measures were kept, taverns
and administrative buildings. All ofthem, as well as merchants’
dwelling houses including a shop, were built round the main
market square of a town, in the streets adjoining the square,
and along the streets (especially shops and taverns) thatwere a
continuation ofroads leading to the town. The town planning,
irrespective of the time the town came into existence and its
geographical environment, was such that all roads leading to
the town led to the market square. Closer to the town centre,
plots were smaller in size and housing density increased. As a
rule, a general plan was approved for each big town that
specified special places where industrial structures and storage
facilities were to be located. In the ıgth century the sphere of
activity of folk builders was limited to dwelling houses and
ancillary buildings in the town suburbs. Those constructions
usually developed the traditional planning, composition and
structural concepts of the local architecture. The planning
became complicated as a result ofthe increase in the number of
rooms and the connections between them. However, the functional zoning ofa dwelling became more distinct. At the same
time many buildings on the outskirts of towns were in no way
different from analogous buildings found in villages. On the
whole, the urban architecture was characterized by the active
development ofthose areas ofland that remained waste in villages, such as flood lands or steep slopes. Buildings, initially
dwelling houses of two or three storeys, were also typical of
urban architecture, and more attention was given to parts and
decorations. Constructions ofbrickand stone were widely used
in towns, and the use of timber as a walling material was
allowed only on the outskirts. SERGEI SERGACHEV

Brick bonds

All early civilizations that used brick learned the importance of 'breaking’ joints. Bricks of regular length that are
laid as ifstacked do not have a secure joint with adjacent bricks.
‘Bonds’, or ways oflaying bricks so thatthere are no coincident
vertical lines ofjoints thatwould cause cracking or wall failure,
are necessary. English bond has alternating courses of stretchers and headers. An alternative to this is to lay a course ofheaders on top ofa course of stretchers, the third and fifth courses
being stretchers, the fourth and sixth being headers.

English garden bond is a variation ofEnglish bond, consisting of three stretcher courses alternating with one header
course. Itis more economic ofthe higher quality bricks used on
the face and was commonly used for minor buildings. Other
variations are American, English cross and Dutch bonds, which
rely on complete courses of headers and stretchers but with
variations in the numbers of stretcher courses and in setting
outjoints, quoins and reveals respectively.

Flemish bond lacks the strength of English bond, but has a
more even texture and is more economic of expensive facing
bricks. In itstretchers and headers alternate in every course. Its
name as well as its popular usage in southeast England and
around the Bristol Channel reflects the influence of the Low
Countries on England in the 17th century, when the ostenta-

‚and tenon joint.

tious use of material was giving way to an appreciation ofform.
As with English bond, Flemish bond has given rise to variants,
Flemish garden, also known as Sussex or Scotch, Monk and
Old English bonds, all ofwhich generate regular patterns with
the headers. Flemish has gradually usurped English bond in
Europe as the most common technique. Chinese cross bond
uses a similar system ofthree stretchers and one header in each
course, with the header always centred on the middle stretcher.

Other bonds have developed and are suited to particular purposes. Rat-trap bond, in which the bricks are laid on edge,
leaves a gap in the middle, consequently being very economic
of bricks and is widespread throughout China. Quetta bond,
which creates vertical columns between the leaves, can be filled
with concrete and was developed to rebuild Quetta in Pakistan
after its devastation in an earthquake in 1935. Chinese bond, in
which stretcher courses of brick alternate with header courses
of brick on edge and with the joints of the stretcher courses
breaking with each other, creates an unusually rigid construction unequalled in the west, which is commonly used in China
to resist the onrush of water in flooding. Conversely, Chinese
box bond is used where there is no need to support a superstructure and is a form of walling economic ofbrick.

Many decorative forms of brick bond are possible, given
an understanding of the basic rules. Popular in Sweden and
Germany is the monk bond where each course consists of two
stretchers followed by a header; when this is repeated alongthe
row a ziggurat pattern emerges. When the course consists of
repeats of three stretchers and a header, however, the pattern
has subtle inversions. These decorative qualities are not always
easily perceived, but bricks come in many colours from grey
through yellows to deep Indian reds, and careful colour selection can be used to heighten patterns. Partially glazed or deeply
burntbricks also have textural or surface variety which a craftsman can exploit. Variants of diaper, cheyron or cruciform patterns can be obtained by such selection, but these can be
considerably extended by the use of bricks laid in geometric,
zigzag or diamond patterns, while cut and shaped bricks, or
blocks of stone or chalk permit a variety of basketwork patterns. Similar shapes are also exploited in decorative panels of
brick ‘nogging’ or infill oftimber framing.

Some bricks of a somewhat softer texture can be ‘rubbed’ to
permitaslighttaper, atechnique which enables brick voussoirs
to be made when constructing an arch, without depending on
thickening the mortar. Some bricks can be carved, permitting
the moulding of brick mullions and architraves, though ‘dogtooth’ and dentil courses could be achieved with angled bricks.
Where local traditions existed, as in Angliaand the Weald inthe
British Isles or in the Loire in France, itis possible to find examples of these techniques within the vernacular, but often the
skills ofthe bricklayer are best demonstrated on the houses of
the gentry or the church, who could afford the additional labour
and time costs involved. Nevertheless, the vernacular buildings
ofthe Netherlands or ofparts ofthe northeastern United States
can reveal innumerable felicities ofbricklaying skills, from ‘hit
and miss’ perforated walls of barns in the shapes ofbirds and
animals, to dates and initials set in coloured bricks.

DEIRDRE McDERMOTT and PAUL OLIVER

Techniques and Skills 17

1V7.b Carpentry

Carpentry is the craft involved in the framing up of
buildings themselves, leaving the specialized craftmanship
required for the preparation ofstaircases, wainscoting and furniture to the joiner, originally the maker of furniture.

In certain cultures carpenters were attributed a high status,
commensurate with their position in the hierarchy ofthe building
process. In Japan the carpenter is generally known as daiku, which
translates literally as ‘great craftsman’ or ‘chiefbuilder’, similar to
the Greek ‘architect’. The carpenter was only one of many craftsmen in the building process, from the sawyer who cutthe timber,
through masons, plasterers, tatami- and cabinet-makers, and
roofers. Carpenters, comprising one third ofallartisans, were the
mostnumerous, outnumbering the plasterers andtatami-makers
by five to one and the sawyers, masons and cabinet makers by ten
to one. Despite their numbers, their wages were maintained at
a high level by decree, reflecting the importance oftheir work.

The ‘way ofthe carpenter’ is described by Coaldrake as comprising four parts: the man, his materials, the craft or method
ofdesign and construction, and his tools. A carpenter’s IO-year
apprenticeship began atthe age of13, when adequate physical
and spiritual stature were believed to have been achieved and
for several years the apprentice was not permitted to use tools,
but had to observe how they were used by the master or his
assistants. Skills ofa more advanced nature were absorbed bya
process known as nusumi-geiko, ‘stolen lessons’. The essence of
the carpenter’s education was the mouldingoofthe person atthe
same time as the professional, before ‘the bones had hardened’
— the apprentices were often taught reading and calligraphy by
the master after the day’s work had finished.

The curriculum of the carpenter’s apprenticeship was
expressed in the most general ofterms, with no regulations in
manual instructions as might have been expected under a

A

PENIS

## S. 145

— —

1v PRODUCTION

shogunate which had embarked on systematic propagation of
neo-Confucian tenets of education in the ızth century. Those
carpenters that rose to the top of their profession, however;
achieved a high level ofliteracy from within the apprenticeship
system. .

Families of elite master-carpenters grew up as the preeminent architectural firms of the establishment and kept their
craft as a closely guarded secret transmitted orally. Only in the
later 16th century, when the need to train large numbers ofcarpenters accurately and rapidly arose as a result ofthe vast scale
ofthe Bdo and Nikko building projects, were written manuals
produced which contained both drawings and written descriptions ofthe kiwari proportions to be used. These manuals subsequently became the most important tools ofthe mind. From
the middle ofthe 17th century they could be printed using the
newiy popular woodblock printformatand were widely distributed amongst the ordinary carpenters of the towns and cities
and eventually throughout the regions.

"The most practical aspect ofthe way ofthe carpenter was also
the way of the tool, of the process of selecting timbers, determining their dimension, fashioning and assembling them.
Tools themselves were divided into four categories, reflecting
this process. There were the instruments or tools ofthe mind,
used to design the building, and tools ofthe hand, used to execute the design and to finish the building. The interaction
between the carpenter and his tools and between the tools and
the materials formed the basis fora complex setofcustoms and
conventions in family workshops, which were expressed in
special rites observed as part ofthe building process. This also
led to the creation ofa special set of ceremonial tools reserved
for ritual purposes.

The assembly of timbers was achieved by joints. As in the
western world, the two chief categories ofjoint to develop were
lap or scarf joints, designed to lengthen timbers, and angled
joints, joining edges to ends oftimber and designed to resist
withdrawal and compression. Hewitt notes the development
ofjoints from a study of carpentry in Essex and describes how
during the ı5th century, a very large number ofbuildings were
constructed, allowing carpentry to advance. During the ı6th
century, joints became highly efficient, although anachronisms
were found where inferior joints were used which were cheaper
to cut, a practice often the case in hidden work. This tendency
to save time perhaps reflects the priorities ofa western system
of market economy, not echoed in the east, where the spiritual
connection of the carpenter to his work was enshrined with a
religious significance over and above his craft and means of
survival. DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

Ceramic cut tiles (Morocco)

Zillij are fired and glazed tiles that are cut into
geometric pieces, each having a separate name, identified by
its form and shape. The craftsmen involved in the traditional
making of zillij are known as zlayjiyyah. Whereas the ceramic
cuttiles used in Islamic architecture in Syria, Iraq and Iran were
known as gishäni, the term zillij applies only in Morocco, where
increasing demand has ensured the continuity oftheir production and use in contemporary architectural projects.

1.V7.C

399

IInnweq Jewes zules &

A prominent feature ofthe decorative architectural arts, the
ceramic cuttiles are used to cover and embellish the interiors of
public and private domestic architecture in a variety ofgeometricand floral patterns. Some ofthe finestexamples ofthese may
be found in the schools (madrasah), mosques and palaces ofthe
cities of Andalucia in Spain and of Pes, Marrakech, Sale and
Tetouan in Morocco, covering floors, courtyards and the
enclosed spaces (e.g. arcades, reception halls and living areas,
bedrooms, bathrooms, passages and stairways), fountainsand
walls. In terms of architecture, both past and contemporary,
perhaps no other artin Morocco is as integrated on the conceptual and planning levels as is theartand design ofzillij.

Traditionally, the main centres of production in Morocco
werein thecities ofFs and Tetouan. Today, however, production

is limited to the new centres known as the ‘zilltj co-operatives’
based outside the city of Fes and the original traditional workshops ofthe old city (medina).

The primary discipline involved in the making of zillij tiles
involves the initial stages of purifyingand kneading the local clay
to the firing process, colouring and tile glazing. The laborious
phases ofthat process testify to the complexity ofthis traditional
craftwhich was based onadisciplinethatcombines the technical
procedures with the underlying conceptual framework and
design oftheart. The process ofmaking zillij in Tetouan and Fes
differs in the cutting stage of the smaller zillij forms. Those of
Tetouan are cut from the moist clay to the required geometric
shape ofthe zillij, fired, coloured and then glazed, whilse those

Morocco,
), Alanya,

‚Africa and

References!

Forester, H
Hewitt, Cecil

ofFes are only cutto the final zillij geometric shape from the fired
and glazed square tile (11.5 x ı1.5 cm [4.5 X 4.5 in]) atthe workshop or on the premises where they are to be applied.

Master-craftsmen work with a different set ofconfigurations
for each separate composition, relying on the geometric
concepts ofIslamic patterns. The value ofeach configuration is
further used to construct and identify the patterns, especially
since each composition is made up of hundreds of the small
zillij pieces, sometimes more, depending on the surface area.

Drawing was an essential skill practised by all zläyjiyyah.
This is evident from the leaf and floral (tawriq and tashjir)
designs and the arabesque patterns constructed with bold geometric lines, polygons and stars. The floral patterns are drawn
with the aid ofa stencil life-size on each of the square cut tiles.
Partial scraping ofthe glazed coat follows so thatthe rest ofthe
surface space reyeals the natural colour of the baked clay in
contrastto the coloured glazed tile with the interlaced forms. In
the geometric zillij patterns (tastir) the master-craftsman executes the design by cutting the tile into the smaller geometric
shapes. The overall pattern, unlike the floral designs in zillij, is
not drawn. The pieces are assembled on the floor back to front
on a pencilled grid of lines in a process known as ‘spreading’
(tafrish). Relying on the knowledge ofthe geometric composition stored in the memory, the specific geometrical guidelines
for each pattern are followed. SALMA SAMAR DAMLUJI
1.v.7.d Conservation techniques
Weather impacts buildings through rain, frost, radiation, expansion and contraction caused by heat and cold,
snow loading on roofs and walls, and wind. Normally, techniques for dealing with these are developed as part ofthe local
building technology; the job of maintenance involves merely
renewing them where they have failed. In some cases, however,
ageing or some other factors have made these traditional techniques inadequate, and then conservation is involved; the conserver has to draw on knowledge gained from experience
elsewhere or invent some new strategy to deal with the problem.

Among issues introduced by ageing are the long-term effects
of warping, expansion and contraction, dampness in walls
or woodwork, cumulative insect attack and so on. In many
cases they can only be dealt with by extensive dismantling
and rebuilding, replacing damaged material with new. A basic
principle of conservation generally accepted today is that
the replacement should be identical with the original to ensure
that the ageing process continues to be authentic and is not
distorted in any way.

Dampness in vernacular buildings frequently results from
inadequate waterproofing at roof level. A damp-proof membrane can sometimes be effectively introduced as part of
conservation strategies. The most inert material for this
purpose is lead, which, although costly, is still to be preferred
as most permanent and least likely to do damage. Other possible materials are copper, galvanized zinc, bituminous felts or
plastic. All may have associated problems which should be
carefully studied before they are used.

Another common source ofdampness is that rising from the

Techniques and Skills 1.7

ground, drawn up by capillary attraction in the wall materials.
Again, vernacular buildings seldom had a membrane layer
introduced into the structure to separate the main body ofthe
walls from the ground, as is common practice in modern building. The introduction ofsuch amembrane (the same materials
would be used as those referred to above for waterproofing
roofs) involves either cutting through the wall just above
ground level and inserting a short length ofthe waterproofing
material at a time, an expensive procedure, or else complete
reconstruction. The only alternative is the injection of penetrating compounds ofsilica or aluminium in the hope thattthey will
form a continuous waterproof layer, an uncertain procedure
because ofvarying densities of building material.

Pollution has an increasingly significant impact on maintenance and conservation. Scientific analysis has demonstrated
thatpoor sewage disposal results in an acidic content in groundwater which causes corrosion in foundations and lower walls.
The corrosive effects on building materials of sulphur and carbon in the atmosphere from fires and exhaust fumes from
machines using oil and petroleum have become an issue of
major concern; these chemicals, when mixed with rain, become
acids and are absorbed by dust particles which collect on the
buildings so that the materials beneath disintegrate. One strategy for dealingwith atmospheric corrosion is periodic washing
with water in a fine spray. Alternatively, various penetrating
compounds of silicon and aluminium may be used, both to
resist atmospheric as well as ground-water pollution. Use of
these is, however, uncertain in its eflicacy, and may result in
differential expansion and consequent spalling of surfaces. On
those stone and plaster surfaces which need to be conserved

rather than replaced, melted beeswax, sprayed or brushed lightly

Risks with use ofnew materials, including even concrete and
cement plasters, are that the materials carry with them, or may
later give off, chemicals capable of forming corrosive acids.

This is also true ofsome new types ofbricks and bonded wood
boards. Other new techniques, such as the use of synthetic
resins, are also approached by most conservers with caution.
The formation oftoo hard a surface or too hard ajoint in traditional materials may lead to their rapid disintegration through
differential expansion.

The maintenance and conservation of vegetable and earth
constructions is particularly difficult. Thatch and straw steadily
disintegrate ata fairly rapid speed. Techniques have been developed for lengthening their life, but these are only oftemporary
benefit. Reconstruction is generally the only way of ensuring
the survival ofthe original form and character. Earth construction is particularly vulnerable to rain, rising damp and insect

392

attack. Again, many techniques have been attempted for
lengthening the life ofearthen walls. Mosteither transform the
appearance unacceptably or accelerate deterioration in a new
way. Generally conservationists accept that some reconstruction is an inevitable aspect ofthe conservation ofearth and clay
structures. RONALD LEWCOCK
Craftsmanship (Tibet)
The traditional Tibetan building uses a rectilinear
plan and is constructed either of mud-brick or stone, with an
internal supporting system ofwood columns and beams. This
building methodology developed from the influence of
Achaemenid Persia and uses construction techniques found
throughout central Asia. The construction typology is based on
the availability and use of local materials. The teaching of
building skills utilizes an oral tradition and system oftraining
which is passed down through the generations, giving the
craftsman an important status in traditional Tibetan society.
While the exterior of the building is monolithic in nature, the
interior is layered both symbolically and structurally.
Foundation construction begins with the digging oftrenches
for the foundation walls. The earth in these trenches is first
compacted using the weight of large stones which are flung
into the trench. On rocky cliff sites the foundation is laid
directly onto the rock base to create a level footing for constructing the exterior walls. The foundation walls, important
both for load-bearing and for preventing damp from rising
from the subsoil, are built of a rubble stone and generally
vary from 45 cm (18 in) to go cm (36 in) in thickness. They
are constructed using dressed stones bonded together with
mud-mortar to build an inner and outer wall, with the space
between these being filled with loose stones and mortar.
Typically, the exterior walls ofall buildings are constructed of
mud-brick or stone, with a form of rammed or tamped earth
used in some areas. The shaping ofstones and the moulding of
mud-bricks are skilled jobs which were often contracted out to
local village experts. The fabrication ofthe exterior walls, also. a
specialized skill, is usually carried out by local stonemasons.
For larger buildings, such as temples, the building process is
often supervised by an experienced builder who co-ordinates
the work ofthe masons and carpenters.
The interior structure is made up of.a rectilinear system of
timber columns and beams, which are usually fabricated by
local carpenters. All these components receive special carving
and decorative details which highlight both the skills of the
local craftsman and the symbolic and structural role the components play. In the forested areas ofeeastern Tibet, Bhutan and
Sikkim windows and doors are constructed ina workshop area
set up at the construction site itself. In remote regions, where
materials or skills are less readily available, these components
are brought in from other areas. The prefabricated windows
and doors are put into their proper location in the walls and
then framed into place by the stonemasons.
Columns and beams are prepared using simple tools, such
as mallets, chisels, knives and saws. The columns are shaped in

1V.7.e

the top. This tenon fits into a mortise carved into the capital

which rests on top ofthe column. The main beam is tied into
the capital using wooden pegs. Interior partition walls are constructed using lighter timbers to form head and sill beams

with a third beam found at waist level. Vertical posts are

. The floors are made ofa series of layers which begin with the

which are held in place using wooden pegs. The joists support
a layer of smaller branches which carıy a covering of small
SIOnES; The stones provide the base for layers of mud-mortar
which are applied to form the floor surface. Fabricating the
mud floors isaless skilled, labour-intensive job, which is often
done in large groups to the singing ofa slow, rhythmic song.

Increasingly, these building methods are disappearing with
the pressure of scarce resources and changing cultural values
Except in Bhutan and remote areas of India and Tibet, wood
components have largely been replaced with eonefele, While
stone and mud-block walls are still used in remote areas, construction using a concrete frame with brick infill walls has

become more common. WILLIAM A.SEMPLE

1V.7.e-i Craftsmanship: transfer of skills (Malta)

As an example of the transfer of knowledge and
skills, Malta’s limeston e-workingtradition playeda crucial part
in providing for the defensive and ideological needs and lifestyle of its people and tuling elites, and favoured interaction
between the formal and vernacular building traditions. In an
otherwise gloomy assessment ofthe island’s potential as their
home base, the Knights of St John identified its harbours and
good building stone as environmental assets. Having settled
there, they founded new cities and built extensive fortification

Techniques and Skills 107

and infrastructural systems. The British appropriated themand
added their own. The experts they brought in recognized th
potential oflocal building skills and materials. “

Local craftsmen working for secular and ecclesiastical &lite
groups absorbed elements of the formal tradition, filtered
them through their consciousness and recycled them in the
work they did for other social groups. They even used its texts
Handed-down editions of Vignola’s Rules of the Five Orders of |

Architecture were the Prized stock-in-trade of illiterate master-

masons until the zoth century, and they would cite the work as

the authority for the design of specific architectural details

Stereotouny and projective geometry were learnt from high:

tradition Italian or French architects and military engineers,

one local master-mason entering folk ‚memory by piercin, 2
skew vault through a bastion, Vernacular building ae
were applied to the production of high-design elements: light
barrel vaults were built from modular roofing slabs and concealed rubble was piled on dome haunches to Dievenn them
from bursting outwards. Conversely, high-design forms and
new materials could be used to refine vernacular building systems and were then absorbed into the tradition: for instance, by
substituting three-centred diaphragm arches for semicitcnlar
Ones, or supporting roofslabs on steel instead oftimber beams
or masonty arches. The baroque high style was thus transmuted into an authentic popular tradition.

The transfer of forms and techniques from one tradition to
the ather generated its peculiar tensions. The island’s archives
contain critical reports on foreign architects’ designs by master-masons, as well as architects’ recorded complaints about
Improperly executed details. Tales of building failures attributable to their foreign designers’ ignorance oflocal methods and
materials survive in the folk memory: bursting aqueduct pipes
crushed foundations, eracking lintels, all eventually put Habt
by local experts. Buildings attributed to foreign architects by
recent scholarship were credited to the master-masons who
executed them by popular tradition. Painful though they may

have sometimes been, the interchanges between formal and
vernacular traditions in Malta do indicate that, given the right
environment, craftsmen can significantly modify traditions

before they hand them down to Posterity. JO TONNA

393

## S. 147

1V PRODUCTION

1.1.7. Inlay (Yemen)

Inlaid ornamentation in Yemen is exclusive to con-

struction with stone or earth blocks. The simplest inlays occur

in stone construction, when the decorative effect is gained from

colour differentiation. Thus, darker stone may be used in

arches, around openings, corners and strips in the wall alter-

nating with the predominant lighter stone. Simple geometric

patterns may be created by motifs contrasting with he: pre-

dominant tone ofthe wall, along floor or roof lines or in tym-

pana of entrance doors. Motorized transportation made it
possible to use stone of various colours from quarries across

country, in the same building, in bands, arches and isolated
geometric forms, a technique which has been widely adopted
since the 1970s, especially in the southern mountamns.
Regional individualizations were emerging at the beginning of
the 19908.

Inlaid work, however, is best represented by reliefs. Stone
reliefs may oversail the wall (e.g. in Thula), but the most
common, both in stone and fired brick, is the repetition ofan
element or group ofelements, setin a recessed panel or frieze
so that the relief effect is obtained with the outer face of the
patterns flush with the general wall plane. At its simplest, this
elementis asquare setas alozenge. As well as the overall effect
resulting from size and number of components, what multiplies its decorative possibilities and creates regional styles is
repetition, frequency, symmetry and extension, in panels,
along floor or rooflines and around openings. Regional forms
may also be characterized by the utilization of other types of
element - half circles, rosettes, dentils, prisms and so on —
combined with or replacing the basic lozenge. In the southern
highlands (Ibb) friezes consist of prisms and dentils; in the
southeast (Al Baydä) schist inlays feature triangular or ‘X
motifs deeply recessed in the wall. In Thula and its area of
influence, stone inlays are the most flamboyant, with a technique and thematic treatments that appear very close to brielework: enriched zigzag and diamond frets counterpointing with
the stone tracery offanlights.

Baked brick may be used alone or combined with other wall
materials. The best expression ofbrick inlays on brick walls is
reached in the Tihama and in San’ä. In the Tihama geometric
patterns and other forms, such as floral stylizations, may ornament the whole facades; in San’ä the basic conventions are
developed both in friezes and in panels and it is customary to
face the brick patterns with white plaster.

Brick inlays appear combined with stone walls in the area of
Dhawran and Dhamär, sometimes providing the only decoration or else sharing with stone inlays. In the area of Rada’ fired
brick is used in both fired brick and mud-block buildings to
frame the openings and in panels with variations on geometric
and calligraphic themes. The town of Dhamär concentrates
examples ofthe combined treatments given in the neighbouring areas (Radä, Dhawran and even San’ä). .

Machine-cut stone was established in the 1980s. Facing
stone provided the material for marquetiy-like versions oftraditional reliefs and experiments with different colours and new

designs.

1.7.8 Joinery (pre-1700)

Joinery, or the craft by which timber buildings were
assembled toresistliveand dead loads and stresses imposed on
them without the use of other materials, such as steel plates
and bolts, developed to its peak during the first halfof the second millennium in Britain.

The development of carpentry and joinery coincides with the
availability oftimber for building purposes. In Japan as timber
becomes scarcer, particularly the long timbers traditionally
needed in timber-framed construction, the craft of joinery
becomes more refined, with the development oftechniques to
join timbers together. In the European tradition, joints can be
categorized as ‘butt’ and ‘lap’ joints, classified by the way in
which they join, whereas in Japan they are classified according
to their function, as ‘splicing’ or ‘connecting’ joints (Seike).

In both cases joints fall into two main categories, those
which join timbers end to end, thereby enabling shorter timbers to be used, as in sole and wall plates, and those connecting
timbers across their axis, such as the crosslap joint, used to tie
beams to their supporting posts.

Cecil Hewitt, in a study of Essex carpentry between 1200 and
1700, notes that both scarf and lap joints developed to their
peak and deteriorated before the craft of carpentry itself
declined at the start of the 18th century, when the use of metal
connectors became philosophically acceptable and was promoted.

At its most basic, the edge-halved scarf joint, with vertical
butts, presented a means of linking two timbers together along
their length. This form of join, however, presented no resistance to the winding forces commonly set up in wall plates or to
extending stresses.

The splayed scarfjoint with stop ends and pegs driven from
the face (or best surface of the timber) enlarged the interface
between the timbers to bejoined. This provided a greatincrease
in strength, further achieved by the use of ‘squinted’, or
obliquely cut, butts.

Increased resistance to extension forces was enabled by the
introduction of.a ‘table’, or a raised, rectangular plane in the
scarf. Lateral resistance to winding forces was effected by the
development of butts which were ‘sallied’, or cut with an
obtuse-angled projection. The further introduction ofa lateral
wedge, or ‘key’, separated the twin tables and closed the under-

” age

ie u

Techniques and Skills 107
squinted butts with enormous pressure. Mechanically, this was
a highly efficient, if long, joint capable of resisting winding
forces as well as central sagging or hogging (tising).

A further development was a joint with bridled butts, which
was able to offer greater lateral resistance than its predecessors, apart from those with sallied, under-squinted butts. But
the evolution of edge-halved and bridled butts established a
form ofjointthatwas to prevail until the end ofthe rzth century.

Tying joints, used to connect timbers edge to length and
to be found connecting the main rooftimbers across their span
to the wall plate and their vertical supporting posts, were also
subject to evolution. By 1250 the lap-dovetail with entrant
shoulders had been developed, the purpose of the entrant
shoulders being to conceal the gap which would open up ifthe
lap withdrew after many years of strain. This joint persisted
until atleast 1485. The lap-dovetail joint, which became invariable for the remaining years of carpentry, was one where the
jowl tenon, linking the principal post to the tie-beam, was
moved off-centre, to one side of the dovetail, to avoid cutting
through the fibres comprising the narrow, root portion of the
lap. It did, however, require a high degree of accuracy of fit to
achieve the necessary withdrawal quality.

Floor joist joints and corner joists likewise went through a
process of evolution, reaching a zenith and declining into a
degraded form long before the 17008. Hewitt suggests that this

process of decline is due to the incessant desire of the craftsman to seek improved variants, not always with the intended

results. DEIRDRE McDERMOTT

EN INedO

## S. 148

1V PRODUCTION

1.\V.7.g-1 Joinery (post-1700)

There appears to have been little or no distinetion
between the work ofthe carpenter and thejoiner until the Tudor
period. Allwoodwork was undertaken bythe carpenter, but, with
the influence ofthe Renaissance and new standards in finish and
decoration, the carpenter became responsible for the structural
framing of the building, its roofs, walls and floors, while the
joiner was responsible for the lighter framing but higher degree
of finish of wall panelling, ceilings, staircases, wooden balustrades, doors and doorcases, window-frames and other details.

In 1678 Joseph Moxon, a contemporary of Sir Christopher

Wen, published an early part-work in monthly instalments, the
Mechanick Exercises, or the Doctrine ofHandywork. Moxon defined the
work ofthe ‘Joyner’ as ‘an Art Manual, whereby several pieces
of wood are so fitted and joined together by Straight Lines,
Squares, Miters or any Bevel, that they shall seem one intire
Piece’ (Moxon, in Briggs). Ajoiner was given freedom ofchoice
in working details: ‘You may (ifyou will) adorn the outer edges
ofthe Stiles and Rails with a small Molding: And you may (ifyou
will) Bevil away the outer edges ofthe Pannels, and leave a Table
in the middle ofthe Pannel.’

With the widespread adoption of classical details in monumental and elite architecture, similar details began to appear in
vernacular architecture. This was evident not only in Europe
but wherever European influence was felt, and where local
skills could be adapted to the classicisttaste. Filletand astragal,
torus and scotia, cyma recta and cyma reversa mouldings, derived
from Greek wood prototypes, were used by joiners for door
frames and cases, staircases and windows. French and Spanish
colonial, English and American Georgian, Adam and Greek
Revival styles, sustained by pattern books and manuals, dominated for a century and a half. They both made demands on
joiners and also perpetuated craft standards, but where climaticor economic considerations necessitated simpler treatment,
the shapes and profiles of classical details were often imitated
and applied without the mouldings. PAUL OLIVER

Oo Paul oliver

396

1.v.7.h Maintenance

Abuilding normally deteriorates because ofage, use
and the impact of weather. In northern Europe vernacular
buildings have been in use, atleast in part, forupto a thousand
years. Nevertheless, without continuous care or extensive
restoration the average life of a building is only two to three
hundred years. In drier climates this lifespan is much longer
(the early Pueblo ruins in the South West United States,
for example, were deserted at least five hundred years ago).
Where buildings were well-built ofstone, they have sometimes
been known to survive thousands of years (e.g. the prehistoric
temples of Malta and the Scottish brochs). In some cultures
buildings are ofsuch frail perishable materials and the technology so simple that their life is very short (e.g., Pacific and African
grass huts) and maintenance or conservation in the striet sense
is diffieult.

Protectingbuildings from annual rains, especial inmonsoon
areas, is a ritual in many vernacular architectures. In Gurunsi
compounds in Ghana roofs are annually rethatched with elephant grass which is specially grown for the purpose. In southern Arabia fine clay is spread over flat roofs just before the rain
falls; it swells when wet to make a waterproof membrane but
soon blows away when dıy. Elsewhere (e.g. in tropical Africa
and Asia) grass or palm thatch is replaced at roughly five-year
intervals. Reed thatch has a much longer life, lasting over forty
years on the leeside ofbuildings.

Grass or palm external walling needs to be renewed completely after only a short life, usually every 5-Io years. Mudplaster is replaced frequently, lasting only about five years in
the best circumstances, if it is at all exposed to rain. Its life
may be extended in wet areas by whitewashing it with lime.
With increasing labour costs, even in traditional societies, the

- maintenance and conservation of some vernacular architec-

tures is therefore becoming expensive. Research is being actively
pursued to lengthen the life of materials without prejudicing
their appearance and character.

External factors which might introduce new problems in
maintenance and conservation include major disasters, such as
earthquakes, hurricanes or typhoons, flooding, and the growing omnipresent conditions of pollution ofthe ground and air.
Many vernacular architectures incorporate earthquake-resistance construction in the form of wooden rings, which are
mortised and pegged and sometimes braced at the corners (in,
for instance, India, Arabia, Ethiopia, Turkey, the Aegean islands
and South America). Recent engineering research has confirmed the surprising efficacy of these. Providing that damage is
not extensive, maintenance can usually concentrate on renewing them. Hurricane-resistant construction is also practised
where necessary in many hazardous areas, such as Jamaica and
Fiji. Here the crucial factors are the tying down ofall floor and
roofconstructions and the exclusion ofwind from the interiors
through the use of heavy shutters, practices which need to be
borne in mind in maintenance and conservation work. In areas
Prone to flooding, such as the Ganges delta and East Asia generally, elaborate care is taken in the construction of substruc-

tures, and it is on these that maintenance and conservation
must particularly focus. RONALD LEWCOCK

Techniques and Skills 1.07

1.7. Paints and painting
Ofall forms of decoration on buildings painting is

the most commonly used, sometimes as a single colour over a
wall, sometimes with small features painted in greatdetail. The
techniques of painting do not vary greatly across the world, but
the degrees of artistry and skill are very different, depending
largely on the kinds of design sought and their respective
meanings. Often the colour palette is very limited; in a study of
colours in some 98 languages, one people, the Jale ofthe New
Guinea highlands, recognized only two colours, black and
white; many languages hada tripartite system with the addition
of red. Yellow or green was common in the Americas and
Africa, but six-colour term languages that included blue were
found only in Africa, Southern India and the Philippines.
Brown was rarely introduced unless it was in one of the languages which used from 8 to ıı colour terms, which together
accounted for a mere 20 per cent of the total: the range
consisted of white, black, red, green, yellow, blue, brown and
purple, extended in some cases with pink, orange and grey
(Berlin and Kay). Berlin and Kay listed indigenous terms and
their analogies when used for colour identification. They made
some reference to the symbolic or associative meanings of
colours and the use of loan words ftom European languages.
But the availability of pigments, which might have had a bearing on colour terminologies, was not examined.

Dyes and stains have been obtained for millennia from plants,
or from the secretions ofinsects, asin the case with kermes used
in the eastern Mediterranean and cochineal, used in Armenia.,
Dyes are soluble in water and are absorbed bythe material; hence
their use in textiles. Some that have been used for painting on
wood include the so-called ‘dragon’s blood’, made from rattan
resin and used in Southeast Asia. Saffron yellow was obtained in
Anatolia from the flowers ofthe autumn crocus, while sumach
produced a similar yellow in Persia. Dyes are generally ‘transparent’ and frequently ‘fugitive’, unstable or fadingin thelight,
and are therefore unusable on exteriors (Bentley).

All colours used in painting bind a Pigment, generally in the
form ofa powder, with amedium which enables itto adhere to
the ‘ground’ or surface. Amongthe most common ofmediaare
animal fats and products such as milk, egg-whites, honey or
blood. Urine and beer can also be used as emulsifying agents
though they have less opacity. Drying oils, or glycerides, which
form a tough skin, extracted from linseed, poppy seed and
walnut, became popular in Europe and the Middle East. Thenondıying oils like olive and castor oils are unusable, but balsams
and resins from conifers may be used where available. Size
glues made from boiling animal hoofs and skin adhere firmly,
and are used on wood panels as a barrier against ‘sinking’
colours; both sides are sized before being painted to counter
warping. If fine work or gold leaf is to be applied, as in some
temple buildings, a ‘gesso ground’ of fine plaster and size is
laid over wood and burnished before painting.

A skilled painter learns to use different media and to mix
them with the dried and powdered pigments; obtaining these
may also be part of his training. Blacks obtained from animal
carbon or charcoal are opaque, cover well and are permanent.
Whites may be obtained from lime or shell, while dull yellows

397

## S. 149

1.V PRODUCTION

and ochres come from earths. The earth colours, including the
siennas and the umbers, which can be calcined or ‘burnt’ to
make red-brown hues, are obtained from ferruginous deposits
in the soil. Traditional pigments persist in theremoter regions:
the Mountain-Ok of New Guinea used red ochre or ferrous
deposits in water seepages, burning it on hot embers to intensify the colour. Yellow ochre and a white chalk are found locally
and black is gathered from roofsoot and mixed with saliva. Paints
are often mixed in shells and applied with the fingers (Craig).

Minerals such as the green and blue ores of copper have
yielded their colours since neolithic times. Verdigris was
obtained in the pre-Christian era by dissolving the ore in vinegar; orpiment (yellow arsenic sulphide) was imported from
Persia, while lead was heated with basic lead carbonate to produce red lead. In medieval Europe the Painters’ Guilds held on
to their secrets, but chemical experiments and the commercialization of the colour trades in the ı8th and ıgth centuries
ensured that a wide variety of paints was available. Chinese
colourmen had also experimented, for example by produeing
vermilion from mercury. Chinese migrants introduced the
paints to Southeast Asia.

So in Bali, where the sanggang guild paints ceremonial hangings, wooden altarpieces and trapezoidal roof panels, the
favoured vermilion is imported from China. But white is made
from burnt and pulverized pig skulls, black from lamp soot,
blue from the indigo plant and yellow from an ochre mixed
with rice-flour. The drawing is done first with charcoal and
then with a bamboo pen dipped in soot-water; finely ground
pigments are mixed with fish-glue and thickened with lime
before the painting is done with a bamboo brush to the satisfaction ofthe perbekel, the head ofthe sanggang (Ramseyer).

PAUL OLIVER

1.V.7J Plaiting: sisal (Ethiopia)

Dasse is a stiff'mat which forms the principal cover or

thatch ofa house, giving it its name, maan-dasse, the ‘stiff-mat

covered hut’. The dasse is plaited by women from sisal fibre

(aloe) and two types of grass, durbi and sericha, found in Dirre.

The plaiting, or weaving, gives a dark brown external weave but
a patterned internal one of red, black and white decoration.

Artistically its interior is decorated with geometric pattern on

the mats. As these mats are tightly plaited they are waterproof
and durable. The length ofthese mats can be between 6 m (20 ft)
and ıo m (33 ft) and sometimes larger. The average time
required to plaitone can be anywhere between 2 and 12 months,
depending on the time devoted to the task and also on the
length ofthe mat. Once the houses poles have been set up the
stiff mats are put on the structure and the work of thatching
(ewissa) begins. This does nottake very long. Thatchinga house
with dasse starts at the bottom of the house and continues
upwards until the roof is covered. For this work a woman
requires awooden (barcuma) stool to stand on. The grassy part
ofthe dasse is on the exterior side, hanging down from the roof,
preventing the grass fibres from dropping into the house. The
function of the grassy fibres is to stop rainwater and strong
wind from entering the interior ofthe house. The dasse is kept in
place by being fastened to the framework of the house by

398

means of short extension ropes of dasse (gafa-dasse, ‘horn ofthe
stiffF mat’). These extension ropes at both ends ofan upper part
of the stiff mat serve to prevent them from slipping off the
house frames. Thatching requires the assistance of another
woman. Once this work is completed the house can be used for
living in. KASSA N. GETACHEW
1.V.7.K Stained glass (Yemen)

Fanlights consisting of screens made of coloured

glass set on gypsum tracery (known in and around San’ä as

takhrim (lace, embroidery’) can be seen anywhere in Yemen,

although the earliest and best examples are in the central high-

lands, from Al Tawila to San’ä. The proportions of the takhrim

reflect, particularly in the oldest examples, those setby the dou-

ble or multiple perforation model of the alabaster fanlights

(gamaria). Older examples associate alabaster with coloured

glass and tracery, but total substitution became noticeable par-

ticularly after the 1960.

The construction oftakhrim is traditionally the responsibility
of the mugassis, the master plaster-carver. The materials and
tools required are plaster, a board on which the screen is made,
compass, trowel, knife, hammer and a glass-cutter. The plaster
isspread on the board, smoothed and left to harden enough for
a pattern to be cut out with a knife. Glass is cut slightly larger
than the voids, so that the edges lie on top ofthe tracery. Liquid
plaster is sprinkled to fix the glass to the frame, followed by
heavier plaster covering the whole. The areas over the glass are
cut outby setting the piece against the light. The screen is fitted
to the intended opening with plaster.

A single panel is usual in most provincial examples and in
fanlights above interior doors between rooms and hallways.
More refined versions have a hollow or plain glass outer screen
and an inner screen (with the coloured glass) with a different
pattern. In many cases the outer panel is an alabaster plate, creating a softer light; otherwise, to reduce glare, a piece of white

cloth is often stretched between screens.

The patterns oftakhrim follow a great variety ofmodels, from
minute designs and bright colours to wider glass panes and
lighter tones, from thin tracery enhancing the colour effect to

jers at work
skirts of Delhi,
See also
1.VIIL5.h Woven al
it, suillemette
981
8, and Lewald (ed.),1983
jando, 1982

1.V11.5-B Surfac
1.V11.5.a Colour; F

Bentley, K.W,19
