Aal
sand stone

Stained glassf
(takhrim) atD:
Wadi Dar, Y

emphasis on the plasterwork itself, with glass being a mere
filling. Calligraphy occurs, but the patterns are mainly geomettic. Noticeably since the 1960s, figurative models have been
added to the conventions adopted by the master plasterers.
That is the case, also seen in other instances of decorative
figuration, ofthe themes broughtaboutbythe Republican Revolution (e.g. the republican eagle, aircraft and weaponty), by
returned emigrants (such as stylizations of mosques, and the
Saudi flag) and other unsophisticated attempts at naturalistic
representation of, for instance, flowers, birds and trees.

The simplicity ofthe technique, the low cost and availability
of the raw materials, allied to its showy results, made takhrim
an expanding business in the construction boom that followed
the late 19708. A great diversity of forms, including complex
arches, circles, squares and octagons, have been created to satisfy the demands of new decorative proposals. At that time
takhrim also became used as a decorative atavism in interiors
ofprestigious buildings like San’ä airportand large hotels and,
more domestically, in situations such as parapets of balconies.

FERNANDO VARANDA
1V.7.| Stone carving
Normally softer stones are used for carving, granites and hard sandstones less so. Stone with a uniform texture
and hardness throughout is preferred and the finer the grain
the less chance ofdisturbing the minerals ofwhich the stone is
composed. Coarse-grained stone, owing to its rough texture
and the size ofits particles, is not suitable for delicate carving.

Carving on stone involves cutting and shaping, mostly freehand, and is a surface finish. In its more elaborated form it
requires the skill of a sculptor. It is in a sense stone dressing
and the same or similar tools are used. However less attention
to geometric tolerances to obtain an even surface is required
than in dressing, as the artistic or decorative effect is predominant. The scale of execution is taken into account. Small carvings are usually placed at a height close to the eye of the
observer, asata more distantrange delicate carving may lose its
effectiveness and details are lost.

Except for carving serving a particular purpose, such as
gargoyles, carving is intended to embellish the masonry work
while at the same time bringing out the colour and texture ofa
stone, especially with the darker stones where the contrast
between the carved surface and the plain surface is more distinct.
Carved ornaments are utilized for plain or moulded courses,
capitals and bases of columns, pilasters, balustrades, friezes
and panels. High or low reliefs may be used, or a combination
ofboth, in the same masonry unit.

Slate carving is very popular in Bhutan for engraving the
images of Bhagwan Buddha and other dieties. In Nepal even
modest dwellings are adorned by one or more pieces of elaborate stone carvings.

Carving is done on building stone and on mouldings. Usually small pieces are carved in a stone yard or workshop,
whereas the larger masonıy units are worked either near the
building site to minimize risks during transportation or in situ
with the stone set in place. The facade can be carved after
the masonry has been completed. Originally all carving was

Techniques and Skills 107

JE ned

handeut, but nowadays simple mechanical or pattern devices
assist in the work, especially in the carving of lettering and
repetitive patterns. More elaborate carving is done from fullsized drawings or from models; in some cases the drawing is
copied on to the material to be carved. Ornaments are roughly
blocked out bya point-chisel or other type ofchisel, very much
as in dressing.

Columns can be cut entirely by hand and, more often than
not, the columns are partly shaped at the quarry. Simple centre
lathes are used to turn the embryo column, which facilitates
both cutting and carving. The stone is held between a headstock and tailstock and revolved against a cutting tool inserted
into a toolpost. After it has been pared down to the required
size, fluting and other finishings are obtained by various hand
tools. For load-bearing columns it is important to observe the
natural bedding direction when cutting the drums and to
ensure that the final position is on-bed. Small columns and
balusters are used in the Bethlehem area in Judaea where several workshops specialize in crafting them, to a great extent for
use in private residences. ASHER SHADMON
1.V.7.Hi Stone dressing and trimming
‘Dressing’ describes a way of preparing stone; the
term applies to any kind or part of finished stonework, includingthe back and sides, whether in adıy stone wall or in walling.
It should not be confused with ‘dressings’ which is a general
term for all masonry that is worked to a finished ‘face’ and used
for decorative features, for example at corners and windows.
Similarly, ‘trimming’ implies shaping the stone, whereas ‘trimmings’ refers to all regularly cut stone not in the form ofashlar
or building stones. These include lintels, sills, caps, mouldings, coping, columns and entablatures (such as cornices,
friezes and architraves), stone steps and arches.

In some parts oftthe world with woodworking traditions the
mortise and tenon techniques ofthe woodworker are apparent,
especially in dry stonework, whether used for fencing or to
secure lintels to jambs.

Dressing and trimming are part of stone cutting and finishing - formal or vernacular. With the advent of easily available
and leasable stoneworking equipment much of the handwork
is assisted by mobile pneumatic tools and machines in ‘do-ityourself’ or self-help building projects.

399

## S. 150

1.V PRODUCTION

Stone can be worked by the inexperienced and the skilled
alike. Dressingincludes breaking, shaping, fragmenting, splitting and cleaving, bruising, flaking, abrading and polishing.
Some ofthese terms refer to shaping and sizing, others to subdivision and the removal of surplus material. Most textures on
stone are achieved by bruising, spalling, flaking, or by splitting
using a hammer or chisel. The stone is bruised, the dust
brushed off the surface and bruising is continued. Similarly,
flaking and splitting are repeated to achieve the desired effect.

Stone is brittle at low pressures. A stress field of great concentration near its surface will cause spalling because of the
predominantly non-homogeneous nature of stone. Spalling is
the basis of stone dressing, and requires comparatively little
energy; it acts by causing instability, using the energy released
by loss ofcohesion to enlarge the break.

Stoneworking involves impact from the hammer and the cutting edge ofthe chisel. These tools are sometimes combined in
cutting-edged hammers.

Dressing types include rock face or pitch face and pointed
work which can be rough-pointed or fine-pointed. Sometimes
margins in various finishes are left on the edges. The dressing
type is often named after the tool used, the names differing
from country to country and even within regions. Usage also
changes periodically. Tools have been adaptedto localresources.
In the volcanic tufa belt ofBulacam, Luzon, in the Philippines,
the predra adobo is the main building material andthetools used
include a pick with ends pointing upwards (pico) and a blunt
pick (bangatagat).

Various finishes can be given, including parallel grooves —
tooled, boasted or droved work - which may be 6-cut, 8-cut or
10-cut, accordingto thenumber ofgroovedtoolmarksto the inch
(or other units). Hand-droved work is somewhat more irregular,
possibly with a more pleasing effect than the even appearance of
machine work. Itis a popular finish especially on the widely used
blue-grey limestone in the Soignies area in Belgium.

Rubbed work gives a smooth finish and ranges from even
texture to ahoned finish, usually where the stoneis fine grained.
For bush-hammering the stoneis first dressed to a fairly smooth
surface and then finished with a bush-hammer in various degrees

400

of fineness, depending on the thinness of blades in the scotia
hammer orthenumber ofpoints on a solid-tooth bush-hammer.

The quality ofdressingdepends on the use ofthe tool. Careful
pounding with a hammer to avoid bruising ofthe stone results
in the survival of the high quality of stonework and its resistance to damage by weathering.

“Vermiculated’ work implies aworm-eaten appearance, whereas ‘rusticated work’ refers to fluting between courses and, less
frequently, between joints forming a sunken, dressed margin.

Building stones are often produced as a by-product during
the extraction and processing of marble and finished on the
building site. Advantage is taken of the primary shaping by
machine, and complete villages and hamlets have been constructed from stone processing plant waste. In some instances
the slurry from the stone sawing is used as a mortar, for example at Kota in Rajasthan.

This is particularly true for regions where slabby stone isquarried from thin layers, and used to face walls and fences. In vernacular practices effortis made to use parallel-side slab material.
Thicker varieties are used in pavements, for example, and are
known as pavers and flagstones. When the stone comes from
fairly parallel, well-bedded layers, the natural bed is often used
with no further treatment other than the cleaning and remoyal
ofdust ora clay-like skin, which weakens the mortar bond.

Rubble is shaped irregularly and the stones are roughly
trimmed to fit against each other in a wall. Applications are in
several forms: polygonal, courses, fieldstone and random. Itis
often used in combination with ashlar as backing or filling
between quoins. Particularly fine, tight-fitting polygonal stonework is used in places as far apart as Carlos La Paz in Argentina
andin retaining walls atKyöto inJapan.. ASHER SHADMON

1Y.7.\-ii Stone: mortared joints

Mortar comprises a mixture of binder and filler,
sufficiently damp to be spread between stones or bricks, both
to provide strength in the wall and to reduce the irregularities
of the building material. Traditionally, the binder, the sticky
element ofthe mix, has been lime and the filler (the bulk) sand
(or earth) and aggregate. Mortar must be softenough to spread
yet dry enough not to squeeze out excessively under the weight
ofthe building blocks. Following local traditions, some mortar
joints were decorated and packed with small pebbles, oyster
shells, broken tiles, slates and so on. Since the later ıgth century,
harder, impermeable mortars made with cement have been
more frequently used. Whilst compatible with high-fired, hard
bricks and concrete blocks, they have caused damage to older
buildings when used indiscriminately.

Different building materials require different types of mortar. An irregular rubblestone wall will have joints of varying
thickness, so a coarse aggregate will help the mortar to set in
the thicker areas. The finest joints between dressed stones or
rubbed bricks may be so thin that only asmear oflime is used.

‚Although there are many types of mortar, the general principle is that the mix, when set, should be marginally softer and
slightly more porous than the building block. Thus, any damage
(e.g. through weathering, rising damp or frost) is restricted to
the mortar joints which may be replaced periodically, without

Masons smoogpj
surface ofsj;

hammersanggı
edged chiseig,

Pokhara, Nepal,

disturbance to the stone, by repointing. A well-judged mortar
will erode naturally with the stone, continuing to protect it by
drawing out and evaporating damp from the wall. Where an
excessively hard mortar has been used with a soft stone, considerable damage is seen especially where frost action affects
the face ofthe stone causing spalling. Eventually, the mortar is
left standing proud ofthe face ofthe wall effectively providing
‘shelves’ where water can collect, thus accelerating the damage
further.

Repointing a wall can protect stones from further decay, but
the same principle applies as in the building mortar. If the
joints are correctly raked outand the mortar well packed to the
original joint width, the overall character of the stone is still
apparent, although the face ofthe mortar is by necessity slightly
inset. Where new pointing is brought out flush with the face of
eroded blocks, the joints are visually greatly widened and may
become dominant. Wherea hard inappropriate mortar is used in
this way, the physical erosion ofthe blocks is again accelerated.

The widespread use of cement in place of lime and locally
occurring materials has been to the detriment ofthe vernacular
tradition. JANE SCHOFIELD
1\V.7.m Toolkit

Vernacular builders frequently possess a range of
tools and develop diverse skills in the use ofthem. Tools may be
classified by the materials from which they are made, such as
stone, bamboo, wood or iron, the action required to operate
them - striking, drawing, driving, impressing — the nature of
the active surface, whether impact, edge or toothed, orthe task
for which they are used: dimensioning, cutting, impelling. Yet,
however they are classified, in practice itis the appropriateness
ofthe tools to the task in hand and the skill ofthe craftsperson
in using them to achieve the desired results that matters. With
a mallet, saw, adze, knife and abrasive stone a woodworker
could make most of the articles he required - including other
tools. An auger, bow-drill, brace and bits could complement
the basic tool kit, but the possession of chisels of different
gauges and edge shapes, a smoothing plane and a vice could
greatly extend the range and quality ofthe product. These were

Techniques and Skills 17

the stock-in-trade ofthe jobbing and journeymen carpenters in
all parts of Europe. Behind them came the knife-grinders who
set up their barrow-styled grinding wheels on street corners.

The traditional notched log ‘cabins’ of North America are
popularly believed to have been fashioned solely with a broadaxe. But the builders have been shown to have possessed a full
set oftools including felling- and broad-axes; froe and shingle
cutter; \hip; crosscut, hand and tenon saws; plumb line, maul;
mitre and framing square; marking gauge and scratch awl;
level; draw-knife,; smoothing jack, jointer and rebate planes;
awl; auger; gimlet; brace and bits; and paring and mortise chisels, as wellas vice, sawingand shingling horses, whetstone and
files for sharpening tools (Roberts). Apart from the wide range
oftools that were required for building the log house the most
notable aspect of the list is its remarkable similarity to the tool
kit ofa carpenter oftwo millenniaago, all butthe brace and bits
being traceable to Roman prototypes.

With growing sophistication and refinement of quality and
objectives the number oftools grows, though they may be variants ofa standard repertoire, as is the case in Japan where stone
tools were still in use until the introduction of metal tools from
mainland Asia in the zrd century AD. Many advances in building technology were concomitant with metal tools, including
houses with raised floors. A tool kit in a 5th-century burial
chamber excavated in 1978 produced 48 chisels, 42 gimlets, 25
planing knives, as well as small crosscut saws, adzes and axes.
It is possible that they may have had ritual significance if the
‘technology conferred on the governing elite the power to
demonstrate its authority with built monuments’ (Coaldrake).

The diffusion of metal tools was considerably advanced with
1gth-century colonialism. In the early r880s the tools of the
Andaman Islanders included stone hammer and anvil, gyrena
mollusc shells for sharpening cane and bamboo knives, and
boars’ tusks for cutting thatch, for cord-marking and as a form
of plane. Twenty years later, in the neighbouring Nicobars,
Kloss noted that ‘tools of European model are now common ...
for fashioning canoes, houses etc’ (Kloss). Traditional houses
were still being built a few years after when iron was beingused
for adze blades, though boars’ tusks and gyrena shell were still
favoured for fine work (Radcliffe-Brown).

Ifthe Andamanese found the transition painless, others did
not. A classic study revealed how the aboriginal culture ofthe
Yir Yoront was affected in the 19308 by the introduction of steel
axes. Not only did stone axes cease to be used, ancient trading
partnerships and the dependency of women and boys on the
men ended, ancient beliefs were undermined as steel axes had
no mythical, totemic value and the Yir Yoront culture was
irreparably impaired (Sharp).

Much more needs to be known ofthe tool kits used by cultures throughout the world. Those employed on traditional
Thai houses in the 19908 represent continuity, similarity and
local inventiveness, includinga plummet for establishing verticals, alength ofhosepipe or bamboo used as a spirit levelwhen
filled with water, atao or marking string with coloured dye, the
sarm-kloer or timber block tamp with vertical handles, the gob
(plane), chisel (siew), and hand-drill (swarn) (Tulayasuk and
Varavarn). The classification made by Adrian de Mortilletin the

401

## S. 151

u 2%

1V PRODUCTION

late-ıgth century still applies:

1 edge tools for cutting which were worked by pressure, such as
knives, shears or planes, or by shock, such as axes, adzes or
chisels, and by friction, as were saws.

2 abrasive and smoothing tools worked by pressure and friction,
such as rasps, files and whetstones, or by shock, notably
bush-hammers.

3 tools for fracturing, crushing and pounding worked by
Pressure, such as chipping and flaking implements, by shock,
like hammers and pestles, or by friction, as in the case of
grinders and mills.

4 perforating tools worked by pressure and friction, such as awls
and drills, or by shock, as are punches and picks.

5 grasping tools, including tongs, pincers and vices, and such
joining tools as nails, lashings and glues (Mortillet, in Mason).

PAUL OLIVER

Tools: stone

The choice of the various tool types for working
stone depends very much on local usage; which types of hammer are suitable, for instance, depends on whether the mason
stands or sits when he works, his stance or sitting position, and
the height ofthe bench or support. The importance of matching the right weight of hammer to the workpiece is as important as choosing the correct size of handle. Similarly, the
appropriate length for the chisel depends on the working position and the width partly on the hardness ofthe stone.

Hand tools currently used for dressing are essentially the
same as the tools used centuries ago. The conclusion thatthese
tools have been more or less continuously used has been
reached after careful comparison between present-day practices and masonıy dressings found in excavations.

The sledge-hammer is an important, all-round tool, and a
spalling or chisel-edge type is generally used, the weight
depending on the size of stones to be cut. Fairly straight edges
and sides are obtained by carefully judging the proper cutting
position ata certain distance from the stone edge.

A bull-set or walling-hammer is used for subdivision of the
stone into units and the shaping of rough forms. The sledgehammer can also be used with the jumper where no power drill
is available. Masons have various preferences for hammer patterns, based mainly on traditional rather than technological
considerations.

The breaking of blocks of stone bigger than can be handled
by sledge-hammer requires a compressor with a jack-hammer
or power drills in conjunction with plugs and feathers, or just
wedges. The stone block is split along straightlines, then again
subdivided with the help of shorter wedges or by sledgehammer.

The hammer is the stoneworker’s most important tool, and
the ability to strike accurately with the minimum of effort is
important. A hammer much used by masons is the Jump- or
elub-hammer which weighs about ı kg (2.2 Ib), with a head
made ftom medium carbon alloy steel. The weights for stonedressing hammers are generally between kg (2.2 1b) and6kg
(13.2 lb), although some sledge-hammers which are used to
break large rock pieces may weigh up to ıo kg (22 Ib).

1..7.m-i

402

Straight breaks can be obtained by moving a chisel or pointedged hammer back and forth along the line where the break is
desired, and pounding with another smaller hammer. A similar
technique is used to trim hard and irregular stones to size,
where blows with a single hammer will straighten the edge.

Breaking of stone can be facilitated by lighting a fire on
top ofthe stone and throwing water on the hotashes. This firesetting tends to fracture the stone which can then be broken
easily. The practice has currently been observed on the island of
Romblon in the Philippines and in India.

The chisel is next in importance to the hammer. Rudimentary
chisels are improvised from large nails or odd pieces of steel,
especially flat steel springs or shafts. Tungsten carbide tips
prolong the life ofthe edge. Care must be taken when using a
chisel, as bruising may encourage subsequent exfoliation,
although excessive or continuous decay is unlikelyto result. The
application ofover-heavy pressure during dressing may give an
effect similar to that ofa blunt chisel. Various chisel types are
used in the formation ofa plane surface on a rough block.

First the stone is squared, the roughest protrusions being
trimmed off’by the pitching tool which is hit hard against the
workpiece and also by a punch which is held ata shallow angle
against the stone, making parallel cuts down the surface. A
smooth plane is then started by having straight draftworked on
the end of the face with claw chisels, which are gradually
exchanged for those with finer claw edges.

The point, or punch, is essential to rough-finish stone, and is
usually 20-25 cm (8-10 in) long. The punch isa heavier version
ofthe point. The stock is hexagonal in section, and ground toa
four-sided, blunt (occasionally sharp) point. These chisels are
used in the preliminary stages of smoothing rough stone by

concentrating the hammering force to shatterthe stone locally.

o
2
3

Coaldrake, Willamn
Kloss, €. Boden,

Tulayasuk, Suntho

in Israel; left,
hammer; righ
toothedandtei

top, matrakahi

usedin preparing

TERERE

H

en
En

The standard-edge chisel is similar to the cold chisel used

in metalworking. The bolster has a bit width of 50-75 mm
(2-3 in), and is used to chisel wide surfaces. The bit is relatively
short and a cutting level is ground on both sides. It is used to
smooth wide, flat surfaces or to split building stone, and it has
the advantage that the wide blade transmits the energy of the
hammer-blow laterally rather than ata simple point. The tooler
has a wider bit and heavier stock.

Claw chisels, or scutches, come in various widths (25, 37 and
5o mm [1, 1.5 and 2 in]) and teeth spacings, sometime with
holders to use the different types ofcclaw edges. Their main use
is to prepare flat surfaces by using claw edges of various
fineness. The claw chisel is used after the point chisel to reduce
the uneven surface into a series of shallow furrows for further
finishing with the mason’s chisel.

The chisel is worked diagonally across the surface away from
the edge. Itis heldata 45° angle and driven with awooden mallet; the cuts are slightly overlapped. The finalsmoothingis then
performed with the mason’s chisel before the surface is ground
down by abrasive means.

In addition to chisels which are mainly used for working
stone surfaces, several tools are available for functions such as
edge preparation and fluuting. The pitch, set or drafting tool is
like the bolster, butis single-ground atassteep angleand is used
to remove excess material, especiallysandstone. The pitch does
not cut but fractures the stone; a club-hammer of 1.1-1.4 kg
(2.5-3 lb) is usually used.

The waste is cut away gradually, about 25 mm (1 in) ata time
from limestone, and 37 mm (1.5 in) from softer sandstone, by
holding the cutting edge parallel to the line at just less than
right-angles to the stone face. The pitching chisel is also used
for rock-faced or pitch-faced work, and the degree of rockfacing depends on the angle at which the chisel is held.

High-quality work shows no tool marks on the finished surface ofthe stone. This is achieved by gripping the chisel near
the head to prevent wavering and pressing firmly against the

workpiece, while watching the chisel pointand notthe head.

Techniques and Skills 107

The plugging chisel, or seam drill, has along, fat skewed bit,
often fluted. Itis used to cut into a stone surface, to fix plugs for
example. A cape, or crosscut, chisel has a deep wedge-shaped
bit, narrower than the hexagonal stock, with a slight flair at the
tip,and is ground on the upper and lower faces to an angle of 60°.

Some types of stone require special tools; for example, the
edges of slate are trimmed with sharp choppers.

ASHER SHADMON

1V.7.n Wood-carving

Cultures that use wood as a construction material do
notnecessarily carve it; but, given timber, time and tools, carving
is often to be found in regions with awood technology. Unlike
carpenters and joiners the wood carver conceptualizes his work
in terms of motif and figure rather than in terms of stresses,
loads and good fit. Some carpenters and joiners are wood
carvers, too, finishinga building with a carved finial or whittling
a handle for a door. Generally, however, the wood caıver has
other functions, creating decorative details, carving symbolic
motifs or making static building elements seemingly animate.
The technique ofthe carpenter may beadmired, butthe creative
skills ofthe wood carver are often perceived with awe or fear.

Modelling in clay or plaster is an additive process, the mod-

eller or sculptor ‘building up’ the forms until they correspond
to his mental concept. Lengths of rolled clay may be pushed,
pressed and extruded into limbs but the removal of mistakes
and substitution of forms present no great difficulties to the
modeller (Beier). Carving is the reverse, a ‘waste’ process
where unwanted material is cut away. Conceptually it is more
difficult, for it requires that the sculptor visualize the potential
completed work within the block, finding and releasing the
forms in the process of removing wood. Often the form of a
branch or the position of knots and smaller branches may
themselves be the inspiration for formal invention, though in
many cultures the carver is obliged to keep within striet conventions of configuration. These made it possible for up to 20
Angami Naga men, apparently with no leader, to participate in
the carving ofan immense gate (Archer).

Naga wood carving is undertaken for anumber of purposes,
including the making of dishes, husking tables or ornaments,
which any male may do. But ‘grand-scale carving of houseposts, grave effigies, gates and log-gongs are more likely to be
done by specialists’. The tools used ‘are simple: the dao, handdrill, adze and chisel’ (Jacobs). In many parts of the world
various knives correspond to the foot-long, all-purpose,
wedge-shaped dao. With the blade at an acute angle to the haft,
the adze is the key implement in wood carving, being used with
great dexterity and subtlety by woodworkers from West Africa
to Oceania. Often sitting astride his work, the carver lightly
strikes the blade down and towards him. With deft strokes an
uneven surface can be levelled and with a draw-knife the irregularities can, in effect, be planed away. A sharkskin or rough
leaves, an abrasive pumice stone or a file may be used to produce asmooth and finished surface when required.

With the flat chisel, edges and surfaces can be trimmed,
while the gouge, or round-sectioned chisel, makes hollowed
surfaces possible; another chisel produces a V-section channel.

403

## S. 152

1 PRODUCTION

Chisels may be struck with a tapered mallet ofa hard wood, or

fitted with a handle and driven directly. Known to the Egyptians

like all these tools, the drill normally consists of a shaft into

which is inserted a slender cutting tool. The shaft is revolved by
a bowstring twisted once round it, the bow being moved later-

allyin asawing action. The pump-dtill, possiblya Roman inno-

vation, turns the shaft by raising and lowering a sleeve which
causes the string to wind round it as it rotates; a wooden disc
acting as a fiywheel keeps the drill in motion. Drills of these
kinds are used in Asia especially to produce the pierced panels
that form an important decorative element in many cultures of
Indonesia and Malaysia.

Extensively used by European peasants, ‘chip’ carving is the
simplesttechnique. A timber post may have thearrises notched
and designs cut in channels, bands and rosettes. A knife and a
small chisel may be all that is necessary to produce the illusion
ofrich decoration. Deeper cutting and removal ofthe ‘ground’
sothatthe ‘figure’ stands proud may still retain the two-dimensional plane ofthe outer surface but give depth by shadows. For
low-relief carving the entire ground is cutaway and graded, but
if full modelling is required, high- or bas-relief is necessary,
with at least half the depth of the motif rising from the background. Some carvers may cutsuch deep relief so as to give the
impression offreestanding figures, butthemost demanding of
the sculptor’s skills is carving in the round, especially where
the limbs ofa figure may be raised or extended.

Such work may be accompanied by rituals that are intended
to keep the potentially dangerous creative force under control.
Some cultures do not esteem their carvers but others recognize
them, like the Asmat, whose carvers achieve status with the
accolade of the name wow-ipits (woodcarver) (Gerbrands), or
the carvers of Star Harbor in the Solomons whose pursuitofthe
status of mwane manira (‘artistic man’) involves four stages of

404

qualification, skill in carving house-posts and in facial
scarification, and successful competition with carvers from
other villages in the region (Mead). With the growth in the
appeal and the appropriation of ‘primitive sculpture’, and the
‘recovery’ ofhouse-posts and decorated elements for ethnological and ‘folk art’ collections, many traditions are waning as
carvers attempt to meet the tastes, and the lucrative market, of
tourists. PAUL OLIVER
1.7.0 Wrought/forged iron

The term ‘wrought iron’ is used today as the name

for puddled iron and not for modern iron, which is termed

“mild steel’. It differs from mild steel in that it has a fibrous

structure and contains much less carbon (approximately 0.04

per cent) than mild steel which contains approximately o.2 per

cent. Wrought iron is therefore easier to weld in the fire, and is

more malleable and ductile, but it has less tensile strength.

Wrought iron was commonly produced up to the First World

War, but is still being produced in small quantities today.

The forging ofiron and mild steel is basically the same. The
iron is heated up to a temperature between 800 °C (1472 °F) and
1000 °C (1832 °F) and then changed to the desired shape with
the help of various tools. The main equipment and tools ofthe
trade are the fire, anvil, vice, hammer, chisel and tongs.

The fire can be anything from a small dip in the ground to a
forge cast in iron, while the fuel used can be charcoal, coal or
coke. In order to reach the required heat, air is forced into the
fire by means of a bellows or fan. The anvil is the support of
steel on which the metal is hammered. It can have various
forms, but generally has a horn called a ‘bick’ atone endanda
flat face with two holes, a small round one and a larger square
one. The small hole is used when punchingholes and thelarger
one for holding various tools.

Aristocratie
Minangkabau, Sum:

2.1.5.0 Naga
3.V11.4.d Bini

‚Archer, W.
Beier, UIli,1%

Jacobs, Juliaı
Mead, Sydne\

IWeathervanes:

The various methods of blacksmithing are drawing down,
upsetting, bending, cutting, punching and welding. ‘Drawing
down’ means forging a section of iron thinner, while ‘upsetting’ means increasing the circumference. Bending is done hot
unless the section concerned is very thin; the iron will bend
between the anvil and the hammer blow. Cutting is done with a
chisel and a hammer. Punching is a way ofmaking a hole without cutting away any material, usinga punch or rod, which can
be zound or square in section and tapered. Welding is a way of
Joining two pieces together to become one. The parts to be
joined must first be upsetto increase the thickness ofeach part,
as part ofthe iron will burn away during the process; they are
then forged so that they fitneatly together. The fire mustbe free
from impurities like clinker. Both pieces are heated up to welding heat - white, with sparks showing in the flames. Then the
pieces are quickly placed together and hammered with light,
fast blows. To prevent oxygen from getting to the iron and

forming scale, silversand or borax can be sprinkled on the iron
while hot.

. Another way offixing two or more parts ofthe work together
is by using rivets and collars. Collars are bands forged hot
around several pieces. When the collar cools it contracts round
the pieces and holds them in place. For fixing handles, knobs

Techniques and Skills 1.07

and ornaments to wood, a chest for instance, cotters and nails
are used.

Some of the most common details for embellishment are
balls, scrolls, coils, leaves, arrowheads, twists and various ways
offinishing an end, such as fishtails.

Balls aremade by weldingacollar on the stockand then, with
the help ofatop and bottom tool, forging itto shape. One ofthe
ways offinishing an end also entails welding on another piece;
this is the bolt end where a round bar is welded onto the end of
a fishtail and then the fishtail is partly bentto make a fluid continuation. Scrolls are often started and finished with a solid

snubend. Leaves are most often cut out of sheet metal and
forged; afterwards the bottom part is wrapped round the bar so
that the leaf appears to grow out of it, and is then welded to
become one with it. Twists can be made with many different
sections to very good effect; for example, aflatbar with around

bar either side. The bar is held firmly in a vice at one end ofthe
fire, the other end ofthe bar being held with the tongs or other
tools; it is then twisted.

The techniques of the blacksmith have not changed greatly
since the Iron Age except that machinery, such as power hammers and drills, has lightened the blacksmith’s work in recent

times.
es CATHARINE LLOYD

## S. 153

OPauloilver

1.VI

SERVICES

To function effectively most buildings require ‘services’, systems of design or supply
which enable them to meet the physical needs oftheir occupiers. Services may include
the provision ofresources essential to life, or they may add to the convenience and
comfort ofthe building even though life may not depend on their direct presence within
the structure. From an architectural perspective it is customary to consider services as
‘systems’, assuming that their provision involves the design and material organization
ofa number ofvariables and anticipating that these will be provided to several
buildings, even to an entire city, within a given context. So, for example, electricity may
be supplied to a single farmstead, though it would be necessary to design a system to
ensure that it was available to all the buildings and rooms where it was required. This
could be provided from a single generator, but it is likely to be more economic and to be
of greater benefit to the community ifpower were provided to several farms and other
buildings. In such circumstances a more complex system would be necessary for the
service to be efliciently supplied.

In vernacular architecture such service systems meeting the demands ofa whole
community are comparatively rare; though vernacular buildings have benefited from
public utilities, only in certain instances have these been ofvernacular origin or
inspiration. Nevertheless, services ofa similar kind are frequently to be found within a
single building type, even though they may be installed independently of other
buildings ofthe same tradition. Both are to be found in Cireulation (1.v1.1), where some
structures are made to meet community requirements while others are fashioned to
satisfy individual needs.

Most forms of communally installed circulation services such as roads and bridges
may be considered as engineered utilities which fall outside the architectural scope of
this Encyclopedia, though a few examples are cited in recognition oftheir importance in
facilitating circulation within the vernacular settlement. Atthe domestic scale the
principal forms ofeirculation that mediate external access, from path to porch, are
noted. Within buildings solutions to the problems posed by vertical movement may
differ, though they are all subject to ergonomic constraints.

Perhaps mankind’s earliest service was the warmth from a fire; traces of hearths are
associated with some ofthe oldest indications ofhuman habitation. The relation offire
to food, ofthe transformation ofthe raw to the cooked, calls into question any attempt
to make a distinction between these essential services. Cooking and Heating (1.v1.2)
considers both, recognizing that some heating systems, such as hypocausts, function as
discrete services, while others are adaptable to culinary pursuits or are designed for.a
dual purpose. Related to heating is the problem ofits control; the need to localize heat
for cooking yet to disperse it in order not to overheat dwellings in some tropical
regions, and the need to insulate in order to conserve heat in countries where

407

## S. 154

1.vl

SERVICES

temperatures are low or where nights are cold. In many cultures these and other
services meet in the kitchen.

Cooking and the consumption offood inevitably result in considerable quantities of
‘waste’ materials in the form ofunwanted by-products, inedible parts ofanimals, and
particularly, human faeces. The organization of Sanitation and Hygiene (1.1.3)
presents relatively few problems in cultures where tropical vegetation, scavenging
animals or arid climate assimilate or desiccate the unwanted matter. In many habitats,
less favoured in these aspects, the elimination of wastes from the dwelling can be both
offensive and a threat to health. The disposal ofexcreta and waste materials in
vernacular cultures is the least studied ofindigenous service systems.

This is less ofa problem with regard to Ventilation and Cooling (1.v1.4) on which
extensive research has been undertaken. To date this has been largely in hot and dry
climates, particularly ofthe Mediterranean and Southwest Asian regions. Research in
passive cooling and ventilation systems has been considerable, if contentious. The
potential application of such systems which do not consume energy resources has
motivated much ofthis research. Within the vernacular contexts however, no services
are more important than those which bring water within reach. Potable drinking water
may be naturally available, but in many circumstances it is necessary for methods of
obtaining a regular Water Supply (1.v1.5) to be implemented. Some, like the ganats of
the Iranian plateau constitute integrated systems which serve whole communities;
others may be installed for the use ofa single household - exemplifying the different

levels at which essential services are designed to meet human needs.
PAUL OLIVER

ıvı Circulation

Living in a house, or occupying any building, is a
dynamic process; one of daily movement within and about it,
whether the building is for dwelling or worship, for work or
pleasure. Some measure of consideration has been given to
movement through buildings, particularly with respect to their
permeability and to aspects ofterritoriality and privacy (Hillier
and Hanson). Ifthe majority of vernacular buildings exist for
the accommodation of activities, or to support them, the study
of activity systems should potentially throw light on how they
are utilized, how they relate to the use of external space and
how the patterns and priorities ofcirculation are defined.

External eirculation
Outside the dwelling the residents may work in fields or forest,
be employed in village or town, or engage in recreation and ceremonies, external activities thatmay take them from home. But
they also come back: the process of departure, engagement in
activity, and return is a cyclic one. Even iftthe activity involves
walking several kilometres to obtain water from river orwell, or
a day’s journey to market, to dispose of, or purchase produce,
the point of departure is also the ultimate destination.
Preferred circulatory routes may be followed after a period of
exploration and experiment, often with the objective ofeconomizing on effort and time. ‘Cutting corners’ is common and
may have helped define the typical triangular shape of many a
British village green, where two routes meet at a T-junction.

Gallow’s Cove,

‚Shortall’s

(Shortens)
Marsh

Eoster Long
‚Marsh

Daniel's

is Marsh
\
Sag
A \3,
= iE An SE Rn

While the relation of paths to economy of movement can be
illustrated in many instances, circulation is not free of cultural
values, and may result in more arduous routes being employed.
As Ernestine Friedl noted in a Greek village, self-respect
(filotimo) required that male villagers should take the more
difficult and even hazardous ways to demonstrate their disregard for personal ease or convenience.

Inscribed on the landscape, with their differing widths indicating the nature and intensity of use, pathways are seldom
plotted in studies of settlement pattern. The paths ofa Tallensi
village between kinship groups, ancestral shrines, farm plots,
marketplace, Roman Catholic Mission House and District Commissioner’s office and the compounds ofthe na’ab or chief, was
“in effect, adiagram ofthe prevailing social structure’ (Prussin).
But many factors, including opportunities for social intercourse,
restrictions and sanctions on movement, access to resources
and habits ofidentifyinglandmarks for purposes of orientation
or navigation, all play their part in the definition of circulatory
routes (Lynch).

A study of Calvert, a Newfoundland community, disclosed a
complex pattern ofroads, lanes and paths through village and
woodland. Yet ‘few landscapes — because of ownership rights
orthe characteristics ofthe terrain - permitan infinite choice of
lines of movement between any two points. Instead, a culture
provides designated pathways categorized by function that are
the normal lines of movement through a community.’ In
Calvert these functions included social and ritual visits covering the settlement with ‘webs ofvisiting networks that overlap,
extending into every kitchen’ (Pocius).

Much outward circulation from the home is for economic
purposes. Research on the spatio-temporal patterns of foodobtaining activities in a village ofthe Oriomo Papuans revealed
their exploitation ofthe environment for the cultivation ofsago
and taro, and for gender-differentiated gathering, fishing and
hunting around their home base of Wonie. Part ofthe training
ofthe youths involved learning the paths in the Oriomo forest,
though their plantations were only a half-hour walk away and
solo hunting expeditions lasted less than four hours from
departure to homecoming (Ohtsuka). In such an instance the
home range was a mere 70-80 sq km (27-31 sq mi). For some
nomadic groups itis an immense terrain, buteven their routes,
orthe seasonal pasturing ofherds, may be regarded as circulatory; in all instances ofnomadism or transhumance circulation
is the essence ofthe system.

In many environmental contexts ancillary structures are necessary for paths and routes to function effectively. The same
hands that constructed the houses, with the same tools and

409

## S. 155

1.v1 SERVICES

technologies, made the catwalks and landing stages, the
arcades and colonnades, theroads cut into or cantilevered from
the mountainsides, the bridges ofbamboo and stone, that ease
the journey from the homeplace, and the return to the security
beneath its roof.

Internal circulation

Most descriptions of vernacular architecture are essentially static, regarding the building as an envelope for anumber ofcells or
rooms which are ascribed specific functions: sleeping (or ‘bedroom’), cooking (‘kitchen’), living (or significantly in the West,
‘sitting room’), store or sometimes, granary, among them.

In almost all types of building apart from shade shelters,
access to the internal spaces is through a specific entrance way
which distinguishes the boundless external world from the
defined, micro-world within. The mostsignificant transition in
anyjourney is the crossing ofthe threshold to or from the building itself. The threshold ‘sustains the middle, in which the two,
the outside and inside penetrate each other. The threshold
bears the between. What goes out and goes in, the in between,
is joined in the between’s dependability.’ (Heidegger)

Once inside, alarge number ofbuilding types are notspatially
articulated by walls and further entrances; differentiation is by
social convention. So, for instance, within the Navajo hogan
there is only one circulation route, the ‘Sunwise (or clockwise)
Path’, which all who enter are required to follow, through it is
forbidden to step over the central hearth, or over the legs of
recumbent occupants. Internal space is dedicated to deities and
specific areas are designated by gender and status, though they
are not physically defined. To leave the hogan it is obligatory to
complete the circular Path. Similar rules apply in the Mongolger.

Numerous cultures that dwell in single-cell structures
differentiate between male and female spaces, or may make
other distinctions which are recognized by all inhabitants.
Sometimes they are indicated by lightly constructed partitions,

butitis common in Guinea Coast societies to builda number of
cells within the confines of a compound. Each wife may have
her own sleeping hut, kitchen and work space, with moulded
entrances set in enclosing internal walls. Circulation is therefore more controlled than the forms may suggest, and may be
further directed or defined by sacred areas. The Islamic Hausa
adaptation ofthe compound often has an imposing threshold
to the zaure, the entrance hut which acts as first filter that leads
toacourtyardanda second reception hut (shigifa), which marks
the domain of the compound head. Forbidden to visitors, a
large courtyard lies beyond with the family head’s sleeping
quarters and the huts of wives and children. Here the women
move freely, but their circulation is restricted within the compound wall (Schwerdtfeger).

Spatial differentiation within the dwelling is most frequently
byasolid wall which distinguishes a sleeping room from an allpurpose living space, such as may be found in many regions of
the Balkans, central and eastern Europe. The crosswall may be
breached by a door into the sleeping room. But two-roomed
houses can be enlarged, like the Hungarian houses which havea
main general-purpose room (nagy haz), with a pantry (kamra)
and before it, an entrance porch (eresz). Generally three-sided,
the porch is an important feature that gives focus to the entrance
but which often opens off a broad veranda (tornac). Though it
has been argued that the entrance is ‘a place ofjudgement, ayes
and no, a disclosure and closure’ (Lang) in the vernacular its
arbitrary nature is frequently mediated by a transitional structure, which provides a sitting out-place, and often a supplementary store or workplace (Balassa and Ortutay).

Multicellular buildings require means of circulation, which
may be doors opening offa common hall, successive entrances
between contiguous rooms, or separate doorways to rooms in
line, opening on a veranda or corridor. With architecture built
on more than one level, like the multiple terraces of houses that
cling to the steep valley walls of Nuristan, different kinds of
access may be necessary. A Nuristani house (ama) may have a
store with grain bins at the lowest level, and a main living area
above approached from within by a notched log ladder. Supported by poles, a covered gallery used both for clan discussions
and as a hay store may projectatthe front. A further notched log
gives access to the flatroof, which provides an important social
and work place. Horizontal circulation between the gallery and
the main room is simple, but vertical circulation and mixed
space use make it more complex (Edelberg and Jones).

Complexity of plan and multiplicity of storeys have implications ofsocial system, economy and status which bear upon circulation within and about the building, seldom more so in the
vernacular than in the havelis of North India, ofwhich more than
a score of types have been identified. Important in the axial
urban haveli is the otlo, the house-width veranda from the central
door of which access is gained to the kahdki or main reception
room. Unlike the Muslim havelis, those owned by Hindus will
have windows opening on to the streets, often with balconies
added, while open terraces and covered galleries overlook the
chowk or multipurpose courtyard faced by the front and rear
blocks. On the side flanks of the chowk may be several rooms,
often interconnecting, including a water store adjacent to the

farmhouse, Nortl

John Asqulth

kitchen. Though purdah is not always practised a covered
veranda at the opposing end of the chowk marks the women’s
sleeping and service rooms. Many Hindu havelis are on two or
three floors, reached by several flights of stairs with the main
room at the front and above the entrance. In spite of its linear
plan on a narrow site the versatile urban haveli about its central
chowkfully exploits its circulatory potential with its large domestic spaces linked to connected chambers, its external and internal verandas, its galleries, terraces and balconies, and with
vertical staircase connections (Prasad; Pramar).

Circulation, or movement from a base to and from a destination is essential both for the sustaining of life support systems
and for the process of living within buildings. In this category
examples are given of some of the structures that facilitate
eirculation outside the building and within, and those that
mediate the threshold between the two. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.1.a Balcony
A projection from an upper storey ofa building, the
balcony is a form of platform with a surrounding protective
balustrade or railings. It is usually supported by the extension
of timber crossbeams, if constructed as part of the original
structure, the word being related to the English ‘balk’ and
deriving from the Old High German word for a beam, balho.
When it is added later the balcony may be supported by wood,
iron or stone brackets, or with corbels, which may be moulded
or otherwise decoratively treated.

Circulation 1.11

The decorative elements of the balcony are indicative of its
essentially pleasurable use. Most balconies are constructed as
extensions ofthe internal living space, often offering restricted
seating room, but used principally as an additional element of
circulation at a level above the ground floor between the interior and external space. Sometimes the balcony is reached only
at one point, generally through a pair of doors; many however,
may link adjacentrooms. Typically they are used as observation
positions, their extension in front of the facade permitting
uninterrupted views in front, and to the sides. Though some
may be built to enjoy a landscape or seascape, many balconies
are to be found in urban situations where processionals, street
festivities, parades or other similar events are held with sufficient
frequency to justify their construction. Balconies are therefore
commonly seen in Mediterranean countries such as those of
the Italian and Iberian peninsulas, or in countries where the
Spanish, Portuguese or Italian presence has been considerable.

On ceremonial or festive occasions balconies may be decorated with flowers, wreaths or palm fronds, or they may be
draped with cloaks, or swags - garlands of flowers, fruit or
foliage. Suspended from the ends of the railings or balusters,
the gown or garland is permitted to hang in the middle, creating a catenary shape that is sometimes repeated in the decoration of a stone or plaster balcony. The tassels or fringes of
gowns or cloaks are also sometimes used as decorative motifs,
emphasizing the festive use ofthe structure.

Aviewing platform is also a position from which to be seen or
address a gathering. Balconies are therefore constructed where
dignitaries, ecclesiastics and those who have been accorded
status or have assumed positions of power or authority, may be
viewed or heard. Though these are most frequent in imperial or

4ır

no ned

## S. 156

1.v1 SERVICES

military contexts, there are vernacular examples to be found.
The fact that the occupants of the balcony are also themselves
on view has contributed to the development ofthe mashrabiyya
and rowshan of Arab countries where the benefits ofventilation,
cooling and observation are tempered by the desire for privacy
or seclusion ofwomen. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.1.b Bridge: cantilever (Bhutan)

The most common bridge for crossing the many
streams and rivers formed by the melting waters from the
Himalayan glaciers is a simple tree-trunk or wooden beam laid
across the small streams. The need to span rivers with a greater
width than the length of a single tree, however, called for more
complex solutions. For this purpose, the Bhutanese people often
builta wooden cantilever bridge. This type ofbridge usually consists oftwo stone bridge-houses, whose weight helps in keeping
the cantilevered beams fixed in place. Two to four beams, firmly
embedded in the bridge-houses’ foundation, cantilever 1.5-2 m
(5-7 fi) beyond the stonework. A short piece of wood is laid
across the end ofthese beams, serving as the support for one or
two more sets of cantilevered beams. These beams project 1.5-2
m (5-7 ft) beyond their supporting point, thus in total bringing
the furthest point 3-4 m (10-13 ft) beyond the bridge-house. If
the same is done from the opposite side oftheriver, atotalreduction of6-8 m (20-26 ft) ofthespan can be achieved. Large beams
are laid over the remaining openingand planks are placed across
them. Handrails (and sometimes a wooden shingle roof) complete the bridge. As is the case in all traditional Bhutanese construction, not a single nail is used. The sheer weight of the
components keeps them in place, while complex timber joints
are applied where pulling forces occur.

The (probably) last specimen of a remarkable type of bridge
can be found in the village of Dogsum in east Bhutan. It concerns a Isth-century iron chainlink suspension bridge,
ascribed to the Tibetan saint Thangtong Gyalpo (1383-1464),
better known locally as Chagzampa, ‘the iron bridge builder’.
The chains for this bridge consist of two types: short and long
linked. Three chains ofshort links, are laterally connected with
metal wire at regular distances. Bamboo mats are then laid
loosely over thechains, thus formingthe walkway. Sixchains of
long links, three on each side, form the handrails. The chains
with the short links pass through holes in the floorbeams ofthe
two bridge-houses and are fixed in place with wedges through
the links at the rear side of the floorbeams. The large-link
chains pass - at different heights - through the masonty ofthe
bridge-houses and are also fixed in place with wedges through
the links. Except for the floorbeams, the two bridge-houses
consist entirely ofstonemasonry with a (recently applied) layer
of cement plaster at the exterior sides. It is the sheer weight of
the bridge-houses that is necessary to withstand the gravity
forces on the iron chains. JOS MASELAND
1.V1.1.b-i Bridge: corridor (Sichuan)

A corridor bridge, on which houses, pavilions and
memorial archways are built, is a type of old Sichuan folk
bridge with a corridor on its roadway. The building above the
roadway is called the corridor house, and forms a combined

412

|
i
Kt

whole with the bridge. As one form of bridge classification, it
has existed for 2000 years, found over all the rivers and streams
in Sichuan, and especially over the streams ofvitalcommunications lines.

All ofthe corridor houses in Sichuan are built on girder and
arch bridges; there is no evidence ofthe type of corridor bridge
whose houses are built on the cantilever. The function ofa corridor bridge is not only to provide a passageway, shelter and
resting place, butalso quite often, a place for the local people to
gather. On some ofthe wider corridor bridges, people live and
shops have opened and the bridges have added their own
attraction to town and country constructions. The roof of the
corridor bridge fulfils another function: to protect the wooden
structure ofthe bridge itselffrom being rotted and destroyed by
rain and water.

There are three types of corridor bridge: saddleback with
hipped eave; saddleback with double-hipped eaves (there are
comparatively more ofthese than the firsttype); and a one-eave
corridor bridge on which pavilions or memorial archways are
built at each end. The size and number of corridors (or passageways) are restricted by the length and width of the bridge
itself. Wider ones have many columns in four lines forming a
wide central corridor - which is the main passageway — and
narrow attached corridors on the left and right. Outside the

attached corridors, there are fixed parapets. Narrower bridges
have fewer columns in only two lines, the number of the
columns being decided by the bridge’s length. The columns,
which can beas much as ıo m (33 ft) high have crossbeams, with
spans up to 6 m (20 ft). The roofs are often covered with small
green tiles, but sometimes with grasses or bark. Flat bridges
usually use giant trees as beams which are then covered with
planks. Stone arch bridges have elegantly carved base stones
under the corridor columns. One example ofa magnificent corridor bridge has its beams elaborately painted and columns
beautifully carved; at the four corners are hung Aeolian bells,
and in the middle there stands a small temple housing the village god. On both sides, there are benches.

JI FUZHENG

1.V11.b-ii Bridge: railroad (America, NW)

The boom in mining, the lumber business and
farming in the second halfof the ıgth century brought the population expansion and the railroad network into the northwestern United States. The strong tradition of building in timber
which was abundantly available, was a natural choice for
bridge-building. The states of Washington and Idaho as a
result have some ofthe finestttimber bridges in the country. The
prototypes include the low, short-span pole bridges, the tall
bridges using squared lumber, and the longer span covered
bridges built with deep trusses.

The short-span bridges had supports spaced up to 4.5 m (15 ft)
apartwith the heightlimited to about 6 m (20 ft), appropriate to
the length of lumber. These bridges were usually built over
creeks and shallow rivers. An example ofshort-span, using pole
supports exists in Albion, Washington. The bridge has 18 spans
of approximately 4.5 m (15 ft) transversing the Palouse river.
Each supportis constructed ofincliningpoles 30 cm (12 in) in diameter. They are tied with crossbracing and connected together
at the top with heavy timber pieces 30 cm (12 in) square. In the
short dimension of the width of the rails the inclining poles
provide the lateral support as they rise. In some cases, timber
sheeting is used instead of crossbracing giving the bridge support the appearance of solid walls. Heavy 20x 40 cm (8x 16 in)
timber spans between supports in the long direction which

Circulation 1.v1.1

Lg

then receives the typical 20x 20 cm (8x 8 in) railroad ties on 30
cm (12 in) centres. The linear form of these low bridges is in
striking contrast with the serene landscapes ofthe region.

The tall, short-span bridges were built to take railroads
across valleys in mountainous areas. The bridge that crosses
the ravine near Newcastle built in 1877 with a height of 42 m
(138 ft) and length of 366 m (1200 ft) is the best example in
Washington. An example from Lapwai, Idaho was built in the
first decade ofthe 2oth century as part ofa horseshoe system of
bridges and tunnels that carried the railroad to the heights of
the mountain. Supports of the bridge are spaced 4.5-5 m
(14-16 ft) apart while the height ofthe supports at mid-span is
about 28 m (go ft). Vertical supports were constructed from 30
X 30cm (12% 12 in) squared pieces 3 m (Io ft) apart, restingon
timber bearing supports laid over a stone foundation. The supports incline as they rise in height and are tied together with
straight and crossing members. The supports are also tied
together with straight elements and crossbracing in the long
direction. The timber used is mostly 30x 30 cm (12 X 12 in) with
the exception ofsome bracing members which are 15 x 25 cm
(6 x 10 in). The dramatic appearance of these bridges across
valleys adds powerful images to the mountainous landscape of
Idaho.

Two examples ofcovered bridges in Washington are in Grays
River and Manning near Colfax. The one in Grays River,
although notarailroad bridge, serves an importantroad and is
still in public use. It was built in 1905 to allow the farmers on
the south side ofthe bridge to getto the north side market. The
bridge was covered for weather protection and longevity. This
importantspecimen is listed in the National Register ofHistorical Places.

413

## S. 157

1.vI SERVICES

Framing section

Framing plan

j

Elevation
30 ft

9m

Near Colfax, Washington, the bridge which was built for the
railroad is now abandoned. Only the walls still remain covered
while the top is open. The walls are formed from deep timber
trusses which are in turn boarded up with wood siding. The
bridge spans approximately 45 m (150 ft) across the river with a
wood truss approximately 8 m (25 ft) deep. Heavy timber pieces
form the chords. Crossing members are built oftimber 25 x 45
cm (10 X ı8 in), two in one direction sandwiching a single
member in the other direction. Joints are tied with iron plates.
Heavy steel tension bars tie top and bottom chords. The truss is
then covered with timber siding detailed like a curtain wall
which covers its two sides. The crisp form ofthe bridge gracefully erossing the river in a beautiful setting where the rolling
hills rise in the background provides a strongvisual experience.

The evolution of bridge-building continues in Washington
moving from timber bridges to cast iron, to concrete, and steel.
From early timber bridges to the graceful arched concrete
bridges, to the rustic cast iron bridges, to the elegant modern
suspension and floating bridges, there is a continuity of tradition from vernacular to modern which has excelled in the state.

RAFI SAMIZAY
1.Vl.1.c Corridor
The word ‘corridor’ derives from the Latin corridore,
or ‘running place’, which in turn relates to the verb correre- to
run. Ifa corridor does not induce running, it does not encourage dallyingeither: in the sense in which the term is used in this
work a corridor is a form of covered passageway which facilitates physical communication and access between two or more
rooms or spaces within a building. Such communication is
essentially linear and directional, but movement may be in
either direction, which exerts some measure of dimension on
the corridor, requiring that two people may pass each other
while moving in opposite directions. This implies a width of1.5

414

m (5 ft) as a comfortable minimum and a large proportion of
domestic corridors are approximately this width. Butwith sideways movementand reduced comfort, a narrow passage is possible and sometimes necessary.

Yet the width of the corridor is conditioned by its use; the
through-passage, or cross-passage, of the northern European
longhouse was designed to permitthemovement of cattle from
the fields to the shippen or byre. While there had to be additional room for the cowherd to move past the animals, the
space had to be sufficiently confined to ensure that the cows
could not turn or return, but were guided to the stalls. Often,
the shippen or cattle side ofthe passage was built of stone, but
the side of the livier, or living-quarters, of the farmer and his
family, was in the form of a wooden screen-wall. Simple and
with broad, exposed panels facing the corridor, itwas moulded
and sometimes richly carved, on the livier side. It was also
removable and easily relocated ifthere was a change of use for
the cross-passage (Barley).

As corridors often give access to rooms on both sides, they
are frequently located within the main mass of the building
rather than along its sides, with a consequent reduction in
opportunities for natural lighting. A corridor which leads from
an entrance may require that the closed door is solid and
secure, further redueing illumination, so that a window over
the transom, or a fanlight, may be the only source of natural
light and ventilation. Corridors may therefore extend the full
width of a building in order to take advantage of a window
openingatthe opposing end.

The world ‘corridor’ is among the rarest in the indexes of
books on vernacular architecture, reflecting both the neglect
that they have received and their passing function. But it also
indicates the comparative rarity of corridors in vernacular
buildings of an early date. If corridors are a means of accessibility they are also an instrument of discrimination. Whereas
the hall and the courtyard (see entries in this category) permit
relative freedom of movement, the corridor defines it. It obvi-

tt, after Reuther

Warren and

INeem _Talar Hosh
he

EIS
N

Ground floorpl
large Baghdadl
showing acces:
corridors tose|
basement roo!
(neems), from
central courtyard

3.v1.3.f grain eleväl

Reference for!
Kirk, ruth, and Al
Carmela, 1990

irbert, 1979

ftanche-Comt&

ates the necessity of crossing a common space and permits
control of access and entrance. The corridor appears with the
greater desire for privacy or seclusion, evident in the many corridors in the ‘family’ houses of the Shakers (Schiffer). The
keeping of servants and staff increased the use ofthe corridor
where the availability of staff on demand required both uninterrupted movement and restriction — a luxury that the residents in the majority ofthe world’s vernacular buildings could
seldom afford.

Insome instances the corridor acts as anarrowed hall or sofa,
becoming a social space; such as is the case in the extended
family houses of Anatolia, or the more developed compound
houses of the Yoruba (Schwerdtfeger). In the latter case the
corridor is a slender courtyard, or acommon covered space
between rooms; in the former the space is on the upper level of
the two-storey house. With the increasing use of symmetrical
facades in Western houses under the influence of classical concepts of proportion and design, central halls were squeezed by
the rooms on either side. A staircase rising directly from the
hallreduces the volume by half, creatinga corridorto the rear of
the building beside the stairs.

Corridors are more commonly found on the upper floors,
where the staircase frequently leads to a landing sufficiently
long to permit the opening of the doors of private sleeping
rooms. For corridors consume space, and the more corridors
there are to increase accessibility, the more ofthe volume ofa
building is devoted to circulation and the less to living space.
Where the building lot itselfis restricted, as in the urban house,
the corridor may be the only means ofreaching the rear ofthe
dwelling. Many shops have corridors that permitthe delivery of
goods to storerooms. PAUL OLIVER
1.VI.1.d Gallery
Though galleries take several forms they have certain elements in common. A gallery is a covered circulation
space external to therooms ofa building, which is partially open
on one side ofits length. External access may be at one or both
ends, and sometimes at other points, while from the internal
spaces of the building windows or doorways may open on the
gallery. As it is customarily open above handrail or balustrade
level, the gallery is not a corridor, though it shares some of its
functions, in particular, access to domestic rooms. Ifthe gallery
is on the ground level and on the outside ofthe building, it may
be properly considered as a veranda. Ifthe ground floor gallery
faces an inner courtyard which it may surround as a cloister, it
usually supports an upper structure which may accommodate
living spaces.

The majority of galleries are at an upper, generally secondstorey leveland are roofed. Often the roofis integral to the building rather than covered by a pentice, as the gallery is integral to
the structure. In stone-built buildings the roof ofthe gallery may
be supported by stone pillars or arcades as is the case with many
ofthe substantial loggias ofthe mezzedria of Tuscany. But in welltimbered areas, the gallery is often ofwood, and theroofis borne
by wooden posts, braced with brackets or capitals. External
access to the second-storey gallery is generally by a staircase,
frequently in line with the direction ofthe gallery.

Circulation 1.V1.1
Most galleries serve other functions than circulation, being
used for sitting out in the shade, or protection from the rain.
With a gate at the top of the stairs and if the balusters are
sufficiently close to give security, the gallery is a popular playspace for children whose activities can be overseen. In hot or
humid weather, or in overcrowded circumstances the gallery
may also be usedas asupplementary sleeping space. Frequently
a gallery is used for drying the clothes and bed linen, mattresses
may be aired over the balustrade, and rugs or carpets may be
beaten to remove dust, as is frequently the case in the Alpine
houses ofthe Valais. Inevitably the gallery is also used for items
of furniture or service artefacts, such as wash-tubs, which are
only used intermittently. Occasionally, a ventilated privy or
latrine may be constructed at one end of the gallery; though
comparatively rare, examples have been noted in rural France.

Some galleries may be cantilevered from the main building
on the extension of main beams, or supported on single or
tiered corbels as is the case in some fine examples in the Bresse
region of Franche-Comte£. As such they may be viewed as balconies; however, the essence ofthe gallery is its external access
whereas the balcony is customarily reached only from within
the building. Exceptional examples of connected galleries
which produced continuous external circulation levels up to 60
m (200 ft) in length link the gards, the deep rows of merchants’
buildings such as Engelgarden, on the Bryggen wharf in
Bergen, Norway. Galleries are to be found in use in most parts
of Europe, and though they do not appear to have been
mapped, their distribution may be noted in domestic, economic and monastic buildings in central and eastern Europe,
in some regions of the Middle East, including the generously
scaled structures in Nuristan, and in parts of the western
Himalayas. Notable examples were to be found in British
coaching inns, where the internal space for the dismounting of
passengers and unloading of freight was surrounded on three
sides by galleries. Though many have been demolished the
15th-century New Inn, Gloucester, or the George, in Southwark, London, retain all or part of their galleries, which were
also used for entertainments by strolling players.

‘Minstrel’s galleries’ were sometimes built within larger,
manorial houses. More broadly, the word gallery is also used to
denote internal tiers or banks of seats and standing room in
churches, theatres and auditoria, and even for long and
confined spaces or tunnels naturally occurring, or excavated,
through rock. Mention should also be made ofthe extension of
the term when applied to long rooms, wider than corridors,
used for the display ofworks ofartand for exercise in inclement
weather where, in effect, the open side ofthe gallery has been
closed for comfort and convenience — usages which extend
beyond the vernacular. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.1.d-i Gallery (Central Europe)

A balcony is a platform projecting from an external
house wall, sometimes accessible by an inside or outside staircase, enclosed by a low wall or balustrade, covered or open and
serving communication, work and social purposes according
to need. It is a feature which often penetrated into vernacular
architecture as a result ofthe influence ofthe examples ofhigh

415

## S. 158

2

1.v1 SERVICES

architecture. It is a frequent element of construction in Balkan
countries and it is especially typical for stone buildings in the
whole Mediterranean area.

In Central European vernacular architecture, however, there
was a greater preference for the gallery, i.e. a covered, half-open
passageway projecting from a storey along the outer, normally
the courtyard wall (in isolated cases also along the gable wall or
directly lodged in the gable itself). It allows access to the rooms
(usually storage rooms) on the higher storey and rests on the
ceiling crossbeams which project over the front wall or on the
corbels. It is accessible from the inside, from the hallway or the
appropriate storey or from the outside by a wooden staircase,
especially in the case of granaries. The gallery runs either along
the entire length of the wall or only along a part of it; in some
cases itis integrated into the house wall in loggia style. It functions as a communicating passage and has secondary uses, for
example as a place for dıying agricultural produce. It is commonly found in areas where houses have corner-timbered or
timber-framed storeys and it was introduced into vernacular
architecture in the early modern period at the latest. Particularly
outstanding are those galleries which have carved balusters,
decorative handrails or even arcades.

At ground-floor level the houses in some areas have covered
porches at the entrance or along the courtyard wall. These are
halfopen communicating passages which give protection
againstthe weather and in summer serve as extended livingand
working areas. JOSEF VAREKA
1V1.1.e Hall
Among the most widely applied terms for an architectural space, the hall is not simply defined. Believed to have
derived from the Teutonic prefix hal- (or hel-, hul-), to cover, the
world ‘hall’ described the single room ofthe early Anglo-Saxon
house, undifferentiated by sleeping or other chambers. In the
centre was an open fire, on the north side of which the raised
table ofthe Saxon lord and his family faced the trestle tables
where servants and workers ate. The hall was open to the roof,
the timbers becoming blackened over time by smoke and tar.
By the 14th century a solar or bedchamber was constructed for

416

the manorial lord at one end ofthe hall, often atan upper level.
Radical changes took place subsequently with separate rooms
and servants quarters being partitioned off the hall with
screens of carved oak.

The majority of surviving examples are manorial or the
houses of wealthy yeomen; smaller hall-houses that come
within the vernacular were being built at the beginning ofthe
16th century. Anumber survive in the Weald and Wessex, cruckframed or with open crownpost roofs, often with one or two
crosswings. In some cases the open halls were ceiled or were
converted with two floors, but the smoke-blackening of the
roof timbers reveals their former use. The types and forms of
hall-houses differed from one region to another, with halls that
ranged in length from 7.5 m (25 ft) to as small as 4.5 m (15 fi).
They changed further as fireplaces became widely used and as,
with the growth in population, more peasant and artisan
houses were constructed.

Though social changes in the Tudor period and thereafter led
to marked distinctions between the houses of the gentry and
those of the peasantry, in one respect the hall, though diminishing in size, retained its function as a filter to other parts of
the dwelling. The main entrance of a house might open onto
the hall from which the parlour and other rooms were reached.
While the lobby-entrance house with transverse chimney virtually dispensed with the hall, other housetypes retained a hall
that also had a staircase in two-storey houses. By the end ofthe
18th century this had shrunk to a vestibule with stairs, especially in the towns where narrower sites required economies in
circulation space.

Once the focus of the medieval house, the hall became a
reception space and a communication route giving access to all
parts ofthe house by way ofdoors, corridors and staircase: this
is still its function. PAUL OLIVER
1Vv11f Ladder
‘He [Jacob] dreamed, and behold a ladder set up on
the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven: and behold the
angels ofGod ascendingand descending on it.’ (Genesis 28.12).
Since Biblical times the ladder has been a metaphor of aspiration, ofascendancy and ofsuccess. Itis still essential to somerrituals, including the access to the roof entrance of kivas in Acoma
pueblo, where double ladders are 2 m (6.5 ft) wide. The word
“ladder’ is of Greek and Old Teutonic origin, which confirms its
ancienthistory. The ladder takes anumber offorms, ofwhich the
notched log is both the simplestand one ofthe most ubiquitous.

Jacob’s ladder was unlikely to have been a notched log, buta
succession of rungs, or bars, fixed between two supporting
poles. Ladders ofthis type are ofunknown antiquity butwere in
use in Assyrian times. They were extensively used in medieval
building construction, as can be seen in numerous European
illuminated manuscripts from the ısth century and Indian
manuscripts ofsimilar date (Coldstream; Ahsan Jan Qaisar). In
these illuminations such ladders appear to be constructed with
the side poles in parallel, though this may be a drawing convention. Customarily ladders are constructed with the top end,
or ‘horns’ closer together than the feet, takingadvantage ofthe
greater stability ofthe truncated triangle.

‚New Mexico.

See also
2.111.3-A Britl

2.111.3.e Devol
