Frolec, Väclav, al
Vareka, Josel,1
Megas, Georg

\ Nicola, 1991
ter,1937

Fitting the ‘rounds’ ortapered rungs into theaugured holes of
theside poles oftheladder was atask usually given to an apprentice carpenter. An ash pole was sawn down its length so thatthe
two halves were semicircular in section, with the split facing
inwards. The rungs were made of oak, sometimes recycled from
the spokes of a wheel, and were tapered at the ends, all rungs
being inserted into one pole before the second pole was fitted
(Rose). Ladders might be as much as 6 m (20 ft) in length, but
their dimensions were dependent on their use. Ladders for
domestic use were replaced with staircases when economically
possible, butaccess to lofts is still customarily by ladder.

Spoke or rung ladders are relatively easy to handle when carried vertically, or from the centre. Their portability enables
them to be used in a great many positions, and assemblies of
two or more ladders may be used by thatchers and tilers when
working on roofs. They were also needed in an emergeney ifa
thatched roof caught fire, and itwas common for ladders to be
hung horizontally on the side of farm buildings ensuring
speedy access to the roof which would enable a worker to pull
offthe burning thatch with a long-handled hook.

Selfsupporting ladders with a hinged, braced frame which
can open to form a triangulated and secure freestanding structure are used by house plasterers, painters and others whose
work would be impaired by the pressure of an inclined ladder
againstthe wall or working surface. Ladders ofthis type usually
have wider supports and flat steps rather than rungs. A plank
between two step ladders can form a trestle which allows ease
ofmovementata level above head height.

Crude ladders can be made simply with sticks nailed or lashed
to two poles; others may be made with great finesse like the
slender bamboo ladders of Bali, with their splayed feet and wide
lower rungs. Still others are made of twisted vines or ofropes,
with rope rungs, which can be hung or coiled when not in use.
Thoughameritoftheladder is its lightness and portability, some
are used in a fixed position in farm buildings or on, for instance
a postmill (windmill) where it may be hinged to permit turning
the mill into the wind. But no matter how it is made, from what
materials or in what context, the ladder is a major contribution

to vertical circulation in vernacular traditions.. PAUL OLIVER

Circulation 1.V1.1

1v11f-1 Ladder: notched

Itis considered that the archetype ofthe stair is the
unique step which joins two different levels — that of the horizontal surface constructed by man and that ofthe natural, surrounding ground. This minimal ladder is nearly always situated
in relation to the entry.

The following stage is the stair as an independent artefact, more
often mobile than fixed. Its character is secondary to the form of
construction. It can be used in a different way and in different
places in the house. The ‘stair as an object’ is an accessory. The
dimensions, situation and fabrication are not the main worry of
the builder. A large number of simple staircases have these characteristics as, for example, African notched ladders, Indonesian
ladders, Pueblo exterior ladders, and Himalayan ladders.

The Kassena and Lobi from Burkina Faso live in defensive
compounds, formed ofhuts with thick walls and blind exteriors,
open to a common interior central courtyard and crowned by
terraces an excellent observation pointand inaccessible ifthe
notched ladder is removed.

The Dogon in Banani village, situated at the foot of the
Bandiagara cliffs in Mali, live in acompound formed ofhouses,
stables and granaries. The main room of the house belongs to
the firstwife and ateither side are wood and cereal storerooms.
In the vestibule lives the master. The high, circular room is the
kitchen, through which there is access by notched ladder to the
closed terrace. The latter is used for drying millet.

The Nuristani from Pashki, in the Parun valley, Afghanistan,
live in villages perched on the steep slopes ofthe Hindu Kush.
The houses are imbricated, one on top ofthe other, andthereare
no flat surfaces for everyday life. However, they are constructed
with flat roofs, which permits horizontal and vertical circulation in the village across neighbouring roofs. These roofs serve

417

sauınog sonbaor @

## S. 159

© Paul oliver

1.v SERVICES
as public spaces where children play, women work and men discuss. Vertical circulation, both interior and exterior, is possible
through the use ofnotched ladders.

FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC
1Vv11.g Lobby
A lobby is a small room or chamber which may
afford protection from draughts, and a measure of privacy
between internal rooms and the exterior. Some farmhouses
and other rural dwellings may have a lobby at the rear, sometimes serving as a connecting link between adjacent buildings.
This emphasizes the function ofthe lobby as a spatial articulator, permitting doors to be placed at right angles to each other.

Derived from the medieval Latin laubia or lobia, a monastic
term for a shelter or booth, the lobby is a space of limited proportions, but it was a feature of houses in the British Isles in
which an axial chimney-stack was constructed atthe centre ofthe
house. A fireplace, or often two fireplaces back to back which
shared a common stack, were placed transversely. Sometimes
the single fireplace backed on to a cross-passage, but the system
economized on space and also ensured that heat from the
fireback and the flue was not wasted by warming an external
wall. In the 17th century the growing appeal of classical, symmetrical proportions with a central door in the facade influenced
vernacular design. As the entrance now faced the centrallyplaced
side jambs of the fireplaces, the ‘lobby-entrance’ house deyeloped. The front door opened on to a small chamber with the
fireplace jambs forming a back wall and often, the support fora
winding staircase. Opposing internal doors opened on to rooms
on either side ofthe lobby entrance.

It has been shown (Mercer) that there were several ways in
which the former domestic and farmhouse plans were adapted
to the lobby-entrance plan, the commonest form being a
sequence ofa fireplace heated parlour, a lobby entrance, the
main room, also heated byafireplace, and a small, attached but
unheated service end. Commencing in the late-r6th century the
lobby-entrance houses spread rapidly, and are to be found from
the Welsh Marches to Anglia, particularly in the south of
England, though somewhat later they permeated the north.

The building of fireplaces and chimneys on the end walls of
houses liberated the plan, though it was less efficient in terms
of heating; the central front door could open on a hall with a
staircase, or, in direct-entty houses, on a central room. Greater
freedom of spatial planning was possible while retaining the

Sue

FHEFTT

u

418

desired symmetry, than was ever practicable with the restricted
space ofthe lobby-entrance.

Lobby-entrance houses are to be found in North America,

but the greater popularity of the I-house plan with end chimneys may have owed much to the freedom that the apparently
formal, symmetrical design offered. In England, many hundreds of 17th- and 18th-century lobby-plan houses are still in
use. Other small, confined spaces in a meeting-house or other
social building where, for example, coats are hung on arrival,
are also termed lobbies ifthey have one or more doors leading
to other rooms. PAUL OLIVER
1V11.h Porch (Finland)
A porch is a roofed entrance structure in front ofan
external door, and protrudes outside the main bulk of the
building into the yard. Itmay have one or two storeys, and may
be open-sided — a mere canopy with supporting columns and
perhaps a balustrade — or enclosed with walls, in which case it
will often have decorative windows. A porch proper always has
access from the yard, and is an intermediary space between the
yard and the main part ofthe building.

A primary function of.a porch with steps is to assist passage
over the threshold, otherwise awkward in buildings with high
plinths. The transition from unsheltered steps to the open
canopy type of porch was primarily due to the desire to protect
outdoor steps and doors from undue weathering, and their
users from rain, snow and water dripping from the eaves. However, the effects ofwind, heavy showers, and driving snow still
prevail, and can only be eliminated by building walls onto the
open porch. Enclosed porches are particularly recommended
in pattern books for buildings having no entrance hall, since an
enclosed porch helps keep cold draughts out and warmth
inside the building. A central entrance hall, when used with an
enclosed porch, pre-heats incoming air for ventilation. Mosquitoes are kept out by a darkened porch area. An enclosed
porch provides a handy space to hang outdoor clothes and its
built-in seating often functions as storage too.

The aesthetic function of all types of porch relates to the
social aspirations ofthe owner. The porch is the main feature of
the elevation to which the eye is first drawn upon approaching
the building. In the vernacular, porch details and colours often
differ markedly from the style ofthe bulk ofthe building. In the
most elegant examples, there may be several porches on the
same building, linked or unlinked to each other. By its size,
form and stylishness, a porch can indicate for example the
proper point of entry for access to the reception room, the
domestic quarters or the kitchen.

Many architectural features of the porch are a result of its
function as a buffer or filter space, so that the inhabitants ofthe
building may to some extent ignore the vagaries of climate —
thus the plinth, roof-slopes designed to throw off rain, snow
and ice, deep overhanging eaves, windows, curtains and so on.
Porches are favoured in constructional traditions of all types,
whether in wooden-, earth- or stone-building regions of the
world. They are also a product of both natural and technocratic
civilizations. The oldest remains of porches have been found in
Stone Age archaeological excavations.

See also joy;

3.V.9.m ri

{Mt Amos, 1969

© national Board of Antiquities, Finland

The Romanesque style ofthe Middle Ages preferred massive
forms; Gothic favoured porches with more slender bevelled
and fluted columns. The external door of the enclosed porch
was often shaped like a keyhole or a horseshoe, as in Norway
and Sweden. In addition to rectangular-plan porches, many
were built with an irregular hexagonal planform, sometimes
called ‘nut-shaped’, and a multi-faceted roof.

Typical ofthe Renaissance porch were balustrade-like pillars
which supported a dual-pitched roof, below each side ofwhich
was a bench. Baroque brought in spiral pillars, and the rococo
went even further with this.

The neoclassicist style, with its antique origins, brought an
increase in the number of columns on the facade from four to
six, the prototype being the antique temple’s portico. Pillars
once more became straight, and the rustication once used in
horizontal courses of stone became translated into wooden
construction, the railings and the walls of porches being made
of shaped horizontal boarding. The first fashion of the time
was fora mansard roofon a porch, but later this became a halfhipped roof. The revivalist styles of the latter part of the ıgth
century brought new features and many more porches were
built during this period. With the Gothic revival the irregular
hexagon-shaped plan returned.

In the reaction against the excessive ornamentation used at
the end ofthe ıgth and beginning ofthe 20th centuries, porches
returned in the 19208 once more to their classical clothing. In the
following decade, as functionalism began to prevail, porches
and verandas became pared down to the bare minimum offeatureless straight lines, often with a lean-to or even a flat roof.
This model is still in favour, but alongside it have emerged
solutions which borrow from the older traditions.

The fashions in porch models spread by copying from the
dwellings ofthe higher social classes (manor houses, vicarages,
and the homes ofarmy officials and merchants) and from public
buildings (e.g. schools and railway stations). The Nordic countries have until quite recently had travelling carpenters in rural

Circulation 1.v1.1

areas who specialized in porches, gradually creatingalocal tradition. Some areas have a particularly rich tradition. For example the
parish of Hälsinglannd in Sweden still has examples ofporches
in every style of the western European architecture of modern
times. Porches were often repeatedly rebuiltfrom the ground up,
for instance by a new owner, ina desire to follow the latest fashion.
In porch-building, as in house-building in general, only
wealthier persons at first relied upon architects and pattern
books. At the end of the ıgth century the pattern books and
home magazines began to appear in greater numbers, and they
aimed to show the ordinary farmer how to build a porch or
veranda. Atthe same time decorative detail-parts for the porch,
such as railings and brackets, became available for purchase
off-the-shelf. TEPPO KORHONEN
TR. GEORGE WOOLSTON

Zudro

There are many different theories about the origins
ofboth the shape and the name ofthe porch called ‘“zudro’. Itis
sometimes derived from the Roman Byzantine portico or from
a simplified arcade gallery (typical of the whole Panonian
region). Although in folk architecture taking over elements
from other, especially older patterns cannot be excluded, the
predominant reason is functionality.

The zudro exists not only in the region of adobe houses, but
similar elements can be seen in brick houses too. Within Moravia
it is in the central and southern regions. Besides that it can be
encountered also in Slovakia, Lower Austria and Hungary.

This element of protected and artistically accentuated
entrance is usually placed in the front ofthe residential part but
sometimes even in front of the entrances of storage rooms,
wine cellars and the like.

The function was not only to provide shelter from the rain or
sun, but also to decorate the entrance and represent the social
position ofthe owner. This can be seen in the artistic treatment
which ranged from the simplest block extensions to porches

1.V11.h-i

419

## S. 160

2

1.v1 SERVICES

decorated with elements taken over from different styles (pillars,
pilasters, ledges, archivolts, and so on). Especially in southeastern Moravia, this element is accompanied with colourful ornamental decoration.

The aim of representing the social position of the owner
(perhaps also in response to fashion) can be seen in cases
where the porch is only suggested with an archivolt made in
earthen stucco above the entrance of a building — sometimes
even of outbuildings. OTAKAR MÄCEL
1VIA.i Ramp
A continuous inclined plane, a ramp is a means of
connecting levels as an alternative to flights of steps. Though
structurally elementary, it requires thata consistent gradient is
maintained throughout its length, thus demanding accuracy in
its construction. Some ofthe most ancient oframps have been
used for circulation devices where animals, such as horses,
oxen, camels or elephants are used as beasts ofburden. Where
the spacing and length of steps that can be negotiated by animals are difficult to estimate, or where they are a hindrance to
themovementofan animal that has to take shorter steps to take
a heavy load, the ramp permits it to adjust with less difficulty.
For this reason ramps were frequently used in forts and defensive structures where armies or officers were mounted.

Similarly, ramps were the only practical means of delivering
loads by wheeled carts or wagons to higher levels. Barns and

420

stores throughout Europe have been constructed so thatfeed or
cereals can be unloaded atan upper level. The ‘bank-barn’ built
against a slope uses the advantage ofthe height ofa hill to give
access at different levels. It has been commonly described, but
itis seldom that it has been constructed without grading ofthe
approach slopes. In many instances the gradient is insufficient
for entryatboth ground and upper floor levels within its length,
and the construction ofa ramp, or artificial hillslope is common. In many cases the ramp approach to a barn has to be so
constructed as to turn a wagon through 90°, requiring the
building ofan incline that follows the turning circle ofa wagon
drawn by a draft animal. To facilitate this and to ensure a
straightapproach to the barn dooraatthetop level, alogor stone
arched bridge between the ramp and the building may be constructed. Such ramp-approach barns which are still in extensive
use have been inadequately identified and documented.

Another function of the ramp is for the handling of heavy
building materials, whose movementis facilitated by the use of
ropes and levers. Heavy tree trunks and monoliths have often
been moved by these means, while the raising of ceremonial
posts or monumental stones has been facilitated by the use of
ramps (Morgan). There have been many theories to explain
how the monumental figures on Easter Island were moved,
Thor Heyerdahl showing that they could be moved with the use
ofa Y-sledge made from a forked tree, and lowered into position by means ofropes and a ramp. Similar means have been
used in Malaysia and the Indonesian islands in this century.

Though ramps have been used for the movement of great
weights, portable ramps were used in pre-industrial building
technology. Some were woven from withies to make a strong
hurdle with crosspieces as steps, in the form ofa combined ladder and ramp. Strong inclined planes built of lashed timber or
bamboo frames of a size sufficient to take two-way “labour
traffic’ were raised against the walls ofa building in the course
oferection in Mogul India (Qaisar).

In recent times ramps are still used by brewers and innkeep-

ers for the lowering and raising of barrels, while men with
wheelbarrows, mothers with perambulators and delivery boys
with bicycles have all had reason to be thankful to theunknown
builders who built ramps beside the long flights of steps that
take them to upper levels oftheir hill-towns.. PAUL OLIVER
1V14.) Road: plank (Sichuan)
China’s ‘Father of the bridge’, Mao Yi-sheng, once
declared that the plank roads were China’s third wonder after
the Great Wall and the Grant Canal. These plank roads are distributed mainly in Sichuan, the reasons being that as Sichuan is
a basin, separated from other provinces by mountains and
rivers with sheer precipices and hangingrocks, itis essential to
build such roads in order for people to pass in and out.

There are four main types of plank road. The standard type
has wooden posts erected in the water supporting beams which
are then boarded. The second type is based on frames built
along the slopes. Holes are dug in the slopes for the beams,
under which posts are placed, also in dug holes, either vertically or at an angle to support the beams which are then
boarded. The third type is only used on steep cliffs where no

posts can be installed in the water. At such a place one hole is
first dug in the upper part of the stone wall into which a long
beam is installed and then another hole is made several metres
lower into which a shorter beam is installed. Finally the upper
beam is supported by the lower one by using sloping posts and
the upper beams are then boarded. As protection against
falling stones and running water, some of these types of road
have ceilings or roofs. The fourth type of plank road has no
posts, but holes are dug in the stone wall into which beams are
installed and boarded. This type occurs in the places where
there are steep cliffs and deep water. JI FUZHENG
1VlA.k Staircase

The staircase is a primary element of architecture, a
human necessity and a unique mode ofexpression. From modest dwelling to spectacular palace, the presence ofthe staircase
generates primary space be it interior or exterior. Sometimes it
becomes the space itself in the conception as, for example, in
some public buildings like stadiums and arenas.

The symbolic power of the staircase can be immense. “To
climb’ represents a difficulty for a human being, butatthe end
or the summit, recompense, rest, tranquillity or exaltation can
be found. “The climb’ is an ideal, a purification and a detachment from earthly constraints.

The staircase defines a transitional space in an architectural
form such as a tower or pyramid. But more frequently, the stair
is an intermediate element between two levels and rarely is it
only a materialization. Consequently, it is impossible to study a
staircase outside of its environment. Its spatial role is ambivalent: atthe same time itis a liaison and separation, a continuity
and an interruption. And the dynamism created by obliquity is
a natural element of contrast against the horizontality of the
flooring and the verticality ofthe walls.

In its placing, dimension and form, the staircase announces
the space to which it leads: from the street to the habitation,

m]

Circulation 1.11

A Sans eyuawaja pue Augny auapaucr

from the square to the church, from the public to the private,
from the profane to the sacred. Among the numerous varieties
ofstaircase constructions, two important groups can be distinguished: those which have the same points of departure and
arrival, superposed, and those which unfold, the point of
arrival being far from its departure.

Human beings have to submit to the force of gravity: they
move through the air but are attached to the ground by this
force. They are situated in a limited zone between two elements: earth and air. This is banal but an important, fundamental reference point. The force of gravity and morphology of
the human body render the adaptation to a non-horizontal
plane quite difficult. In effect, inclined planes and ramps can
only be used ifthey are nottoo steep. For humans, a horizontal
surface is indispensable.

Perfect horizontality does not exist in nature except for the
surface ofwater. Uneven ground is rolled and rendered even for
domestic life. It is difficult to imagine building a shelter without preparing flat ground or choosing a very level site. The
meeting point between the natural ground and the flattened
part, the point which limits the natural level and the prepared
level, is, in effect, the archetype ofthe staircase. Even ifitis only
a simple intersection between a natural sloping surface and a
prepared horizontal surface which is crossed with one step, itis
a human creation that permits this as, for example, in a house
at Konwewa, Sri Lanka, with two raised platforms facing each
other in along, open space. Sometimes one or two steps define
the normal passage, facilitating ascension, but in general,
mounting is possible everywhere. The height of platforms is
suitable for use as a seat or table.

If space is wanted or for the imperative of spatial distribution, the length ofthe staircase can be made shorter by curving
the line oftravel while the flight remains rectangular. It is possible to place one or more landings laterally rather than facing
the stairwell. In this case, the treads must be turned. Another
reason for turned stairs is to permit more steps in the same
available space. Turned stairs sometimes necessitate several
operational adjustments.

Of course, staircases do not have only straight flights. Everyone is aware ofthe potential extravagance and embellishment of
astaircase, like those ofChambord and Fontainebleau castles in
France. In this regard, the small, vernacular construction is not

421

## S. 161

EEE EETEESSTEREIEBRIEERL ER LTEN En ner

1.v1 SERVICES

the best area for investigation: the device of embellishment or
splendour is non-existent and the effect of extravagance is
found rather in major architecture. However, even if the staircase in the vernacular construction is rarely ornamental, it can
be built in a complicated layout. But when there are spiral, or
curved staircases, the reason for them is essentially practical.

A spiral staircase is a turned staircase as all the treads are
trapezoidal and the line of travel is circular or elliptic, of the
kind where the path turns on itselfat each stage. A rectangular
stairwell is not necessary. This is illuminated by ‘La Caree’ at
Poliez-le-Grand, Vaud, Switzerland. This differs from other
Vaudoise farms, which are typically tripartite, by dissociation of
the animal and human quarters and by superposition of more
than two levels, giving it the appearance of a tower. The staircase is situated in the northeast ofthe house and begins with
some straight steps followed bya spiral stair round a pinenewel
post. The latter is made of pine logs held together bytenon and
mortisejoints. FREDERIC AUBRY and PLEMENKA SUPIC
1.V11.K-i Staircase (England)

The earliest type of wooden stair in England, used
throughout the medieval period, was the stair-ladder. It consisted of solid triangular blocks ofoak nailed onto two sloping
beams (Martin). Solid treads were also used in some newel
stairs in the 16th and early-r7th centuries. By the late-ı6th century the composite stair had been developed, with separate
treads and risers forming the steps. Many newel stairs were of
this type, as were the similar and very common winder stairs.
This compact design, concealed behind doors top and bottom,
remained the norm in smaller houses and cottages until the
20th century. More spacious houses had a straight stair or the
more ostentatious dog-leg or open-well stair, which were
developed by 1600. The simpler versions of the dog-leg have a
partition wall between the flights, butin the later 17th century it
was common to have a balustrade. The upper and lower flight
balustrades usually ran one behind the other, with a double
newel post or pair of posts at the junction. Occasionally more
complex designs with a single shared newel post were created.
Open-well stairs were popular forms of display in larger
houses. Sometimes the well was closed, either by a solid
masonty core or by a stud, lath and plaster framework. The
builder ofa stair in Kent ingeniously created a series ofbuilt-in
cupboards in the unusually small well. LINDA HALL

1V11.k-i Stairs: external (Australia)

Elaborate front stairs are often a dominant component ofthe typical wooden houses of subtropical and tropical
Australia. Comfortable but not lavish in dimensions, these
houses rapidly filled the new subdivisions in the growing towns
and cities until about the Second World War. The almost universal construction method comprises astud frame, suspended
on hardwood stumps 2 m (6.5 ft) or so high, set into often sloping ground, and reached by timber steps at frontand back. The
“front steps’ and ‘back steps’ became associated with respectively formal and informal domestic behaviour.

In a streetscape of houses similar in form, block size, and
date ofconstruction, the treatment offront steps, their variable
length and route to the ground afforded some opportunity for
individual expression at little cost. Irregular hills and gentle
slopes made up the characteristic topography ofmany towns on
the eastern coastal slopes. The conventions ofa gridded street
plan imposed on this diverse topography, and ofhouses addressing the street, created a pattern of rows of one-storey houses
with stumps, verandas and steps. The visual dominance ofa
projecting staircase could serve either to restate a frontal symmetty, or to further enhance the asymmetry ofa sloping site.

Inabenign climate these houses do notrequire impermeability. On the contrary the progression from public to private space
is punctuated by various building elements, which progressively
modify the microclimate. Leading from publicly visible front
gate and garden to partiallyscreened veranda, front doorandinterior, the frontstairs provide a ceremony ofapproach and entry.

There might be as few as five steps or upwards of twenty,
framed with hardwood stringers, risers and treads often ım @ ft)
wide, sometimes much wider. Flanking handrails are usually
balustraded with vertical hardwood slats, dowels, or in earlier
examples decorative cast iron, to match the adjoining landing
and veranda. Frequently the partially enclosing balustrade is
carried up to frame an open canopy, further enhancing the effect
of ceremony. High flights of stairs offer the opportunity for
another landing at half-height, the lower flight then turning at
right angles parallel to the house front, or not uncommonly
descending to the ground in opposite directions as a doubled

lower flight. IAN SINNAMON

1.v11.l Steps
‘Suspended steps’ or ‘a la frangaise‘ (Guadet) are steps
which are attached on one side only to a wall. The other side
seems to be suspended in mid-air. The solidity and stability of
these steps depends on three points: the deflection of each step
which is proportional to the square of the length of the overhang; the shearing ofthe step support, which must be secured,
and the load distribution over the biggest possible area; and the
equilibrium of the load because only the wall, inside of which
the step is put, assures the stability. It is necessary that the part
embedded in the wall is loaded in equilibrium so thatthe weight
ofthe step and its working load can be borne.

The materials to construct these steps are stone and wood.
The stone must be cut in a thick slab to minimize its own
weight and to be sufficiently resistant to deflection. A crystal
rock in layers simple to separate, such as gneiss or granite, is
chosen. Wood, despite its performance in flexion and its lighter
weight, is cut more easily than stone but has less resistance to
loads perpendicular to its fibre. Also, if the wood is inside the
wall it rots so is less easy to use.

In the Tessin valleys in Switzerland stone is abundant. This
stone is used because it is easily cut into thick slabs for use in
masonry- horizontal layers for walls or vertical layers for vaults
- or for coating materials, roof covering, kitchen floors and
stairs. A semi-detached house in Sonogno, Val Verzasca,
Tessin, is high and compact with a superimposed cellar,
kitchen, room and granary and all levels are served by steps ina
particular way. The cellar is partlyunderground, the store being
under the earth while the long passage, which gives access to

Circulation

COUPE A-a

the cellar through doors, is on the lower part ofthe slope with
only its lower wallagainstthe earth. Steps lead down to the passage. The kitchen is reached by crossing a stone ‘bridge’, the
roofofthe passage, and the room above by an external double
stone flight with a shared wooden platform leading onto a
gallery from where there is entry to the room. Finally, a third
step gives access to the granary, going from the end of the
gallery round the corner and arrivingatthe door in the centre of
the gable wall. The steps, platform and overhanging lintel are
embedded in the wall. The steps are audacious, apparently
fragile and simple. They seem to be in flight and do not affect
the mass ofthe construction.

FREDERIC AUBRY andPLEMENKA SUPIC
1V11.l-1 Steps: external (Greece)
One characteristic phenomenon of buildings with
flatroofs is a variety of steps onto these roofs. To understand the
structure and form ofthe steps itis necessary to describe how the
roofs are used in the Greek islands. First, they cover therooms,
but they are also used for sleeping (although this is unusual on
Siphnos because ofthe high relative atmospheric moisture and
nocturnal dew), for collecting rain water for cisterns (their main
use on Siphnos) and for dıying fruits and leaves as food for animals (usually on the roofs of secondary buildings).

People set foot on the roofs which are built as basins for collecting water only for cleaning, repairing and drawing water;
the roofs, therefore, are not built for permanent burden, since
no oneislivingon them. Neither are the steps onto them representative ofregular flights, comfortable or secure. Rather, they

423

## S. 162

WW 035

1.v1 SERVICES

are extremely high with uncomfortable sequences and a minimal width (about 40 cm [16 in]) and lack the security of banisters or walls. They can only be used with a high level of
concentration — in contrast to the unconscious use of comfort-

able and secure stair flights in dwelling areas.

As there are no standard requirements, there is great flexibility in the form of structures which perform as external stair
flights to theroofs, all such steps are effortlessly integrated into
existing situations of buildings with no one step similar to
another. There can be simple projections atthe edge ofa building, walls which are built up as steps to the roof, flights along
walls and steps from one roofto another. Often one kind ofstep
turns into one or two other types in one situation. So simple
elements are used to develop various forms corresponding
to the typical structure of Cyeladic architecture.

ERIEDRICH C.WAGNER

1VI1.m Veranda
Verandas were an indigenous medieval feature ofthe

vernacular architecture of the northern provinces of Portugal,
such as Entre Minho e Douro and Träs-os-Montes, and to amore
limited extent ofthe border regions of Spain. The word veranda is
found as earlyas the end ofthe 15th centuryand the beginning of
the 16th in Spanish and Portuguese (Minshen: ‘Varanda, railes to
leane the brest on’ baranda in Spanish) and apparently is derived
from Latin vara, aforked pole orrod. Vasco daGama’s chronicler
used the term to describe the characteristic exteriors of the
Indian houses seen from the sea before landing in India.

Verandas appear in the surviving drawings ofearly Brazil and
were in use before the end of the 17th century throughout the
West Indies, as well as in Portuguese and Dutch possessions in
the East. They were used to provide shade, both for open-air
terraces and to protect walls and windows from the heat ofthe
sun. They also served, in climates with heavy rain and some
humidity, as dry open-air spaces in which cooling breezes
could be obtained. In some vernaculars, verandas served to
provide covered access between rooms, taking on the function
ofgalleries.

424

From the West Indies the veranda was imported into the
North American colonies, where it had a tremendous vogue
during the ı8th century, sometimes under the neoclassical
name of ‘piazza’. Veranda railings were frequently decorative.
At first they were made of wood, using patterns that were
frequently Chinese and seem to have been adapted from early
chinoiserie pattern books. Sometimes latticework enclosed
the upper parts of verandas as well. Later, wrought iron, and
eventually cast iron, railings became fashionable.

Portuguese and Spanish colonial verandas were generally
built along the fronts or backs ofthe houses, but by 1640 some
buildings of Jamaica, and by 1740 some buildings in Georgia,
had verandas on three sides or all round, that is they were progenitors ofthe later 1gth-century ‘veranda house’ (Nichols). One
ofthe earliest recorded instances ofthe veranda in England was
a house of that form called, not surprisingly, an ‘American
cottage’ (Plaw).

The adoption ofthe verandaas a feature offashionable architecture in Britain and on the Continent followed rapidly. In Persuasion (published 1818) Jane Austen introduced a cottage with
a verandah, French windows and other prettiness’. Bythe period
ofthe Regency they were at the very height of fashion, and had
begun to appear (especially in the form ofroofed balconies) in
Bath, Cheltenham, and London.

There is no doubtthatthe veranda was par excellence a colonial
importation into Britain, and examples in even such new settlements as Australia (1788) appear to antedate the great popularity ofthe device in northern Europe. When the exile ofNapoleon
brought St Helena into prominence in 1816, many ofthe buildings there already had verandas, as can be seen from the
numerous engravings ofthe island published at the time.

Verandas continued to grow in popularity throughout the
19th century. Single- or multi-storey verandas were characteristic features of architecture in late-ıgth and early-2oth century
American vernaculars, as well as throughout colonial Asia,
Africa and Australia, and were often built in northern Europe.

West African vernacular roof overhangs on poles, ofveranda
type, may be an indigenous development, but it is quite possible

that they reflect the influence ofPortuguese and other European
colonial buildings from the 16th century onwards.
RONALD LEWCOCK

1V11.m-i Veranda (Australia)

The veranda was part ofan extensive global multinational vernacular architecture found in European colonial settlements and trading factories which arose in response to the
climatic challenge presented by the tropics. It solved a number
of problems simultaneously: firstly, it provided an outside
gallery circulation between rooms; secondly, it protected walls
from damage during torrential rain; and thirdly, it shaded the
house and kept it cool. The veranda was exposed to breezes
coming from all sides, moreover, windows and doors could be
left open during heavy downpours when the humidity was high.

The firstveranda builtin Australia was in 1793, only fiveyears
after Sydney was settled. By the end of the Napoleonie war in
1814, along with the Picturesque came cottage verandas, either
as simple cottages, with verandas added to the front and sides

Tsakau, E.
Wagner, Fi

See also

ofthe basic Georgian box, or as bungalows with verandas on all
sides joined into the roofatthe roof plate.

The deployment of verandas was at first confined to viceregal and other high-style residences belonging to military
officers and the colonial elite to indicate their superior status.
Within 15 years it had spread to the homes of well-to-do merchants and wealthy settlers, with the exception of Tasmania.

Their initial acceptance was restricted by the availability of
suitable roofing materials for the canopy, but with the arrival of
corrugated iron in quantity after 1850, verandas became an
established feature in the country and city alike. At first corrugated iron was used for quickly constructed veranda awnings
over the footpaths. A light frame of hardwood joists and battens was thrown between the facade ofthe building and widely
spaced timber posts at the kerbside. By 1833 verandas were an
integral partofthe building scene. Itwas soon necessary to regulate them by passing a Building Act which specified that street
awnings should beatleast2.ım (7 ft) high and the posts placed
close to the outer edge of the doorway. In Melbourne, shade
was provided by uniform verandas or verandaways that had to
be 3.7 m (12 ft) wide, at least 3 m (10 ft) high and made of
masonry, brick, metal or hardwood.

In countty towns there were no such restrictions. Verandas
found their way into the main country towns byway ofthesmall
country towns, many ofwhich had sprung up around isolated
rural houses and inns where the virtues of verandas had long
been appreciated. In Western Australiaand Queensland, verandas ran around the house and the plan was often extended later
by adding a further two rooms at the back in the form of a lowpitched skillion.

ERAIAIA

I

Circulation 1.V1.1

When the bungalow from India reached Australia in the late
1820s verandas were extended on all sides. This type survived
for over a century, until 1940. The bungalow is a familiar sight
all over country Australia with its high crowned pyramidshaped roof and dropping verandas. During the Victorian
period, the bungalow plan was modified by making one front
room, usually on the left-hand facing the street, thrust forward
in line with the front veranda which was thereby curtailed. The
veranda was omitted from one or both sides and the front
veranda reduced to little more than a porch. Its one remaining
function was to shelter the front door.

The ‘Queenslander’ is a special case of the bungalow reerected on stilts or extended stumps with deep 3.7 m (12 ft)
verandas on all sides. The hotter the climate and the rougher
the life, the more the veranda was used. In the tropical north of
Western Australia, in Queensland and the Northern Territory,
the pioneers started on the lines laid down in New South Wales
butthey soon found thatthe veranda was more than a shelter to
the rooms - it was a living-room in its own right. Gradually it
grew to 3.1-4.6. m (10-15 ft) in depthand therooms contracted.
In the dry season it was elaborately furnished with dining-room
table, chairs and side-tables, even pictures — all the paraphernalia ofa cool climate were moved into the heavilyshaded open
air. Even the most primitive slab-and-bark huts had verandas,
sometimes covered with bark or shingles.

With the coming of row housing in the cities after 1860, narrow ı.2m (4 ft) verandas were attached to the fronts ofterraced
houses in the new inner city suburbs that sprang up, notably
during the booming 1880s where they were embellished with
imported and locally made cast iron balustrades and column
bracket decoration. Elaborate timber brackets based on American patterns were also extremely popular. They were placed on
the street frontage regardless oforientation and were shallower.

The veranda is represented in an impressively diverse range
of building types. The early verandas had stone flagged terraces, with canopies of oilcloth on timber boards being used at
first, and often bark or shingles in the cruder constructions; flat
metal sheets replacing them for the more sophisticated

Regency draped tent-canopy. With the advent of corrugated
iron, bull-nosed, and double curved cyma recta and cyma reversa
profiles came into wide use. The typical veranda was 2.4-3 m
(8-10 ft) deep. Hotel verandas were more generous, ranging
from 3.7 m (12 ft) to 5.5 m (18 ft) deep and were generally twostoreyed. In north Queensland, the first veranda was sometimes surrounded by a second in two layers called the ‘Charters
Towers’ veranda. Verandas on country hotels and station
homesteads could be quite extensive. PHILIP DREW
1.V11.m-ii Veranda (North America)

The veranda or ‘porch’ as itis customarily termed in
the United States, is a roofed living area on the ground floor
(often on araised foundation), that may be open, partiallywalled
orlatticed, or almost fully enclosed. Itprovides horizontal space
for socializing, working and sleeping, and a centrifugal relation to the external environmentand social sphere. Because itis
between the dwelling’scorerooms and the outside, the veranda
mediates the inhabitants’ relationships, providing both shelter

425

## S. 163

EEE

1.v1 SERVICES

and safety from, as well as the positively preferred range and
mode of relation to, their natural and social surroundings. It
modulates climatic effects, providing access to more warming
sun or cooling breezes and to experiences of nature, and provides a site for social relations, the intersection of private and
public realms, and manifestations of status. .

The features and appearance of specific verandas vary with
the overall form oftthe building. Nevertheless a basic five-type
taxonomy in terms of roof, floor and walls holds across local
and regional articulations with various materials, forms, uses
and symbolism.

Averanda may either be integral to the envelope ofadwelling
or be an addition to it. In terms of construction, the integral
veranda is made by not completely walling one or more of the
rooms; in terms of form, it appears as a void within the main
structure’s envelope. It is perceived as integral to the main
structure because it completes the volume suggested by a continuous roof and a continuous floor. A veranda is attached
when its pitched or flat roof and floor make an already whole
form more complicated.

e: Fr

426

Whether inset or attached, the veranda may extend upwards
partially or to a building’s full height. Galleries and balconies
are similar elements found on a building’s upper floors; supported by columns or cantilevered, these may occur in conjunction with a veranda or independently. The veranda may appear
on a dwelling’s front, back, side or corner; in extent it may be
only partial on one side, multi-sided, or encircling. The degree
of enclosure has a wide range: open except for supporting
columns or poles, lower articulation (e.g. with a balustrade)
between columns, fully articulated (e.g. latticed), fully articulated ina more dense manner (e.g. wire- or mesh-screened), or
almost fully articulated and enclosed with solid material (if
fully closed with solid material the veranda normally is considered to have become another room and not a veranda). The
overall appearance ofthe veranda especially varies in relation to
the complexity ofits and the dwelling’s principal roof, so that
striking differences often result depending on whether the
veranda is beneath a continuous, continuous-pitched, dualpitched, dropped or attached roof. The possible combinations
that are the product ofthe variations ofthese five forms largely
accounts for the enormous variety among verandas.

Though simplicity itself, the veranda is mysteriously complex
in meaning and relation to similar elements. Problems of
description and analysis arise at every turn, especially through
issues oforigin. Theverandais related to areas under open-sided
freestanding shelters, to areas undertheraised covers oftents on
poles or posts, and to areas under platforms or buildings raised
for protection. ‘Veranda’ is not atall a univocal term: often the
same element is called by other names such as piazza, galerie,
portale, loggia, cardak, and ajat; yet, just as often the veranda is
thoughtto be distinct in some aspects from such elements.

ROBERT MUGERAUER

ıvı2 Cooking and Heating

The hearth in all its diversity is the commonest signification of dwelling across the widest range of cultures.
Regardless of climate, it is atthe heart offood preparation and
cooking and usually hospitality too. The fuel which is burnt,
and the attendant utensils for the production of fire and its
husbandıy and for culinary activities, tell usa greatdealabouta
culture. These utensils, along with those for harvesting and
killing, have always been amongst the commonest heritable
possessions of those that use them. In 14th-century England
the substantial breastummer beam that supported the hearth
opening ofthe peasants’ cottage was a valued heritable item far
more durable than the flimsy walling that it supported. Similarly, the three-legged bronze or bell-metal skillets ofnorthern
Europe, passed on from generation to generation, demand the
construction ofa very simple form of hearth for their adequate
function, and no doubt acted as a brake on the development of
the cooking hearth, prior to the spread of coal and its derivatives as common fuels. Changes in the nature and configuration of fuel, crop and cooking, usually mark major changes in
lifestyle and culture.

The human imperatives in the seasonal and diurnal rounds
for the husbandıy, preparation and cooking offood ensure that
these essential and repetitive activities always leave their mark
on our habitations. ‘Hearth and home’ are inextricably linked
in most cultures and the arrangement offood preparation and
cooking is no less structured in the domestic setting of many
nomadic groups than it is in the more durable dwellings of
more sedentary peoples.

The relationships between the hearth and cooking and the
other dwelling areas vary considerably - and not only as a
response to climate. The segregation of these activities from
the other activities ofdwelling is often a product ofthe desire to
eliminate or diminish the effects of smoke and the moisture
and odour of cooking. In cooler climates this is often only
achieved where fuel is plentiful — permitting the use of more
than one heat sourcee-and where non-flammable materials are
available for the construction oflues.

In many cultures the work activities surrounding the hearth
are mostly or entirely female and the separation ofthe hearth
from the male domain achieves a segregation that is rooted in
thelifestyle. Insome places the hearth is conversely the focus of
mixed or masculine sociability and hospitality, sometimes permanently and sometimes partially separated in the daily cycle
from the activity of cooking. The Barabaig herdsmen of East
Africa have separate hearths in the rooms or huts ofhusbands
and wives, the former almost exclusively for heating, whilstthe
wife prepares meals on the fire in her own room.

The areas set aside for food preparation and cooking are
often the exclusive domain ofwomen and attitudes to food, its
preparation, storage and consumption, are key sources of cultural attitudes, knowledge and transfer. However, the basic
gender separation of such activities is often not rigid. Whilst
cooking is primarily an activity for the women of the Ainu of
southern Sakhalin, the men will boil and simmer meat when
the women are gathering plants.

Symbolic foods and occasions for eating are rehearsed in an
often explicit reaffirmation ofthe co-operative-and subjugative
-telations between participants in terms of gender, status and or
rank. Here again, such acts of hospitality and the conventions
surrounding them may often differ, depending upon the specialness ofthe occasion or the status ofthe guests. Igbo women of
southeast Nigeria may dispense hospitality to neighbours and
their children from the regular family kitchen on a reciprocal
basis, whilst the sharing of kola nuts with an important guest
requires considerable ceremony and the active recognition and
participation ofthe social hierarchy.

The relationship of food storage and preparation to the
hearth is often dominated by shelter responses to climate. Perishable food storage tends to be more extensive in sedentary
communities and particularly in temperate and Mediterranean
settings. Across a wide range of cultures smoking and fumigation are key methods employed to assist food Preservation.

The husbanding ofthe fire as the heat source for cookingand
for comfort, is an essential part of the daily round. On open
hearths in much of Europe the coupe de feu, or curfew, evolved to

427

## S. 164

1vı SERVICES

maintain the embers over night, whilst in alpine and aiher central European and Scandinavian regions, after cooking, the
embers would be returned to the enclosed oven, sometimes
fuelled from the kitchen or passage to warm the parlour (stuebe)
with its family table and Herr Gottes Eck (God’s corner). .
In many cultures the domestic hearth has provided the primary night-time light source. In Europe, the larger firedogs
that were used to support burning logs were developed to
incorporate cressets or openwork cages on top in which
torches could be burnt to extend the lighting effect of the
hearth. More flexible iron ‘peermen’, wooden-based iron holders, were used in countries where fir-trees were abundant. Dry
splints of fir were lit from the hearth and wedged between the
jaws ofthe peerman. Most domesticlighting used the proces
or by products ofcooking- oil for lamps, oils and fats for rushlights and tallow and beeswax for candles.

The quest for better controlled, longer burning, saferand on
smokey space heating, led some cultures to the development o
an enclosed combustion chamber. Surrounding a firebox with
a mass of usually clay-based material prolonged and extended
the heating effects by using a larger surface area and the thermal storage potential ofthe mass construction. The introduction ofa jacket of air between the firebox and the surrounding
structure further extended the capacity of an oven to provide
heating- by conducted warm air- to rooms other than the one
in which it was situated. This sort of system was elaborately
developed in ancientChina in the kang to provide both localized
and background warmth. Enclosed ovens were also known io the
ancient Greeks, who used them for baking and water heating.

In central Europe, tiled ovens for heating have been traced
back to the th century AD. These ovens for room heating, and
some limited cookingareacommon feature ofcentral European
and alpine regions, Scandinavia, the Baltic and Russia. ce
Tyrol, ovens such as these were primarily constructed in brickwork with a lime-plaster render and whitewash finish. Surrounded by timber benches on the two sides projecting into the
room, these ovens like many others, also had a range oftimber
racks and rails for dryingand warming clothingand foodstuffs.
Occasionally, a shelf above the oven provided a Warm. bunk.
Since the 18th century ovens functioning on the same prineiple
have also been constructed almostentirelyinsheetand castiron.

In cooler climates these requirements for human warmth

©

have generally led to food storage away from the main living
areas, either in connected pantries on the sunless side of the
dwelling, or in dairies, earth-dug root sheds, ice houses and
caves beyond the house. The dependency on summer crops and
animals too expensive to feed through the winter has led na
peoples to specialized food storage in the cellars and ventilated

attics oftheir homes. j 1

The development of food preservation techniques in
different cultures has also led to particular approaches to cooking. Smoking, in particular often dictates that the domestic
economy is best served by regular cooking on open hearths,
where meats may be preserved in the flue at little or no extra
cost to the requirements of heating and cooking. Sometimes
more specialized systems of curing have been developed
involving purpose-built smoke houses. In general, the heating
of water for culinary and cleaning purposes is also usually
achieved as a by-product of fuel burning for heating.

There remains a fundamental distinction between those cul-

“ tures that cook almost exclusively over the fire, and those that
regularly employ enclosed, oven-style cooking. In most of Asia
and the Indian subcontinent cooking is mainly over the fire,
with notable exceptions such as the use of the clay oven dan
door) in northwest India. The aboriginal peoples of Australia
also primarily cook over the open hearth, though they too have
contrived enclosed cooking techniques with hot stones and
foods buried in the sand for slow cooking. In tropical, subtropical and even Mediterranean climates wherever possible cooking takes place in sheltered external spaces. Colonial settlers
across the world used kitchens separated from the mail
dwelling by breezeways to minimize the additional heating of
the dwelling by the cooking fires.

For many ordinary households in cultures that enjoy the
products of oven-based cookery the baking of leavened bread
and the oven-roasting of meat has often been beyond everyday
means, leading to the artisan specialism of bakery even in
remote rural locations. There was a tradition amongst the
urban poor in England during the 18th and ıgth centuries of
taking the rarely enjoyed joint of‘butcher’s meat’ to the baker,
to beroasted in his bread oven. Where households could afford
the construction and running costs ofa bread oven, this was
usually adjacent to the main hearth, so that the fire might be
easily transferred and so that domestic spaces could benefit
from the warmth ofthe oven. In many cultures the occupation

Outdoor Clay oyg,
northeast india,

Ian J. G., 1989

älfence, 1964

@Pauloliver

u - Se a EEE EEE EEE EEE

ofliving-room which is separate from theroom containing the
culinary hearth is taken as a mark ofcivilization or sophistication. The dictates ofdomestic economy often result in this second living-toom remaining unheated. This in turn means that
even in relatively industrialized economies, this cool room may
be utilized for casual food storage.

Just as the hearth dictates the nature ofthe basic living-room,
the access to water often dictates the location and configuration
of the activities of cleaning. In the northern hemisphere, the
Pervasive smells and vapours of heating and cooking are often
tolerated in ordinary living spaces. Generally, the presence of
flowing cold water is not. Internal wells, pumps and piped water
outlets are normally housed in rooms away from the hearth.
Floors elsewhere warmed to the touch by strewing or carpeting
give way in these rooms to unadorned relatively impervious
materials. In meat-eating or cheese-making societies the
requirements of preparation using copious quantities of water
further justify this separation. In many places the external location ofpumps, wells or streams for domestic water is accompanied by the provision of external tables, sinks and slop stones
for animal slaughter and other food preparation, as well as the
cleaning of utensils and clothes. RICHARD HAYWARD
1Vl2.a Andirons, spits and firebacks
Probably the earliest piece of domestic equipment
used to make the hearth more serviceable was the firedog, anumber ofexamples of Celtic origin having been excavated. It consists ofa length ofbar-iron which terminates at each endinan
upright, supported byarched legs, which have the effect ofraising the bar several inches. The vertical supports, which were
sometimes worked into stylized animal heads, permitted logs
ofunequal length to be placed between a pair offiredogs. With
the evolution of the wall fireplace firedogs faced forward, the
vertical risingatthe hearth opening. ‘Creepers’ were small dogs,
with short uprights, which supported logs and firebrands withoutobstructingthe movement ofa chimney crane and its burden.

Firedogs and brand-irons were sometimes elaborated with
wrought finials, or legs formed with scrollwork. Larger dogs,
termed ‘andirons’, were toothed like crooks (see 1.V1.2.g-i), or
they had three or four hooks welded on the upright. Against
these would rest the wrought iron firetongs, toasting forks,
meat forks, skimmers, salamanders and other long-handled

Cooking and Heating 1.v1.2

implements of the hearth. Some uprights terminated in iron
cup-shaped frames, the so-called ‘brandy-warmers’.

Across a pair of andirons fitted with hooks, long spiked
‘spits’ were placed, which could be threaded through the carcases ofsuckling pigs. Some had wire baskets in which to roast
fowl. With a crank handle they could be revolved by.a ‘turnspit’, a child or minion who endured the heat ofthe fireto roast
the meat. A pulley wheel replaced the handle and a chain drive
from a small, wall-mounted, dog-powered treadmill was
sometimes used. Consisting of a pair of uprights with their
leading edges shaped to receive the spits, the ‘clevey’ (Irish) or
spit-rack was usually affixed to the chimney-breast. In larger
houses the draught of air from a fierce fire was used to turn a
fan in the chimney which in turn Ooperated the spits; the
‘shadrack’ or small, underfloor blast furnace had a similar
effect, a few still being in use before World War I, though clockwork-driven spit-jacks, made in iron or brass had been in use
for two centuries.

By the 17th century heavy, often erude, cast-iron dogs were
being made, sometimes bearing simplified classical devices

which contributed to the popular dissemination of palm leaf,
fleur-de-lis and volute motifs. The heat generated by log fires
could cause considerable damage to the rear ofthe chimney,
causing the stones to crack and bricks to burn. Cast iron
firebacks were used both to protect the walls and, though
smoke-blackened, to reflect the heat. Moulded in sand they
were castby the ironmasters with anyarticles thatwere athand,
such as items of cutlery, being impressed in thewooden mould
to make decorative shapes. Dates, initials, toses, hands,
heraldic bearings, even allegorical figures were cast, and the
iron plates crested with Renaissance-derived pediments, as the
ironworkers became more proficient and designs from the
Netherlands influenced their work. Iron firebacks were made
throughout western and central Europe, and in North America.
Many were still being made in the Igth century and remain in
use with open fires. PAUL OLIVER

1.V1.2.b Bake oven: Greek

Whether the domed bake oven diffused from the
East, or whether it developed separately in Greece is as yet
unknown. The earliest archaeological evidence for a built form
of oven in Greece dates to the early neolithic era (6200-5300
BC). The bake oven continued to evolve from various types of‘
mud-based cooking structures for several millennia, The standardization in fabric and building technology which occurred
during Roman times remains unchanged to the Present.

In rural Greece, the outdoor bake oven (phornös) is built of
mud-bricks, fired bricks or broken terracotta rooftiles bonded
together by mud to form either a corbelled or vaulted dome.
Located outdoors within a few steps ofthe dwelling, the oven is
either freestanding or abutting an outbuilding; some are built
continguous to the house. The average oven floor diameter is
about1.2m (4 fü; thedome averages aboutım (3 ft) high. Usually constructed on a platform, the oven is frequently encased
by walls ofstone or cement block, and covered with ashed roof
oftile or corrugated metal. In addition to purchased building
materials, the mud and stone are gathered locally, while the

429

## S. 165

EEE

1.vl SERVICES

rooftiles and mud-bricks may be re-used from older structures.
Ignited kindling of dry weeds or faggots, fired on the oven
floor, heats it. When the desired temperature is reached, the
embers are raked aside to make room for pans of food. The
oven mouth is then closed for baking.

Earlier in the aoth century, it was often women who built the
ovens; today itis more usual for a male family member to build
them. When affordable, however, the preference is to hire a
master-craftsman (mastoras) who can constructa superior oven
in less than a day, assisted by either a hired helper or by members ofthe family for whom the oven is being built.

Older, rural women regularly bake bread and other foods
such as meats, vegetables and sweets in the outdoor oven. Girls
learn the methods of food preparation in the oven, and even
young boys perform related tasks. Men use the oven only on
festive occasions. The introduction ofmodern, indoor cooking
equipment and the wide availability of commercially manufactured bread in recent decades, however, makes the oven redundant; therefore, many younger women have abandoned the
outdoor bake oven. H. B. FOSTER

1.V1.2.b-i Bread oven: Syrian

Ofallthe crops grown in the eastern Mediterranean,
wheat holds the select position; made from it are bread and
bourghoul, or cracked wheat, ofage-old importance in the Levant, and indeed, decisive elements of its history. In rural Idlib
and Aleppo in northern Syria, dry crop-growing regions where
wheat and barley make up the basis of rural life, the peasants
have always ground their grain, kneaded their flour and made
their own bread. In allthese areas, breadmaking, a traditionally
female ritual, is carried out in each family for several days. The
bread is made ofdough mixed with yeastand kneaded atlength
by hand; it is then divided into balls which are carefully
stretched out into a flat, round shape, very wide and thin.

Bach extended family owns a rustic bread oven (tannour), built
inside a fire-chamber: a vaulted space surmounted by a domestic cupola flanked by the kitchen, the hen-house and the dovecot
in front ofthe house, and located in the shady work area specially laid out in the yard surrounding the house. Families owning no oven can cook their bread in that oftheir neighbours, as
longas they provide the firewood. Thetannour is made by means
ofan ovoid cavity ofbeaten earth roughcast with glazing on the

430

inside; the design is round and of decreasing diameter so as to
form a cone half open at the top. The diameter measures 1.5 m
(5 fi) at the base, and about ı m (3 fi) at the top, constructed
according to the corbelled vault system ofunfired earthen bricks
measuring 18x 35 Xı5 cm (7X14X 6 in). As for the process of
building the fire-chamber itself, it is fairly analogous to the
method employed for building the silo cupola.

To heat the oven the fire is filled with branches or brushwood
and thistle. Once it has reached the right temperature, the
woman puts her broad flat loaves onto the overheated inner
sides ofthetannour through the large upper opening ofthe cayity with the aid ofa round ‘cushion’ of straw wrapped in cloth
and floured and which is slightly larger in diameter than the
loaf. Once cooked the floppy golden bread is taken out quickly
by hand. This ancient type ofrural oven predominates practically everywhere in the Mediterranean Levant.

GHIYAS ALJUNDI

1.V1.2.b-ii Bread oven: täbün (Palestine)

Traditionally throughout Palestinian villages, Palestinians used an oven room (täbün) located within the courtyard
house. Itwas used for cooking, baking daily bread and creating
wood embers for winter heating. The self-built oven room,
approximately 1.5 x 1.5 x1.8 m (4.5 X 4.5 X 5.5 ft), is usually
very small and completely enclosed except for a short door
about ım (3 ft) high. The only direct heat source, a mound of
smouldering straw and dung, is centrally located, surrounding
the food container’s sides and covering its lid. The room’s
walls are constructed ofmud, straw and stone pieces with a flat
roof of wooden logs, kindling, mud and straw interspersed
with random gaps between the logs.

The ceramic food container located below the ground is typi-
-cally about 25-30 cm (10-12 in) deep with a48 cm (19 in) diameter opening. It is made of local white clay moulded into a
flattened bowl shape. Once the ceramic container has been
formed, it is dried for several weeks until it is bone dry. A hole
deep enough to hold the ceramic container is dug in the centre
of the oven room floor. It is fired, using straw, olive pulp (jift)
and dung (zibil) for two days and then with its ceramic container
for a further two days. The oven structure is now ready for use
and small fiint stones about 2.5 cm (1 in) square are placed
inside the container covering its floor. The container’s lid can

northern Syria:

Cross-sectiont
(täbün), palestine‘

also be made of clay, but is most often made of metal since the
clay covers tend to break and are heavy. To start the initial fire,
kerosene is used to light the dung and straw located around the
container’s sides and mounded above its lid. This fire is kept
smouldering throughout the day and its heat controlled by the
amount offuel added. The ceramic food container becomes red
hotand the flint stones inside serve to collect and distribute the
heat. Over time the oven room develops a thick layer of ash
which attaches itselfto the walls and ceiling.

For the oven to maintain its heat, old ash is dug out from

around the container’s sides and scraped away from thetop ofthe
container’s lid. New straw, dung and sometimes some olive pulp
to add more heat, are then added into the cavities around the container and above the lid, replacing the old ash, which is then used
to recover them. This procedure of adding new fuel, around and
above the container, to keep the fire smouldering, is called tazbil
and must be done twice a day to maintain the oven’s heat and
proper use. The tabün is well known for its special bread, referred
to as tabün bread, which is flatand about 30 cm (1 ft) in diameter.
The dough is placed directly onto the hot stones which make
indentations that become crispy and give the bread its distinctive
look and taste. Currently there are very few tabuns in operation
owing to their required daily maintenance and the popularity of
the modern electric stove and oven. DENA S. ASSAF
1.V1.2.c Braceros (Hispanic, CA)
Early California kitchens of consequence featured
masonry braceros, charcoal burning stoves of variable size with
stew holes; they had iron grates for coals, ash clean-outs below,
and arches forming storage areas for metates or firewood. Commonly, abracero is counter- or waist-height, often builtagainsta
masonry wall, and features a flat terracotta tiled (ladrillo) countertop. Constructed of adobe, stone or ladrillos, or a combination thereof, it may extend the length of a wall. Bracero designs
vary depending upon the number of holes and arches, with
some lackingarches altogether. Some appear to have been built
upon low platforms. The cook house ofthe ‘oldest house’ in St
Augustine, Florida, features a bracero vented by slits high in the
wall near the roof.

Braceros have a very long history of use in the Mediterranean
dating back to archaic times and in the New World as well. In
Canada, the French version is termed a potager. In southern New
Mexico the bracero is called an estufa (the modern Spanish word
for stove) and a fogön in southern Colorado. Manucy, author of

Cooking and Heating 1.v1.2

the definitive work on the Spanish architecture ofgt Augustine,
Florida, illustrates a typical bracero, His research in Spain, indicates that they abounded there in traditional or folk housing,
along with other hearth forms not yet encountered in North
America. Examples recorded in Spain usually featured hoods
and chimneys above.

Braceros are usually used by cooks standing upright. The stew
holes may be used to heat an iron griddle (comal) used to cook
tortillas, as readily as a pot. Footed iron grills (parillas) are pictured in association with braceros in period Mexican paintings
suggesting thata parilla could be used over a stew hole to charcoal-grill meat or fish. A bracero need not be counter-height;
some examples found were quite low, suitable for use by cooks
seated upon the floor. The French artist Gentilz recorded one
such in the neophyte quarters at one of the missions of San
Antonio, Texas, which is similar to one depicted by genre artist
Casimiro Castro in mid-ıgth-century Mexico. Variants upon
the bracero theme existed including the masonry charcoal stove
excavated in the community kitchen (pozelera) of mission San
Antonio de Padua, and an unusual corner model constructed of
stone, formerly in the historic kitchen (cocina) ofmission Santa
Barbara. EDNA E.KIMBRO
1V1.2.d Chimney (Central Europe)

The oldest provision for getting rid ofsmoke was the
hood, hung over an open fireplace. The hood fed the smoke
into a roof space from which it could then escape outside by
openings in theroofand gable. It was usually conical in shape
with lined walls ofwicker, wattle and daub, corner-timbered or
plank construction. Hoods ofthis kind were known in the later
Middle Ages in most European countries; it was gradually
replaced in the countryside by the walled chimney which in
contrastto the hood, was so constructed as to provide artificial
draughtand was generally structurally connected to the heating
equipment. The introduction of chimneys represented a significant change in the quality ofhousing and this took place in the
towns as early as the late Middle Ages. Chimneys were introduced, especially in newly built houses, from the 13th century,
the innovation being diffused throughout Europe from north-

431

## S. 166

© Josef Scheybal

1.v1 SERVICES

ern Italy. In the countıyside the introduction of chimneys,
which acquired a distinct funnel shape, was closely linked to
the building of the so-called ‘black kitchens’, the decline of
which was linked to the appearance ofthe stove in the ıgth century. The old walled chimneys were broad, tapering in the roof
space, rectangular or even circular in form, constructed from
stone and later from brick; they also served for smoking meat.
The chimneys above roof level were faced with the same material as the roof and as a consequence of fire regulations were
later faced only with hard material. The chimney heads,
finished in a variety ofways, were the work of country bricklayers and those which remain are mainly from the ıgth century.
The chimney is protected by a simple lid or a variously shaped
rooflet on top of which there is sometimes placed a cross,
mushroom, cone, spike or similar object. Less open chimneys
usually have two or more circular or rectangular vents on their
sides through which the smoke escapes. The chimney held a
significant place in folk customs, such as marriage and Shrovetide activities. JOSEF VAREKA

432

1.V1.2.d-1 Chimney: Slavic

The development of the Slavic, Central European
heating system is related to the conduction of smoke escaping
outside. The medieval pattern ofa smoke oven without a chimney survived until the 2oth century in some south Austrian and
Carpathian houses. In houses in the eastern part of central
Europe, when the oven is heated, the smoke stays under the 3 m
(10 ft) high ceiling of the room and leaks out slowly through a
vent above the door to the passage and then up to a loft, but in
the houses ofthe western partthe smoke escapes through holes
above the windows. In German-speaking areas there was a
wooden hood above the hearth, and latera flueto conductsmoke

Cooking and heating arrangement

‚showing the
