tyfarmhouse,
Courtes,

into awooden chimney in the hall which went up to the roof. In
Slavic areas the development began with a short clay-basket
chimney above the open hearth which projected upwards through
the ceiling into the loft, smoke escaping through the roof. This
has survived in north Carpathian villages.

In regions with an advanced economy, the chimneys (both
wooden and basket) reached higher than theroof. Intowns and
castles, chimneys were of stone from the beginning; fire precaution regulations led to this becoming the norm everywhere,
especially in the 18th century. In large and richer houses there
were also stone-walled kitchens with a vault-shaped entry into
a large open chimney, built as a basic structural element ofthe
house. The chimney was added to all heating stoves of rooms
situated around the kitchen. During the zoth century peasants
adapted the system of chimneys to modern industrially produced heating arrangements and extended them into more
rooms, the bedrooms included. JIR! LANGER
1.V1.2.d-ii Chimney: Saracenie (France)

The Saracen chimney is a combination of a house
heating system (foyer chauffant au large or foyer central), an open
hooded chimney towards the centre of the living-room and an
external decorated chimney only ever associated with farmhouses. The external chimneys appeared like small towers or
minarets, 3-5 m (10-16 ft) high, with one or two layers ofcarved
or modelled openings and topped by a wrought iron or brick
cross. Several basic types, with variants, have been identified.
These include: the ‘square’ form topped with a pyramid, sometimes a little curved, showing Roman (as at St Trivier de Courtes),
Eastern and baroque influences; the ‘Gothic reliquary’ (e.g. Le
Mont, Chevroux), rectangular with sloped roof and brick decorated ridge, similar to a Middle Ages reliquary; the ‘conic form’
(e.g. La Bourliere — the oldest surviving chimney), a squareshaped base followed by a polygonal section topped by a conic
shape with an inner dome, showing Roman, Gothic and oriental
influences; and a ‘dome shape’, mounted on a square base, with
a lantern on top. They were constructed from brick with a limestone rendering; bricks thatare visible come from later repairs.

The fireplace, below the chimney hood, was not in the middle ofthe main room (la maison) butto one side divided from the

Cooking and Heating 1.1.2

©
>

restoftheroom bya large beam. The fire was laid on bricktiles,
a flat stone or on a cast iron plate (platine). Curtained beds
would also be positioned in the halfofthe room closest to the
fire. Two subsidiary beams, which also supported the pothanger (comacle), spanned from the main beam to the end wall.
The three beams supported the hood which was of half-timbered construction, covered with wattle and daub, which also
rested on the end wall. Againstthis wall was the ancestors’ high
backed bench (archebanc), a place of honour, similar in appearance to a church bench and consecrated when it was brought
into the house. Under the beam, close to the fire, was the table,
with the various nooks and crannies in the beam above being
used for cutlery, condiments and other domestic items.

Afew scattered examples ofSaracen chimneys can be found in
Bresse, France, between the Saöne, the Jura mountains, the River
Doubs and the Dombes ponds. They date from the ızth and ı8th
centuries although it is known that such chimneys were constructed before them. The term ‘Saracen’, in old French, meant
someone with a different civilization but it is not clear why the
term was applied to this particular type of fireplace and chimney
design. Most of the thirty that still exist with external chimneys
are now protected by the French Corpus ofFine Arts.

Similar examples offireplace hoods exist in the Auvergne, Lor-

raine, Norway and Wales, but without the decorative chimney,
whereas examples ofthe chimneys existin the Jura, Alsace, Aosta
valleyand Spain. PHILIP ALLISON and LOUISE DURNING
1.VI.2.e Chimney complex (England)
The chimney-stack is often associated with integral
structures. The combination ofstack and staircase is commonplace in late-ı6th and 17th-century buildings, where a newel
stair turns alongside a stack and is contained within the same
external projection.

Large hearths sometimes included bench seats; small niches
in the back or sides of the fireplace were probably for storing
salt. Bread ovens, now redundant, are commonplace and
widely distributed over England, and date from the ı5th to the
early-2oth century. Usually found in a fireplace cheek, they may
project externally as domical or rectangular structures. The
round or oval oven has a corbelled roof and is lined with neatly
dressed stone or, later, brick. In the 18th and ıgth century clay
linings were manufactured. Small twigs were burnt in the oven,
raked out and replaced until it was hot. Food was cooked in

433

## S. 167

1.v SERVICES

sequence, according to the heat needed. Ovens were also used
for drying and airing clothes. Smaller ovens, for keeping food
warm, are sometimes found in rooms other than the kitchen.
Ham and bacon could be cured by simply being hung in a
chimney-stack. Curing chambers gave more control over the
process. The simplestarrangement was to constructa round-onplan chamber to one side of the hearth, opening into the
fireplace. The food was cured by the smoke from the kitchen fire,
filtering into the chamber. This arrangement was improved by
building a low wall to the fireplace and then by making a vent in
the base ofthe wall to allow the combustion of sawdust or chip-
Pings in the bottom of the chamber for flavouring. The most
complex versions were completely sealed off from the fireplace
with a separate flue joining the main stack above the chamber.
Food was placed in the chamber either through a doorway on the
inside ofthe fireplace, or on the wall fronting into the kitchen.
Corn-drying kilns are known from Somerset. These are usuallyaccommodated in a rectangular two-storey external projection rising beside the kitchen stack. They were heated from the
hearth or from a separate charcoal fire in the kiln, which malted
barley or dried seed corn or beans on a shelf partway up the
structure. Separate flues to the kilns usually discharged into the
main kitchen stack. JO COX and JOHN THORP
1.V1.2.e-1 Chimney oven complex: Macaranese
The chimney complex, mainly related to the kitchen
functions, has a remarkable number of different variants. It is
in its most elaborate form in the area of Lisbon and coastal

434

Beiras; it also exists in Atlantic islands such as Madeira, the
Azores and the Canaries. Particularly in the Azores, it exists in
an almost systematic way, as much in rural as in urban homes.
Wilhelm Giese believes that the complete system evolved
in Madeira and the Azores archipelagos. It seems to have
developed mostly in areas where different, contradictory,
‘north-south’ influences emerged and combined with each
other, in an individualistic context.

The Macaranese fireplace-oven-chimney system relates and
combines the typical southern and Mediterranean elevated
hearth with the characteristic oven from cold northern countries, bringing it inside the kitchen and into the house - even,
sometimes, in places with very hot climates, such as Madeira or
Canaries. In its most simple form the system has the cylinder
volume ofthe oven at one inside corner ofthe kitchen, with its
‘mouth’ opened to the fireplace (lar); there is no chimney and
smoke disappears through the roof tiles. More original and
complex forms include a chimney, built over an autonomous
and juxtaposed fireplace area; the chimney is sometimes very
large, as in Alentejo examples.

However, the most complex and aesthetically interesting
combination includes — as a kind of ‘kitchen-machine’ — the
fireplace, the chimney and, built on the outside, the oven
volume, opened only to the interior part ofthe kitchen (with its
‘mouth’ under the chimney, into the fireplace). The three elements combined follow in a symmetrical order, creating a
strong plastic sequence of geometrical pure forms.

JOSE MANUEL FERNANDES
1.V.2.f Cooking hearth: Lela
Houses of the Lela of Burkina Faso contain three
cooking areas. One is situated outside in the courtyard, two are
inside. Containers for daily use and storage are kept in the two
interior rooms. Round-bottomed earthenware cooking pots
vary in size between 20 cm (8 in) and 25 cm (1o in) in diameter.
Spherical pots are used for sorghum porridge (gül, commonly
called t6), the staple food. Pots in which accompanying sauces
are made are hemispherical. The pots rest on three supports, a
fire being made on the ground between these supports.

The exterior hearth stands against a courtyard wall, with a
cone-shaped protusion at the base of the wall serving as one
support for the cooking vessels. The other two supports, either
old dirt-filled pots turned upside down or stones covered with
earth, rest on the ground. The two interior cooking areas are
situated to the left and right oftthe central room of the house.
These ‘hearth rooms’ (kil-ji) are also used as sleeping rooms in
cold weather. One is called the ‘room of preparingtö’ (gül-swäl-
), or ‘the tö room’ (gile-ji); the other, in which sauces are
cooked and food is smoked, is called ‘smoke room’ (zö-ji).
(Bourdier called these rooms djena and zoni, respectively.)

The ‘hearth for tö’ is situated on the ground at the base ofa
half-wall (x m [3 ft] high, 0.75-2 m [2.5-6.5 ft] long) which
stands in front ofthe circular wall oftheroom. These two walls
are joined by two low perpendicular walls at either end ofthe
half-wall. The space in between is used for storing ash and in
the rainy season wood is stored on top of the perpendicular
walls.

See also
2.1V.5.k Macaı
Islands

Reference 0
Williams,

The earthenware smoker is conic in form, measuring at the
base 15-20 cm (6-8 in) in diameter, and wideningto 70 cm (27
in) diameter atits heightof1.2 m (4 fi). Itis either made from a
bottomless oldjar (zha) which is placed on a cylindrical base, or
is purpose built. Meat, fish or shea nuts are placed in the
smoker on an earthenware disc with holes. The smoke
emanates from a hole located at the base ofthe cylinder, where
the hearth is situated. Curved walls, 50 cm in height, join the
smoker to the wall oftheroom. The space created between the
smoker and walls is used to store ash, which later is spread in
the field adjoining the house, and is used to decorate the walls
of the house and for preserving food. Some combustibles are
chosen for their potash content, as potash is used for making
soap and in food.

Cooking pots used in these two interior rooms often reston
two different round earthenware pots. One ofthese pots (dedo)
has a flat cover; it is filled with water which is used for cooking
and washing. The other pot (kulpu) is a larder; t6 placed in the
bottom remains warm and moist overnight when covered by
the deep lid.

Small granaries (35 x 60 cm [14 inx 2 ft] to 60 cmxı.3m [2
X 4.3 ft]) and round pottery used for storing assorted foodstuffs
are kept in the hearth rooms, as they are always dry. Grains,
condiments, leaves, nuts, potash, or tobacco, generally harvested by women, are stored separately in these containers,
and, when mixed with ash, may keep for several years. After
abundant harvests, women make additional granaries, which
are more hermetic than clay pots. Containers are also used in
daily food preparation. The sorghum, harvested by men, is
stored outside in large granaries and distributed regularly to
women by men in baskets, which are often hung from beams in
the ‘room for preparing tö’. LUC PECQUET
1.V1.2.f-1 Cooking hearth: Slavic
Central European Slavs combined the hearth with
the bread-baking oven which opened on to it. Situated in the
central house the position ofthe oven complex is behind a partition in the living-room. Only in some Carpathian and southeast Alpine regions did the older forms of the open hearth
survive in the living-room. The open hearth without an oven is
used in the Carpathian seasonal house (chalet). In regions with
a German-speaking population the hearth has a larger space
with an oven, oritis placed in a position separate from the oven
opening. In this case the oven complex is in a hall or entrance
passage; alongthe Bohemia-Bayarian border itwas outside the
house. In southeast areas (Danube), the oven was sometimes in
the open-air courtyard.

Peasants cooked on a hearth with copper or iron kettles
hanging over the fire, and later, in cast iron pots with legs, or
pots on an iron trivet. Meat grilling over the hearth was used
mostly in German cultures. Ovens were used especially for
bread baking, but also for meat boiling. Meat baking in ovens
came later. In the southeast (the major part of the former
Hungarian kingdom) the oven is dome-shaped (occasionally
impressed with saucer-shaped tiles); in the west it is prismatic,
while in the Alpine regions it is also prismatic, capped with a
dome.

Cooking and Heating 1.012

In the ı8th and ıgth centuries ovens were joined with prismatic-shaped heating stoves, which had an iron box for baking.
Beside a hearth, peasants built a low cooking stove with cast
iron plates, which replaced the open hearth. In east European
regions cooking stoves spread from the late-ıgth century. Meat
was smoked in a large chimney or in the spaces where smoke
leaked free - in Alpine regions under the hall ceiling, in the
Carpathians within a loft. Meat-smoking cabins placed beyond
the courtyards were also used. From the late-ıgth century, peasants in some places also used industrially produced iron
kitchen-stoves.

From the mid-18th to zoth centuries hearths became extinct
as a result of fire precaution regulations. ‘Civilized’ development situated the cooking in a separate kitchen; however with
increasing pauperization cooking stoves were re-introduced to
living-rooms. Where more families lived together, the principle
remained that each family should have its own cooking place
(mostly a hearth or stove). The hearth was associated with
magic customs and retained a dominant place in the peasant
world-view. Similar significance was attached to the oven
which was placed in a corner diagonally opposite the saint-corner oftheroom, with its devotions atthealtar table.

JIRf LANGER

Fire-irons
With the development of the chimney flue cooking
was largely restricted to servicing the hearth from the front.
The typical fireplace form in western Europe with a wood or
stone lintel spanning the opening, and often, a bread oven to
one side, somewhatrrestricted the use ofthe hearth for cooking
purposes, though it provided a cleaner and safer environment.
Access to cooking required specialized fixtures of which the
simplest was a rod of wood, usually holly, which spanned the
flue behind the lintel and was socketed into the chimney, from
which chains took the weight of suspended pots. Large links
permitted adjustment and hanging chains were still in use
among Scottish crofters and Irish peasants in the 2oth century.
Generally, however, an iron bar spanned the flue, from which
pot-crooks were hung. A pot-crook or hanger was a forged iron
strap with a hook at each end from which cooking pots and
griddle pans were suspended.

Iron is not the sole material used for fire-irons and fixtures;
brass, through often too expensive in the vernacular milieu,

11.2.8

435

©

## S. 168

1.v1 SERVICES

was used in the making of spitjacks and other artefacts, while
copper is still used for culinary artefacts in Mediterranean and
Middle Eastern countries.

Some types of fire-irons, often of great antiquity, have
declined in use in the 2oth century as the functions that they
served have been largely replaced by modern devices. Many of
these are related to energy and its consumption for domestic
activities. It may be noted that the extensive electrification of
the United States did not take place until the 19g0s, following
the Tennessee Valley Authority’s great electrification programme. In western Europe the process of electrification and
modernization had an uneven history; while the development
of the ‘servantless home’ in the mass housing and suburbanization ofthe British Isles between the Wars was made possible
by electrification, villages little more than 80 km (50 mi) from
London had no electricity or even mains water until the 19508.

In parts ofcentral Europe the process of supplying mains services has still to be achieved, while throughout much of the
world there is still no such supply available to the majority ofthe
population. Consequently, traditional methods of heating,
cookingand lighting persistand though many trade goods have
replaced some items ofequipment, others have remained in use
in the 2oth century. Among these are iron furniture for the
hearth, such as firebacks, tripods and trivets, chimney cranes
and grates. Devices to assistfood curingand preparation and for
cooking processes, such as cauldrons and stove-plates, form a
related group. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.2.g-1 Crooks and crane

The simple crook was not adjustable and pots were
hung at fixed heights, determined by the length ofthe crook. As
early as the 13th century adjustable pot-hangers using a ratchet
principle were in use, the crook being forged as a wide blade
with a serrated edge on one side. A sleeve atthe top accepted a
hangingrod with a fatloop, or pawl, attached, which could engage with the teeth ofthe crook blade, permitting itto be shortened or extended. This form of adjustable jib-crook was in use
in many parts ofEurope until the second halfofthe 2oth century.

Though the crook could be adjusted vertically, itstill required
that the cook should reach across the open fire to reach the pot
handle. The chimneycrane, an L-shaped and braced iron frame,
pivoted at one side of the chimney overcame this problem:
crooks could be suspended from the horizontal bar. The crane
or ‘gallows’ was sometimes made of bog-oak in Ireland, but
usually it was smith-forged. Appropriately known in Scotland
asa‘sway’,itcould be swung forward and away from the flames
to deliver a pot, kettle or other vessel to the cook. In its simplest
form the crane had a single movement, rotating on the vertical
harr-hung rod; where the size ofthe fireplace demanded it, an
extendable bar, offering a second movement was used; a third
movement, permitting vertical adjustment by means of.a lever
and a series ofstops was sometimes incorporated.

With an adjustable crane and jib-crooks the cook could usea
variety ofsuspended cooking pots, kettles and other vessels. As
some pots had a capacity ofas much as 95 1 (25 gall) the weight
supported by the crane was considerable. A large kettle ofboiling water was difficult to move or to pour from, but the forged

436

iron ‘idle-back’ or kettle-tilter overcame the problem. Typically
it consisted of an iron stirrup attached by a short shank at the
crest, with an eye from which it was hung. The prongs of the
stirrup arch terminated in hooks to take the kettle handle while
along arm from the arch acted as a lever, permitting the kettle
to bettilted with minimal effort; simple locking devices or stops
ensured that the kettle did not slip in the tilted position.

Three-legged trivets, bakingirons, and bread-baking ‘Dutch’

ovens, were among the items of cooking hearth equipment,
designed either to stand on a side hob or be suspended from
the crane. The ı8th-century autoclave or iron pressure cooker,
and the fountain, a large iron vessel with an extended brass
spout and tap could also be hung. Though only the crane itself
was a permanent fixture, the crooks, hangers and vessels were
wholly associated with it. So effective was the equipment ofthe
traditional cooking hearth that all such items were still to be
found in use in rural areas of several western European countries until the 19708. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.2.h Heated bed: kang (China)
The heated brick bed (kang or huokang) and heated
wall heating systems (huogiang) have long been used in the
northern provinces of China where temperatures often drop as
far as-40 °C (-40 °F).

During the day, certain daily activities are carried out on the
kang, which is heated by the constantly lit kitchen stove. The hot
air passes through a system ofconduits under alarge brick platform, thus providing heat. In cities, the kang is located against
the northern facade of the dwelling while in the countryside,
where chairs are rare and the kang is used for sitting as well as
sleeping on, it faces south. Frequently in the same dwelling
there is one kang located to the north and another to the south.

The length of this platform is determined by the distance
between the jian, the space between two poles, which is often
the width oftheroom. The width ofthekang is generally 1.8 m (6
ft) and the height is that ofan adult’s knee, 0.65-0.7 m (2.1-2.3
fo. It is built on low brick walls and covered with fireproof clay

kitchen,

See also for
2.11.3.8 Galloı

References
Lindsay, J. 5,1
Murphy, Bailey‘

References
Evans, E. Esl
Lindsay, J. seymoi

2

x
\
%

O Feng Yuagiang and Frangoise Ged

Plan section

coated with a mixture oflime, earth and natural fibres, respectively 1:3:1 in volume. Wealthy families sometimes add a thick
layer ofpaperas a final touch. Up to the window ledge, the three
sides ofthe kang which are up against the wall are covered with
wood panels decorated with sculpted lower motifs. The conduits through which the hot air circulates - two, three, or four,
according to the builder’s decision during construction - are
defined by the space located inside between the low brick walls.
Between these walls, 30 cm (1 ft) of compact earth is added to
reduce the height ofthe conduit for the hotair and to reduce the
amount of wood used to heat the kang. Two control mechanisms, one on the outside, the other on the inside, allow for the
regulation of the intensity of the hot air and therefore for
temperature adjustment. Two cavities, located in the wall and

Cooking and Heating 1.v1.2
under the kang make it possible to extract the ashes which build
up in the system. In September, the kang is taken apart for cleaning, on.a strict yearly basis if straw or charcoal are used for the
fire, and every four to five years ifwood is used.

Although this heating system is valued because it reduces
fatigue by bringing heat to the body, as prescribed by traditional Chinese medicine, it nonetheless has some disadvantages. One of these is that the heat given off is limited to the
kang while the restoftheroom remains cold; another is thatthe
constant friction of the straw matting which covers the kang
produces a considerable amount of dust.

FENG YUEQIANG and FRANCOISE GED

1.V1.2.h-i Heated wall: huogiang

The principle of the heated wall (huogiang), in use
since the Han dynasty, is the same as that ofthe kang, but for
vertical inside partitions. It is a double brick wall with a gap
between the bricks which creates three or five heat conduits.
Wealthy families who wish to have more even heating with less
dust often use this system combined with a connecting kang.
The kitchen stove can provide heat for both the wall and the
kang, or aseparate stove especially for this purpose can be used.
The heated partition is 30 cm (1 ft) thick, 2 m (6.5 ft) wideand 2
m (6.5 ft) high. Because it is important that the conduits be
smooth to facilitate the passage of hot air, they are sanded
down during construction by using a mixture ofsand and dirt
andawetcloth. The outside ofthe wall is whitewashed, though
recently people have begun covering it with sheets of steel.
Space is left at the bottom ofthe wall to be able to clean out the
build-up ofash. The advantage of the heated wall, aside from
evenly heating the room, is that it can be placed almost anywhere in the dwelling.

FENG YUEQIANG and FRANGOISE GED

1.V1.2. Heating system: gloria (Spain)
The gloria is a central heating system which can be
found in many parts ofinland Spain, especially in the northern
plateau.

Itis a modern system that was introduced around the end of
the ıgth century, and was especially evident in the first 30 years
ofthe 2oth century. The effect of the gloria was to separate the
actions of heating the kitchen and cooking, the combination of
which was a feature of the traditional kitchen. Its origins have
been found in the Roman hypocaust, examples of which exist
in Castillian Roman houses where the system was used in
certain rooms.

The gloria is normally constructed in a room on the lower
floor, which doubles as a dining and living room. It is here that
an elevated floor ofsome ı5 cm (6 in) in height is built of brick
and supported by partitions ofthe same material. On the outside
ofthe room, in the hall or the patio, is a rectangular fireplace,
normally sunk to a depth of4o cm (16 in). The fireplace is open
atfloor level, and is covered bya pierced metal sheet, the lack of
oxygen in the closed space permitting slow combustion.

The hotairand the smoke escape through a channel which is
placed below the elevated floor; ifrequired, there can beanumber ofthese channels below the floor, depending of course, on

437

## S. 169

1.v1 SERVICES

chimney

the size ofthe room. The brick walls of the channel can have
holes, allowing the warm gas to spread and subsequently heating the floor more efficiently. The channel ends, at the far side
ofthe room, in a chimney through which the gas can escape.

The natural circulation of the air below the floor makes a
room, that is normally both a living and dining room, have a
heated floor for winter which becomes a cool floor in summer.

The fire is lit with hay, paper and pinenut shells, and later,
after ithas been fuelled with more material, the metaltop ofthe
fireplace is closed. Occasionally it is used to burn rubbish and
unwanted items.

Before the introduction of the gloria there existed what was
called the ‘trivet kitchen’ (cocina de trebede). It is still possible to
find some examples of this kitchen which is often confused
with the gloria. In the trivet kitchen the fireplace is found below
asmall elevated floor, around 2X 1.5 m (6.5 x 5 ft), which takes
up only a part ofthe room. The cooking pot is heated over the
trivet, and once this is done the table and chairs are placed on
the heated floor, so as to allow the people to take advantage of
the warmth generated by the flames while eating.

JOSE LUIS GARCIA GRINDA
1.V1.2.j Hypocaust (Korea)
The hypocaust system in Korea is termed the ondol, a
compound word composed of two syllables: on means ‘heating’ and dol meaning ‘stone’. The heating stone, ondol, had its
origin in northeastern China, from where it diffused into the
Korean peninsula. Itis related to the Chinese kang which, however, occupies only part ofa room and is raised above the floor
like a broad bed.

Korea’s hypocaustsystem has become an inseparable culture
element in Korean vernacular houses. In terms of the natural
environment and mode of life, the ondol is a product of the
Korean culture which is more developed in the northern partof
Korea than in the southern part. This relates to the coldness of
the winter.

438

Most rooms of Korean vernacular houses have this underfloor heating system with the exception ofthose with awooden
floor. From ground level up to the level of the floor Koreans
built with mud and stone, making three or four flues, which
converge into a chimney at each end of the room. The whole
floor is then covered with large slabs ofstone about 5 cm (2 in)
thick. Thejoints are carefully sealed with cementor mud so that
no smoke can come through into the room. A thin layer of
cementis spread over the stones, and then the whole is covered
with a heavy oiled paper. The ‘basement’ ofthe kitchen lies at
ground level, from which runs the flues for the floor heating
system, which is operated from the kitchen. The fireplace for
this universal, very practical Korean national heating system is
built into the large, low kitchen stove.

The arrangement can be classified into two types. First is the
house with a few rooms, one under-floor heating system, and
one fireplace and chimney. This type ofondol is distributed in the
northeastern part of Korean peninsula and needs a lot of fuel,
because the broad room has to be heated all at once by one
fireplace. It is evident that the room near the fireplace will be
overheated and therooms near the chimney mightnotbe heated.

The second type is one in which each of the rooms has a
fireplace, so less fuel is needed for heating a room. This type
of fireplace arrangement is distributed in the southwestern
Korean peninsula and the pattern coincides with the distribution ofa single-row room housetype.

Fireplaces can also be classified into two types, namely, those
used exclusively for heating the room, the type predominant in

°
z

Cut-away axoı
showing theoı

the fuel holes!

indand
‚ofthe sou

southwestern Korea, and those used fora combination ofcookingand heating. In the latter case three or four kettles are puton
a kitchen range in order to cook rice while the floor is also
heated. This type of fireplace is broadly distributed over the
northeastern part of Korea, the region of the double row room
housetype.

The smoke comes out of a chimney hole at the end of the
flues. The height of the chimney differs according to region.
For example, the chimneys in North Korea are higher than
chimneys in South Korea.

The advantages of the ondol are as follows: the cost of construction is relatively low and does not require specialized
knowledge; the fuels needed, e.g. firewood, briquette, dried
grass, and dried rice-straw, are comparatively abundant and
inexpensive; the control of fire in the fireplace is relatively easy,
a housewife making the fire twice a day, namely morning and
evening; and the ondol provides comfortto the family duringthe
winter.

However, there are also disadvantages. The ondol is unsuitable
for alarge room with an under-floor heating system but only one
fireplace. Second, aroom ofthe ondol is a relatively closed space.
As the under-floor heated rooms are well-ventilated through
opened windows in winter, so the room temperature can drop
easily. Commonly, the rooms of Korean folk houses have few
windows and these are not large. Finally, the air of under-floor
heating rooms becomes dry as a general rule, with low humidity,
and impure air and excessive dryness in the room can generate
unhealthy conditions. BO-WOONG CHANG
1.V1.2.k Insulation: Lehmwickel (German)

Lehmwickel (German for “earth-winding’ or ‘earth
wrapping’), also known as fisees or torquettes in France, vikli or
szalmapölyas födem in Hungary, välätucs in Romania, and ‘Dutch
biscuits’ in the United States, is a composite building technique, or family of techniques. It is used especially as a sound
and heat insulating system in the floor structure between the
principal rooms and attic ofa house, or between floors in full
two-storey structures.

The basic principle is that pieces of wood are wrapped
around in earth and straw to create mud cylinders, which
are then packed against each other to make a continuous horizontal or vertical layer, as the case may be. The timber is occasionally a round stick, but more commonly a stake split to
roughly rectangular cross-section, and the ends are usually
pointed or wedge-shaped, to fit into drilled holes in the frame
or into continuous grooves. The mud and straw mixture may
be applied in a rope-like strand, as is commonly the case in
France, in a series of flat straps, or in a continuous mat, as
in most Australian and American examples. But there is no
absolute geographical distinction, and in Hungary, where they
have been well-documented, the complete range of types can
be found.

Usually the upper and lower faces are plastered over smooth,
but sometimes, especially in colonial examples, they are
unfinished. The technique is also used in partitions and external walls of fachwerk buildings, with at least the exterior faces
plastered smooth.

Cooking and Heating 1.v1.2

## S. 170

1.vI SERVICES

Lehmwickel is found principally in France, Germany, Germaninfluenced areas of eastern Europe, and German-influenced
European colonies. The earliest identifiable French and German
examples date from the ı6th century, but if they derive from
common sources the technique is probably very much earlier,
and perhaps Frankish in origin. French examples occur in Normandy, Brittany, and in at least one instance in the Loire valley;
German examples can be found in Baden-Württemberg; Hungarian ones are in the Great Hungarian Plain, eastern Transdanubia, Transylvania, and Moldavia (but there only amongst
the Csängö ethnic group); and Romanian ones occur in the
Transylvanian plain. The technique has also been reported in the
Czech Republic.

In Hungary especially, the technique is believed to have been

associated with German colonization in the 18th century. In the
United States it is likewise associated with German settlers,
beginning with a Lutheran parsonage at Scholarie (New York)
ofthe 1730s, and including others in New York, Pennsylvania,
Fredericksburg (Texas), and the originally Rappite settlement
of New Harmony (Indiana), the colonists of which came from
Würtemberg. A Canadian example at Kingston (Ontario), however, has no known German connection. In Australia the technique is found in the German villages ofthe Barossa valley, and
in western Victoria, Sydney and Brisbane. MILES LEWIS
1.V1.2.1 Kitchen (Aragön)
Traditionally, the kitchen in Aragön has been the
centre ofsocial life inside the house, serving as a basis to organize familyrelations and even close neighbourhood ties. Due to
this quality, its dimensions tended, in general terms, to be large
in proportion to other rooms in the house. It had the fireplace
at the centre or close to one ofthe walls but always retaining a
sense of centrality, with an open chimney occupying most of
the ceiling with the form ofa huge truncated cone suspended
on the hearth. Its mouth, popularly known as the ‘bell’, was
crossed by a beam with a chain attached, suspended over the
hearth (el cremallo) with one or several hooks on which to hang
the pots and boilers over the fire. The hearth was low, built of
stone and having a parallelepipedal form, and the walls ofthe
chimney were made of tufa, a porous, isothermal and light
stone. The low hearth or the bell-like truncated cone contributed to the popular names used to define these kitchens
called ‘bell kitchens’ or ‘low fire kitchens’. A very important
element in them were the benches surrounding the hearth
(cadieras), which were occupied by the members of the household following a particular pattern according to their status
and authority. These were wooden benches which usually
had small boards attached by hinges that could be folded or
displayed and used as tables for meals.

Generally located in a very strategic situation which controlled the passage to more inner and private areas of the
house, the kitchen has acted as the symbolic boundary, permitted to visitors once they were identified. Being the dominion of
women, as were also the inner parts of the whole house, the
activities that took place in there during daylight were considered feminine. As a result of such cultural interpretation, men
were notexpected to stay in there except for daily meals or after

440

EEE Een
er
ee min -
ern zn um wa a Et er
2 Tee a
un m

en

m

mn
I
| .

Den

finishing their working day, which were the moments when all
members ofthe domestic group gathered. While there, since
they were in a feminine domain, men usually kept relatively
quiet, resting, talking, eating or even doing some menial male
handiwork, sitting around the fireplace. The master, who was
the only inheritor and owner of the house and its patrimony,
had the right to occupy the best seat around the fireplace, away
from cold air currents and protected from disturbance. Children and women had no specific places, and the latter used to
be occupied in their tasks, cooking or serving the meals or
washing dishes while the males were there. Such activities usually took place in the next room called the ‘scullery’ (recocina),
where there were the sink, the cupboards with the kitchenware,
and lately the electric or gas stoves for cooking. Such a distribution of elements and activities proves that the Aragön type of
kitchen was mainly a place ofsocial interaction rather than one
dedicated to household chores, which were performed in a separate room. Finally, in the afternoons, while men were working
outside, groups of women neighbours sometimes gathered in
the kitchen, around the fire, to talk and perhaps to sew.

JOSE C. LISÖN-ARCAL
1V1.2.l-i Kitchen (Baltistan)
In the Islamic distriet of Baltistan, in Pakistanadministered Kashmir, the extreme climate ofwarm summers
combined with winter temperatures of-20 °C (-4 °F) demands
that lifestyles alter with the seasons. The domestic architecture
is influenced by that of the Buddhist districts of Ladakh and
Zanskar to the east but does not have the same consistency of
form. The variations are reflected in aspectrum ofarchitectural
terminology. Interviews with villagers in different parts of
Baltistan revealed nine differentterms which can mean kitchen.
Most ofthe distinctions relate to seasonal differences:

aserkhung The modern ‘white’ kitchen
dukang, zankhung Aspring kitchen
abierdouk, zuerduk The summer kitchen
tseley A summer kitchen as acorner ofaveranda
balti, zankura A kitchen for spring, autumn and mild winters
katza Deep winter kitchen

Not.all ofthe terms occur together nor are the boundaries ofthe
meanings constant. One term may have different meanings in
different parts of Baltistan. For instance, the katza is a window-

The large chim; .
Aragön kitchen,

References forj,|
Barabäs, J., and]

Prudon, T, H,
Riviere,

Stoica, 6.,1989

Tünde, Z., 1991

2.1V.5.c Aragonese

References
Lisön-Arcal,

Rivas, Ana,

berscreen
ilesthe clean

© Eric Dudley

less room on the ground floor ofa two-storey house or occasionally it is found in a basement. In much of Baltistan katza means
fodder store. Since fodder not only makes good insulation but
also generates heat as it decays, the fodder room is naturally
warm and so is a sensible refuge in times of extreme cold. In
some locations there may be onlya few days ofthe year when the
climate necessitates retiring to the katza. In a mild winter, orata
lower altitude, there may be no need to use it at all. In these
places, the family will regard themselves as camping in what is
primarily a fodder store. At higher altitudes, it may be necessary
to live for two or three months of the year in the katza. In these
cases, the room is fitted out with a wooden floor, shelves, cupboards, decorated timberwork, and a hearth. The fodder itself
may be pushed out into adjacent rooms which isolate the katza
from the external walls. In the highest communities, theroom is
divided by a low timber partition. The katza is regarded as occupying one side of the partition while the patal or tagra, on the
other side, is used by cattle which generate further warmth. The
high altitude katza may be entered by means of stepladder from
above while at lower altitudes entry is exclusively from theanimal
quarters. At high and intermediate altitudes, the centre of the
katza is occupied by an elaborately decorated column (arbus).
Often this will be the only decorated column in the house even
when the katza is principally used as a fodder store. While the
arbus was also found in parts oflower Baltistan, people there generally said thatthe arbuswas a column only used in a mosque.

In the high altitude communities katza unambiguously
means ‘the winter room’. At intermediate altitudes it is a place
for improvised refuge. In lower Baltistan the idea of living in
the fodder store is met with apparent incredulity and katza is
sometimes used to refer to the whole ofthe ground floor. With

Cooking and Heating 1.v1.2

increasing prosperity and access to roads more people can buy
firewood and hence fewer people need to use the katza. The
geographical and chronological changes in the boundaries of
meaning of katza affect the categories which relate to it. In
upper Baltistan only the large houses will have a balti, the
spring and autumn kitchen. In lower Baltistan, the balti may be
either the principal kitchen, to beabandoned onlyin the heatof
summer, or exclusively a winter kitchen. The balti is generally
on an upper floor and often, but not always, is surrounded by
other rooms to protect it from the cold. ERIC DUDLEY
1.V1.2.-ii Kitchen (Bangladesh)

In the rural Bangladeshi homestead cooking is
often carried on outdoors, in the courtyard or spaces between
the buildings. Over time the cooking area is covered for Protection against the elements and is converted into a kitchen, which
is then supplemented by an additional outdoor cooking
area, especially for the dry, winter season. The kitchen is quite
often attached to the main dwelling unit, but can also be an
adjacent separate structure, arranged around the central courtyard.

Cooking is seldom done inside the main dwelling unit and
the kitchen is an ancillary structure. Like most other ancillary
structures itis made of perishable or less durable materials like
Jute-sticks or reeds and thatch. Sometimes the lower part ofthe
Jute-stick wall is plastered with earth to protect it from groundwater seepage. Such structures often need to be relocated during expansion of the homestead, hence the modesty of their
construction. Usually small, around 2.5-3 m (8-10 ft) longand
1.75-2.5 m (6-8 ft) wide, and ofa low height of1.75-2.5 m (6-8
fi), such kitchens accommodate the women who, for the most
part, work inside them.

It is common to make earthen stoves by digging into the
ground, and leaving openings for cooking and feeding in
firewood. Portable earthen stoves are also used. Cooking
implements include clay pots and wooden ladles, where the

N

ni.
hi

## S. 171

1.v1 SERVICES

cup ofthe ladle is made from a coconut shell sliced in half. The
fuel used is firewood, but quite often dried cow dung dried on
jute-sticks used as skewers, is also used in Bangladesh.

In large joint families, the men are usually served food inside
the dwellings and after they have finished, thewomen eatin the
kitchen. Ifthe family is small then it frequently eats together in
the kitchen. On special occasions, especially for large gatherings, food is served outside in the open courtyard.

IFTEKHAR AHMED

1.V1.2.|-ii Kitchen (Pomerania; Brazil)

Of the German immigrants who came to Brazil, a
fifch came from Pomerania, a region characterized by circular
villages (rundling); the houses were arranged around a circular
central plaza. These dwellings consisted ofaverandaleadingto
a vestibule with access to the kitchen and the living-room. The
kitchen, the source ofheating in the residence, communicated
with the stable, which was divided into various spaces, each
one designated to a specific type ofanimal. Above these stalls,
hay was stored for the long winter.

Among the people from the Rhine and Pomerania, the
kitchen was less complex than that of the Westfalians. The
space was divided into two zones - one for preparing the food
and another with a robust table for eating.

In Brazil, thistype ofarrangement was repeated on equal conditions, presentinga central opening, with the cooker placed at
one side ofthe opening and the table on the other side.

An interesting phenomenon was observed regarding the
evolution ofthe plan ofthe Pomeranian house. With the separation of the ‘house’ from the ‘kitchen’, the corridor crossed
the house from one extreme to the other, havingat one side the
living-rtoom/bedroom and at the other side a spare room used
for other purposes, for instance as a school or as another bedroom. The circulation, however, was modified as soon as the
technical means permitted. With the advent of the plated
cooker and later on the enamelled cooker, the danger of fire
hazards caused by open fire was minimized. This resulted in
the addition of the kitchen to the body of the house. By this
means, the ancestral form of the house built in Germany was
re-established as much as possible. GÜNTER WEIMER

1.V1.2.-iv Kitchen: Bugis

In the simpler Bugis houses in southern Sulawesi of
the commonest type, the kitchen is invariably set at the rear of
the house, on the same side as the entrance door which is atthe
front; ifthere is a lowered lean-to, the kitchen is setat the rear
of this. There may also be an appended kitchen house (bola
dapö’), also at the rear of the house, and usually with its own
entrance. Traditionally, kitchen floors and walls are most often
made ofbamboo lattice, the latter otherwise using plaited bamboo instead. This both facilitates the evacuation of waste
beneath and allows the smoke to be ventilated out; it can also
escape through the roof’s thatch. The hearth (dapureng), always
oriented transversally to the house’s axis, is made ofa wooden
frame containing hardened earth; on it are set five stones
(taring) which enable two cooking pots or pans to be used at
the same time (these stones have now often been replaced by

442

IT] IN || Il Il

I un \\

I

)

iron bars). In the case ofbig feasts, additional cooking fires are
provided by the use ofmovable terracotta braziers (dapö’).

There are neither cupboards nor a meat-safe. Above the
hearth, on a small open platform (para-para) are stored provisions of firewood and salt, while prepared dishes which are to
be kept away from insects are hung there in plaited carriers. A
jar of water with its dipper, another with the provision of
husked rice and a grill pan are always near at hand. Specific
implements are the sokkoreng, a plaited cone for rice steaming
and palekko, terracotta dome-shaped tops which, being preheated before being puton cooking pans, make up for the complete lack of ovens in Bugis material culture. A rack (para or
a’tanrdangeng) or wooden grating, cantilevered from the outer
wall under the roof edge, may be available for the storage of
containers, baskets, pans and other utensils such as basins,
colanders and sieves. Hemispherical pots may be seton basketwork bearings. Small kitchen utensils such as ladles, forks,
spatulas and knives, are often fitted in the plaited wall. The
women cook in a squatting position or sit on very low benches.
There is no permanent worktop. Washing the rice in basins or
pounding spices and ingredients in wooden or stone mortars
is done on the floor, where platters are laid for mincing or
chopping meat or vegetables. CHRISTIAN PELRAS
1.V1.2.-v Kitchen: Newar

The kitchen (baiga) of the Newar people is typified
by its placement on the upper floors of.a traditional Newar
house. This location serves both functional and spiritual purposes within the Buddhist or Hindu religious structure of
Newar culture. All food, water and firewood is carried up steep
ladder-like stairs to the kitchen.

Rooted in Indian philosophy, Newar art and architecture
evolved from the theory that the universe developed and radiates from a central point (bindu). In Newar society, as in many
traditional societies, to build a house or to construct a sanctuary was to recreate symbolically the shape ofthe universe (Mac-
Donald and Vergati-Stahl). Therefore, typically the Newar
dwelling grows vertically rather than horizontally. In spiritual
terms the house may be viewed as a symbolic representation of
the transition from profane to sacred, from the everyday to the
house ofthe gods. Rooms within the Newar dwelling are organized around this concept, with the ground floor containing
everyday living supplies: wood storage, farm and livestock
tools, moving up to the sitting room/bedroom, to the family

The hearth inthe
kitchen.ofa Bugis,

south Sulawes|, Sıha

See also for, v2,
2.1.5.0 Bengalee

Haq, Saif-Ul, 1994 2

2.11.2.d Bugis

Pelras, Christian, 1
