3.Y.4.h-1 Pomeranlan

'DTera Michele cahn

room and finally to the top floors where the kitchen, associated
with cleanliness, health and spiritual purification is located.
Next to or above it is the family shrine. Traditionally, outsiders
are not allowed into Newar kitchens: Newars consider it
impure to allow other than family members to enter.

The typical Newari kitchen is small, about 2-3 m (7-10 ft)
wide and 3-3.5 (10-12 ft) long. The ceiling is low, usually
1.7-1.8 m (5.8-6 ft) tall, as itis throughout the house. The floor
ofthe kitchen is mud, which is reapplied every two to three days
to prevent cracking, drying and dirt. It consists ofa mixture of
clay, straw, dung and water, mixed to a fine pulp and filtered to
a spreading consistency. The stove, or wood burning hearth,
sits in one corner raised about 30-38 cm (12-15 in) above the
floor. It too is constructed of mud, with a small centrally
located side opening for wood. The stove top may contain two
to three round fire openings for pots. These are used daily for
cookingrice, lentils and vegetables, beans or corn. Amuch bigger opening is used for feasts (vhay) and during festivals, which
are frequent in Newar culture. Beside the stove a small wood
seat, 20 cm (8 in) wide, 30 cm (12 in) longand 5 or8cm(20r3
in) tall, rests on a mat. Itis used by the cook while tending the
fire and cooking food.

Brass water jugs are as much a tradition of Newari kitchens
asthe mud hearth. Because the kitchen is atthetop storey ofthe
house, water must be brought and stored in these jugs to use
for cooking, drinking, and controlling fires that may erupt
from the wood stoves. The jugs may be located next to the
hearth or near the door. In the cities, water is drawn from a
community well located in the courtyard of 10-20 houses.

Cooking and Heating 1.v1.2

The typical Newar house contains a large store room, often
located behind the kitchen, where grains are stored, while vegetables are usually brought from the garden in the morning by
the women of the family. There may be niches in the kitchen
walls for storage of cooking equipment and staple foods. But
because space is limited in kitchens, many pots and dried food
erops such as garlic, chillies and corn, are hung from the top
floor windows or stored outside under the eave overhangs; they
also manifest visible signs ofwealth.

Each element of the Newar house contains religious or
symbolic significance, and many are related to food or rituals
associated with food. Inside the entry doorway a rice-pounder
(kuti) is often found along with other domestic implements. If
there is a family room in the house, large movable drum-like
rice-containers (bhakari) are often found along with other
household items. Rituals connected with cooking, eating and
keeping house demonstrate a highly organized way to life. In
Newar culture cleanliness is understood as a means to prevent
disease as well as a means to purify one’s spiritual self.

Equipment typically found in Newari kitchens is a ladle (dhawa) for lentils (dal), a spatula (chataun) for rice, tongs (chinta)
for tending the fire and cooking fried bread (roti). A stone mortar and pestle (loma and macha), which translates as ‘mother
and baby’, is used for pounding spices, rock salt and any other
ingredients. Pots are made of brass, in the Newari craft tradition, and salt is stored ina wooden bowl.

Strict caste-determined sanctions regulate cooking, interdining and the giving and taking ofwater. Boiled rice (ja) is the
staple food of Newars. Its preparation follows strict religious
regulation as does the handling ofit. Rules define the use ofthe
rice utensils and who may touch them. Traditionally only family members and occasionally members ofthe same caste may
eat together. Before eating and drinking and upon first greetings, Buddhist Newaris will chanttaraymam as a form of praise
to Buddha. Buddhist Newaris typically eatmeals sittingon long
woven straw mats facing each other from either side of the
room, men on one side, women opposite. Hindus sit separately
from one another without mats, following their beliefs that itis
impure to touch another when one eats.

A small balcony-terrace (kausi, pronounced kawsi), sometimes
only 60 cm (2 ft) wide by 1.5 m (5 ft) long and usually located
opposite the hearth, serves many purposes. Often the most private part of the house, it is kept clean and neat, and is used for
early morning and evening prayers (pujas) as well as for washing
dishes and vegetables. Itis sunny and usually provides a view, and
iflarge enough may often be used as asocial gathering place.

Traditional Newari hearths do not contain chimneys. Therefore, the roof vernacular developed in unique response to coping with fire and smoke. In traditional houses a small opening
atthe roofeaves allows smoke to escape. Open to the outside, it
is covered byarounded tile, anda wide overhang to preventrain
from entering. TARA MICHELE CAHN
1.V1.2.m Stoves (Central Europe)

Tiled stoves are a form of heating equipment for
warming up rooms with closed fires whose fireproof ceramic
coating radiates and preserves the heat. In addition, such

443

## S. 172

1M SERVICES

stoves were also used in certain parts of the countryside for
cooking inside directly over the fire. In some cases, the food
was baked in the oven, only being reheated and kept warm on
the stove. The stoves usually stood in the corner of the livingroom, not being heated from within this room, however, but
from the neighbouring hallway, the entrancerroom or the ‘black
kitchen’. Depending on their shape, these tiled stoves either
stood upright or were horizontal and were known as ‘prism’,
“cylinder’, ‘conic’, or ‘cupola’ stoves among other terms. They
appeared in Central Europe towards the end ofthe ızth century
and were found in the cultural area of southwestern Europe.
Here the central East European bake ovens then became tiled
stoves due to the influence of Roman stoves; the bake oven
cupolas that are covered with pot tiles are the simplest types of
such stoves.

Alinguistic analysis -the connection between the Latin term
cacalus, the German kachel, the Czech kachel, the Slovak kachl’a,
the Lithuanian kakalys — points to the Roman influence on the
creation oftiled stoves. From the southwest in the 14th century,
tiled stoves headed in a northern and eastern direction. They
first covered the German language region and through Lower
Germany they reached Scandinavia; they gradually reached the
Czech lands and Hungary to the eastand later on, were found in
parts of Slovakia and then Lithuania through Poland. In the
south, the cultural area oftiled stoves was to be found in Croatia, Slovenia and Upper Italy. As with other cultural phenomena, the use of these stoves expanded due to the mediation
efforts ofthe various monasteries, castles and palaces. Atalater
date they appeared in town houses and finally were acknowledged by various rich farmers. In the Czech lands, for instance,
tiled stoves became a general occurrence in town houses atthe
turn ofthe ısth and 16th centuries and by this time they had
also begun to penetrate the homes of rich farmers. While the
medieval tiled stoves still used a large quantity ofwood, about
70 per cent ofthe heat being literally wasted, modern technical
improvements helped to save energy and to make the stoves
more efficient. Compared with bake ovens, tiled stoves were
more economical and cleaner. The stoves, consisting of a
fireplace, ventilation system and a chimney, are covered with
burnt clay tiles. Medieval tiles are shaped like pots, onions and
‘chambers’. Modern tiles are divided according to their function, according to their location within the body of the stove,

© Josef Scheybal

SE

a

444

and according to whether they are potters’, faience or fire-clay
tiles; consideration is also given to the artistic work on the
external surface, whether it besmooth or with amoulded decoration. Most ofthe stoves in the countryside were green, brown
or blue. A great deal of attention was devoted to installingand
decorating the stoves in the living-rooms ofthe southern Tyrol,
in Switzerland, and the Austrian Tyrol in particular, where they
are the most expensive pieces ofindoor furniture.

In the countryside, tiled stoves were built next to the bake
ovens to which they were sometimes directly attached, in certain areas of Slovakia for instance. More often, however, bake
ovens gave way to the stove, and ended up in neighbouring
areas or outside the house. Traditional tiled stoves disappeared
in central Europe from the interiors ofrural houses as cookers
were introduced atthe end ofthe first halfofthe ıgth century. A
part ofa cooking stove that was also covered with tiles ata later
stage usually consisted of a tile stove extension including one
or two baking ovens and a water boiler that is well known in
connection with the tiled stoves. Like the tiled stoves, the upper
surface ofthe tiled extension served as a place for putting the
dishes as well as for drying purposes. This modern heating system that is also known as a ‘stove’ should not be confused with
the traditional medieval tiled stove. JOSEF VAREKA
1.V1.2.m-i Stove: Finnish

Wood-burning stoves constructed in masonry, of
stone or home-made bricks, are common throughout Finland,
and also Sweden, Germany and Russia. Masonry woodstoves
permit intermittent combustion of fuel, but continuous heating, unlike lightweight or even cast iron woodstoves, which
cool down much more rapidly.

The earliest Finnish stoves were simply piles ofstones over a
dry stone vault or lintel slab, and lacked even a chimney. They
heated chimneyless smoke huts, or early sauna dwellings. In
later stoves the combustion gases gradually give up all their
heat by lowing through the masonry inside lengthy convoluted
channels, before finally reaching a chimney. There is adequate
draught despite extended flues including both horizontal and

®

Early 20th-ce,
ul
green-tledsuu
front Sittingpog; n
Mladä Bolesjaypj

central Bohemj;

see also
2.11.4-B.Jizba

References
Bedal, Konrad, 1972
Schier, BrUnO,

49th-century!
with bake ovel
east Moravia

U northwest

n, Alfred, and
j0, A.,1981
jeorge, 1983,

Ylnlan stove

down-draught passages, partly due to the low outdoor winter
temperatures. After a few hours of burning, when about 10-15
kg (22-33 lb) of wood have been consumed, the chimney
dampers are closed, and the heat then discharges from the
mass for up to 24 hours after the fire is out. The chimney
dampers are also used to regulate draught. A certain amount of
daily temperature swing is inevitable, but with practice this is
easily minimized. A high level of thermal comfort is attained,
since there is a lot ofradiant heat from a large surface. Variant
stove types have baking ovens, cast iron hotplates and kitchen
ranges, open fireplaces, and sleeping platforms all incorporated into the same massive structure. There are also masony
sauna stoves and hot-water heaters, butthe commonestsinglefunction woodstove is a tall room-heater for chambers. These
were greatly improved in efficiency in the late-ı8th century, up
to an astonishing 85-90 per cent fuel conversion efficiency, and
as a result are almost totally non-polluting. A properly built
masonry woodstove has a lifetime of 50-80 years.

The stoves were at first bare stone or brickwork, later rendered
with a slurry oflime or chalk mixed with curdled or skimmed
milk and flour, and lately encased in ceramic tiles or galvanized
sheet. Stone is granite, schist or soapstone, while bricks are
small and home-fired. The soft mortar is often clay-based to
reduce cracking. Metal parts are cast iron or copper and firebox
doors are double, so that the outer door does not become dangerously hot.

Stoves are often clustered around one or two vertical chimneys
(on the ‘warm heart’principle), in the corners ofrooms, butlong

Oo Moley 19aa

Cooking and Heating 1.1.2
slanting or near horizontal chimneys were often built through
theattic,permitting greater dispersal ofstoves in the house plan.
During the period 1930-60 woodstove air-convective heat-ing
and also radiant heating by hollow brickwall radiators arrived.
Further developments were cut offby the advent ofcoke boilers,
oil-fired central heating and water radiators. Nowadays, particularly in rural areas, most new houses in Finland have at least
one masonry woodstove, usually built to modern pattern-book

designs, and based in the end upon the vernacular originals.
GEORGE WOOLSTON

1V1.2.m-ii Stove: Russian

Archaeological findings show thatthe ‘Russian stove’
was already in use before the roth century. Similar stoves were
incorporated into eastern European houses and into vernacular
buildings in Central Europe, the Caucasus, Central Asia and
Siberia, where the hearth stove and fireplace predominated.
The strongly traditional positioning of the Russian stove, the
formal area, table, fixed bench, and so on, were all specific factors in the furnishing and internal layout of the living space.
Diagonally across from the stove was the formal area, variously
termed the front (peredny), ‘best’ or ‘posh’ (krasny) or holy (suyatoi) corner or area (ugol). This relationship between the stove
and the formal area is a characteristic feature of eastern Slav
dwellings. In the formal area hung the icons, while in the place
of honour stood the dining table. The walls were hung with
coloured patterned material and popular prints and there were

flowers. LYUDMILA CHIZHIKOVA

445

## S. 173

Sanitation and Hygiene

1.V1.3

The layout, construction and services of vernacular
buildings and traditional human settlements are influenced by
local site conditions and ecological conditions at the much
larger scale of the biosphere. However, the built environment
also influences local sanitary conditions. Vernacular buildings
can benefit and/or harm human health and well-being. On the
one hand, the needs of people for shelter, security, hygiene and
nutrition can be met by applying sound ecological principles
that account for microclimatic conditions. There are numerous
documented examples of traditional building layouts and construction methods that provide vernacular houses with effective
insulation against cold and hot temperatures and adequate
cross-ventilation when required. On the other hand, people may
be inadequately protected against unsafe water for consumption, toxic wastes, polluted air and soil, and uncontrolled vectors
of disease largely owing to the proximity of animals and
humans, coupled with poor site drainage (WHO, 1991).

Beyond the geographical scale of specific building sites,
indirect effects ofthe built environment may occur through the
depletion of resources, especially arable land and forests, and
by the inadequate management of wastes discharged into
the atmosphere, water and soil. The factors that adversely
influence sanitation and impact on health can be divided into
five main categories, the first of which is the insufficient carrying capacity ofthe ecological system atlocaland much broader
scales. The insufficiency occurs when emissions and waters are
discharged at a greater rate than they can be transformed by
either natural or human processes, when renewable resources
are consumed ata greater rate than they are renewed, and when
non-renewable resources are consumed even though renewable substitutes for them have not been found. A second category is that of environmental accidents, such as floods,
earthquakes and landslides. The likelihood of such accidents
has often been predicted by studies of site conditions and geological factors at a larger scale. However, there is a growing
amount of evidence which shows that many floods and landslides also result from human activities, such as deforestation
and mining.

Inadequate air quality, potable water and waste disposal also
adversely influence sanitation. The ineffective drainage of surface water and the inadequate treatment of excreta before they
contaminate supplies of drinking water are crucial aspects of
sanitation around buildings. According to the World Health
Organization (1993), ‘ensuring a safe and ample water supply
is mankind’s most important public health measure. About
80 per cent of all illness in the developing world is said to be
associated with lack of safe water.’

446

Fourthly, inmany developing countries sanitary problems stem
from the close proximity of animals, and the use of biomass
fuels and coal for indoor cooking and heating, encouraging the
presence of pathogens, toxins, vectors and other factors. Sound
building principles include adequate ventilation, site drainage
and impervious floors and wall. The fifth factor is the inadequate shelter provided by buildings. In contrast to the makeshift
abodes in a growing number of squatter settlements on the outskirts of many cities, traditional vernacular buildings comonly
embodya wide range oflayout principles and construction techniques thatrespond positively to the specific climatic conditions
oftheir site.

Health has been interpreted in diverse ways. Here the definition h
of the World Health Organization (1946) will be used. Accordingly, ‘health is a state of complete physical, mental and social
well-being and not merely the absence ofdisease and infirmity’. 4
It is generally accepted that there is a relationship between the
conditions of built and natural environments and the health of
the inhabitants. Although there is no consensus aboutthenature
ofthat relationship, there is agrowing recognition ofthose con- j
ditions thatshould be improved to promote health, for example,
site conditions with inadequate surface water drainage and solid
waste disposal. Nonetheless, it is not a simple matter to extract
and isolate conditions of built environments from other attributes which form an integral part ofthe lifestyle ofthe inhabitants,
and, therefore, influence their health. Adequate nutrition, for
example, and restricted access to medical care have an importantrole which can be interpreted in diverse ways. For example,

Latrines projectingovef
ariver, Nigeria.

x

6, et al,,1993

90

WVagit,1992

1977

Jome, and Sim-

üheon left ofa
Ihbort Degirmen,
MAnatolia,

©

malnutrition can be considered in terms ofsocioeconomic factors
as well as seasonal variations in thesupply offoodand thelack of.
sanitary facilities for storing and preparing food. There are important conceptual and methodological questions thatneed to be
examined ifthe relationships between vernacular building conditions and health are to be understood inacomprehensive way.
In principle, all buildings can have both positiveand negative
impacts on health. Vernacular buildings are no exception, but
they usually differ from ones of modern design according to
one or more ofthe following characteristics: orientation on the
site; layoutand uses of space, fittings, equipment and services;
construction materials and methods; and effective occupancy
conditions. Consequently, itis appropriate to examine the kinds
of impact these characteristics can have on health and wellbeing, as Imamöglu has done in his study of traditional residential buildings in the historic Turkish town of Kayseri. He
found that the local residents acknowledged the difficulties of
living in old timber houses, owing to the lack ofrunning water
for ablutions and human consumption, inadequate heating,
absence of an indoor toilet, and the task of continual maintenance. Nonetheless, the residents were unanimous that it was
healthier to live in these houses rather than modern flats built of
brick and concrete. The older residents maintained that ‘concrete buildings induce rheumatism, sleep disorders and more
frequent influenza’ Such beliefs reflecta simple and humble life
based largely on a religious interpretation ofthe cosmos. This
interpretation has been replaced by the acceptance ofsecularism.
Traditionally, vernacular buildings were oriented and constructed to account for the microclimatic conditions of their
site, such as the water table, shelter from inclement winds and
maximum exposure to sunshine in cold climates. Such customs, when combined with the appropriate layout and use of
rooms as well as construction materials, often provided opti-

Sanitation and Hygiene 1.v1.3
mal indoor climatie conditions (Martin). Often, itwas common
for cooking to occur outside the main building. Studies show
that when traditional outdoor fireplaces have been replaced by
indoor hearths that use biomass fuels or coal, or by gas or
paraffin stoves, the harmful consequences offumes and excessive heat have counteracted the positive qualities ofthe indoor
environment. Rickets have been identified in situations ofpoor
indoor air quality and lack ofsunlight (Benneh etal.).

Chagas’ disease is a trypanosomiasis (‘river blindness’) that
is transmitted by an arachnid found throughouttropical America. This vector lodges in the cracks of wall partitions. Therefore, if these partitions are plastered then the vectors’ lodging
places can be eliminated and the risk ofthe transmission ofthe
disease is greatly reduced.

Intestinal parasitic diseases are largely the result ofthe cohabitation of people with domesticated animals, and poor site
drainage and sanitation, such as the contamination of plants
in a vegetable garden by a latrine. Poor drainage provides a vector for the transmission of schistosomiasis disease (bilharzia)
and mosquito-borne infections. Drainage construction is an
effective, preventive health measure in preference to the use of
insecticides. Parasitic zoonsoses (diseases caused by animal
parasites) including leishamniasis (transmitted by dogs) and
psittacosis (transmitted by birds) result from human beingsand
domestic animals sharing the same internal spaces. Microscopic pathogens can be found in the excreta of animals and
infected humans. Poor food hygiene also promotes this kind of
disease which is transmitted in meat (Nriagu and Simmons).

Enteric diseases stem largely from non-potable drinking
water and inadequate sanitary disposal of excreta plus other
solid and liquid wastes. One consequence of these diseases
(which include typhoid, paratyphoid and infectious intestinal
illnesses) is a high infant mortality rate. Piped supplies of
potable water not only counteract the transmission of waterborne diseases but also the incidence of skin diseases.

Tuberculosis has been linked to poor housing conditions
especially overcrowding, as wellas inadequate heating, ventilation and exposure to sunlight. In Greenland, for example, Eskimos have been relocated from shacks to modern houses in
order to successfully combat the incidence of tuberculosis
(Giroult). Although such sanitary engineering projects haveled
to improvements in the health ofpeople, theindiscriminate use
of imported construction materials and building layouts can
also have unforeseen negative consequences on indoor air
quality, comfort and the use of domestic space and facilities.
Therefore, both the costs and benefits to health and well-being
should be borne in mind when assessing the impacts oftraditional and innovative approaches for the construction of built
environments. RODERICK J. LAWRENCE
Ablutions
Standards and practices in ablutions, in particular
washing ofthe face, hands and body are influenced by factors
related to access to water, to values concerning the person, and
expectations of appropriate or ritualized behaviour. Cleanliness in the dwelling need not necessarily imply cleanliness with
regard to the person; nor is the converse any more predictable.

1.V1.3.a

447

## S. 174

1.v1 SERVICES

So for instance, from the days of Captain Cook the cleanliness
ofthe Tahitians was repeatedly noted by visitors to the Society
Islands. James Morrison, who had sailed with Bligh on the Bounty
recorded the Tahitian habit of washing before and after eating
and their revulsion at the contact with a dead lizard or mouse,
or with a deceased person. If a man had a running sore his
house and all its contents were burned. Bligh himselfobserved
that the Tahitians bathed twice daily but ‘they are nevertheless
filthy about their dwellings’.

Perhaps under Christian influence, Tahitians in the 2oth century have been meticulous in their cleanliness ofvillage, house
and person. Tahitians still bathe atleastoncea day, and clothes,
even ifoold, are clean. Wash-houses and privies are constructed
within the house yard, which is swept daily. Yet away from the
house both men and women may urinate or even defecate in the
company of others of the same sex, and there is little embarrassment ifthey are observed (Levy).

Bathing may be desirable and may contribute to health and
social acceptability, but it is nota physical necessity. It is largely
dependent on an adequate and suitable water supply, but its
frequency, regularity, thoroughness, even the pleasure to be
gained from it, and the provision of some measure of facilities,
are culturally determined. ‘It must indeed be admitted of the
Eskimos that they are not overfond of washing’, wrote Birket-
Smith in the mid-ı930s. In Alaska they used a form of steam
bath, rubbingtheir bodies in specially heated houses with amixture of snow and stale urine. This had ‘excellent fat-dissolving
properties’ opening the pores and permitting the flow ofperspiration, resulting in a higher level ofcleanliness than thatamong
the Central Tnuit (Birket-Smith). In Greenland urine was used
for hair-washing, and further south the Nootka also practised
urine washing though they were close to sea and rivers.

Traditionally the steam bath (temascal) of pre-Hispanic origin,
has been used in Mexico. A low dome built of adobe or stone, it
has a small entrance and an adjacent fireplace where lava rocks
are heated. When these are glowing they are taken into the steam
bath and doused with water which produces intensely hot steam,
causing the occupants to perspire profusely. Taking a regular
steam bath is believed to promote health and to clean both the
inside and the outside ofthe body (Yampolsky and Sayer). Among
the Maya, a weekly bath was customary, the women in some villages taking a sponge bath in the bedroom, while others had a
small, screened bathing shelter ofwicker or reeds, equipped with
water vessels and a gourd dipper; in the latter case every household had a deep well. When public showers were installed they
were regularly used by peasant farmers (Covarrubias; Foster).

In a number of societies people may bathe only two or three
times in their adult lives. This may relate to the lack ofavailable
water, and economy in its use, but it may also bea cultural preference. Among the Dani of Irian Jaya chunks of pig grease,
sometimes mixed with charcoal, were smeared over the body
and in the hair as a cosmetic. However, the Dani ‘occasionally
swim but practically never bathe in the sense of washing the
entire body with water’, wrote Heider in his ‘holistic’ ethnogra-
Phy, his sole comment on the subject ofablutions.

Detailed ethnographic examination ofbathing habits is rare;
an exception was Elwin’s account of bathing among the Muria

448

Gonds of Bastar, Madhya Pradesh, India. Groups of adolescent
boys (chelik) ofthe youth house (ghotul) publicly bathed naked.
Their counterparts, the groups of pubescent girls (motiari),
bathed naked in a stream, using mud as a soap, but in a public
tank or bathing place they manipulated their clothing so as to
keep itdry while bathing the body. Within the house a hollowed
tree-trunk trough was used for washing, with an outletthrough
the wall taking used water to a sago or other favoured tree. A
long-handled dipper of water was held under the chin and
tipped to wet both hands for washing. Yet, in spite ofmeticulous
washing by motiari during and after menstruation, the Muria
seldom washed before eating, and did not wash after defecation, while elderlywomen who had notthe strength to go to the
river or carry water to the house were often dirty (Elwin).

That water supply, sanitation, hygiene and health are interrelated is universally recognized; recommendations for the provision ofstandpipes, composting latrines, and warnings ofthe
dangers ofwater-borne vectors and bacteria are numerous (Darrow etal.). Ritual bathing as part ofrites of passage or washing
as religious practice are commonly described, but analysis and
comparison ofvalues related to bathing as matters ofhygiene is
necessary if such measures are to be effective. The subject of
secular ablutions and traditional household provision for them,
is still seriously under-researched. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.3.b Baths: Japanese
Bathing as a means of achieving personal cleanliness is influenced to a significant extent by climate. In hot climates, cleanliness has frequently been achieved by washing in
water courses or outside the dwelling, rarely heating the water.
In colder climates, the tub filled up in front ofthe fire has been
used widely. The advance of living standards in the 2oth cen-

- tury, particularly the provision of piped services to individual

households, has steadily been increasing, leading to the decline
in traditional forms ofbathing.

In Japan, bathing, whilst based on the individual family unit,
has an important social as well as cleansing function which can
be shared byrrelatives and neighbours who have no bath. Beardsley describes the sharing of bath and well in Niike on the

see also
2.11.8.f Tahitiang.
3.

-VI.1.P West Grognlang
Eskimo uy

References
Birket-Smith, Ka
Covarrubias, Migı
Darrow, Ken, Kell
and Pam, Rick,
Elwin, Verrier, 1947
Foster, George M,19%
Heider, Karl 6,1
Levy, Robert |,
Yampolsky, Marlar
‚Sayer, Chloe,

Washingatt! \
Varanasi, UttarPrali
north India.

house
‚umt Souk,
ansia, Fuel
jentrance to
stleft-

sior 1.V1.3.b
Ichard K.,
and ward,

island of Honshu, as the simplest form of co-operative activity,
ina society where 25 per centare without baths.

Body cleanliness is an old and honoured tradition atall levels

ofJapanese society. Public baths are widely distributed and used,
with segregation ofthe sexes and with artisans bathinganumber
of times a day (Morse). Almost all better-off and many poorer
households have ample arrangements for hot baths within the
home.

The baths themselves come in a variety of forms, from a simple iron pot, ora copper, to atub made from timber; they are set
permanently on a base of bricks. Invariably they are large and
deep enough for the bather to sit with the knees drawn up. The
water is heated direct, commonly by lighting a fire in a small
copper chamber near the bottom ofthe tub. In another form, a
copper funnel or tube running from the top through the bottom
of the tub is filled with charcoal which heats the surrounding
water, while in a third version a fire is contained in a copper
tube within a small barrel and connected to the tub by a bamboo tube, which can be closed offifthe water gets too hot. Most
tubs are fabricated with transverse bars or a timber duckboard
inserted to prevent the bather from coming in contact with the
hot fire chamber. A more elaborate version straddles the partition between two rooms, with water freely circulating through
the partition, through a series of bamboo tubes. The firebox is
contained in the outside section so that the smoke does not
affect the bather. The bath cubicle is often located close to the
kitchen so that the same straw pile may be used to fire both.

Amongst the inhabitants of Niike, the senior member ofthe
household bathes first, in the hottest water, with the remainder
following in order of sex and age precedence. After soaking in
the steaming water, the bather stands or sits outside the tub on
draining boards and scrubs with soap and a light towel, and
rinses off with water scooped from the tub, to keep the water as
clean as possible, before climbing back in for a final soak. Small
children bathe with their parents, and neighbours and friends
without baths come to chatand wait their turn. Fuel is precious
and in summer, well water is drawn in the morning and warms
to air temperature during the day, thus saving fuel. Some households only fire the bath, which is a welcome respite from the
cold, every two or three days. DEIRDRE MCDERMOTT
Bathhouse: Islamic
Public baths are widespread in Islamic cultures and
it is generally accepted that the origin of the Islamic hot bath
lies in the provincial or late classical bath of the Near East,
where the Islamic empire ofthe Umayyads was based, although
the practical promotion ofcleanliness, as opposed to entertainment, has led to the development ofa much less elaborate form
than in ancient times. The prevalence of public baths arose
from the importance of cleanliness to the religion of Islam and
to prescriptions related to personal cleanliness and hygiene,
with an additional requirement for personal ablution after sexual intercourse. This latter prescription may account for the
incidence of secret passages from the houses of the wealthy to
the women’s section ofthe baths in parts of central Iran.

The bathhouse (hammam) comprises at least two rooms: a
changing room where people sit on raised slabs facing each

1.V1.3.C

Sanitation and Hygiene 1.013

other and leave their clothes, and a bath hall, with raised seating slabs around the walls and a communal hot pool and a
warm pool to rinse in. Here, people soak in the hot bath before
rubbing themselves with a soap-impregnated cloth andrinsing
off with water from the rinsing pool. Workers (dallaks) are generally in attendance to do the cleaning and rinsing. In larger
hammams, the plan is more complex and there are many rooms
which become progressively hotter until the hot pool is
reached. People frequently spend hours in the hammam, which
is arelaxing and comfortable place in which to socialize.

The changing room is most often a large high-domed space,
expressed externally, while the remainder ofthe rooms are low
in order to keep up the temperature. In Iran they are built in
fired brick or stone, being too damp for the dried mud-brick in
which most ofthe other buildings are fabricated. They are usually built into the ground to conserve heat, with thevault, dome,
chimney and an entrance above ground. The water is supplied
bya ganat and flows down through the heating chamber fed by
brush and wood fuel, to the baths. The smoke from the fire
heats the bath room from ducts under the floor before exiting
through the chimney.

The Ottomans used a highly durable plaster internally
together with sandstones; the latter are selected for their resistance both to the high pressure around the oven and the copper
cauldron, and to the moisture on the floor above the hypocaust.

The use of public baths for the performance of sacred ablutions often led to them being located near major mosques and
each neighbourhood has a hammam. Referring to hammams in
San’ä, Serjeant and Lewcock note that there are 14 different
hammans in the city, each open to the different sexes on alternate days. No particular allegiance is shown to the neighbourhood hammam and people tend to use the nearest one, available
to their sex on the day. DEIRDRE McCDERMOTT
1.Vv13.d Bathing ghat (India)

The bathing ghat, found throughout India, is a collection of broad steps at the edge of a water-source - a river,
lake, well or manmade reservoir — that serves as a place for
bathing, washing clothing or performing devotions. Ghats have
been built for centuries and continue to be built today. While
some are cut directly from a stone embankment, mostare composed of stone, usually granite, red sandstone or marble, that
has been quarried and transported to the site. The term ghat

449

## S. 175

SO Peter Engel

1.v1 SERVICES

refers primarily to steps at the edge ofa river, while steps completely surrounding a body of water are usually called a ‘tank’
(kund). In south India, theterm kalyani applies to abathingtank
beside a temple. A narrow flight ofsteps descending to a well is
known as a stepwell (vav). The two mountain ranges that run
along India’s coasts and step down to the Arabian Sea and the
Bay of Bengal are referred to as the Western and Eastern Ghats.

Forall Hindus, and most other Indians, water is sacred, symbolizing the origin oflife, fertility and purity. Cleansing ofthe
hands and feet is required prior to enteringa holy place, and for
this reason ghatsand kunds are often located adjacent to temples
or shrines. A typical series of steps and platforms descends
from 3 m (10 ft) to 0 m (32 ft) below ground and is made of
stone to retain the sloping earth. The ghat continues below the
surface ofthe water so thatwhen rainfall ortides fluctuate there
will always be landings at water level. A small village ghat may
consist ofno more than three or four platforms atarriver-bank,
while a large, formal kund may measure 35 m (115 fi) x 5om
(164 ft) or more and be 15 m (49 ft) deep. A kund is often rectangular or square, its pattern ofcascading terraces reminiscent of
the symmetries of the mandala. The geometry of the steps is
enhanced in the larger and more elaborate kunds by intricately
caıved pavilions and by shrines, devoted to different gods and
goddesses, placed at intermediary levels.

The ghat carries important emotional connotations for many
Indians as a place of transition, a place of arriving and leavetaking. The most trenchant example is the ghat at Varanasi in
Uttar Pradesh, where cremated bodies are sent out onto the

450

Ganges river to begin their final earthly journey. Otherghatsand
kunds are on the banks of the Ganges at Hardwar, Uttar
Pradesh, Surya kund in Modhera, Gujarat, Surkhej kund and
Kapadvanj kund, both near Ahmedabad, Gujarat, and Banganga
kund at Walkeshwar in Bombay, Maharashtra. DEhER Eden,

1.VI.3.e Bush and ocean disposal

The assumption that indigenous practices for the
disposal of human wastes are unimportant has meant that ‘the
potential for upgrading existing practices has largely been
ignored. Once existing conditions are understood as a starting
point, certain solutions will be more compatible with resources
available’, observed Rybezinski et al. in 1978; regrettably there
has since been little evidence of greater recognition of such systems of waste disposal. In part, this is due to the lack of any
structural provision in many cultures. Peoples in tropical forest
regions frequently use the ‘bush’, or forest, for purposes of
excretion where the matter is rapidly consumed by animals,
insects and bacteria, or washed into the soil by rains. Generally
areas of forest cover near a village community may be designated for male and for female use. Some forest cultures who live
in longhouses tether pigs and other scavenging animals below
the raised floor, to consume offal, excreta and other waste.

In desertregions, thin scrub cover will often afford the members ofanomadic group sufficient privacy for excretion, should
this be culturally required. Fluids evaporate within minutes,
whole solids are rapidly dessicated or consumed by desert
rodents. The sea is more vast than the deserts, and coastal or
island communities frequently use the ocean both for washing
and for excretion. There were no latrines on Tikopia as late as
the mid-1960s, though islanders who had travelled elsewhere
were well aware ofthem. It was taboo for anyone to excrete in
the neighbourhood of the chief’s house; it also risked a fatal
sickness. Instead, all people used the sea, which Firth contended
was effective in killing potentially dangerous organisms, though
he attributed the continuity of the custom to the failure ofthe
chiefs to consider sanitation as other than a private matter.

Elsewhere in the Pacific the use ofthe ocean is still personal,
ifmoreregularized; in Kiribati (Gilbert Islands) each family builds
its own house latrine (kainakotari) over the water, approached bya
plank walk on slender pole supports. The latrine house may be
aslargeas 2m (6.5 ft)x 3 m (to ft) and is constructed with apole
frame and woven mat or cane siding. A substantial palm thatch
roof, occasionally hipped, keeps the interior dry. The latrine,
covered with a plankin which ahole has been cut, is directly over
the water. While there are serious problems of transmission of
diseases in relatively narrow waters, or in enclosed seas such as
the Mediterranean it is possible that the comparatively small
populations ofisland communities in Oceania were atlittle risk.

Nevertheless, as populations grow and small communities
become large villages or townships, the problems of sewage
disposal become more acute. In the Indian subcontinent the
lowest castes were often responsible for keeping the streets
swept. So for instance, the phore caste were required to gather
and dispose of the faecal matter which fouled the lanes ofthe
Newar towns. In the side alleys the system was more honoured
in the breach than the observance.

See also

1.VLS. Step .
References
Herdeg, Klaus,
Jain, Kulbhush,
Kagal, Carmen
Party, Eric,agg7

Bathing ghatat|
of the river Gange
India. ih

1,1981
I, Witold,
en, Chongrak, and
It}, Michael, 1978
N, Tony,1979

re latrine which
donce ayear at

Water as a means of disposing of excrement has a long history. Cesspools are pits of considerable size over which, for
many centuries, latrines have been built. Often lined with brick
or clay and wattle, they contained sufficient water to cover the
faeces and reduce its offensiveness, at least for a period. Eventually the stagnant water in which dead animals, offal, vegetable refuse and other unwanted matter were often thrown
became so offensive as to require either complete filling in or
cleaning out. Part ofthe problem was that the water was static;
flowing water removed the excrement from the garderobes or
latrines built over them, even ifit carried the offensive matter to
others living downstream; much of the matter fell to the river
bottom to be consumed or broken down by bacteria.

There are few reports of the provision of public latrines in
traditional communities. The Asante are an exception; visitors
to the West African kingdom in the ıgth century were impressed by the lavatories in the Asantehene’s palace and other
important buildings, which were usually placed under an arch
in a rear corner. Sometimes they were situated in asmall closet
at an upper level with a hollow pillar that supported the floor
also servingasachute. The pits were ‘dugto asurprisingdepth,
and boiling water is daily poured down, which effectually presents the least offence’ reported T. E. Bowdich, as early as 1819.
Village sanitation was also approvingly noted by travellers, and
over 150 years later the Asante villages still had yaane, separate
public latrines for men and women situated at the fringe ofthe
bush. Alow clay wallenclosed.a deep trench across which poles
were laid to support the users; other poles supported a protective roof thatched with palm-fronds with a gap for ventilation.
The yaane ‘are almost the sole structures still to be plastered
with the bright red clay formerly used for decorating the lower
parts ofall major buildings’ (McLeod).

All means of sewage disposal, including the expensive and
resource-consuming water-borne flushing systems, have their
problems and disadvantages, but often within specific circumstances and local scale they have functioned adequately.
Improvements may be desirable, even vitally necessary, but
innovations that accommodate and profit by vernacular methods and customs may be the most likely to succeed, culturally,
environmentally and economically. PAUL OLIVER
1v1.3.f Chutes (Philippines)

Chutes used to play an important role in the waste
disposal system of affluent households in central Luzon during
the 1900s to 19308. In their two-storey houses, the dwelling unit
was located in the upper storey, as were latrines. These were
wooden structures on stilts situated atsome distance behind the
house, separated by a causeway. Wooden boxes with holes opened
above a pit in the ground below - often close to the pigsty. To
connect the latrine holes with the pit, large bamboos (Bambusa
blumeana), measuring 7-10 cm (2.7-4 in) across, were hollowed
outand tied to the latrine boxes with wire or rattan. Shell-lime
poured inside discouraged worms. Manure from the pitwas dug
up and used as fertilizer, claims one informant (Santos-Viola).
But another says that, when used, manure came from cattle or
horses (Pablo). Bamboo chutes disappeared when flush latrines
became common in the 1940s. FERNANDO N. ZIALCITA

Sanitation and Hygiene 1.v1.3

1.V1.3.8g Composting latrines

The principle ofrecycling human excrement as fertilizer is widely recognized. In many cultures this is practised
simply by defecating in the fields. In some places latrine systems have been devised in which the faeces are composted with
vegetable matter in order to produce a rich organic fertilizer. In
1952 it was estimated that 70 per cent of Chinese excrement
was recycled in this way (Winblad and Kilama). These systems
may be divided into two types - communal and point of use
composting.

The communal collection and processing of human excrement was a response to the sanitation problems of urbanization. In China, many city houses would have bucket latrines
which could be emptied from a hatch outside the house. In
1956, it was reported that up to 15 000 rural commune members would enter Guangzhou (Canton) every night with carts to
collect the compost (Winblad and Kilama). In Yemen, longdrop latrines are used in both rural and urban houses. The
latrine is on an upper floor and either cantilevered out beyond
the outer wall or over an internal shaft adjacent to the outer
wall. In urban areas the waste falls to the street or to an open
chamber at ground level where it is collected. In the major
conurbations, such as San’ä, this practice has been replaced
with water-borne sewerage systems.

Point of use, composting latrines managed by individual
households seem to be particularly favoured in the cultures
influenced by Tibetan Buddhism in western China, northwest
India, and northern Pakistan. In Ladakh, Zanskar, and Baltistan
virtually every house has a two-storey latrine as an integral part
ofthe house. The lower storey forms a large tank which is emptied at sowing time by unblocking a walled up opening to the

Aapna 9130

## S. 176

1.v1 SERVICES

outside. In Baltistan, there are often animal pens on the upper
floor in order to provide dung to feed to the latrine. Vegetable
matter such as fallen leaves and harvest residues are collected
and put into the latrine. A pile of earth is maintained in the
cubicle some of which is swept down into the tank after each
use. In Zanskar and generally in Ladakh the squat platform is
surrounded by walls and a roof. In Baltistan it is common for
the platform to be open to view. Typically the squat platform
will have three or four holes. In the Hunza valley there are often
two latrines associated with each house. The chukan is some
distance from the house and consists of a low platform over a
small tank which is open at the back. Animal dung is not put
into the latrine and only a few householders report adding
leaves from the fruit trees. The second latrine, the gem, is adjacent to the house and is primarily for use by infants and the
infirm but in poorer households it may be the only form of sanitation. The gem is simply a walled enclosure with no tank. The
excrement is occasionally cleared out and put on the fields. In
Upper Hunza (Gojal), where it is colder the cattle shed (tarkang)
may double as a latrine which in some cases is reserved for use
by women. Earth closet systems were used in Europe, notably
Scandinavia and Ireland.

The preyalence of domestic composting systems in the high
altitude areas of Asia may be a product ofthe dry atmosphere
which avoids the excessive moisture that has proved problematic in attempts to introduce composting latrines in tropical
climates. In addition, at high altitudes there are fewer flies and
there are often extreme problems of poor soil fertility.

ERIC DUDLEY
1.V1.3.h Garderobe
Probably of medieyal origin, the term ‘garderobe’,
from the French garder to keep, and robe, cloak, referred to an
armoıy or small room for keeping outer garments. Subsequently applied to a small storeroom or closet it became a
euphemism for a form of privy chamber, which contained a
stone or wooden seat with a hole in the top, concealed by a
door. A number of examples exist in medieyal castles in the
British Isles, normally constructed within the thickness of the
wall, or corbelled out from it. Avertical shaftor chute permitted
the excreta to drop into a pit, or outside the castle wall, to be
cleared at intervals. Some were elementary, as at the ı2th-century Orford Castle; others were clustered as at Langley Castle
where a four-storey turret had a group of four at each level or,
like Southwell Palace, grouped around a central shaft with separate chutes converging on a pit.

At what date such garderobes were to be found in vernacular
buildings is unclear, but there is evidence that they were constructed in private houses as early as the 12th century, and were
frequently recorded in subsequent centuries. Many overhung
common open sewers and drains, water courses or cesspits. A
drainage channel for the clearance of the garderobes ran
behind one range of domestic lodgings at Dartington Hall,
Devon, but the fall ofthe land was considered sufficient below
another.

Sewers and cesspits had to be cleaned out periodically, often
by latrine cleaners (gong fermors) who were well paid for the

452

2661 1BNIO INE4@

unpleasant work. Smell and contamination was the subject of
court cases which have provided documentary evidence of the
progress in sanitation. Paintings by genre painters such as the
16th-century Flemish artist Pieter Brueghel frequently depiet
garderobes on vernacular houses, sometimes in use. Usually
these were projecting wooden structures supported by wooden
brackets, which could have rotted readily, accounting for the
small number that have survived. Many were demolished when
backyard privies were constructed following the mid-ıgth-century Public Health Acts. Yet, though they were separated from
the farmhouses two-holer garderobes were constructed over
streams in Cumbria and were still in use in the 1950s.

2661 2ONIO NEE

Garderobe gg}

tt
Wealden oc

England,

ntishIsies

am

See also for f.Viag.

2.1.9.n Zanskarl

Winblad, U., anı

1978

fahan, Iran.

Cantilevered or bracket-supported garderobes were in use in
mountain regions of Western Anatolia in the1g8os constructed
so that excreta could discharge over manure heaps piled close
to the back or side wall offarmhouses. Frequently mixed with a
long dung-fork the chopped material was spread over the peasant fields. An alternative that was more discreet but functioned
similarly was an internal garderobe approached by a short corridor from the sofa, the central room situated in the upper floor.
The garderobe discharged on the manure heap ofthe cattle byre
in the lower floor. Garderobes ofasimilar kind have been noted
in China. PAUL OLIVER
1.v13.| Mikveh: ritual bath

According to traditional Jewish law, the mikveh
(meaning ‘collection [ofwater]’), a bath ofclear spring or rainwater for immersion ofthe body to provide ritual purification,
is the first structure built in a Jewish community. The structure
has been documented in archaeological remains of ancient
sites in Israel and continues to be in use today, especially
among Orthodox sects. The bath can be in a separate building
or in part ofa building but it must follow several specifications
codified in Mikva’ot, the sixth tractate in the order ofthe Tohorot
in the classic Jewish texts ofthe Mishnah and Tosefta. The water
must not have been ‘drawn’ and thus not carried in a vessel or
receptacle. The minimum quantity for immersion is a square
cubit (a cubit equals approximately 50 cm [20 in]) to the height
ofthree cubits (estimated in rabbinical tracts as a volume of 40
se’'ah, symbolizing abundance).

The mikveh cannot be prefabricated, since thatwould make it
an invalid ‘vessel’. Itmay be carved out ofstone or builtin orput
on the ground, and various materials are used in differentregions
ofthe Jewish diaspora. Care is taken to ensure that water flows
freely; if the water is stopped, then it becomes invalid ‘drawn
water”. It must not reach the bath through vessels of metal or
other materials that may be ritually unclean. To avoid these
invalidations, builders often attach pipes to the ground, so that
they cease to have the status of ‘vessels’, or rain spouts lead
directly into the bath rather than to the exterior ofthe structure.

Builders typically use a ‘contact store’ or ‘store of seeding’
system for the bath. In the first system, ordinary water flows
onto the rain-water through an aperture in the wallofthemikveh.
In the second system, water is added from an absorbent trough
which is dug into the ground or made oflean concrete (i.e. concrete that has less cement in it than that for structures). The
added water is considered to be coming from the ground and
therefore not through a vessel. Other systems include a combination ofthe previous two or the construction ofa dut, a cistern
ortankbuiltinto the ground to store rain-water. Theritual baths
can be found in the basements of synagogues or in separate
buildings. When in a separate building, additional rooms may
be added such as hairdressing salons (some women follow the
belief that hair must be washed before immersion), dining
rooms, showers, and storage rooms. Seven or eight steps are
usually built into the bath. The buildings are usually rectangular
matching the common rectangular shape of the baths, but
octagonal designs may be found in sites such as Ashdod, Israel.
The complicated regulations for constructing the mikveh mean

Sanitation and Hygiene 1.v1.3

thatspecialists and rabbinical supervision are often required for
its erection. Tradition has been adapted to new materials, such
as the acceptance ofporous concrete forthe trough ofvalidation
and floor tiles to prevent leaking of water.

The primary use ofthe mikueh today is for the ritual purification of menstruating women (considered an impure state of
niddah). In addition, the mikveh is used for men or women who
have become ritually unclean because of contact with the dead
or any other defiling object. Mikveh immersion is a required
part ofthe ceremony of conversion into Judaism. It also fosters
spirituality, especially on the eve of the Sabbath and festivals,
particularly the Day of Atonement. Itcan beadditionallyused to
purify vessels, especially new dishes and utensils purchased
from non-Jews. SIMON J. BRONNER
1V1.3.j Night-soil

Many methods of sanitation require the removal of
excreta at regular intervals. Often the sanitary facilities may be
a latrine or privy, either outside the dwelling or within a small
room off a courtyard and adjacent to an outside, rear wall,
where ran a back lane or service alley, narrow but wide enough
to accommodate the scavenger and his buckets, donkey with
panniers, or dung cart. Common in many parts of Europe, and
the Mediterranean regions, the latrine would generally be emptied atnight, the scavenger openinga flap to obtain the excreta
with a pan or shovel. Some pans were removable, being in the
form ofa metal box, known to British colonists as a ‘thunderbox’ from the resonant sounds of emptying and replacement.
The large volume of excrement was sometimes simply carted
awayand dumped, but more frequently itwas mixed with earth
and used as fertilizer; hence the appropriate and descriptive
term, ‘night-soil’.

Less coy or squeamish than many ethnographers, Hommel
described the use of night-soil in China, where it was applied
‘almost exclusively’ for manuring the fields. The excreta were
stored in large earthenware pots 0.75 m (2.5 ft) or more in
diameter, which stood unsheltered or were sunk to the rim in
the ground, in which case they were normally covered by an
open privy hut. This was for the use ofmen only; women useda
wooden bucket with a cover kept within the house but emptied
into the outside pots. When removed for manuring the contents were transferred to pairs of wooden buckets with pierced
lugs slanted inwards. Through these were bamboo strip loops
by which they were suspended from the customary bamboo

EL6LIBNIO INEAC

453

ee eu SERIRERBEREHSS

## S. 177

1.vı SERVICES

pole yoke and carried to the fields or paddy-fields. A small
wooden dipper was used to pour a quantity ofmanure on each
plant. The bamboo loop would be pulled back and held by the
peasant’s foot when the bucket was nearly empty, so that the
bucket was tilted and could be cleared with the dipper without
handling it. In addition, manure from animals was always
scraped and collected from the roads by boys who used a flatbladed hoe to fill pairs oflarge, scoop-shaped, baskets, similar
in form to winnowing baskets. This would be added to the mix
for fertilizer, which was sometimes poured into trenches to be
mixed and kept until it was applied after the first green shoots
had appeared. The age-old and environmentally compatible
Chinese system of night-soil collection and use continues in
the rural provinces. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.3.k Outhouses (Maine)

Outhouses were built to provide a place for human
waste. These might be located a short distance from the house,
preferably on a well-ventilated site and to the leeward of the
prevailingwind, ormightbeinashed attached to thehouse. An
outhouse usually consisted ofa small building, approximately
1.2-1.4 m (4-5 ft) square in area, with a pitched roof. The door
was usually located in the gable-end with asmall window in the
opposite gable providing light. A box frame, without a base,
would be built against the rear wall and have one, two and
sometimes more holes, cut in the plank top, on which to sit.
Sometimes there was a smaller box at a lower level with a hole
in it for a child to use. A lid would be placed over each hole
when the outhouse was not in use.

The outhouse would be built over a hole dug in the ground for
human waste to fall into; itcould be moved over a newlydughole
when the firstone was filled. Alternatively, atimber box would be
put under the holes to be emptied and replaced when necessary.
Lime was sprinkled over the waste to help it break down more
quicklyand this also helped reduce the smell. An outhouse might
be attached to a building on the edge ofthe shore or on a dock
and the human waste would either fall directly into the sea or on
the rock, shingle or sand to be washed away by the tide. Sometimes itwould only be washed away ata very high tide.

Many houses on the islands still do not have plumbing and
outhouses are used. They are no longer permitted along the
shore and ones on the land may only berebuilt on existingsites.
New houses are required to have composting toilets which can
be placed within the house.

454

Kenasıo

Outhouses were constructed of timber, with the framing

members 5 x 10 cm (2 x 4 in) or larger and the spacing at
approximately 40 cm (16 in) centres or more. The frame was
covered in horizontal boarding, which in turn could be covered
in shingles. The roof would be covered in boarding and shingles and pitched between 35° and 45° to allow therain to run off
quickly. Occasionally the roof would be pitched in one direction only, away from the entrance. Often the building would be
left to weather naturally, rather than bepainted. DEB MAacy
1.V1.3.1 Privy (British Isles)
Derived from the French prive and the Latin privatus
meaning ‘privy’ or ‘private’, the word ‘privy’ has been applied to
domestic latrines for atleasteight centuries. The privy is customarilya small wooden shed or stone building containing a latrine
with a wooden seat, and often, with a dry system of disposal of
excrement. Frequently such privies were situated over deep pits
or middens, sometimes in pairs side-by-side or back-to-back. In
the late-gth century immense numbers of such pits were left
undrained and remained unemptied in spite ofthe noxious smell
and, by this date, known risks of disease.

Many rural privies were casually maintained, though they
might be plastered inside and annually whitewashed. Beside
the latrine a box of earth and a shovel would be placed for the
user to cover the faeces and thus eliminate noxious odours and
infestation by flies. To be effective the earth had to bessifted and
italso had to be dry; the earth was sometimes dried in the sun

d
An Outhougg, 1 BEehe
ShIngIe elag je land

2.1.4 China, South,
Reference
Hommel, Rudojfp,ıg:

3.v1.3.m Maine
‚An outhouse
short distance owingthe
house, Maine, Ngofthe
states. dlavatory
(emen.
Tan
‚1984
1954
Ny,1986
MG.,1915
Kilama, Wen

© Pauloliver 1974

when this was possible, or in a tray on the kitchen hearth
(Pudney).

Ashes from domestic fires, which were used for many purposes including the making of Iye soap and the hardening of
mud-plaster, were also used to cover offensive matter. One
innovation was a side opening with a flap, which enabled the
ashes to be projected on to a chute. A screen beneath was activated by pressure on the seat permitting fine ash to fall through
and larger particles to be saved for possible re-use on a fire. Ash
though, was totally sterile and inhibiting nitrification, could
not be mixed with faeces to make manure. Charcoal from
domestic fires was also used, a dry charcoal made from seaweed reputedly having an exceptionally high absorption rate.
Other materials such as sawdust, mortar, lime or sand were
also placed in buckets for use in privies, the latter having the
added advantage during World War II that it could be used to
extinguish incendiary bombs.

In Britain the Public Health Acts of 1848 and 1874 were
designed to combat the intolerable conditions in urban areas
throughout the country, where back-to-back housing, stinking
cesspits and totally inadequate treatment of excrementitious
material promoted infestation and disease. Drainage and
sewage disposal systems, and minimum standards in planning
and servieing brought improved living standards (Quiney). A
report at the outbreak of World War I on rural housing conditions noted that ‘the utterly detestable privy-midden is still the
commonest receptacle for excreta’. Ofgo houses in the village
of Port Isaac (Cornwall) in 1912, however, 64 were ‘without
closet accommodation of any kind.’ Privy-middens were only
slowly being replaced with the ‘far preferable’ bucket (pail)
closets, the helmet-type coal-scuttle being recommended for
this purpose (Savage).

Though it was first invented in the 16th century, the use ofthe
flushing ‘water closet’ was denied to millions of domestic
houses until the mid-2oth century when, in rural areas within
fifty miles ofLondon, privies still continued to be in use (Hartis). At the close of the 2oth century almost all houses in the
British Isles whether, elite, popular or vernacular, had flushing
‘toilet’ systems. But by this time the vast quantities of water
used for this purpose, the poor air near sewage disposal plants
and the discharge of effluent into rivers and the sea, made the
composting toilet-a sophisticated privy-an attractive alternative for many environmentalists.

PAUL OLIVER

Sanitation and Hygiene 1.1.3
1.V1.3.m Sanitary system (Yemen)

One of the remarkable characteristics of the old
houses ofSan’änoted by al-Hamdani in the gth century AD, was
the cleanliness and freedom from odours of the bathrooms,
which contained bathing facilities and lavatories. By then the
vernacular architecture was well established, and bathrooms of
the same type are still in use today, not only in San’a but in the
whole central plateau, and with small variations throughout
Yemen.

Large traditional houses have bathrooms on more than one
ofthe upper floors. They are usually superimposed, with a vertical waste shaftwhich passes through the lower bathrooms and
grows larger atits descends. This is builtas a plain stoneboxon
each level, plastered so that from the inside it appears to be part
ofthe walling ofthe house. The waste liquid passes through the
walls to a vertical draining surface on the outside, which is usually shared by all the bathrooms. The walls of the bathrooms
are finished with hard, smooth, gypsum plaster which is easyto
wash. The floors are of stone with waterproof plaster joints.

The bathing facilities consist ofa pair ofsquare stones; spaced
approximately r2 cm (5 in) apart, on which the user squats, and,
centrally in frontofthem, acylindrical stone which has arecessed
top to receive a container of water. The container is usually an
open-necked earthenware pot about 20 cm (8 in) in diameter,
without handles. Often a much larger earthenware vessel for
water is placed in one corner ofthe bathroom, or in the lobby
outside, from which the bather’s supply can be replenished.

The lavatory is a stone platform 70 cm (27 in) square with a
square hole in the centre. The latter opens into a vertical shaft,
which drops down into a square stone receptacle underneath
designed for collecting the solid waste. In front ofthe lavatory
is a sloping section in the stone platform, which leads urine
down to a channel in the stone floor. The channel takes the liguid through an opening in the outside wall, whence it runs
down the face of a specially constructed and formed vertical
draining surface made of waterproof gypsum plaster. Frequently these vertical drains are elegantly shaped and decorated. When itreaches the ground, the liquid disappears into an
underground drainage sump, from which it is led either to a
‘French’ drain, for disposal into the soil, orto a cesspit.

Odours are completely eliminated from the lavatory in two
ways. First, by ensuring that liquids are not allowed into the
“long-drop’ shaft or the chamber beneath, the solid waste is
allowed to dıy very quickly (San’a has a low humidity) and
becomes odourless. Secondly, the surfaces over which urine
has run are swilled down with water after every use ofthe lavatory. Later the dried night-soil is shovelled away and burnt as
fuel in the public baths ofeach quarter, the ash serving to fertilize the market gardens around which the houses are grouped.
Thus an ecological cycle exists that is remarkable for its simplicity, hygiene and economy. RONALD LEWCOCK
1.V1.3.n Sauna (Finland)

The sauna has been an essential part oftthe Finnish
folk tradition for thousands of years. It is nowadays internationally generally recognized that Finland is the only European
country in which a bathhouse culture has evolved. In 1994 there

455

## S. 178

1.V1 SERVICES

were about 1.6 million saunas, which makes one for every three
people, and illustrates just how importanta part ofeveryday life
the sauna is to the Finns.

The Finnish word sauna has common roots with the old Sami
word in the ancient sakna and suodnje. In Finland the sauna is
also referred to by the words pirtti and kyly. It has been claimed
on etymological grounds that the sauna originally took the
form ofa depression excavated in the ground, which was covered for bathing purposes. At the bottom of the depression
stones were stacked to form a bathhouse stove for the production ofsteam.

The principal function of the sauna has always been for
sweatbathing. Many customs, rites and practices have become
associated with it, whose kernel has always been the high
regard in which the sauna is held. The sauna is compared in
many proverbs to a church; thus a peaceful and even a devotional attitude has been expected ofbathers. Noisy behaviour in
the sauna has been considered unfitting, as has nocturnal
bathing, since this would offend the guardian spirit of the
sauna. When leaving the sauna it was proper to thank this
spirit. The sauna was also a place for the curing ofailments and
ofinfertility. Remedies and incantations were used, and these
form an essential part of Finnish folk poetry.

The use ofwood as a building material, since at the latest AD
800, altered the construction ofthe sauna, so thatit began to be
built partially or wholly above ground level. In wilderness and
areas ofswidden cultivation the saunas were small and built for
bathing only, whereas in the open field agricultural areas of
western Finland they were often large, and many other household uses became common. Saunas were used, for example, in
making the malt liquors required for distilling spirits and
brewing beer, for drying grain, for childbirth, for healing (e.g.
cupping), and also for the smoking of meats.

The true smoke sauna has no chimney. Only after the woodfire has been allowed to die down, and the glowing embers and
smoke have largely disappeared, may bathing commence. Only
much later were saunas built with chimneys, which allow the
passage ofsmoke and the presence ofbathers simultaneously in
the sauna. The earliest smoke saunas were generally log-built
and square on plan. The floor was of bare earth or planks, and
theroofwould be tree bark, straw, turfsods or wooden shingles.
Different types of sauna are distinguished mainly by their size
and thearrangementofthe interior. Atypical sauna is 8-30sq m

(86-320 sq fi), with most being 9-16 sq m. Ceiling heights vary
from 1.8 m (6 ft) to 3.6 m (12 ft).

During the ancient period ofsmoke saunas, which lasted up
to the end of the ıgth century, innumerable local sauna typevariants developed. Ofthese, perhaps 2000-3000 examples are
still preserved in usable condition in museums and in private
ownership. A revolutionary change occurred at the beginning
ofthe 20th century, when the sauna stove began to be equipped
with a shaftto carry off gas and smoke. Metals began to be used
forthemaking ofstoves, which made possible the utilization of
several new inventions in their construction.

In addition to saunas normally built for one-family dwellings,
from the ıgth century up until quite recently, public saunas were
built for the convenience of city dwellers. This tradition has

456

Anold Sauna
in
central Finland pi
