declined rapidly with the spread of both individual and communal saunas in apartment blocks. Atthe same time the saunas
provided in swimming halls and sports centres have compensated for the lack of public sauna facilities.

In modern Finland the sauna remains a vital social institution, closely associated with many family and other social
events. A visit to the sauna is an essential part of many a social
evening, by which a relaxing and refreshing disposition is
achieved after the rush and bother of workaday life.

A suitable bathing temperature is about 80-90 °C (176-194
°F). Humidity and temperature may be controlled during
bathing by throwing water onto the hot stones on top of the
stove to create steam (löyly, the old meaning of which is the
soul or spirit of the sauna). A switch of birch twigs (vihta or
vasta) is traditionally used, the significance of which is associated with the expulsion of evil from the person, by means of
beating on the bare skin.

Wood is much used as a traditional material for sauna construction. The bathers sit on raised wooden benches or platforms, in an opposite corner and somewhat above the stove. A
wood-heated boiler may be used for heating water for ablutions. Water tubs, a ladle, awashing bench and linen cloths are
essential accessories. Today’s operationally designed sauna
usually has a separate dressing room and a washing room,
besides the steamroom itself.

Many ofthe mostvalued characteristics ofthe Finnish sauna
and its associated bathing ritual have been forgotten, particularly abroad. There is now, however, a renewed interest in the
meaning ofthe sauna tradition, and a desire to apply it in the

building ofnew saunas. RISTO VUOLLE-APIALA

TR. GEORGE WOOLSTON

1.V1.3.n-i Bathhouse: pirts (Latvia)
The bathhouse is the earliest known building ofthe
Latvians, changing little over the centuries. The oldestexamples
are huts made of logs with warm, fireproof turf roofs under
which a boulder stove stood in the centre and several benches or
sweating shelves along the walls. The method of heating the
water was simple-white-hot boulders were dropped into basins
or bartels full ofwater. The heated stove-boulders retained their
heat for a long time and turned the water poured on them into
steam. The bathers lay down on benches and started perspiring,
a process they intensified by lashing themselves with leafed
birch twigs. Childbirth and attendance of the dead as well as

Sanitation and Hygiene 1.1.3

meat-smoking and malt-drying for ale were all undertaken in
the bathhouse. The roof of the one-rtoom bathhouse was later
extended over the front end to form an open porch sometimes
supported by posts. This porch was gradually converted into a
vestibule-changing room by being closed in with walls. ‘This
type of bathhouse was used by the Baltic peoples, the Finno-
Ugrians (the sauna of the Finns) and the ancient Slavs. It
expanded in a western direction in later times, also reaching
Scandinavia’ (Kundzins). JÄNIS LEJNIEKS
1.V1.3.0 Sweatlodge (Alaska)

Native American sweatlodges in the western Arctic
and sub-Arctic regions of North America are small, low structures used primarily for bathing, ritual purification, prayer,
socializing, storytelling and healing. Types vary from semisubterranean structures, excavated up to I-1.2 m (3-4 ft) into
the ground and attached to a house-group dwelling as a small
ante-room, to a small dome-shaped wood framework covered
with animal skins or brush, located near village houses. Two
methods of bathing were used in the Arctic and sub-Arctic
regions: the dry heat sweatbath typical of southwest Alaskan
Eskimos, and the vapour steambath, in which stones heated in
afire outside are carried inside where various barks and grasses
are placed on them. A hole in the earth holds the heated stones
which are sprinkled with water to produce steam. Detached
sweatlodges are in use today.

Vapour and sweat baths vary among Arctic and sub-Arctic
Native American tribes. While the traditional esteemed form of
bathing ofthe Aleuts (ofthe Aleutian islands) was in the sea or
cold water, the dry heat sweatbath was acquired from Russian
and Siberian influence (Damas). Their post and log semi-subterranean dwellings (barabara) covered with skins, grass and
sod with mat floors and roofentry were modified to a more typical western house including a wall entry, plank floors, window
and stove.

The Ahtna tribe of south-central Alaska built sweatlodges,
attached to a large multi-family rectangular house of vertical
plank walls and gable or hipped roofs insulated with moss or
heavy spruce bark. Hot rocks were passed through a circular
door in the back wall into the low rectangular steambath with a
flat roof and removable bear-gut skylight. Men and older
women bathed in the steambath; unmarried youths, men or
young girls might sleep there during the winter (Helm).

The Tanaina, also of south-central Alaska (extending from the
Kenai peninsula north to Talkeetna) historically lived in semisubterranean dwellings (nitit) consisting ofan entry tunnel intoa
main room, 6 m (20 ft) square with attached ante-rooms about
3.7 m (12 ft) square, the steambath among them. It is possible
that these steambaths became detached from the house in the
late-ıgth century following Russian contact (Helm).

The Athapaskan language group tribes ofthe Alaskan plateau,
including the Ingalik, Yukon Koyukon, and Koyukuk Koyukon,
constructed sweatlodges separate from their semi-subterranean
winter house, which varied from a dome-shaped circular pole
framework covered with animal skins, moss or dirt, toapoleand
log house. Stones were heated inside a separate small skincovered bentwood dome-shaped enclosure. The steambath in

457

## S. 179

g
&
5
°

1.v1 SERVICES

this region, according to Hosley (Helm), served primarily as a
pleasurable and relaxing social activity used year-round, providing relief from mosquitoes and black flies in summer and from
the extreme low humidity ofsevere winters.

The Bering Strait Eskimos held sweatbaths in their kashims,
large versions of their family dwellings, which contained a
ground-level passage in summer and an underground passage
in winter. Respirators of grass or wood shavings were worn
across the mouth to protect a man’s throat and lungs from the
heat; a birdskin cap was also worn.

The sweatlodge, one ofthe mostwidespread traditions across
Native North America, held sacred rituals and symbolic meaning. Bruchac notes that for some tribes the poles represented
the four directions, the covering, like a night sky, was considered a womb ofa great animal, such as bear. Once a lodge is
covered it becomes a living being. Pacific Northwest nations
considered the sweatlodge the body of the great spirit chief
transformed to help humans (Bruchac).

Arrangements of elements within the sweatlodge symbolize
the ritual within it: the glow ofthe heated stones represents the
return to the first dawn of creation. Considered to be alive, the
igneous stones are universally treated with respect and spoken
to asonewouldtoaan elder. The sweatlodge is a manifestation of
a holistic view oflife and healing whereby the sweat experience,
a symbol of death and rebirth, is a means to the restoration of
life itself. TARA MICHELE CAHN
1.V1.3.p Vectors and parasites
Any building is occupied by thousands, even tens of
thousands of creatures, of whom many live by consuming the
grain and other stores of the human residents, or eating the
crumbs and particles of food left after meals. Others live as parasites on human skin and hair, or on the hides of animals who
share the same living space. Or they live on faeces-ofwhichall
also leave their own. Western homes are frequently subjected to
onslaughts with insecticides, germicides and other preparations intended, at a price, to destroy bacteria and render the
domestic environment harmless. With less access to such
remedies designed to destroy pests, and little education on
hygiene, the risks of infection and epidemics can be greater in
countries where harmful insects or rodents are endemic.

458

Vernacular buildings in all parts of the world are prone to
harbour parasites. Though the levels and methods of housecleaning differ widely, many ofthese are virtually impossible to
eradicate with conventional means. In some cases the discomfortcaused by multiplying cockroaches, beetles or mosquitoes,
can cause whole villages to moveand rebuild, as is the case with
the Yanoama and other Amazonian peoples. The problems are
not simply those of the tropics; many infestations occur in all
parts ofthe world, and are easily introduced by way ofimported
goods and clothing.

Among the most preyalent are fleas, of which the flea ofthe
plague, or black, rat transmitted the bubonic plague which
wroughtsuch havoc in ı4th- and 17th-century Europe. Fleas are
borne by domesticated animals but they are also carried by
birds. Flea larvae mature in humid conditions and flourish in
the nests of birds that live in earth houses. Nests are also subjectto infestations oflice. Human body lice are frequently associated with famine, which weakens resistance to typhus of
which the body louse is a vector.

Of other household parasites one of the most prevalent is
cemex lectularius, the flat, bloodsucking bed bug, believed to beof
Mediterranean origin and a parasite of bats which adapted to
human hosts. It inhabits cracks in the wall, furniture and bedframes, its bite painful and the smell offensive. For those who
can afford it, painting the bed frame with turpentine helps
reduce the infestation. Flies convey disease infections through
settling on household food. They feed particularly on horse
manure, which formerly was party combated by covering the
manure with cow dung, though the menace was not reduced
until the decline in the use of horses. Limewashing the house
both inside and out are widely considered to be a defence
against flies and mosquitoes, as are the application of coats of
cattle dung on walls and floor, hardened and polished.

Ofthe common insects mosquitoes are the most pernicious,

Sweatlodges
widely usedp,
native American, .
eultures, eg,
Idaho,

References for1,
Bruchac, Josep|
Damas, David ((
Helm, June (ed,
Shuttles, Wayne

Highriskof (am Gunn, S.,
contamination from
animals, and from dung
cakes onsale

water container,
Pradesh, India,

‚Arthur, 1967

13.p-i

ya Quechua
ehorı.v.3.p-i

John,1991

the different species of anopheles being the vectors of malaria.
The pupae grow in stagnant water, but the adult mosquitoes
may winter in cellars and lofts. Immense clouds of mosquitoes
are seasonally encountered in the great forest regions of the
north, but the principal habitat is in the tropies where malaria
remains a serious threat. Mosquitoes deeply infected whole
populations which were also weakened by other water-borne
diseases, such as bilharzia, spread by continuous irrigation,
and cholera, a bacterial disease spread by excrement in drinking water.

Among the mosttragic of diseases is trypanosomia, the ‘river
blindness’ transmitted by the simulium fly (also known as the
‘black fly’ or ‘buffalo gnat’) whose lifecycle was unknown until
the 1960s. It was clearly related to water, and villages were
located as much as 3 km (2 mi) away from the Volta river to
avoid the persistent bite of the fly, so that women had to carıy
water on their heads for this distance, until it could be eradicated. In the 1990 enteric and water-borne diseases are still
rife in developing countries, among them schistosomiasis, of
which the vector is Bulinus truncatus, a snail which lives in
irrigation ditches and shallow stream beds.

While some health hazards are associated with the dwelling
and practices in its use, others are transmitted by contaminated
water and the disposal ofanimal and human wastes. But infestation ofdwellings and diseases among their occupants are not
eradicated by pure water supply or better sanitation facilities,
though these greatly improve the expectation oflife. Dwellings
are almost universally vulnerable to rodents, which are known
to have been present in human habitations in neolithic times.
Mushroom-shaped staddle stones and ratguards of rings of
spikes are widely used to protect granaries from their attacks.
Buttree-rats live in roof-ridges, and all undisturbed corners are
prone to infestation by mice. Snakes are found in the roof
rafters in some places, poisonous spiders in others; wasps and
hornets nest in walls, lines of cockroaches advance over them.
While grain weevils attack the granaries, termites eat away
timber supports.

Hygiene, cleanliness, and keeping the building fabric in
good repair do much to reduce the risks to which the inhabitants of millions ofvernacular buildings are prone. Millions of
dwellings are kept scrupulously clean within the knowledge

Sanitation and Hygiene 1.v1.3

and means of their occupiers. Debilitating diseases will not be
eradicated by demolishing homes. It is improved infrastructure
that is required, not the total replacement of vernacular architecture with mass housing, nor of the materials of which they
are constructed, with steel and concrete. Access to pure water
supply, effective sanitation methods, natural pest control systems, immunization against prevalent diseases, education in
personal hygiene and instruction in domestic maintenance
may be an expensive programme, but it would constitute an
economic investment in the future that would not rob cultures
oftheir architecture or their skills to buildit. pauL OLIVER

1.V1.3.p-i Chagas’ disease

Chagas’ disease is a form of trypanosomiasis found
in the rural areas ofLatin America and is transmitted by bloodsucking triatomid bugs, known as vinchucas or chinches, which
live in poor quality housing.

In its chronic phase, Chagas’ disease is incurable and can
result in irreparable damage to the tissues of the heart, brain
and intestines, leading to eventual death from heart failure,
brain damage or intestinal blockage. The trypanosome responsible for the disease (Trypanosoma cruzi) is transmitted in the faeces ofinfected triatomids; the bugs defecate on the host’s skin
when they have finished feedingand, as the bite is irritating the
victim may scratch allowing the trypanosome to enter the
bloodstream.

Triatomids are found in much of South and Central America,
especially in the Andean valleys of Peru, Argentina and Bolivia,
where the disease is believe to have originated. The insects are
slow-moving and are only found in association with human
habitation; they have no means ofdefence other than their ability to hide and they feed at night, descending from their hiding
places in search ofa host. They are mainly found in houses with
thatched roofs and unplastered adobe walls - especially as in
many parts of the Andes adobes are laid without any mortar
being placed in the vertical joints. They are often found in association with domestic animals, such as hens, guinea pigs and
dogs, on which they also feed.

Chagas’ disease can only be effectively controlled through
eradication ofthe vectors. This requires that the population be
both aware of the danger posed by the vectors and willing to
take the necessary measures to improve standards of hygiene
and housing. Spraying with insecticide is another alternative,
but the pesticides have to be residual in their effects, as the
triatomids hide in inaccessible corners; they should also be of
low toxicity to humans. The most suitable products are
pyrethroids, but they are expensive for use in mass campaigns.

The main efforts to improve housing should include plastering the walls, especially the inside walls ofthe rooms in which
people sleep, and measures to improve the roofing: by replacing thatch with tiles, plastering the underside ofthe thatch, or
constructing a false ceiling between the roof and the sleeping
quarters, using cloth or plastered canes to prevent the bugs
descending from the roof. Additional measures should include
the relocation of hen coops and corrals at a greater distance
from the house, the improvement of ventilation and regular

cleaning ofthe rooms used for sleeping. J. C. RENSHAW

459

## S. 180

ına Ventilation and Cooling

ıvıa-Aa Cooling

Thecoolingeffectofairmovementcan be considered
to work in two ways. The first is the encouragement of cooling
by evaporation. The other is the replacement of warm air with
cooler air, thus lowering the ambient temperature.

The provision of air movement is an important means of
cooling the body. Under normal conditions of comfort, heat
loss from the body is by convection (30 per cent), radiation (45
per cent) and evaporation (25 per cent). Both convection and
evaporation are increased by air movement. When the body is
hot, perspiration followed by the evaporation of moisture from
the surface ofthe skin results in a heat exchange process which
cools the body. With low relative humidity typical of hot dry climates, dry air absorbs moisture easily and evaporation takes
place quickly. In hot humid conditions where theair already has
a high moisture content, perspiration is less easily absorbed
into the air, and skin surface cooling slows down, or, ifthe air
becomes saturated, ceases to take place; discomfort increases.
Thus, under hot humid conditions air movement is essential to
replace saturated air near the skin’s surface, by air which has a
lower level of humidity and allows more evaporative cooling.
As velocity increases, so too does the evaporative capacity and
the rate of cooling. Significant cooling occurs at air movement
speeds between o.2m (0.7 ft) and 2 m (6.5 ft) persecond, buttoo
rapid air movement can also be irritating. In very cold climates,
the same evaporation effect is called the chill factor, and results
in perceived temperatures far below the temperature recorded
byadryairthermometer.

Natural cooling

Exposing the body to breezes is thus one way to achieve cooling, and many houses in warm climates have been designed to
receive the benefitofprevailingwinds during hot seasons, asin
the example of the mezzanine level sleeping terrace (talar)
found in the Gilan province houses ofnorthern Iran, or the covered platforms for eating and sleeping which are an element of
nearly all Laotian houses on the Vientiane plain. In temperate
conditions cooling is achieved naturally by opening doors and
windows provided thatthe outside temperatures are cooler, but
in humid conditions any ventilation helps, and to achieve this
whole walls are often builtas delicate screens through which air
can pass: the date palm-frond screened walls ofthe Al Bätinah
coast houses in Oman allow cross-ventilation and benefit from
sea breezes, whilstprovidingvisual privacy and control ofglare.
The intricate wooden mashrabiyya of Egypt perform a similar
function; screened windows are still manufactured in Timbuktu
(in Mali) and Marrakech (in Morocco). In humid regions openings are often low level so thattthe breeze crosses the bodies of

460

the occupants and encourages evaporative cooling. Ceiling fans
are also used throughout warm countries to provide air movement in rooms.

Induced natural cooling

Cooling can also be induced because hot air rises and cold air
sinks. In many old Middle Eastern houses, the deep central
courtyard, surrounded by rooms and the thermal mass ofthick
walls, acts like a pit into which cool night-time air sinks and
cools down the rooms around the courtyard. In turn, warm air
that builds up during the day in the courtyard rises out of the
courtyard as night-time air temperatures drop. The same use of
pressure differences between cool and warm air are used in
twin courtyard houses, ofwhich fine examples existin Cairo: in
the sunny large courtyard hotair rises, to be replaced by cooler
air drawn from the adjacent shaded small courtyard. In turn
cooler air from high up above the building is drawn down into
the small courtyard. A covered seating area or iwan in-between

etal.,1976
bergen, 0.H..etal.,
41980
Screened wind chers in Sind,

north India. in.
(see also fig. 114.

Oo wiles Danby

the two courtyards benefits from this breeze. Similar induced
cooling occurs in the streets ofOld Cairo, where there is ameeting between narrow shady east-west aligned alleys, and the
sunlit broader north-south main streets. In Egyptian houses
porous water jars, maziara, have been placed to exploit this air
movement: water evaporates from the surface of the jar, and
cools both the air and the water in thejar. Thejars can also filter
water. In Oman similar jars have been mounted in specially
constructed windows to cool air and water. Cooling has traditionally been achieved in India by wetting mats. In many countries water in pools and fountains is used as a cooling element
combined with a cross-ventilating arrangement of openings.
The wind-catcher (or wind tower) is another cooling system
used in houses in Cairo, in the old quarters of the Gulf State
towns, on the north Al Bätinah coast of Oman, in desert towns
ofIran and Iraq, and in the Sindh region of Pakistan. The tower
projects above rooflevel to catch cooler, and usually less dusty,
high-level air, which is than channelled down into rooms

Ventilation and Cooling 1.V1.4
below. Those in Cairo and Baghdad are quite small structures,
butbecause temperature decreases with altitude, higher towers
can draw in cooler air. In the desert towns of Iran, many ofthe
towers rise high above the roof level. Some wind-catchers are
oriented to receive cool breezes only from a single direction, as
in the malgaf of Cairo which has an opening only on one side,
and in the wind-catchers of the Sindh which are oriented
towards the sea. Those of Iran, Oman and Dubai are multidirectional, with openings on two or four sides, and with several
vertical shafts. The wind-catchers on the Al Bätinah coast are
often simply made of’poles and sacking, and they can be taken
down in winter when cooling is unnecessary. The wind-catchers in the Bastakia quarter of Dubai, and those in Irani towns
like Yazd are often elaborately decorated. In all cases there is an
outlet vent, such as a skylight, as well as the inlettower - in the
cloth wind-catchers ofOman air actually comes down one shaft
ofthe tower and goes back up the other. This effect is encouraged by suction caused by air blowing over the outlet. The outlet
vents can be domes, and delicate plaster or wood lattice-work is
often used for the screens through which air escapes. Air movement can be generated by hot air leaving through the escape
vent, creatinga vacuum in the room below which in turns sucks
in cool air down the tower— butifthe air outside is warmer than
that in the room, air movement does not take place, and thus
the coolest air stays in the room. Recent scientific appraisal of
these paradoxically often very sophisticated traditional cooling
systems has led to a renewal of interest in their use for cooling
in new buildings. JOHN NORTON

ıvı4-B Ventilation

Ventilation is the exchange ofair between the inside ofa building and the outside. For natural ventilation, the flow of air is
caused by the pressure ofthe wind or by the buoyancy effect of
temperature differences between inside and outside. Airflow
takes place through openings in the building envelope ranging
from small cracks to large purpose-made openings.

The purpose of ventilation is to maintain air quality for the
occupants by replacing used oxygen, removing exhaled carbon
dioxide, water vapours, odours and other pollutants. Ventilation
is usually quantified in air changes per hour (ac/h), the volume
ofair changed each hour in units ofthe whole building volume.
Typical values for the purposes above would be between 0.5 and
5 ac/h and is driven by thermal buoyancy and/or wind pressure.

Inacold climate ventilation will create an unwanted heatloss
which would logically be minimized by reducing ventilation to
a minimum. However, as ventilation is reduced, the concentration of pollutants increases and so a compromise has to be
reached. Ifthe rate of pollution is very high, such as in the case
ofan unflued fire, ventilation rates have to be high. This results
in large heat losses and indoor air temperatures only slightly
above outdoor temperatures. An example of a medieval farmhouse in southeast England has smoke vented through large
unglazed windows (or ‘wind eyes’). Field studies showed that

461

## S. 181

1.vl SERVICES

typical air temperatures were only one or two degrees above
ambient, and that comfort was largely provided by radiant heat
from the fire (Sinha).

It is interesting to note that the use of high ventilation rates
obscured the benefit of thermal insulation, since temperature
differences between inside and outside were typically small.

In very cold climates, high ventilation rates would be unacceptable. Inuit snow houses are very airtight and controllable;
ventilation is provided by a small opening in the apex of the
dome. This low rate of ventilation is possible, due to the relatively low pollution rate of heating, provided by burning seal
oil, and by human body heat. However, Mathiasson reports that
his experience of air quality in these houses was that it left
much to be desired.

Inveryhotclimates, thermal coupling due to ventilation would
cause unwanted heat gains. The response to this is again to curtailventilation, particularly at, and shortly after noon, when ambientaair temperatures are likely to be well above comfort level.

Physics ofventilation

Air moves through a structure in response to pressure differences generated byeitherthe thermal buoyancy (stack effect) or
wind.

Buoyancy pressures are generated by air warmer than its surroundings as the warmer air is oflower density than the cooler
air. The pressure generated is dependent upon the average temperature difference between inside and outside and the height
of the ‘stack’ or column of warmer air. It is not dependent on
temperature gradientas is often mistakenly thought. Ifthere are
openings at the top and bottom of'the stack, the cooler heavier
airwillenter the lower openings and displace the warmerlighter
airatthe top.

This is known as ‘displacement ventilation’, and ifthere is a
source of heat which continues to warm up the indoor ait, the
flow will continue. It is important to note that in these conditions air temperatures low down will be close to outdoor temperatures and those higher up will be warmer. This often has
important benefits to comfort conditions in the occupied region,
when ventilation is for the purpose of providing cooling. It is
often to be found in animal housing where the high density of
the animals demands high ventilation rates.

In the case where an opening is made only in the top, warm
air will initially flow out under buoyancy pressure, resulting in
a drop of pressure in the building. This will then cause cool air
to flow in via the same opening to replace the lost warm air. The
two-way flow ofair through the single opening, creates mixing
and prevents a temperature gradient. This mode is referred to
as mixing ventilation, and is appropriate for ventilation in cold
conditions. In this case the chilling effect ofthe cold air is mod-

©
[0)

© Nicholas Baker

462

© Nicholas Baker

erated by the mixing with the warmer air. An extreme example
can be found in the houses of Ladakh in Pakistan. Here, in the
very cold winter,a hole in theroof provides ventilation and smoke
venting. However the mixing action distributes the smoke
around the house with detrimental effect to health.

A pressure is generated on a surface whenever moving air is
obstructed or deflected. The distribution of pressure is dependent upon the wind direction and the geometry ofthe surfaces.
Pressures will generally be positive on the windward sides of
buildings and on windward steep-pitch roofs, and negative on
leeward sides and flatand low-pitch roofs.

The lateral pressure distribution gives rise to cross-ventilation;
that is airflow from the windward to the leeward side of the
building. This requires that the interior of the building is not
sealed by dividing walls, or thatwhere rooms are double banked,
openings at high level are provided. For very open structures,
the airflow can be considered as a continuation ofthe external
wind, reduced by a friction factor due to the construction and
obstruction ofthe building. Buildings in the warm humid tropics
are often well adapted to maximizing wind-induced ventilation.

The negative pressures generated on roofs and particularlyat
ridges, offer opportunities for ventilation by extracting air via

„chimneys, flues or ventilators located in the roof or ridge. The

advantage of this configuration is that the airflow induced by
wind pressure will supplement airflow induced thermal buoyancy (stack effect). This is obviously important where flues are
being used to ventsmoke, but also may be advantageous when
stacks or flues are used to provide ventilation cooling. Positive
pressures may also be generated by intercepting the airflow
with wind-scoops (badgirs or malgafs), as found in the traditional architecture ofthe Middle East. NICHOLAS BAKER
1.Vl.4.a Air funnel (India, s)

Southern peninsular India (latitudes 8 °N to ı8 °N),
comprising the states of Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Karnataka and
Andhra Pradesh, is generally hot (30-40 °C [86-104 °F]) with
the mainland being dry and coastal regions humid. Traditional
ventilation techniques in this region vary from those in the
densely arranged urban settlements to those in the loosely
packed rural developments. The former settlements consist of
dwellings of different strata of the society grouped around a
focal entity, usuallyatemple.Traditionallytheir design and construction were based on the tenets laid out in the Vastu Shastra
according to which all forms ofenergy thatexist in the universe
are interlinked and a good design is one which ensures a consonant coexistence of these forces. Hence good ventilation
would result from a sound interdisciplinary strategy which

Mathiasson, 1928

deceleration 0

Vidyä
‚South and Sri

‚Oo stiram Ganapath

funnel

integrates these energy forms. The guiding principle was to
effect cross-ventilation through a series of openings from the
entrance door, through a central courtyard and out ofan opening at the rear. This is the ‘air funnel’ (vamsamoolam, ‘lifeline’)
of the house. The entrance lobby (rezhi) acts as a wind tunnel
focusing the incident wind into the courtyard that lies on this
air funnel which in turn ventilates the living areas grouped
around it.

Narrow streets with houses on either side minimize direct
insolation on the walls as they shade one another. While deep
overhangs cut off most of the sun on the walls, the entrance
veranda further protects the interior spaces. In general, walls
are made thick to reduce heat conduction. The central courtyard (anganam or muttram) ofa typical traditional house is a passive solar device used in both hot humid and hot dıy regions.
Considered the lung ofthe house, it is part oftheair funneland
is the main source ofair exchange from inside the builtspace to
the outer free space. In drier regions, water bodies are placed in
the courtyards to cool and humidify theincomingair. The main
function of verandahs (alindram) is to prevent the direct heat
and glare of the sun from entering the house. The external
verandas are provided with seats and head rests (tinnais) made
of stone which are cool and comfortable places for informal
gatherings. Similarly, the internal walkways adjoining the
courtyards, keep the heat out and provide for air movement to
the interior built spaces. SRIRAM GANAPATHI
1.V1.4.b Awnings and shades
In hot climates methods of protection from exposure to the sun are often improvised or devised on an ad hoc
basis. At their simplest in terms of human effort, but deliberately selected for this purpose, are the ‘shade trees’. Any species
may be chosen which has a broad span of dense branches and
foliage to provide cover; they also cleanse and filter the air.
Many African sayanna compounds are built close to such trees
which actas council or meeting places, and are sometimes used
byelders for the teaching ofchildren. Where shade trees are not
available light frameworks may be constructed, over which
vines are trained to provide dappled shade, as is customary in
the insular Mediterranean.

Passages through Arab towns often meander, reducing the
prolonged exposure of any part to the sun. Additionally,
awnings, or large sheets of coarse cloth may be drawn across
the circulation routes. Branches and straw may also be used,
spread over a light frame erected by agreement between households, to provide shade as needed. Several layers of awnings,
widely separated to allow the passage of air, may be used often

Ventilation and Cooling 1.v1.4

FRUCHT nl 117 A
LiEie 7 Nr, N

to provide a covering to a souk in advance ofa covered bazaar.
A variant ofthe awning was a suspended sheet (punka), used in
India and operated manually, or even with a string tied to the foot
ofa punka wallah, as a slowly moving fan to assist air circulation.

Over some North African lanes, as in Tunis, wooden frames
support wood or fabric awnings to shade open shop fronts,
some being capable of being folded flat against the wall when
the shutters are closed. Light awnings or blinds may be suspended over an open entrance, and frequently in southern
Europe a curtain ofwooden beads threaded on strings will be
used; the latter is easily parted and falls back in place with the
individual weight of the beaded strings. In northern Europe,
including the British isles, where humidity may heighten the
discomfort felt in seasonal summer heat, shops were equipped
with roller awnings. These were mounted in the fascia and
could be drawn over the sidewalk, being held in position by a
cantilevered iron frame; shaped suspended blinds also shaded
the sides. Popular in the first half ofthe 2oth century their use
over shop-fronts declined thereafter, though smaller, domestic
‘roller blinds’ and slatted ‘Venetian blinds’ are used internally

over windows. PAUL OLIVER

463

## S. 182

© Paul Ollver 1967

1.v SERVICES
1V1.4.c Courtyard

The central courtyard oflarge massive houses in hot
dıyclimates isafeaturethathasbeen used for coolingbytrapping
cool night-time air which sinks into the shaded bottom of the
courtyard. Being heavier than the daytime ambienttemperature
ofthe air, this cools surrounding rooms for much ofthe day.

The principle ofusinga central courtyard as a means for keeping adjacent rooms cool is based on the pressure difference
between cool air and warm air. Cool night-time air from high
above the courtyard, heavier than warm air, sinks into the courtyard, and replaces the slightly warmer air that has accumulated
there during the day and which is unable to escape because the
ambient daytime air temperature above the courtyard is warmer.
This effect works at its best in climates where there is a significant range between hot daytime temperatures and cold nights.
Butfor the cool air to then stay in the base oftheyard, the external perimeter walls of surrounding rooms must be built of
materials which provide insulation from outside heat because
oftheir thermal mass, and there should be almost no external
openings; a breeze blowing through the rooms into the courtyard would replace the cool air. This same thermal mass in walls
nextto the courtyard will also conserve the ambient coolness of
the yard and adjacent rooms, but it is the volume of air in the
yard and the rooms which acts as the main ‘reservoir’ of coolness. In order to achieve the cooling effect, it is also essential
that the courtyard be sufficiently deep compared to the height
of surrounding walls to ensure that the base of the courtyard
remains in the shade for most, ifnotall, ofthe day. Ifthe courtyard is either too large, or the walls too low, the cool air ‘sink’
effect will not be achieved.

The success of the cooling principle of courtyards thus
depends on acombination of climate, building shape and wall
materials. Because this rightcombination is notalways achieved,
some researchers have been led to doubt the efficiency of the
cooling principle of courtyards. But the cool deep courtyards
that existed in Salalah, on the southern coast ofOman, and the
same which can still be found in old town houses of Cairo are
witness to the efficiency ofthe courtyard’s cooling effect. Some
of the most extreme examples exist in the tightly clustered
dwellings in the ksar settlements ofthe Dräa valley in Morocco,
where deep narrow courtyard shafts penetrate into the depths
ofthe buildings. JOHN NORTON

1.V1.4.c-i

Courtyard: convection cooling

In hot climates, the sun is the dominant heat source.
Courtyards can decrease solar gain through building aggregation
and through shading. Because courtyard buildings are organized
around central outdoorspaces, they can bejoined attheir external
perimeter, minimizing the overall building area exposed to the
sun. This aggregation can be seen in many urban situations from
Marrakech to Suzhou. The space ofthe courtyard can be shaded,
by overhead canopies such as trellises and arbours, or by design
proportions ofthe courtyard. Courtyards that are taller than their
width will generally exelude sunlightto the ground plane for most
ofthe day. By decreasing solar gain, the courtyard is made comfortable and habitable throughout the day and night (Al-Azzawi).

Hot, arid climates are characterized by large temperature
differences between day and night. Massive building materials
such as adobe, mud or stone have large heat capacities. When
used to build the walls and roofs ofthe spaces surrounding the
courtyard, they provide thermal lag: the materials absorb heat
during day and radiate heat to the cooler sky at night, diminishing the overall diurnal temperature swing and providing cooling during the day (Dunham).

In courtyards ofsubstantial area without shading, a strategy of
air movement by means of convection can be employed. When
the sun strikes the courtyard floor, the ground becomes a heat
source thatwarms the lowest layer ofair. The warmed air rises to
bereplaced by coolerair from surrounding spaces. This constant
replacement of warmed air sets up a breeze, which affects
human comfort through the evaporation of perspiration. When
thermal convection is reinforced with massive materials, alarger
contrast in temperature is established between the warm and
cool air, generating stronger breezes. In the evening, the warm
air ofthe courtyard rises and is replaced by the cooled night air
which cools the surrounding rooms. The courtyard serves as
point ofexchange for rising hotair and sinking cool air. In using
convection cooling, the courtyard itselfis uninhabitable during
day; a porch or shaded intermediate space needs to be added
between the courtyard and surrounding rooms in order to take
advantage ofthe air movement, or multiple courtyards must be
arranged thatalternate shading and sun (Fathy).

1.V1.4.d Grilles and vents (England)

Ventilation in English houses was provided for food.

storage and preparation areas, such as pantry cupboards and
dairies, and sometimes for bedroom cupboards which were
presumably used for clothes. The usual position for ventilated
cupboards was beside the fireplace or under the stairs, the
usual form being a ventilation grille above the door, composed
ofturned or splat balusters; most date from the later rzth and
the 18th centuries. Diamond mullions were used in a ı7th-century Wiltshire house to ventilate the buttery, and a ı7th-century
Gloucestershire house has a panel with a pierced design instead
of balusters. Another Wiltshire house has some reset early-
17th-century panelling, in which the upper panels are pierced
with narrow vertical openings; possibly this formed a small
buttery partitioned out ofthe hall (Slocombe). Many 18th-century grilles have wavy splat balusters whose profile is derived
from the twisted balusters used in staircases of the period.
Sometimes at this date there was a matching pair of ventilated
cupboards on either side of the kitchen fireplace. Ventilation
grilles with straight slats continued as a ıgth-century feature.

Grilles were also used to light areas which lacked a window,
such as a staircase, a passage, or a small boxroom between two
attic bedrooms. A house in Durham has reset staircase balusters to light and ventilate a tiny servant’s bedroom partitioned
outofthe attic.

Sometimes cupboards were ventilated by a grille set in the
door itself;an Essex example dates from 1685, and a Gloucestershire one ofsimilar date belongs to a small cupboard set above
the main bedroom cupboard. More often doors were pierced by

- Splat balusters, Gloucester

Ventilation and Cooling 1.1.4
oval, heart or lozenge shapes, common in the late-ı7th and the
18th centuries, or bya series ofsmall holes. When such ventilation is found in the door ofthe hall orkitchen it is to provide sufhicient draught for the fire to draw properly and prevent smoke
filling the room. In this case the hole may have a cover which can
be opened or closed according to need. LINDA HALL
Ice houses

Ice houses have been built since the beginning of
recorded history, in every continent except Australia. In them
natural ice, harvested in winter from ponds, lakes, streams or
rivers, was packed, insulated and stored until, in the heat ofthe
summer, itwas periodically removed and used for cooling food,
drinks, rooms and people. In some areas snow was similarly
collected and stored. The ability ofice to last for as long as two
years in an ice house, whether above or below ground, is dependent on careful siting and insulation of’ the ice house, and on
the sheer volume ofthe ice contained in the ice well, which is
generally stored in a form to present a minimum surface area
for melting. Ice was also generally packed down tightly within
the ice well, sometimes mixed with salt to lower its freezing
temperature, and insulated within the walls of the ice house,
with either straw, reeds or timber.

A cuneiform tablet of 2000 Bc tells of the ice house of an
Assyrian governor who used ice to chill his wine in summer,
andas late as 1984 ice and snow were still being stored in pits in
the mountainside near Al-Amärah in northern Iraq, a stone’s
throw from this Assyrian site. Ice houses were widely used in
the Greek and Roman worlds and it may have been at this time
that the technology spread throughout cities and villages of
western Europe and the Mediterranean. In the 14th century the
Egyptian Barqug dynasty imported ice into Cairo from the
mountains of Lebanon, and stored it in vast urban ice repositories. China also had ice stores from antiquity, used for instance
in the area of Ningpo for freezing fish catches between seasons. South America hadaa thriving ice storage and distribution
industry in the ıgth century. In Britain ice houses enjoyed a
revival after being re-introduced from the courts of Europe by
James I who built a snow well at Greenwich in 1619. After the
civil war ice houses became fashionable with courtiers who
builtmany on their country estates. Gradually over the nexttwo
centuries ice in summer became a luxury available to all-by the
late-18th century to the squires ofcountry estates, and eventually,
bythe end ofthe ıgth century, to the urchin on the street corner.

The remarkable ıgth-century ice trade began in Boston,
America in 1805. From the time ofthe first settlers in America
ice was stored to alleviate summer heat. Today four different ice
house types can still be found. The first is the small domestic ice
house. The second was small in size but only builton dairy farms
to chill meat and milk. The third was the larger urban repository ofwhich one or two per settlement were built. The fourth
type were the huge industrial repositories built by the primary
sources ofwinter ice such as the Hudson River in New York State,
the Kennebec river in Maine and the lakes outside Boston.

It was at Lake Wenham outside Boston that the great American ice industry began when Nathaniel Wyeth invented the ice
plough which made it possible to harvest huge tracts ofice; the

1V14.e

465

## S. 183

1v SERVICES

ice was then stored and exported via train and then boat, firstto
the West Indies and South America and then later to Europe,
India and even as far as China. Norway later joined the ice trade
and became the largest supplier of imported ice to Britain, a
trade which stopped only with the start of the Second World
War. When ice arrived at British ports it was unloaded into ice
houses at the docks, and then transferred for use by horse and
cart, or train. The ice house became commonly obsolete in
Britain during the First World War when estate workers left for
the war and could no longer harvest the ice. By the 19308
paraffin and electric refrigerators were introduced on country
estates, but itwas not until the early ı960s that itwas common
to see refrigerators in private houses.

The greatest of all ice houses are the massive egg-shaped

examples of the Iranian plateau. Their mud-brick and pise
domes rise up to 14 m (46 ft) above the pits which are as much
as 12 m (40 ft) deep. The ice for these desert ice wells was actually manufactured in shallow shaded pans beside the house,
where rapid re-radiation ofheatfrom the earth’s surface caused
water in these pans to freeze overnight. This ice was then harvested before dawn and the pans refilled for the next night’s
freezing. A similar practice existed in northern India where the
ice was manufactured in porous pots. In Britain where over
3500 examples of extant ice houses were recorded in the 1980s
there were eight ice house forms noted: cup and dome structures; circular chambers; square and rectangular chambers;
tunnel-shaped chambers; timber and thatched ice houses;
unique structures such as octagonal, hexagonal, caves and
multiple chambers; temporary stores, such as ice stacks; and
urban ice cellars. The most common British ice house is the
brick egg-shaped cup and dome structure. However, there are
strong regional differences in construction, with the majority
ofice houses being built ofthe locally available materials. Thus
in Hertfordshire brick egg-shaped structures predominate,
while in Cumbria square and rectangular chambers ofstone are
more common. SUSAN ROAF
1.V1.4.e-i Glacieres (France; Mediterranean)
Before the arrival of artificial refrigeration, people
utilized natural ice (i.e.snow and ice-fields) or frozen springand
rain-water to keep food fresh orto preserve it, to prepare special
dishes, and to treat feverish diseases, headaches and the like.

Natural or manufactured (through artificially induced frost)
ice was stored in either natural or dugout cavities covered with
perishable materials, or in specific masonry constructions: the
ice houses. These are cylindrical towers, totally or party underground, showing varied and often impressive dimensions
(10-20 m [33-65 ft] in diameter, and 15-25 m [50-80 ft] and
even 50 m [165 ft] in height). Except for rare cases (such as the
Roman villaofComminges, in Santander, on the French-Spanish border), there are few ice houses from classical antiquity or
the Middle Ages. The majority date from the ı7th, 18th and ıgth
centuries and are found in Eurasia and North Africa. With
development of urban life, came the establishment of privileges, state taxes and monopolies of various kinds concerning
the ice trade. Ice houses became prolific. Besides private ones
serving farmhouses and secular or ecclesiastical estates, groups

466

ofice houses were administered to furnish towns and villages
according to precise contracts. Important groupings are to be
noted in regions such as the Sainte-Baume range (Provence,
France), the Pyrenees, the Sierra Espüina (Murcia, Spain), the
mountain ranges north of Rome and Naples (Italy), the Atlas
range (Blida, Algeria), and the high mountain tables of the
Near East and Iran. Climatic and geographical particularities
favour this development wherever exposed and cold mountains
are situated relatively near market-places.

Ice houses present an exterior aspect suitable to traditional
regional architecture: tiled roof in southern France, thatched
roof in central and northern France, brick graduated roof in
Iran. Jambs of openings, facades and roof finishes are also in a
local style. A superstructure frequently envelops the ice reserve
transforming it into a commonplace building or integrating it
into an urban landscape. In the town of Arles in Provence for
example, ice houses are integrated in the rampart. The exclusive use of local materials, such as tuff in Catalufia and river
pebble in Roussillon, facilitates integration.

In spite ofthese differences, there are several constants: the
underground part ofan ice house is cylindrical and narrower at
the bottom, the structure is covered by adome or by a semicircular vaultarticulated on lateral cheeks, and it is equipped with

Doms in Kent

asump oradrain to take away melting ice through the slope supporting the building. Various features such as buttresses, horizontal or vertical access corridors, enclosure walls for shade
ensured thermal insulation and optimized natural conditions
offreshness. Dry stone terraces and channels ensure that water
is drawn into adduction in ‘icing-pools’, meadows or plain surfaces bordered or supported by dry stone walls. Ice houses are
generally built in rubble and mortar and are rough-cast in the
interior. Dry stone masonty is used for coatingthe underground
parts where natural rock is also often visible. A ‘work-shop’ for
processing the ice frequently accompanies the ice houses,
either as a separate building or attached and situated in front of
the main entrance.

Some ice houses were occasionally in use during the firsthalf
ofthe 2oth century (in Spain, Italy, and North Africa). For mountain economies this kind ofrefrigeration is sometimes more convenient for preserving milk products, as in the Alps, or for watering flocks, as in the Abruzzo, and cheaper where there is insufhicientelectrification, asin Iran. ’ADA ACOVITSIÖTI-HAMEAU
1Vv1.4f Louvers
Dairies and cheese-rooms or cheese-lofts in England
required good ventilation to keep them cool, butalso needed to
keep out any birds or animals that might enter by an open door
or window. This was achieved by replacing the opening casementwith a fixed lattice-a framework ofwooden bars arranged
diagonally, or at right angles to the side of the opening. They
are difficult to date; although the form may have been used earlier few surviving examples seem to be older than the late-ı7th
century, and mostare probably later. Alarge Kent farmhouse of
c. 1690 has a lattice window between the ground-floor cheeseroom andalobby, and a partition ofhorizontal slats separating
the cheese-room from the dairy. The same house also has a
small lattice panel in the door to the cupboard under the stairs.
Gloucestershire cheese-rtooms were usually in the attics. In the
17th century cross-ventilation was provided by windows in the
large dormer gables on either side, but in the 18th century they
were ventilated by floor-level lattice windows in the gable-ends.
These were additionally protected by external wooden louvers—
in which a number of horizontal, inclined wooden slats admit
lightand air but prevent rrain-water from entering-and could be
closed by internal shutters. Some cheese-lofts were separated

I

Kent cheeseroom c. 1690

AN

es
RS

Ventilation and Cooling 1.1.4
from the stairhead area by floor to ceiling lattice partitions, and
asimilar partition, complete with lattice door, survives in a Sussex cellar. Lattice or slatted doors were used in cheese-lofts and
dairies. External dairy doorways were sometimes provided with
two doors, one solid and one ventilated; the latter may be a
wooden lattice or slatted door, or a simple wooden frame covered with a metal mesh. This was a feature ofthe later 18th century onwards, along with the metal grilles and perforated zinc
sometimes used for dairy and pantry windows. A unique external door exists in a Gloucestershire house dated 1707; the upper
panel of the two-panelled door is hinged to fold down on the
outside, revealing an internal lattice panel. There is in addition
a lattice grille over the door (Hall). LINDA HALL
1V1.4.8 Mashrabiyya

The use ofscreens on windows or balconies has been
a noticeable feature of Islamic architecture. Exquisitely carved
stucco screens typify the delicate, ephemeral nature of late
Andalusian and Maghreb architecture as much as those ofcarved
marble the precision ofMoghularchitecture. They occur also in
Iran and Iraq. In hot, humid climates screened balconies,
(mashrabiyyat or rowshan) performed two essential functions:
providing privacy to the occupants, while permitting currents
ofair to circulate through the rooms by means ofgrilles. In Arabia they occurred on the coasts ofboth the Red Sea and the Arabian or Persian Gulf, but were quite different in character,
stressing the different cultural influences affecting each region.

In Hejazi architecture they were a major and intrinsic elementinthe design ofthe house. Whole sides ofrooms devolved
into screens, sometimes two sides ofa room on the corner of
the house were merely screens separated by the corner
masonry. Sometimes, the masonry was scarcely visible because
ofthe numerous rowshans. Often, an extra screen of shish latticework supported by brackets, shielded the centre part, which
could be opened, for the base was solid and the top section
fixed but usually containing a grille.

They were made of teak wood carved in the East Indies and
were a product of the East Indian trade. Their patterns were
taken from traditional Islamic designs which were repeated in
different combinations to create dramatic effects with their silhouettes in the interiors ofthe rooms. They also prevented the
glare ofthe sun from penetrating into the rooms.

1V1.4.h Roofs (India, 5)

available material, method of construction and ventilation and .
Second layer of bricks

The rowshan was supported by consoles embedded in the walls.
These protruded approximately 60 cm (2 ft) from the building
but with another 60 cm (2 ft) from the depth ofthe exterior wall
made a comfortable alcove or oriel for the room inside. They
were 2.4 m (8 ft) wide and approximately 3 m (ro ft) in height,
coinciding with Greenlaw’s description ofrowshan atSuakin on
the westbank ofthe Red Sea. They were favourite places to relax,
where one could lie down or sit and chat. The consoles might
be carved, decorated with lambrequins, that is, asuspended cornice or fringe, or perhaps disguised with stalactites (mugarnas).
The top might be finished with a deep moulded entablature
showing Turkish or European influence or perhaps sportahood
decorated with a filigree fringe. In spite of many Turkish or
European details their basic form had much in common with
the mashrabiyyat of Cairo, although the woodwork was more
robust than the turned wood ofthe Cairean model, for the bars
were simply notched and scalloped so thatthey formed attractive
patterns when crossed on the diagonal and slatted together.

A different type of balcony appeared in the Gulf region near
Ad Dammäm. These were long screened balconies at first-loor
level, less than 2 m (6.5 ft) wide, sometimes, with roofs. They
were cantilevered from the exterior walls and their supporting
cantilevers disguised with simply sawn lambrequins in the Persian or Indian manner. They were a much later innovation, of
the 20th century, and were made ofsawn softwoodand, although
cruder than the balconies of Hijäz, they made an attractive and

useful feature. MICHAEL W. EARLS

468

cooling devices. The materials that contribute to cooling include
lime, timber, clay bricks, mud, laterite, stone (usually granite)
and thatch. Lime, the principal building material for centuries
is used as bonding mortar, for concreting, fooring, plastering
and weatherproofing. Mud, which is used as pise or adobe for
walls, and laterite, ahard-formed porous clay cutand sized into
building blocks, are naturally cool materials. Deep overhanging roofs are used to protect these materials from rain, in the

opening allowing
continuous air movement

"South and

Stone used for walls or open paving is often pitted. This uneven
surface reduces heat retention as small protruberances shade
the depressions and act as self-shading devices. Thatches of
different types of palm, grasses and reeds are less expensive
roofing and wall materials. Certain black marsh reeds in coastal
Tamil Nadu swell up when wet thus preventing rain-water penetration but have the inherent ability to ‘breathe’ and create air
pockets that actas heat insulators.

‘Madras terrace roofing’ is acommon flat roofing technique
in which the roof is spanned with timber rafters, with one or
two layers of diagonally placed bricks. A lime and mud screed
bed is laid over it. Flat clay tiles bedded on a lean mix of lime,
broken brickbats (surkhi) and jaggery (a viscous sugar traditionally used as a binder for lime concrete) form a weather-proof
and heat-insulating layer on the terrace. In high rainfall
regions, tiled or thatched roofs are sloped at angles greater
than 30°, thus providing a high ceiling inside the house which
allows the warm air to riseand be displaced by heavier cool air
in theliving zone. Gable ventilators and roofvents provide constantair exchange and hasten cooling.

To enhance cooling of interior spaces, a false ceiling of timber boards is created with a lime and mud screed overlay. This
attic space, which is provided with ventilators is used as a grain
store enhancing the cooling ofthe room below.

Mostventilation devices in South India are in roofs and walls.
Avery effective device prevalentin Keralaand Tamil Nadu is the
ridge-vent, where the ridge ofthe roof is raised higher than the
normal slope and runs parallel to the wind direction. As the
wind passes through the vent at high pressure, air gets sucked
outofthe spaces below resulting in air movement in the dwelling. Partial ridge-vents are also common where a part of the
ridge is raised, and also actas an exhaust for fumes when placed
over thekitchen. Wind pavilions (kattru pandal) are unique windcatching devices found in the coastal regions. They are oriented
to catch the south breeze, no matter which direction the house
faces and hence provide good ventilation. The device is sloped
to eliminate the north monsoon winds and the thatched roof
construction prevents the air for getting overheated. Most
other roof devices actas exhausts for fumes and hotair. In most
parts of Kerala, a raised flap is created along the leeward slope

Ridge vent

Partial ridge vent

Wind pavilion drawing the south wind

rt

[as

Ssrıram Ganagathi

Raised flap

of the roof, ı5 cm (6 in) above the roofing tiles usually over
cooking areas which allows smoke to escape through it. Smoke
exhausts (pugai koodu or pugai pokki) are devices not unlike conventional chimneys which also bring in daylight. Smoke-holes
on flat roofs covered by terracotta cowls also act as hot-air ventilators and admit diffused daylight. The smoke tile (pugai odu)
is a modified terracotta roof tile which removes hot air. When
placed over cooking areas, itdoes not normally face the prevailing wind direction. Another common practice is to have a gap
between the wall and the roof- to a maximum height ofı5 cm
(6 in) or two bricks - to allow for continuous air movement.
SRIRAM GANAPATHI

469

## S. 185

1M SERVICES
1.V1.4.i Screens: jali

An architectural device widespread throughout the
hot areas of India, the jali is a perforated screen of stone, wood,
brickor other material that protects the interior ofabuilding from
direct sunlight while allowing essential cooling breezes to enter.
The many small openings filter and diffuse harsh sunlight, preventing glare, and in arid areas reduce the amount of dust that
penetrates the home during dust storms. Because thejali restriets
views from outside the home, it provides a measure of privacy for
the occupants, an important commodity in societies where
women have traditionally been kept from public view. The word
jali derives from the Sanskrit word for lattice-work or netting.

In the hot, arid region from the Punjab and western Uttar
Pradesh to Rajasthan and northern Gujarat the jali is often part
ofan elaborate cross-ventilation system that includes a central
courtyard (chowk), overhanging eaves (chajjas) to block direct
sun, and a water body to humidify the air. The jali, like the rest
ofthe building, is often made from kotah, a strong soft, yellow
stone capable of being carved into intricate patterns with holes
so minute they can barely fit a finger. Entire walls ofthe local
houses (havelis) are often broken into modular, non-loadbearing panels, each carved into a different geometric pattern reminiscent of Islamic inlaid tile work. The jali also confers a
measure of security; to deter thieves, the openings are never
larger than the size of fist. Jalis on a projecting balcony aid in
drawing cool air from a shady street into the house. In densely
packed communities, where balconies on opposite sides ofthe
street are so close that neighbours could reach out and shake
hands, jalis occasionally have downward-sloping holes to limit
views directly across the street yet permit haveli dwellers to
enjoy the comings and goings below.

In these dry regions, the jali works by a process known as
‘adiabatic’ or ‘evaporative’ cooling. When a water body is
placed adjacent to a jali, water will evaporate by drawing heat
from the surroundingair. The inhabitants of havelis often place
apool, atrough ofwater, ora moistened cloth or screen nextto
ajali for this purpose. The perforations in ajali actas miniature
wind tunnels, drawing a large volume of air through small
openings at high speed, and thus accelerate the process of
evaporative cooling.

In the humid south, jalis do not require such small openings
and can be made from wood or brick. In coastal Gujarat, where
durable, well-seasoned teak arrived by ship in the ı7th to ıgth
centuries from the Malabar coast (now the Indian state of Kerala), Daman, and Burma, the havelis and their jalis were made
from wood, and the patterns are both geometric and representational; the jalis often depict flowers, the carved frames and
brackets human and animal figures. With the limitations ofcost,
construction ofintricately carved jalis is today limited to affluent
homes and temples. In the southwestern states ofMaharashtra,
Keralaand Karnataka, an inexpensive contemporary jali is often
made of sun- or kiln-dried brick. The gaps formed by spacing
apart bricks and by cantilevering one brick over another allow
ample air penetration and make decorative patterns reminiscent
of India’s woven ikat fabrics. Brick jalis can be formed within
single-width thick, load-bearing walls and are commonly used
to provide ventilation for interior rooms and for water closets.

470

Finely carved examples ofjalis appear in the havelis of Ahmedabad, Baroda, Suratand Patan in Gujarat, especially those ofthe
Patel, Bohraand Desai families, in the havelis ofJaisalmer, Jodhpur and Jaipur in Rajasthan, particularly those of Jain and Soni
families;and in the south, in the curved wood balconies ofhouses
and Hindu temples in Kerala. Devices similar to jalis exist in
many other hot, arid cultures throughout the world. Examples
include the mashrabiyyat of Egypt and the claustra work of the
Middle East. PETER ENGEL
1V1.4.i-i Pierced walls (India, S)

Traditionally, walls were treated more as an enclosure
affording privacy and security. Windows emerged laterthrough
foreign contact. Narrow horizontal openings (palakani, ‘many
eyes’) with stone-latticed grilles inserted high up in the wall,
ensured air movement within the house. The stone lattices were
later replaced by iron rods and thick timber lattices. Honeycomb
brickwork and ‘air bricks’(jalis) are other permanent ventilators.
In urban settlements, windows facing the inner courtyards are
larger while those facing the street are smaller and higher than
eye level. Windows always face each other to ensure cross-ventilation. Double windows are also in use, the lower one providing
privacy when closed. A less expensive wall construction technique was the use ofinterwoven palm thatch panels, whose gaps
provided constant airflow within. SRIRAM GANAPATHI
1V1.4j Serender (Black Sea)

A serender or seren (‘cool place’ or ‘airy place’) is characteristic ofthe eastern Black Sea’s rural settlements of Artvin,
Rize and Trabzon. In Läz settlements such as Hopa, Arhavi,
Findikli, Ardesen and Pazar, itis called nayla or nalya.

These buildings emerged out of the need to preserve food
which, ina humid environment, requires air circulation. Cereals
and dried food (e.g. maize, green bean, potato, onion and horse
bean), green and dried vegetables, and fruit (e.g. nut, chestnut,
apple, pear and prune) produced once a year and consumed
periodically are all stored in serenders, as are animal products,
such as cheese, butter, honey and roasted meat.

Construction material used is durable wood such as chestnut,
juniper and walnut, the structure being made of ground-floor
framing (gatma) with posts connected by beams on the top, and

e

India.

horizontally (taraba), wooden upper floor with ‘neck’ and ‘wolfneck’ cornerjoints. On top ofstacked stone foundation walls, are
base beams joined horizontally at 90°. Cornerposts are supported by payanda or ‘elbow’ braces connected at 45° to the base
beams on two sides of the post. A grid floor is constructed by
placing discs on top ofthe cornerposts and interval studs to carry
the main beams on which the floor beams are attached. The
upper floor consists ofan enclosed storage room, and a balcony
(gardak or hayat) with a frame structure open on three sides, used
for resting and viewing. Wooden stack wall top beams are
attached to gardak posts’ top beams, and stair supports are
attached to the wood frame. The pitched roof consists of a footing beam, roofing plates, roof truss posts, collar beams, ridge,
sloped purlins, rafters, and roofcladdingwhich is either ofwood
shingle (hartama or cablama) or tile. Apart from the joints in the
roof and the door hinges the wood is totally connected through
slip joints without nails. All three facades have ventilation grids,
and the roof cavity is not closed to ensure better air circulation.
Serender size and form are determined by thewood beams connecting the ground-floor posts, which can optimally be crossed
without deflection in 3 m (10 ft) lengths. One side ofthe plan is
at most twice this length, repeated only once into a square or
elongated along the entry axis. Consequently thesmallestserender
may have four poles (3 x 3 m [1o x 10 ft]) while a medium size
building has a two-pole entrance and three-pole side facades. A
large serender may have either all four-pole, or two-pole by fourpole facades, while the largest has 10-12 poles with three-pole
by four- or five-pole facades. There might be a single gardak on
the entrance facade, or an L-shaped gardak on the entrance and
side facades. A more elaborate U-shaped gardak on the entrance
and two side facades may be built and in some cases the carsdak
surrounds the whole building. CENGIZ ERUZUN
1V1.4.k Vented walls
The small opening, or series ofsmall openings, used
for the provision of ventilation and daylighting in the vernacular architecture ofa number oftribes in the Bemba grouping of
northern Zambia, is known as nsolokoto in the Cibemba language. Originally associated with the use of several traditional
building materials and their resultant building forms (Schmetzer), nsolokoto are now mainly associated with the emergence of

Ventilation and Cooling 1.v1.4

Light penetrating through the open
space between rafters is reflected by
the underside of the thatch.

Little direct sunlight enters the room.

Light dispersal
range.

O Patrick M. Topham

N

Most light is from sunlight reflected
off the external swept ground.

a series of details in rural burnt brickwork, which has become
the main traditional walling material for much ofthe northern
part of Zambia. Studies ofdetails have been made amongst the
Ng’Umbo and neighbouring tribes in the south of Luapula
province, Zambia, but similar patterns ofopenings can be found
in many parts of Africa, from Maghreb (Jacque-Meunie) to the
Sahel (Denyer) and Zanzibar. In Zambian rural society, the family homestead consists ofa number of'huts arranged around a
communal open space, which acts as a living and work area.
The huts are used mainly for sleeping and storage and so the
provision ofventilation is of greater importance than the provision of natural daylighting to huts. Kitchens and sanitary facilities are housed in separate structures.

The use ofburnt brickwork was introduced by Christian missionaries around the beginning ofthe 2oth century along with
the training of local bricklayers. This gave rise to the evolution
ofa local vernacular size of burnt brickwork, which starts to
appear on buildings built around 1930. This new size of brickwork evolved from imperial brickwork sizes, probably due to
over-allowance for the shrinkage of clay during manufacture.
This gave a modular size of four stretchers to the metre and 12
courses to the metre. Since the birth of Zambia as an independent state in 1964, there has been political pressure for traditional leaders to encourage their subjects to build in burnt
brickwork, which has led to the traditional detailing ofnsolokoto
developing to allow the construction ofa variety ofmulti-opening screen patterns.

As huts are small with few rooms, the entrance doorway
plays an important part in the lighting and ventilation of the
main room. All rooms receive air and reflected light through
open spaces around roof structure spars, which occur between
the underside of the roof thatch and the top ofthe brickwork
wall. These spaces also help to limit termite attack. Owing to
the high angle ofthe sun, much ofthe light entering a hut has
been reflected off the surrounding ground, which is handswept daily to maintain a clean reflective surface. Reveals ofthe
openings also play an important role in reflecting light into a
room, for the large size ofthe vernacular brick helps to create
sizes ofopenings which are practical for these purposes.

In ber, northwest

ple, found atthe Bagh-e Dowlatabad in Yazd, Iran, isan octagonal brick tower 32 m (too fü) high; ithas 1.2m (5 fi) high vents at
its head which channel wind from all quarters into the garden

Unlined openings are voids formed by the omission ofheader,
stretcher, soldier bricks, or clutches of bricks. Panels of such
openings are usually used on kitchen structures and pitlatrines.

Ze u

Rectilinear lined openings are used singly, or in horizontal
series, where soldier brick linings become mullions. Such
mullions are sometimes laid on a bias of 45°. This is useful in
improving ventilation and effecting privacy, when mullions are
closely spaced. The openings are bridged with a lintol stretcher
brick, or by centilevering a stretcher brick supported by a solider
brick. With closed-spaced mullions there is no fixed support
pattern between the bricks ofthe opening head and the soldier
mullions. Both wide and close spacing tends to create a wind
scoop effect, but the close-spacing arrangement also restriets
vision to create a privacy zone within the building. Inclined-lined
bricks can be used singly or in a variety oftracery patterns while
a combination ofrectilinear and inclined linings and tracery may
be used for decorative effect (Topham). PATRICK M.TOPHAM

1.V1.4.K-i Vents: coral slab (Arabian pen)

The harsh climate ofthe Arabian peninsula requires
that every possible means be used to improve the internal
microclimate conditions of the houses. Despite the important
cultural factor of privacy, the houses on the coast have to be as
permeable as possible in order to allow ventilation by the cooling winds from the eastand the north. With the introduction of
vents (openings in the exterior or courtyard walls on all floors
and around the roof), the necessary ventilation is provided.
They are however, situated in such a way that itis impossible to
look into the house from the outside. Since theroofs are used in
the hot summer both in the daytime and for sleeping at night,
vents are normally placed in the parapet wall around the roofs.
Some vents are fitted with shutters for the regulation ofthe wind.

Outside the air enters at the top, passes down through the
cavity wall and enters the room at the level of people sitting on
the floor. Cushions are placed on the floor around the room
where the majlis receptions are held.

The portion of the room or veranda which is closest to or
immediately beneath the vent is the favourite place for sleeping. In many cases there are vents on two opposite walls in one
room, in order to increase the movement of air, thus giving a
cooling effect. Vents are closed during the winter months and
inasandstorm (shamal).

There are several types of vent including pierced-plaster
screens located above the doors or windows on the upper section ofthe room which allow the hot air to escape. In addition
this vent provides light to the room. The screens are carved in
decorative geometric and floral designs, in a semicircular form
approximately 80-100 cm (2.5-3 ft) wide and 50-60 cm (20-23
in) high. Another type ofventis asmall hole in the wallabout 20
cm (8 in) in diameter, located in the upper portion of the wall
and opening to the exterior ofthe house.

The location ofthe vents within the house is of prime importance. They are usually built into the walls facing in the direction ofthe preyailing wind. They are also built along the walls
with more open space to allow movement of the air. The width
is on average 5 cm (6 in) and the length 80-100 cm (2.5-3 ft).

1V1.4.| Water cooling

Water is used in hotand arid climates to reduce local
temperatures by evaporative cooling, and to humidify the atmosphere, processes which occur when water is transformed from
a liquid to a vaporous state. Such a transformation requires
energy which is absorbed from the immediate environment,
such as nearby walls. It has been calculated thatthe temperature
ofıcubic metre ofair will be reduced by 1°C by the evaporation
of 0.5 g of water (Evans). Or, as interpreted by Basam Behsh,
‘0.05 litres of water will cool aroom ofdimensions5x5x4m
[16x 16x13 ft] containing dry air by 1°C.’

Among the simplest of cooling methods used in Egypt and
other Arab countries is the placing ofjars ofwater in the mashrabiyya screen so thatair may pass over them, cooling both theair
that enters the room and the drinking water in the vessels. Curtains or blinds of barasti woven palm leaves may be sprinkled
with water at intervals to achieve a similar result, and windscoops may have such hangings over the intake openings which
are intermittently saturated with water.

More permanent and planned cooling methods are achieved
in Muslim towns by the distribution of water, if gradient permit.
This was the case with the talea of Damascus old city, where water
was raised to a high level and then circulated via channels and
basins to the houses, the quantity delivered being regulated
according to entitlement. In Isfahan, Iran, the waters ofthe Zaindeh river were partially diverted by the jub system ofopen channels, the remaining water rejoining the river downstream. The
diverted stream here, as in other towns where the water is channelled, is regarded as the River ofParadise, the Salsabilwhich has
given its name to an external evaporative cooling system. Ifthe
force ofthe water is insufficientto servea fountain, itis madeto
generate a spray by being diverted over an inclined slope generallyofmarble, which is carved in reliefwith a decorative pattern
reminiscent of waves that agitates the water in its descent,
increasing air humidity and assisting evaporation (Fathy).

Where springs are ofsufficient force theyaretappedand used
to provide water from public fountains in many parts of the
world, though the fountains ofthe city of Fes, with their richly
decorated niches of ceramic tile mosaic, are among the most
