2.1V.1 Arabial

memorable. If the springs are numerous, or the current of
water adequate for the purpose, a domestic fountain system
may be used. Frequently, Muslim houses have large pools (bahra)
in their courtyards of circular, octagonal or other geometric
shape, usually in association with shrubs or trees which further
assist in providing shade and modifying the microclimate.
Cooling is dependent upon the amount ofwater thats available
and that can be spared for the purpose, and a number of other
factors such as the atmospheric pressure, the air temperature
and the movement ofair. Ifthe water in the bahra or basin is fed
by.a central fountain and circulated this greatly increases the
cooling effect. A spray creates water droplets which dampen
surfaces and also cleans the air by capturing dust particles. A
system of pipes may channel the water from the courtyard
fountain to other outlets in the cooking or living spaces.

A precious commodity in hot dry climates, water is managed
to obtain the maximum advantage in both private and public
utility. But the psychological value in aiding mental relaxation
and spiritual comfort, though unquantifiable, is probably the
greatest benefit of the water cooling systems in conditions of
extreme heat. PAUL OLIVER
1.Vl.4.m Wind-catchers: badgirs

Wind-catchers are built on the roofs of houses to
catch prevailing breezes and channel them into therooms below
to cool and ventilate them. The forms of wind-catchers vary
greatly. Most simply they consist ofa vent facing towards the
prevailing wind linked by a hole in the roof ofthe room below
through which theair is channelled. The most elaborate exam-

pavilion below.

Wind-catchers have been builtin the Middle Eastsince atleast
2000 BC as evidenced by pictures on tomb walls and models of
houses found in the tombs ofancient Egypt. They were fashionable in various regions during periods of economic prosperity.
In zıth- to 14th-century Cairo, poets sang the praises of windcatchers whose proportions reflected their owners’ status. In
the Gulf ports in the ıgth century the houses ofthe wealthy were
crowned by great wind-catchers. In Yazd on the central plateau
of Iran where the greatest concentration and variety of windcatchers are to be seen today, the greatest ofthetowers datetoa
period between 1868 and 1900 when, as a result ofthe opening
up of Hong Kong by the British as an opium port for China,
Yazd became the largest producer of opium in Iran, and windcatchers reflect the great prosperity ofthe period for the city.

The type of wind-catcher used in different regions of the
Middle East depends directly on the severity of the climate of
the area, and in turn on the local physiological requirements for
cooling ofthe occupants ofthe buildings. In very hot dry areas
such as Baghdad in Iraq (where summer temperatures can
reach 50 °C [122 °F]), wind-catchers are used only to remove
high humidities and odours from basements which are occupied during summer afternoons by up to fifteen to twenty members ofan extended family. In the hot dry regions where mean
maximum air temperatures do not typically exceed 40 °C (104
°F) large wind-catchers serve both basements and ground-floor
summer living-rooms. In the latter they are important at night
for cooling the structure ofthe rooms, and during the day for
cooling the room’s occupants by convection — and at higher
temperatures by evaporation, by passing moving air over the
skin to remove sweat. In the extremely hot humid climates of
the Gulf, where the only effective strategy for physiological
cooling at temperatures above skin temperature, around 35.5
°C (96 °P), is through evaporative cooling, itis essential thatair
is continually circulated over the body and great square windtowers are built to achieve maximum air movement.

The wind-catcher functions by trapping the faster movingair
above the dense settlements of the region; it exploits pressure
differentials existing between the vents above roof level and
room outlet level; and it commonly employs direct cross-ventilation ofsummer rooms to enhance the cooling potential ofthe
airstream. At the same time it excludes direct solar radiation
from summer rooms. While in the larger towers an average
temperature drop within the tower, from top to base, is round
2°C 5°P) air attemperatures above room air is still introduced
on summer afternoons. In many hot climates this can be compensated for by nighttime cooling of spaces which lowers the
temperatures of the structure of the building to a level where
mean radiant temperatures of surfaces are acceptable during
the day, compensating for high mid-afternoon air temperatures. When such a delicate balance cannot be maintained and
resulting temperatures are too hot for comfort, wind-catchers
are either used onlyto ventilate basements orarenotusedatall.

SUSAN ROAF

473

## S. 187

1.v1 SERVICES

1.v1.4.m-i Wind-catchers (Pakistan)

The southern part ofthe province ofSindh in Pakistan has longand very hotsummers. However, the evenings and
nights are made pleasant by the sea-breeze which blows from
the southwest for nine months ofthe year (March to November)
and whose effect is feltup to 100 km (60 mi) inland. The remaining three months constitute winter which is mild and pleasant.

Allbuildings in lower Sindh have used wind-catchers (mangh)
since ancient times, to funnel the sea-breeze into the house.
The mangh consists ofa small hole in the roof, and ifthe rooms
are on the lower floors then a vertical shaft leads to them. The
air inlets are placed high above the roofterraces where thewind
velocity is greater and less laden with dust. To ensure the maximum effectiveness for the wind-catcher, care is taken that each
house is built well beyond the ‘wind shadow’ ofthe neighbouring houses.

The typical wind-catcher is ı-1.25 m (3-4 ft) square and protrudes 1.5-2.25 m (5-7 ft) above the roof. Itis made of timber
and dried mud-brick infill and is inclined to about 45°to the prevailing wind. After passing through the rooms the air disperses
into the courtyard, around which the house is invariably built,
pushingthe warm air upwards. This creates a continuous air current cooling the interior ofthe houses and the courtyard. Sometimes the incoming air is cooled through contact with porous
water jars or damp matting. In winter, or whenever the hot
desert wind replaces the sea-breeze on summer afternoons, the
wind-catcher openings are closed by cord-operated trapdoors.

Although major changes have taken place in terms of materials and design of domestic architecture in lower Sindh, the
wind-catcher not only survives but is being increasingly used in
contemporary architecture ofthe region. ARIE HASAN

474

1.V1.4.m-ii Wind-scoops: barasti

In hot-humid climates ventilation rather than insulation is ofprime importance. In the Arabian peninsula, arange
of structures were devised to deflect outside airflow into building interiors to ventilate the rooms, thus creating a more comfortable environment for their inhabitants.

Among all types of wind-scoops, barasti are the most basic
and the simplest to erect along the coastal areas of the Gulf.
They are multidirectional with four sides and square in plan.
These wind-scoops which rose 6-10 m (20-33 ft) above ground
level were built as square towers divided into four equal triangles in the form ofan X and left open on the outer sides to catch
the breeze from any direction. The wind striking one side ofthe
tower was thus channelled down into the room below.

Barasti wind-scoops are usually associated with barasti houses,
creating an ideal situation where the incoming breeze from the
wind-scoop is channelled through the many openings of the
barasti walls.

The basic framework of the barasti wind-scoop is mangrove
poles or any other straight wood covered in clothing (sacking
material) ina cubic form. The top and bottom ofthe tower is left
open with the wind inleton the top and the outlet on the bottom.

The opening at the bottom of the wind-scoop is as low as
possible (1-1.5 m [3-5 ft]) in order to increase the effectiveness
ofthe tower by forcing the wind into a higher velocity. The roof
on the tower is covered with barasti. This roofing completes the
construction.

Underneath the barasti wind-scoop is usually the favourite
spot for conversations, eating, and holding a majlis. Extra matting covered with cushions beneath the tower is often used for
sleeping during hot summer days. Wind-scoops can easily be

dismantled during cool winters. DARIUSH ZANDI

ıns-a Water Supply

Although water is one ofthe basic necessities, a large
percentage ofthe world’s population has no access to safe water
for daily needs: 60 per cent of people in developing countries are
estimated to live without adequate supply of drinking water.
While techniques for water supply have been perfected over six
thousand years, economical, political and social factors often
pose allocation problems. Methods to seek, collect, store and
distribute water for food production, domesticand industrial use
are described here and the influence of water supply on social
organization, settlement location and house form is traced.

Today, environmental and water pollution, and a rise in
water consumption owing to population explosion and industralization have led to water shortages; reserves for the future
are threatened by extensive deforestation and ground-water
pollution.

Development ofthe water supply system

The sun’s energy and Earth’s gravity control the hydrological
cycle ofthe Earth’s water supply. The supply ofwater for human
use can be from rainwater, ground-water, surface or spring
water, together constituting less than 1.5 per cent ofthe Earth’s
water reserves. The rest exists as sea-water (97.6 per cent) and
polar ice (2 per cent). Factors of quantity, quality and transport
make water a limited resource.

It is speculated that ancient valley civilizations flourished
because oftheir access to water and development of irrigation
techniques. However, highly developed countries with knowledge ofirrigation and water management existed in other locations too.

The oldest wells (4000 Bc) are to be found in Mesopotamia
and Mohenjoaro. The latter had an urban infrastructure system
with wells, public baths, canals, drains and sewage treatment.
AncientEgyptrelied on a canal system to direct the yearly recurring Nile floods, while Mesopotamia placed emphasis on a
dam system more appropriate to the topography. The Egyptians were even familiar with methods of filtering the water
through sand and boiling itto purify it.

While location choice of early settlements was influenced by
the presence of adequate drinking water, other factors, for
instance of a military, commercial or administrative nature,
dictated the choice oflater settlements, and drinking water had
to be transported over long distances. In times of siege, access
to it needed to be ensured: in Jerusalem 7th-century tunnels
(sinnoirs) atthe water-table level leading to wells outside the city
gates, have been found.

Transporting water under pressure and over long distances
posed a problem. The Romans laid one of the first extensive

water distribution systems with aqueducts, public fountains
and baths. In the ıst century AD Rome had 450 km (280 mi) of
water pipes- 64 km (40 mi) as aqueducts and 2.5 km (1.5 mi) as
tunnels. Water was transported in underground channels or
aqueducts. The system required constant maintenance and
hence collapsed soon after the fall ofthe Roman Empire.

While in ancient times water systems were developed for the
common good, for example by providing better irrigation, in
the Middle Ages the numerous countries and kingdoms saw in
water a means of control (e.g. irrigation) and representation
(e.g. fountains and pleasure gardens in palaces). Their knowledge of water supply was based on the experiences and techniques of the ancient civilization which reached Europe
indirectly from Arabia.

A network could be built only after the introduction of iron
pipes in the 14th century, and a central water distribution system came into existence in the ızth to ı8th centuries. The
invention ofthe steam engine and pump in the ıgth century led
to city-wide networks, as in London and Vienna. Increasing
urbanization has made it necessary today to plan centralized
infrastructural systems. In urban areas, domestic water consumption may account for 50-70 per cent of the total consumption -therestis used for industrial, commercial or public
facilities. Improving the access to safe water of low-income
households in developing countries in qualitativeand quantitative terms will alleviate health problems and lead to betterliving
standards.

In traditional rural communities water is notjustan agricultural resource but also affects social relationships within and
between communities. Mention should be made of Wittfogel’s
controversial theory on the role of water as the basis of organization of early civilizations. Rules to establish control over the
distribution and maintenance of this (limited) physical
resource and are necessary. For example, in the Moalan chiefdom of the Fiji Islands upstream users are ‘owners of water’
and responsible for its distribution. In Mexico, water is seen as
acommunity resource and distributed accordingto the number
ofadults in the community.

Water distribution systems: traditional and modern

The ingenuity of pre-industrial societies in lifting, storing and
transporting water is well documented: the shadouf, noria
(na’ura) quanat (Kareze, foggaras, hattaras), sagiya, and hafırs are
inventions of the Middle East, also found in North Africa and
Spain. Waterwheels are the most efficient of traditional technologies for lifting water. They are either animal-powered
(noria, tympanum, saqiya or pot-chain pump found in south-

475

## S. 188

1.v SERVICES

ern Irag) or current-driven (e.g. na’ur known as early as 65 BC).
The Archimedes screw, invented by the mathematician in the
2nd century BC, was soon used for irrigation and draining tunnels for mining, and in shipyards; simplified versions made of
wood are still in use.

Several devices were developed for lifting water from wells,
for instance the paternoster water pump, in use in China even
today. Counterbalanced bailing buckets (shadouf) were common in ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia. This simple but
effective device was also used in Poland, Canada and Scandinavia and is still used in many parts ofthe developing world, as
are animal-powered technologies. Dams were built for retainingand storing water especially for irrigation. Aqueducts, used
to transport water over long distances, were extensively used by
the Romans. Qanats, manmade underground tunnels which
collect water from ground-water sources or from wadis and
transport it over long distances, are still used in Iran. In Nagaland where bamboo is abundant, channels of split bamboo are
used to transport water to the house. Nomadic tribes in Africa
and South Africa make use ofa traditional technique oftapping
ground-water in dry regions by capillary action. In extremely
dry regions of Africa drinking water is obtained from roots of
certain plants whereby locating the underground source is a
skill in itself. In Ghadames, an oasis city in Libya, water from an
artesian well was transported by underground canals and distributed to the fields up to the 19608. Residents had access to
water only in mosques and public washing places.

Water can be stored in cisterns, wells, reservoirs, dams and
watertowers. Based on natural reservoir forms, wells and cisterns were built as early as 4000 Bc. Wells and fountains were
meeting places and architectural elements of the cityscape.
Rain-water harvesting is a traditional method still used in arid
regions. In rural China rainwater, preferred as drinking water,
is collected from roofs in earthen vats. In western India, rainwater falling on to the terraces and inner courtyard is collected
in a basement cistern. Industrialized countries have a centralized water supply system which delivers water to individual
households. The benefits and side-effects of new large-scale
water projects, for instance Narmada Dam in India, are the
subject ofmuch debate.

In developing countries most households relyon community
taps and tubewells with a minority having individual connec-

tions. Where networks do not exist, for example in urban fringe
areas or squatter settlements, vendors sell water from barrels
ortrucks.

Planning a water supply system entails the determination of
the amount needed, choice of sources, method of withdrawal,
storage, treatment, transportation, distribution, network
maintenance, and treatment of wastewater. Factors affecting
the choice of water system to be constructed are the location
and type ofsource, the level ofinfrastructure, the water quality
and quantity, and its compatibility with waste water systems.

Development of special built forms for water supply

The symbolic nature of water and its close relationship to religion is seen in many cultures. Predicting when the yearly flooding ofthe Nile would occur was the responsibility of the high
Priest who made offerings to the river spirits to avert floods. In
India major rivers are personified as goddesses and worshipped.

Examples where the functional quality of water systems is
combined with aesthetics to form a special kind of subterranean architecture are the water tank (kund) and stepwells (vav.
Baoli) built in the 7th to 16th centuries in India. Using the form
language of steps, bridges, platforms, light and shadow they
are architectural statements celebrating the element of water.
In stepwells found in western India, water is stored several
metres underground shielded from the hot sun to minimize
evaporation. Inaddition they provided shaded, cool, rest places
for travellers. A small temple or shrine located in the vicinity
indicates the symbolic importance of water as a life sustaining
element, allowing fertility and growth. A modern, urban form
of water reservoir is the watertower which can even become an
architectural symbol ofa city, as in Kuwait.

In the face of environmental pollution and high energy consumption the search for alternative and appropriate technologies
has led to a review of current practice and adoption oftraditional
low-energy techniques, such as rain-water harvesting, and the
use of wind energy, as well as an examination of the viability of
desalination: there is a growing awareness that water is a limited

and precious resource. RITA MROTZEK-SSAMPAT

A well near Matı

Smith, N.,1988 '
wittfogel, Katl

1vı5-B Water Structures (Gujarat)

Gujarat is an area which suffers periodically from
severe drought and there are few permanent sources of fresh
surface water. Rains falls in one season of the year, during the
monsoons ofJulyand August. When the monsoons fail drought
conditions prevail. There are several historical records ofsevere
drought having occurred when the monsoon rains failed for
five or even seven successive years. The construction of water
structures is therefore an essential undertaking and is considered a notable charitable act. In the past elaborate water structures such as Rudabai’s stepwell (vav) at Adalaj (1499) and the
Kankaria reservoir (1458) in Ahmedabad were built but today
the provision of water is mainly pumped, supplied from artesian or tubewells.

The vernacular tradition of water structures and the cultural
life associated with them are in the throes of radical change -
informal daily gatherings around the well head are being superseded by the introduction of modern plumbing. Although the
tradition of building water structures has fundamentally
changed, historicexamples continue to be used and are still the
focal points of secular and spiritual activities, remaining an
important part ofthe vernacular tradition.

Water structures can be found in every settlement and along
established roads. They are an important point of social intercourse and often had resting areas and gardens associated with
them which are now neglected. Sources for drinking water are
distinct from those used to provide water for washing and
bathing and for animals. Almost all water structures, whether
they are associated with religious buildings or not, continue to

Water Supply 1.V1.5
be of ritual and symbolic importance. Acts such as symbolic
cleansing and purification are performed daily and various
practices to promote fertility and wealth are common. Many of
these rites are related to specific phases ofthe moon. Decorative patterns and deities found associated with the water structures indicate the degree of symbolic or ritual significance.

Thevernacular building tradition of Gujarat has been enriched
by commercial activity in the Arabian/Persian Gulf and Indian
Ocean previously determined by the monsoon winds and now
by the petroleum economy. From 1407 to 1573 Gujarat was an
independent Sultanate. The influence ofIslam and Muslim rule
popularized the use of brickwork and arched construction and
introduced new ideas about city planning and garden design.
Combinations ofindigenous and foreign methods ofconstruction were used - in the Amrut Washini vav, Ahmedabad, for
example, stone arches reinforce stone lintels. Water structures
constructed entirely out of brick are not common but they do
exist and examples such as the Koriar vav in Nadiad illustrate
the Persian influence. Brick now plays an increasingly important part in vernacular architecture and kilns can be seen on
major routes such as the road from Baroda to Ahmedabad.

There are four principal types of vernacular water structure
still in use in Gujarat: wells, stepwells, underground tanks and
reservoirs. The most common type of water structure is the
ordinary shaftwell. Dowsers are often used to select a suitable
site. A shaft is sunk and the sides of the well lined with burnt
brick or cut stone. The surrounds are built-up above ground
level to prevent dirt or people or animals from falling in. Elaborately carved brackets are found in many old wells for supportinga waterwheel, others have simple pulleys for drawing water
with ropes and buckets. The area around the well is often paved
in stone or brick to prevent the ground from getting muddy and
being churned up. Small reservoirs are often built a few yards
away from wells to provide water for animals. In rural areas
shaftwells continue to be used with few modern-day alterations.

In the Mt Abu area, Persian wheels with clay pots powered by
oxen are still used as they have been for many centuries to pro-

vide water to irrigate the fields. In urban areas, however, old

wells have had tubes sunk into the existing shaft so that uncontaminated water can be been plumbed into amodern grid using
pumps. Examples can be seen in the old pols of Ahmedabad.

Stepwells have been built in virtually every settlement in

Gujaratand are also to be found on old roads. Ancient histories
(Khan) and inscribed stones near the entrances ofwells such as

the Dada Harir stepwell, Ahmedabad indicate that they were

often built as a charitable gesture to the community and a

memorial to the donor. A flight of underground steps con-

nected to the shaftwell, leads down to the water’s edge. Facilities for drawing water are the same as in simple shaftwells

(pulley or wheel) so the flight of steps is not necessary for lifting

water. The subterranean stairwell remains cool throughout the

year. Measurements taken at the Calico Museum at midday
demonstrate the effectiveness of going underground to keep
cool — the ambient air temperature on the ground floor was

measured at 46 °C (115 °F) whilst below ground itwas 25 °C (77

°F). Steps provide a resting place in the quiet peaceful under-

ground atmosphere where lapping water creates a mesmeris-

477

## S. 189

1.v1 SERVICES

ing sound and soft, reflected light gives visual relief from the
glare ofthe sun.

Moststepwells are faced in stone and the sides ofthe stairwell
reinforced with stone lintels similar to the Sundaria vav in Mangrol. On larger examples such as the Adalaj (Rudabai’s) stepwell
the sides of the well are supported with arcades of arches or
beams, or both. The size and shape ofthe stepwells vary. Simple
examples have a clear width ofapproximately 4 m (13 ft) which
relates to the spanning capacity ofthe stone and most stairwells
descend three storeys with a length of over 70 m (230 ft).
Although stepwells are no longer built many are still in use and
provide drinking and sacred water to local communities.

Underground tanks storingmonsoon rainwater are a feature
to be found under courtyards of mosques such as the Jami
masjids in Khambhat and Dholka, as well as in urban housing,
for example the tank in Karia Jethabhai pol, Ahmedabad. Most
ofthe domestic examples are being superseded by piped supplies and this type of water structure is, like the stepwell,
becoming part ofthe vernacular history. Existing underground
tanks in mosques are still in use and remain an important
source ofwater in times ofshortage.

The tanks are built in brick or stone and rendered with limeplaster to make them watertight. Access is provided to allow
periodic repairs and maintenance to this lining to be carried
out. Roofs are washed with the first monsoon rains and then
the small apertures under rainwater spouts are opened for
clean rainwater to be collected and stored. This water is used
primarily for drinking or ablutions. In domestic examples a
well-like opening approximately 60 cm (2 fi) in diameter is

© Bookart 01707 875253

478

e

located in a specially assigned room (called the ‘water room’)
adjacentto thekitchen. Theraised opening is covered with alid
to prevent the water being polluted. In mosques the opening is
more like a well with a pulley mechanism for drawing the water.
Some examples, such as the mosque at Sirkhej are further elaborated by being connected to a wall channelled to provide water
for ablutions for several people atonce. The used water is then
utilized for watering plants or runs into a large reservoir.

There are two distincttypes ofreservoir still in use in Gujarat.
Reservoirs associated with temples, such as the example in
Dedodara, are characterized by their distinctive geometric, rectangular shapes defined by stone steps and punctuated with
niches for idols and perpendicular reinforeing blocks.

The second type are reseryoirs for secular use which are usuallylocated near mosque (Sirkhej), tomb (AlifKhan, Dholka) or
temple (Malav Talav, Petlad) complexes, or on the outskirts of
urban developments usually in planned garden suburbs such as
Kankaria, Ahmedabad. These reservoirs were constructed for
washing, bathing, agriculture and leisure and for animals.
Stone ramps give animals easy access to the water and stone
steps around the perimeter are ideal for washing and bathing.
Some reservoirs have wells sunk in the bottom ofthem which
are used in times of drought. YASMIN SHARIFF
1V15.a Canal
Canals are artificial waterways that join lakes, rivers
or seas for purposes ofinland navigation, such as transport of
people, goods and services, provision of water, irrigation and
drainage, and the production of power. The forms ofthe canals
and the settlements associated with them depend upon the
topography ofthe settings: inclined or level.

Canals in settings in which there are elevation changes are
formed of level stretches (or pounds) and locks, water-filled
chambers into which a craft enters and is raised or lowered to
the .nextlevel through the fill or release of water into or from the
chambers. A fundamental problem for this type ofcanal is conserving the water supply in the system. Most water is derived
from a point near the summit level, from springs and streams
or from deep springs and artesian wells, limiting supply. Water
loss occurs predominantly with the passage of boats through
the locks, as well as being caused by evaporation and seepage
through the sides and bottom of the canals. Water supply is
protected through the design of the locks and canals and

Water-lifting Mech,
to access Watergı,
In undergroung

reservoir befoy, 7
Courtyard, Tankayıın
Cambay, Gujarat,

Arches and brickworkol
the Moonjeshwar

stepwell, Dhar

2.1.6-B Gujarat and
Rajasthan

References

Commissarlat,
1938

‚Jain-Neubauer,).

Khan, A.M.,1928,1965

Shariff, Y.,1982

izhou
Padanian:
‚lagoon

ee Y,,1980
a Eric,1950
hard J.,1985

‘etothe cistern at Aljibe de las Tomasas, Granada, Spain.

through shading of canals. Transportation by canals was once
dominated by barges drawn mostly by horses on towpaths that
ran parallel to the canal, lending the characteristic two-tiered
section.

Canal systems in flat settings in which there are minimal
changes in elevation are not required to provide vertical lifting
ofcraft. Water supply isample and occasionally tidal. Given the
linear nature of the waterways, settlement forms are different
since they can be laced into networks of canals.

As density of development increases along canals, scattered
individual buildings form a continuous edge along the waterfront with each building requiring some frontage. Ifthe canal
is the dominant mode of transportation, as in Venice, or if
pedestrian and vehicular access run next to the canal, as in the
Canal du Midi, France, then the character ofthe canal is public
with buildings fronting the canal. Ifthe other modes of access
run perpendicular or parallel but removed from the canal, the
service and utilitarian nature of the canal is reinforced with
buildings ‘backing the canal’, such as in Suzhou and Zhujiajiao, China (Chow).

Growth and extension away from the canal occur both perpendicular and parallel to the canal. In the example of the
Venetian lagoons, frontage on the canal is critical since the
canal serves as the dominant means of access. Lots are narrow
along the canal butrun quite deep into the backland. Secondary
means of movement (calli) run between the lots from the canal
to the next waterway (Goy). In Chinese villages around Shanghai, the canal is predominantly utilitarian in purpose with a
pedestrian network that runs independently of the canal. In
Zhujiajiao, the pedestrian street runs parallel to the canal, but
one room’s depth away. The pattern of settlement focuses on
the pedestrian street, one side bordered by one-room deep
houses which have access to the canal, the other side bordered
by the more typical Chinese courtyard fabric, narrow lots with
common walls atlot-lines and a series of courtyards and buildings within the depth ofeach lot (Chow).

RENEE CHOW and THOMAS CHASTAIN

right Sections and plans

Water Supply 1.v1.5

1.v1.5.a-i Canal cistern (Granada)

Atthe beginning ofthe rıth century Granada became
the capital city ofa small Islamic kingdom, after the Cordoba
caliphate disappeared and al-Andalus splitup. The city was provided with a complex water supply system. Water came from
springs as well as from two rivers close to urban settlement, the
Genil and the Darro, and flowed through open channels, dug
out ofthe soil without masonry works, until itreached the city
walls. The ravines were crossed with small aqueducts. The
most important ofthese channels is the Acequia de Aynadamar
(Sägiyat ‘Ayn al-Damä‘), that carried water from Fuente Grande,
located at Alfacar about ıo km (16 mi) north of Granada, down
to the highest city districts of Albayzin and old Alcazaba.

After entering the urban enclosure, channels went underground, being built with bricks and slabs of stone. Then they
branched out through a set of pipes made with short ceramic
tubes (atanores). In this way, water was delivered to public cisterns as well as private ones, and large earthen jars; it was also
brought to gardens and orchards. The perfect design of this
complex distribution and storage net ofdrinking water allowed
it to work continuously from the ııth century until the 19508.
The high number of public cisterns let families have access to
water at an average distance of less than 100 m (330 ft) from
their houses.

Although nowadays the system has fallen from use, there are
still 28 Islamic cisterns in good condition in Granada. According to their effective capacity to store water, they can be divided
into three groups: the largest are the three whose capacity is
over I5o cu m (5300 cu ft); five are of medium size, storing
between 50 cu m (1765 cu ft) and ı5o m (5300 cu ft); the 20
small ones can store less than 50 cu m (1765 cu ft). Five have
several naves, separated by solid brick pillars, whereas the
remaining 23 have only one nave. Their walls were built with
rammed soil and lime concrete (tapial), or bricks, but vaults
were always made with bricks — barrel vaults with semicircular,
pointed, or keel-shaped sections, groined and cloister corner
vaults, or a dome. Although these vaults are usually under-

Section

ejanyuo oluoIUy ©

479

## S. 190

1.vI SERVICES

ground or are used as basements of houses, some emerge
above the pavement level of streets and squares. Vaults and
walls of cisterns were covered with reddish lime-plaster in
order to make them waterproof. Their pavements were made
with the same plaster or using floor ceramic tiles. The main
facades of the cisterns were often monumental, having huge
horseshoe arches decorated with glazed tiles.

Among the type of cistern with several naves, possibly the
most interesting one is Aljibe de las Tomasas, located near the
nuns’ convent of Santo Tomas de Villanueva. It has a nearly
square ground plan, 6.6%x 6.5 m (22% 21 ft) and a height of 4.5
m (15 ft), with four pillars that support groined vaults. It can
store 154 cum (5438 cu ft) of water. Itis supposed to have been
builtin the rıth to ı2th centuries.

The most well-known cistern with only one nave is Aljibe de
Trillo located, like the former, in the enclosure of old Alcazaba.
It measures 5 x 3.5 m (17 X ıı ft) and is 5.25 m (17 ft) high. Its
capacity is 50 cu m (1765 cu ft). It is divided into two sections,
the first covered with a cloister corner and groined vaults, and
the second one with a huge barrel vault. It has a fine horseshoe
brick arch in the main facade. It was built in the ı4th century,
the period when nasrid architecture flourished most.

ANTONIO ORIHUELA
1.V15.b Channel: falaj
During the past centuries complex systems of cultivation were constructed in the oases, wadis and mountain terraces ofthe Arabian peninsula. The sweet-water available from
springs, deep wells or winter rains was carefully collected and
guided to the fields, oasis or simply a palm tree.

The existence of many towns and villages in Arabia was
solely dependent on the availability of water and the means of
transporting itinto the fertile lands. Permanent or even temporary dıying of the water channel (falaj) owing to shortage or
drought would cause the crops to die and often the migration
or even death ofthe village inhabitants. In many cases the falaj
has been the cause ofthe rise and fall oftowns and villages in
the Arabian peninsula throughout the centuries.

Land surrounding the village was sculpted to yield the maximum possible harvest. In the mountains, where slopes were
terraced with stone walls which supported the fields, water was
led in little channels from the hillsides above or along the cliffs
of deep wadis on cleverly constructed ledges. Water channels
were dug underground as well. The complex falaj systems of
the Hajar mountains of Oman and the United Arab Emirates
and the ganat of Bahrain, brought water from its source at the
foot ofthe mountains or the highlands, through underground
tunnels to the fields.

The length of the falaj varied from a few hundred metres to
many kilometres. Its width and depth depended on the quantity
of available water, the terrain it travelled through, the geographical location and the type of ground. The width varied
from 20 cm (8 in) to 100 cm (go in) and the depth from 30 cm
(12 in) to several metres.

Similar in its function to the ganat in Iran, the falaj carried
water from natural water sources and reservoirs into the fields
for cultivation and for human and animal consumption. The

480

main difference between the ganat and falaj is in the nature and
depth of the channel. Falaj usually run in an open channel —
with a few exceptions where tunnels are dug to avoid the need
for a longer length when a barrier is reached. In principle the
qanat is an underground tunnel; when it surfaces the water is
then distributed by the ganat in a similar way to the falaj.

The most .critical element ofthefalaj system is its slope which
has to be very carefully graded. It is not known whether there
were any actual mathematical calculations used in the construction of the falaj, other than taking into consideration the
shortest practical route, the terrain, the geology, the distance
travelled and the level difference between the source and the
farm. The calculation principle was both to facilitate the movement of water and control its rate. The ideal gradient was one
which neither allowed the water to run so quickly that its force
would wash away the sides of the falaj nor so slowly as to
become stagnant.

It is not unusual to find an oasis with several levels of falaj
running in different directions to the various fields. The falaj is
still very much in use today and is maintained by one or more
farmers depending on the size ofthe falaj system. However, all
the farmers of the village are responsible for the day-to-day
watering oftheir own crops and for any major repair works.

1V1.5.b-i Channels and leats

The need to distribute water to field sites has often
proved to bea vehicle for the definition oflandholdings, as water
rights are established and controlled by communities. Such is the
case in Valencia or the Granada plain in southern Spain where
the Arabic huerta system of cultivation of mixed crops still survives. Canals and irrigation channels distribute water in accordance with ancient rules administered by community tribunes,
such as Valencia’s Tribunal of Waters which controls the irrigation ofovera quarter ofa million acres (Houston; Perpillou).

In regions where cultivation in the plains depends on the
melting of summer snows in the mountains, systems of controlling water by the building of dams and holding of water in
reservoirs are often necessary. Large dams require considerable
attention and maintenance, and the problems of silting are
such that vernacular dams are frequently small in scale. Water
regulation is often effected by the use of sluices which can be
simply constructed and operated. This is the case with the complex system of canals, aqueducts, channels and sluices of Bali,
which are controlled by irrigation societies or (subak). Membership ofthe subak is the entitlement ofall landowners, renters or
sharecroppers with rights within the area ofits control. A subak
is also a religious association whose authority stems from joint
respectand worship atthe temples sited atthe sluices and atthe
centre of the rice-fields. Several irrigation temple groups may
manage the waters ofone river, and the residents ofa single village may be represented in several subaks. Though they draw
their membership from all ranks and castes the subaks are egalitarian with decisions, including the fining of defaulters,
arrived at by consensus. A fine network of shallow, brimming
trenches and ditches capable of being opened or closed with a
board sluice or even a few clods ofsoil, feeds the paddy-fields at
the point of distribution (Geertz).

Apart from its importance for irrigation, water is managed for
the power itcan generate. In order to bring streams to watermills
and other water-powered machines, leats or artificial channels
are constructed. The purpose ofthe leatis to direct the volume of
water to the mill without waste or spoilage. Often the leatmay be
a division from the main stream, its gradient controlled so as to
provide a steady flow of water to the mill wheel. Undershot mills

may require no more than a narrow channel lined perhaps with

EN

Water Supply 1.1.5
stone or slate slabs, but breastshot and overshot mills require a
jet of water which almost invariably necessitates an aqueduct,
whose length is conditioned by the fall ofthe land and the distance ofthe water-source from the wheel. Such leats may be constructed of stone, in the form ofa thick wall, channelled on its
upper surface, as in Syriaand parts ofthe Mediterranean Levant.
Alternatively, they may be constructed ofwood, like the leats that
served the waterwheels of the Appalachians, which were open
wooden boxes supported from below on timber structures, and
braced by wood or iron straps.

Similar leats were used in mining, especially in the goldfields and other mining regions following the ‘rushes’ of the
late-ıgth century in North America, Australia and South Africa.
Leats, sometimes of considerable length, were constructed to
‘tail’ the water to the diggings, where it was used for washing
the gravel and ‘pay-dirt’ dredges in the ‘diggings’. Though
mechanized systems were introduced by the mining companies, use of many of the leats continued among independent
gold miners, and has prevailed among prospectors in Latin
America who construct them of lengths of bamboo with the
interstice walls removed.

Bamboo provides an elegant, strong but lightweight aqueduct in many parts of Southeast Asia or Latin America. In
Colombia for example, a variety of water-bearing half-section
bamboo lengths supported on crossed legs also made of bamboo, carry a fine rivulet of water for irrigation or domestic purposes. Some ofthese are an inclined channel]; others descend in
a succession of steps, each dropping a narrow stream into the
bamboo channel below. Bamboo affords an ideal material for
this purpose, but in countries less fortunately endowed, logs
may be splitand gouged to provide a channel, or bored to make
a water-bearing pipe. Both are used in parts of Europe even up
to the present.

Assessment in 1970 of water resources in go developing
countries revealed that ofa total population ofa billion people,
88 per cent were withoutaccess to pure drinking water (Dunn).
In spite ofglobal modernization and the increasing adoption of
western technology there is little evidence that vernacular
methods of water provision have been replaced, or thatthe proportion of people without direct access to potable water has
lowered in the intervening years. PAUL OLIVER
Cistern
Whereyer settlement exists without sufficient sur-

1.V1.5.c

face water to meet daily requirements there is a need for water
storage. Even animals seasonally take advantage of natural
water reservoirs to extend their ecological habitats. Man has
always built cisterns which are simply water reservoirs. They
can be excavated into impervious rock or into more porous sedimentary substrata, in which case they must be lined with a
waterproof material. The simplest method of waterproofing a
pit is to line it with puddled clay which may be mixed with
organic material to prevent cracking. Impervious rock linings
for water tanks were commonly used throughoutthe Greekand
Roman Empires and in India. In many Islamic countries cisterns, domed or vaulted, were built ofstone, or more typically
of baked-brick and lime-mortar walls lined internally with

481

## S. 191

|
|
|

1.v1 SERVICES

waterproof plaster which is made from mud, lime, burnt ash,
seeds, seed pods and reeds, mixed with water. Some shallow
eisterns in Iran are made by excavating a square trench for the
walls of the cistern, filling it with a slaked-lime mortar mix,
excavating the interior ofthese walls when they have dried, and
then pouring a floor of lime-mortar. The cistern is then lined
with waterproof plaster when dry. One famous roth-century
eistern outside Persepolis in Iran was lined with bitumensoaked canvas between the masonry walls and the waterproof
plaster.

Water sources can be either seasonal or perennial, examples
oftthe latter being rivers, springs or ganats. Seasonal sources of
water include rain and snow. One remarkable ı6th-century
snow-fed cistern at Sah-gol, near Tabriz in Iran, was 330 m
(1080 ft) square, 6 m (20 ft) deep in partand with a small island
Pavilion in its centre. The tank was partly carved outofa mountain wall and filled in spring with melting snow and ice from
the mountain. Thus not only were the peasants protected
against the spring floods, but they also had an ample summer
water supply and the wealthy owner also benefited from a cool
island retreat in the heat ofsummer. The most typical seasonal
method of filling cisterns is by harvesting the seasonal rains
and channelling them into the cistern. Often raised banks of
earth are extended out from the cistern to direct the sheet flow
of water into the cistern entrances. Considerable debris can
also be carried into the tank and such seasonal cisterns need to
be cleaned regularly to avoid silting up.

482

Good water quality is crucial ifwater is to be drunk. A cistern
can either be covered by a roof or be open. An open cistern is
hot as direct sunlight on the tank will heat the water, and the
walls of the tank. Water loss from uncovered tanks is also
significantly higher due to evaporation. In covered tanks, water
is cooler, receiving no direct radiation. Sunlight also encourages the growth of photosensitive organisms which may cause
fouling ofthe supply. Covering a tank limits the ingress ofairborne debris and pollution and leads generally to better water
quality. Roofinga cistern adds greatly to its expense. Many covered cisterns are ventilated by windows or wind-catchers which
direct breezes across the surface ofthe water oxygenatingitand
cooling itevaporatively. Oxygenation ofthe water can speed the
process oftthe disintegration of organic materials in the water
through the action of aerobic micro-organisms. Well-ventilated cistern water can be around 20 °C (36 °F) cooler than the
outside air temperature. Salt was sometimes added in Iran to
large cisterns on filling each year to improve water quality.
Where water is not tapped from the base ofa cistern it can be
lifted by bucketand rope, windlass or pulley; some contamination ofwater can occur from such open water lifting practices.

In cities water was, and is, stored in vast defendable reservoirs traditionally located within city walls. Jerusalem always
prospered because the limestone plateau on which itis located
was ideal for the building ofcisterns, large and small, that supplied water to the city until the turn of the 2oth century when
many became contaminated, a fate common to many urban cisterns. Istanbul was served by numerous covered and open cisterns, either rain-fed, or filled from the Belgrade springs. Both
the great Byzantine cistern of Acadius behind the Sultan Selim
mosque, measuring 186 094 cum (6571720 cu ft)andthegreat
eisterns of Aspar and Mokius were uncovered while the Bin Bir
Derek cistern with 224 columns and the Yeni Batan Serai cistern with 420 columns have vaulted roofs. In Cairo, as in many
other cities, double-storey cisterns held the water tank beneath
and a loggia (for study or resting in the heat ofthe day) above,
cooled by the water below and in turn protecting the tank from
penetration of direct radiation. This is a classic cistern type
where a deep circular or rectangular tank is sunk, often up to 20
m (65 ft) deep. Water is collected from atap near the base ofthe
eistern which is usually reached viaa portaland a descent ofup
to a hundred steps. The depth ofa tank is related to its volume
and to the temperature ofthe water within it. Capacities ofsuch
eisterns range typically from 300 cu m (10 600 cu ft) to 3000 cu
m (106000 cu ft) but can hold as much as 100000 cu m
(3 500 000 cu ft). Urban cisterns are usually filled from permanent sources via systems of sluice gates and settling tanks. In
some desert cities, such as Yazd in Iran, each house traditionally had its own square or rectangular domestic water cistern
fed by ganat.

The need to secure a safe supply of water within settlements
is particularly true for military strongholds. Water is also used
for many religious rituals and cisterns are constructed in the
forecourts of many churches, mosques and temples for such
needs. Beyond the walls of cities, patterns of cistern building
can be identified. Drinking water cisterns are found in settlements for domestic provisions, in agricultural communities for

opotamia and

#ofthecistern is
kt,

irrigation, and strategically located for the watering of focks
within the territories of pastoral societies. Along major overland routes watering points -ivers, springs or cisterns- will be
spaced atregular distances along the route to coincide with the
stops required on the days’ marches. This applies to the great
caravan routes of the world, and to pilgrimage routes such as
those to both Jerusalem and Mecca. SUSAN ROAF
1.v1.5.c-i Cistern (Mediterranean)

A cistern which is underground or built out ofthe
soil, represents an indispensable domestic service system for all
regions thatare arid, semi-arid or simply submitted to an irregular streamflow. Prolonged dıyness alternating with violent but
short-length rainfall, rarity of perennial springs, abundance of
perched settlements and agro-pastoral establishments are factors that require the use of cisterns. In and around Mediterranean countries this type of water supply is therefore very
frequent, and consists generally ofa built and carefully roughcast room incorporating or flanking rural houses, sheepfolds,
peasants’ cabins, enclosures or terrace walls. The cistern is
filled by water streaming from the roofand channelled through
gutters and spouts. The streaming surface is often augmented
by exploiting stone-heaps and wide walls covered with tiles.
The collected water is drawn outthrough a trapdoor openingin
to the interior ofthe house, or on to an open space. In this last
case the trapdoor is surmounted by a superstructure similar to
that ofa well. The opening is sometimes arranged through the
front of an enclosure or terrace wall. The practice of burying
jars to collect water inside these walls or along the terraces is
another form of using cisterns oflittle capacity but well suited
to local resources. Ceramic and stone vessels or even metallic
casks are also used to this effect, placed beside houses or in the
fields below dripping points. Water seeping along rock or cave
walls is exploited in the same manner either by blocking its
Passage or by channelling it to natural or built pools. The term
‘eistern-caves’ was used in antiquity and the Middle Ages, and
is still used now. Works that prevent the dispersal of spring
water have asimilar function to cisterns. Wholly or partlyunderground, they generally have a vaulted ceiling and a disposal
(e.g. steps) in order to access the water level.

A. ACOVITSIÖTI-HAMEAU

Water Supply 1.V1.5

1.V1.5.c-ii Cistern and ganat (Iran)

Over much ofthe Iranian plateau the natural watertable is too low for water to be easily available from wells so an
alternative supply and means of storing it is essential. Water is
brought from the mountains to the villages by means ofunderground tunnels (ganat) usually over a distance of5-Io km (3-6
mi). The precious water is then stored in cisterns (ab ambar) dug
into the ground and roofed to prevent it from evaporating and
to keep it clean and cool. These cisterns are usually circular,
5-10 m (16-33 ft) in diameter and 5-6 m (16-20 ft) deep, and
have domed roofs constructed of corbelled brickwork sitting
on a low rough stone wall, its top levelled off with mud. Dome
and well are plastered internally.

Access to the better cisterns is via underground flights of
steps which lead down to the base ofthe well where the water
collects. Such cisterns are cooled by wind-catchers (badgirs)
which means that although some water is lost by evaporation
thatremaining in the well is cool. In the more ordinary cisterns,
water is drawn off by a bucket through a low door at ground
level.

Water from rare flash floods is also collected and is stored
either in round, domed cisterns or in long, rectangular barrelvaulted cisterns which could be found beside desert roads away
from habitation in the 1970s (Beazley and Harverson).

ELISABETH BEAZLEY

1.V1.5.c-iii Cistern-well (Syria)

In rural Idlib and Aleppo in the inner plateaux of
northern Syria, semi-arid areas characterized by the scarcity,
inadequacy and irregularity of water-sources in a steppe-like
Mediterranean microclimate, the supplying and storage of
water has been one ofthe mostancient and spectacular aims of
human settlements since earliest times. It is with the aim of
finding a regular water supply independent of capricious rainfall that the inhabitants of these regions carry out hydraulic
undertakings ranging from dams to cistern-wells, and have
tapped the underground water-tables. The majority of these
natural reservoirs, some of which are particularly rich, holding
several million cubic metres of water, are connected with the
surface in each village by means of cistern-well (bire-jam’a).
This is a cylindrical shaft dug deep into the soil and lined with

483

## S. 192

© Ghiyas Allundı

1.v1

SERVICES

dry stones; it measures 1.2 m (4 ft) in diameter by ı5 m (50 ft)
sometimes more, in depth, and passes down through the alluvion layers until reaching the impermeable aquiferous stratum.
The conception and execution of the well’s coning is carried
outbyexperienced village craftsmen, deep down into the soil in
symbolically important sites that are easily accessible with neither enclosure nor stele to mark the site. Thewomen come here
both morning and evening, equipped with their large jars, to
draw the water needed for the upkeep of their household.
The well thus becomes one of the privileged meeting points
in village social life, water being collective property and the
centre of feminine life, where women can gather away from
masculine glances to converse and express themselves without
inhibition.

The water store of the cistern-well is renewed each year by
the winter precipitations, thanks to a harnessing encatchment
network that favours infiltrations near utility points; it comprises canals, infiltration ditches, and absorbing excavations
dugjudiciously within the feeding zone ofthe cistern across the
main circulation axes inside and outside thevillage. Theannual
replenishment ofthe water reserve is generally weak compared
with the large storage capacity of the cistern-well, yet it is
always the natural supply that reconstitutes the layer and guarantees its long-term upkeep. Only during years of continuous
droughtresulting in a critical drop in the water level, do the villagers intervene to safeguard their daily need ofwater by going
to draw some from favoured areas in the banks ofthe Orontes
or the Euphrates, which they transport home in cistern-lorries

and pour into the local eistern-well. GHIYAS ALJUNDI

484

1.V15.d Irrigation

Essential to life, water has to be brought to, or be
within reach of, every human dwelling, and as itis necessary for
cultivation, methods of raising water for irrigation are important. Settlements are frequently located near streams or rivers
for this purpose and many methods of raising water have been
invented and employed. Among the oldest and most widely
used is the well-sweep; a variant is the water-scoop which is
swung from a tripod, both giving some mechanical advantage
for the single operator.

Where constant flowing water was not easily available but a
continuous supply of water was required, alternative methods
were devised. Forms of pedal-operated water-lifting devices were
developed in China, including the ‘endless chain’ system. The
‘chain’ consisted of a series of wooden links, to each ofwhich
an inclined wooden paddle was attached. A blindfolded draught
animal walked in a circle, revolving a large-toothed wheel,
which in turn drove the top axle over which the chain turned.
The chain travelled in a trough ofthe top level ofthe apparatus,
the paddles each drawing a quantity in the lower trough on the
return, discharging into an irrigation channel. The same system
could be operated manually with hand cranks, by one or two
men. Avarianthadawooden frameatthetop end which spanned
the upper axle as it turned on low forked supports. With steps
fitted on the axle the apparatus could be turned by the footpower ofmen standing on the frame on either side ofthe chain.

The screw principle is also used to raise water. A continuous
wooden blade is turned in a spiralabouta barrel which in turn is
attached to a shaft. The apparatus, which may be 2-3 m (6.5-10
fi) in length, is encased in a wooden tube. It may be bound with
rope to ensure closeness of fit, though not so close as to hinder
the revolution ofthe screw mechanism. With one end socketed
into a post standing in a stream, and the other raised on the
bank and similarly socketed, the ‘Archimedes screw’ is operated
atan angle ofapproximately 20° by turning the upper end ofthe
shaft by hand or feet. The Archimedes screw was employed in
Aden until the 1950s and is still in use in Egypt.

Well water may be used for irrigation, but in order to maintain a steady supply some farmers found it necessary to overcome the waiting intervals between obtaining quantities of
water. One method used in China probably derives from raising
earth while well-digging, a windlass with cranked handles cut
from distorted branches being erected on shear legs over the
wellopening. Bamboo frames divide the wooden axle, with one
bucket attached to a rope which is coiled around it in one section, while another bucket is lowered to the bottom oftthe well
onasecond length ofrope attached to the axle. As the windlass
was turned, the rope attached to the top bucket was unwound,
lowering it into the well, while thatattached to the second, lowered bucket was wound on the axle, drawing it to the surface.
Another, less frequently used type is animal-powered. A continuous rope is attached ateach end to a bucket, oneraised, one
lowered. The rope passes over two pulleys between which a
horizontal crank is rotated by the encircling of the animal, to
raise and lower the buckets alternately.

Raising water by the use ofwatermills was extensively applied
to the drainage ofland in The Netherlands, and in regions where

mills used for

Village cistern-well,
innerplateauof
northern Syria,

Dutch expertise was soughtor emulated, as in the Anglia region
ofthe British Isles. The same principles were applied to the lifting of water for irrigation purposes. During the period of the
development of the Prairie Plains in the United States in the

1870s windmills were employed for the raising of water. The
majority ofthese were annular, multibladed mills with selfregulating mechanisms and could be boughtand delivered cheaply.
Among the first was the Halliday Standard Mill manufactured
in Connecticutand used by United States Forts and garrisons in
the Plains and West regions. A wide variety of ‘wind engines’
was marketed as improvements were made, the 72-blade Eclipse
being among the most popular. Though not vernacular mills,
many were imitated, adapted, improved or repaired by farmers
and local smiths, while the supporting structures were frequently self-built. Annular mills that are raised on locally built
vernacular base structures and pump-houses are still extensively used in the Mediterranean, notably in the central plains

ofMajorca and Crete. PAUL OLIVER

Water Supply 1.1.5

1V\.5.e MNoria

In the Arab Islamic world, the Mediterranean coastland and some parts ofIran, Mesopotamia and Asia are the only
regions which have sufficient rainfall to supply the domestic,
industrial and irrigation needs. Everywhere else people have
had to use other means of raising and drawing water. Among
them the noria system is the only one which is efficient enough
for alarge population.

In English as well as in French the word noria refers to any kind
of machine which raises water through a rotating movement.
The word has been borrowed from Spanish which, however,
makes a distinction between the noria desangreand the noria de corriente, the former refers to machines driven by animal power
whereas the latter defines machines driven by water. Both types
are called noria (na’ura) in Arabic-speaking countries, each region
also having its own terms to designate these raising-machines,
whether in a generic way or according to the type of machine: for
example, the Persian düllab (wheel), the Egyptian sagiya.

The animal-powered noria is composed oftwo wheels; one is
horizontal and driven by a camel or an ox, the other is vertical
and geared into the first one. A chain of buckets connected to
the vertical cog-wheel raises water from a well or a canal. In
Egypt where this type ofnoria is still used water can be raised up
to 1.25 l/sec (0.33 gall/sec) as high as ro m (33ft) or moreabove the
water-table. This kind ofsystem has existed throughoutthe Mediterranean region and in Asia since atleastthe zrd century zc.

Other more complicated machines have been achieved with the
same principle of gearing wheels, an example of which is the
13th-century noria to be found in Damascus nextto the Moyyedine
mosque: a paddle-wheel driven by water is geared into a vertical
axle several metres high which carries another wheel mounted on
ahorizontal axle; this carries a chain ofbuckets which draw water
from a channel and carry it far above the level ofthe banks.

The water-driven noria is composed of only one wheel
mounted vertically in frontofan aqueductso thatthe lower part
of the wheel is submerged. The wheel, provided with radial
paddles, is turned by the impact of the water on the paddles.
The rim of the wheel is also provided with a chain of buckets

485

## S. 193

5
5

1.v1 SERVICES

which draw water in their course below, raise it to the banks
and discharge it into the channel ofthe aqueduct.

This elegantsystem can only be built in an abundantriver with
a regular flow and in cases of irrigation the banks of the river
should not be overly steep.

Such machines existed in Asia, Iran, Mesopotamia, Turkey,
Morocco, Spain and most of all perhaps in Syria, on the River
Orontes. From each part of the city of Hamäh there are more
than 100 sites ofnorias. Among the numerous norias which still
exist the diameter of the largest one is more than 20 m (65 ft).
Some of them can raise more than 50 I/sec (13 gall/sec) thus
enabling an irrigation of 25 ha (62 acres) of gardens. It seems
that methods ofmaking, maintainingand using norias have not
changed in more than a thousand years.

A mosaic of AD 369 discovered in the ancient city of Afamia,
on the Orontes, shows a noria and constitutes the oldest historical evidence oftheir existence. The principle ofsuch machines
had been described bya Roman engineer in the ıst century BC.
Nevertheless, the question of the origins of such a technique
remains a puzzle. All that is known is that it was developed in
an arid but productive region, next to an abundant and constantly flowing river. GERARD ROBINE

1V1.5.e-i1 Moria: wind-powered

A variant of the Spanish noria is the noria de viento
which applies the drive principle ofthe windmill to the waterraising mechanism. The small farms of Murcia in southeastern
Spain often had two windmills, oneto grind cornand oneadapted

486

to raise water. Built between the ı6th and ıgth centuries this
type of windmill relates to that of the insular Mediterranean
and was probably Arabic in origin, though the early history of
mills in the region is problematic.

Beneath the undulating Murcian plain the water-tableistapped
to fillthe cisterns, mounted with domed well-heads, which supply the water for irrigation and for farm use. The basic principle
employed was that of the Cycladic and Balearic islands with a
revolving cap supportingan inclined windshaft. Triangular cloth
sails are reefed on eightthin stocks, inserted into the windshaft;
three-dimensional rigging between a bowspritand the circle of
sails enables the structure to respond to wind forces while being
keptin tension. The technology is maritime, relatingto thatofthe
small boats that plied between the Balearics and the mainland.

Formerly the rigging was ofropes, but metal rods were introduced, probably in the ıgth century, in place ofthe ropes, with
S-shaped links or narrow iron boxes with screw-threads permitting the tension to be adjusted. Reefing was made more
practical by mounting the rubble stone tower on a stone plinth,
from which the miller turned the cap by means ofa tail spar. A
thatched roof or cone ofthin planks lightened the cap while its
revolving rim was kept greased. As the sails could be turned
into the wind without affecting the drive mechanism, water
could be raised almost continuously in the breezes blowing
from the Mediterranean or the coastal lake ofthe Mar Menor.

Within the tower, a conventional toothed brake-wheel turns
a large wooden crown-wheel which drives a vertical shaft and,
by a spur-wheel and cog, a horizontal axle through the tower
wall to an external pit-wheel. The pit-wheel is ofthe noria type,
and drives a chain with buckets attached, which empty into an
inclined channel, orleat, feeding the cistern. Though the majority of norias de vientos simply served a cistern some fed a more
complex system. The wind-tower was set above one cistern with
an overflow running into the irrigation channels that were fed
ftomasecond, sluice-controlled cistern filled by the leatfrom the
noria. These efficient passive-energy water norias declined in use
in the mid-2oth century with the introduction ofelectric pumps.

PAUL OLIVER

1v1.5f Oasis (Augila)

The Sahara, covering abouta third ofthe total surface
of Africa, has been inhabited since prehistoric time. Despite a
harsh climate and definite limits ofextension and local resources,

the desert oases are some ofthe oldest continuously inhabited
places ofthe world. Throughout their existence, the societies of
the desert have assimilated externally derived features such as
various subsistence techniques and cultural elements. As nodal
points on the traffic routes, they have been subjected to alien
exploitation. The Romans used the oases as way stations on
their Sahara trade routes. The Arabs set in motion an effective
pattern of co-operation between themselves and the sedentary
people ofthe oases, a mostly peaceful and symbiotic exchange.

Augila is an oasis housing three settlements in the desert of
Cyrenaica, located at a1°17'E, 29°05'N, 220 km (136 mi) southeast ofthe GulfofSirte. The main village is Al Bilad. The oasis
lies at the point where major east-west (from Al Jaghbub to
Marädah) and north-south (from the Mediterranean coastto Al

Kufrah) caravan routes intersected. Cold western and northern
winds prevail in winter while the southern summer winds are
E hot. Temperature fluctuations between day and night are extreme. In the early 1980s, there were over 30 000 palms and nearly
10 000 trees of other xerophytic species. The shrub and grass
cover consists of salt-tolerant and drought-resistant species.

Trapped beneath the oasis is a water supply critical to life as
surface water is non-existentowingto amean annual rainfall of
only 9 mm (0.35 in). Periods of up to two years without rain
have been recorded. At roughly five-year intervals sudden, daily
downpours in excess of 20 mm (0.8 in) are experienced. The
boundary ofthe vegetated area coincides with the 35 m (115 ft)
contour, below which the roots of natural species can tap the
water-table, comprisinganareaof85 sq km (33 sq mi). Augila’s
water supply was traditionally extracted from wells 7-13 m
(23-43 ft) deep and lined with palm branch stumps around the
entire circumference ofthe well from top to bottom.

The cultivable land consists ofcoarse sands and sandy loams
which absorb and retain water quite efhiciently. Productive trees
provided the traditional economic lifeline and their protection
imposed an absolute physical constraint on the location of
urban development. There are two major growing seasons. The
subsistence economy of specialized horticulture and aboriculture is based on garden and palm tree produce, respectively, in
permanently maintained plots and gardens. Areas of intensive
palm tree plantations surround the three nodal settlements.
Roughly one halfwas productive in the early 1980s.

1V15.f-1 Oasis: irrigated (Cuyo, Argentine)

Since its foundation by Spaniards in the middle ofthe
16th century, Mendoza’s development has been strongly conditioned by two factors inherentin the natural physical background:
the aridity in a desertic environment, and the high seismicity of
theregion.

Human settlements have been linked, since pre-Spanish
times, to the presence of water. The region’s economy, based
on agriculture and the distribution ofthe population is highly
dependent.on artificial irrigation. This has determined the conformation of ‘irrigated oasis’ ecosystems, using and systematizing hydric nets fed by rivers. In the case of Mendoza these
oases occupy a minimum part (4 per cent) ofthe province’s surface where 97 per cent ofthe population is concentrated.

The Hispanic city was founded in 1561 on an indigenous
Huarpe agricultural settlement, that had systematized the use
of the water coming from a branch ofthe Mendoza river. The
rigid orthogonal trace was conditioned from the beginningand
maintained through its consequent evolution to the hydric net.

Water constituted a structural element in the oasis organization. The evolution ofthe city evidenced a strong subjection to a
primary net, organized in the pre-Hispanic times from the use
and systematization ofthe natural water courses in relation to
the topography and the slopes. This hydric matrix conditioned
thelocation ofthe Spanish cityand the structural plotting ofthe
territory: the streets, theroads, theurban and rural cadastreand
the location of buildings and cultivated areas in the flat plains.

This meant that the city had quite a rural appearance due to
the low density, the presence of many orchards, vineyards and
cultivated fields; the surrounding agricultural land, organized
by the irrigation net, had buildings as good as those ofthe city.

The vernacular architecture has been generally characterized
by two factors:the prevailing developmentofunbaked mudarchitecture, and the low, one-storey buildings. The available materials ofan arid environment were mud, reeds, found by rivers and
lagoons, and very scarce wood. The lack of wood heavily influenced the building traditions up to the ıgth century. In particular, this lack made it impossible to install brick kilns that used
wood as fuel. Instead adobe was used for curious roofings such
as the mud dome, with or without the supporting framework.

487

1961 onıIo Inedo

## S. 194

1 SERVICES

The consolidation ofthe agricultural oasis, the availability of
wood from the crops (fruitand olive trees) and the introduction
of some foreign species that rapidly spread, such as the poplar
tree (Populus alba), gave way to important modifications in ıgthcentury architecture. Buildings became progressively lighter
and modulated. Columns and breastsummers appeared in the
traditional galleries (semi-open spaces as extensions ofinhabited locales), instead ofthe solid pillars with arcades of previous
years. Dome covers and roofs with a marked pitch disappeared.

SILVIA A.CIRVINI
1Vv.5g Qanat
A ganat is a gently sloping tunnel which taps the
water ofan underground aquifer and channels itto a settlement
where water is scarce. This water, depending on its quality and
taste, may be used for drinking, cooking, washing, milling,
watering animals, and irrigating crops and gardens. Groundwater can either occur in sedimentary or igneous rock aquifers
where itaccumulates as aresultofthe downward percolation of
seasonal rainfall or other surface water. Qanats are a special feature ofthe Persian plateau where they are commonlyassociated
with alluvial fans at the foot of the mountain ranges that surround the desert ofthe central plateau.

The earliest recorded ganat was built by the Assyrian king,
Sennacherib (705-681 sc) and still carries water to Erbil in northern Iraq. His father, Sargon II, claimed to have learnt the secret
oftapping underground water in his campaign against Uratu in
Turkey. With the establishment of the Persian Achaemenid
empire ganats appear to have spread westwards to the Mediterranean and Egypt, and southwards to Oman and southern Arabia. The second major diffusion of ganats took place in the early
centuries of Islam when they were introduced by the Umayids
into Spain and thence to Marrakech (where they were called
khattaras or rhettaras), the Canary Islands, Mexico and Chile. In
the roth century they spread to southern Algeria (called foggara
or fakkara). They are also found in Cyprus and other areas ofthe
Middle East, such as Afghanistan (kariz), China, Korea (mannun-poo) and Japan (mambo) where they were introduced as late
as the 17th century.

Qanats in Iran are built by specialists mugannis whose knowledge and excayation skills are largely passed on from father to
son. The first well to be dug is a trial well to prove the presence,
depth and characteristics of the water-table. If conditions are
suitable and the slope between the water source, and the settlementis nottoo steep, which would lead to erosion ofthetunnel
walls, this well becomes the ‘mother well’, and work will commence on the rest ofthe tunnel system, starting usually from its
lower end. The tunnel is constructed from a series of vertical
shafts typically spaced 20-150 m (65-490 ft) apart from which
the tunnel can be dug either up or down to the next shaft. The
tunnel generally measures about 120 cm (4 ft) high by 80-100
cm (2.5-3 ft) wide, and is usually unlined but if it passes
through soft soil it may be lined with baked clay hoops.

Key factors that influence the discharge of ganats are the
depth of the mother well, and the length of the ganat tunnel.
Mother wells in Iran are generally less than 50 m (165 ft) deep
although one in northern Tehran is some 150 m (490 ft) deep,
and one atGunabad in eastern Persia is said to be325 m (1070 ft)

488

deep. Beaumont’s analysis (Beaumont 1989) of over 2000
Iranian ganats showed that 36 per cent of ganats were 0.5-2 km
(0.3-1.25 mi) long and 81 per cent were less than 5 km (3 mi),
although some are known to be over 50 km (30 mi) long. The
rate of water discharge can vary from o-300 cu m/hr (0-10 600
cu f/hr) and Beaumont found that 66 per cent produced less
than 60 cu m/hr (2120 cu ft/hr) while 45 per cent produced less
than 30 cu m/hr (1060 cu ft/hr). Qanats have seasonal variations in
the amountofwater they produce with high flows coincidingwith
high rainfall periods and low flows with low rainfall periods.
There are also longer periods of flow variation that coincide with
cycles ofwetand dry years. The future of ganat systems is being
increasinglyjeopardized by a decrease in the availability ofskilled
labour to build them, changing patterns ofland ownership, their
very high initial capital costs, and the practice of mechanical
deep pumping of aquifers that is increasingly resulting in the
dıying up of mother wells on the Persian plateau today.

SUSAN ROAF
1.V1.5.g-i Foggara (Maghreb)

A North African form ofthe ganat, the foggara is an
underground channel system which carries water from many
well shafts to converge on principal channels that supply towns
in the Algerian desert. An immense underground reservoir, the
Albien lies ata great depth, between 700 m (2300 ft) and 2000 m
(6560 ft) below the Sahara desert. Though itis fartoo deep to be
raised without advanced mechanical means, the rock galleries
and ground-water from subterranean water-bearing layers of
rock of the hamada have been tapped for centuries by the construction ofthe foggaras.

Specialists ofthe Iranian Barmakid tribe were brought by the
Arabs with the conquest of North Africa in the gth century, to
build the foggaras. The system was introduced throughout Maghreb, and they are known to have been constructed in Marrakech
by aD 1078. Elsewhere in southern Morocco in the oases of
Ouarglaand Tefilaletand in the Atlas mountains are significant
complexes, but the most important are below the Tademait
plateau in southern Algeria, in the vicinity ofthe oases of Touat
and the town of Timimoun. Because the sands oftheGrand Erg
actas a natural filter removing saltand contaminants, the water
from this region is considered to be ofthe purest quality.

the central
plateau.
