There are some 4000 km (2500 mi) of the so-called “fingers
oflight’, though it has been recorded thatittook four Barmakid
men a year to tunnel ı km (0.6 mi). Ventilation shafts were
bored from the surface to the tunnel at intervals ofı2 m (40 fi),
the opening shaded and protected from drifting sand by encircling walls. Though the quantity of water gathered by a single
tunnel was relatively small the convergence of the tunnels produced a flow which emerges at the town of Timimoun in an
open basin. Here the water was divided and rechannelled by the
members ofthe Barmaka tribe, believed to be the direct descendants of the Barmakid and still custodians of the system. A
number of bench-high sandstone bridge-dams (wesseria) were
used to redirect the flow of water, stone sluices being removed
to regulate the quantity delivered to the areas to be irrigated. A
main stream (seguia) flowed from a gap in the wesseria to provide
the main water supply of the town to all who needed it. After
feeding an open laundry it was diverted to the oasis gardens.

By the 1960s the foggara system was in decline with many of
the tunnels blocked, and the oases suffering in consequence.
Reduction in the annual rainfall lowered the ground-water
table, resulting in dying palm grovesand advaneingsand dunes,
which threatened the oases and choked the ventilation shafts.
But a further contributory factor was the emancipation of the
Barmaka by the French, early in the 2oth century. Formerly
slaves, the Barmaka were now able to offer their labour for hire.

Inadvertently, the French abolition of slavery contributed to
the deterioration ofthe foggara. The wealthier landowners were
able to meet the new charges and to purchase shares in a syndicate, formed to run the system. But the poor farmers, unable to
meet the costs, gradually abandoned the oases, hastening the
decline and increasing the expenses for the syndicate members.
Nevertheless, the management of the foggaras continued with
the shareholders payingrental into a fund (gherafsa) forits maintenance. In 1939 Symons reported thattheunitofmeasurement,
the temen, equivalent to 2.5 l/min (0.7 galllmin), was valued at
500 francs per year. Valuation ofthe water was the responsibility
ofthe water measurer (kial-el-ma) who used a brass plate (louah)
pierced with holes, to dam the flow ofthe seguia in order to quantify and value the payment owed by each user. The foggara continues in use butiits future remains uncertain. PAUL OLIVER

Water Supply 1.1.5

1.V1.5.h Spring house (North America)

Constructed in well-watered settlements that are
subject to seasonal high temperatures, the spring house is
designed to keep food cool. Widespread in Pennsylvania and the
South ofthe United States the spring house was an important
service building for the farmhouse. Essential to its functioning
was the steady flow of fresh water from a natural fountain or
spring. The water entered the spring house at one side, and in
the simplest structures, was channelled through a trough with
aslight gradient to exit on the downside ofthe house. In larger
buildings the trough was taken round three sides of the interior, either as a masonry channel embedded in the floor, or,
where possible, raised on a plinth or other supports above knee
height. Pottery crocks or pans containing milk and other dairy
products were stood in the troughs to make efficient use ofthe
evaporative cooling effect ofthe flowing water.

As the spring water came directly from the ground in many
cases it was necessary to ensure that the source was uncontaminated and the channel in which it flowed was free of decaying
vegetable matter or any obstructions. Unless a sluice board was
fitted, which was unusual, the flow could not be regulated so at
times the floor of the spring house was covered with water,
requiring the laying of a base of stone or brick, or of cement
when it was available. There was still a considerable risk of
deterioration at floor level, so masonry spring houses were preferred. In the Appalachians even so, notched log spring houses
with dugout troughs, were not uncommon. Unchinked, they
were well ventilated. This was important and grilles or louvres
were fitted close to the ceiling or eaves, often on opposite sides
of the house to facilitate cross-ventilation. They had to be
sufficiently close-fitted to ensure that birds did not enter the
spring house, while the entıy and exit points ofthe water flow
had to be protected with mesh to deter rodents.

Frequently the spring house was built over a stream, with
access from either bank and the water flowing across the plan.
A situation where spring water emerged from a hillslope was
preferred as this permitted the water to be channelled at a
height convenient for handling the pottery cooling vessels.
Some spring houses were two-storeyed, the upper level being
used for churning butter. Others doubled as wash-houses or as
root cellars. As they were generally substantial structures many
were adapted as pump-houses when refrigerators were introduced on the farms.

o
e

## S. 195

© coll. Cheryi Perko

1.v1 SERVICES

Production ofrefrigerators commenced during World War],

but by the early-ı920s there were still only 20 ooo in the entire
United States. As electrification ofthe South was not extensive
untilthe 1930s, in manyrural areas refrigerators did notreplace
the spring houses untiltheendofthedecade. PAUL OLIVER
1.V1.5.i Stepwell (India, nw)
Located in the hot, dry northwest of India, the stepwell (vav, baoli, vapi, or kuo) is an underground structure that is
open to the sky and consists ofa path of steps descending to a
well. Found primarily in the state of Gujarat, these structures
were built within a village, often adjacent to a temple complex,
or along a pilgrimage or trade route. They provide water for
domestic purposes and religious rituals while offering temporary shelter from daytime temperatures that can exceed 45 °C
(113 °P). (The same region is also home to the related bathing
structures known as ghats and kunds.) When geologic conditions allow, stepwells can also act as reservoirs, storing monsoon rain within their lower storeys. Their depth varies from
two to seven storeys below ground, depending on the level of
the water-table ateach site.

The simplest method of creating a stepwell is to carve it from
rock. The earliest known example ofa stepwell, Naughan Kuo,
was sculpted out of soft rock near the town ofJunagadh, in the
region ofSaurashtra, between AD 200 and 400. A flight ofsteps
descends 8 m (26 ft) below ground to an open courtyard
equipped with built-in bathing structures and benches. A second set of stairs wraps clockwise around a square well shaft
and descends steeply 52 m (171 ft) to the water. Brackets integrated into the top ofthe well shaft, typical of most wells, were
used to hoist water to the courtyard or to ground level. The
nearby Adi Chadi vav, attributed to the roth century, isassimpler
example ofa rock-cut stepwell. This utilitarian structure, con-

490

sisting of little more than a flight of narrow steps leading
directly to a deep well, is typical of the linear form from which
the stepwell typology evolved.

Beginning in the 7th century, most stepwells were constructed of cut stone or brick rather than simply being hollowed out
of rock. To counteract the lateral thrust ofthe earth, constructed stepwells required the use ofbattered side walls or horizontal retaining members at each floor level. These horizontal
members evolved into a sophisticated form of climate control,
interspersing shady pavilions with open shafts of light. The
surfaces ofthe stepwells, often elaborately carved, break upthe
harsh sunlight and scatter sound, softening the light and minimizing echoes.

Using geometric proportioning systems ftom classic Hindu
texts on architecture such as the Shilpa-Shastra, stepwells were
built in a wide variety of shapes: linear, L-shaped, T-shaped,
cross-shaped or spiral, with square, circular or octagonal well
shafts. Later stepwells, such as the ısth-century Rudabai stepwell in the village of Adalaj, were built with two wells, one for
secular purposes and another for religious ceremonies.

The stepwell as a source of water, the provider oflife according to Hindu belief, is often associated with women and
goddess worship. Many stepwells, such as Mata bhavani in Asapuri, were named after Mata, the mother goddess. Others, such
as Rani vav in Patan, were built in honour of famous women.
Religious ceremonies associated with fertility (both in terms of
progeny and bountiful harvest) and cleansing or purification
were conducted at stepwells.

After the Muslim invasions ofthelate-ı4th and ısth centuries,
some stepwells began to be patronized by Muslim rulers.
Although many Muslim-sponsored stepwells continued to be
built on the basis of Hindu building traditions, they were built
more for utilitarian and pleasurable purposes. Many took on
the character ofcool, underground retreats, often equipped with
individual relaxation chambers, such as Sodali in Mehmadabad.

Today, many stepwells have been abandoned and have fallen
into disrepair as the wells thatserved them have dried up. Others,
however, continue to be used by villagers as sources for water or
places for worship. While hundreds ofstepwells have been documented, no new stepwell is known to have been built for sixty
years. The government of India has begun to restore several of
the most prominent ofthe older stepwells, including Rani vav
and Rudabai stepwell. CHERYL PERKO
1M.5.) Tankhouse (California)

Atankhouse is a walled watertower most commonly
associated with farmhouses in California and other parts ofthe
USA. In about 1865, California farmers began placing water
storage tanks on platforms elevated some 5-7 m (16-23 ft)
above ground. The elevated water tank was the key part ofa
gravity-pressure water supply system for the kitchen and garden
made practical by the American self-regulating windmill and
improved water pumps popular in California. It was also an
economical solution to acute water shortages during California’s long, drysummers, while unthreatened byicedamageinthe
mild winters. To improve the tower’s looks and usefulness most
farmers enclosed it with walls, thus creating rooms beneath the

See also
2.1.6 India, Northwestand‘
Indus

References h
Burgess, James,1874-3,
1900,1905
Herdeg, Klaus,1991
Jain, Kulbhushan, 1985
Jain-Neubauer, Jutta,19
Kagal, Carmen (ed.
Vora, Kartik,1982

Plan and section
stepwellat thevi h
Adalaj, sujarat, Int

lest and Pacific

ÜWertanklfornia, United

platform useful for miscellaneous storage, extra bedrooms or
laundry space.

The tower was built of heavy timbers sufficient to buttress
the weight ofa 7000-36 000 | (T800-9400 gal) tanks. The main
load-bearing supports typically consisted of four or more
upright posts 15 x 15 cm (6x 6 in), orIox 15 cm (4% 6 in) in
girth. Lighter studding and crossbraces were usually added in
the spaces between the heavy posts; their placement affected
the positioning of doors and windows in the tankhouse. The
tank platform was also of sturdy construction, typically consisting of a plank floor nailed to two ı5 cm (6 in) joists placed
edge-up, spaced abouto.5 m (1.5 ft) apartand attached to heavy
horizontal members fastened along the top ofthe tower posts.

Asthewalled enclosure ofa watertower, the tall, narrow shape
ofthe tankhouse was distinctive from all other farm structures,
and because builders could choose from several construction
options, tankhouses in all districts exhibited a variety of types
and styles. Tapered tower types were achieved by positioning
the top of the support posts inward, producing a tower whose
top dimensions were smaller than those at ground level. Some
tapered tankhouses had tankrooms with a smaller or identical
diameter as the top of the tower. But because tapered towers
could support heavy tanks, tankrooms at the top of tapered
towers usually had diameters larger than that of the tower
below. Tapered tower tankhouses were especially popular in
Northern California’s wine-grape growing districts (Pitman,
1992). Straight tower tankhouses, constructed by placing the
support posts exactly vertical, predominated in the San Joaquin
Valley. Differences among straight tower types were notable

Water Supply 1.1.5
when the diameter ofthe tankroom varied from that ofits supporting tower. Butmost straight tower tankhouses were walled
from ground to eaves as a single unit, giving them a box-like
look.

Variety in tankhouse style was also affected by options in roof
construction, building materials, height and architectural
embellishments (Pitman, 1976). Most had hipped roofs that
sloped upwards at low angles from all sides, but gable roofs
and steep pyramidal styles were also common. Tankhouses typically had three floors including the tankroom, but four floors
were not uncommon. Though most tankhouses were completely detached from the adjacent farmhouse, their close association with the household economy was often indicated by
their resemblance to the farmhouse in building materials,
colour and outer stylistic treatments. Sometimes tankhouses
were attached to the farmhouse or incorporated into the floor
plan of the farmhouse, a practice made possible in the early-
2oth century when new pumps energized by electricity or petrol
replaced windmills.

By 1940 the water storage purpose of the tankhouse had
become obsolete as most farmers had acquired air-pressured
water tanks placed at ground level or buried. But because
tankhouses remained useful for household storage they were
preserved long after their water supply role ceased. In the 1ggos
many tankhouses can still be seen in rural California, and with
lesser frequency in other regions ofthe American Pacific coast
and in the Great Plains (Kent). LEON S. PITMAN

491

## S. 196

5

1.v1 SERVICES

1.V1.5.k Wash-house

Attitudes to personal hygiene, to bodily cleanliness
and to wearing ofsoiled or clean apparel vary greatly in different
societies, and are expressed in the nature ofthe facilities thatare
constructed to meetthe demands ofthese functions.

In farming communities in Europe washing clothes was far
from being a frequent custom. Even in countries where peasants starched and pressed costumes for ceremonialand special
events, washing of the clothes might take place only at long
intervals. ‘The big washing days that were held usually twice a
year’ could be held with more frequency in a separate washhouse, which was equipped with a boiler, washtubs, washboards and ironing tables for pressing (Gschwend et al.). This
form of wash-house was attached to the larger or more prosperous farms.

Washing days were similar in Brittany, where ‘clothes were
washed only infrequently, which helps to explain why even very
poor people often had large numbers of shirts, inventories
often listing two or three dozen per person. In the pays bigouden,
even as late as the 1920s, washing was only done twice a year, in
spring and autumn, and dirty clothes lay in a heap for six
months’ (Meirion-Jones). Notwithstanding this uneconomic
solution to the realities of soiled clothing, the Breton city of
Vannes possessed one ofthe finest of communal wash-houses
(lavoirs) in northern France, which was still in use in the middle
ofthe 2oth century.

Community wash-houses are found in many regions, but particularly in southern European, Mediterranean and some Latin
American countries where the laundering of clothes was also a
social occasion for the exchange of news and local gossip.
Instances have been reported from Mexico oflocal resentment at
the installation ofpiped water in homes that has deprived women
ofthe social intercourse atthe village well or laundry (Foster).

Washing ofclothes can take place in ayard, byariver orin the
vicinity ofa well; a shelter is notalways necessary, though itcan

protect the women from the sun on their backs, or from strong
winds and rain. From the Ardennes to the Greek islands, many
wash-houses are covered, though they are open on at least two
sides, to permit a breeze to flow through the structure and to
reduce heatand humidity. Customarily, the wash-house is built
close to a spring and on a slight incline. Fresh water may be
directed from a fountain, entering the wash-house by way ofa
Pipe or opening in the wall, from which it falls into asink, ora
channel which directs the flow to large stone or cement basins.

Where cold water only is used for washing this may require
that the clothes be beaten or scrubbed against a stone surface,
which may be roughened or corrugated. Lye soap, made from
tallow with the addition of lye ash, was formerly used to dispose of the dirt though it made hands red and raw; later carbolic and household soaps replaced the rural product. If hot
water is used, large cauldrons may be installed above crude
stoves, from which wooden or zinc tubs may be filled. In either
case, basins for soapy water and for clean rinsing water may be
built in, the overflow ofused water being channelled through a
floor of cobbles or stone setts, to be dispersed at a distance
from the wash-house. Nearby lines may be used for the dıying
of clothes, though in the Mediterranean regions the spreading
ofthe washing over a sunny slope or wall is common. Sufhicient
space must be provided within the wash-house for the sorting
ofclothes, for laundry baskets and pitchers, and for freedom of
movement in the washing operations. Though there is considerable variety in their form and internal arrangements, the
houses are often approximately 8 m x 5 m (26 ftx 16 ft), with
the basins in line in the centre, affording the laundresses freedom ofaccess to the exterior.

With the introduction of piped water to houses and farms,
the use of community wash-houses generally declined in the
second half of the 2oth century, though in more prosperous
areas the launderette retained some ofthe social function.

PAUL OLIVER
1V1.5.1 Waterwheels (Iraq)
The Euphrates river valley in its northwesterly course
towards the Iragi/Syrian borders, has always been characterized by the use ofwaterwheels. The river, which for millenia has
carved a deep valley into the desert plateau, lies below the surrounding lands and renders irrigation, without the use ofsuch
mechanical devices, impossible. For centuries, waterwheels have
been associated with the towns of ‘Änah, Räwah, Al Hanitha
and Hit. Their impact on these towns is not only visual but
extends to the functional organization ofthe town fabric and its
movement network. “Änah alone is recorded to have had 63
waterwheels, 36 ofwhich were present in the early 1980s.

Waterwheel irrigation relies on two main components; the
wheel itself, and the various walls associated with it. The waterwheel is made oftimber, mainly thick tree branches, in a fashion very similar to a carriage wheel. It is sandwiched between
two masonry walls one of which supports it and the other
receives its discharge. Attached to the wheel’s outer circumference are a series offired clay containers which scoop the water
from the river and transfer it to the required level. The wheel
which is partially submerged in the river, is positioned at an

See also

1.1X.7.e-il Horse
laundry

2.1.5 Galllc

References
Foster, George

Papaioannou, Kt
5.1983

Wash-houses®
inuse uptO ws
Brittany, FraNE®"

"Vence, France.

Meters

angle to the direction ofthe water flow in the river. Three components complement the wheel’s operation, the first of which
is amasonry wall (juriyah) positioned in the river to supportthe
waterwheel. The second masonry wall lies parallel to the first
and carries an open channel which receives the water discharged by the wheel. The water carried by the channel is
deposited at the farthest, often the highest, point in the land to
be irrigated. From this point the water flows down in the direction ofthe river through a network of open troughs irrigating
orchards and arable lands. The two walls that sandwich the
wheel are over ı m (3 ft) thick, built ofstone and lime-mortar,
the lime being rresistant to the damaging effect ofwater humidity. The third component comprises a low stone wall (sukur)
which lies in the river and is constructed to directthe low ofthe
water current towards the waterwheel.

The dimensions ofthe waterwheel depend on the size ofthe
land to be irrigated and its elevation above the river level. The
waterwheels ofthe upper Euphrates are generally smaller than
those found further up theriverin Hamäh andare notas solidly
constructed. The average height of the aqueduct ranges from
8 m (26 ft) to 12 m (39 ft). The water discharge for awheelg m
(30 fi) in diameter is approximately 142 l/min (37 gall/min).
Because of its limited capacity, it is not uncommon to find

Water Supply 1.V1.5
several waterwheels serving a large area. Waterwheels are constructed in the late summer when the water level in the river is
atits lowest. The process ofconstruction isateam effortinvolving the co-operation of family and neighbours.

Although some waterwheels are still operated, the introduction of fuel-operated water pumps, and a general decline in
agriculture, has led to the gradual abandonment of this traditional method ofirrigation. JALA MAKHZOUMI

1.V1.5.m Well (Mediterranean)

Wells are vertical excavations that give access to a
water-table. Water is drawn out either with buckets by hand, by
a windlass or by a pendulum system, or with a more or less
elaborated pump. The well is dug with a pick in relatively soft
soils; use ofexplosives is mostrare and recent. The well-digger
consolidates the excavation wall with beams as he gets lower
and lines it with stone or brickwork according to the region.
When the construction is finished, the timber formwork is
removed. The well is generally surmounted by a low wall and
frequently by a superstructure in the form ofa small cabin or
porch-roof which is always in accord with local architecture.

Also included in the class of wells are built structures that
allow access to localized water pockets, lying at shallow depths
and depending on rainfall. These structures generally have the
exterior aspectofa well butcan also be simple access disposals:
ramps or steps. Structures that trap surface water are even simpler. Streaming often occurs naturally in rock or earth troughs
which can be partly secured by being cleaned and kept in good
repair with small wall barriers, and clay and lime coating. They
serve to water cattle and to attract hunted species. In a hill or
mountain context troughs coincide sometimes with territorial
limits, often marking the trails of transhumant flocks. Wells
can also be of communal use. The practice of sealing wells by
local authorities and reserving them for periods of drought is
well known all over the Mediterranean.

A. ACOVITSIÖTI-HAMEAU

## S. 197

1.v1 SERVICES

1.V1.5.n Well-sweep

The sweep (or swipe) for drawing water is an ingenious eleyating device using first-order lever turning in its middle
about a pivot and carrying a vessel at one end and a counterweight at the other. A slight variation of the weight on one of
the two arms of the lever is sufficient to set the latter rocking.
Erected over a well, cistern, pond, or stream, this lifting apparatus makes light work of what would otherwise be a tiresome
job. A well-known instance ofa sweep is the Egyptian shadouf.

Already extant in antiquity and common in the Middle Ages,
the technique is, or used to be, widespread from France to
Japan. It is- or was - to be found not only in the Far Eastand
Western and North Africa, but also throughout Europe. In
France alone, in 1986, there remained a few surviving examples
ofsweeps in as many as 36 departements, where they are known,
according to regional dialects, as cigogne/cigounho, canlevo, banlevo, manleve, gruo or brimbale. This last designation, originating
in Charente, migrated across the Atlantic to French-speaking
Quebec together with the device.

The sweep for drawing up water, as encountered in Europe,
consists of four elements. A fixed vertical element (the support
or upright), generally either a tree trunk topped by a fork, or a
post ending with a fork-like mortise, or sometimes twin posts,
acts as a pivot or fulcrum. Secondly, a rotating horizontal element, i.e. a metal or wooden axis running through the fork or
the fork-like mortise, enables the sweep to swivel. A mobile
horizontal element (the beam or sweep proper), consists of a
longtapered rod resting on the upright. To its thinner arm -the

494

load arm or jib — on the water’s side, is fastened the hanging
system; to its thicker arm — the force arm or tail- on the opposite side, is fastened a counterweight. The fourth, articulated
hanging, elementisawooden bar oran iron rod fastened to the
jib’s end bya short upper chain and prolonged by a long lower
chain with a hook. Because of the counterweight weighting its
tailarm, the beam, when atastandstill, tilts to the side opposite
the water, resting either on the ground or in a fork, or again on
a trestle.

In practice, it is necessary for the bar to be pulled down in
order to lower thejib arm and sink the vessel into the water. But
once the vessel is filled, the two arms of the beam come into
balance again, and it only takes a slight pull upwards to initiate
a rocking movement that will lift the water-filled bucket.

While the pulley is employed in connection with deep aquifers,
the use of the sweep is confined to shallow water-tables and,
naturally, water streams. Several sweeps can be placed in successive levels to raise water to the required level, as in Egypt, on
the Nile banks, or they can be operated side by side on the same
level to achieve continuous irrigation, as in the Sahara oases.

In the European rural environment, the sweep for raising
water is likely to have been built by an individual farmer, either
onariver-bank to irrigate a kitchen garden ora cultivated field,
orina farmyard to draw rainwater from a cistern. Or a small
community might have built one on communal land inside a
hamlet or a village, to provide drinking water to people and cattleorto supply a wash-house. Craftsmen, such as brickmakers,
might also have used one to collect the water necessary to
mould clay bricks or tiles.

Today, the tall silhouette of the machine is gradually being
obliterated from the rural landscape by technological progress
introducing, for instance, mains water supply and pumps.

CHRISTIAN LASSURE

1V15.0 Wells and pumps

In ruralvillages throughout the world a major source
of domestic water is from wells, dug and lined with stone or
brick down to the water-table. Drawing water from the well
may be with the use ofa long-handled ‘dipper’, by the use ofa
well-sweep or by a pail lowered into the well by a rope over a
beam, either manually or with the use ofanimal power. Permanent wells may have a revolving axle or windlass fixed over the
opening, to which the well-rope is attached and which can be
turned bya cranked handle. A more ingenious development of
the windlass divides the axle in two parts, with one halfhaving
theropetakingthe pailwound round it, while the other halfhas
apailsuspended byarope. As one pail iswound up so the other
is lowered into the water, a system which is also used to raise
the unwanted clay when well-digging. All these methods may
be seen today in China.

Though some wells are provided with a cover, either over the
well-head or roofed with a small well-house, the problem with
most of them is that they are subject to dirt, rubbish, windsweepings and jettisoned objects, notto mention birds, insects
and rodents falling into, or otherwise contaminating the water
supply. Even the dipping of. a pail that has been standing on
muddy ground can add to the risk. In addition, many wells are

‚the partsofa
ump-

\hucket’ (plunger)

Water Supply 1.v1.5

located, for practical reasons, close to the dwelling, adding to
the danger of pollution, especially where a privy, earth closet or
other latrine is situated nearby. Cleaner water could often be
obtained with less effort by the installation ofa pump. In many
villages in northern Europe in particular, the pump was made
by the village carpenter, sometimes with the aid of the local
blacksmith.

A wooden pump was made oftwo unseasoned elm butts that
were free of knots and branches, each some 3 m (10 fi) long.
One was 40 cm (16 in) thick, the other about 30 cm (1 ft) square,
being required for the lower section. After being felled, the
‘green’ elm log was placed on a trestle and bored. A giant auger
was used, with cutting ‘shells’ thatcould borea 12 cm (Sin) hole,
guided by a ‘boring horse’ or centering frame. Two men turned
the auger, cutting a few inches at a time; once halfway the log
was bored from the other end. The two lengths were made to
sleeve one over the other. The upper section was given a spout
ofturned elm and an iron handle which fitted in aslot. The handle operated a shaft and a wooden ‘bucket’, which created a
vacuum when made to rise by the leverage of the handle. The
bucket was a hollow block of wood fitted with a leather ‘clack’
valve weighted with lead, an outlet for the water and a leather
flange or collar to ensure a tight fit within the 12 cm (5 in) tube.

The lower section was drilled with intake holes 45 cm (18 in)
above the well bed to avoid the accumulated silt. After being
fitted with a leather clack valve it was lowered into the well, to
besleeved into the upper section and sealed with hot mutton fat.
The pump was then ready for testing and use, the sap-filled elm
being tolerant of continued immersion in water. Elm pumps
were often preferred to the cast iron pumps that replaced them
because they did not freeze in winter. PAUL OLIVER

## S. 198

© Ppauloliver

linew houses
pn toaregional
hlacar, Spain.

1.VIl

TYPOLOGIES

The propensity to classify is not only necessary for the understanding ofthe world, itis
also a vehicle for symbolization and for communication. Only by identification and
labelling can classes and categories be identified that make reference possible to unseen
or recollected phenomena. While vernacular architecture is as susceptible to
classification as is any other kind ofhuman artefact it presents special problems due to
the complexity ofthe subject. What constitutes a type ofartefact is, however, a complex
problem in itself; awheel may be an artefact type, so may be a spoked wheel. A ship’s
wheel is also a type, as isawooden wheel with an iron tyre, or an iron wheel with a solid
rubber tyre. They may all be types of wheel; but each ‘type’ ofwheel may also be a
category or set in itself, or one which has a number of subsets. When these are further
related to function, from a chariot wheel to a steering wheel, from a spur-wheel to a
fly-wheel, it is evident that these may be separate classes, each subject to numerous
variables.

A further factor which affects the nature oftype, is its development over time: a roller
may be perceived as a forerunner ofthe wheel; a solid wheel may be regarded as an
antecedent ofa spoked wheel. Seriation, or the placing of the type subsets in a sequence
which is often one oftime, is a characteristic of many typological exercises, though it is
notan essential feature ofthem. It is however, one ofthe most frequent of means
whereby architectural typologies are devised. They may also be considered in terms of
relative dimensions, or placed geographically and historically according to their origin
and persistence. Archaeologists make extensive use oftypologies in order to
understand and compare artefacts that share certain characteristics, so that their spatial
or temporal distribution may be plotted. When the features ofa particular type of
artefact, such as an amphora, have been identified it is possible for placing and dating
to be determined from pottery fragments. Nevertheless, even in archaeology there is
considerable disparity between methods employed and the criteria used in determining
the classification oftype categories, types and subtypes of artefacts.

Whereas archaeologists have to work with what is uncovered in excavation, often
severely restricted by the durability ofthe materials of which they had been made,
architecture presents fewer limitations ofthis kind. Though arguments have been made
that only one typological classification is relevant to architecture, that ofform, the
complexity ofbuildings as artefactual assemblages is such that they offer opportunities
for an abundance of structural, material and spatial typological analyses. Yet their
Pursuit, recording, listing and seriation has often been an end in itself with little or no
consideration ofthe usefulness or application of the results.

The typologies that have been compiled for this section are based on the descriptive
terminologies most frequently applied to the study ofvernacular architecture. They seek
to provide a basic list of ‘types’ within ‘typological categories’ or groups within which

609

## S. 199

1.VI1

TYPOLOGIES

there are a number of‘types’, which can be augmented, or further classified into
‘subtypes’. Elevations (1.vI11.1) are the dimensioned and scaled facade, or front plane,
sides and rear ofa building. They are thus most appropriate to rectilinear constructions,
but as perspective is not used, though shadow projecting may be, the dimensions ofa
cylindrical or other volume ofbuilding can be expressed in elevations drawn from
cardinal directions. Elevations may be considered as the surfaces of Forms (1.v111.2),

or the three-dimensional masses ofa building and, in certain circumstances, the voids
that they may partially define. The ‘form-types’ summarized relate to Platonic solids and
by aggregation may describe the volumetric character of most vernacular constructions.
They may also be classified in ‘subtypes’, for example by truncation, or further defined
by sections.

As elevations relate to the surfaces ofthe forms, so the forms relate to the Plans
(1.v111.3) ofbuildings. These may be considered as horizontal slices, or sections,
through the building, often at the ground or floor level, or ata measured distance above it.
A ‘plan’ may be used to constructa visual record as a dimensioned axonometric or
isometric projection ofthe form, which can communicate the relationship ofthe plan to
both form and elevation, and to each other. Within the structure the Spatial
Relationships (1.vI1L.4) are especially significant in functional and social terms; in this
typology the proposition is made that the way in which the internal volumes are
combined or made distinct reflects the social complexity ofthe culture that uses them.
A final category, Structural Types (1.v111.5), classifies the fundamental systems by which
commonly available materials are assembled to create the spaces and define the forms
ofvernacular buildings.

Though the type categories that have been defined here are those most used in
architectural description, the most common classifications of building type relate to
their Uses and Functions. These are separately listed in section 1.1x.

PAUL OLIVER

610

ih Typology of Elevations

The observation, documentation and understanding of vernacular architecture is a daunting task. Typology is a
tool ofarchitectural research which addresses this problemina
methodicaland comprehensive fashion. Architectural typology
may be simply defined as ‘the analysis, classification and
grouping ofarchitectural phenomena’.

An important distinction in typological studies is between
typologies based on the analysis of function, and typologies
based on the analysis of form. To group and analyse architecture by programmatic use type (e.g. grain storage structures)
without limitations of geographical, technical or historical
factors, is likely to be too general. Essential formal structure,
which results from the above-mentioned factors is considered
more useful.

For the historian, typological analysis is a method for distinguishing and sorting style and formal characteristics in time
and place. As a theoretical and pedagogical tool, typology
allows for the reduction of complex phenomena to essential
principles to convey arguments or ideas. Design implications
for typological analysis range from the explicit- replication of
documented models (pattern books) to the implicit— personal
interpretation oftypological observations.

All ofthese uses of typology apply readily to the study of vernacular architecture. Indeed, the rigorous typological analysis
of vernacular built form is particularly important for several
reasons. Much ofthe vernacular world is at risk of being lost to
modern industrialized construction; documentation is a form
of preservation. As opposed to a romantic or picturesque portrayal ofthe vernacular, typological series offer a thorough and
accurate description. The popular appreciation of traditional
environments is not based simply on ‘charm’, but on the essential continuities (or patterns) of indigenous form with minor
variations. These continuities can be abstracted, understood
and applied to contemporary architectural production by way
oftypological investigations.

A consistency of form, dimensions and materials in a group
of buildings is an indication of a formal typology. Elevational
typologies are probably the most common form, as opposed to
plan or section typologies, because oftheirvisibility. Elevations
can also provide the initial indication of a building’s function
and internal organization.

An example of an elevational typology is that of the Ayder
seasonal middle-meadow houses (mezra) of the Black Sea
mountains (see below). The typological investigation of the
Ayder mezra involves a photographic series ofa consistent view
and distance from the elevations, analytical diagrams which
reduce the elevations to essential elements and their range, and

descriptive text. The study serves both to introduce an obscure
building type, and to offer amethod of analysis.

Typological research by nature focuses on patterns, or continuities in built form, rather than monumental, or exceptional
architecture. This coincides with an inherent interest in the
study of vernacular architecture to understand and learn from
the vast body of built form rather than selected examples of
‘high architecture’. The collective form with individual modifications that constitute vernacular typologies are akin to theme
and variation in musical composition, and offer similar pleasure in their observation. BILL BOEHM

1vı1.ı-8 Classification

From a typological point of view, elevations have
many differentelements to take into account. First ofall, an elevation is a matter ofthree dimensions. A ‘type’ has to deal both
with the volume of the building and with the bidimensional
Projection ofits different sides, particularly with the ‘facade’ or
main side. In a second instance, a type can be applied to the
study ofthe features ofelevations in individual units andalso to
a number of units arranged in a row or a set, for example an
urban block or a kasbah. In both cases, the three-dimensional
form (volume) and the facade (surface) are representative typological instances. These four instances form a matrix in which
the different typological properties ofelevations can be applied
in each ofthe instances generated: two-dimensional individual
unit (facade); two-dimensional set of units (facade); three
dimensional individual unit (volume); and three-dimensional
set of units (volume).

Typological study of elevations in vernacular architecture
requires the definition of which of the many features of the
buildings are taken into account. In this case the shape ofthe
form and the facade, and the composition of its parts are the
two principal features thatare taken into accountin thestudy of
elevations.

The reading of a typological elevation should start with the
shape of the building. Shapes are intrinsic to the volumes of
buildings, to the form of the walls and roofs and can be
enriched by the use of characteristic ornaments and additions
to these elements. The shape ofa building in a cultural environment provides a familiar image which defines most ofthe identity ofthe built environment. Pointed roofs, cubic or cylindrical
shapes, flat or textured surfaces, are elements of the cultural

611

A

## S. 200

1.vı TYPOLOGIES
image of.a place. They impress the eye and the mind both of
inhabitants and visitors.

Shapes are regular or irregular. The regularity of a shape
comes from its unity and/or its symmetty. The form presents
itself as a complete, coherent volume, the facade is clearly
defined as a plane. Irregularity obviously comes from the fracture ofthe form or the elements ofthe facades in different fragments asymmetrically arranged.

The composition of.an elevation is the second main feature
to be taken into account in the reading of typologies. It has to
do with the disposition ofthe different elements ofthe form or
the facade within their shape. It also has to do with the visual
order of the elevation. Symmetry, repetition, asymmetry and
rhythm, are compositional features of an elevation. In the case
ofregular shapes, it is possible to relate the composition to the
general rules given by the geometry of the form and/or the
facade. In the case of irregular shapes, composition becomes
more a matter of chance and it may be difficult to establish an
underlying controlled order.

Ornament should not be taken as a significant element in
elevation typologies. But in some cases there are ornamental
elements that become part of the image of a building, in its
shape or in its composition. In such cases the type includes
those ornaments as integral parts.

ALBERTO SALDARRIAGA ROA

1vı1-c Composition and Aesthetic

The elevation may contain important information
about a building’s status, content, and organization, and that
information is given clarity through the use of certain basic
compositional strategies that organize and give unity to the elevation. These compositional ideas turn outto be shared among
different cultures, even as the content oftypical elevations may
be quite different. This use of similar compositional strategies
arises because ofa desire, shared among different cultures, to
give the elevation a visual focus or a series of related focuses,
that are related to important structural elements, openings, or
other parts ofthe elevation.

From a compositional point ofview, the most important element of an elevation is a focus or ‘center’ (Alexander; Arnheim). The centre is an element that usually has a high degree
of symbolic importance, e.g. a roof or gable-end, or a door, a
window, or wall in which such an element is located. Often, the
elevation will be composed so that this element takes on an
increased importance by virtue ofits placement or by the placement ofother elements in relation to it. For example, centringa
door under the peak of.a gable-end helps make the door the
focus by placingiton an axis ofsymmetry that goes through the
peak. Placing two windows equidistant on either side of the
door increases the sense of focus by intensifying the symmetty
around the axis. Putting a carved lintel above the door - rather
than, for example, on one or another ofthe windows — keeps
the focus on the door. Each one of these elements - the door,

612

sequence ofbays formed by the vertical posts. Each one ofthese
bays is filled in with brick, wattle and daub, or windows;
the posts themselves are painted a colour that contrasts with
that of the infilled bays. In this case, the elevation contains a
sequence of equivalent focuses, or centres, that are the bays.
Each one ofthese bays is symmetrical (even ifthe entire elevation is not) and is given its definition and is visually intensified
by the ver-tical posts and horizontal lintels, of contrasting
colour, thatsurround it. Butthe posts themselves are notacting
only to separate the bays: they are also visually strong foci in
their own right, given their own definition partly bythe expanse
ofinfill bays that separates them. What results is a visual phenomenon in which alternating focuses, or centres, are, in gestalt
terms, sometimes figure and sometimes ground; this tends to
lead the eye along and unify the elevation. Elevations of many
buildings with repetitive structural bay systems exhibit this
phenomenon.

Differences of scale are also important in giving an elevation
unity. Small detail is important to ornament and intensify
slightly larger entities, which in turn help give focus to major
entities, like window and doors — which themselves may help
form the major elements of focus of the elevation. Since a
building is seen from different distances, the existence of centres at different scales also helps ensure that the elevation sustains visual interest.

More complex elevations can often be looked at in the above

terms: as linked arrangements of focuses, or centtes, at different scales, that interact through mechanisms of symmetry,
contrast, repetition and scale differences to produce an overall
visual unity. HOWARD DAVIS

the two windows, the lintel above the door - has the effect of
supporting and intensifying the focus of that part ofthe elevation that is below the peak oftthe gable. In this case, the elevation gains its unity from the way in which the door is placed and
visually intensified by other elements. The single-focused centre, intensified by its placement and by surrounding elements,
may be found in buildings such as a small Aegean island
church, with a central doorway topped by a small bell-tower, or
a Norwegian loft, with a doorway under an overhanging gableend and framed by carved posts.

Consider another case: a half-timbered cottage in Denmark.
In this case the unity ofthe elevation comes not from the single
focus of an important door, but rather from the repetitive

Typology of Elevations 1.VI.1

(vu-n Example: Mezra

The seasonal middle-meadow houses (mezra) of
Ayder are located in the eastern Black Sea mountains of northeastern Anatolia. Typically built on sloping valley floors, in section the mezra accommodates the storage of silage (above),
dwelling (middle), and a barn (below). In plan, the dwelling is
rectangular, roughly 40-60 sq m (430-650 sq ft), and is subdivided into a series of multifunctional rooms.

Each elevation is composed ofa distinct set ofelements, ofa
range of materials and dimensions, which are consistent from
house to house within the typology. Variations occur within the
range which give each house a distinct character while maintaining a collective consistency. The elements of the elevation
relate to structure (foundation, retaining wall, bearing wall), or
function (access, light, ventilation, closure).

The rear, or uphill elevations of mezra are limited, in terms of
elements, which make them a good study in formal typology.
Six essential elements make up the elevation: the massive
masonry retaining wall, the chimney(s), the roof, the inner wall,
the door and the stair. Each of these elements appears in most
elevations with a definable range of dimensions and materials.
The ranges are limited by availability of materials and craft traditions, and expanded by the introduction ofnew technologies.
Metalroofs, for example, have replaced the originalwood roofs;
cement-block and poured concrete have become an alternative
to heavy rubble retaining walls.

The overall formal organization, or type, as exemplified bythe
elevation, is an established local tradition that accepts individual variation as well as technical innovation. BILL BOEHM
1.V1ll.1.a Accretive

A primary characteristic ofvernacular architecture is
a lack of pretence, or self-consciousness. As opposed to ‘high
architecture’, or architecture by architects, vernacular form is a
result of craft traditions, local materials and their limits, the
landscape, functional demands and the gradual transformation
over time, or ‘formal accretion’. While symmetrical or axial
organizations are sometimes found in vernacular architecture,
evolving programmatic demands, and the ability to add-on as
necessary tend to produce asymimetrical, assembled structures,
whose elevations become a record of their gradual transforma-

## S. 201

oil Boehm

1.v1

TYPOLOGIES

tion. The irregular, unpredictable form of vernacular environments produced by incremental growth stand in sharp contrast
to rigid, controlled environments ofplanned developments.

Explicit rules and codes in planned environments typically
control where and how one builds, limiting the range of formal
possibilities to what is considered safe, visually acceptable,
or compatible with the surrounding environment. In the vernacular environment, such rules are implicit and flexible,
allowing individual interpretations of building conventions.
This permits an essential freedom to build, which allows
for incremental, small scale interventions, as need arises.
Add-ons, such as sheds, back porches or room additions are
evidence ofthe phenomenon. Such interventions are typically
built of the same materials as the original structure, and are
smaller, or subsidiary to the original structure. This produces
variable assemblages of form in which the accretions are not
necessarily distinct, and the underlying original structure
remains dominant and visible.

The elevation of the Uzungöl house is the record of many
years ofchange to accommodate the shelter and feeding oflivestock, and the shelter and domestic activities ofthe occupants.
The heavy timber frame in this case forms an armature for
unplanned subsequent additions, or accretions. In other cases
the accretions may be distinct, independent elements (e.g. outbuildings) that constitute the assemblage. The critical phenomenon in either case is the attitude and ability for formal
evolution over time. A related phenomenon is that of modular
accretion. In this case, growth occurs in regular modules that
are discrete, but linked entities. They may share a common
wall, or common roof plane, or may be linked by a perimeter

614

fence. The modules are consistent in size and materials, and
are added on over time as necessary. A row, or string of such
modules will typically surround a common shared outdoor
space, or courtyard, thus performing a protective function.
Modular accretion is often at the familial or community scale,
where each module may house a family or members ofa family.
The resulting form often creates an interesting rhythm or pattern oflinked elements.

The accretive process, including the modular approach, produces elevational typologies which are distinctive in their lack
ofuniformity and a tendency to transform gradually over time.

BILL BOEHM

1.VIll.1.b Asymmetric

Typologically speaking, an elevation is ‘“asymmetric’
when several elements are arranged in an irregular pattern and
no compositive axis can be defined in the general image.

Asymmetry in elevations can derive from the original structure ofthe buildings (original asymmetry) but italso may come
from different modifications of an originally symmetric structure. In this case, traces of the original symmetry could be
found in the type and this could be considered ‘derivative asymmetry’. Ifsymmetry is considered to be a very frequent feature
in vernacular architecture, asymmetry immediately becomes
the main exception to that rule. Those original situations that,
from the very beginning, display an asymmetric pattern are, in
this sense, ofgreat typological interest.

Asymmetry in the form ofa building or in a set of units is in
some sense the ‘true’ asymmetıy. Several elements join
together in an asymmetric complex. The architecture of Greek
islands, the kasbah in the Arab world, the ancient Pueblo
dwellings in southwestern areas ofthe United States are good
examples of originally asymmetric complexes.

Asymmetric composition ofa facade is a different matter. It
takes into account the disposition ofthe elements that form the
surfaces of the building. It is possible to find asymmetric
facades in symmetric forms. The rule of form symmetry does
not always project into the composition ofthe sides.

1.vı1.1.c Axial

An axial elevation is one in which there is a perceived
or felt relationship of elements about one, or more axes. This
affective symmetry is notan axiality based on mathematical symmetry or ‘handed’ or ‘mirrored’ elements. Often the perceived
axis may correspond with an importantsymbolic location on the
elevation, such as the main door, a different important opening,
oravertical building element such as a tower or gable.

The axis or affective symmetry is initially evident in the overall shape ofthe elevation or piece ofthe elevation itself: a steep
gable or a tower, for example, are structural elements that
imply an axis by their verticality alone. At a smaller scale, the
axis may be made evident by elements ofthe elevation that are
placed in relationship to it. These may include openings, structural or construction elements, or ornament, placed so that
they draw attention to the axis. This is the case, for example, in
some Rajasthani houses, where the painted ornament around
the door, and the niches on either side ofthe door actvisually to
reinforce the axis that is only barely implied by the door itself.

The irregularity and roughness of traditional construction
techniques, coupled with intuitive ways of perceiving buildings
that do not rely on precise measurements, may lead to the development ofaxial elevations that provide visual but not mathematical symmetıy. The tiny church atKastro on the Cycladic island of
Sifnos, for example, has an axis defined by the door and belltower. The windows on either side of the door help to intensify
theaxis, and the centrality ofthe door- but those windows are at
different distances from the door, which is itself notat the exact
centre ofthe wall. Buildings on the island, including this church,
are builtoflarge flatstones, plastered and whitewashed. The failure to have an absolutely symmetrical elevation may have arisen
from the contingencies ofbuildingwith the available material, or
with some feature of the plan. But perceptually, the elevation is
axial, with its elements relating to an implied vertical axis
through the door— and we may conjecture that the builder ofthe
church built an elevation in which he saw the door to be the
focus, and for the bell-tower and windows to elaborate on that
focus by creating an implied axis near the centre ofthe wall.

An axial elevation is often the end elevation ofa building that
has an axiality or perceived axiality in plan. In such cases the
axiality ofthe elevation is a representation ofthe axiality in plan
- often the case for religious buildings.

© Gregory Brokan

Typology of Elevations 1.v1l1.1

An elevation may exhibit multiple axiality, inwhich anumber
offocal points each set up an axis or implied axis. This may be
the case, for example, with buildings that have repetitive structural bays that are expressed on the elevation: a series of gables
in wooden construction, or ofdomes in brick construction.

HOWARD DAVIS

1.vıl.1.d Balanced

A balanced elevation is one in which visual resolution or harmony is achieved not through striet symmetry about
an axis, but through the balance of dissimilar elements placed
so thattheir visual weight is similar, on either side ofan implied
focus or series ofimplied focuses. Balanced elevations are typically found in many differentkinds ofsituations: as the side elevation ofa building for which the frontelevation is symmetrical
or axial; as the front elevation of a large building, with many
elements, where the entrance is prominent enough by itself to
provide a singular focus; or as the elevation of a building or a
cluster ofbuildings that have grown in an accretive way.

Visual balance of dissimilar elements may come about in a
number of different ways. A narrow vertical element may be in
balance with a low, horizontal element. A strongly defined
group ofopenings may be in balance with awall containing few
or no openings. A single large opening may be in balance with
a group ofsmall openings. A certain quantity ofone material —
with a certain surface characteristic and ‘grain’ of detail - may
be in balance with a different quantity ofanother material.

The roof may play a strong role in the apparent balance of
the elevation. Particularly within building traditions that have
steep, gable roofs, the roof may be a singular element with
little variation other than the detailed texture of the surface:
shingles or thatch, for example, covering an extremely simple
overall shape. In this case the balance may occur between the
solid and singular form of the roof and the wall below, which
may be considerably more complex, and incomplete by itself.
In this case, the roof is visually so strong that it tends to overshadow the differences that may exist between parts ofthe wall
below, and in effect help unify that wall. This can be seen in
such situations as rural Japanese houses, Danish farmhouses,
and many others.

—

Mmeyosg Augen

615

## S. 202

1.vıl TYPOLOGIES

Some of the wood and masonıy houses of northern Greece
and the Balkans, mostly built before the middle ofthe rgth century, are good examples of buildings with balanced elevations.
These buildings generally have a base ofrather undifferentiated
masonry, containing the first one or two storeys, topped by an
upper floor or two of wood, which are mainly covered with
stucco but are also highly elaborated with exposed wooden
rails, eaves, and ornamental work. The detail ofthe top is balanced by the simplicityand weightofthe bottom. Furthermore,
these buildings - particularly their upper floors - often have an
asymmetrical plan, in which a main room, open to the breeze,
is in a position overlooking the garden and the view beyond.
This room may be cantilevered over the wall below, or have a
projecting balcony, and have more windows and woodwork
than other rooms on the same storey. Another balance of contrasts is thereby set up, between the detail ofthe main room as
expressed on the elevation, and the other rooms. This characteristic also occurs in many of the monasteries of Mt Athos,
largely built within the same tradition, but where building
additions built at different times, often act together to produce
unified and balanced visual compositions. HOWARD DAVIS

1.VvIl1.1.e Repetitive

Repetition is a basic principle in typological analysis. In elevations, repetitive cases include both the sets formed
by several identical or similar elements and the repetition of
identical or similar elements in the composition ofa facade. In
a very rough way, repetition has repetitive themes or subjects
that build larger patterns.

Sets ofindividual units can be considered as ‘serial’ in that
each unitis similarto, orrelated to, the preceding one, as itis to
its successor. Repetition and variation are possibilities of unit
combination within a serial. In the case of variation ofa basic
type, the set displays some irregularity that is common in vernacular architecture. Identical units are common in large massproduced settlements.

Repetition has an underlying rhythmic pattern, amodulation
that is sometimes explicit as in the case of arcades or colonnades and at other times is implicit, as a regulating grid. Measures are acommon way to regulate repetitive patterns, both in
sets of units and in the composition of a facade. Purely visual

order is sometimes achieved withouta real modulation.
Repetition is common in examples of urban vernacular
architecture. Urban types are repeated in streets or blocks and

form a complex where repetition allows unity and homogeneity. However, itseems difficultto find in vernacular architecture
an exact repetitive pattern analogous to those mass-produced
in modern architecture. Variation is more frequent than
absolute repetition. ALBERTO SALDARRIAGA ROA
1.V11.1.f Symmetrical
Symmetry in elevations has to do with axiality and

equilibrium. In some sense, itis a matter of composition. Symmetrical elevations are thus defined by the ordering presence of
an axis that defines the general distribution of the parts and
their equilibrate relationships where one half is ‘handed’, or
the reverse ofthe other about the axis.

Symmetry has also to do with the idea of wholeness and
totality. Itis a frequent feature in vernacular architecture, to the
point of becoming almost spontaneous or natural to it. There
are many interpretations ofthis tendency towards symmetry in
primitive huts, in iglus, in the maloca ofthe Amazon basin, and
in many other examples through the world. Simplicity ofform,
symmetry and other qualities were attributed to the ‘primitive nn me 1
hut’ in Laugier’s Essai in the 18th century and became a para- sketes (Greece) ”
digm in western classical architecture.

The term symmetry does not apply to repetitive patterns. It
requires the presence of different, unbalanced elements that
come to equilibrium through the order ruled by the axis. Symmetry requires also some sort ofhierarchy because there is usually a central space, marked by a door, an elevation of the
facade, a void, or other significant element. As in a plan, the
axis in an elevation indicates a sense of importance and the
symmetrical division of a body or a facade indicates in some
way this feature ofthe house.

Symmetry has asymbolic meaning. Itcan berelated to a cosmological understanding ofspace, as a representation oforder
and as a representation ofthe human body. Vernacular architecture through the world displays a considerable amount of see.also
symmetrical patterns in plans and elevations. In some ways in roeon Ci
“archetypal’, itgoes beyond frontiers oftime and culture. In this
sense, symmetry has a sacred spirit that is still appreciated in er er
some culturalenvironments. ALBERTO SALDARIAGA ROA 1753

square in Tarnov, a
