NETTE

ıın2 Typology of Forms

The act of building, one of man’s most enduring
and important social activities, inevitably gives rise to the production of form, for it is through the physical manipulation of
material, either to enclose space or to create a solid body
(mass), that architectural form is created. As artefacts (objects
shaped by human workmanship), all vernacular buildings possess form, and itis by means ofthis form thatwevisually apprehenda building’s principal physical characteristics. Ifbuilding
form is thought to consist of constituent ‘elements’ such as
‘mass’ (denoting a three-dimensional entity), ‘space’ (the volume enclosed) and ‘surface’ (the boundary to mass and space),
itis useful to refer to their interrelationship when describing or
analyzing form (Norberg-Schulz).

Form and shape

The terms ‘form’ and ‘shape’ are often thought to be synonymous, and they are frequently used interchangeably in discussions ofvernacular architecture, often leading to descriptive and
analytic confusion. The aforementioned notion that form simultaneously involves aspects of mass, space and surface indicates
that the term refers principally to an object’s three dimensional
characteristics; ashape, on the other hand, isatwo-dimensional
area orsurface defined by an edge. The edge that delimits ashape
can betheresultoftonal difference, as with a silhouette; alternatively, ashape may be defined by an ‘outline’, a terın referring to
the perimeter line - either real or apparent - that encloses the
shape. The term ‘profile’ denotes the outline of.a form, and it is
frequentlyemployedto designate a typical or distinctive aspect of
an object’s figural identity. From a distance, a building form may
appear as a shape (or. a series of shapes) against the sky or landscape; in this case, the eye perceives tonal variations that it interprets as two-dimensional planes, the other characteristics of
form being visually indiscernible.

Because form is the result either of modelled mass (in the
case of.a solid object) or the enclosure of three-dimensional
space (or volume) by means of surface, and shape is defined as
delimited two-dimensional surface, it can be seen that shape
assumes an important role in the perception of form.

Building form is physically realized through relationships
between lines, planes, surfaces and masses, and it thus establishes the primary visual conditions, or aesthetics, ofa building
or structure. Along with geometric properties by which they
may be generally identified, additional features such as size
(determined by dimensions), colour, and texture are identified
with form. Surfaces may be oflow or high reflectivity, affecting
the amount oflighteither absorbed or reflected, an important
consideration in many climates. Itis also by means ofmass and

surface that shade and shadow are made visible. Correlative
terms such as ‘scale’ (relative size), ‘proportion’ (the relationship between part and whole) and ‘rhythm’ (repetition of elements) may be employed to describe additional aspects of a
building’s visual appearance. These, in turn, ultimately
influence the psychological effects of building form (Arnheim).
The significant role of sensory perception in the manmade
environment, and the importance ofbuilding form in affecting
it, is an aspect of gestaltpsychology thatshould be considered in
this regard (Kohler). In the case of dwellings, it could be said
that the building form constitutes both the physical place of
habitation as well as the act of living, thereby involving both
artefact and process in an important reciprocal relationship
(Oliver).

Classification method

The typology of form hereupon proposed is based on two fundamental propositions. Firstly, building form, as previously
mentioned, is contingent upon mass, space and surface for its
realization. Because it is the surface that effectively bounds
both mass and space, ithas a dominant role in the definition of
form. Secondly, since building form may be described as having certain geometric properties, geometry can serveasauseful
means by which to both define and classify form. Based on fundamental principles of solid geometry, which deals with threedimensional space, this typology utilizes the correspondence
of building forms with primary geometric forms as its fundamental classificatory criterion.

This is therefore a typology dealing only with pure form and
its presence in the external appearance of buildings and structures; as such, itis not fundamentally concerned with the effect
of culture, material, technology, meaning, climate, and so on,
on building form.

It is clear that the forms found in vernacular building are
such that they may often only physically approximate the geometric properties of the primary forms identified below. Further, some building forms simply cannot be described in terms
of these primary forms; nonetheless, it is believed that the
majority ofvernacular architecture can be described in terms of
the following primary forms.

Primary forms

In descriptive geometry, that branch of mathematics dealing
with three-dimensional forms and their depiction on a plane
surface, the surfaces of a solid form may be generated by
the motion ofa line that is either straight or curved. For the purpose of this typology, the forms that are thus generated will be

617

## S. 203

1.v1ll TYPOLOGIES

designated as ‘primary forms’, and only those surfaces and
related forms that appear most often in vernacular building are
discussed.

Surfaces generated by moving a straight line are called ‘ruled
surfaces’, and they may be subclassified as polyhedral, singlecurved or warped; when generated bythe movement ofa curved
line, ‘double-curved surfaces’ result.

Polyhedral (many-sided) forms, or polyhedrons, have sides
(faces) thatare all equal polygons (closed plane figures bounded
by a minimum of three line segments). The five ‘regular polyhedrons’ (the tetrahedron, octahedron, cube, icosahedron and
dodecahedron) are also known as the five ‘Platonic solids’.
Ofthese, onlythetetrahedron (with four faces, each an equilateraltriangle) and the cube (a hexahedron, with sixsquare faces)
have flat bases and would be relatively straightforward to
construct as a building form. Despite its structural stability,
the tetrahedron does not appear frequently, however, and the
cube is the only regular polyhedron that has any major role in
vernacular building.

Any polyhedron having two parallel and congruent polygons
(coinciding if superimposed on one another) as its bases and
parallelograms as its sides is known as a ‘prism’. In arightrectangular prism the two bases are rectangles, while the sides
perpendicular to them are also rectangles. Rectangular, hexagonal, octagonal and triangular bases are the most typical in vernacular prismatic forms, rectangular and triangular prisms
being the most common. A ‘pyramid’ is a polyhedron having a
polygonal base and triangular sides which meet ata common
point, or vertex. Although the bases of pyramids may be ofany
polygonal shape (e.g. square, rectangle, triangle, hexagon) the
right square pyramid (having a square base and vertical axis at
right angles to this base) is the most commonly found pyramidal building form.

Cubic, prismatic, and pyramidal forms may thus be designated as the three primary forms thataare polyhedral solids with

faces composed of plane surfaces.
b
d

© Gregory K. Hunt; drawing by Michael Licht
| ” | 1

1uorı [eeypun Kg Bummep Zunk ALOBS10 0

c D

Of the forms possessing single-curved surfaces, those
termed ‘conical’ and ‘cylindrical’ are of major importance
because of the frequency of their appearance in vernacular
building. Each ofthese single-curved surfaces may be geometrically generated by moving a straight line in such a way that it
is always in contact with a curved line. Since each ofthese two
surfaces may also be generated by a straight line being rotated
about aa vertical axis, they may also be termed ‘surfaces ofrevolution’. Horizontal semicylindrical forms may be generated
either by sliding a vertical curve along a straight line or by
sliding a straight line alonga vertical curve. Consequently, they
are also referred to as ‘translational surfaces’, or ‘surfaces of
translation’.

Whereas the sequential positions of the line producing
single-curved surfaces are either parallel (as in a cylinder) or
intersecting (as in a cone), the generating straight line of a
‘warped surface’ is in contact with two skewed (non-parallel,
non-intersecting) lines. Because they employ straight lines in
their generation (and are thus considered ‘ruled surfaces’),
warped surfaces can theoretically be constructed out oflengths
ofwood or bamboo, for example, but it would appear they are
not encountered in vernacular architecture.

In contrast to single-curved surfaces, those considered
double-curved can be generated only by rotatinga curved vertical line about a vertical axis. Often referred to as ‘surfaces of
revolution’, they contain no straight lines and they result in
whatare termed ‘domical’ forms.

The overall domical form thatis generated may be either simple, aswhenasemicirculararch is rotated, or complex, as when
a curve changing from convex to concave profile is rotated.

The term ‘membraneous’ is here introduced to describe those
forms whose surfaces are generated by flexible sheet materials
(membranes), under tensile stresses. Because it is the surface
itself that produces the form as a result of both stresses and a
required support system, membranes can assume any number
of forms, from right triangular prisms to more complex hyperbolic paraboloids. Due to the tendency ofmembranes to assume

Single-curveds,

A circular cone

B eireular cylinder

© horizontal semicylinder

D hemisphere (rotatag
semi-circular) dom,

Urfaces,

Polyhedron forms:
a cube (hexahedron)
b rectangular prism
c triangular prism
d square pyramid

certain curved profiles under their own weight, membraneous
forms often exhibit surfaces of double curvature.

Undifferentiated and differentiated forms

Undifferentiated form occurs when there is no visual exterior
distinction between the wall and roof of a structure. Consequently, these forms often have a distinctly monolithic appearance, and their singularity of form is usually emphasized
through their monomaterial construction. When freestanding
in the landscape, they exhibit pronounced geometric simplicity
and clarity of profile in contrast to their surroundings.
Undifferentiated forms may be conical, domical, prismatic
(tight triangular only) or semicylindrical.

Conjunctive form

The majority of vernacular buildings can be classified as conjunctive forms, as they consistofa combination oftwo or more
primary forms in their massing. Each ofthese forms is created
in response to certain needs, be they utilitarian, technical,
climatic, symbolic or cultural, and the specific ways in which
they are combined are influenced by similar and often interrelated factors. In some cases the forms appear casually additive
(dwelling and barn additions), while others are more consciously integrated with a more formal view of the total whole
(courtyard houses, North American farmhouses, African compound houses). At the scale of the building wall, the form
and number of appurtenances such as door canopies, balconies, awnings, porches and roof drains can either enhance
or diminish the perception of the primary building form
(or forms), and they are an important part ofthe overall building aesthetic.

In many cases, that portion ofa building or structure delimited by walls may assume one type ofform, while the roof structure may assume another that is of completely different
geometry while being spatially and structurally related to the
form that itsurmounts.

Given the capability of a roof to shed both rain and snow,
modify wind forces and/or provide for shade or heat retention,
along with the fact that it universally symbolizes ‘shelter’, the
roof plays a very important role in the overall building form. In
Japan, for example, the word for roofis yane, denoting ‘houseroot’ and indicating the significance of the roof in the concept
of Japanese dwelling (Grillo). Roofs may take on a number of
profiles and be highly ornamented, especially their gable-ends
(Toba Batak houses of Sumatra) and ridges (Japanese minka),
and when they become the more dominant of the conjunctive
forms, they are often used to express social, economic or
political status, as well as cultural importance (ritual houses,
churches, mosques).

Roof forms may be classified as recurring subtypes of both
ruled and double-curved surfaces and further subelassified
according to their pitch (angle ofinclination from the horizontal) and geometric properties, resulting in three primary categories related to their profiles: flat, pitched and curved. The
number ofvariants and combinations possible within this subclassification often reflects structural, symbolic, decorativeand
stylistie concerns in addition to purely climatic considerations.

Typology of Forms 1.v1.2

Variants of pitched roofs include single pitch (monopitch, or
shed), double pitch (or gable), hipped, mansard, gambrel and
pyramidal, each ofwhich may be described in terms ofthe geometric properties of prismatic form previously discussed. Both
conical and domical (with elliptical, parabolic, hemispherical
variants) roofs are commonly found surmounting various
prisms and cylindrical forms, and semicircular roofs are usually
conjoined with cubic and prismatic forms (rectangular prisms).

Roof forms themselves may also be conjoined, as when two
right triangular prisms are joined at right angles (intersecting
gable roofs), or a rectangular prism meets one face ofarighttriangular prism, as when arectangular chimney passes through a
gable roof.

Arighttriangular prism may also be truncated (therresultofa
solid being cut with a plane, in this case parallel to the base of
the prism) and surmounted by another right triangular prism
ofsteeper pitch; the lower prism can also be left in its truncated
state, meaning that its top is a flat roof.

In the case of cubic and rectangular prisms that are not surmounted by any additional roof forms, the flat roof gives additional emphasis to the definitive overall profile ofthe prism, as
there is no other form to compete with itvisually. Dependingon
their particular visual character and frequency of appearance,
additional forms such as those of chimneys, wind-traps (Iragi
badgirs, for example), trellises or awnings can significantly alter
the overall perception ofthe flat-roofed prism.

Aggregate form

The term “aggregate’ denotes those group forms that result
when several primary forms are assembled into a larger collective whole. The term is not meant to describe any grouping of
primary building forms, but rather those that utilize a repeated
primary form as a ‘unit’ form. The configuration ofthe many
dome-on-cylinder conjunctive forms that comprise a typical
Massa homestead in Cameroon, for example, is such that the
one conjunctive form is readily perceived as the basic ‘building
block’ of the entire complex (Fraser). Aggregate form results
only when the perceptual clarity of the identifiable primary
form (the unit) occurs within the greater whole.

Voids

Bounded by surfaces and capable of sharing the basic characteristics of primary forms, voids have a very important role
in building form. Because they can assume a number of different profiles and depths within a thick wall, recesses such as
windows, doorways, and niches achieve very defined threedimensional characteristics; further, they can create myriad
visual patterns of both shape and shadow, and depending on
the overall ratio of solid to void, either the wall surface or the
voids themselves may achieve visual primacy.

Although they may also be thought ofas deep recesses, some
loggias effectively serve as large outdoor rooms in some climates, and when of vaulted ceiling with semicircular profile,
they illustrate, in effect, the reversal ofthe semicylindrical form.
In the case of courtyard houses, the exterior void (the courtyard)
is a three-dimensional space whose form is configured directly
by the enclosing walls.

619

## S. 204

1.vı TYPOLOGIES
Form determinants
Collectively considered, vernacular building forms exhibit
sufficiently discernible physical traits to be classified according
to their correspondence with the aforementioned geometric
properties. It is well understood that the decisions involved in
the selection of one form over another in vernacular architecture are varied and complex, involving as they do an interplay of
factors such as function, climate, topography, economics,
availability of materials, building technologies, culture and
tradition. Although one or several of these factors may affect
the use ofa form, it is apparent that they do not actually determine it. GREGORY K. HUNT
1.Vv11.2.a Conical

As single-curved surfaces geometrically generated
bya straight line passing through a fixed point (the vertex) and
rotated about an axis so as to follow a curve, cones are also
classified as ‘surfaces of revolution’. If the axis of rotation is
vertical and the rotated line is in contact with a circle, a right
circular cone is produced. When further considered as a surface generated by a right triangle being rotated about its long
leg, the right circular cone can be seen to possess a triangular
cross-section that contributes to its structural stability. Most
conical forms found in vernacular architecture are ofthe right
circular variety, and they appear in both undifferentiated and
differentiated states.

The fact that these conical forms terminate in a single point,
have inwardly inclined surfaces and are circular on plan makes
them relatively easy to construct. In its primary, undifferentiated state, for example, a conical structure may be formed by
positioning one end of a series of inclined, straight poles in
a circular pattern and joining their other ends at the vertex,
or peak. If this simple structural skeleton is then covered with
animal hides, felt or bark, a structure commonly known as
the ‘conical tent’ results. Due to its material and structural
efficiency, light weight, ease of erection and portability, this
tent type can be considered one of the primary structures of
rural nomadic architecture. The tent (tipi) ofthe North American Plains Indian, the summer tent (tupig) ofthe Alaskan Eskimos, and the transportable conical hut of the Yamana
of Argentina exemplify the wide climatic range to which this
basic conical form has been adapted. These frame and skin
methods do notalways produce true conical forms, however, as

z / \\
E IN
Pe) \
| \
Fu] \
®2--t-rb-N\
N
© SS _.N3-
5

the spacing of the framing members may be such that a more
faceted surface (as opposed to a truly curved conical surface)
actually occurs. When not required to meet portability requirements, undifferentiated conical forms may be achieved by
using densely spaced poles, sticks orreeds followingacircleon
plan and inclined inwards towards a peak, as in the conical huts
ofthe Najrän region of Saudi Arabia, or they may be built up of
flat stones which are then plastered, as in the conical stone
granaries found in Mexico.

The fact that cones have a vertex and inclined surfaces make
them well suited to serve as roof forms, and owing to the shape
of their bases, right circular cones most commonly appear as
roof forms surmounting cylindrical rooms or structures. Conical roof forms may assume a variety of profiles, from slopes of
low inclination to those that are more steeply sloped and are
either slightly convex (‘trumpet-shaped’) or exaggeratedly concave. When made ofthatch, their surfaces may exhibit complex
‘herringbone’ patterns or pronounced ‘rings’ of decreasing
diameter toward the vertex of the cone; when constructed of
stone, as in the stone dwellings (trulli) found in the Apulia
region ofltaly, the horizontal rings ofstone of changing diameter may be left exposed, emphasizing the incremental, layered
construction and giving a pronounced, highly textured visual
profile to the conical roofs. Whether constructed of thatch or
stone, the pinnacles (vertices) of these conical forms may
assume symbolic importance and take on unique profiles, asin
the finials (cucuerni) ofthe trulli.

The skilfully woven millet-straw roofs that surmount the
small-diameter circular rooms ofa Muktale family compound
in Cameroon, West Africa, the steeply sloped, slightly concave
roof of felts found in the Firozkhoi yurt of central Aghanistan,
the large, radially framed thatch roof of the communal
dwelling (churuata) ofthe Ye’kwana of Venuzuela, and the intricately framed, heavy timber roofs ofthe North American round
barn, are but four examples that collectively demonstrate the
diverse scales, profiles and materials of conical forms. With
regard to building type, conical roof forms are found in
dwellings, mosques, granaries, silos, windmills and barns.

Although they are most often found on circular structures,
these roof forms are also used to surmount dwellings that are
square in plan, as in the Bamileke dwellings of Cameroon,
where an elaborate interior pyramidal framework and two circular platforms furnish the primary structural support system
for the conical thatched roofs of these family huts; similarly,
the tapering rectangular prisms ofthe Dogon granaries in Mali
are occasionally roofed with thatched conical roofs which,
due to their lack of internal stiffeners, assume a noticeably
distorted profile when placed on their supporting walls.

Semiconical forms, or those with a semicircle for a base,
appear in an undifferentiated state as the apsidal ends ofsome
structures taking the form of elongated prisms of triangular
cross-section, such as the communal houses of the Bari in the
jungles of Perija, Venezuela, or one type of thatched dwelling
(the halatzuka) used by the nomadic Sarakatsani of Greece.
With their semiconical short ends, these structures gain added
structural stability, and in the case of the halatzuka, which
tapers slightly on plan, the shortened curved end faces north,

the direction ofthe prevailing winds. In the Tukanoan community house (malocca) found in the Vaupes region of South America, which is also an elongated triangular prism, the apsidal
west end with its semiconical roof has cosmological significance and denotes the female domain ofthe house.
GREGORYK. HUNT

1.V11.2.b Cubic

When Plato identified the cube as one of the five
primary solids, he ascribed a great deal of importance to this
particular form, for to him it represented earth, the most
immovable of the four elements of the universe. Because each
surface ofthis regular polyhedron is a square, the relationship
between the cube’s length, width and height may be expressed
with the ratio of 1:1:1, and this equivalence of the shape, size
and proportion of all six surfaces has long contributed to the
cube’s association with stability and, by some, with static perfection.

In contrast to arectangular prism, a cube encloses the maximum amount ofvolume with the minimum amount of surface
area, and itthus exhibits a certain material efficiency as a building form. This aspect of cubic form most likely accounts for
its frequent appearance in vernacular architecture; moreover,
the cosmological importance attributed to the unique ratio of
the proportion ofthe cube’s six faces may also be ofimportance
in some cultures, particularly in the architecture of churches,
temples, mosques and cemeteries. For the purposes of this
typology, those indigenous buildings that are square on plan,
havea heightequal to (or nearly equal to) their length and width
and are flat-roofed may be considered cubic. Because many rectangular prisms have profiles and dimensions approaching
those ofactual cubes, particularly in dense village or community settlements, the term ‘“cubic’ is also sometimes used to
collectively identify these agglomerations.

Since the flat roofs of cubic structures are not typically visible, they offer no contrasting profile to the overall structure,
and the walls themselves assume primary visual importance.
Cubic forms thus tend to emphasize their surfaces (or wall
planes) as the essential space-enclosing elements, and when
the walls are unbroken by large numbers of window and door

Typology of Forms 1.v1.2
openings, freestanding cubic structures can take on an elemental, ‘building block’ aesthetic. With their geometric clarity and
unadorned surfaces, the whitewashed stone buildings found
throughouttthe Greek islands are considered by many to be the
‘archetypal’ cubic forms. As descendants of the ancient narrow-fronted Greek house (megaron), these structures are found
as both independentand aggregate building forms, the identifiability of each cubic form always maintained by means of its
geometry and proportion, despite the uniformity of colour.

Because of their flat sides and roofs, individual cubic forms
may be easily joined both horizontally and, in some cases,
vertically, resultingin dense groupings ofstructurally interdependent, visibly interlocked buildings. In these cases, a cubic form
may serveas basic unit, or‘cell’, to berepeated ina dense building
complex ofseveral levels. The flatroofs ofthe cubic forms may be
used as outside terraces providing additional living, working or
storage spaces, and the presence of exterior staircases, ladders,
roof pergolas, and the like can introduce additional visual complexity to the building profiles. In the case of Amerindian villages
(pueblos), mostnotably those at Taos and Acoma in New Mexico,
the dwellings are arranged in multi-storey, terraced clusters of
high density and a unified visual order of essentially monochromatic coloration. When clustered along the contours ofhillsides
and built of local stone and mud, the cubic dwellings of some
Berber villages in the High Atlas mountains ofMorocco achieve a
similar discernible cohesion and continuity, and their sharply
delineated profiles produce a strong geometric contrast with the
surrounding mountainous landscape.

Cubic structures may also exhibit subtle variations in profile
and they may berreadily combined with other forms. In the farm
compounds (gida) of the rural Hausa of Nigeria, for example,
cube-like structures with softly rounded edges, many of which
are painted white, are joined with both rectangular prismatie
and cylindrical forms into walled clusters of rich formal complexity. Forms that are square on plan and have walls tapered
along their vertical axis to a height equal to their width can be
considered a variant of the cubic form, and they are found in
structures ofstone and adobe construction in some countries.
Ifa pyramidic or conical roofsurmounts a cube, the cubic form
is usually recessed, the resulting overhang of the roof giving
added visual emphasis to the independent geometries of the
two forms. GREGORY K. HUNT
1.V1ll.2.c Cylindrical

The single-curved surface ofa right cylinder is generated by a vertical straight line rotated about a vertical axis
while following the circumference ofa circlein such a way that
the line is always parallel to its initial position. Ifthe line being
rotated is inclined, a tapered cylinder is formed. When the
height ofa tapered cylinder is small relative to the diameter of
its base, it could also be considered as a truncated cone. Any
material that can be made to follow.a curve is effectively capable
of forming a cylindrical form, and because of the efficiency of
its surface-area:volume-enclosed ratio, it appears frequently in
vernacular architecture.

Since a cylinder may be constructed with a structurally
continuous surface, it can withstand substantial external lat-

621

## S. 205

1.v1l

TYPOLOGIES

eral forces due to its form. In many lighthouses and windmills,
cylindrical forms of pronounced verticality are employed to
resist strong wind loads, and their typical vertical tapering is
the direct result ofthe structural need for increased material at
the base. This structural continuity of curved surface is also
capable of withstanding internal forces, and for this reason
cylinders are also used for many vernacular storage facilities.
The flat-roofed, freestanding cylindrical storehouses and granaries found throughout West Africa, for example, are constructed in a wide range of sizes and proportions, and their
strategic food storage function is given appropriate visual
expression through their simple and often elegant forms, particularly when they are the only circular structures in a building
complex. Similarly, the cylindrical forms of the storage silos
commonly found in rural farmsteads throughout North America often serve as strong visual counterpoints to the basic prismatic forms ofthe house, barn and outbuildings that make up
these complexes. Constructed ofwood, steel, concrete, brick or
tile, they may possess a variety of surface textures, colours and
finishes, and, depending on their height, they often become
prominent aesthetic features in the landscape.

Although they may be constructed with flatroofs, cylindrical
forms are more frequently surmounted by other primary forms
serving as roofs.

Because both the cone and the dome are also surfaces of revolution and have circular bases, they are ‘geometrically compatible’ with the cylinder and easily combined with it. The
conjunctive ‘cylinder and cone’ form occurs in many different
regions and at many different scales, especially as dwellings,
their greatest concentration being in West Africa.

Depending on the building materials employed and the height
ofthe roofform relative to thatofthe cylinder, either the cylinder
or the form which surmounts it may have more visual emphasis.
Alternatively, the forms may visually merge, often resulting in
whatappears to bean undifferentiated structure. For example, in
the case ofthe.circular seasonal huts (lacheq) built by semi-sedentary farmers and pastoralists in northern Afghanistan, the continuous wall forming the cylinder is ofwoven reed matting, while
the hemispherical roof is of plaited grass or flattened reed matting; although both cylinder (wall) and dome (roof) can be
identified, they perceptually merge into a monochromatic,
monomaterial whole from a distance. In contrast, when conical
roofs ofdark slateand high inclination surmountstone cylinders
oflighter colour, as in many western European fortifications, or
when conical roofs ofthatch surmount the cylindrical mud walls
of Tswana homesteads but are supported by an exterior ring of
forked posts, the conical roofforms visually dominate.

Apart from their structural and space-enclosing attributes,
cylindrical forms also have important symbolic significance in
some cultures. The layout ofa typical Dogon dwelling found in
the Bandiagara cliffs of Mali, for example, has both anthropomorphic and cosmological meaning, with the shape and position of each room related to Dogon mythology. Within the
overall physical form ofthe ordinary house (ginu sala), the cylindrical form ofthe cooking area (obolo) is the only circular volume in the dwelling and it embodies specific meaning within
this mythology.

When a number of cylinders are combined to form more
complex structures, they may be joined by straight or curved
walls, or they may be arranged contiguously. Inthe case oflarge
urban grain elevator complexes found in North America, concrete storage cylinders rising over 33 m (L1o ft) in height are
contiguously joined and arranged in rows that can exceed 150
m (490 ft) in length.

The aggregation of cylindrical huts to form walled family
compounds is common in many West African societies, and the
size of these extended structures varies from the multi-room
houses ofthe Batammaliba in Togo and the small ‘castles’ of
the Somba in Benin to the large extended family compounds of
the Gurunsi in Burkina Faso, which may reach a diameter of
some 65 m (214 ft). On plan, the cylinders may be combined
with oval, elliptical or polygonal shapes in these compounds,
and their form may be either partially visually articulated as an
integral part of the delimiting wall, or they may be fully
expressed as the primary spatial unit, or ‘cell’, of the entire
complex. GREGORY K. HUNT

1.vl.2.d Domical

Domes may be considered ‘surfaces of revolution’,
or ‘rotational surfaces’, as they are generated by the rotation of
a plane curve about a vertical axis. Depending on the type of
curve that is rotated, they may assume a variety of profiles,
including low-sloped, hemispherical or parabolic, among others. If half of an ellipse is rotated about its major axis, the
resulting surface is termed a ‘spheroid’; ifa parabola, a ‘paraboloid’ is formed. When the curve is generated bya round arch,
a continuous dome results; if a pointed arch is rotated, a
pointed dome occurs.

Domical forms may be constructed additively by using small
increments of material (twigs, branches, wooden poles, blocks
ofsnow, bricks, stones and so on), or they may be made ofmud
or concrete. If externally exposed structural arches are
employed, a ‘ribbed’ dome is formed and the domical surface
becomes visually divided into discrete sections. In their
undifferentiated state, domical structures enclose the greatest
amount ofspace with the least amount of surface area, while as
roofforms they offer a very efficient means ofspanning interior
space. They therefore constitute a veryversatilemeans bywhich
space may be both covered and enclosed.

Roofs of domical forms are easily conjoined with cylindrical
structures, but they can also surmount hexagonal or octagonal
prisms, in which case a segmented, or ‘faceted’ dome results.
When used to roof cubic or rectangular prisms, additional
building elements such as squinches (diagonal arches used to
span corners) or pendentives (spherical triangles) are used to
support the outer edge ofthe dome. Whether used singularly,
as in some variants of the felt- or skin-covered portable
dwellings used by nomadic peoples throughout central Asia
(e.g. the Mongolian ger), or in aggregation, as in the rhythmically repeated, undulating, low-profiled mud domes of the
Iranian bazaars (bazars), the distinctive curves of domical roofs
area direct expression of the interior volumes thatthey span.

As undifferentiated structures, domical dwellings can vary
greatly in size, construction techniques, and material. For the
nomadic !Kung Bushmen ofthe Kalahari desert, domical huts
approximately 2 m (6.5 ft) in diameter are made from small
sticks, branches and grass and serve as impermanent structures for storage and sleeping. In contrast, the domical communal dwelling (churuata or pereka) of the Panare of Venezuela
typically has a circular plan approximately 16 m (5o ft) in diameter and a central structural mast almost 9 m (30 ft) in height
which supports an elaborate concentric framing system of
wooden pole ‘rafters’ arranged radially to meetat its top. With
its continuous thatched surface rising directly from the ground
in a high curve and its ingeniously framed, focused and spacious interior, this large structure embodies and reveals the
important shared values of communal dwelling through its
domical form. The snow house (iglu) of the Inuit takes full
advantage ofthe factthata hemispherical dome, in addition to
being one of the most efficient forms to enclose space, is also
one of the strongest structural forms that can be built. Constructed with blocks of snow that are cut so they may be setin a
spiral pattern, the iglu needs no additional framework for its
construction, and its form is extremely efficient in reducing the
wind pressures to which it is frequently exposed.

When domical forms are constructed entirely of mud, as
found in the hemispherical roofs that surmount some large
cylindrical granaries in West Africa, they can achieve a monochromatic unity of form and smoothness of surface that is
frequently absent in the appearance of many domes of incremental construction. Some ofthe most unique domical structures in vernacular architecture are the undifferentiated
parabolic dwellings of the Mousgoum in northern Cameroon.
Built entirely of mud and without any internal framework, they

Typology of Forms 1.v11.2
are true domes in a structural sense, and the series of shallow,
elongated and staggered projections that follow their parabolic
forms produce a richly textured domical surface.

GREGORY K. HUNT

1.VIl.2.e Membraneous

True membranes are thin, flexible sheet materials
(animal skins, woven or plastic fabrics) that are considered
structurally unstable because they are unable to support themselves. When held byrigid frames, rings, ropes, cables or poles
and subjected to tensile stresses, however, they are able to span
orenclose space, and membraneous forms result. Because they
cannot carry compressive or bending stresses, membranes
require tension to assume any form, and it could be said that it
is the stretched membrane itselfthat constitutes the form, and
the form, in turn, constitutes the structure.

Given its thinness, moreover, the membrane is considered a
‘surface structure’ when under tension, and this surface can
take oneithera plane ora curved profile. Depending on the type
and configuration ofthe supporting members, the shape ofthe
membrane and the nature ofthe tensile forces, membraneous
forms may consist of polyhedral, single-curved, warped or
double-curved surfaces.

In order to reference the array of forms that these surface
structures can achieve, it may be helpful to consider the membrane itselfas anet formed by a system ofcables. When equally
spaced cables of equal length are suspended from two parallel
horizontal supports, the cables assume the profile ofa catenary
(the curve described by a suspended cable fixed at both ends
and uniformly loaded along its length) when viewed in a section perpendicular to the supports. If the supports are curved
upwards, the resulting curved surface follows a catenary in a
section taken parallel to the supports. When the supports are
curved downwards, a saddle-shaped surface is formed (Otto,
1966). Given that supported, flexible sheet materials under
tension constitute membraneous forms, this brief reference to
the behaviour of cable nets and their resulting profiles should
assist in understanding the frequency with which the catenary
curve appears in these forms. Significantly, the stresses acting
upon the membrane may be biaxial, meaning that they come
simultaneously from two directions, and when these stresses
are of differing magnitudes, a warped surface may result.

In vernacular architecture, the tent is the most ubiquitous of
membraneous forms, and its many variants perhaps best illustrate the range of physical and aesthetic characteristics that

1uorı jgeuaın da Auimeıp Bun 'y AloBaug ©

623

## S. 206

1.vı TYPOLOGIES

membranes under tension can assume. For example, when the
support system for a simple tent consists of a single row of
vertical wood poles over which the membrane is placed and
then subjected to tensile stresses by means ofguyropes, a simple peaked tent is formed. In this case, the resulting membraneous form is fundamentally that of an undifferentiated
triangular prism, even though the tensile forces induced by
the guy ropes themselves produce a slight curvature at the
lower edges of the two inclined faces of the prism. If the
number, spacing and type of vertical supports of a tent structure increases, both the direction and distribution of the
tensile forces become more complex, and curves thatapproach
catenaries in profile appear. These are due both to the stretching of the membrane and the tendency of most membranes,
particularly heavier ones, to readily assume the profile of a
catenary when draped over two or more horizontal or vertical
supports.

As warped surfaces, membraneous forms may also approximate hyperbolic paraboloids, the surfaces of which are composites of parabolic and hyperbolic curves. In this case, the
various areas ofthe overall membraneous form become curved
sections, each visually outlined by a combination of lines of
tensile stress and supports. Ifa membrane is supported by a
number ofvertical poles, as well as by one or more nearly semicircular wooden hoops, as in the vaulted black tents of woven
goat hair cloth built by the nomadic Durani of Afghanistan, the
membraneous form becomes a composition of graceful curves
ofconvex and concave profile. When the surfaces of membraneous forms are curved in two different directions, maximum
stability against wind forces is achieved; doubly-curved surfaces, however, require thatthe membrane is cutso as to achieve
this curvature when stressed.

The high, quasi-rectilinear black tents ofthe Qashgai in Iran,
the simple, variegated open-front cotton tents ofthe Jugis and
the barrel-vaulted tents ofBaluchs, both found in Pakistan, and
the low-slung, ‘humped’ black tents of the Bedouins illustrate
but a few of the numerous aesthetic variations that may be
achieved by membraneous forms.

True tents are to be distinguished from ‘frame and mat’ and
“frame and skin’ portable structures (like the Tuareg mathut or
the Amerindian tipi), in that they do not rely on membranes in
tension to achieve their form; rather, their thin, flexible outer
coverings are simply attached to, and are supported by, a skeletal frame of branches or wood poles. Those structures relying
solely on the frame to give them form, therefore, are not considered as membraneous forms in this typology.

Given the diversity ofsize, complexity and profile thatmay be
achieved by membraneous forms in vernacular architecture,
the subject warrants further typological study.

GREGORYK. HUNT
1.vı1.2.f Prismatic
A prism is a polyhedron having lateral faces that are
parallelograms and parallel bases that may be ofany polygonal
shape. Ifthe lateral faces are perpendicular to the bases, a ‘right
prism’ results; if they meet at any other angle, the prism is
‘oblique’. Right prisms, however, are the predominant prismatic

624

form in vernacular building, and they may be surmounted by a
wide variety ofroof forms.

Frequently used as free standing, flat-roof structures, right
rectangular prisms may exhibit simple or complex profiles,
depending on their plan and vertical section, and the edges of
their faces may be either sharply defined (if made of stone or
bamboo), serrated (if made with overlapping logs), or softly
rounded (if made ofmud).

As with cubic forms, the wall planes (faces) of prisms are the
dominant volume-defining elements of the structure, and
depending on the size and number ofwindow openings and the
building materials employed, they may emphasize the lightness
of minimal enclosure or the solidity of maximum protection.

Because of their form, undifferentiated right rectangular
prisms are easily conjoined both horizontally and vertically, and
when aggregated into dense building clusters, as in some
Moroccan walled towns or African and Iberian villages, they
may also be combined to produce an overall continuity of
building surface or a complex of contiguous but identifiably
individual forms.

When oblique prisms occur in vernacular building, their
faces are usually trapezoidal, a condition that also characterizes some truncated pyramidal structures; oblique rectangular
prisms, however, are more accurately described as those whose
height is low relative to the proportions of their bases, the
whole form approaching that of a rectangular prism. These
trapezoidal faces are due to the increased thickness ofthe walls
atthe base, and they tend to visually emphasize the stability of
the structure. In the one-storey, undifferentiated desert courtyard houses found in Rajasthan in the extreme western portion
of India, for example, the inwardly sloping walls visually
“anchor’ the clay buildings to the desert floor while withstanding the shifting sandstorms to which they are subjected. At a
different scale, the detached mud tower farmhouses found in
the Asir highlands of Saudi Arabia are rectangular on plan and
taper upwards to heights ofthree and four storeys, terminating
in a flat roof often used as a sleeping terrace. Constructed of
successive layers of mud (madamek), these oblique prisms with
small windows and smooth surfaces, stand most assertively in
the rugged rural landscape, and because of their overall proportions they are classified as oblique prisms in this typology.

Prisms with polygonal bases that are other than rectangles
are found in a range of structures, including dwellings, windmills, lighthouses, barns and both roof lanterns and cupolas.
Usually hexagonal or octagonal, as in the multi-sided Navajo
dwelling (hogan), most of these prismatic forms have sides
of equal length. One exception, however, is the six-sided

1göy Michael Licht

O Gregory K. Hunt

thatched-roof traditional Yapese dwelling (tabinaw) found in
Micronesia. With two long parallel sides and two pairs of
obliquely angled end walls ofshorter dimension, the house sits
on a raised stone base of similar configuration. In addition to
rectangular, hexagonal and octagonal prisms, right triangular
prisms (two triangles as bases, and three rectangular lateral
faces) may occur as undifferentiated building forms when
turned horizontally, the simple ‘A-frame’ structure being perhaps the best known example.

More frequently, however, horizontal righttriangular prisms
serve as highly versatile roof forms that can be constructed in a
wide variety of profiles and in configurations that can be
adapted to any structure that is a quadrilateral on plan. When
viewed as a right triangular prism with two sloped faces and
two triangular vertical bases, the gable roofis perhaps the most
elemental of roof forms, and its protective, shade-giving,
watersheddingattributes contribute to its being considered the
‘archetypal’ roof form in many cultures. If the vertical bases
(the gable-ends) of the triangular prism are inwardly inclined,
resulting in a shortened ridge (the line of intersection of the
two sloped faces), a hipped roof is formed. A hipped roof may
also be truncated, resulting in a flat area above the inclined
faces; this truncated roof may also be surmounted by a gable
roof, as found in barn structures in many countries and many
vernacular dwellings with continuous perimeter porches on
the ground floor. A truncated triangular prism with faces ofone
angle ofinclination may also be surmounted by another triangular prism having faces of a lower angle ofinclination, as in a
gambrel or mansard roof.

The nature of the roof framing, the manner in which the
roof itself may overhang the form that it surmounts, and the
angle of inclination of the faces of the triangular prism affect
the overall profile of the roof. When the major supporting
members are curved, as in the thatched hipped roof ofa traditional Serbian house of horizontal log construction, or when

ar

—e—

c d

Typology of Forms 1.v1.2
certain faces ofthe prism are given very pronounced curves, as
in the modified hipped roofs found in Chinese and Japanese
vernacular structures, the roof form takes on a markedly
different character.

The faces ofthe lower truncated prism ofa mansard or gambrel roof may also be curved (either convexly or concavely), and
the contrast with the orthogonal nature of the structure
beneatlı often gives a strong aesthetic emphasis to the roof
itself. The large overhanging roofs of the Toba Batak houses
(rumahs), with their graceful, pronounced ‘saddleback’ profiles,
can be described as obliquely truncated triangular prisms with a
long edge (tidge) of concave profile.

The simply framed gable and thatched-roof raised lake
dwellings of south Dahomey (Benin), the wide overhangs and
complex profile ofthe massive, ‘tent-like’ shingled roofs ofthe
Bernese mittleland farmhouses of Switzerland, the exquisitely
crafted, steeply pitched modified hipped roofs ofthatch found
in traditional Japanese ceremonial buildings and shrines, the
softly curving thatched roof profiles found in many areas of
England, and the complex superimposition and combinations
of gabled and truncated hipped roofs found in wooden Norwegian stave churches illustrate the range of aesthetic variation
the triangular prism can achieve when used as a roofform.

GREGORY K. HUNT

1.v1.2.g Pyramidal

As polyhedrons with polygonal bases and inclined
triangular faces that meetatacommon point, or vertex, pyramidal forms may assume a variety of profiles. These variations
depend on the shape ofthe base, the altitude (the perpendicular
distance from the base plane to thevertex), and the proportional
relationship between the dimensions of the base itself and the
altitude.

Ifthe altitude ofthe pyramid is relatively small, alow angle of
inclination results; conversely, a large altitude on a base of proportionately small dimensions produces the high angles of
inclination characteristic of steeply pitched pyramidal form.
Because each side of a pyramid is a triangle whose two nonbase edges are collinear with the edges oftwo other triangular
faces, pyramidal forms possess geometric characteristics that
make them inherently stable ifthese edges are made in such a
waythatthey can mutually transfer staticand dynamic forces to
the ground. At the same time, regardless of the shape of the
base, the pyramid’s tapering profile (largest at the base and
smallest at the vertex) is also advantageous in dealing with
these external forces. Despite their potential for structural stability and their adaptability to varying building methods, how-

VUN

625

Or IBeuaın Aa Bulmeıp Aunh '% Ai08a,0 ©

## S. 207

| |

1.vıll TYPOLOGIES
ever, undifferentiated pyramidal forms are not commonly
found in vernacular architecture.

Truncated pyramidal forms in an undifferentiated state,
however, are encountered more frequently, and in awide variety
of building types, structures and climates. The huts (goattes)
constructed of logs, bark and turf by Scandinavian Lapps for
winter use and the log, brush and turf-covered traditional
dwellings (balagans) ofthe Yakuts ofeastern Siberia are approximately square on plan and their inward-sloping faces, truncated to give adequate headroom and to minimize interior
volume, provide sufficient structural resistance to the heavy
snows of the Arctic region. Truncated pyramids consisting of
small interconnected wood or metal members forming open,
skeletal structures are often used for utilitarian structures such
as electrical transmission and oil pumpingtowers, as wellas the
bases for water tanks and lighthouses. In these examples, the
pyramidal form utilizes the open skeletal structure to provide
both structural stability and the least resistance to wind loads,
both important considerations in the design oftall structures.

When undifferentiated truncated pyramids have bases that
are small on plan and very high angles ofinclination, their pronounced tapering profiles may assume symbolic importance,
as in the minarets ofnumerous West African mosques, many of
which are builtofmud and have a gently curving vertical profile
characterized by a concave taper. In the case ofthe mosque at
Agades in Niger, the pyramidal minaret reaches a height of
nearly 30 m (too ft), and its many projectingwooden poles that
serve as beams and scaffolding provide a strong textural counterpoint to the smoothly finished tapering ofthe mud walls of
this highly visible structure.

Full pyramids occur most often as roofforms, principally due
to the rain-shedding capability of their sloped faces and their
ability to have bases ofany polygonal shape. They are frequently
used to surmount either cubes or rectangular prisms, and their
adaptability to varying angles ofinclination, building materials
and construction methods accounts for their presence in many
cultures, building types and climates. Some dwellings of the
Ngelima in Zaire, for example, are approximately 2 m (6.5 ft)
square on plan with steeply pitched pyramidal roofs of thatch
rising to a height of 6 m (20 ft), the proportional relationship
between the plan dimensions and the altitude ofthe pyramidal
roof giving avery pronounced vertical emphasis to the dwelling
itself. When the plan dimensions ofthe base ofa pyramidal roof
are larger than the cube or rectangular prism that it surmounts,
the overhanging pyramidal form ofthe roof may appear as a protective, sheltering building element. If the overhanging roof is
partially supported by a series of exterior poles around the
perimeter, as in the dwellings ofthe Bafut peoples ofCameroon,
a very differentvisual emphasis is given to the dominant pyramidal form. With their capacity to incorporate any polygon as their
base, pyramidal forms also serve as the roofs of barn cupolas,
lanterns and ventilators, ofclock towers and belfries, and as the
elongated spires of numerous churches. In these cases, where
the pyramidal forms are relatively small in size, the faces may be
convexly curved before meeting atthe vertex.

Used conjunctively, pyramidal roofs may also surmount
truncated pyramidal roofs of the same, or different, angle of

626

inclination, a short vertical wall being used to separate the two
roof forms, as in some dwellings and Malaysian mosques; if
three or more pyramidal roof sections of equal inclination are
similarly arranged, and the faces comprise terraced, carved
stone slabs such as those found in some Indian Hindu temples,
the pyramidal form achieves a highly textured, more complex
profile. GREGORY K. HUNT
1.Vll.2.h Semicylindrical

Horizontal semicylindrical forms are classified as
single-curved surfaces. They may be considered ‘surfaces of
translation’, in that they can be generated by any vertical curve
translated (or moved laterally) along a horizontal straight line.
The vertical curve is normally that ofan arch and the cross-section of the semicylinder can therefore vary greatly depending
on the arch profile involved; semi-elliptical, semicircular or
parabolic variants, however, are most typical.

As surfaces geometrically generated by translation, horizontal semicylindrical forms may also be formed by a series of
arches which serve as the vertical curves along which a horizontal line is moved, a condition which these forms can
assume in their undifferentiated state. In this case, although
the form resembles thatofatrue vault, the arches actually carıy
the major structural loads andaa true structural vaultis therefore
not formed. The arches provide unobstructed floor space, and
the resulting semicylindrical form visually assumes the curvature of the arches themselves. The dwellings of the marsh
Arabs of Iraq, for example, utilize bundled quasab reeds for
their primary arch supports and horizontal bracing members;
when woven reed mats are then placed so as to follow the curve
ofthese arches, an undifferentiated horizontal semicylinder is
produced.

Because of their cross-section, horizontal semicyclindrical
forms may function structurally as a series of arches or as
vaults, and they are therefore frequently used as space-spanning roofelements. The choice ofwhich curve is employed may
be based on the ayailability of materials, the building technology employed and the dimensions of the space to be spanned.
Ifthe translated curve is a parabola, the resultingvault utilizes a
cross-section in which the steeper angle of curvature produces
certain structural advantages (the lower the angle of curvature,
the greater the bending stresses in the vault).

Structural vaults of semicircular cross-section are often
termed ‘barrel’ vaults, and they typically surmount rectangular
prisms, the long axis of the vault running parallel to the long
dimension of the prism, establishing a more pronounced
directionality to the prism itself. Found in numerous buildings

throughout the Aegean island of Thira (Santorini) in Greece,
for example, these vaulted roof forms display a profile of geometric simplicity and visual clarity. Here, whether used individually on the simplest of one-room houses (monospito) or in
combination with other vaults and domes in island churches or
chapels, the gentle whitewashed curves of the smoothly
plastered semicylindrical roofs of stone become memorable
aesthetic counterpoints to the basic prisms ofthe island architecture. Ifa series ofarches is structurally incorporated into the
vault itself, a ‘ribbed’ vault is produced, and the roof form may
take on greater visual ‘texture’ ifthe arches, orribs, arerevealed
on the exterior surface ofthe vault.

Increased visual (and structural) complexities occur when
multiple vaults are used. For example, iftwo barrel-vaulted roof

Typology of Forms 1.v11.2
forms intersect at right angles, the semicylinders establish
visually opposing axes above the rectangular prisms they surmount, creating a complex of roof profiles with alternating
transverse and longitudinal directions. The lines of intersection (or groins) ofthe vaults themselves also become important
aesthetic features ofthe roof profile. In their aggregate form, a
series of parallel semicylindrical roofs can form a continuous,
rhythunically repeated profile in which the identity ofeach vault
is visually accentuated. The multi-level, stone storage compounds used by nomadic and semi-nomadic peoples in southern Tunisia, for example, consist ofsuperimposed vaulted units
(ghorfas) whose cross-sections approach that of a semicylinder,
and whose unit repetition is gracefully articulated through the

gentle curves oftheir roofs. GREGORY K. HUNT

627

## S. 208

1.VI11.3 Plans

The plan is a fundamental architectural drawing
that represents the relative arrangement as well as detailed
shape, in the horizontal plane, ofrooms and outdoor spaces in
a building or building group. Plans are used to show spatial
organization, proximity relationships, social hierarchies and
relationships, as well as aspects of structure and construction.
Indirectly, they connote aspects ofsymbolism and cosmological world-views of those who inhabit the buildings. Plans fall
into distincet geometric types that correspond to social organization as well as environmental and contextual responses. The
detailed plan shape that buildings and building groups take on,
within particular typological arrangements, is in turn influenced
by the specifics of site and contingencies that arise during the
building process itself.

Plans fall into distinet morphological types and combinations of types. These shapes and combinations form what is
essentially a taxonomy of plans by which most vernacular
buildings in the world may be classified. The simple plan
forms, such as squares and circles, may be combined into more
complex arrangements, such as concentric rings of circles, or
courtyard forms made of square, peripheral arrays of smaller
square or rectangular plan elements, or rectangular plans
made of a linear sequence of squares or smaller rectangles.
Complexarrangements often arise as the resultofan evolutionary process that begins with simple forms. Basic plan shapes
and their arrangements have various attributes that help
explain building arrangements: square and circular plans connote centrality and focus; linear plans may connote axis and
directionality; plans in which rooms or elements are peripheral
to a larger space may connote a great importance ofthe larger
space; plans in which spaces are layered with respect to each
other may connote increasing levels of privacy with respectto.a
more public realm.

Ata smaller scale, rooms, spaces and their adjacencies are
given form through combinations of various elements: solid
walls, which prevent movement or communication between
the space; arcades, which give spatial definition but also allow
free movement; windows, which allow for views and passage of
light and air, but prevent human movement; and arches and
doorways, which preserve the identity ofthe wall and allow for
human movementand the control ofsuch movement.

Three particular plan elements and their relationship in a
building or building group are often critical in the overall plan
organization, and are used as analytical tools for understanding. These elements are the entrance and connection to the
public realm; the location and shape of the principal space
or spaces; and the principal path of movement (‘eirculation’)

628

connecting the rooms and spaces. These elements are given
their importance and character through the overall morphology ofthe plan. The entrance for example is the symbolic and
functional connection between the public realm and the primary path ofmovement through the building. Its location relative to the interior therefore has great importance. In the
traditional Irish farmhouse for example, the entrance leads
directly into the kitchen which is the main social space of the
house and thereby reinforces the use of the kitchen as an
importantsocial space ofthe community; whereas in the North
African dar the entrance leads into a passage that shields the
main courtyard (which is the primary social space of the
house), from the street and thereby reinforces the private
nature ofthe house with respect to the public realm.

Although plans differ widely from culture to culture, certain
morphological features may be shared among them. For example, regarding the connection of the house to the street, consider three widely differing plan components: the columned
androofed front porch ofa house in the American Midwest, the
raised platform (otla) on the front ofa Hindu house in Gujarat,
and the open entrance courtyard, surrounded bya low wall, ofa
house on an Aegean island. These all serve the same function of
providing an outdoor space that is associated with both the
house and the street, in which various household activities
(preparing food, enjoying the evening, watching children) may
be carried out in a way that allows for neighbourly activity to
take place atthe same time. Spatially and socially, these places
help to join the house to the streetwith functions thatbelongto
both. From a typological point ofview, the spaces are similar in
the three cases: they all act as edges to both the house and the
street, and they are all strongly defined spaces in their own
right. Other such shared features may include spaces that have
centralizing functions (e.g. courtyards, halls and main rooms)
or linear arrangements that help to connect spaces together
(e.g. arcades, galleries, corridors and chains ofrooms).

The plan may be a symbolic representation ofthe cosmological picture held bythe dwellers, helpingto connect daily life with
religious experience and ultimately with the spiritual world. In
many cultures there is a strong relationship between plans of

SInBa PsEROH ©

Plan ofa Hungarian
house.

a entrance

b mainroom
path ofmovement

dings:
Wsisland village
ÜgeCairo house

Davis

Okoward

Front porch

Courtyard

houses and those of religious structures; in Islam, for example,
the first mosque is said to have been the courtyard house of
Muhammad, with a simple shelter erected on one side to shield
those praying from the sun. The circle often carries with it the
symbolism ofthe world or dome ofheaven, with the circumference representing the horizon; the square may be representative
ofthe four cardinal directions. Within the plan, the typical positions of important people, objects or structural members may
help to reinforce the overall cosmological symbolism.

Through their representation of physical arrangements,
plans often provide an accurate map of both social organization as well as the cosmological picture held bythe inhabitants.
Although there is not necessarily a perfect one-to-one mapping
between spatial arrangement and social organization, there
is often considerable correspondence. A highly centralized
plan, for example, in which buildings have similar, equidistant
relationships to an outdoor space (as in a circular village ofthe

Plans 1.V11.3
Trobriand Islands), or in which rooms have similar relationships to a central courtyard (as in a circular collective dwelling
of Fujian province in China) is indicative of a social organization in which the occupants of the various buildings or rooms
have an equal status with respect to each other, but with all
owing allegiance to the group that is itself represented by the
central space. Likewise, in a settlement pattern that contains a
dominant building (for example atthe end ofa streetaas in various villages in Indonesia and Melanesia, or atthe entrance to a
courtyard as in house compounds in sub-Saharan Africa) or a
dominant room (for example the room housing the elders of
the family in some Chinese courtyard houses or the reception
room [ga’a] of large courtyard houses in the urban Middle
East), it can often be inferred that the dominant building or
room houses a person who is socially dominant, or represents
a function that is socially important. An axis of symmetry ina
plan is often indicative ofa direction that is socially or symbolically important, with an important place atone or both ends of
the axis; axes of symmetry which cross at right angles may be
indicative of an important place symbolized by the crossing;
similar elements in a similar relationship to an axis or focal
central space may house people ofsimilar status or relationship.

Plans are indicative of building use, through internal
and external proximity relationships, spatial hierarchies, and

sine piemon 0

## S. 209

1.vıll TYPOLOGIES
orientation to the sun. Two adjacent rooms, oraroom adjacent
to an outdoor space, may be closely related in use. A room
closer to the entrance may be more public in use, while one
deeper into the plan may be more restricted, or belongingto an
important person. The extent to which individual functions
(cooking, sleeping, entertaining visitors, and soon) are
differentiated from each other may also be indicated by the
plan, as well as the ways in which those functions change their
spatial component from winter to summer, or from day to
night.

Building plans are often dependent on climate. Compact
courtyard plans are often found in hot dry climates, where
the shaded courtyard helps cool the house interior, and wherea
relatively short length of exterior wall reduces the total solar
gain. More dispersed and open plans are found in hot, humid
climates, to allow free circulation ofmovingair that helps evaporative cooling. Cold climates often have buildings with compact plans and a heatsource atthe centre, to retain heat, and/or
linear plans, to allow for windows that admit sun along the
southern face ofthe building.

At the same time, plans are constrained by construction
systems, and particularly by the dimensions and structural
spanning capabilities of different materials. A square plan may
be indicative ofa centralized construction system in which the
four walls have the same structural role (as in the trulli
dwellings of Basilicata in Italy, in which the stone walls of a
square room support conical vaults). Arectangular plan may be
indicative of a linear series of repetitive structural bays. Traditional Scandinavian or eastern European log building may have
square plans, with the length ofthe walls limited by the length
ofavailable logs.

Plan types evolve over time, and change as a result of geographical diffusion. Variations may occur ftom culture to
culture, as well as within a particular culture, representing
different stages of typological development, different sizes of
buildings, different contextual conditions of density and/or
position in the settlement, or even different stages in the life of
a particular building. In some cases these changes represent
only minor transformations, in which the original plan is still
clearly evident, as in the differences in the placement of
entrances in typical two- or three-room linearly organized
farmhouses in England. In other cases the plan may change so
radically that the original plan is almost completely hidden
after generations of changes, as in the emergence of attached
and semi-attached row houses in American cities, out of symmetrical, freestanding Georgian antecedents, that themselves
emerged out oftwo-room ‘hall and parlour’ farmhouse plans.

HOWARD DAVIS

1.V1l1.3.a Apsidal

Anapsidal plan is one that includes a projecting elementorapse, usually semicircular in plan, often atone end ofa
larger linear or rectangular shape. The apse helps to emphasize
the linearity or directionality of the shape. Apsidal plans are
often characteristic ofbuildings that have specifically religious,
non-domestic functions, where the apse helps to orient the
building in a particular symbolic direction, and may help

630

enclose a more sacred part ofthe building. This is the case for
example in Eastern Orthodox churches (themselves derivative
ofearlier basilican forms), where the apse marks the end ofthe
space that is the realm of the priests and normally not accessible to the congregants. Apsidal plans are also found in domestic buildings — both those that have similar roots as religious
buildings within their culture and those in which the apse helps
to meeta particular spiritual or symbolic function.

In addition, the apse may arise for pragmatic reasons ofconstruction, in buildings where light-wood rafter-like members
span between the end ofaroof-ridge anda beamatthe top ofa

Plan in Gujarat,

semicircular wall. This eliminates the need to builda gable wall
on at least one end ofthe building. Plans associated with such
construction techniques are found among Tukanoan and
Desana community houses in Brazil and Colombia, the Jivaro
of Peru and Ecuador, in community houses of the Motilon in
Venezuela and Colombia-which have an apse ateither end ofa
building that is about 50 m (160 ft) long-and in Bhil houses of
western India.

The symbolism and use of space within an apsidal plan may
relate strongly to the differences between the two ends, beginning with the placement of the plan on the site. In the
Tukanoan community house the square end of the building
contains the men’s door, and leads into the part ofthe house
associated with men’s rituals; whereas the curved end of the
building contains the women’s door and leads into the part of
the house associated with women’s functions, such as cooking, as well as the individual family units. The plan as awhole is
seen to be suggestive ofthe sexual act.

In the Bhil houses, the main part of the plan is a square
formed by four smaller squares that are marked by cornerposts, and the rounded end by three additional posts forminga
semicircle on one side of the larger square. There is a single
door at the squared end of the plan with the cooking hearth
located deep inside, at the rounded end. Immediately outside
the doorisaveranda containing water-storagejars, and outside
this is a semi-enclosed space of a different, lighter structure
than the house itself. The main part ofthe plan, just inside the
door, is used for various household functions, including the
preparation of food prior to cooking. Variations of this plan
include a circular house without a squared end, and a doubleapsed house, with two semicircular ends. In this case the
veranda occupies the long, straight side ofthe house, between
the two apses.

Apsidal plans may have a strong typological connection to
rectangular plans without apses. The plans of small stone
churches on the Aegean island of Sifnos have one or two naves
each of which ends with an apse. Except for the apses themselves, the plans ofthese buildings are similar to those ofnonreligious function in the same place, notably some potteries
found at the water’s edge, some of which contain two parallel
rectangular rooms. Eastern European log churches, some of
which evolved an octagonal apse at the eastern end ofthe sanctuary, have a basic tripartite plan (narthex, nave, sanctuary),
formed ofsquarelogrooms. These are similar in plan to thetripartite linear arrangement (storage room, entrance passage,
living-room) of many dwelling houses. HOWARD DAVIS
1.V11.3.b Atrium

An atrium is an enclosed court, surrounded by
rooms or other roofed spaces, that is often ata focal position in
the plan ofa building. It may be partly roofed, as it was in some
larger ancient Roman houses, and as it is, for example, in
houses in the fortified villages (ksour) of North Africa. The
atrium is closely related to the courtyard, and the distinction
between them is somewhat ambiguous. For the purposes of
this discussion, the atrium may be considered to be a highly
organized courtyard with formal symmetries thatare similar to

Plans 1.V011.3
those ofthe other rooms ofthe building, and in which the plan
dimensions are relatively small in comparison to the height of
the walls that surround it.

The atrium plan is found most predominantly in hot
climates, and at densities where houses are close-packed with
respect to each other, so as to maximize the privacy of outdoor
space that belongs to the house. In many cases, the relatively
high walls ofthe atrium help to guarantee shade during a good
part of the day, which in turn sets up a temperature gradient
that helps to flush hot air out ofthe house. The position ofthe
atria within the interior often means that family life is almost
completely hidden from the public realm, although in some situations, for example certain haveli in the Indian states of
Rajasthan and Gujarat, there is in addition to the interior
atrium a raised terrace (otla) between the house and the street,
on which various household functions also occur.

Many different variations of the atrium plan may occur.
Buildings may be organized around one or more atria; atria
may be on or off the principal axis of the building; they may
extend up from the ground floor or from some upper floor; they
may be surrounded by walls, arcades, or some combination of
the two; they may be unroofed or partly roofed.

The houses called haveli in Indian towns are good examples
of atrium plans. These houses sit like row or terraced houses
relative to the street or other public space, and are bounded by
party walls, perpendicular to the street, on either side. The
buildings vary in width, depending on wealth and status. The
atrium, called a chowk, is located in the interior ofthe building,
sometimes occupying in small houses the entire width of the
house, providing light to the rooms on either side, and a place
for women’s chores, men’s socializing or sleeping. In this case
the use of the chowk may change during the course of the day.

5

631

sinea paemoH ©

## S. 210

1.vı1 TYPOLOGIES

The room on the street side ofthe chowk is used to receive visitors, and those on the interior for storage and cooking. In wider
houses the kitchen and water storage place may be located
between the chowk and the party wall. Larger versions of these
buildings may have more than one atrium; in some cases, such
as in some houses in Jaisalmer (Rajasthan), the floor level of
different atria may be on different levels ofthe building.

In the ksour in the south ofthe Atlas mountains in Morocco,
the houses represent variations ofthe courtyard plan found in
many North African cities. But because the possibility of outward expansion is extremely limited, houses grow upward and
in a way that often causes the courtyard to be wholly or partly
roofed, often leaving only a small opening in the centre to
admit light. In these cases the central space is like an atrium in
some ancient Roman houses, where the relatively small opening in the roof was used to collect rainwater and to provide
some light. In these ksar houses, the ground floor is often used
for animals and storage, and the upper floors for humans.

HOWARD DAVIS

1.v1l1.3.c Circular

Circular plans are common both for houses and
buildings that serve various agricultural functions. The circular
plan is often aresult ofthe particular construction system used,
but at the same time may result in a symbolically powerful
building form. More than any other shape, the circle represents
the idea of ‘centre’ and buildings that have the most simple
plans, such as circular granaries, may have strong connotations ofeconomic and spiritual value. Among the Pueblo ofthe
North American southwest, the kiva is a circular semi-underground room maintained for ceremonial functions; its location
symbolizes the emergence of people from the underworld and
the connection between earth and heaven; other sacred circular
structures are often also symbols ofthe creation myth.

The circular plan is usually ofaa freestanding, single-room
building, but sometimes may be intricate and labyrinthine,
such as the kraals ofthe Ambo and Zulu of Africa, which contain many individual buildings. It is more typical in buildings
that have wall construction systems thatare ofearth, small unit
masonry, or woven materials rather than those oftimber. In situations where the wall construction is ofwetmud or sun-baked
brick the round plan may be essential to the structural stability
of the building. This is also true for the central Asian yurt,
which is made ofa wooden latticework covered with felt.

The use ofspace within a circular plan may correspond to its
geometry, with various members of the family having fixed
places around the circumference; in many cases the ceremonial
path ofmovement through the plan is in a particular circumferential direction. In many cultures of sub-Saharan Africa, the
circle is the basic plan form for single-room buildings, each
housing a particular function or person. Usually these buildings are aggregated into compounds that house extended families, and in some cases, such as amongthe Massa of Cameroon,
the compounds themselves take on a circular form. In this case
the centre is reserved for the head of the family, with huts
around the circumference for other members.

The circular plan is often found among agricultural build-

632

ings, including granaries that may be justa few feetin diameter,
windmills, animal-driven mills in which the circle accomodates a large wheel that drives the grinding mechanism, grain
elevators, and large barns housing animals and farm equipment. In these cases, the plan is a direct result ofthe particular
function or mechanism contained within the building.
