Circular collective dwellings exist among the Yanoama of
northern Brazil, where lean-tos housing scores of people form
a circular courtyard that may reach around 80 m (260 ft) in
diameter; in some cases the lean-tos nearly reach the centre of
the circle, almost transforming the courtyard into a high interior space. In China, collective dwellings in Fujian province are
made up of circular courtyards up to 50-60 m (165-200 ft) in
diameter surrounded by three storeys of rooms. A single
entrance provides access to the courtyard, and several stairs
give access to interior galleries on the upper two floors. Each
family may occupy rooms on each ofthe three floors, using the
common stairs to communicate among their rooms.

HOWARD DAVIS

1.Vv11.3.d Courtyard

Courtyard buildings and building groups are
among the most ubiquitous in the world, and occur in many
cultures and climates. A courtyard is an outdoor space that has
buildings, rooms or building elements around enough of its
perimeter to give the space clear definition; the courtyard building consists of this outdoor space together with the built elements that surround it. The shape ofthe courtyard is very often
rectangular or square, although other shapes such as round
may be found as well. Its size may vary from just a few metresin
length (as in small houses in Middle Eastern, North African
and Japanese cities) to much larger spaces (as in farmsteads of
North Africa, collective dwellings of China, or urban caravanserai of Cairo).

Courtyard buildings often have an inward focus rather than a
primary orientation to the external street or public space. The
courtyard itself may act as the main ‘room’ of the house,
accommodating a good deal of everyday family life, including
the preparation offood, eating, the reception ofvisitors, or the
passage of animals. In such cases the rooms around the
perimeter of the courtyard may have more specialized functions, such as storage, sleeping, prayer, or they may be rooms
for special occasions or sleeping rooms for particular people or
groups. In many situations the focal nature of the courtyard is
given emphasis by its formal and ornamental treatment: it may
be the most planned and symmetrical space ofthe house, and
the exterior building walls that face inward to the courtyard
may be the mostlavish in terms ofopenings, detailand decoration — with the exterior walls that face the street receiving no
special architectural treatmentatall, save for the main entrance
itself.

The courtyard house is common in the Middle East, North
Africa, the Nile valley, as well as places such as Andalucia, West
Africa, China and Hispanic America. At the edge ofthe Sahara
in Morocco, in situations where the uncertainties ofagriculture
make settlement relatively impermanent, the courtyard of a
farmstead may be irregular in shape, and formed by bushes and
one or two simple circular buildings or rushes and twigs. In
more permanentsituations, courtyards take on more rectangular forms. In traditional Nubian villages in the upper Nile valley,
many of which were flooded after construction of the Aswän
Dam, the courtyard may be rectangular, formed by rooms and
walls made of plastered mud brick, with a covered place for

Plans

1.V11.3

Courtyard

Livi
® Sleeping
> Visitors

| | l l 20ft
j len

Sıneq DIemon ®

water storage along the south side of the courtyard (in the
shape ofthe south wall), and livingrooms alongthe north side,
including a kitchen, guest-room near theentrance, and private
room for a newly married couple. As density increases, courtyards may become even more inwardly focused. In cities that
have been influenced by Islamic traditions and particularly by
the need to protect the privacy of women and the family, the
courtyard is hidden from the street by a labytinthine passage
just inside the entrance, Such buildings may be more or less
elaborate depending on the wealth and status ofthe family: the
courtyard may include covered verandas adjacentto it, and large
reception rooms, sometimes three storeys high, adjacent to it.
In China, the courtyard house ofan extended family is typically laid out on a rectilinear grid, with the main entrance and
theroom housing the elders of the family on one axis, and the
rooms of other family members in subordinate positions. In
some cases (the sanheyuan) a wall separates the courtyard from
the street; in others (the sioheyan) a wing ofthe building that is

1.vıll TYPOLOGIES
passed through after entering separates the courtyard from the
street.

In central, eastern and northern Europe, farmsteads may
be organized around courtyards that have looser collections
of buildings than those in the Middle East. Norwegian farms,
for example, are typically organized around one or two courtyards, with unconnected buildings that each serve a particular
function: where there is a double courtyard, one is typically
for work and family use, and the other for farm animals. In
Germanic and other central European cultures, some farmsteads consist of courtyards formed by connected buildings
and walls containing gates. HOWARD DAVIS

1.v1.3.e Hall

A hall plan is one that is organized around a main
room, called the hall, that is often (but not always) located in
close proximity to the main entrance. This room is usually the
largest in the house; it acts as the primary social space of the
house interior; and provides directaccess, without the intermediary of a corridor or passage, to at least some of the other
rooms. In this sense the interior hall is similar in function and
relationship to other rooms as the exterior courtyard, and
indeed there are halls, such as the sofa ofthe Anatolian house,
thatare thought to be derived from courtyards. Hall plans may
be found in China (where the hall may be adjacent to the courtyard, in what is essentially a hybrid between the halland courtyard types), around the Mediterranean - particularlytheLevant,
Anatolia and the Aegean islands - and in northern and western
Europe including the British Isles.

In the British Isles, the so-called ‘hall and parlour’ house
consists of two adjacent main rooms - the hall housing everyday activities and the parlour traditionally used more for special
occasions. This plan type is related to both the connected terraced house ofEngland, in which a chain oftwo orthree rooms
is separated from adjacent houses by party walls, as well as to
many houses in North American that had their antecedents in
the English colonies.

The central-hall house of the Levant (Lebanon) has its main
room on the upper oftwo storeys, often facing a view, with private rooms on two sides and open on the other two. The plans
ofthese houses are often symmetrical around the hall. In western Anatolia and the southern Balkans, houses may be three or
four storeys tall, with the main hall or sofa on the top floor.
These main living rooms are open on one side to the view, and
have more intimate parlours and bedrooms on the other side.

Ground floor plan Ground floor plan

First floor plan First floor plan

These rooms may be used more in the summer, when the openness allows for a cooling breeze; the lower floors of the house
tend to be more enclosed, with fewer openings, and often used
more in the winter. Variations of these houses appear also in
the Aegean islands, where the main hall is more enclosed,
highly symmetrical, and communicates with bedrooms immediately adjacent. Similarly, many Venetian canal houses are
organized in three parallel bays perpendicular to the canal. The
central bay contains whatis essentiallya hall on each ofthe first
two floors. These are the portego on the ground floor, originally
for the warehousing and shipment of goods, and the salone or
great hall on the first floor, for the reception of important
guests. HOWARD DAVIS
1.v11.3.f Rectangular

Rectangular plans describe buildings whose overall
plan is rectangular, as well as those that are composed of a
series of small rectangular rooms or building parts. The
classification is quite broad, and may include buildings that fall
into certain of the other ‘plan’ categories as well, such as
atrium, courtyard, hall and square. This entry is restricted to
buildings that do not fall into any of these other categories; it
nevertheless includes a very wide variety of buildings, ranging
from the communal longhouses of cultures in South America
and Southeast Asia, to the Japanese timber house and from the
plastered village houses ofthe Hungarian plain, to the Anglo-
American row (terraced) house.

The geometry ofthe basic rectangular configuration gives a
good deal of flexibility in the development of plans, since the
rectangle may be easily divided into smaller rectangles (as in
many urban terraced houses), or combined with others to form
more irregular combinations (as in some Japanese houses).
Plans are often characterized as either ‘single pile’ (which are
composed ofa single linear chain ofrooms, as in the so-called
‘shotgun house’ ofthe southeast United States) or ‘double pile’
(composed of two parallel linear chains ofrooms, as in larger
houses of colonial New England). Rectangular plans are often
associated with construction systems that are composed of
repetitive structural bays that are organized linearly, for exam-

b

ple the timber bents of barns in northwest Europe and the
United States, or the square cells, each roofed with a round
vault, on the brick buildings of the Souf, in Algeria. Within
these plans, the division between structural bays may be
marked by a wall forming distinct rooms (as in the Algerian
buildings) or may be free ofa wall (as in the barns).

From a climatic point of view, the rectangular plan lends
itselfto spatial and room organizations in which rooms may be
naturally placed to that there is maximum exposure toward the
sun (which is often important in temperate climates) or so that
there is maximum opportunity for a cross-breeze (most important in hot, humid climates). Furthermore, the rectangle, particularly when it is fairly elongated, lends itself to subdivision
into individual social or family spaces (as in many communal
longhouses or northern European farm buildings) orallows for
a gradient of privacy or social importance from one end to the

Plans 1.v11.3
other. Often a porch or access corridor or other linear circulation space is parallel to the length ofthe building, either inside
or outside, giving access to the various rooms. This can be seen
in examples such as the Japanese gallery (engawa); the gallery
that often appears on both long sides of the Ukrainian log
dwelling; or the access hallway in the Anglo-American terraced
house. In other cases circulation may be directly from room to
room.

Because of the ease with which rectangular forms may be
combined with each other, the temporal evolution of particular
rectangular plan types is often the result ofadditive processes,
in which additional units are simply added on to the original
building, the expanded building then acting as type or model
for those that are built later. This can be seen, for example, in
the evolution of both Norwegian and Ukrainian log buildings,
as well as English timber-framed houses, many ofwhich began
as single-celled buildings accommodating both people and
animals; then had a second room added to separate the animals; then evolved to include entrance passages and rooms for
specific functions — the basic rectangular plan being maintained throughout all this evolution. HOWARD DAVIS
1.v11.3.g Square

The square plan is the most compact rectangular
plan form. It is found in buildings all over the world, of
different functions and types ofconstruction. Square plans may
be of buildings that are entirely roofed, or of buildings with a
courtyard or atrium in the centre. The square plan may have
importantreligious connotations, (as for example in houses of
the Creek Indians of the American southeast) since axes that
cross at right angles are often seen to be symbolic of the four
cardinal directions. Itis also common for towers, minarets and
fortified buildings.

Square plans, or square units of more complex overall plans,
may be associated with construction systems and materials that
lend themselves to two-way symmetry or exterior walls ofequal
length. These include single-celled logbuildings, in which logs
of similar size are used in the walls, buildings with round
masonry domes supported on squinches that spring out of
walls in a square plan, and buildings with pyramidal hipped
roofs, as for example some Queensland bungalows. Many log
churches of eastern Europe are composed of two or three
squares, linearly arranged, which have pyramidal log roofs. In
another example, the trulli of southern Italy, rooms of square
plan are capped with conical masonty roofs. In both of these
cases, the square plan is roofed with a system that has symmetry with respect to the perpendicular axes ofthe plan below.

From a climatic point of view, the compactness and minimum perimeter of the square plan allows for maximum heat
retention in winter, and such buildings, often of log construction, are found in extreme northern climates, and in temperate
climates with cold winters. It should also be noted that the
proximity of square plan units of earthen buildings in desert
arid zones are cool because they expose fewer surfaces and thus
reduce heating by insolation.

The organization ofrooms and spaces within a building that
has an overall square plan may itself spring from the centraliz-

635

## S. 212

© Howard Davis

1.vıll TYPOLOGIES

ing nature ofthe square shape. Such buildings may consistofa
single space, occupying the entire square, a central space surrounded by smaller spaces on the perimeter (as in many courtyard and atrium houses), as well as buildings where the square
is divided into four or nine smaller squares, (as in some houses
ofthe Bamilike of Cameroon, in village houses in the Balkans,
where one of the four smaller squares may be an open courtyard, and the so-called ‘foursquare house’ of the United
States). The traditional houses ofthe Atoni of Timor have plans
that are roughly square, with four supporting posts (“mother
posts’) on the diagonals inside the four corners. These posts

form a central zone that contains the hearth, and a peripheral
zone that contains various functional elements including
raised platforms, water jars, and storage of household items
and ritual objects.

Collective dwellings, built as a singular unit rather than
being the result of an accretive process of growth, may take on
a square plan shape. Traditional houses of the Bororo of the
Amazon are large collective buildings, with common spaces in
the.centre and individual space for families along the edges.
Moroccan ksour were laid out with each equal side being perhaps 60 m (200 ft) long. Within this square overall plan,
entered only in the middle of each side, a network of narrow
alleys has evolved over time to serve an area that is densely built
with houses thatare themselves ofbasically square courtyard or
atrium plans. In China the Hakka built large square courtyards
surrounded by multi-storey buildings, housing up to several
hundred people; these are similar in organization to the round

dwellings ofthe Hakka. HOWARD DAVIS

References

Allen, Edward, 1969

Norberg-Schulz, ©, and
Bugge, 6., 1969

ma Spatial Relationships

As a heuristic device to understand the built environment on a cross-cultural, cross-temporal level, it is productive to classify domestic dwellings in terms of the amount of
architectural partitioning or compartmentalization and the use
ofspace within those boundaries. We are able to constructsuch
appropriate typologies because culture, behaviour (i.e., the use
of space), and material culture (i.e., architecture) are all interrelated in a predictable and cross-culturally consistent manner.
This predictability and consistency is vital in order to generalize and to understand, the diversity of architecture and the use
ofspace within and between societies.

A useful typology for understanding spatial relationships is
one that is cross-culturally invariable and is based on the
sociopolitical organization of a society. Such a typology stems
from cross-cultural research that demonstrates a consistent
relationship between the complexity of a society, its use of
activity areas, and its partitioning of architecture. In this case,
complexity refers to the amount of social and political
stratification (i.e., more or less equal segments) and hierarchies (ranked or otherwise unequal segments), economic specialization and diversity, and rigidity in the division of labour
and gender roles.

In sociopolitically complex societies, gender roles are usually striet and males and females are thought to be, and are
treated as ifthey are, fundamentally different. In other words,
differences between males and females are emphasized in
more complex societies and are de-emphasized in less complex societies. Groups that organize their social system into a
stratified, hierarchical manner usually organize their political
system similarly (i.e., byage, status, gender, and/or ethnicity).
Complex societies also have economic specialists or individuals who provide services for goods butwho do notacquire food
directly themselves. In other words, such societies conduct
similar tasks to obtain their subsistence (e.g., everyone is a
farmer or everyone is a hunter or gatherer). All societies divide
their culture, behaviour, and built environment to some
degree; however, the amount varies from group to group
and from time period to time period. Complexity and partitioning represent continua. For example, the ‘Basic abode’
described below represents the least amount of complexity
and partitioning while the ‘segmented residence’ represents
the most.

Itis important to note thatthe terms used to specify different
types in this typology are not necessarily the only ones possible.
They are merely convenient labels that are more
useful than numbers (Kent, 1990, 1991). These terms are also
more useful than traditional anthropological labels which

correspond to the ones employed in table ı (i.e., band, tribe,
complex tribe, chiefdom, state). Anthropological terminology
is problematic because there has been no consensus on precise
definitions and because the terms were used in the past as an
evolutionary sequence that no longer is acceptable. The typology proposed here is more narrow in scope than the traditional
anthropological typology so that the ambiguities inherent in
the former are not present. This typology also focuses more
closely on issues important for understanding the use ofspace
and architecture.

Table ı. Examples ofsocieties that belong to each type

Type Examples

Basic Abode
Basarwa, Hadza, Mbote, Ona, Yahgan
Differentiated Dwellings
Blackfeet, Fang, Iban, Inuit, Jivaro, Maasai,
Navajo, Pomo, Tukanoan, Zuni, Yanoama
Divided Domicile
Ainu, Ibo, Iroquois, Kapauku, Kikuyu,
Mountain-OK, Pawnee, Tiv, Tallensi
Partible House
Bhil, Cuna, Fijian, Nootka, Sedentary Somali,
Tikopian, Tlingit, Tswana, Yapese
Segmented Residence
Akha, Anatolian, Azande, Balinese, Burmese,
Greek, Han, Hausa, Iranian, Japanese,
Manchu, Nepalese, Portuguese, Rundi,
Russian, Sinhalese, Slovakian, Tarascan,
Wolof, Yoruba

Typology and architectural partitioning

As noted above, all known societies can be classified as more,
less or equally complex than other societies. The same is true
for spatial relationships. Behaviour may result in the demarcation of activity areas (loci at which activities are performed)
which are separated by the function of the activities or by the
gender, age or status ofthe individuals involved. In some societies, boundaries are conceptual — no physical divides or partitions exist. Nevertheless, conceptual boundaries create discrete
activity areas as rigidly controlled as those created by physical
walls or completely separate structures.

Architecture, like the use ofspace, can be divided into separate activity areas through the use ofphysical partitions, elevated
or sunken areas, changes in construction, interior decoration,
or furniture (e.g., changes in wall design, colour scheme, floor

637

## S. 213

1.vıl TYPOLOGIES
material). A few societies have architecturally similar partitioned loci in which the use ofspace is duplicated in each room
or locus. (For example, houses ofthe Kapauka of central New
Guinea are often partitioned into two male dormitories and
nine rooms for females, and a veranda; the rooms are not
differentiated functionally (e.g. kitchen), butall functions take
place in them, though male and female rooms are distinct; in
other words, the function ofthe different rooms is redundant.)
Commonly, architecturally segregated areas are functionally
differentiated and/or separated by the gender, age or status of
the actors. An example from Euro-American society is that certain rooms are used for related activities (e.g., kitchen, dining
room, or bathroom) or primarily by women or men (such as
laundry or sewing room, or study or garage). Other societies
have architectural partitions that separate individuals of
different social status and/or older and younger individuals.
All modern humans have architecture of some kind,
although it may not be recognizable to people not familiar with
different types. The link between social organization, spatial
relationships, and architectural types occurs in all societies.
For example, gender-segregated activity areas and partitioned
architecture occur only in societies that have some gender segregation in their division oflabour or social or political organization. Groups lacking such segregation do not use many
gender-specific activity areas. The same is true for the separation of functionally discrete activities — use-areas are not
specifically bound by activity functions (only cooking in the
kitchen), age or status of the actors. Although the labels for
how space is organized or for societies with different levels of
complexity may be arbitrary, the types themselves are not. In
fact, the types have been documented in every society examined
(at this point, in over 80 western and non-western ones (Kent,
1990, 1991)).

Architectural and spatial partitioning around the world
Thereliability and utility ofa typology can be tested by applying
it to very different societies from very different regions. It is
necessary to test the reliability of any typology by applying it to
diverse groups. Is the relationship described above consistent
in diverse groups and if not, why? Further, when situations
within a culture change, can we predicttthe influence and direction ofthatchange?

One example is the African pygmy groups such as the Efe
who,when nomadic, used few function-, gender-, age- or statusspecific activity areas (e.g. Fisher). In contrast, Baka pygmy
groups that have been sedentary for decades use kitchen areas
that are detached from the main habitation hut, in addition to
habitation huts which are now physically partitioned (though
not necessarily segregated by function or sex). In a similar response to sedentism, some formerly nomadic Basarwa (‘Bushmen’ or San), who had a relatively non-complex sociopolitical
organization while mobile, currently have been sedentary for
decades or longer. As a result of this sedentism, they have
developed a more stratified sociopolitical organization, a weak
status hierarchy with the introduction of formal leaders or
chiefs, weak economic specialization in the form of part-time
specialists such as healers, and so on (Guenther; Hitchcock and

638

Holm; Hitchcock). Other Basarwa who have become sedentary
more recently (inthe1g70s and 1ı980s) lack these features (Kent,
1989). Thus, it is possible to see the effect of sedentarization
on architecture and the use of space among former nomadic
peoples.

On the opposite end ofthe typology, highly segmented dwellings with much internal compartmentalization (often through
the use ofphysical partitions), is mostcommonly found in highly
segmented, complex societies such as Western or Chinese,
among others. Examination oftheir sociopoliticaland economicorganizations, and other facets of culture, shows much stratification, specialization and hierarchy. Consistent with this
finding, these groups also segregate space and compartmentalize architecture.

Complex societies often separate activities areas into domesticwork and non-domestic work loci which are usually located
in different buildings. What happens to this pattern when nondomestic work is introduced to the domestic dwelling, an
increasingly common occurrence in many Western societies?
As would be predicted from an understanding of the typology,
non-domestic work is segregated from domestic areas. A study
ofBritish houses and the use ofspace within shows thatwhenever possible, people physically separate the non-domestic
work and testrict itto specific rooms or parts ofrooms within a
house (Mars and Mars). In other words, people still maintain
their functionally discrete activity areas. They accommodate
changes in the workplace within the context of activity-segregated space and partitioned architecture.

The use ofa typology based on underlying principles of how
and why people segregate space and architecture is important
to be able to understand past, present and future built environments. Because the associations among culture, behaviour,
architecture and the use ofspace are not arbitrary but co-occur
ina predictable fashion, we can see that the sociopolitical segmentation is a primary principle that influences varying
amounts of spatial and architectural segregation in societies
around the world today and in the past. Mobility patterns also
influence the segregation of space and architecture, but to a
lesser degree. One reason mobility is influential atall is because
it also impacts the amount of sociopolitical stratification and
hierarchies present in a group (Kent, ı989b). In other words, at
one level, all ofthese variables are interrelated - sociopolitical
organization, spatial organization, architectural organization,
and mobility patterns (or the organization of settlement patterns). The use and usefulness of the typology in table ı will
depend on the user’s knowledge of not only the built environment, but also of a group’s sociopolitical organizations and
mobility patterns. The information gained from this knowledge will enhance discussions and understanding of why
eross-cultural differences exist in architecture and activity
areas. SUSAN KENT
1.V1ll.4.a Basic abode

The least partitioned and most basic structures are
small and undifferentiated huts, wind-breaks, and other minimally altered shelters. Groups who inhabit this type of ‘basic
abode’ architecture tend to be highly mobile hunter-gatherers

See also
2.11.6.g Kapauka
3.v11.2.d Baka

References
Fisher, John, 1986
Guenther, Mathias, 1975
Hitchcock, Robert, 1987
Hitchcock, Robert, and
Holm, John, 1985
Kent, Susan, 19892,
1989b, 1990, 1991
Mars and Mars, 1992

‚wawind-break,
‚a, Botswana.

also
‚n Mbote
(10a Basarwa

Ktences

Susan, 1989, 1992,
),forthcoming

ioV, A., 1984

Linda, 1987
‚Richard, 1979

jauer, George, 1981

who have little or no sociopolitical or economic stratification or
specialization and no gender, age or status hierarchies. The
Basarwa (or ‘Bushmen’, San) hunter-gatherers ofthe Kalahari
desert, Botswana are an example ofthis type. The Basarwa are
strongly egalitarian compared to other societies, with very little
or no stratification or hierarchies in any facet of their culture
(Kent, 1989, 1992, 1993; Lee; Silberbauer). Informal leaders
tend to have power that is situational and ofbrief duration.

During the dry season, Basarwa perform daily activities
around a hearth located out-of-doors. They also sleep at an
open hearth that is partially surrounded by a wind-break made
ofbrush. This type of very basic structure makes sense in terms
ofthe Basarwa’s nomadic mobility pattern. During the rainy
part ofthe year, the same Basarwa use asmall, non-partitioned
hut, often made ofbrush. Huts are primarily used to escape the
rains and/or oppressive heat when shade is not available,
although most activities are still conducted at an outdoor
hearth. Notonlyarethe hutand wind-breaknotconceptuallyor
physically partitioned, but there are few, ifany, activity loci that
are used exclusively by one sex or a particular age group.
Neither are loci used for arelated range of activities, thatis loci
are not function-specific. No gender hierarchies or rigid division oflabour occur, such as those in western cultures (Kent,
1993).

We can examine a number of nomadic non-complex (in
terms of their sociopolitical organization) hunter-gathering
societies inhabiting regions around the world and find that
they organize space similarly to the Basarwa. African pygmy
groups, such as the Mboti, Aka, Baka and others, occupying
tropical rain forests of Central Africa are good examples ofthis
particular type. The Agta of the Philippines and Yahgan of the
tip of South America also belonged to this type. ‘Basic abode’
societies are notrestricted to tropical or non-tropical locations.
In this typology, climate and the general environment are not
very influential.

There is nothing inherent in the hunting-gathering subsistence strategy that encourages the use of unelaborated abodes
as are found in this category. There are numerous semi-seden-

Spatial Relationships 1.VI1.4
tary or sedentary hunter-gatherers who do not belong to this
type (e.g., the American Northwest Coast Indians or some
American Plains Indians). The latter groups have more complex sociopolitical organizations and, as a result, a more partitioned built environment. Although there is an apparent
correlation between mobile groups and architecture, itis nota
causal one. There are some mobile non-hunter-gatherers who
do not belong to the basic abode type (e.g., some pastoralist
groups).

The key to architecture type is not, then, the subsistence orientation ofa people, nor the environmentthey occupy. Mobility
is influential only because it is associated with the amount of
sociopolitical complexity present in a group - it is not influential in and of itself (i.e., mobility and spatial and architectural
partitioning is a spurious correlation rather than a causal one;
the causal association is the one between spatial and architectural partitioningand sociopolitical organization). So, although
not common, there are groups who are nomadic, for example
the Bedouin, who are relatively sociopolitically complex,
despite their mobility pattern. Because of their sociopolitical
complexity, they do not use non-partitioned basic abodes as do
the groups subsumed under the basic abode type. Instead they
tend to divide their tents into gender- and status-segregated
activity areas. In one sense they carry their architecture with
them in that they do not completely abandon their dwellings as
do the Basarwa and others. Complex nomadic groups tend
simply to move the location, but keep the dwelling (i.e., the
tent) and relocate it (Kent, forthcoming). Some scholars claim
that such mobile, highly complex societies as state-level pastoralists are not independent societies. They form instead an
integral part of an overall sedentary agriculturalist society
(Khazanov). These examples of nomadic, but sociopolitically
complex, societies illustrate that although mobility, sociopolitical complexity and spatial partitioning are often correlated,
only the latter two are causally so. Thus groups such as the
Bedouin come under ‘segmented residence’ in table ı above, in
spite oftheir mobility. SUSAN KENT

1.Vl1.4.b Differentiated dwellings

The category ‘differentiated dwellings’ includes
multifarious subsistence strategies, mobility patterns, environmental zones, and habitation construction materials and
forms. Groups’ subsistence practices vary from hunting-gathering to horticulture to pastoralism. Mobility patterns range
from nomadic to sedentary. Members of this type occupy environments which include deserts, rain forests, savannas, step,
tundra and others. Some societies are organized into lineages,
others into clans, and a few moieties (dual division ofthe society). What the societies in this otherwise diverse category have
in common are limited sociopolitical stratification, economic
specialization and hierarchies. They also share limited spatial
partitioning and limited architectural segmentation. Their
division of labour is more restrictive than it is for the societies
who belongto the ‘basic abode’ type, butis less rigid than those
found in the ‘divided domicile’. Groups in this differentiated
type tend to use more bounded activity areas, which are sometimes architecturally segregated by a partition.

639

## S. 214

© Susan Kent 1984

1.Vv1l

TYPOLOGIES

The unifying attributes in this type are the societies’ weak
social stratification, part-time economicjreligious specialists,
slight to moderate gender differentiation, centralized authority
in the form of one or more informal, part-time chiefs determined by achieved status, and weak hierarchies. These attributes occur in concert with huts or tents that have some
differentiation based on gender, age status or activity function.
Often this differentiation is based on conceptual boundaries
which are codified by the use ofspace within dwellings and are
enforced by unwritten rules that specify the appropriate use of
specific loci.

Good examples of this type come from the various Pueblo
Indian farming societies that inhabit the American Southwest.
Unlike groups in the ‘basic abode’ type, most societies have
clans and lineages and/or moiety systems, in addition to anumber ofceremonial or otherwise specialized associations headed
by shamans, chiefs or other types of leaders that cross-cut the
tribe (Dozier). Pueblo Indians traditionally live in masonıy
apartment-like houses that formed a village, today known by
the Spanish term, pueblo, meaning town. They do not use gender-segregated space but do use some physically partitioned,
functionally discrete loci. For example, they use rooms exclusively for storage, such as granaries specially built for storing
surplus crops of cultivated plants. There are also separate
structures (kiva) for ceremonialand community-wide activities
and village dance plazas that are at least partially bounded by
apartment rooms or a low wall. The latter are used for public
rituals which, among some groups such as the Tewa Indians,
are performed atleast four times ayear ateach ofthe four dance
areas in a village (Ortiz).

Kitchens are often located on a roof or patio and, according
to some early descriptions, they are conceptually segregated
use areas (Cushing). Small rooms, including many situated on
the ground floor, are specially built for the baking of the thin,
blue paper-like piki bread. A small field house consisting of a
few rooms or just a brush shade (ramada) is often located near
the fields and used during planting and harvest seasons (the
kiva area is usually absent) (Mindeleff). Pueblo Indians clearly
segregate their use ofspace and partition their architecture by
activity and function more than do societies belonging to the
‘basic abode’ type.

A large number of societies belong to this ‘differentiated
dwelling’ type. Copper Inuit use iglus in the winter and tents in

640

the summer and have a separate dance house for ceremonies
(Jenness). In contrast, mobile Blackfoot Indians use tents
throughout the year, butthey use hides or blankets physically to
partition a separate storage area for ceremonial paraphernalia.
Navajo Indians conceptually, through use, segregate male
hogan space from female hogan space, a hogan being the traditional winter dwelling. However, their summer dwelling, or
ramada, is not conceptually or physically divided (Kent, 1982,
1984). The Amazonian Jivaro Indians build only one functionally discrete area. These areas are storage platforms located
along the walls of a multi-family dwelling (Harner). Like the
Navajos, they have conceptually separated male and female
space within the dwelling.

Archaeological remains indicate that some groups in the
pastexhibited similar features in their builtenvironmentand in
what can be reconstructed of their sociopolitical organization,
for example for Pueblo II, mid-ı000 AD, Mesa Verde Anasazi
prehistoric Indians (Kent, 1992). This allows us to see thatthe
typology is relevant not only for modern societies, but also for
prehistoric societies. SUSAN KENT

1.Vill.4.c Divided domicile

Groups which belong to the ‘divided domicile’ type
practise diverse subsistence strategies, but tend to be either
semi-sedentary or sedentary in their settlement patterns. As is
true for the other types, this category is notrestricted to any single environment or geographical location. Societies encompassed in this type have moderate sociopolitical stratification,
hierarchies based on gender and status, and part-time specialists. While the position of the chief is achieved rather than
ascribed, it is actively sought and maintained by ambitious
individuals. These chiefs are sometimes referred to as ‘big
men’. Occasionally there are chiefs who are hierarchically
arranged with greater and lesser authority. Differences between
men and women are emphasized. The division of labour is not
as flexible as that found in less complex societies. Society is also
frequently partitioned by age grades and religious societies
which provide varying status throughout one’s life.

More physical partitioning is common in this category. Segregation by gender is particularly common; rooms for men and
women are often separated by physical walls. Those societies
without physical wall partitioning usually have conceptual partitioning that is equally rigid. Functionally discrete areas are
also more prevalent but not as common as they are in more
complex societies. Architectural differences based on wealth
first occur in this type, although they become much more frequent and ostentatious in more complex societies. Similarly,
conceptual or physical partitioning by age is present in these
dwellings, but become much more pronounced in more complex societies.

Semi-sedentary Japanese Ainu hunter-gatherers are a good
example ofthe ‘divided domicile’ type. Traditionally there were
separate summer and winter houses. Both consisted ofa single
room partitioned by mats (Ohnuki-Tierney) into sacred areas,
gender-specific loci, and generation-segregated loci wherein
children slept apart from adults (Munro). Separate storage loci,
including huts used exclusively for storage, were organized

See also
3.V1.7.h-ii Navajo
3.V1.7.1 Tewa

1992 u

Mexico, United states“

nee earth lodge.
‘ko, Oklahoma,

also

sk Pawnee

etences

„110, Neil, 1963

\ki-Tierney, Emiko,
2

“llish, Gene, 1965

according to the gender ofthe owner of the objects. The Ainu
chief”s dwelling was much larger than the others and was used
for ceremonies (Munro).

The Kikuyu are another group in this category in Kenya. They
occupied compounds that consisted of separate huts for men
and women. Women’s huts were segregated into compartments which minimally included a bedroom, storeroom,
daughters’ bedroom, and a space for goats and sheep (Kenyatta). In contrast, men slept in separate huts which commonly
were partitioned into a sleeping area and a general entertainment area. Kikuyu, then, have gender segregated space, functionally-specific space, and age-separated space.

Pawnee Indians from the North American plains provide
another illustration. They traditionally used tents (tipis) while
in transit, ramada-like structures in the summer, and earth
lodges during the winter (Dorsey and Murie). Chiefs and
shamans had conspicuously larger dwellings than others, an
example of wealth differentiation not found in less complex
societies. Also, individuals of higher status sat near the west
wall ofthe lodge and no one of lower status sat close to them
(Weltfish). Willow mats partitioned space including the sacred
altar area within the earth lodges (Linton). Mats also screened
offthe sleepingareas ofwomen. Hide curtains hungatallother
beds to separate the sleeping areas from the multipurpose centre of the lodge (Weltfish). Specialized storage loci also were
segregated.

While not identical, these dwellings as a group have more
segregation by gender, age, status and activity function than
any of those described in the previous sections. This segregation is consistent with the segmentation in the sociopolitical
organization, division of labour, and economics of the societies. Because gender roles are more rigid and differences
between males and females are emphasized more, gender-segregated space becomes more common and important. Because
male and female activities differ and are hierarchicallyarranged
according to specialization and status, functionally discrete
lociare more common than in less complex societies. Children
routinely have their own partitioned areas, which indicates
increasing differentiation between adults and children. This is
sometimes organized into age grades or age sets.

SUSAN KENT

Spatial Relationships 1.v1.4
1.Vl1.4.d Partible house

Societies that compose the ‘partible house’ type are
sociopolitically complex. They have a rigid division of labour
based on gender and often on status, a stratified and often
ranked social system with sociopolitical classes, and a pronounced status, gender and occupation hierarchy. Unlike less
complex societies, status is usually ascribed (born into) rather
than achieved. The hereditary position of chief commands
much political power. Chiefs usually have assistants who are
from the noble or upper class. These individuals are stratigraphically under the chief but are above the rest of society.
Paramountchiefs often preside over lower chiefs who rule a single village. Full-time occupational specialization is common
and is often based on gender and/or status. ‘Partible house’ category societies are usually sedentary. (American Northwest
Coast Indians are a possible exception being semi-sedentary.)
They are based on a mixed economic strategy of horticulture,
domesticated animals and fishing and/or hunting.

Although in some respects the Northwest Coast Indians are
exceptions to the general pattern of sedentism and use of
domesticated animals in that they were traditionally huntergatherers, they provide a good example in terms oftthe rest of
their culture and in terms oftheir use ofspace and architecture.
Houses owned by nobles, particularly those who were also
chiefs, are often larger than those owned by commoners. Longhouses that are partitioned by stacks of boxes or mats (among
other ways) are occupied by multiple, related families. Houses
are separated into status-discrete loci. In some Northwest
Coast societies, slaves are often forced to occupy the least
comfortable part of the house located next to the entrance
(Emmons). In these houses, sleeping platforms are separated
from the sitting platforms underneath them and are an example of functionally segregated areas. Many Northwest Coast
groups build separate structures for ceremonies, for housing
canoes, or for dry-smoking fish to prepare it for storage (e.g.
Drucker). In the 1990s, Northwest Coast Indians inhabit Western houses and, unlike less complex societies also occupying
Western housing, such as the Navajo, they use different rooms
for functionally restricted activities.

Polynesian houses are conceptually separated by gender,
age, and class and/or rank. Parts of the houses are also functionally specific (Firth; Thompson). So, although physical
boundaries such as walls, are absent, conceptual ones are rigid
and dictate the use of space within the dwellings. Functionspecific cook houses and canoe houses are completely separate
buildings. Similarly, separate storage huts are expressly used
for the storage of various types of objects. Separate bachelor
houses are used by unmarried men. Some Polynesian societies
have separate male clan houses which are occupied by adult
men only. Consistentwith the sociopolitical organization, gender stratification and division oflabour, and economic specialization, the Polynesian built environment includes houses
which exhibit differentiation based on status and/or wealth, the
function of the activity performed, or occupation primarily by
one gender.

The same patterning occurs among the very different Tswana
agro-pastoralists of Botswana. Compounds are composed of

641

## S. 215

1.vı TYPOLOGIES

separate storage, sleeping, and cooking huts (Larsson and
Larsson). When possible, older children sleep in genderspecific huts rather than in the huts oftheir parents. Servants of
the wealthy live in different dwellings from their employers
(Hardie). Political activities are conducted at a separate locus
called a kgotla, which is often not located within the compound
ofa chief. The chief sits in a reserved status-specific spot at a
kgotla, and is surrounded by his advisors who are also of high
rank.

Complexity refers to a whole composite of parts. The built
environment in complex societies definitely consists of many
parts and many segmented activity areas just as their sociopolitical organization consists ofindividual classes and ranks that
form hierarchies. As these societies interact with western culture, interesting changes can be seen in building materials and
architectural form, but not necessarily in the use ofspace or in
the partitioning of the built environment. From Cameroonian
chiefdoms to American Northwest Coast Indians, people are
adapting Western architecture to non-Western needs. In
Cameroon, for example, thatched roofs are beingreplaced with
aluminium roofs and indigenous building styles are replaced
with western architectural styles, but each of a chief’s wives
maintains her own separate hut, the meeting hall for the nobles
remains in a spatially segregated house or area, and the chief
occupies a large, opulent dwelling in the community. In these
cases, the traditional use of space remains, although the construction materials and architectural form may change (Ritzenthaler and Ritzenthaler). SUSAN KENT
1.VI.4.e Segmented residence

The ‘segmented residence’ type comprises the most
sociopolitically complex societies. Although perhaps most
often epitomized by Western cultures, it actually consists of
many more non-Western cultures and those of industrializing
countries (the latter representing a blending of traditional and
Western cultures). These groups have extreme sociopolitical
stratification and rigid hierarchies. The Indian caste system is a
good example ofthe latter. Classes are notonlyseparated based
on social status and political power, but on economic wealth
and often ethnicity as well (in contrast to ‘partible house’ type
societies). Unlike the other types described, leaders do notrule
by kinship and there are non-kin control groups (e.g., military
and/or police). Full-time economic specialization is common
and the division of labour is quite rigid. Differences between
males and females are emphasized and gender hierarchies
transcend politics, status, and economic/subsistence pursuits.
Most ofthese societies are sedentary agriculturalists. Extremely
complex societies can be characterized as having a segmented
culture (e.g., stratification in social segments and hierarchies
in vertical segments) and behaviour (e.g., occupational specialization in economic segments and a rigid division of labour
based on gender-specific segments).

In these extremely complex societies, culture and behaviour
are highly segmented as are the use of space and architecture.
This can be seen in the frequency of gender-specific activity
areas, which are partly a consequence of the rigid division
of labour and partly a consequence of the gender hierarchy.

642

Axel-var, 1976
jippe, Pierre, 1956
(cisL. K., 1948

Jana, 1987
1973

Segmented architecture takes the form of rooms or houses
with functionally restricted, age-restricted, gender-restricted,
ethnicity-restricted and/or status-restricted physically partitioned areas. These segments or compartments may be physically partitioned by mats, walls, elevation (i.e., multi-storey see also for 1.y11ag
structures), curtains, sunken floors and furniture. Individuals 3-Y!'.n Tlingit

" R R BR 3.V1.2.i-i Kwakiutl
can, and sometimes do, further modify physically partitioned 3.vı.10.h Tswana
areas through specialized usage ofa part ofaroom.

Chinese houses provide a good example of an extremely
complex society’s segmentation of the built environment.
Domestic dwellings are quite distinet from non-domestic
buildings used for religious, health, educational, or bureaucratic activities. A two-storey courtyard house in Yunnan,
China, is usually partitioned into individual bedrooms, storerooms, living-rooms, shrine rooms, kitchens, weaving rooms,
toiletand courtyard (Hsu). Even the smaller houses ofthe poor
minimally have separate bedrooms, a shrine room, a storage
loft/room, and a livinglworking room. Houses of the wealthy
often also contain guest-rooms, studies and servants’ rooms
(Kulp). Note that there are a profusion of functionally restrictive activity areas which are physically partitioned in this (and in
other) extremely segmented societies. Gender-restrictive, status-restrietive and age-restrictive loci are also common in these
dwellings. In addition, the use of non-domestic buildings and
space become very important in societies belonging to this
type. These include separate structures for religious, business,
education, medical, and political activities, justto name a few.

It is interesting that some extremely complex societies use
different one-room huts to partition space as Westerners use
rooms. For example, the Azande agriculturalists of Zaire live in
compounds thatconsistofmonofunctional and gender-specific

huts. A compound has sleeping huts, kitchen huts, storage
huts and/or elevated granaries (under which a sitting room is
located), age-specific huts (adults and children) and genderspecific huts (DeSchlippe). Many storage areas are
separated into a living/work room and separate storage room
bya blind or by suspended banana leaves.

Zulu of South Africa also have compounds with a variety of
huts. Great huts have three areas - the far back ofthe hut, the
hearth, and the doorway. The former is used only for storage

Spatial Relationships 1.V1.4

and is partitioned from the rest of the dwelling by being elevated above the floor (Berglund). Dwellings are divided into
male and female areas and sleeping and seating arrangements
are arranged according to status (Raum). Compounds additionally contain separate cooking huts, individual huts for each
wife, beer-brewing huts, storage huts, individual huts for
unmarried adolescents of both sexes, a private hut for the
compound head and sometimes a separate hut for each of his

married sons (Oswald). SUSAN KENT

## S. 216

ans Structural Types

In architecture the term ‘building’ encompasses all
structural assemblies, constructed to define or enclose any
artificial space, which combine to accommodate a certain
human activity, while the term ‘structure’ in its broadest sense,
i.e. not limited exclusively to carrying or supporting structures,
generally designates each material constituent ofthe form ofa
building. Within the logic ofa gradual progression in the architectural process, structures can be considered as the stage
between the initial material statement of the building concept
and the final, completed building.

This dual definition gives rise to two compatible orders of
factors: on the one hand, those that are more specifically natural, and related to the material resources of each specific geographical area, and, on the other hand, the more specifically
human and cultural factors, especially the tendency to build to
satisfy certain needs as much for expressive as for utilitarian
purposes.

As for the assessment of natural factors, there is no doubt
that the prevalence ofa particular material usually impresses
certain characteristics on local buildings which in time become
typical constants. In this sense, four leading geomaterial areas
can be distinguished (Cataldi, 1986): three linked to the most
widely diffused natural building materials - wood, earth and
stone - and the fourth, in more restricted particular areas, to
the remaining possible specialized materials, including
artificial ones.

However, in assessing human and cultural issues numerous
other factors have bearing, including historical ones concerning the temporal progress ofindividual human groups in their
various natural environments. In the specific field ofbuilding,
these elements are theoretically summed up synchronically by
the definition ofbuildingtype as an a priori synthesis (Muratori)
(i.e. as an operative dwelling concept, which varies in spaceand
time for each individual social group), and diachronically from
the definition of typological process (Caniggia). Taken as the
logical development ofa series of changes in a certain type of
building, this is a process whose main transformations can, in
some measure be scientifically rebuilt a posteriori.

In other words, traditional structural techniques appear to be
the result of a slow process of identification and convergence
between environmental resources and human building aspirations. This process is not necessarily linear and tends to optimize certain building solutions, best suited to making asystem
of multivariable congruent and compatible design equations,
thus narrowing the range of possible individual buildings.

To have an overview ofthe structural typologies ofvernacular
architecture, it is necessary to refer methodologically to the

644

basic principles at the root of each architectural structure
(Cataldi, 1981): the static system, or the technical procedure of
material aggregation; and the functional position, ortherole of
the structure in the organic building complex.

Static systems

A static system does not necessarily entail a dependent and
derivative relationship between the aggregative mechanism
and the material forming the structure. In practice, however,
the creation and optimization of a certain building technique
seem to be historically linked, in the majority of cases, to the
availability or lack ofa certain type ofbasic material in a particular geographical area.

In this sense, the previous conventional delimitations ofgeomaterial areas macroscopically define also the geographic
range ofthe two leading static systems: the elastic system connecting elements byjoints which distinguishes many timbered
areas, and the heavy system of placing elements one on top of
the other, characteristic ofstony areas.

These two distinct structural positions seem to have generated through derivation and specialistic adaptation, two major
categories of static systems: the so-called ‘taut system’ (a specialistic derivative of the elastic system), in which stability is
provided by cable traction; and the so-called ‘driving system’, a
specialist derivative of the heavy system, where stability is
obtained through the introduction ofsloping or wedge-shaped
elements in mutual interaction. The resulting picture is
enhanced by the possible in-between solutions, which provide
evidence of cultural exchanges and gradual evolution of methods, and above all completed by the simplest, most basic primary system, the ‘self-cartying’ system, in which statically
autonomous and independent material components are reciptocally juxtaposed, aggregated purely through their being
present together.

Therefore, it is necessary to point out that even though the
above-mentioned systems are established only in certain geomaterial areas, where they assume technically and figuratively
stable conformations particularly in relation to the use of the
best suited materials, their static generating principles are
nascent in the most elementary wooden structures.

To give an example, various structural schemes were compared in a table. Here the interpretation is given by the columns
placed side by side ofthe various static systems which, seven in
number, proceed from the top downwards in progressive complexity. Thereciprocal differences increase the furtherone moves
away from the centre towards the lateral columns (self-supporting static systems, respectively based on compression and trac-

tion), according to the distinction (fundamental in timber technology) between stiff, heavy woods and light, flexible woods.
The latter are considered in terms of the limits of flexibility of
vegetable or animal fibres which are used in the purely tensile
system ina condition where they are constantly stressed.

The central columns placed side by side represent the two
above-mentioned main systems, the bending elastic system,
and the heavy system, which is more developed from the technological point of view, because thicker trunks are used. The
middle columns indicate the spurious transitional systems
between the ‘taut-compressed’ system — from which the black
Bedouin tent, hammock and suspension bridge originate-and
the ‘driving’ system — the structural matrix of conical tents
and the more sophisticated framework of the chorama diu of
Siberian Evenkis.

Functional positions

The assessment ofthe position and role ofa certain structural
element in a building, from the constructional point of view,
does not only lay down a hierarchic criterion which distinguishes between individual parts ofthe total structure, but is of
fundamental importance in order to have a general understanding of the evolutionary mechanisms of vernacular architecture.

In this sense, the numerous various functional positions can
be classified into four main categories.

The first category, which can be defined as basic, originates
from the fundamental human relationship with the ground,
with which itestablishes a horizontal dwelling erection surface
- required for sleeping on, if for nothing else — and is secon-

darily sometimes raised off the ground as a hygrothermal
defence against damp and flood. An archetype is the pallet, in
its more evolved version of the platform bed, which in turn
influences the matrix offloors.

The second category, defined as elevation, originally served
the purpose of delimiting surface space, tending mainly to distinguish natural outer space from internal space which is ofrelevance to humans. This is important architecturally and
socially, especially with regard to privacy and ownership, part
ofthe legal foundations ofsedentary cultures. Fences and their
archetype, already nascent in outer foundation furrows and in
ritual alignments, which may be presumed to have given rise to
walled structures.

The third category, defined as the roof, is unquestionably the
mostimportant from theaarchitectural point ofview on account
of its capacity to create an enclosed space. In this sense, it is
fundamental to the concept ofthe shelter dwelling archetype,
which, with the interposition of raised structures, later developed into a hut to enable daily functions to be also carried out
within.

Lastly, the fourth category that we can define as ‘voiding’,
serves the purpose of opening up holes or rooms in the previous structures; these voids are necessary not only to provide
access to people, objects and animals but also to let in air and
light. This problem can easily be solved in discontinuous bearing and open-sided non-closing structures but becomes complex in continuous, load-bearing and enclosed structured,
where the opening ofa hole usually alters the static equilibrium
ofthe structure, which has then to be restored with a technical
artifice, such as a lintel or ring beam.

645

## S. 217

1.v1l

TYPOLOGIES

Structural evolution

Before reviewing major structural typologies, it is necessary to
indicate their presumed original relationships according to the
results ofa broad evolutionary approach to vernacular architecture. This picture, in the form ofa table, schematically includes
the most widely diffused wooden and earth structures in the
world ofethnological and traditional cultures. However, itdoes
notttake into accountthe more complex and historically evolved
stone structures, which lie outside the vernacular traditions.

In the table, the first horizontal strip of archetypical access
structures clarifies and fixes the basic structural idea: from
fixed, elastic or rigid poles, to successive variants of the bent
arch, the mutual contrast arch, and the trilinear structure and
wind-braced pole ofblack tents.

Each ofthese diagrams in turn produces vertically the structural equivalent ofa type of building of primary importance in
the evolution ofhuman homes.

The lower half of the figure takes into consideration raised
structures, whose palisades are, as stated beforehand, the
archetypes in the self-supporting version with separate elements.
They seem to have given rise to special curtains and trellises,
developed respectively in nomadic desert and proto-agrarian cultures, intertwining flexible branches.

This last type of structure in turn initiated the first building
use ofearth: wooden interstices are either covered by a continuous layer of earth - an early form of plaster, which progres-

sively lined both sides ofthe trellises to thicken until it became
areal wall with inner reinforcement- or, ifthey are more widely
spaced, they are plugged with daub, made out ofearth by hand.
Hypothetically those initial structures engendered two more
evolved earth construction techniques, the piseand adobe, which
differ on account of the dimension of materials and assembly
type: in the former, blocks are pressed inside special forms,
while in the latter, crude prefabricated bricks are placed one on
top ofthe other in layers and made integral by layers of mortar
in between, that are mainly ofearth.

Lastly, the table includes two archaeologically documented
wooden techniques still widely diffused in Europe, in central
and northern regions: in the Alps the log construction (see
1.IX.5.d) and in the British Isles the box frame, probably evolved
from variations ofthe palisade. GIANCARLO CATALDI
1.V11.5.a Box frame

Aconstructional technique thatis generally applicable using any flexible material, in traditional architecture, is
almost always fabricated in wood; in fact, it is one ofthe most
advanced structural uses of wood, on account of its excellent
stability relative to the quantity ofmaterial used.

The box frame is basicallya static elastic system consisting of
frames forming a three-dimensional network of rods and
joints. Technically speaking, these terms are applicable to any
kind of linear elements, whether horizontal, vertical or diago-

646

me dovecote,
Mercia, England.

nal, reciprocally bound at the ends. Connecting procedures
vary depending on the know-how of the building culture that
implements them. From this point ofview, vernacular architecture features a wide range of solutions, from rudimentary fastening to the more practical, quick nailing, to the pegging or
riveting of various types of fixed joint. Albeit in an extremely
rich, complex range, the latter can generally be referred to as
the ‘mortise and tenon’ type, likewise called female and male,
that best enables structural continuity between linear elements
forming the joint. The halved joint is among the most important ofthe alternative jointing systems.

In this regard, the diagonal reinforcement and wind-bracing
rods that involve complex joints are statically necessary as they
geometrically reduce the quadrangular networks of the structure, which are subject to possible lateral deformations, to
stabilizing triangles.

In this way, alight, resilient cage is obtained, whose carıying
capacity is due essentially to the contribution of every component to the strength of the entire structure. More specifically,
bending, or compression and traction composite stress, characteristic ofthe elastic system is transmitted, through joints, by
the horizontal purlins to the vertical uprights so that the frame
ofthe entire structure reacts jointly and homogeneously to the
various load situations.

However, in order to be complete, the building requires a
protective enclosing envelope, which usually has to be external
and fitted between consecutive piers. These walls can be builtin
various ways and in different materials, and they tend to stand
out from the supporting grid, which is usually left on view. This
gives rise to a decidedly picturesque ensemble founded on the
colour or texture contrast between the linear wooden elements
and the infill panels. These frequently consist of a light woven
wall and sometimes in special cases, of more solid decorative
brick nogging.

The two main ways ofdividing up the walls ofthe box frame,
with long, narrow rectangular panels, or with square panels,
externally reveal two different structural conceptions of roofs.
These are both based on the concept oflightness, and may lead
to the exclusion of heavy purlins, such as ridge purlins, which
however, are characteristic oflog construction. In the former
case, the roof is supported by a close-set series of couples
of sloping rafters, joined at the top and stiffened just below by
a short element or tie known as a collar; in the latter case,
some of these couples (at regular intervals and with a corresponding number of tie-beams) are reinforced and more
solidly joined, assuming the role of triangular trusses.
Sometimes these feature ‘crown’ posts; each supporta longitudinal collar purlin, on the tie-rod in a central position, on which
the collars of the couples of intermediate struts rest in
turn (Brunskill, 1971).

Box frames are documented archaeologically in Europe
from as early as the Iron Age, such as can be seen in the
remains of the building excavated in England in Meare Lake,
near Glastonbury (Davey). Its structural typology seems to be
similar in construction to that of the Etruscan houses of
Acquarossa (Östenberg). Chronologically speaking, if there
were more reliable assessments of ancient dwelling finds, like

Structural Types 1.v1ll.5

the pile-dwellings in the Alpine and Danube areas (Buti), the
box frame system might be shown to date back to the neolithic
age.

During the Roman age, this technique must definitely have
been popular. Vitruvius cites it in his treatise, while advising
against its use in view of the risk of fires and cracking. Box
frames are to be found in Ercolano in the so-called ‘lattice
houses’ (woven wall) they hint at an uninterrupted, historic
continuity from the Iron Age to the threshold ofthe industrial
age.

However, it was during the Middle Ages that the box frame
reached its peak and formal perfection, becoming in Great
Britain, as in Germany and in the majority ofcentral and northern European regions and in the Balkan peninsula (Buti), the
most widely diffused structural type, found in both the country
and town alike. In fact, itstill characterizes buildings in numerous regions, cities and small towns, where it has also been used
in important buildings, such as in the famous Kammerzell
house in Strasbourg.

It may be noted in this regard how the medieval area of
diffusion of the box frame more or less corresponds with that
ofthe great European Gothic ecclesiastical tradition implicitly
raising the issue of ts possible initial influence on the developments ofthis style ofarchitecture.

Lastly, in non-European vernacular architecture, the box
frame is likewise present and diffused here and there, especially in the Asiatic areas of the Far East, where it manifests
interesting specific characteristics, such as the use ofbamboo,

647

## S. 218

SITES TON Tre ERTL roren

1.vıll TYPOLOGIES
or the lack of diagonal wind-braces. In particular, in Japan its
use is historically associated with its good seismic resistance.

The balloon frame is a recent timber building technique
derived from the box frame and first used in the 1830s, in
Chicago. On account of its practicality, low cost and speed of
assembly, it spread rapidly after the colonization ofthe North
American West. Its invention has been attributed to George W.
Snow who, as pioneer, entrepreneur, timber merchant and
public administrator, was the first to think ofadapting the box
frame with close posts, imported from England in the 17th
century by the early settlers in New England.

GIANCARLO CATALDI

1.Vl1,5.b Cruck frame

A traditional wooden technique, the cruck frame is
documented extensively in Great Britain, and also in Ireland,
parts of Brittany and other areas in France. Probably dating
back much further, it was diffused during the Middle Ages
especially in the countryside, being built sporadically there
until well into the 18th century, gradually being replaced by the
box frame.

Typically it consists ofa longitudinal series of large pointed
arches with a slightly bent frame, customarily formed by the
two square halves of the same trunk, mainly of oak or elm,
placed on end and in mutual opposition. The cruck timbers,
known as ‘blades’, may rise from a timber sill or from a low
stone base; they may meet or cross at the apex, or may terminate below the apex and be joined by a short yoke (Mercer). Its

static behaviour can be compared with thatofan imperfectarch
with three hinges, in which oblique forces may be partially
absorbed through reinforcements with tie-rods near to the
cusp in the case ofthe open trusses, and with tie-beams about
halfway up in the case of closed trusses, also used as boarded
floor beams.

Bach arch is assembled on the ground and is then raised, to
be connected to the others by longitudinal purlins. Particularly
important for this purpose are the ridge and two side purlins,
which not only fulfil a static role but are used to mark the
boundary between wall and roof. This is a crucial problem for
the process of development of the cruck frame, which originally may sometimes have resembled the sloping-roofed shelter, appearing, once the outer thatch roofing was complete, like
alarge roof supported directly on the ground.

With the diffusion ofthe box frame, this archaic cruck frame
form began to undergo an imitative process, which resulted in
its resembling a box-framed rural house, both through the
framing ofthe external vertical walls and the construction ofa
pitched roof oversailing the internal arches.

Its main variants are self-explanatory. The first, the base
cruck, has blades which rise from ground level but are truncated well below their potential apex and are joined together at
the head byatie or collar which carries the principal members
of the roof. In the raised cruck, the blades met at an apex but
start not from ground level but from some way up a wall. In the
upper cruck the blades rise to an apex from a tie-beam at orjust
below eaves level. Finally in the jointed cruck a curved or
cranked form is given to a truss by joining two lengths oftimbers, neither ofwhich is itselfa cruck (Mercer).

GIANCARLO CATALDI

1.V1l1.5.c Curtain wall

‘Curtain wall’ usually refers in architecture to
opaque elevational, non-supporting structures, specifically
destined to actas closure and separation buffers but unsuitable
on account oftheir nature to carry loads. Within the vernacular,
it is desirable to extend this definition to the cladding structures of shelters - even those that are not usually walled structures as it is from them that the formative processes of this
structural typology initiated. Certain walling processes can be
taken as having progressively arisen, through four consecutive
stages, corresponding to the same number of technological
categories: two mainly natural, in the form of vegetable mantles and animal hides, and two more specifically artisan-made,
such as reed mats and fabrics.

Vegetable mantles in leaves or bark are the simplest, most
immediate and efficacious system for protecting an elementary
structure against inclement weather, primarily against rain.
Documented in particular among tropical Australian and
Oceanic populations as sloping-roof shelters, their use in
building, albeit simple, still requires certain technical artifices
during preparation and laying. Leaves, in themselves waterrepellent, must be selected, aligned, partially overlapped and
reciprocally fixed with thorns or nooses; kinds of bark, which
in some cases, on account of their curved shape, can function
on their own, or with one or more self-supporting pieces, actas
