© Paul oliver 1975

protection (Rapoport). Normally cut in wide strips, they are
treated before being joined and made to adhere to the wooden
lath framework as in certain conical summer shelters of the
Asiatic and North American tundra.

Conceptually analogous, but more laborious procedures
involve the preparing and making ofanimal hide mantles, that
form the enclosing surfaces oftents scattered all over the globe,
where transhumant stock-rearing is generally practised. What
varies in these cases in the quality ofhides, naturally dependent
on the type ofanimal reared - bison, camels, llamas, reindeer,
yaks, and so on-thatrequire different processing according to
variations in size, shape and furs. However, generally speaking,
the greater weight ofthe covering structure entails the need to
reinforce the inner frame which in these dwellings offers distinctive solutions, that range from the Native American Indian
tipito Patagonian toldoesand the tents of Tuareg, Samiaand Inuit.
Separate mention must be made ofyurts in Central Asia, whose
felt covering of pressed animal hair forms a sort of blanket
cladding with good thermal heat insulation, making them
particularly suited to the severe climatie conditions ofthesteppe.

Reed mats, the third category of curtain walls, are also widely
documented as mantles of shelters and frame tents including
the dome-shaped agals of Somalia and the overturned-keel
shaped tent characteristic ofthe Ad Sceks, a nomadic tribe of
East Africa. Generally speaking, transhumant peoples in perideserts use reed mats, taking advantage of their lightness,
capacity for transpiration and, above all, their versatility. Apart
from the aforementioned coverage role, they are also often
used as floor coverings and litters, and as flexible devices to
close walls and entrances. On the Samoan islands, rolled-up
reed mats form, between one pole and another, the outer walls
oftypical oval huts, which system enables them to vary ifneed
be, their closing capacity according to both climatic and functional dwelling requirements. As for technical building procedures, they can be summarized in the basic types ofreed mats
with close, crossed and crossover linear elements.

Fabric curtains are inevitably associated in building with the
‘black’ tents of nomadic Bedouin tribes. In this type the roof
membrane, formed from a number ofwoollen strips woven on
a loom and stitched together longitudinally, plays a static and
functional role. The static role is related to its capacity to resist
cable traction, on which the overall stability of the tent ultimately depends; the functional role, however, is linked to its
capacity to create shade with an acceptable microclimate, on

Structural Types 1.v111.5
which the possibilities of survival in a desert environment
depend. Other curtains in a lighter fabric are generally used in
black tents as outer closures and inner divisions between the
living areas ofthe men and guests, and the sleeping areas of
their families and women folk; side curtains are often lengthened in the front to mark the pertinent areas.

In sedentary building cultures, the curtain wall tends to
assume characteristics of greater stability, ending up typologically and technologically, as a self-supporting closure. Curtain
walls of discontinuous wooden structures such as prefabricated bamboo panels, are widely employed in Southeast Asia
where their removability or partial convertibility are usually
closely related to the need for ventilation in topical areas with a
high rate of humidity. In other geographic areas with similar
climatic conditions - such as in Madagascar — fixed reed mat
walls are likewise documented, offering a rich range of formal
solutions. GIANCARLO CATALDI
1.Vv11.5.d Mass wall

The mass wall is a type of continuous wall structure
fundamentally based on the static principle of the heavy system, with materials placed one on top of the other, achieving
structural stability through the weight stemming from the progressive accumulation ofloads. Practically, this means that the
capacity of the structure to remain solidly anchored to the
ground depends on the total weight exerted on the foundation,
which in turn must be capable of offering the necessary resistance to provide equilibrium. Applications ofthis principle are
many, especially in relation to the kind ofmaterial used, including all types ofwood, earth and stone, whose specific gravity is

## S. 219

1.v1

TYPOLOGIES

usually the main static factor determining the form of these
structures.

Thelog construction technique- in German block-bau, where
yet this word is normally referred to more developed technique
oftrimmed and squared timber - is widely diffused, especially
in Europe, where it characterizes Alpine building, belonging to
mountain and Nordic areas with forests of conifers. Found
sporadically also in Asia, Japan and North America, where it is
more a product imported by colonials, it dates back to European protohistory. In fact, the archaeological sites of Biskupin
and Hallstatt date back to the Iron Age; these finds give a probable idea of the original formation mechanisms. In Biskupin
the trunks placed one on top of the other are introduced with
tenon and mortise between right-angular supporting poles; in
Hallstatt, however, stiffening was implemented autonomously
with the canonic solution of saddle trusses between alternately
staggered trunks of adjacent orthogonal walls. The angular
joint characteristic is statically efficacious if applied in closed
structural systems such as marking the limits of quadrangular
rooms. In these cases, block-bau forms a sort of box wall of
frames of mutually integral trunks, placed one on top of the
other whose total resistance depends on the type and quality of
the joint used.

In predominantly earthy areas such as the clayey alluvial
plains ofwide river basins, heavy wall structures are made with
two different constructional techniques: adobe and pise de terre.
Theair-dried brick- adobe in Spanish - is the oldest, most ductile and diffused prefabricated material in the history of building. Documented archaeologically in almost all major protourban cultures, including Egypt, Palestine, Mesopotamia, India,
China and Central America, clay Jump was shaped by hand
into various forms; but subsequently it was ‘mass-produced’
with the use ofwooden moulds. Laying in rows one on top of
the other necessitated the interposition of layers of mortar
to homogenize the overall static behaviour of the structure.
The subsequent innovation of the furnace-baked or fired
brick resulted in the improvement ofcompactness, weightand
resistance.

The pise de terre (derived from the French piser, meaning
‘press’) is a technique by which large rectilinear walls can be
built by compacting earth mixes with a low water content, in
specially-made moveable formwork. The thick and heavy layers
of earth, generally on a stone foundation bed, are connected
with special care at the corners, which are sometimes reinforced with diagonal wooden tie-rods or stone quoins. In certain Italian regions (Piedmont, Marches) and in Devonian
Britain the pressed earth wall, dried without the aid offorms, is
cut square along the outer surfaces with a large blade before
being finished with a protective coat of plaster.

Stone, as an essentially heavy material, is the best natural
resource with which to build amass wall. Regardless ofthetype
of stone available it is necessary to establish the form and average sizes of the quoins (or corner stones) and, in relation to
these the congruity ofthe mortar, which is proportional to the
tendency ofthe lithic element to shrink. Hence a loose distinction may be drawn between megalithic, mesolithic and
microlithic masonry, which may be further assessed with the

650

distinction made between ‘wet’ and ‘dry’ masonry; the latter is
defined thus on account ofthe variable presence ofthe binding
mix, which is interposed superficially to absorb friction
between the quoins. It becomes indispensable in the building
of masonıy in pebbles, shingles or stones, and even more soin
concrete masonry where lithic aggregates are progressively
reduced to the minimal dimensions ofpebble, gravel and sand.
As for the shape ofquoins, the general trend is the evolutionary
passage from an irregular structure to the ‘squared’ structure,
in which the horizontal rows of stone naturally allow improved
transmission of loads. However, it must be noted that in the
majority ofcases lithic vernacular architecture consists ofirregular, or rubble masonry. The partially modified elements are
cemented together with abundant thicknesses of mortar:
square quoins or bricks are most frequently used in corners and
in door and window openings. Sometimes they may also be
found in walls in regularly spaced layers, to enable the realignment ofthe irregular structures of rubble masonry.
GIANCARLO CATALDI

1.v1.5.e Post and pole
Itis probable thatthe archetypal reproduction ofthe
image of the trunk of a tree rooted to the ground represents,
from the evolutionary point of view, the beginning of human
construction processes (Cataldi, 1988). By driving a branch
with a pointed end into the ground, a fixed post thus statically
becomes a form of vertical cantilever. The ground performs as
a vertical bracket fixed to the foot, permitting a thin pole to be
more or less bent in the form ofan arch to the limit ofthe natural elasticity ofthe branch. In fact, ifthe branch is quite thick
and stiff, its structural behaviour changes as the post can transmit only vertical loads to the earth including its own weight. If
it is further supported with a series of ropes anchored to the
ground, the traction they sustain on its free end exerts a proportional increase ofvertical pressure. This compressive effect
can also be obtained by making other stiffburdening branches
restin a forked post. These three different static conceptions
schematically give rise to the main constructional applications:
palisades, wind-braced poles and carrying support posts.
Palisades are fences consisting ofa number ofclose-set fixed
posts. In this way, they form one ofthe clearest and immediate
examples of the self-supporting static system. However,

Farmhouse with
buttressed mass stone
walling, Murcia, Spain,

beyond their intrinsic repetitiveness, they imply the acquisition
of new compositional problems (which are as functional as
they are semantic), with regard to the elementary principles of
unity and polarity inherent in the solo role ofthe central post.
These problems, linked to the concepts of symmetry, axiality,
modularity and rhythm should probably be considered to be
a resultofthe process which derived from the original installation of the lateral entrance door-jambs, as early sedentary
cultures used palisades as boundary markers. However, this
same structural typology was also adopted in certain sporadic
examples of cylindrical-conical and quadrangular huts. This
applied to circular wooden fortress-walls, often reinforced
with ramparts, such as the boaires, or late medieval Irish
fortified farms (Culleton). But in recent times the closure ofthe
walls of a Kikuyu hut, for example, is obtained by setting the
posts close together. The principle of wind-braced poles,
which base their stability on the traction of cables anchored to
the ground that compresses them above, is the basis for the static and constructional mechanism ofthe ‘black tent’, typical of
nomadic Bedouin shepherds in the Arabian desert. Referred to
several times in the Bible, it is not only the prototype but the
mostrepresentative example ofthis structural category. Itarose
as a response to the extreme environmental challenge of living
in the desert. The constraints of very scarce material resources,
especially ofwood, inspired the invention of fabric as a thickly
protective membrane covering permitting a reduction in the
number of supporting poles, the traction of the cables on the
canvas roof driving the vertical pole into the ground. The pole
has to be modified at the top with a short ridge-piece, rounded
to decrease friction and hence to preventthe material from tearing. Where the sand or soil does not enable a secure fixing for
the poles, a sufficiently flat, wide stone may be placed at the
base ofthe pole to distribute the overall weight on the surface.
In geomaterial areas where wood abounds and the ground is
more resistant, it is the weight ofthe loads placed on top ofa

Structural Types 1.v111.5
post that determines the static equilibrium of the structure. In
fact ofall wooden buildings, the use of carrying support posts
appears to be the most universally diffused technique. Apart
from the appropriate environmental conditions, the method
assumes a technological and cultural level sufficiently
advanced to include the manufacture of tools or equipment
suitable for working stiff branches and average-sized tree
trunks. One of the most persistent vernacular architectural
conformations is the support provided by the forked post (Lassure, 1985, 1988). The trilinear structures of two forked posts
and a crossbeam are probably the most important structural
application, no doubt anticipating the trilithic structure, from
which in turn the evolutionary theory ofclassicism engendered
the architectural orders (Cataldi, 1983). In fact, it is this supporting structure which made possible two ofthe main wooden
typologies, shelters with flat or pitched roofs which initiated
the processes that gave rise to such widely diffused structures
as platforms and floors. GIANCARLO CATALDI
1.vı1.5.f Rigid arch

Asstructural typology characteristic ofthe static driving system, the rigid arch is based on the principle of mutual
contrast. According to this principle, building elements maintain areciprocal equilibrium by virtue oftheir opposite sloping
directions, transferring loads to the ground along oblique lines
rather than vertically by weight, as occurs in the heavy static
system. The resultant stress, defined as thrust, in the absence
of adequate countermeasures, would tend to make the structure pull apart, as the ground support would note sufficientto
counteract the horizontal forces. However, certain technical
artifices enable the system to lie within the safety limits.
Excluding the unreliable case of ground friction as a potential
self-balancing reaction, the elimination ofthe thrustofan arch
can be obtained with ties, props of buttresses. Ties are elements particularly resistant to traction: rods, wooden beams,
iron bars, and the like, placed transversally in the structure to
absorb in toto the horizontal components ofthrusts which have
atendency to pull the structure apart. These thrusts can also be
counteracted by props or buttresses, solid outer restraining
masses placed at the foot of the arch, whose effectiveness
depends on their height and length. Ifthe arch rests on direct
bearings and supports a superior weight, as did Roman triumphal arches, the vertical load tends to counterbalance the
thrust. Buta buttress acts as a lateral extension ofthe structure,

## S. 220

1.vıl TYPOLOGIES
is in Romanesque and Gothic churches, transferring the thrust
to the ground (Cataldi, 1974).

In vernacular architecture, therigid arch may have originated
as a technological development of the taut flexible arch
(Cataldi, 1988), converging with the spatial configuration of
the tripod, geographically one of the most widely diffused of
structural forms. Well documented in several functional roles,
it may be used as part of more complex structural systems; in
the chorama diu of Siberian Evenkis for instance, where many
tripods in a circle carry the weight ofthe roof, as functional furnishing for suspending receptacles over fires, hanging up food
for dıying, and especially in the solo role of supporting structure of conical shelters. Lighter and simpler in construction
this evolved as the frame and mat tent, one of the most widely
diffused nomadic dwellings in the world. In this type of tent,
which comes in numerous variants, the inspiring principle is
always more or less the same: the inner tripod - the first structure to be assembled - is the framework supporting the other
lighter arcs, which in turn bear the weight ofthe outer cladding.

In the subsequent developments of the cylindrical-conical
hut the tripod disappears — except for the rare, example ofthe
African Black Arabs (Cipriani) to be replaced, in prefabricated
variants withouta central pole, by an elastic system of converging rafters, built on-site and kept together by several circles of
restraint rings, enabling the conical roof ofthe hut, once completed, to be placed on the supporting outer walls.

But it is with the adobe that rigid arch structures take on the
circular profile, which in time becomes their most marked
characteristic. In this case, equilibrium is achieved by placing
mortar in between individual bricks, which from being parallelepipedal become in effect, wedge-shaped, thus bringing the
thrust forces into action. The oldest examples have been documented archaeologically in Mesopotamia (Davey), the circular
arch probably arising where wood was lacking, from the need
to span a room. Constructional mechanisms are ingenious,
varying according to the ground plan. Ifthe latter is rectangular
and lengthened, the arches are made to rest on oblique beds
specially prepared at the opposite end of the header walls. In
circular or square cells concentric and sloping arches are constructed with a gradually increasing radius, thus coming to
form opposite, conoidal self-supporting sectors: two diamettical sectors in the case of the circular plan, and four diagonal
sectors, squinches or pendentives from the corners of the
square plan which meet in the connecting points with the use
of ‘herringbone’ techniques of laying bricks. In some areas of
southern Iran this technique is still used to build dome-shaped
vaults in unfired brick (Davey). A different system ofrigid arch
made from corbelled earth reinforced with azara poles is built
by the Husa (Moughtin).

The transposition of the arch and vault principle to stone
construction entails greater constructional and technological
complexity, because of the considerable increase in loads
exerted by the stone mass and because ofthe intrinsic processing difficulties due to the hardness of the raw material. The
complexities of the latter give rise on the one hand to theneed
to have a strong, if provisional, wooden supporting framework
during assembly and on the other to asophisticated capacity to

652

cut the wedge-shaped voussoirs with a high level of craftsmanship, thus guaranteeing the adhesion of adjacent surfaces for
dry assembly. To conclude, it may be noted that this lithic structural typology is, from the point ofview ofthe history ofarchitectural language, particularly significant, because it is the
geometty of the form that determines the static behaviour and
not vice versa, as is the case in the majority of other structures.
GIANCARLO CATALDI

1.v11.5.g Tension arch
The tension arch is a basic wooden structure consisting offlexible branches, sufficiently long and fine to be able
to be driven into the ground and bent into an arch. In its pure
forms, presumably original, it is mainly documented among
nomadic hunters and gatherers (Jorrand), in natural environments that were rich in soft timber, easy to tear down without
specialist equipment. With a number of tension arches, intertwined in various forms, itis possible to build different types of
lightvaulted roofs. Statically, thatis an elastic system where the
carıying capacity of the structure is due to the arching of
branches, which can be considered a form of pre-tensioning
inverse and opposite to the principles of an architrave carıying
aload. This behaviour, specific to wood, varies according to the
degrees of stiffness ofthe kind ofwood used and the cross-section ofthe branches used, which must be neither too fine, as
they could break, nor too thick, because they could not be bent.
Herein lies the substantial distinction, primarily cultural and
secondarily technical, between the bent.arch, in its elementary
round arch form, and the semi-bent arch or equilateral arch
(Cataldi, 1988).
A single fine branch bent in the shape ofan arch is statically
an asymmetrie structure because its ends are bound to the

88

1940

ground in different ways. In fact, the rod is initially driven into
the ground vertically before being curved in a semieircle and
bound to the ground at its thinner end. This gives rise to what
can be defined as an imperfect hyperbound with a joint and
hinge, comparable to the principle ofpropelled weapons or the
automatically returning dynamic traps ofnomadic hunters. As
a dwelling structure this arch is usually used in hemispheric
dome-shaped shelters, whose frame consists of comparatively
close-set crossed orthogonal interlacements with a gradually
decreasing radius (Cataldi, 1986, 1988).

The semi-bent arch works differently from, albeit probably
originating from, the previous type, following the progressive
improvement oftools and the subsequent capacity to obtain tree
branches that are larger and stronger, but also less flexible. In
fact, the greater stiffness requires the use ofatleasttwo poles per
arch, making the structure symmetric and isostatic. As the two
rods are fixed to the ground, and bent until they are tied together
at the top, they are probably the prototype ofthe modern threehinged arch. With a greater number ofsemi-bentrods placed in
a circle, the cuspidate or pointed dome-shelter could be built
with its discontinuous ridge-point higher on average than the
continuous ridge ofshelters made with entirely bentarches. The
adoption ofthis variant, which permits standing space, has to be
completed with parallel interlaced arches which serve to encompass and stabilize the primary one, as exemplified by the
dwellings ofthe Gamos and Sidamos ofEthiopia (Cipriani). The
greatest technical difficulty lies in the securing ofthe upper joint
atthe meeting oftheribs, where the tension is greatest.

There is a great deal of ethnological evidence of these structures in Africa; among very varied regions and cultures, interesting applications ofboth the aforementioned typologies exist
in arange ofindividual variants, due to the convergence ofboth
human and environmental phenomena. Among forest pygmies, for instance, the dome-shaped shelter with bent arches
seems to be the most widely diffused form of dwelling, which
may be found with the same static principle in Kenya, Ethiopia,
Cameroon, Zaire, and elsewhere (Cipriani; Andersen). The
roofcladding varies and can range from a covering ofleaves or
branches of shrubs to a prefabricated roofing of reed mats or
animal hides. The latter are mainly associated with the frame
and mat tents of nomadic shepherds, such as the Somalian
agal. Significantly, with agriculture, straw becomes the principal protective roofing material, often accompanied by improvements in the lath frame. Sometimes laths are made with an
extremely precise weave, as in Zulu domed shelters, which are
so high and spacious that they usually require one or even several, support poles. Numerous applications of semi-bent
arches are documented in Africa, which are of great interest on
account of their technical and formal wealth, including the
homes of the Arussis (Kenya, Ethiopia), Cunamas (Ethiopia),
Kambas and Pokomos (Kenya), and others (Cipriani; Andersen; Cataldi, 1986). GIANCARLO CATALDI
1.v1.5.h Woven wall

Weaving, taken in its wider meaning ofintertwining
of vegetable and animal filiform elements, is probably one of
the mostancienttechniques devised by man overthethousands

Structural Types 1.1.5
of years of building activity. In his Der Stil in den Technischen und
Tektonishen Künsten (1860-3), Semper places it at the root ofthe
building process, deriving from it, inasmuch as it necessarily
consists of ‘soft’ matter, many of the rigid secondary forms of
historic architecture. This derivation is accentuated in the decorative and stylistic apparatus oflithic orders, where the formal
persistence ofnaturalistic elements, in particular vegetable elements, are a convincing sign of the historic existence of this
ancestral ‘textile’ skill (Egenter).

In vernacular architecture, the use of flexible branches may
even have pre-dated the real invention ofweavingin its more precise meaning ofthe artisan capacity to work woven fabrics on the
loom. In fact, in the earliest dwellings the construction principle
ofweaving seems closely connected to the static mechanism of
dome-shaped shelters, both in the original version with parallel
arches and in the cuspidate variant with meridian arches; in both
ofthem, structural solidity is guaranteed by the presence ofthe
crossed warp that elastically joins together all the tension arches
in a single resistant system. The more the weaving technique is
perfected, the more these circumstances are accentuated, as in
the characteristicexamples ofGamö and Zulu dwellings; itis not
by chance that the latter is called indlu, or ‘knitting’.

In reed mats the same principle applies in building-closing
and non-supporting prefabricated surfaces that are both light
and transportable; these qualities help to widen the functional
range in that they are used as roofing, floors, litters, division
walls, and so on, and expand the geographic area of their
diffusion. This technique thus becomes a customary prepara-

653

## S. 221

1.vıl TYPOLOGIES

tion for the advent of woven curtains. The latter in turn, as
roofing structures, closures, and membrane tents, reach a high
degree of efficiency and perfection, to the extent that they are
counted among the most typical products of all cultures survivingin a desert environment.

When applied to fences, the weaving technique reached its
prime, leading to the formation ofthe woven wall. In fact, these
non-supporting walling systems, originally consisting ofjuxtaposed, spaced-outwooden elements, not only fulfil the primary
role of defining space but physically obstruct trespassing by
closing the interstices between individual poles. Rigid posts
are horizontally connected with flexible rushes to form fixed,
continuous reed-mat walls well anchored to the ground. Ifthe
distance between the poles is excessive, transverse ring beams
are fixed in place, one at the top and the other slightly off the
ground in order to avoid soil dampness; between these in turn
are placed close-setvertical laths to form the warp for the weaving of flexible rushes. This probably gave rise to the designa-

654

tion under the common name of ‘trellis’ of both the rigid grid
ofthe frame, and its inner woven curtain wall, that is, whatwas
then to become the ‘trellis’, aword whose etymology (tri-licium)
clearly refers to the weaving technique of being “among
threads’. This conceptual ambiguity subsequently led scholars
to confuse the two terms, thus creating numerous errors of
interpretation.

During the Middle Ages, the diffusion ofthe box frame was
consolidated almost everywhere in Western Europe, in particular in the rural and town building of the wooded central and
northern areas. The use of this technique was probably inherited through the filter ofthe Roman world from more ancient
local building traditions. Adopted without significant variation
in the panels of the supporting frame, and in inner division
walls, the inner wooden trellis was usually completed on both
sides with a layer of an earthy mixture in turn protected with a
layer of mud plaster, in a process commonly known as wattle
and daub. GIANCARLO CATALDI
